THE
PARLIAMENTARY DEBATES
(OFFICIAL REPORT)
IN THE
FIRST SESSION OF THE THIRD PARLIAMENT OF NORTHERN IRELAND
APPOINTED TO MEET THE TWENTY-NINTH DAY OF MAY, IN THE
TWENTIETH YEAR OF THE REIGN OF
HIS MAJESTY KING
GEORGE V.
VOLUME XI.
THE SENATE.

---

Wednesday, 29th May, 1929.
The First Session of the Third Parliament of Northern Ireland appointed to
meet on 29th May, 1929, in the twentieth
year of the Reign of King George V, was
opened by the Governor (His Grace the
Duke of Abercorn, K.G., K.P.) in person.
The House met at Twelve of the Clock,
Mr. SPEAKER (Most Hon. the Marquess of Dufferin and Ava, D.S.O., D.L.)
in the Chair.
The Clerk read the Proclamation dissolving Parliament as followeth :-
:-
WHEREAS it is provided by section.
eleven of the Government of Ireland Act,
1920, as modified by and in pursuance of
certain enactments amending the said Act,
that the Governor of Northern Ireland
shall, in his Majesty's name, summon,
prorogue and dissolve the Parliament of
Northern Ireland.
AND WHEREAS it is desirable that
the present Parliament of Northern Ireland, which stands prorogued to Thursday, the sixteenth day of May next, should
be dissolved and that a new Parliament
should be called:
I,
JAMES
NOW, THEREFORE,
ALBERT EDWARD, DUKE OF ABER-
CORN, Knight of the Most Noble Order
of the Garter, Knight of the Most Illustrious Order of Saint Patrick, Governor
of Northern Ireland, by this Proclamation,
do publish and declare his Majesty's
pleasure that the said Parliament be as
from the date of this proclamation dissolved, and that the Senators and Members
of the House of Commons be discharged
from their meeting and attendance on the
said Thursday, the sixteenth day of May
next.
AND I do hereby make known to all
his Majesty's loving subjects his Royal will
and pleasure to call a new Parliament of
Northern Ireland, and do further declare
that I have taken order that writs shall be
issued forthwith in his Majesty's name
under the Great Seal of Northern Ireland,
in due form and according to law, for calling a Parliament of Northern Ireland to
meet at the City of Belfast on Wednesday,
the twenty-ninth day of May next, and for
causing the Senators and Commons who
are to serve in the said Parliament to be
duly returned to and give their attendance to his Majesty's said Parliament on
Wednesday, the twenty-ninth day of May
next, which writs are to be returnable in
due course of law.
Dated this 2nd day of May in the vear
of our Lord One Thousand Nine Hundred
and Twenty-nine.
ABERCORN.
His Grace the Governor of Northern
Ireland, being seated on the Throne, commanded the Gentleman Usher of the Black
A
Election of Speaker
Members Sworn
THE SENATE
Rod to let the Commons know "It is His
Grace's pleasure they attend him immediately in this House," who being come,
His GRACE was pleased to speak as
follows:
Members of the Senate and of the House
of Commons of Northern Ireland, in the
name and on behalf of His Majesty I have
to let you know that, as soon as the Members of both Houses shall have been sworn,
the causes of His Majesty's calling this
Parliament of Northern Ireland will be
declared to you; and, it being necessary
that a Speaker of the House of Commons
of Northern Ireland should be first chosen,
I declare His Majesty's pleasure that you,
Members of the House of Commons, do
proceed to the choice of a Speaker, and
that you present such person whom you
shall so choose here for His Majesty's
Royal approbation.
Then the Commons withdrew.
House adjourned during pleasure.
House resumed.
Speaker of the House of Commons.
His Grace the Governor, being seated on
the Throne, again commanded the Gentleman Usher of the Black Rod to let the
Commons know "It is His Grace's pleasure
they attend him immediately in this
House," who being come,
The SPEAKER-ELECT OF THE
HOUSE OF COMMONS (The Hon.
Henry George Hill Mulholland) said:
Your Grace, I have to acquaint you that
in obedience to His Majesty's most faithful
Commons of Northern Ireland have, in
the exercise of their undoubted rights and
privileges, proceeded to the election of a
Speaker, and that their choice has fallen
upon myself. I now present myself to
your Grace, and submit myself with all
humility for His Majesty's gracious approbation.
His GRACE: Mr. Mulholland, being
sensible of your zeal in the public service, and of your ample sufficiency to
execute all the important duties of the
position which his faithful Commons of
Northern Ireland have selected you
discharge, I do, on His Majesty's behalf,
most readily approve and confirm you as
their Speaker.
to
The SPEAKER OF THE HOUSE OF
COMMONS: Your Grace, I submit myself with all humility and gratitude to
His Majesty's gracious commands. It
is now my duty in the name and on behalf of the Commons of Northern Ireland to lay claim by humble petition to
the rights, privileges, and immunities assured to the Commons of Northern Ireland by the Government of Ireland Act,
1920, and that the most favourable construction shall be put on all their proceedings.
In regard to myself, I pray that, if in
the discharge of my duties I should inadvertently fall into any error, it may be
imputed to myself alone, and not to His
Majesty's most faithful Commons of Northern Ireland.
His GRACE: Mr. Speaker of the Commons of Northern Ireland, in the name
of His Majesty I do most readily confirm.
the rights, privileges, and immunities to
which you have laid claim.
With respect to yourself, Sir, although
His Majesty is sensible that you stand
in need of no such assurance, His
Majesty will ever put the most favourable construction on your words and
actions.
Members of the Senate and of the
House of Commons, it will now be the
duty of Members of both Houses of Parliament of Northern Ireland to take the
required oath.
To-day, at half-past Three o'clock, the
causes of His Majesty's calling this Parliament of Northern Ireland will be declared to you.
Then the Commons withdrew.
House adjourned during pleasure.
House resumed.
Members Sworn.
The Senators following took and Subscribed the Oath, or made and subscribed
the Affirmation required by law and
signed the Roll.
Mr. Speaker (the Most Hon. the Marquess of Dufferin and Ava, D.S.O.,
D.L.).

House adjourned until 3-30 p.m.
House resumed.
KING'S
SPEECH.
His Grace the Governor, being seated
on the Throne, commanded the Gentleman Usher of the Black Rod to let the
Commons know "It is His Grace's pleasure that they attend him immediately.
in this House."
The Commons being at the Bar with
their Speaker.
His GRACE delivered the following
Speech from the Throne:-
Members of the Senate and of the House
of Commons.
I feel confident that the new Parliament now inaugurated will conduct
with wisdom and moderation the important matters entrusted to its control.
My Government, recognising as it
does the great extent to which the
material prosperity of so many of our
people is dependent upon the revival
and development of our staple industries, will continue to foster the demand for Northern Ireland products
in British markets by a continuance of
the policy of active participation in the
various displays organised by the Empire Marketing Board
Board throughout
Great Britain, by encouragement of
the efforts made to stimulate a demand
within Northern Ireland for the goods
produced by Home manufacturers and
by the constant study and the dissemination, in close collaboration with the
overseas officers and the relative Departments, of His Britannic Majesty's
Government, of information respecting
trade conditions and trade openings
in Dominion, Colonial and Foreign
Markets.
My
Although I regret that many industrial workers are unfortunately still
unemployed, I observe with gratification that there has been a gradual
and steady improvement in the employment position since last summer.
Government will strive by every practical method to encourage Agriculture
and to foster the progress and the
expansion of Industry, and will continue to render financial assistance to
public bodies to enable them to promote
works of permanent benefit to the
community, which are designed to
afford additional employment.
It is my earnest hope that harmonious relations between employers
and employed should remain undisturbed and that a general spirit of
goodwill in Industry should prevail so
that nothing may be lacking which
would tend towards the fostering of
prosperity in our midst.
Proposals will be laid before you for
according relief of rates to Agriculture
and to Industry and for making appropriate financial provision for recouping
to Local Authorities sums in lieu of the
amounts from which Industry and
Agriculture will be exempted.
I am
glad that it has been found possible to
make arrangements, so far as agricultural land is concerned, that this concession shall operate retrospectively as
from the 1st April last.
You will be invited to pass a Bill
embodying proposals for the relief of
flooding in the area of Lough Neagh
and the River Bann.
Papers Presented
King's Speech
THE SENATE
It was found impracticable last
Session to consider proposals relative to
the amendment of the Law in connection with the control of Licensing of
Public Service Vehicles, empowering
the Royal Ulster Constabulary to
exercise the powers of inspection under
Part 1 of the Weeds and Agricultural
Seeds Act, 1909, and the amendment of
the Law relating to Bankruptcy. Bills
dealing with these matters will be submitted in due course for your consideration.
Other measures will be laid before
you dealing with Street Trading and
Dairying.
Members of the House of Commons.
The Estimates for the Public Services
will be laid before you in due course.
Members of the Senate and of the House
of Commons.
I am glad to observe that the importance of Electrical Development is
fully realised and that every encouragement is being afforded to schemes
designed for the purpose of linking up
the industrial centres of Northern Ireland by main transmission lines.
The Harbours around our shores
constitute such a valuable asset for
affording facilities for profitable employment that I am greatly pleased to
learn that the necessary work of dredging, rock removal and other improvements are to be vigorously proceeded
with by efficient up-to-date plant now
available for the purpose.
My Government will continue to
direct its efforts to the maintenance of
that atmosphere of law and order and
of contentment amongst the people in
which alone the establishment of new
industries may be encouraged and
the prosperity of existing industries
fostered.
I pray that the blessing of Almighty
God may rest upon your deliberations.
Then His Grace was pleased to retire,
and the Commons withdrew.
House adjourned during pleasure.
House resumed.

(N.I.), 1927, for Calendar Year, 1928.

for Qualification as Assistant Teacher
and as Junior Assistant Mistress.

Examinations for Teachers in Manual
Training, Domestic Economy and Drawing, Amending Regulations No. 1, 1929.
Certification, Salaries and Employment
of Teachers in Preparatory, Intermediate
and Secondary Schools, Regulations,
1929.
Regulations for the Secondary School
Certificate Examinations, 1929.

The National Health Insurance (Approved Societies) Amendment Regulations (No. 3) Provisional Regulations,
1928.
The National Health Insurance and
Contributory Pensions (Voluntary Contributors) Amendment Regulations
(Northern Ireland), 1929.
Presented (pursuant to Act) and
ordered to lie on the Table.
LOCAL BILLS.
Mr. SPEAKER laid upon the Table
a certificate that the Regulations embodied in the Resolution of the House
of Commons of the eleventh day of April
had been complied with in respect of the
following Bills:-
Belfast Water.
Londonderry Corporation.
Newry Urban District Council and
Newry Port and Harbour Trust.
Bangor (County Down) Improvement.
Timbey's Divorce.

I
Debate on Address
Debate on Address
29 MAY 1929
Mr.
SPEAKER having read
King's Speech,
Colonel SHARMAN
the
CRAWFORD:
(in the uniform of a Deputy-Lieutenant): I beg to move-That an humble
Address be presented to His Grace in
the following words."
Your Grace,
We, His Majesty's most dutiful and
loyal subjects, the Senate of the Parliament of Northern Ireland, beg leave
to offer our humble thanks to your
Grace for the Speech which your Grace
had addressed to both Houses of
Parliament.
In rising to propose that an Address
of Thanks be presented in reply to the
Speech from the Throne, I feel it as
a special compliment that I have been
selected to do so. It is usual on similar
occasions that a young man at the beginning of his career in the House is
chosen. But in my case, I fear it is at
the other end.
First, may I say how we all rejoice
that Divine Providence has permitted the
restoration of health to His Gracious
Majesty the King, and we will all shortly
join with the nation and the Empire in
joyful thankfulness for the same.
The Prime Minister before the late
election said that if he was returned to
power the next Session (the present one)
would not be spent in passing a series of
many Bills, but would be occupied in trying to improve the material prosperity of
our staple industries, which would lead
to the reduction of unemployment, which,
I am glad to say, is gradually
being done. Prosperity in trade means
a great reduction in unemployment,
and it also means an increase in
wages. It is now recognised that
wages do not account for much when
trade is good, but when trade is bad
wages hang round the life of business
like the burden of "the old man of the
sea.
Pardon me for this little digression,
but from the Speech just read we are glad
to see that the Government is going to
carry out its promise, and instead of the
usual flood of Bills to be introduced, it
foreshadows some useful Measures, in the
carrying out of which, in my opinion,
all parties can join. The first mentioned
is one which, like all other classes of production, is of no practical use unless you
have a market to sell its goods, and the
successful carrying out of this, as outlined in the paragraph dealing with it,
will accomplish this object.
The third paragraph provides for a
continuance of financial assistance to
public bodies which already has produced
such good results as is shown by the
merry music of the busy riveters on the
Queen's Island. The fourth paragraph
mentions a fact now well recognised, that
unless labour and capital work together
it is impossible to resist foreign competition.
Now I come for a few minutes to the
Bill of the programme, namely that of
derating, or according relief of rates to
agriculture and industry. I do not think
farmers have yet realised the great benefit
which this Measure has already begun to
give them more than twice the relief they
obtained under their reduction of rent,
rates having pressed on them so much
more than rent. At last a bright ray of
sunshine is spreading into what has been
for many years a darkened and unfortunate condition, and now, what with derating, and being in possession of their
lands at a reduced rental, and many approaching the period when all rent will
disappear, and they will become the actual
possessors of their farms, and with major
and minor schemes going on for drainage
I do not think I would be a false prophet
if I say a new era of prosperity is dawning
for farmers in the Northern Province.
And with the prosperity of the farmer
that of the labourer will also go on hand in
hand.
And now I come to the sixth paragraph
which proposes the introduction of a Bill
for the relief of flooding in the area of
Lough Neagh and River Bann. If I may
be permitted to say so, this is an old
chestnut, but at last the time is approaching when those who have suffered from
the flood will soon be on dry land.
Some other small Bills are to be introduced in which I can see nothing very
controversial.
In conclusion, I think the programme is a
practical one, and one for the benefit of our
whole Province. And I think therefore
the Prime Minister and his Cabinet are
entitled to the good-will and thanks of the
whole community not only for what they
have accomplished but for what they
intend to do. They have done everything
Debate on Address
Debate on Address
THE SENATE
[Colonel Sharman Crawford]
in their power to foster the cause of peace
and industry, realising that only through
the harmonious co-operation of all engaged
in industry can we hope to regain our
former high level of prosperity. I beg to
move.
Colonel PAKENHAM (in military
uniform): In rising to second the Address
of Thanks for the Gracious Speech from
the Throne, I feel very deeply the honour
paid to myself, a very junior Member of
this House, on being entrusted with this
duty, and the compliment paid to the
County from which I come, which last
week sent seven stalwart supporters of the
Government to sit in another place.
(Hon. Members: Hear, hear).
I need not apologise to this House if, as
the seconder of this motion, I shall have
to re-traverse some of the ground covered
by my right hon. and gallant Friend in
my remarks. It is impossible for me to
strike an entirely new line. We may, I
am sure, feel confident that this new Parliament will act with the wisdom and
moderation desired in the first paragraph
of the Speech. We have in this new Parliament new men, and we have old men who
have been there before, and they are all
imbued with the same sense of wisdom
and moderation. We know that the new
Government, which is the old Government, having been entrusted by the
democracy of Northern Ireland with a
further lease of power, with an increased
majority behind them, will continue to
work for the good of all the people.
Moderation and fairness to all has been
their slogan, and we can be certain it will
so continue.
First and foremost in the programme of
legislation for this first Session, stands the
Measure which, as my right hon. Friend
has said, will be introduced early and
passed, for the lifting of the burden of
the payment of rates on agricultural land
as from 1st April of this year. This
great boon given to our farmers transcends anything ever done for the industry
of agriculture, and I do not think its
value is yet realised through the country,
as there were undoubtedly large numbers
of the men to be benefited who, last week,
voted against the party promising this
generous gift. But perhaps they had
shrewd suspicions that their votes would
not much matter and that they would get
the benefit in any case. This splendid gift
a
calls for a worthy return by the recipients.
Now the way the farming community can
thank the Government is by looking forward
and by modernising their systems, to pay
attention to the good precepts and rules
laid down by the Department of Agriculture, not by hiding his talent under
napkin but by trying to use if for the benefit of his farm and for the benefit of the
agricultural labourer by affording him
more employment and better wages. If
that is done this Measure will be a blessing
to all, and it will apply equally to the derating of industrial concerns which is to
take place later in the year.
There is the declaration in the Speech
that the Government is alive to the possibilities of enlargement of markets and
will, to this end, work in the closest
collaboration with the Imperial authorities. I hope that among these activities
the Government will be able to impress
on the proper authorities the necessity of
their giving all possible consideration to
the claim of our great linen industry for
inclusion
among the Safeguarded industries.
Like my right hon. and gallant
Friend (Colonel Sharman Crawford) I
welcome the Bill, which I hope will become an Act this Session for the prevention of flooding on the banks of the
lough on which I live, Lough Neagh.
This old chestnut, as it has been described
by the right hon. and gallant Senator,
has been constantly cropping up since
1852, and we shall certainly at long last
be very glad to see an end of it.
The final paragraph of the Gracious
Speech, which says:-
My Government will continue to
direct its efforts to the maintenance of
that atmosphere of law and order 、nd
of contentment amongst the people in
which alone the establishment of new
industries may be encouraged, and the
prosperity of existing industries
fostered
We
will find an echo in all our hearts.
pray that peace may be maintained, and
that both Governments and people may
work together for the good of the country
so that with a sympathetic administration
of Acts of Parliament by the Government, and due appreciation by the people
of the difficulties of Government, a common ground of progress may be established and maintained. I beg to second
the Motion.
Debate on Address
29 MAY 1929

Debate on Address
man who seconded it, on the very able
manner in which they discharged their respective duties. Although the right hon.
and gallant Senator referred to his
seniority in this House, we all hope he will
be a Member of it for many years to come.
(Hon. Members: Hear, hear.) Like all
the Members who have already spoken, I
join in the expression of gratitude to
Providence for the restoration to health
of His Majesty the King. I regard the
legislative programme before the Government very favourably from the point of
view that although it is not to be very
lengthy it is to be of a very practical
nature. I think that in some respects
we have been rather overdoing it in the
matter of legislation, and I am very glad
to know that this Session will be occupied
in dealing with a comparatively few important Bills.
Mr. DORMAN: I should like in the
first place to congratulate the right hon.
and gallant Gentleman (Colonel Sharman
Crawford), and the hon. and gallant
Gentleman (Colonel Pakenham) on the
able manner in which they
very
respectively proposed and seconded the
Motion for the Address. I welcome the
decision of the Government to continue
to foster the demand for Northern Ireland products. In a Free Trade State
it is absolutely necessary that the Government should interest itself in producing the greatest possible proportion
of use values here. From that point of
view it seems to me that for many years
we in this country have lacked the necessary preferential treatment for the protection of our own capital and labour,
and consequently we have been from year
to year making a present to other
countries of our most valuable asset,
which is commonly called surplus labour,
in the form of emigrants.
SO
I am unable to conceive the great advantage the Government and their supporters think will be derived from the
derating scheme, as it affects either agricultural land or industry. It entirely
depends upon how the recipients of relief
under the Bill will allocate the grants
they receive and whether they will allocate them in the interests of the community as a whole. However, this is not
the place to enter fully into that question as the Bill will be brought before
this House in due course, but
far as the agriculturists of Northern
Ireland are concerned it seems to me that
the suggestion made by the right hon.
and gallant Gentleman (Colonel Sharman
Crawford) that the farmers should pass
on the relief they receive to those who
consume their produce is one well worthy
of consideration by the farmers. They
would thereby stimulate the demand for
agricultural produce and assist in
absorbing labour into the industries of
the State. In conclusion, I desire to
join the previous speakers in their expression of thanksgiving to Providence for
the restoration to health of His Majesty
the King.
re-
Mr. GLENDINNING: I rise to say
just a few words in support of the Motion
before the House. I congratulate most
heartily the right hon. and gallant Gentleman
who moved the Motion for the
Address, and the hon. and gallant Gentle-
I rose with the particular object of
expressing the hope that the Government
will not neglect to carry out the legislation they have already passed. Last
Session a very important Bill was passed
by the Government that had as one of
its main objects the making of the roads
of Northern Ireland safer for the public.
In that connection I desire to draw the
attention of this House and of the Government to an exceedingly serious motor
accident which occurred in the neighbourhood of Londonderry a few days ago,
in which a man and his wife lost their
lives and three children were seriously
injured. One of the purposes of the Act
passed last Session was to enable local
authorities to compel the owners to keep
hedges at corners and other dangerous
places cut so as to reduce the possibility
of accidents at those places, and the
accident to which I have referred could
have been avoided if the hedge at the
spot where it occurred had been cut.
The Resident Magistrate who adjudicated in the proceedings which arose out
of this accident remarked that he would be
glad to see legislation passed by the
Government to compel the owners of land
having hedges at dangerous parts of the
road to keep those hedges cut down to the
lowest possible level. If that had been
done in this particular case this unfortunate accident would not have happened.
A similiar accident might occur
at any
time, and particularly at this time of the
season when hedges are growing. In conclusion, I fully approve of the legislation
that is before the Government, and I have
great pleasure in supporting the Motion.
Debate on Address
Debate on Address
THE SENATE
Mr. McCORKELL: I rise to concur in
the Motion for the Address in Reply to the
Speech from the Throne, but at the same
time I would like very briefly to sound a
note of warning in regard to the matter
of derating, particularly in its application.
to agriculture. As the Mayor of a city in
Northern Ireland it may be thought
strange that I should take it upon myself
to make any comment upon a matter of
this nature, but I would remind this House
that that city depends very largely on the
agricultural community. If it were not
for the agricultural community in its
vicinity it would find it hard to live, even
with its own active industries. It is true
that this effort to relieve rates on agricultural land and buildings is the best thing
that has been done for agriculture in my
time, but at the same time the farmer is
a man who at the moment is not looking
forward to great prosperity. Like the
people on the Bann, he is looking for something to stop the waters that are about to
drown him, for something that will take
him out of a state of absolute want.
I think it is a fact that the only farmers
who have kept themselves going for many
years are those who are their own
labourers. Those who have had to pay
labourers have paid them out of their own
pockets. Therefore it would be impossible
for the farmers to hand over directly the
benefits received from derating, but they
can be handed over indirectly, because if
derating had not come about many more
farmers would have had to give up, and
there would thus have been more unemployment. On this account I welcome the
Measure, although it will merely touch the
fringe of the question. Agriculture in
this country cannot be sheltered as other
industries can. It cannot be given the
same shelter from the wind of foreign competition. This country is flooded with
every conceivable kind of foreign agricultural produce, whether it be healthy or unhealthy. Therefore, I just rose to sound
this note of warning. Still the farming
community welcome derating and we who
represent Derry City, which flourishes on
agriculture, regard it as a very statesmanlike Measure, and are grateful for it.
Mr. LESLIE: The primary purpose of
the Resolution at the moment before the
House is, of course, to express our thanks
for the gracious Speech from the Throne.
But I do not think that that is the full
measure of our gratitude, for I think we
must not omit to return thanks to the right
hon. and gallant Gentleman who moved
the Address, and to the hon. and gallant
Gentleman who seconded. We are all, I
know, delighted when we have an
opportunity of hearing our old Friend,
the right hon. and gallant Gentleman,
addressing the House. Some of us
cast our memories back to those years
when the right
right hon. and gallant
Gentleman was one of four whom we still
remember and who did much for us.
The gallant Senator who seconded the
Address spoke of himself as a recruit to
this assembly. I do not remember any
recruit who came here with higher credentials-service on London County
Council, and contesting a seat in the
interests of the party to which most of us
belong, in addition to long and honourable
military service. I do not think any of us
in County Antrim would have forgiven the
hon. and gallant Senator had he appeared
this afternoon otherwise than in the uniform of that distinguished regiment with
which his family has for so many years
been honourably connected.
Passing to the Speech itself the main
point is undoubtedly the intimation that
the necessary legislation to give relief in
rates will be brought before us during this
Session. I have spoken more than once at
length and in more places than one of the
advantages which may be expected to flow
from that Measure, but I confess that I
have heard this afternoon of an advantage
which I had not thought of before. I owe
to the hon. Senator in front of me (Mr.
Dorman), who succeeded the seconder of the
Address, the suggestion that this derating
relief can be used to enable the recipients
to put more capital into their business. I
think if derating is used as a means of increasing the capital of the farmers many
benefits may be expected. It will certainly
be possible, as has been already stated by
the hon. Senator the Mayor of Derry, to
keep up the standard of the number of
labourers employed, and perhaps also of
employment. It may be possible to improve the methods of production and perhans-though I doubt if this last proposal
will commend itself to farmers-it may be
possible to reduce prices. At all events
we shall all recognise that the derating
proposal is a very honest endeavour to give
help where help is very sorely needed.
It has been said that the ensuing Session
will not be prolific as regards legislation—
that is perhaps with regard to public
legislation, but I hope time will be found
to deal with certain private Bills which are
Debate on Address
Debate on Address
29 MAY 1929
of some importance. I am thinking, of
course, particularly of the proposal to reconstruct the bridge leading to the City of
Derry. I am sure we should all regret if
this Session came to a close without that
necessary Measure being added to our
Statute Book.
In conclusion, it remains only to say I
heartily support the Address which has
been moved.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: In
rising to speak in support of the Resolution
I would like first to correct what I think
was a mistake. The right hon. and
gallant Senator who moved the Address
spoke of himself as getting on in years and
another Senator agreed. I think amongst
the Senators here there is not a younger
man in the room-(laughter)—than the
right hon. and gallant Senator. I would
like to put on record a correction of what
I think was a mistake.
I am very glad to see that the Government is going to push on the trade of the
country. I would just like to call attention to one thing. I have always thought
it necessary to advertise our goods and to
push our goods, but I think we should try
to avoid that competition which we meet
with from other markets. We send
damask and other flax goods out, and
against them we have competing cotton
goods with a linen finish. I think the
Government might in some way provide an
inspector to go into places where these are
offered. If we could have our linen goods
verified and certified as pure linen and not
cotton I think it would help us in our
competition.
There is another thing omitted from
the Speech which I would like to see in
it. That is, there should be some way
by which all appointments made and paid
for out of public money should be
Gazetted. I think it is unfortunate that
if a public servant is appointed it is not.
Gazetted. It would be a simple thing to
do, so that a man would not have the
right for more than a couple of months
to the salary or wages until he was
Gazetted. It would not interfere with the
appointment, but it would give the public-those who found the money-an opportunity of criticizing these important
appointments. I just mention these
points.
I have great pleasure in supporting this
Address and in saying how happy I feel
that the Government are not starting
with an impossible programme. I think
the programme is one they intend carrying out.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): Before dealing with the
legislation referred to in the gracious
Speech, I should like to echo the feeling
of thankfulness at the recovery of His
Majesty the King, expressed by the right
hon. and gallant Member who moved the
Address and by other speakers. (Hon.
Members: Hear, hear).
I must offer my congratulations to the
Mover and Seconder of the humble Address to be presented to His Grace. I
need not dwell upon the qualifications of
either of them, as they are both well
known and held in honour in our Province. The eloquent manner in which
they have performed their task has been
a pleasure to us all.
To-day is the first meeting of the third
Parliament of Northern Ireland, and I
think that the Government may congratulate itself upon the vote of confidence
which it has received from the electorate.
It has been returned to power with a
substantial majority, which brings with
it the responsibilities which power always
brings. Those responsibilities are, I can
assure the House, fully realised by the
Government, and it will be our aim to
pursue a programme of legislation which
has as its end the restoration of prosperity to our country, and the improvement of the conditions of our peopleobjects towards which, I think we may
fairly claim, substantial progress has al-
Governments cannot,
ready been made.
in my opinion, create prosperity. They
can, however, do a certain amount to assist in its creation, and a great deal which
might hinder it. I can with all sincerity
say that I believe our Government has
given every possible assistance, and refrained from any possible hindrance during its term of office. We shall continue
this policy and I am convinced that it
will have its reward, allied, as it will be,
with the known character and common
sense of our people, which will ensure
those cordial relations and co-operation
between employers and employed which
the right hon. Senator who moved the
Address rightly regards as so important.
With regard to the question of the safeguarding of the linen industry, I can assure the House that the Government will
take every possible step to assist the effort
which is being made to prevent the dump-
Debate on Address
Debate on Address
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
ing of foreign goods into the home market. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear).
Certain of the measures foreshadowed
in the Gracious Speech are Bills which
the Government had intended to introduce last session and have already been
dealt with by me on a previous occasion
in this House. One of the most important of these is the Local Government
Finance Bill, which will provide for the
bringing into operation of the Government's Derating Scheme. There is one
modification of the original proposals
which will afford a substantial relief to
our agricultural industry-the rating relief for agricultural land and farm
buildings will be granted as from the 1st
April last. Every Member of this House
will, I am sure, agree with the right
hon. Senator in his estimate of the beneficial results which must accrue to agriculture and industry from the Measure.
Amongst the Bills postponed from last
session there are also the Bankruptcy Bill,
which will provide the much-needed
modernisation of the Law of Insolvency,
and a Bill dealing with the control of
road passenger transport.
Probably one of the most interesting
Bills which will be brought before you
will be that providing for the carrying
out of a scheme for the better drainage
of Lough Neagh and the River Bann. I
know of no subject which has caused so
much local interest, and about which so
many resolutions have been passed, as
Bann drainage, and, indeed, those of us
who are familiar with the problem and
have knowledge of the damage which is
caused year after year by the flooding of
the River Bann and its tributaries cannot but feel thankful that steps are to be
taken to deal with the drainage of the
catchment area.
The problem has been receiving the
carnest consideration of the Government
for some years past; the Government's
engineers have been considering the
technical aspects of the problem, and
their proposals have been submitted to
and approved by other experts; the financial details have been dealt with by committees, and by conference with certain
of the local authorities concerned, and
we are now in a position to proceed with
the execution of a scheme which will, it
is hoped, result in the reduction of
flooding to a minimum. I am glad to say
that it has been possible to devise a
scheme which, whilst it deals adequately
with the problem of flooding, does not
prevent the utilisation of the water of the
catchment for the production of electricity, and that there is every prospect that
within a very few years the flood waters
of Lough Neagh and the River Bann,
which have so long been a menace and a
nuisance to our country, will be converted
into valuable assets.
With regard to Education, it is not proposed to introduce during the present
Session any legislation for meeting the requests for amendment of the Act of 1923
which have recently been put forward, but
I may say that the Government has been
successful in reaching an agreement on
broad principles with those who urged
amendment of the Act, and we shall, I
believe, be able to go a substantial distance
towards meeting their wishes. The
Government feels, however, that any legislation must be the subject of mature and
earnest consideration from every point of
view
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD:
Hear, hear.
Viscount CHARLEMONT-and that
the details of any amendments to be made
should, as far as possible, be settled after
consultation and by agreement with all the
parties interested, so that none may be
antagonised, but rather that all may be
given the fullest opportunities to assist in
achieving the object at which each of them
aim-that is, the provision of the very best
education facilities for the children of
Northern Ireland. If we all keep that
object in view, I do not think we shall have
very much difficulty in bringing in an
agreed Measure which will go a long way
towards resolving some of the doubts and
fears which have been prevalent of late in
certain quarters.
I do not propose to delay the House
longer by going into the details of the
various other Measures which we propose
to bring before you. Many of them consist
merely of minor amendments to cover
matters of difficulty which have been experienced in the administration of existing
legislation, whilst others will commend
themselves as being Measures for bringing
the law of Northern Ireland into line with
progressive legislation in other countries.
I think that we shall be able to congratulate ourselves when we have completed the session's programme.
Sittings of the House
Adjournment
29 MAY 1929
Looking back over the years that are
passed, we
our present
may regard
position as very satisfactory, for, although
no doubt our trade and prosperity are not
everything that might be desired, nobody
can deny that we have made great progress.
Our financial stability is unimpaired; the
social conditions of our people have been
maintained, and in many directions improved; our children are receiving better
education, and in more healthy surroundings, and so far from falling behind other
nations in the world's progress, we are in
as favourable a position as any to take advantage of that new era of prosperity
which will, I am convinced, come, and
come soon. (Hon. Members: Hear,
hear).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Resolved: That a humble Address be
presented to His Grace in the following
words:
Your Grace,
We, His Majesty's most dutiful and
loyal subjects, the Senate of the Parliament of Northern Ireland, beg leave to
offer our humble thanks to Your Grace
for the Speech which Your Grace has
addressed to both Houses of Parliament.
Ordered: That the said Address be presented to His Grace by Privy Councillors
of Northern Ireland.
Bankruptcy (Amendment) Bill (S.)
Moved, "That this Bill be now read a
First time."-(Viscount Massereene and
Ferrard).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill ordered to be read a Second time
on Thursday, 6th June.
Viscount
Adjournment.
CHARLEMONT:
Before
actually moving the Adjournment of the
House I should like to consult the wishes
of hon. Members as to the date to which
the House should be adjourned. The
Bankruptcy (Amendment) Bill is in the
hands of the printers, but it will not be
available to hon. Members until Saturday
or Monday morning. I do not know
whether hon. Members would consider that
they had not sufficient time to study that
Bill if I moved the adjournment of the
House until Thursday week, on which day
we would take the Second Reading of the
Measure. As it is rather a technical Bill
perhaps Members would prefer to adjourn
until the following week, but if we take it
then that is the week after next-it
will be necessary for the House to sit tomorrow to dispose of certain formalities in
connection with the appointment of Committees.
Mr. LESLIE: I cannot help thinking.
that to-morrow is not a particularly desirable day for the sitting of this House.
It occurs to me that if, as one may anticipate, the main purpose of the Bankruptcy Bill is to assimilate the law in
Northern Ireland to the law in England,
it should not occupy so very long in what
I may call the preliminary stages.
course, if there are any startling innovations in practice it will be a different
matter, but otherwise we might be able
to take the Second Reading on Thursday
next week, if that meets with the approval
of the House.
Viscount
Of
CHARLEMONT: Then I
beg to move, "That this House do now
adjourn until to-morrow week."
Mr. GLENDINNING: I should like
to ask if the noble Leader of the House
can give us any information as to the
future sittings of this House.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD:
I understand that after sitting for a short
time to get through the necessary business the other House is going to adjourn
for a long period.
I cannot
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
give the House much information, because the sittings of this House depend
very largely on the business in the other
House. So far as this particular House
is concerned we have the Bankruptcy Bill
to consider, and we may also get the Expiring Laws Continuance Bill. Half the
Members of the Senate are due to retire
on or about 11th June, when an election
will take place, and I should like to get
whatever legislation we have passed by
then, for obvious reasons.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at
Twenty-five minutes before
Five o'clock until Thursday, 6th June.

Bankruptcy (Amendment) Bill [S.]
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): In asking the House to give a
Second Reading to this Bill, I think I
can safely say that the reforms which it
proposes to bring about are such as will
be welcomed, not only by those professional interests associated with the administration of Bankruptcy Law, but
also by organised commercial opinion,
and traders of the country generally.
Hon. Members will be aware that in the
Autumn of 1926 a small but highly
specialised and representative Committee
was set up to consider what amendments
in Bankruptcy Law, as it applied in
Northern Ireland, experience had shown
to be desirable. We were fortunate
enough to have as Chairman of this Committee my hon. Friend, the Parliamentary Secretary to the Ministry of Education, who, as hon. Members are aware,
is a recognised authority on Bankruptcy
Law. The Committee secured evidence
from all the most important commercial
and professional interests in Northern
Ireland, and presented a unanimous report, which was adopted by the Government as the basis of the Bill now before
the House.
Furthermore, as soon as the reforms
which it was proposed to bring about had
assumed concrete form they were once
more submitted to the critical examination, not only of the members of the Committee of Inquiry, but also of the
principal commercial and professional
I
Bankruptcy Bill—
Second Reading
6 JUNE 1929
associations, so that the Bill, as ultimately
drafted, embodies in a complete and
acceptable form the results of the considered advice of those best qualified to
express an opinion on this intricate and
important branch of commercial law.
Before referring in any detail to the
provisions of the Bill, I should like to
make a brief reference to the legislative
position as it exists to-day. It may be
said that for all practical purposes the
Law of Bankruptcy in Ireland is just
over 70 years old. The law took definite
shape in the Irish Bankrupt and Insolvent Act, 1857. This Act was amended
in some respects by the Bankruptcy
(Ireland) Amendment Act, 1872, but
beyond this there has been no attempt
a comprehensive revision in
Ireland, and so, to-day, the Act of 1857
still stands as the corner stone of our
Bankruptcy Code. In this respect we
to secure
are far behind Great Britain. There the
whole Law of Insolvency, relating both
to Bankruptcy and to private arrangement, was revised and consolidated in
1913.
Bankruptcy and arrangement
matters were given separate Acts in 1914.
Even the Bankruptcy Act of 1914,
complete as it was, was amended in certain important particulars in 1926, by an
Act which owed its inspiration largely to
Chambers of Commerce, Bankers, and
business men generally, who desired to
see the law still further modernised to
bring it more into accord with post-war
business conditions. I do not suppose
that there is any branch of commercial
law where the legislative position in
Northern Ireland is in such contrast to
that in Great Britain as that which the
House is considering to-day, and hon.
Members will appreciate that, in view
of the close commercial links which bind
Northern Ireland with Great Britain, it
has been necessary, in drafting this
Measure, to have constant regard to the
legislative position in England, so as to
eliminate as far as practicable the radical
differences in the law of the two countries.
With regard to the changes which we
propose to make, I should like to say, in
the first place, that it is not our intention
to
make any radical change in the
machinery of administration. On this
point I may, perhaps, be permitted to
quote from the Report of the Committee
of Inquiry:-
"All the professional witnesses familiar
with local bankruptcy practice told us
that it worked very well, and that the
people had become accustomed to it.
Under these circumstances we do not
recommend a change in the existing
system."
The Committee did, however, make one
suggestion for speeding up the procedure
which the Government has considered it
very necessary to adopt. I refer to the
provision contained in Sections 4 and 5
of the Bill for speeding up the court
machinery by abolishing the second sitting
before the court both in arrangements and
The
in Compositions after Bankruptcy.
saving of time, not less than a fortnight,
and of expense will be found to be of the
greatest advantage, coming, as it does, in
the earlier stages of the proceedings. I
do not propose to weary the House with a
detailed reference to all the Clauses of
the Bill, some of which are of a highly
technical nature. There are some, however, of great practical importance, which
it is incumbent upon me to explain to the
House.
In the first place, a radical change has
been made with regard to the scope of
bankruptcy offences and the penalties provided. Hon. Members will notice on
reference to Clause 22 that the three
principal commercial irregularities, which
post-war conditions have considerably
aggravated, have been singled out for the
special penalty of penal servitude for any
term not exceeding five years, or to imprisonment for a term not exceeding two
years, with or without hard labour. These
offences in relation to bankruptcy are:
obtaining money by fraud; obtaining
property under the false pretence of carrying on a business, and disposing of property otherwise than in the normal course
of trade.
:
The last-mentioned offence--disposing
of goods otherwise than in the ordinary
course of business-is also further distinguished the pledgee or receiver of the
goods with a knowledge of the circumstances is deemed to be guilty of a
misdemeanour. The next Clause, No.
23, also contains a very necessary reform,
making it a misdemeanour for an undischarged bankrupt to obtain credit to the
extent of £10, or upwards, without disclosing his bankruptcy. Important evidence was tendered to the Committee
touching the losses sustained by Northern
Ireland traders through the weakness of
our law in these respects, and it has been
Bankruptcy Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
felt that these offences are such as should,
in the interests of the creditor, have a
I can
greater penalty attached to them.
assure the House that the honest and unfortunate debtor is in no way prejudiced
by the provisions I have mentioned. Our
obfective is the dishonest, evasive, or
reckless debtor, and I feel sure that hon.
Members will agree that against such a
one a creditor deserves all the protection
he can get.
Another important provision is the
liability to keep proper books of account
imposed by Clause 25 of the Bill. In
English law the failure of a bankrupt to
keep books has always been regarded as
a serious bankruptcy offence, justifying
suspension or the refusal of a discharge.
but it was not until the Act of 1914, to
which I have referred, that it was made a
misdemeanour, and even then only on the
trader's second bankruptcy. Now, under
Section 7 of the Bankruptcy Act of 1926,
this distinction is swept away, and a bankrupt failing to keep the usual books of
account as defined by the Section is liable
to prosecution on his first bankruptcy
The Clause to which I am now directing
the particular attention of the House
virtually adopts the legislative position as
it exists in England to-day.
Hon. Members will observe that distinction is made between a first-time
bankrupt and one who has previously
failed. The former is not to be prosecuted
unless his liabilities amount to £500 or
over, but in the case of a debtor who has
previously failed prosecution may be
instituted if the liabilities amount
to £100 There is no doubt that
in the matter of keeping even elementary accounts there is great need
for improvement, especially among the
small traders in our cities and country
towns, although in recent years the operation of the Income Tax Acts has
brought about some improvement in
this respect. On the question of
possible hardship, I think it will be
agreed that the limit of liability to
which I have referred will prevent
the Clause from pressing unduly on the
very small trader whose turnover is so
negligible as to render the keeping of
accounts comparatively unimportant. It
is not the intention of the Government,
however, to introduce these particular
provisions as to the keeping of accounts
without warning, for it will be seen that
a period of two years from the passing of
the Act is being allowed so that traders
may put their house-or rather their
books in order during the period of
grace.
We also propose to make some radical
changes in the Law of Bankrupty as it
affects married women. These changes
are given effect to in Clauses 10 and 20
of the Bill. Some of the witnesses who
appeared before the Committee advocated
the imposition on married women of rerestrictions of a somewhat drastic
character in regard to trading. It has
been felt, however, that it would be inadvisable to proceed in advance of Great
Britain in this matter. There a married
woman who carries on a trade or business
is liable to bankruptcy proceedings irrespective of whether her trade or business
is carried on separately from her husband
or not, and is also liable to be served with
a Bankruptcy Notice on a Final Judgment or Order, as though she were personally bound to pay the debt. Furthermore, the husband of a bankrupt married
woman
is not entitled to claim any
dividend as a creditor in respect of any
money or other asset lent or entrusted by
him to his wife for the purposes of her
trade or business until all claims of the
other creditors of his wife have been
satisfied. Similarly, in the bankruptcy
of the husband, the claims of the wife are
postponed to those of other creditors.
""
In Ireland the position of a married
woman is goverened by the Married
Woman's Property Act, 1882, Section 1
(5) of which provides that "every married
woman carrying on a trade separately
from her husband shall, in respect of her
separate property, be subject to the Bankruptcy Laws in the same way as if she
were a femme sole." Thus, as the law
stands at present, a married woman in
Northern Ireland cannot be made bankrupt, unless she carry on a trade, and
carry it on separately from her husband.
Hon. Members will, I am sure, agree that
for married women to continue to enjoy
this privileged position constitutes an
anomaly which is completely out of accord
with post-war business conditions, and
one which should be removed without
delay.
Another matter to which I desire to
make a brief reference is the scope of the
various Acts of Bankruptcy, as defined in
our Bankruptcy Code. Here there has
been no great widening of the net through
Bankruptcy Bill-
Second Reading
6 JUNE 1929
In
the years, for there is a remarkable simi-.
larity between the Acts of Bankruptcy,
as enumerated for England in Section 1
of the Act of 1914, and the list for Ireland
set froth in Section 21 of the Bankruptcy
(Ireland) Amendment Act, 1872.
this matter we propose to go a step further
than the legislative position in England,
by making, in Clause 1 (b), a Return of
"No Goods" by the Sheriff, an Act of
Bankruptcy in Northern Ireland. Apart
from the fact that this reform was advocated by nearly all the professional and
commercial interests that gave evidence
before the Committee, it is difficult to
appreciate why a debtor, who has no goods
to seize, should escape this penalty, which
attaches to debtors who have goods subject to seizure. In one respect, however,
we propose to bring the law into line with
England by making, in Clause 1 (d),
"Fraudulent Preference" an Act of Bankruptcy.
Other reforms to which, perhaps, a
passing reference may be made at this
stage are-
The sweeping away of the old distinctions between traders and non-traders,
which left the latter in a very sheltered
position (Clause 1).
Preventing creditors from unreasonably withholding their consent to a
Deed of Assignment outside the Court
where the debtor has secured a proper
measure of support for his proposal
(Clause 2).
Giving an option to a creditor of a
deceased insolvent of proceeding in the
Bankruptcy Court, instead of in the
Chancery Division (Clause 21).
Imposing penalties for rash speculation or gambling by bankrupts (Clause
24).
I have now, I think, explained the
Clauses of most importance, and those
to which the commercial community will
give the greatest consideration. I should
say, in conclusion, that the Government is
entirely in agreement with the views expressed by the Committee as to the necessity for a Measure consolidating the law
of Bankruptcy. We shall take this into
consideration at a later date. We are
glad to have present to-day the Minister
of Commerce, who will reply to any points
raised in the course of the debate. I beg
to move.
Mr. LESLIE: I must agree, as I am
sure the House will agree, that the time
is fully ripe for the introduction of a
Measure of this description. It may seem
an unchivalrous thing to say, but I am
afraid that one of the chief reasons why
it is necessary that we should deal with
the law of bankruptcy in Northern Ireland is the increasing trading on the part
of married women. I am afraid that
that is so. Again, it is obvious that the
distinction, the remaining distinction, between traders and other debtors to which
the noble Viscount drew attention, cannot
be allowed to continue.
Before dealing with the Bill itself I
should like, for a moment or two, to pay
my humble tribute to the Report of the
Robb Committee. It is, if I may say so,
a Report most admirably conceived in
order to introduce to the entirely lay mind
a very difficult and a very technical subject. It is, I think, fairly possible by
studying the Blue Book to obtain a knowledge of the law of bankruptcy as it at
present applies in Northern Ireland,
which otherwise would have to be sought
with diligence in the pages of the monumental volume prepared and edited by the
hon. and learned Member for the University (Mr. Robb), who presided over the
Committee.
Passing from that, I should like to say
that the point in the Report which meets
with my peculiar approbation is the point
to which the noble Viscount alluded at the
conclusion of his speech, and that is the
reasonable prospect of an approaching
consolidation of the law. That consolidation is much to be desired. I have
already said the subject is very technical.
As a matter of fact, the Bill before us is
really a rather difficult Bill to follow.
think it has been made as easy as possible,
but until we get consolidation it is quite
imposible to produce any Bill dealing with
the subject which does not in every page
of it violate our views or our preference
for the avoidance of legislation by reference. In a Bill of this sort you simply
cannot help it, and it has not been helped
on this occasion.
I
The law of bankruptcy itself is the
creation-I will not say of civilization,
but, at all events, it is the creation of
Statute Law, and when we try to deat
with it we have to approach the question
with a very wide point of view before us.
We must not approach it with any view
to the satisfaction of Shylock, nor, on the
Bankruptcy Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
other hand, are we particularly concerned
to secure the salvation of Antonio from
sacrifice. We must treat the thing as
fairly as we can, and I was glad to recognize in the speech of the noble Viscount
that that point of view had been before
the Government. There was to be no undue pressure on the man who had been unfortuntate. That, I think, is a matter on
which we should congratulate ourselves.
I
There is one particular point in the Bill
aiming in that direction, and that is for
giving greater authority, shall I say, to
the acquiescence of the main body of the
creditors in a scheme of composition.
am sure that is a matter which is of very
great importance. It will obviate a
certain amount of hardship and will work
to the great advantage of the great
majority of what I may describe as reasonable creditors. I certainly agree that it
is very desirable that we should save time
by abolishing what may be described as
the second meeting, and I also agree that
we are doing well in avoiding any violent
changes in the law. If we are doing well
in that we are doing still better in making
our law in general accordance with the
law which prevails across the water.
Having regard to the alliance of our commercial interests here with affairs across
the water, it is, I think, plain that business must be restricted perhaps to some
extent, or at all events impeded if there
is any great difference on such a vital
question as to what is to be done in the
case of insolvency. It will be to the
advantage of both sides of the channel
that the code should be, as nearly as
practicable, similar.
I am glad that no attempt is being
made in the Bill before us to deal with
the somewhat difficult question of the
trading of minors. I am sorry, but I
must approve that it has been necessary
to take the course of leaving out of consideration the question of the priority of
Crown debts in Northern Ireland. I am
afraid it has to be admitted that it would
not have been within the competence of
this Parliament to deal with that question, and so to introduce the advisable
reform of assimilating the position in
that regard in Northern Ireland to the
position prevailing in Great Britain.
another point, perhaps it is as well that
we have not tried to clarify the somewhat
obscure situation as regards the position
of Members of this Parliament in matters
of this kind. There are, I am afraid,
On
some differences still between the law of
insolvency in Great Britain and Northern
Ireland as it affects Members of Parliament. The whole subject is anything but
clear. I rejoice to think it is not of any
great importance, and I do think we are.
well advised to avoid tampering with that
question.
The Noble Viscount, spoke about one
change which it is proposed to make, and
that is making in Northern Ireland a
return by the Sheriff of "No goods" an
act of bankruptcy. That is not an act of
bankruptcy in Great Britain, and I must
say that the logic of the report on that
point leaves me rather cold. I am not
satisfied that a case has been made out
in this respect for departing from the
procedure in Great Britain. I do not
think anybody can dispute the equity of
the conclusion the Committee has arrived
at, and which it is proposed to put in the
Bill, but I think a better case ought to
be made out before us for this departure
from the law in Great Britain. It does
occur to me that the reason why this reform and I admit it is a reform-has
not been adopted in Great Britain may be
that the people against whom a “no
goods" verdict is returned are people of
straw, and it is not worth while to allow
their default to become an act of bankruptcy. I think this is a matter which
should be dealt with in this House, perhaps, by the right hon. Gentleman, the
Minister of Commerce, in replying to the
debate, or, perhaps, it might be more convenient to deal with it in Committee on
a formal Motion, put down to produce
discussion, for the omission of the words
concerned. It is nothing more than that,
but I do think it is a point as to which
a little further information should be
given to the House before the Bill is
passed.
Again, although it is proposed to
abandon finally the distinction between
traders and debtors, and although that
again appeals to all of us, I think we
ought to consider whether we are to bring
farmers within the new provisions as regards the keeping of books and accounts.
It will be remembered that in the earlier
Act of 1857, I think in Section 90, farmers
were put in a somewhat special class. They
were not classed with the makers of alum
and some other people. They lost something of their privilege, such as it was,
when the Amending Act of 1872 came in
and when, speaking generally, but only
generally, the greater part of the distinc-
Bankruptcy Bill-
Second Reading
6 JUNE 1929
tion between traders and debtors was
abolished, but the fact remains that the
farmer of to-day is not in the habit of
keeping anything approaching elaborate
books, and his circumstances are not such
as to enable him to do so. It may be
thought that the farmer is sufficiently protected by the proposed provision in the
Bill that the new arrangements as to books
are not to apply unless the unsecured
liabilities amount to £500, and that a
prosecution is not to be initiated without
the consent of the Court. That is a
matter for consideration, and again I
think it is a matter we should deal with.
If it is necessary to deal with it, in the
Committee stage, it would be necessary to
insert a few words excepting agriculture
from the scope of a trading business, and
to add a short Clause defining what is
meant by agriculture. Such a Clause is
readily to be obtained from the Small
Holdings Act of 1908, or even from the
Agricultural Credits Act of last year, but
any change of that sort would involve
two amendments. One cannot help thinking that the recommendations of the Committee as regards this matter of accounts
were, perhaps, drawn up rather more in
consideration for the conditions that prevail in urban districts, and that, perhaps,
sufficient regard was not had to the difficulties of agriculturists in the rural dis
tricts who are not, unhappily, exempt
from possibilities of insolvency or difficulties with their various creditors. Again,
that is not a matter of first-class importance, but I do think it is a matter worthy
of our attention.
To pass again to a matter which arises
partly in connection with these accounts
and other questions, the two years to be
given to people to get their books in
order, as the Leader of the House
put it, I quite agree that we should
follow the precedent in Great Britain.
and give a period, but I do think
that instead of naming two years
from the passing of this Act we should
name a definite date. I would rather
follow one of the English precedents and
say "within two years from"-it would
be, as a matter of fact, the 29th May of
this year. That is the date when this Bill
was first introduced. I think it would
.be better to name a definite date rather
than two years from the passing of this
Act, which is not immediately apparent,
and a course of that sort is difficult when
it comes to a question of consolidation.
If it should happen that we should consolidate our bankruptcy law within two
years we shall be in a little difficulty if
we leave the provision as it stands in this
Bill. If, on the other hand, we do not
consolidate until more than two years
have elapsed the whole question would become immaterial, because the Clause will
disappear. The adoption of my suggestion would involve merely a verbal
alteration in two passages of the Bill.
I
I am afraid that in mentioning those
matters I have gone a little into detail.
but, as we know, the circumstances under
which this House is sitting to-day are
distinctly abnormal. They are circumstances that cannot recur for four years,
and it happens that time is short.
therefore ask for a little more latitude
than I am strictly entitled to on a Second
Reading speech to say something about
other very trivial Amendments which
seem to me to be required. It is, perhaps, worth while to mention the matter
now and save time later on. I think that
as we assimilate our law we should be
careful to assimilate our language also,
and that we should take advantage of a
Statute which is now in operation in Great
Britain and Northern Ireland, but which
was not in operation either in Great
Britain or Northern Ireland when the
Imperial Act of 1914 was passed.
I think, therefore, that when we have
to deal with the matter of punishment we
should leave out the question of "with
or without hard labour." The House
will remember that under Section 16 of
the Criminal Justice Act, 1914, the Act
which immediately precedes the Bankruptcy Act of that year in the Statute
Book, the question of whether hard
labour was or was not to be imposed is a
matter entirely for the court, but that
Act of 1914 dealing with hard labour did
not, as the House will remember, come
into immediate operation, and it was
necessary in the Imperial Act of 1914 to
put in words which are reproduced in the
Bill before us, but which have now served
their purpose and ought to disappear.
If we remove it we shall follow the latest
English Bankruptcy Act of 1926, and I
suggest we ought to do that.
There remains one other point. I think
that in Clause 25 of the Bill before us,
dealing with books and accounts which
have now to be kept, and in which we are
following again the main course of
English legislation, it will be seen that in
Sub-clause (4) of that Clause, a Clause
Bankruptcy Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
which is, perhaps, at first sight, a little
difficult to follow, we are introducing a
new class of document. We have dealt,
or rather we are proposing to deal, in the
earlier part of Clause 22, with certain
offences in relation to books and documents
generally, and in Clause 25 we have introduced this special class of book or document which is really material to the
comprehension of the state of the man's
affairs, and we are dealing specially with
that. I think it is important that we
should clarify a little further, and hon.
Members who have studied the Clause will
see how much has already been done to
make this difficult subject as clear as it
can be made. But I think we can do
something more, and I think that if we
merely add the two little words "or
accounts" in the passage "in their application to such books as aforesaid," and
made it read "such books or accounts as
aforesaid," we should avoid the possibility of misunderstanding which is,
perhaps, always worth while.
I have mentioned several trivial matters
which in the ordinary course would be
left to the Committee stage. My excuse
is that I have already given, namely,
shortness of time, and I think it will be
noticed that the matters I have mentioned
are such as could, if it suited the convenience of the House, be dealt with after
the Third Reading, rather than on the
Committee stage. That is not a matter
for me, but it is a possibility that might
be borne in mind, if we have to consider
the desirability of further expedition.
There is nothing I have suggested which
in any way affects the main structure of
the Bill, and there is nothing in the criticisms, such as they are, which I have ventured to pass, which I should wish to be
interpreted as offered other than with a
profound appreciation of the manner in
which a very difficult task has been performed, and admiration of the finished
result which has been presented to this
House as a preliminary to consolidation
in the future. I support the Second
Reading of the Bill.
The MINISTER OF COMMERCE
(Mr. Barbour): I would like to express
my appreciation of the very kind manner
in which the hon. and learned Senator
(Senator Leslie) has alluded to the Bill
which has been presented for your consideration. I particularly appreciate the
satisfaction he has expressed at the care
which has been devoted to the study of
this difficult and technical subject by the
Parliamentary Secretary of the Ministry
of Education and, I may also add, by those
professional gentlemen who so very kindly
devoted so much time to the consideration
and study of the subject, and to whose
long experience and knowledge of the law
of bankruptcy this Bill is due. I am glad
to see that the Measure has met with the
approval, generally speaking, of the hon.
and learned Senator. It is one of those
Bills, in respect of which the Government
has followed a course which, I am sure,
recommends itself to this House, namely,
that ample time has been given, during
its preparation, for its submission to and
consideration by those different trade and
which
professional organisations
specially concerned. Through these
organisations the business community has
be enable to give expression to their
views as to any changes they might think
it was necessary to make.
are
I think the main criticisms of Senator
Leslie centred round two or three points.
There were other points of detail which,
I think, could be discussed to better advantage in Committee, but there are one
or two matters of principle to which I
would like to refer. I would like to
assure the hon. and learned Senator that
they are matters which received very close
and careful consideration during the preparation of the Bill. With regard to his
question as to whether the term, "traders
and debtors" had been framed to bring
farmers within the scope of that particular
Clause, I may say that this matter was
considered by the Committee, and having
regard to the limit placed on the sum involved, and, more particularly, having
regard to Clause 25, Sub-clause (1), paragraph (b), which gives certain latitude to
the Court where the debtor is able to show
that, in the circumstances in which he
carried on business, the omission was
honest and excusable, it was thought that
sufficient protection was aflorded to a
class of the community, who, we all know,
cannot keep books in the same manner as
an ordinary business man.
As regards the return of "no goods"
being made an act of Bankruptcy, this
was very strongly supported by the
trading community, and it was felt that
a debtor, who had succeeded in making
away with all the goods he had, should
not be placed in a better position than a
debtor who had succeeded in spiriting
I
Bankruptcy Bill
Adjournment
6 JUNE 1929
away only a portion of the goods under
his control. I think these were the
principal points raised by Senator Leslie,
and, in conclusion, I should again like to
express my appreciation of the very kind
reception this House has accorded to the
Bill.
On Question, Motion (That the Bill be
now read a Second time), agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
whole House for Monday next.
Adjournment.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, "That this House do now adjourn
until Monday next." On that day I
propose to move the suspension of Standing Orders, for the purpose of taking the
remaining stages of the Bankruptcy
(Amendment) Bill. After Monday, the
House will not sit again for a considerable time, but I am not yet in a position
to name the actual date.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at
Four o'clock until Monday
next.

Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in Clause
22, page 17, line 5, to leave out the words
"with or without hard labour." The
words which I suggest the printer should
leave out are words which it is no longer
the practice to insert in an Act of Parliament. What has happened is that since
the Statute from which the Clause was
taken came into operation, a later Statute
applying both to Great Britain and Northern Ireland has come into operation,
giving the Court complete power over the
question of hard labour, whether the
particular act alleged to have been committed did or did not warrant such an
imposition. For the sake of general conformity I suggest here that the words,
"with or without hard labour," should
be omitted.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am prepared to accept that Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.

Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in Clause

possible to insert a definite date, and,
therefore, I suggest we should follow one
of the English precedents, and make our
two years run from the date when the Bill
was presented to this House. There is
an advantage, as the House knows, in
having a date printed in an Act of
Parliament, which is readily ascertainable. It is one of the things we can do
in this world to avoid legislation by anv
form of reference. I beg to move
accordingly.

proper books of account throughout
that period and
and throughout any
further period in which he was so engaged between the date of the presentation of the petition and the date
of adjudication of bankruptcy, or the
date on which his proposed arrangement
was approved by the court, as the case
may be, or has not preserved all books
of account so kept:

Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in Clause
25, page 20, line 13, after the word

Mr. LONG: I am afraid the provisions
of this Clause requiring persons to keep
books or accounts will hit very hard a
section of the community which is not in
the habit of keeping books. I refer to
the farming community. I do not think
there is one farmer in a thousand, or in
ten thousand, who keeps books or
accounts, and before I can support this
46.
Bankruptcy Bill-
Committee
10 JUNE 1929
Clause I shall require an assurance from
the Leader of the House, or the Minister
of Commerce, that farmers will not be
prejudiced by its operation. It has become a pretty well-known saying in the
country during the past seven or eight
years that farmers were being "stared
in the face by the bankruptcy court."
It might be reasonable to assume that
when farmers were thus confronted with
the bankruptcy court they would be
familiar with the chief features of bankruptcy law; but, paradoxical as it may
seem, very few of them have even a
nodding acquaintance with bankruptcy
law. They are guileless sort of men, and
are not familiar with compositions, dividends of so much in the pound, and that
sort of thing. They merely go down and
out when they are in difficulties, and,
therefore, I should like to have an assurance that they will not be injuriously
affected by the provisions of this Bill.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I would
suggest that the hon. Senator should repeat his question on the Motion for the
Third Reading.
Mr. LONG: I will do so.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.

Mr. LONG: I suggest that the Minister of Commerce should consider the
point I have raised, namely, that the
corroborated evidence of a debtor
gaged in farming operations should be
accepted in lieu of the production of books
which the great bulk of the farmers
would be unable to produce, because
