There
Mr. LESLIE: I myself, on the Second
Reading, referred to this matter of farmers' accounts, and the right hon. Gentleman, the Minister of Commerce, who
was with us then and is, I am glad to say,
again with us to-day, gave the same explanation, referring to the protection
in paragraph (b) of Clause 25.
are also, of course, the other matters to
which I drew attention, that is, that a
prosecution should not be initiated except by an order of the court, and should
not apply to anybody unless the sum of
£500 was at issue. But when I was
speaking about accounts, and had it in
mind to move an Amendment, I did not
receive any support from those in this
House who more directly represent the
farmers' views, and it, therefore, occurred
to me that perhaps the matter might be
left over in order that when this Bill
goes to another place-because it originated in this House-those interested in the
matter there can, if they wish, draw
attention to the matter again, and, perhaps, put forward some Amendment
which might, or might not, prove
acceptable.
There remains the other point, the
question of a return of "no goods." I
pointed out before that the suggestion in
the Bill before us was a departure from
the English law, and it occurred to me
that as the English law had been revised
so recently as 1926, and it had not been
thought necessary in England or Great
Britain, to insert such a provision, some
further information might be given to
this House on the Third reading, as to
why it was considered desirable to make a
change. The House will remember that
the Report of the Robb Committee most
strongly, and clearly, recommends that
the change should be made, and defends
it on the ground which I have referred to
as somewhat cold-blooded logic. There
is no disputing the logic of the decision or
its equity, but the fact remains, that we
are making a distinction between our law
and the law in Great Britain, and a little
more might be said to us about it.
Bankruptcy Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
The MINISTER OF COMMERCE
(Mr. Barbour): To deal first with the
question raised by Senator Long, I would
like to draw attention to a fact of
which hon. Members are of course fully
aware, and that is that in our Province
of Northern Ireland the farming industry
by far outweighs any other industry. I
dare say you will find that in relation
the number of people engaged in any
other industry there is a far larger proportion of farmers in Northern Ireland
than there is in either England or Scotland. This matter was considered, and,
of course, the ability of the farmer to
keep books was also taken into consideration, by the Robb Committee, and, I
think it gave rise to the provision to
which the hon. and learned
(Senator Leslie) has drawn the attention
of the House, namely, that if the farmer
proves that in the circumstances in which
he traded, or carried on business, the
omission to keep books was honest and
excusable it will be a sufficient excuse.
Having regard to that provision I think
I can give the hon. Senator the assurance
he asked for. I think, that in the Courts
of Northern Ireland, the law is always
justly administered, but at the same time,
I think it is administered with a good
deal of mercy and consideration for the
just claims of those who are brought before our judges. Therefore, I hope this
assurance will be sufficient to meet the
fear expressed by Senator Long.
an
Senator
He
As to the Clause dealing with a return
of "no goods" by the Sheriff constituting
act of bankruptcy, the arguments
employed by the hon. and learned Member (Mr. Leslie), himself, in his very
mild opposition to the proposal, both in
the debate on the Second Reading and on
the Third Reading to-day have been
academic rather than practical.
appreciates, I am sure, that the sheriff's
officer may be compelled by circumstances
to make a return of "no goods" in cases
where property or goods have been spirited
away temporarily, or are not in a state
readily seizable. As the law stands at
present, there is no risk of the stigma of
bankruptcy attaching in these circumstances, whereas, if this reform is carried,
a debtor, in such circumstances, would
think twice before attempting to defeat
his creditors in this way.
This particular question was, I understand, very fully considered by the Committee, and the unanimous conclusion of
that body, composed of leading lawyers
and business representatives, was that
return of "no goods" should be declared
to be an act of bankruptcy. Logically it
is difficult to see how they could have come
to a different conclusion, and, in the circumstances, I hope the hon. and learned
Gentleman will agree that this step which
he, himself, has described as a reform, is
amply justified. The hon. and learned
Member based his criticism mainly upon
the fact that in the matter of this provision we are departing from the bankruptcy
law in England. He will, I am sure,
readily recognise that this is not the only
respect in which our law does not coincide
with that obtaining across the Channel.
For example, the Committee recommended that, although in England it is
an act of bankruptcy for a debtor to give
notice to any creditor that he has suspended, or is about to suspend payment,
they did not consider that such a step in
Northern Ireland should be made an act
of bankruptcy since this would be too
drastic a proceeding, and one not suited
to our existing bankruptcy system. Therefore, although it has been stated that the
provision in this Bill as regards a return
of "no goods" is different to the law in
England, I believe it will suit the requirements of traders in Northern Ireland.
On Question, Motion (That the Bill be
now read the Third time) agreed to.
I beg to
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
move, "That this Bill do now pass.
Mr. LESLIE: I rise to take the opportunity of thanking the Minister of Commerce for the explanation he gave us. So
far as I am concerned I am perfectly satisfied, and I have no doubt there will also
be that satisfaction in the Province which
is expected.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
And a Message was sent to the House
of Commons to carry down the said Bill,
and desire their concurrence thereto.

Laid before the House (pursuant to the
Act) and ordered to lie on the Table.
Belfast; Mr. Joseph Cunningham, of 194
York Street, Belfast; Mr. James Graham
Leslie, of Leslie Hill, Ballymoney; Mr.
Thomas Stanislaus M'Allister, of Ballymena; Mr. William John M'Dowell, of
144 Woodvale Road, Belfast; Mr. John
McHugh, of Pettigo; Mr. John Porter
Porter, of Lisbellaw; and Colonel Thomas
Sinclair, of 22 University Square, Belfast, were, on the 20th day of June, 1929
duly elected Senators to serve in the Parliament of Northern Ireland.

Business of the House. 52
Business of the House.
THE SENATE
Business of the House.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I beg to move, "That Standing
Order No. XXV be suspended for this
day's sitting, for the purpose of taking the
Second Reading of the Local Government
(Rating and Finance) Bill, School Teachers
(Oath and Declaration) Bill, the Street
Trading (Regulation) Bill, and Ministry of
Home Affairs Provisional Order Confirmation (Carrickfergus Water) Bill)."
In rising to move this Motion I desire to
say that I do so for the reason that owing
to there having been an election of
Members to this House the business of the
House has been seriously delayed, and also
because I believe it will suit the convenience of the majority of the Members
that the Standing Order should be suspended to enable the Second Reading of
these various Bills to be taken to-day.
Mr. McALLISTER: I rise to oppose
this Motion on several grounds, one of
which is the ground of principle. I think
this Motion would create a precedent
unique, unparalleled and unprecedented
in any Parliamentary assembly. We have
only been sworn in during the past three
minutes, and I think it would be an unparalleled step for any Parliamentary Assembly to take to move the closure-for
that is what it amounts to-within three
minutes of half the House being sworn in.
We are all aware that the suspension of
Standing Orders in any form is the first
link in the chain of the guillotine machine
in Parliamentary procedure, and I think
it would be deplorable that the first step
in what is practically a new Senate should
be to manufacture a guillotine before any
other business is transacted. After all,
the rights and privileges of private Members in any Assembly are worth jealously
guarding, and as a private Member I protest against the deprivation of the rights
of private Members and the denial to them
of a proper opportunity to investigate and
examine the legislation on which they have
to give their opinion and vote.
I could understand the adoption and
application of such a Standing Order at
Westminster, where you have a Parliament responsible for the administration
of legislation for a vast area like Great
Britain, a Parliament that has on its
shoulders the cares of a far-flung Empire,
and a Parliament that has to devote a
considerable amount of its time to surveying the whole field of international
politics. I could understand that that
Parliament should have the right to suspend Standing Orders to facilitate them
in getting through their business. I remember in another place opposing such a
Motion as this, and the Prime Minister
justified the moving of that Resolution on
the ground that, when a Member of the
Imperial Parliament, he had seen a similar Motion introduced nearing the end of
a Session. But this is only the very inception of the business of a new House.
We are not yet five minutes in Session,
and therefore, I say a Motion of this sort
is entirely unprecedented and unparalleled in any Parliamentary assembly.
I oppose this Motion for another
reason. Whilst the noble Lord, the
Deputy Leader of the House, in moving
this Resolution, did not directly say so.
I rather gathered from his introductory
remarks that the Government were endeavouring to put this Motion through in
order to keep time with what is called
the Parliamentary time-table. I protest
against any other place having the righ
to dictate to any assembly of which I
have the honour to be a Member, and to
say when and in what manner we, as a
legislative assembly here, are to devote
our time and attention to the matters
that come before us. As long as I am a
member of any assembly, I will consider
it my first duty to guard the privileges,
and the rights, and prestige, and
authority of whatever assembly I have
the honour to be a member. On that
ground I protest, as a Member of this
assembly, against any outside influence
from whatever source dictating to us as a
House, and telling us at what time and
in what manner we should carry through
the business of this House. On principle,
I wish to accord my disapproval of the
Motion for the Closure at this stage.
Mr. CAMPBELL: Very few days have
elapsed since the election to this House
of one-half of its Members, and we are
asked to-day to pass, at one sitting, four
separate Measures. One of these
Measures is a Measure of first-class importance. I refer to the Local Government (Rating and Finance) Bill. This is
a Measure of very wide and general importance, and I submit as a matter of fair
play to some, at least, of the new Members that a little longer time should be
given for due consideration of a Measure
of such gravity and of such importance
to the general public.
Business of the House. 54
Business of the House.
24 JUNE 1929
I
which intervenes between the Second Reading and the Committee for considering very
carefully the details of the Measure and
what Amendments, if any, ought to be
introduced.
While speaking on that I should like to
remind the noble Viscount, the Leader of
the House, of what I urged on a former
occasion, that steps should be taken to
ensure that the print of the Second Readdebate should be in the hands of
Members before the Committee Stage is
fixed.
I think, as I have said, that the course
proposed will result in a saving of time and
I do not think it is a course there is any
serious objection to. I beg to support the
Motion.
Mr. LESLIE: I should like, in the
first instance, to congratulate our two
new Friends on appearing in this assembly and also, if I may say so, on their
maiden speeches in this House. If the
hon. Senator who replied to the noble Viscount had confined himself entirely to the
question of principle I should have been
entirely with him. I think I may claim
to have urged as often as any other Member of this House the necessity for allowing
ing full time to elapse between the
several stages of Bills, particularly important Bills. But I venture to hope
that on this occasion the hon. Senators
will not feel it necessary to pursue their
objection to its logical conclusion.
think, perhaps, I may venture to remind
them that in this case the Bills to which
we are asked to give a First and Second
Reading are not Bills which originated in
this House. They are all Bills which originated in another place. Two, I think,
the Street Trading Bill and the School
Teachers Oaths Bills, were circulated to
Members of this House as soon as it was
possible to get into touch with newlyelected Members, and I received my own
copy on Friday morning. The more important Bill, the Derating Bill, was so
circulated and we received it on Saturday
morning, practically in the form in which
it left another place. It was not quite
the final form, because there were some
trivial Amendments made. The other
Bill, the Provisional Order Confirmation
Bill, explains itself.
I cannot but think that having regard to
the circumstances in which we stand that
the work of this comparatively new Parliament-I say comparatively new, because
the general election was only a short time
ago the work of this comparatively new
Parliament was interrupted by the necessity of renewing this House. But that
I
does not make it a new Parliament.
think it would be in accordance with the
general convenience if this Motion were
accepted, and that would allow the noble
Viscount, the Leader of the House, in
arranging the Parliamentary business to
give us rather longer time for the stage
Armstrong, Mr. H. B. W.
Bangor, Viscount
Barclay, Mr. William
Charlemont, Viscount
Cunningham, Mr. Joseph
Campbell, Mr. T. J.
Mr. DORMAN: I rise to offer my
protest against the suspension of Standing
Orders, especially with regard to one of the
Measures before the House. I think one
of the Measures, the Local Government
(Rating and Finance) Bill is an extremely
important Measure and one that every
Member of this House should get
reasonable time to digest before being expected to rise and deliver a Second Reading
address on such an important subject as
this.
I therefore wish to express my regret, and to express the hope that the
Government will see their way at least not
to pursue the Second Reading of this particular Measure. The other Measures are
not of so important a nature.
In my
opinion this is one of the most important
Bills brought before us for consideration
for a long time. It involves very serious
constitutional questions.

Street Trading (Regulation) Bill (N.I.).
Viscount CHARLEMONT: In proposing the Second Reading of this Measure I
should like to say that under it it is proposed to give powers to local authorities to
cope with a practice which has long been
a source of inconvenience, particularly in
market towns, and has of recent years increased to a serious extent. I refer to the
use of the streets of provincial towns by
itinerant traders from Belfast and other
places for erecting stalls from which they
sell their goods on market days.
Most of the markets in Northern Ireland
are held in the public streets, and it is obvious that if large numbers of traders
attend these markets and erect stalls on the
streets without being subject to proper control, serious obstruction is bound to be
caused with resulting inconvenience to the
public. I am sure hon. Members have
Street Trading Bill-
Second Reading.
24 JUNE 1929
often been impressed by this fact when
going through some of our provincial towns.
on market days. In addition to the inconvenience caused by obstruction, refuse in
considerable quantities is left behind by
the traders, and this has to be collected
and taken away at the expense of the ratepayers.
I would here like to say that we do not
wish to interfere in any way with the
means of livelihood of these traders-all
we want to do is to regulate this trading
in as fair a manner as possible, so that it
I will not be a nuisance or a source of inconvenience to others. The Bill is not intended to apply to a farmer selling his
farm produce, and, in order that this may
be made quite clear, I propose to bring
in an amendment on the Committee Stage,
specifically exempting this type of trading.
I shall now deal very briefly with the
various Clauses of the Bill. Under Clause
1, it will be necessary, after the 1st of
January next, for every trader selling
goods from a stall or other receptacle occupying a stationary position in any street
in an urban district or town having town
commissioners to have a licence from the
local authority, authorising him to do so.
It will be noted that a proviso has been
added to this Clause to the effect that persons, whom I might call perambulating
traders, such as ice cream vendors and
others, who move about from place to
place when selling their goods, shall not
be affected by the Bill. It is a common
practice in some country towns for traders
to sell their goods from the surface of the
street without using a stall or other receptacle, and this Clause makes it clear
that such traders must comply with the
provisions of the Bill..
Clause 2 deals with the procedure for
obtaining a licence, and empowers a local
authority to refuse to grant or renew a
licence or to revoke or vary a licence
granted to any person if on account of misconduct or for any other sufficient reason
he is, in their opinion, unsuitable to hold
such licence; or the space available in the
street or streets to which the application
for the licence relates is insufficient for the
selling of goods by the applicant without
causing undue interference with, or inconvenience to, the traffic in such street or
streets; or the street to which the application relates is not a street ordinarily prescribed by the local authority for the purpose of street trading. It is, however,
specifically provided that the applicant
for, or holder of, a licence shall not be
prejudiced by reason of the fact that he
does not reside in the area of the local
authority, and that local residents shall
not receive preferential treatment.
So as to enable the local authority
properly to control street trading they
may insert conditions in the licence
specifying the position and streets in
which the licensee may sell articles, the
class of the articles which may be sold
under the licence, the days and times on
and at which the licensee may sell such
articles, and the number of stalls, etc.,
which may be used under a licence.
It will be seen that before
a local
authority refuses to renew a licence, or
revokes or varies any licence, the applicant must be given an opportunity of
being heard regarding any such refusal,
revocation or variation. Further, a
right of appeal to a court of summary
jurisdiction is given under Clause 5 to any
person aggrieved by the decision of the
local authority.
The fee payable for a licence will be 5s,
and licences will, in general, remain in
force for one year. The fee is really only
a nominal amount, as it is not our desire
to enable local authorities to make a profit
out of street traders, but we want to
provide sufficient money to cover the cost
of administration of the Bill.
Clause 6 gives the local authority power
to make by-laws regulating the days on
which, and the times during which, street
trading under licences may take place;
the allocation, dimensions, and arrangement of stalls, and so forth. They may
also provide for the storage and sanitary
supervision (while at the place of intended sale) of articles of food, and they
may prescribe other conditions under
which articles may be sold under the
authority of the licence.
Traders will be protected against any
unreasonable use being made of this
power to make by-laws, as the local
authority will have to go through the
usual procedure and confirmation by the
Ministry of Home Affairs will be necessary before they can take effect.
Clause 7 authorises the local authority
to make and recover charges for services
rendered to any person in removing refuse or otherwise, and under the previous
Clause they may, by by-laws, fix the
amount of the charges for such services.
It is clearly only just that the street
traders should pay for this work.
59 Street Trading Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading.
[Viscount Charlemont]
It is provided by Clause 8 that a person
holding a licence may employ any other
person to assist him without a further
licence being required. The penalties
for contravention of the provisions of the
Bill are fixed by Clause 9.
As hon. Members are, no doubt, aware,
the markets in some of the towns in
Northern Ireland are the property of
private individuals who hold them under
rights granted by charter, and collect tolls
Where those
in respect of their user.
markets are held in the streets it is obviously desirable that the local authority
should acquire the market rights before
putting in force the provisions of the
Bill, so as to prevent any duplication of
control arising as a result of the licensing
powers proposed to be given.
Accordingly, under Clause 10 we propose to authorise the local authority, on
payment of adequate compensation, to acquire or extinguish compulsorily any such
right of holding a market in a street, and
to prohibit the local authority exercising
the powers conferred by the other provisions of the Bill until such rights have
been acquired or extinguished. The procedure provided for the compulsory acquisition or extinguishment of market rights
is such that there will be no danger of any
person being unfairly treated. The local
authority will have to give due public
notice of their proposals to all concerned,
and will have to make application to the
Ministry of Home Affairs for an Order
authorising them to acquire or extinguish
the market rights.
It will, of course, be necessary for the
local authority to pay compensation to
the owner of the market rights, and if
the persons concerned cannot arrive at
an agreement as to the amount, the compensation will be determined by arbitration.
Whilst it is, of course, principally in
the urban districts and towns which have
town commissioners that the practice of
street trading exists, there are certain
other towns which have important markets, and it is, therefore, desirable that
the rural district councils should have
power, where necessary, to deal with
street trading. Accordingly, under
Clause 11, a rural district council is empowered to apply to the Ministry of Home
Affairs for an Order applying any of the
provisions of the Bill to such town in the
district.
Street trading does not exist in the same
acute form in the two County Boroughs in
Northern Ireland-Belfast and Londonderry-as in provincial towns. We think,
however, that if the Councils of the
County Boroughs consider the provisions
of the Bill would be useful to them they
should have power to obtain them, and,
accordingly, Clause 11 also enables the
Council of a County Borough to apply to
the Ministry of Home Affairs for an Order
putting the provisions of the Bill in force.
in its area. I beg to move.
Mr. LESLIE: The object of the Bill
to which we have been asked to give a
Second reading seems to be simple enough.
It is, as was said, to give local authorities
powers to regulate street trading for which
they have asked. I believe I am correct
in saying that applications for such powers
have been put in by the majority of the
urban district authorities in Northern
Ireland.
The request is quite a reasonable one, and I am sure that there is no
desire to refrain from granting it, but the
subject and the Bill itself seem to me to
be beset with an unusually large number
of difficulties of a minor character. The
difficulties are minor, but there are a
great many of them. Some of these minor
difficulties are inherent in the subject itself, and some are artificial difficulties introduced by the Bill.
Taking the inherent difficulties first,
it is not too much to say, I think,
that in Northern Ireland there are
no two places exactly alike, nor in
scarcely any two of these towns are the
conditions absolutely identical. We have
also towns of one sort and another
where the Lighting Acts, the Towns Improvements Acts, and the Markets and
Fairs Acts have been adopted. We have
other towns where none of these adoptive
Acts have been taken advantage of. Then
we have to consider necessarily three interests. There is the interest of what I
may describe as the resident trader in the
locality-the man who keeps a shop for
selling things. He complains that the
visitor with his barrow and stall and so on
interrupts his trade. We have the interest of that man with the barrow and
stall to which the noble Viscount referred.
We have, thirdly, the interest of the
general public who for some reason or other
desire to buy from the man with the
barrow and do not desire to buy from the
man with the shop. Perhaps that is because of the fact that the goods sold from
the barrow are cheaper than those sold
from the shop.
I
Street Trading Bill-
Second Reading.
24 JUNE 1929
Then we have, as bearing on the whole
subject of street trading, the general law
relating to obstruction in streets and
thoroughfares. If I understand the Bill
correctly, it is not intended to interfere in
any way with that general law. That is one
of the points on which I would like further
elucidation. Passing to the artificial difficulties, as I call them, I think, perhaps,
the first is the rather marvellous expression
-"stationary position."
I cannot myself
think of any position at the moment which
is other than stationary. I could wish
that the author of the Bill had boldly
called the thing a pitch. We should then
have all understood what he meant. As it
is there is perhaps some little difficulty
there. Then the Bill does not give any
definition Clause.
The Bill also introduces this question of
purchasing market rights as they are called
from private individuals and also proposes
to confer certain powers in rural districts,
and also proposes to give some exemptions
from the general law in certain circumstances. These difficulties perhaps explain
or help to explain why the Bill should be
of such length, why it should be so very
verbose. There is one recurring passage
which is painfully reminiscent of the flute,
the harp and the sackbut and so on.
Perhaps it also explains why the Bill, as it
seems to me, is very much more in need of
revision as regards words and phrases
than is usually the case.
I think, perhaps, that some parts of the
Bill may have been borrowed from some
local Bill more than forty years old, and
that may be an additional excuse. However, our business is, of course, to produce
a Bill or rather to see that the Bill when it
leaves this House is a workable Measure,
and not only workable but easily workable
by all who may be concerned with it.
The Bill really falls into two parts, first,
the part dealing with street trading in
general, and, secondly, the part dealing
with the acquisition of market rights.
As regards the first part, ordinary street
trading, I think that we have to observe
that the Bill is a compulsory Bill. It
does not say directly that the local authority shall make arrangements for granting
licences, but it does say that a man requires
to get his licence before he trades on the
street, as he cannot trade on the
street without a licence from the local
authority. It follows that the local
authority, whether they like it or
not, must make provision for providing licences.
This would show that the
Bill will not be of the adoptive class. It is
presumably intended that local authorities
shall work this Bill for which they have
asked-it seems to me a reasonable expectation-and that they will take proper steps
for the allocation of pitches. They are
given powers to do all the things that are
necessary in that way. It seems to me
that if those powers are to be effectually
executed there must be an arrangement
whereby the expiration of licences issued
under the Act shall be synchronised. It
will be seen that the present proposal
leaves it possible for two classes to prevail
in perpetuity, that is, the licence which
terminates on the 31st December and the
licence which terminates on whatever date
may be fixed for the annual meeting. It
seems to me that that is an impossible
position, but we may divert from it till
later.
It follows, and, indeed, it was impressed upon us, that if licences are to be
issued in the ordinary way, there must
be a right of appeal by any person who
thinks himself aggrieved. It is proposed
to bring that appeal before an entirely
new court. The proposal, is not, of
course, to take away any power from any
existing court, because the power does not
exist, but to give the right of hearing
such appeal, not to the ordinary bench
of magistrates sitting in petty sessions in
the district, but to a resident magistrate
sitting alone, or with another resident
magistrate. That is not the purpose for
which resident magistrates were created.
It may be thought that the local magistrate. who knows the circumstances,
would be the best person to deal with the
matter, and that thought might be
strengthened were it remembered that
members of the urban district council
who happen to be magistrates could not,
of course, sit.
In dealing with the question of the
itinerant, that is, the person who moves
from place to place-as I think it is expressed in the Bill-in pursuit of his
trade, it seems to me that we have to be
very careful lest that person moves in
such a way as to defeat entirely the intention of the Bill. It seems to me that,
as things stand, the really careful man,
resolved not to take out a licence and
to pursue his trade, can do so and
snap his fingures at the people
at the
the Act, unless he happens
to trespass on an allocated pitch. I
am very much afraid it will be necessary to strengthen the law regarding
can
or
63 Street Trading Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading.
[Viscount Charlemont]
obstruction in that connection, and it
could be conveniently done, I think, by
giving power to make an additional bylaw, but before actually suggesting that
course I should like to hear whether that
aspect has been considered, and whether
there is any desire to deal with it in that
way.
That, I think, is all I can say to-day
as regards street trading proper, and I
pass now to the proposal to allow local
authorities to acquire market rights. So
far as I can ascertain, that proposal is
quite new both in principle and in detail. I cannot find anywhere that there
are any general provisions applicable to
transactions of that description, and if
there is some general enactment covering
the situation it would seem plain, at all
events, that no such enactment has been
applied in Bills before this.
That seems
to render it necessary that we should inquire very carefully whether these
powers of regulating street trading cannot be exercised by the local authorities
by agreement with the owner of the market rights, and so relieve themselves of
the necessity of paying out money for the
purpose of requiring a man to sell something which, perhaps, he does not want
to dispose of. Why should they not proceed by agreement if they can ?
Again, when the local authority have
acquired these market rights, should we.
not take some steps to prevent them from
parting with them? Are they to be at
liberty to put them up to auction and sell
them to the highest bidder? Further,
are they to abide by the existing rates
and tolls, or are those to be regulated;
and. still further, what is the local
authority, having acquired the market
rights, going to do with the profits? I suggest that these are matters which should
be made quite clear in the Bill, unless
they are covered by some existing enactment. But I must not anticipate the debate which will necessarily ensue on the
Committee Stage and which---may I take
it?-will occupy some little time. I will
conclude by expressing the hope that the
result of our labours may be satisfactory,
and that we shall succeed, at all events,
in ameliorating, if we cannot entirely remove, some of the grievances that have
been brought to our notice. I support
the Second Reading.
Mr. McALLISTER: I rise, in the first
instance, to inquire from the Leader of
the House if he is prepared to give ample
time between now and the Committee
Stage, so that those of us who have not
had an opportunity of examining the Bill
in detail may have such opportunity. I
find myself in agreement with the hon.
Senator (Mr. Leslie), who has just sat
down, on some points, and in disagreement on others. I am entirely associated
with him in his suggestion that where a
licence is refused the right of appeal
should be to an ordinary court of magistrates, who, as he has very truthfully
stated, with their local knowledge, are a
highly efficient and competent court to
deal with a matter of that description.
Where no great or profound principle of
law is concerned no better tribunal could
be found than the ordinary bench of
magistrates. No tribunal administers
justice with more common sense or consideration for those who come before them
than the bench of ordinary magistrates
in the county in which I have the privilege to practise. I can see no reason why
this particular appeal should be limited
to resident magistrates. After all, it may
cause great inconvenience, because you
have a court of petty sessions constituted
at any petty sessions. You always have
two magistrates there, if required, but
those of us who are associated with the
law know that frequently several courts
may pass before a resident magistrate is
able to be present at a particular court
at which particular duties are to be performed; and so a man may be deprived
of the assurance that he is going to have
his licence renewed, perhaps, for two or
three months owing to the resident
magistrate, through no fault of his own
or of the Government, being unable to be
present.
With regard to the other point stressed
by the hon. Senator, he seems to be more
or less under the impression that should
markets owned by private individuals be
acquired under this Clause some hardship
or injustice might be done to these people.
I was rather of the other frame of mind.
One of the weaknesses in this Bill, in my
opinion, was the fact that you have a
private owner of the markets letting out
pitches every market day-which would
perhaps be every week-at say 5s, or 10s.
per year, whereas the local authority, who
keep up the streets and the town at the
ratepayers' expense, have only the right
of claiming a licence fee of 5s. There is
a differentiation of treatment between the
two interests there that I would like to
65 Street Trading Bill.

24 JUNE 1929
School Teachers' Bill.
see adjusted, but I do not think the
method suggested by this Bill of compulsory acquirement by the local authority
of private owners' rights will meet the
case. I have a case in my mind at the
moment in which the local authority
thought it desirable to acquire the rights
of a certain private company in regard to
lighting. They were offered the rights of
the private company for something like
£14,000, but they were under the impression that they were paying too much.
They went to compulsory arbitration, and
I think they eventually paid £30,000.
The hon. Senator need have no fear that
private owners will be at any disadvantage.
Mr. LESLIE: I did not say they would.
Mr. MCALLISTER: He rather suggested that when he suggested that the
local authority should be compelled to retain possession of the property when they
got it and not sell it at a profit. I am only
trying to ease his mind on that particular
point. I would like an assurance from the
Government that we will have a reasonable
time to look into this Bill and to consider
how the joint efforts and collaboration
of all Members of the House it can be improved and made a practicable and satisfactory Measure.
Mr. DORMAN: I think one of the
difficulties in connection with this Bill will
be the difficulty of the representatives of
the people when they come into contest
with the granting of licences where private
interests have been established. I welcome
the conferring upon the local authorities
the right to acquire compulsorily individual
interests in these markets. If my reading
of the history of this particular part of the
country is right, most of these interests
were forcibly taken away from various
citizens in the North of Ireland in the early
part of the seventeenth century by deliberate confiscation and abolition of the rights
of the people and the appointing by his
then Gracious Majesty of a more God-fearing class of individual, upon whom he reconferred the private right to levy tolls
upon the people of the area.
If that particular right is got back at a reasonable
cost it would be to the advantage of the
people in the various centres of Northern
Ireland.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: In regard
to the various points raised, I think the
most important one is with regard to the
appeal to a resident magistrate. It will
be obvious to the House that local magistrates might be placed in a very disagreeable and awkward position, because they
might very well, and probably would be,
shopkeepers who would be affected by
street trading. Though I think myself
that ordinary Justices of the Peace are as
fair-minded and unaffected by prejudice as
anybody-being one myself I could say no
less at the same time I think it unfair
that they should be asked to adjudicate in
a case where their private outlook must be
very much-or might be very much-at
variance with their public outlook. For
that reason I think the appeal is better
made to a resident magistrate, who has not
only a knowledge of the law which the
ordinary Justice of the Peace has not, but
who is also a perfectly disinterested person
and not liable to be either tempted or
carried away by any private feeling. I
should say that application was made by
the Municipal Association for a Bill regulating street trading, and a copy of the
Bill was sent to them before it was introduced in the House of Commons.
As for the other points raised, I should
like to give the information at a later date.
I am not quite in a position to reply to all
the points raised at the moment.
I agree
with the Senators who have spoken that a
reasonable time should be given to prepare
any questions for the Committee Stage,
and when I move the Adjournment of the
House I will be in a position to say,
more or less, how long I shall be able to
give.
On Question, Motion ("That the Bill be
now read a Second time') agreed to.
School Teachers (Oath and Declaration)
Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: This Bill is
quite a short one and its provisions are
not, I think, likely to prove in any way
controversial.
It has been found that large numbers of
public elementary school teachers, by
reason of the transfer of the schools in
which they are teaching to the Local Education Authorities under the Education
Act of 1923 have become obliged to make
a declaration of allegiance in the form prescribed by Section 5 (2) of the Local
Government Act (Northern Ireland), 1922,

[Viscount Charlemont]
whether or not the teacher concerned had
already subscribed to the oath of allegiance
required under the Promissory Oaths Act
(Northern Ireland), 1923. The form of
oath prescribed under the latter Act is, in
fact, almost identical in wording to the
form of declaration prescribed under the
Local Government Act of 1922, and it will,
I am sure, be agreed that there is no
reason why teachers who have taken the
oath should also be required to make the
declaration.
The Bill now before the House provides
that a teacher who has at any time taken
the oath prescribed by the Act of 1923 shall
be deemed to have complied with Section 5
(2) of the Act of 1922 (under which the
declaration is required) in respect of any
office or employment as a school teacher
held by him to which that sub-section
applies. I beg to move.

Local Government (Rating and
Finance) Bill.
This
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
Measure, which is one of the most important Measures with which this House has
had to deal, prescribes the machinery for
bringing into operation the Government's Derating Scheme. So much has
already been said about the scheme itself
that I am sure hon. Members are all
sufficiently acquainted with the main
principles of the Scheme and the advantages which will accrue from it. I do
not propose. therefore, to detain the
House by dwelling on this aspect of the
matter, but will confine my remarks to
the text of the Bill before you.
Speaking generally, the Bill makes
provision, in the first place, for the relief of agricultural land and farm buildings from all rates (with one or
two minor exceptions, such as, drainage rates, malicious injury rates, and
certain water rates); the relief of
productive industries and transport.
undertakings from 75 per cent, of
their rates, upon the condition in the
case of transport undertakings that the
relief is passed on to the producers in the
form of rebates in respect of the carriage
of raw materials and feeding stuffs; and
the relief of salmon and eel fisheries from
60 per cent. of their rates.
Secondly, the Bill provides for the payment to the various local authorities from
the Exchequer of sums to recoup them
in respect of the reduction of the revenue
from rates which will result from the reliefs to be granted under the Bill. It
will be realised that the devising of a
system which, whilst ensuring fairness to
the various local authorities concerned and
preserving the interests of the various local
services, such as, for example, Public
Health and Education, and at the same
time safeguarding the Exchequer from
heavy inroads by local extravagance presented a problem of no little difficulty.
The Government, has, however, given
very earnest consideration to the matter.
A Committee was appointed to examine
the whole question, and upon its report.
written after the hearing of evidence
from the various interested bodies, the
Government's proposals are based. I
shall explain them as briefly as possible.
In the urban areas the loss of revenue
from rates will be a comparatively small
part of the total at present collectible.
and we propose, therefore, to recoup to
the various urban authorities concerned
the actual loss sustained in each year. In
the rural areas, on the other hand, approximately 80 per cent. of the local
revenue is derived from agricultural
land; obviously, we could not proceed
here as in the urban areas, as the enormous contribution to be made by the
Exchequer to the local expenditure must
be accompanied by adequate safeguards
against extravagance. It is proposed,
therefore, to pay to the local authorities
each year a "standard deficiency grant,
based generally on the normal expenditure of the past
three vears; this
"standard deficiency grant" will be
stereotyped for five years, at the end of
which period it will be revised, if necessary, to meet altered conditions. In
addition to its "standard deficiency
grant." it is proposed to pay to the local
authorities for each year a supplemental
grant to meet sanctioned increases in expenditure, which cannot be met out of
savings. This supplemental grant
limited in amount to the produce of a
rate of sixpence on the derated hereditaments in the county, and the amount of
supplemental grant which will be paid.
any individual local
government
authority will be calculated so that, so
to
69 Local Government Bill-

24 JUNE 1929
Second Reading.
long as the limit of a sixpenny rate for
the county is not exceeded, no ratepayer
will have to contribute more towards the
increased expenditure than he would
have had to pay had derating not taken
place. I might point out that to meet
the converse case we have taken power
under Clause 13 (4) to reduce the standard
deficiency grant, in the event of the
year's expenditure falling below the
standard.
There is one very important point to
which I must direct the attention of the
House. Educational services are not included in the arrangement to which I have
just referred, but are treated quite separately. The Government realised, that
in view of the comparatively short period
during which the Education Act had
been in force in the counties, it would
be unwise to restrict their growth. We
have, accordingly, decided, in the first
place, that the standard deficiency
grant shall be based on the normal expenditure of last year only, and not the
past three years, as in the case of other
expenditure, and that the supplemental
grant to be paid shall not be subject to
the limit of a sixpenny rate. This will
allow the unrestricted development of
this new local service, whilst at the same
time I am convinced that the Ministry
of Education possesses sufficient control to
curb any tendency towards extravagance
on the part of Education Authorities.
These are the main outlines of the
system by which we propose to deal with
this problem, and I think it will be found
to work out with fairness to all concerned.
There are one or two matters of detail to
which I should like to refer briefly.
Clause 7 of the Bill provides that, in
future, certain services, the cost of which
is at present borne by special areas of
charge, namely, waterworks and sewerage,
shall be borne as rural districts charges.
It has been found that to restrict the area
of such charges entirely to the local area
affected has hindered in many cases the
carrying out of these works, which are
so necessary for the public health.
Provision is made whereby any increased
charge in respect of existing works of
this nature which would otherwise fall
on certain of the ratepayers will be borne
by the Government.
Clause 14 of the Bill sets forth the
times at which the standard and supplemental grants will be paid over to the
various local authorities, and Clause 20
safeguards the rights of the existing rate
collectors, who would otherwise, of
course, suffer undue hardship owing to
the much smaller poundage of the rates
collected.
The administration of the scheme for
freight-transport rebates is dealt with in
Part III of the Act, and the proposed
scheme is described in detail in the
Schedule. In the Schedule will also be
found particulars of the goods selected for
rebates. The object of the Government
has been to secure that the limited
amount of money available is utilised
in a manner calculated to give the
greatest possible advantage to agriculture and industry. I beg to move.
The
Mr. CAMPBELL: I rise not to express any opposition to this Bill, but to
welcome and approve of it. This is a
Measure which gives some relief at least
to our struggling farmers. Agriculture.
is the chief industry of Northern Ireland,
and the prosperity of Northern Ireland
must rise or fall with the prosperity of
agriculture, so that anything that relieves the farmers ought to be welcomed
by all classes of the community.
farmers of Northern Ireland have been
facing hard times with conspicuous
courage and patience. I welcome the
Bill for the principle which, as it appears
to me, it embodies and enshrines. It is
a recognition of the right and duty of
the Government to apply the principle.
of derating in aid of deserving classes of
the community. Let us not forget that
there is in our urban centres-in Belfast
and other towns--a very large and very
meritorious section of the population who
are paying rates either directly or indirectly through their rents, which they
can ill afford to pay. There may be
reasons-valid reasons-why the principle
of derating, which has been brought in
in aid of struggling farmers, should not
be applied to men of that class. There
may be reasons-valid reasons why
there should not be conceded to them
exemption, total or partial, from the
rates on their humble, wretched dwellings, but I do not know of any such
reasons. I welcome the Bill because it
appears to me to embody a principle
which may be more generally applied
to
the greater good of the entire
public, and my heartfelt sympathy goes
out to the very deserving and helpless
class of the community of whom I have
just spoken.

Mr. LONG: I have already in this
House at some length expressed the profound gratitude of the farming communities of Londonderry and elsewhere
for the great boon that has been conferred by the passing of this Bill, and I
do not propose to weary the House with
a repetition of those reasons and arguments. I wish to express my gratitude
to the Government on behalf of those
Mr. LESLIE: It is unnecessary to
say very much in support of this Bill.
It seems to me to be generous in conception, and admirable in execution.
This Bill does not contain any unnecessary legislation by reference which is so
often objected to by my hon. Friend on my
left (Mr. Cunningham). Of course, there
is a certain amount of reference to previous enactments, but the Bill is SO
drafted as to make everything of that
sort quite easy to follow if the Rating
and Valuation (Apportionment) Act of
last year are borne in mind, or, better
still, kept at one's elbow. The scheme
has been debated again and again. This
Bill seems to me to be remarkably equitable in regard to the arrangement made
for repayment of any overpayment of
rates which has already been made, and
also in regard to the relief which it proposes to give to the fisheries.
The progress of the Bill should be, I think, unusually swift in this House. So far as I
can see there is really nothing to delay it,
except that there is one missing word
that has to be supplied, and perhaps
Clause 5 may require more examination.
I should like--if I could get it now it
would save trouble-an explanation as
to the purpose of sub-clauses 2 and 4. I
want to be sure that the power of still
collecting in two moieties will be made
applicable to what I call special and
petty rates, which do not come under the
exemptions referred to in Clause 3. I
am thinking particularly of those drainage rates which would fall, and do fall,
on a very small locality, and which might
very easily run to several shillings in the
pound. Those who live in the
immediate vicinity of the River Roe, in
County Derry, have recently undertaken
a comparatively inexpensive scheme which
involves, I believe, a rate of 3s in the
pound.
In a case of that sort it is obvious that
it would be more convenient that the rate
should be continued to be levied in two
moieties, as at present, rather than that
it should be levied in one lump. I am
not sure that in cases such as I have
mentioned it is the county council that
makes that particular rate. That I shall
have to look up. As you know, this particular Clause has undergone alteration
recently. I should like some information
about that matter, and if it is not convenient to let me have it now I should
like it at a later stage. As I have already
said, I welcome this Bill.
bodies for this great boon. I am particularly pleased that the land attached
to labourers' cottages is to be exempted,
and with regard to the matter that
Senator Leslie has raised as to the collection of moieties, at present it seems
to me that in purely rural districts the
rates could be collected once per annum,
but this, I think, would be rather a hardship in small towns where there are not
urban councils, and in which they do not
benefit to any considerable extent through
these derating proposals. In small towns.
such as Kilrea, Magherafelt, and Garvagh, the collection of the rates in one
sum would, perhaps, be a hardship, but
in purely rural districts, where the farmers will have in future a very small rate
to pay, there will be no difficulty in paying in one sum. This will save considerable expense in printing for the county
council, and also for the rate collectors.
I, therefore, support the Bill.
Mr. McALLISTER: I am not disposed to offer this Bill anything in the
nature of hostile criticism, but I cannot
accept, I am sorry to say, the eulogistic
references which have been made to it by
various Members of this House, and in
another place. It is undoubtedly, in
their judgment, a picturesque and attractive-looking Bill, but when subjected to
examination and dissection it seems to
me to be a Bill which will not at all confer upon the people generally the advantages expected. I notice that the Minister of Home Affairs in introducing this
Bill described it as an epoch-making Bill.
I would have thought that the Minister
of Home Affairs would have had a more
ambitious legislative outlook than to
describe this Bill in that particular manner.
was
With regard to the reference to the
farming community, I think it
necessary to differentiate between the
various farmers to see how this is going
to benefit the farmers. I have no doubt
it will be of considerable benefit to the
large farmers, but I cannot see where it
is going to be of any benefit, certainly no
73 Local Government Bill-
24 JUNE 1929

Second Reading.
We
material benefit, to the small farmer who
only pays £4 or £5 a year in rates.
are all aware that in recent years the
whole system of the transport of the farmer, his family, and his produce has
been revolutionised and changed. The
farmer in years gone by went into the
market town on the market day by either
his public conveyance-a horse and car----
or by his own van. Now, with so many
buses running every day, he goes into
the provincial town, not necessarily on
market day, but on practically every day
in the week that suits him. His family
also go several miles into school daily en
these buses.
The major portion of the compensation
for the loss of rates is going to come out
of the petrol tax, and the carrying companies have already given notice to put
into operation excess charges on account
of this particular Measure. If the small
farmer who pays £4 or £5 in rates in the
year is going to have the transport to
and from the market towns of himself,
his wife, and his family fairly substantially increased, I do not see where
there can be any saving to the small
farmer.
Again, I would point out that in this
question of transport in years gone by
the farmer carried his produce into the
market town, or to the railway station,
but now it is the custom, and it is a custom which will undoubtedly continue, for
motor lorries to come to his door and take
away to its destination, to the steamer,
or wherever it may be going, his produce,
including his potatoes. These companies,
if they have to pay an extra amount for
petrol on account of this legislation, are
bound to charge the farmer, large or
small, an increased amount on account
of his transport. Therefore, when I do
agree that there will be benefit in respect
of the large farmer, I fail to see how
there can be any benefit in regard to the
small farmer.
Of course, the small farmer is not a
man who keeps an account. He will
know that he pays no rates, but he will
have to pay another sum in lieu of what
he paid for rates, in my judgment, at
least as much, and perhaps more, than
the rates he did pay. Consequently, I
think that if this legislation is passed for
the purpose of really relieving the farmer
of the terrible depressing and uneconomic position in which he is at present
this Bill is a wrong way to go about it.
I do not intend to develop the subject,
but I do say that in lieu of a Bill like
this the proper course to adopt would be
to assist the organisation of farmers in
Northern Ireland to secure better markets for their produce, and to encourage
them as far as possible in a system of
co-operation.
For instance, if we take last year, we
find that potatoes ranged from about 2s
to 8s per cwt. I would suggest to whoever is responsible for the administration
of affairs in Northern Ireland that if they
had assisted the farmers in business
methods, and encouraged them in organisation, there could have been a pooling
system whereby every farmer would have
been assured of 4s to 4s 6d per cwt.
Thus every farmer would have had an
economic return, and would have been
satisfied. That would have been a
fairer method to have adopted in regard
to the general bulk of the farmers than
this Bill seems to adopt.
With regard to the question of rebate
in respect of the transport of productive
goods, I hope to hear an assurance by
the right hon. Gentleman, the Minister
of Finance, when he comes to reply in
reference to the actual machinery and
detailed method that he is satisfied that
the advantages will go to those to whom
it is intended they should go in this particular Bill. We all know that no one
can be more lucid than the right hon.
Gentleman, and that he can make the
most complex financial question clear to
the most ordinary Member of any House.
Therefore, I would ask him to let us
know by what method he is satisfied,
and I have no doubt he is satisfied, that
the rebate on the cost of transport will
go to the producer, to whom it is intended
it should go.
I would also like to ask an assurance
from the noble Viscount, the Leader of
the House, more particularly in the case
of this Bill than in the case of the last
Bill, that we will have an opportunity
before the Committee Stage of examining
the details of this Bill, and to satisfy ourselves that we have ample time to produce, by our joint efforts, the best possible
available Bill out of the structure we
have before us at the moment.
Mr. DORMAN: I am very pleased
to see the right hon. Gentleman, the
Minister of Finance, here to-day. I presume that this Bill is brought in as the
Government's contribution towards a
Local Government Bill—

THE SENATE
[Mr. Dorman]
partial solution of the post-war difficulties
the State has had to contend with by the
depression in trade, and the large volume
of unemployment that unfortunately
prevails in our midst. I welcome this
Bill, but not from the same point of view
that other Members welcomed it. I
welcome it as a proof and admission on the
part of the Government that the great
trinity, private enterprise, competition,
and incentive to action, has failed, and
that the Government is recognising it has
failed inasmuch as it is compelled to step
in and utilise the finances of the State
for the specific purpose of assisting these
three things I have mentioned.
This Bill is proof that from the point
of view of the development of raw 1esources. of manufacturing industry, and
of the distributing sections of industry,
our modern system of producing and distributing wealth has failed. If it had
not failed, but had succeeded, it would
have been unnecessary for the Government to have legislated in this direction.
The Bill will not, in my opinion, give
relief to the extent that is expected.
a matter of fact it would be impossible
for one to speak intelligently upon this
particular Measure unless one had
sufficient time to make inquiries from the
various big industrial undertakings in
Northern Ireland and ascertain the rates
that were paid by them. With this in
view I sought to obtain the valuation of
one
As
or two industrial undertakings in
Belfast, to ascertain what relief would be
given to them, and to what extent it
would assist them to re-absorb into industry men at present on the unemployment list. I found that the valuation
of one large establishment, the York
Street Flax Spinning Company, was
£5,962 per annum, 75 per cent of that
would be £4,471, and 75 per cent of that
amount would be £2.235. and assuming
the rates were 10s in the pound, that is
the amount of the relief that would be
given to the York Street Flax Spinning
Company. Whether or not that relief is
to be of any help in solving the unemployment problem, which I take it is the intention of the Government, depends on
the manner in which that relief is
allocated. If the proprietors of that
large industrial establishment decide to
pass on to their shareholders in increased
dividends the amount of the relief they
obtain from the rates, then, in my opinion,
it will not contribute to the solution of
the problem which I believe the Government are honestly endeavouring to solve
by means of this Bill.
If, on the other hand, they pass it on
to their employees, it will only enable
that large industrial undertaking to add
one shilling a week to the wages of 860
workers in their employment to-day, and
I cannot see by what stretch of the
imagination it can be concluded that by
relieving that large industrial undertaking by that amount it is going to contribute in any material way to the solution of the unemployment problem, or to
the stimulation of industry itself. It
appears to me that a similar sum of
money spent in advertising the advantages of the products of that industry
would perhaps be a more effectual method
of assisting the revival of trade and
absorbing some of the unemployed.
The shipbuilding firm of Messrs. Harland and Wolff are rated at £24,900 per
aunum, and treating that in the same
way as I have treated the York Street
Flax Spinning Company, it would mean
that they could only contribute an increase of a shilling a week in wages to
3,540 of their employees. If the Government insisted that the people who are
going to get this relief of rates should
pass it on to those workers who necessarily spend their earnings from week to
week they would be doing infinitely more
towards solving the problem of unemployment than they will do by the scheme
outlined in this Bill. If this benefit was
passed on to the working classes, whose
standard of comfort at present is so low
that they seek every opportunity of
spending, that would be a stimulus to the
general trade of the neighbourhood, and
would thus contribute in some practical
way to the solution of unemployment.
Bill.
On the other hand, the people of Belfast have a very large productive industry of their own in the gasworks.
The valuation of the gasworks is £12,000,
but apparently it is not going to participate in the relief that is given under this
It appears to me, from my restricted
range of reading, that the
principle underlying the right of the
State to expropriate the property of any
of its citizens by means of taxation is
that it should be taxation without differentiation that whatever taxation 1S
levied upon the community should be paid
equally by every member of the community. It appears to me that in this
particular instance we are going to rob
the taxpaying Peters to contribute
a
Local Government Bill—
Second Reading.
24 JUNE 1929
dole to the ratepaying Pauls. I understand it is the intention across the water
that the major portion of the money required to finance this scheme should be
provided by the petrol tax, and that
means that every person who rides a
motor-cycle will be taxed for the purpose
of relieving another class of the community. That seems to me to violate the
basic principle of justice in taxation.
I do not desire to in any measure
oppose this Bill, but I do not think it
will bring about that stimulus to trade
that the Government evidently think it
will. It entirely depends upon the allocation of the relief that is given as to
whether or not it will be of practical
assistance in the solution of the great
problem of unemployment.
I
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM:
would like to say a word or two from a
workman's point of view in support of this
Bill. I want to tell the House that a
great deal is expected of this Measure by
the workers. We have heard a good deal
about how it is going to benefit the farming class, but I also believe that those industries that are in competition with
foreign countries are bound to benefit by
this Bill. It has been stated that a man
who rides a motor cycle will be taxed to
pay for the relief that is to be given to the
farmers and industrial concerns, but I
always understood that a certain class of
people were out for the taxation of
luxuries for the benefit of the industry,
and that is one of the things this Bill will
do. We are going to tax luxuries for the
purpose of assisting those struggling industries that are endeavouring to exist
in competition with foreign industries.
When the first Derating Bill was before this House the Minister of Finance
told us of the great reduction it would
mean in the cost of plates that were
brought to our shipyards for the building
of ships. Therefore the relief afforded by
this Bill is bound to afford considerable
assistance to our shipyards by enabling
them to compete with foreign countries
in securing orders for ships. Surely every
man in Belfast should be interested in
the success of our shipyards, because a
very large number of our workers depend
for their labour and their wages, and, of
course, the maintenance of their wives
and families, on the building of ships. I
have watched the effect of this Derating
Bill since it was first introduced, and with
the exception of a strong Measure of protection, I do not think there is anything
that will tend more to restore our 111dustries to their former state of prosperity.
The amount of relief in rates that the
shipyards and the York Street Flax
Spinning Company will get may be small,
but it is the reduced cost of the raw
material that are used by these and other
firms that will benefit them and enable
them to compete with foreign countries.
I take a broad view of this question, and
I would not attempt to oppose the Bill on
the narrow grounds which I have heard
advanced here to-day. I was surprised
at the argument put forward by Senator
McAllister that the small farmer would
not benefit by this Bill, because of the
increased bus fares he would have to pay.
If the small farmer wants to have a joy
ride every day of the week, instead of
once a week or once a month, as formerly,
and if he wants to spend the money he
will be relieved of paying in rates, he
will do so for his own pleasure, and I wish
him well. At any rate, he will have the
satisfaction of knowing that the rates on
his land and farm buildings will not
accumulate, and that he will not have to
pay them in one lump sum as he formerly
had to do.
I give this Bill my whole-hearted support, particularly on behalf of the working classes. I believe that it will be of
great benefit to our industries, and that
it will be a godsend to the poor farmer
who, it is said, has been struggling so
hard to make both ends meet.
The MINISTER OF FINANCE (Mr.
Pollock): The last speaker (Mr. Joseph
Cunningham) has crystallised the arguments in favour of this Measure so fully
that on the main features of it I do not
think there is much left for me to say.
To appreciate properly the full effect of
this epoch-making Measure, as it was
called by the Minister of Home Affairs
elsewhere, one must have regard to its
intention, and that is, to relieve productive industry. I want to underline
that word productive. It is not contemplated that it should extend its area over
any other commercial or industrial
operations other than those that are
productive. My hon. Friend opposite
(Mr. Dorman) who gave us some information on general economics, took a rather
limited view when he gave particular instances of the effect of the full operation
of this Bill.
79 Local Government Bill--

THE SENATE
Second Reading.
[Mr. Pollock]
A large spinning mill, such as he indicated, will undoubtedly benefit to the
extent he stated, but he failed to take
account of the fact that in the material
that was used there had been effected
long prior to its entry into the mill a
considerable reduction in the cost of production. That is to say, the farmer who
grew the flax had had his rates reduced,
eliminated, indeed, altogether; the
scutch mill-owner was relieved of his
rates to a large extent, and the spinner,
the weaver, and the bleacher were also
relieved to a large extent of the payment
of rates, and, as I think I explained in
this House on a previous occasion,
cumulative effect of all this was very
material indeed. As Senator Joseph
Cunningham indicated in respect of one
most important staple industry, namely,
that of shipbuilding, the operation of
the derating scheme in Great Britain,
combined with its operation here, will
materially affect the cost of producing
an article, and will enable those who are
producing it to compete with other
countries in respect of articles intended
for sale.
the
The effect of this relief is particularly
apparent in the case of shipbuilding, because it is well-known that since the
war, countries that formerly purchased
their ships in Great Britain or in
Belfast had started large shipbuilding industries of their own, and at
One period indeed they threatened
the very existence of our industries
in
United Kingdom.
the
We saw
British ship-owners actually sending
ships to Rotterdam for repair and rebuilding, when all the shipbuilding yards
around our entire coasts were lying idle.
The effect of this derating scheme will be
most beneficial, and it has already been
most beneficial because of the encouragement, and the life it has introduced into
industry. I will go backwards a little
with the comments that were made by
Members. I noticed that my Friend,
Senator McAllister, rather depreciated
the effect of these Acts. In his opinion
the advantage in the rates was balanced
by the imposition of the tax on petrol.
Well, if you take the aggregate as any
method of comparison, the petrol tax in
the North of Ireland produces £250,000,
while the farmers are benefited to an
extent exceeding £500,000.
Even if you
assume for a moment-and I do not
assume that the petrol tax is such an
imposition on the farmer that it hurts
him very materially—————
Mr. McALLISTER: I differentiated
between the farmers. I said that the
small farmer would not benefit, but the
large farmer would. You cannot take
the collective imposition of taxes as a
test.
Mr. POLLOCK : The large farmer
will benefit certainly in a larger measure,
because he is rated more highly. The
small farmer benefits in a smaller degree.
As a matter of fact, in the calculation it
was my duty to make when going into the
financial arrangements of this Measure I
found that the farmer, large and small,
received a greater benefit than he did by
the transfer of the land from landlord
to tenant, and the reduction of rent
from rents to annuities. It was really
from the point of view of actual money
a more beneficial Measure than the Land
Acts, that is, as I say in regard to
pounds, shillings, and pence. (Hon.
Members: Hear, hear). Therefore, I do
not think the hon. Senator's argument
in respect of the small farmer can have
full application. I am perfectly satisfied
with this, that if you submit to the farmer, small or large, the question whether
he will accept rate relief, or relief from
petrol tax, I have no hesitation in saying
what the character of the vote would be.
Mr MCALLISTER:
esque Bill.
It is a pictur-
And a practical
Mr. POLLOCK :
Bill. As regards the criticism of Senator
Leslie, I do not regard it as criticism,
but as comment, because it was in the
friendliest possible spirit. The question
of whether the rates are collected in one
or two moieties does not seem to be an
important matter, because it rests in the
hands of the local authorities, and the
presumption is that the local authorities
have got sufficient interest in the matter
in respect of the ratepayers, that whatever method is most suitable and most
convenient will be adopted by them. So
that I say, as far as the Senator regards
this as
an important issue, it can be
arranged without difficulty.
On the whole this Measure has been
received with a great deal of commendation, and deservedly so, because I may
tell you here quite frankly that I got
somewhat of a shock by the financial
responsibility that was imposed, not by
Local Government Bill.
Noxious Weeds Bill.
24 JUNE 1929
the Act itself, but by the rather startling
change that was made by the British
Government when it decided to ante-date
the operation of agricultural relief from
the first of October to the first of April.
It involved the provision of six months
relief to the agricultural interests, which
was out of all proportion to the relief
that was given to British agriculturists--
heavier I mean for this reason, that agriculture is a very small portion of the relief which is given in Great Britain,
whereas here in Northern Ireland agriculture is vastly larger in proportion.
When giving six months relief in Great
Britain the British Government were not
sacrificing very much of the Suspense
Account. We, on the other hand, were
called upon suddenly to provide a proportionately very much larger amount.
To that extent, therefore, the farmers of
course, are benefiting. I may tell you
quite frankly it did upset my calculations
a little.
However, I think we are all agreed
that the farmers are a long-suffering class.
They have suffered a great deal from
many conditions, and more especially
from the fact that their produce must
And
compete with the world market.
when Senator McAllister indicated that
our Government would be very much
better occupied than in passing relief
Measures of this kind, if they endeavoured

to secure larger prices for the produce
of the soil-that is a very tall order--
and various economic matters of that
kind. Our Ministry of Agriculture, let
me say, has been interesting itself in many
of these matters. It is beyond the power
of man to determine the price for a commodity that is competed for in every
country in the world. I do not knowit seems rather curious in discussing this
Measure to introduce it, but the hon.
Members may have noticed that one very
important crop of the farmer could not be
One
given away during the past week.
must have seen reported in the newspapers
that potatoes were being used as manure
or being allowed to lie and manure the
ground, and yet that is our primary

Mr. MCALLISTER: I said by pooling
and organisation it would be.
We
Mr. POLLOCK: Our Department of
Agriculture has directed its energies to
wards co-operation for some time.
have 120,000 farmers of whom 30,000
hold under ten acres, and co-operation is

have no doubt at all that, given the necessary time, and if we do not begin pulling up the young plant before it has time
to grow, the result of the Government's

apart altogether from the direct benefit
