I do not think there is anything very
recondite in this Bill which requires any
further explanation from me than what
has been given here and elsewhere, and
in the public newspapers. I myself
have dealt with it on public platforms and in the House and elsewhere.
I think the great mass of our people, the
farmers and others, are fully satisfied
not only with the comparative simplicity
of the Measure, but with the enormous
value of it.
On Question, Motion: "That the Bill
be now read a Second time," agreed to.
Noxious Weeds Bill.
satis-
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: This short
Bill is designed to render more
factory the machinery for securing the
destruction of certain noxious weeds.
The existing legislation on the subject is,
as hon. Members are probably aware, contained in the Weeds and Agricultural
Seeds (Ireland) Act, 1909.
Clause 1 of the Bill now before the
House gives power to the Ministry of
Agriculture to make an order declaring
that all or any of certain species of plants
are noxious weeds throughout Northern
Ireland. It will thus be possible to obtain uniformity throughout the Province
in the matter, whereas under the existing
system plants which are regarded as
noxious weeds in one county may not be
so regarded in an adjoining county. Thus
the lands of a farmer in, say, County
Tyrone may be seeded by weeds in County
Londonderry of a species which, in compliance with the existing law, he must
I am sure that
take steps to destroy.
hon. Members will all agree that uniformity is very desirable.
Noxious Weeds Bill-
Second Reading.
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
Clause 2 enables the police to be used
for enforcing the destruction of noxious
weeds at the proper time. At present,
this duty is carried out by inspectors temporarily employed by the Ministry of
Agriculture, but it has been found that
in practice it is impossible for any reasonable number of such inspectors to give
anything like adequate supervision of the
farms; in fact it has not in any year been
possible to have more than 10 per cent.
of the agricultural holdings in the Province inspected. The Government considers that as the weeds concerned can be
readily identified without any expert
knowledge of botany, the work of inspection can be quite conveniently carried
out by the police. I should, however,
point out that under Clause 4 (1) of this
Bill a prosecution for an offence could
not be brought by the police until the
Ministry of Agriculture had had an opportunity of reviewing the circumstances
of the case.
By Clause 3 it is proposed to fix minimum penalties for offences under the Ac
and, at the same time, to reduce the
maximum penalties at present in force.
The fines inflicted during last season
ranged from sixpence to ten shillings,
amounts which obviously do not entail
sufficient hardship for this class of offender, and are not large enough to
secure compliance with the Act. I beg
to move.
Colonel PAKENHAM: I rise to welcome this Bill in many parts, but there
are one or two things to which I take
exception. I agree that punishment for
allowing these weeds should be made
general over the whole North of Ireland.
It is very wrong to make ragwort a
noxious weed, in County Derry say, and
not elsewhere, to make it penal there, and
not penalise the neighbouring county. I
am all in favour of that part of the Bill.
The part of the Bill I take exception to
is the imposition of fines as they are proposed under this Bill. Under the old
Act there were fines which, as the noble
Viscount, the Leader of the House, has
said, ranged from 6d to 10s. We are
not out really to make money out of the
farmer. We are really out to get the
farmer to agree to destroy weeds which
are of no benefit to him or to anybody
else. We do not really want to punish
him so heavily. I think that the imposition of the £1 minimum fine is a very
great hardship. If I may say so, I think
it would be far better to leave it as it
was in the 1909 Act, without expecting
to get large sums of money. If the farmer is so negligent of his own farm as to
grow weeds, and he is fined time after
time 10s for three or four years running, he will get sick of it. I think it is
too much to put on a £1 minimum. We
know there is another minimum fine in
connection with another Act in this
country which is not producing very good
work anywhere. These fines are a hardship in very many circumstances. Where
it is quite right that one man should be
fined a pound, in another case it is an
extreme hardship, where a man only
breaks the law very slightly. I am very
much against the imposition of a minimum
fine. I would far rather fix the maximum.
perhaps, a little higher, and leave it to the
discretion of the magistrates, after the
Ministry of Agriculture have given them.
a lead. Otherwise, I heartily support
the Bill.
Mr. LONG: I also wish to support the
Bill, but I regret to say I disagree with
the last speaker with regard to the amount
of the fines. In my judgment, a man
who permits weeds to grow to seed on his
land and damage the lands of his neighbour is not unduly penalised by a fine of
£1. The damage to his neighbour may
be out of proportion even to that sum
and amount to a very much larger sum.
I should like to point out that this Act
was permissive in the past, and it was for
the county councils to adopt it or not. I
am glad to say that all the county councils
in Northern Ireland adopted the Act.
That circumstance in itself is somewhat
unusual because I find that where an Act
is permissive as a general rule it is not
adopted. I am glad to say that this Act
was adopted by all the county councils in
Ireland. There was, as the House has
heard, a difference of opinion among local
authorities as to what really were harmful
or noxious weeds. For instance, in some
of the counties ragwort was not included
among these weeds. There is quite a difference of opinion in country districts
about the harmfulness of ragwort. The
story of the old blind farmer who rode on
horseback to purchase a new farm is often
quoted. The old blind farmer on arriving
at the farm that was for sale asked for
ragwort sufficiently strong to tie the horse
On finding that there was no such
thing on the farm he immediately returned
home and gave up all thought of buying
that farm.
to.
Noxious Weeds Bill.

Be
As a general rule ragwort grows on farms
that are very fertile. When this land is
broken the
up
is
crop very good. Farmers
think that ragwort conduces in some
measure to the increased or good crop obtained. I do not agree with that.
that as it may, I welcome the fact that
there is uniformity throughout Northern
Ireland in regard to this matter. I think
the suggested change to the police will be a
distinct improvement, because the inspectors being too few in number are unable
to inspect all the land in Northern Ireland. They arrive usually in a motor car,
travelling at a furious rate, and they only
traverse the county roads. There is a
considerable amount of land that does not
adjoin the county roads. It is necessary
to go up farm lanes to see the land.
our district the police are required to do
systematic patrols eight or nine hours per
day. They are not strangers as the inspectors are. They get familiarised with
a place and know the people, and they are
in a very much better position to detect
cases of this nature than strangers who are
passing along the road rapidly.
In
Last year I saw a field of thistles being
reaped. It was a thick growth of thistles
and it was being reaped by a mowing
machine. At the end of August the
seeds had become disseminated over a
very considerable area of adjoining land.
I think that is an abominable practice
and should be put an end to at once.
This sort of thing occurs not on the farms
occupied by farmers at all-hardly ever;
it occurs on the land which is let on the
eleven months system. The temporary
occupier has no abiding or real interest
in what is good for the land or for his
neighbours either. I therefore support
the Bill, and I think it will be a great
improvement.
Mr. CAMPBELL: There is one part
of the Bill which strikes me as being out
of the common. That is the Section which
vests in certain officers and constables of
the Royal Ulster Constabulary the
power to act in the execution of Part
I of the principal Act and this Act as
if they were officers of the Ministry of
Agriculture.
The police have many duties cast on their
shoulders at present, and where are these
duties to end? I wonder in the case of
town recruited constables how many have
any conception of what some of these
noxious weeds are, of the difference
between a ragwort and an ox eye daisy?
Mr. LESLIE: I think that this Bill,
although it is a very short one, is deserv-
I am
ing of a critical examination.
afraid I have to say, at the outset, that
all I like about the Bill is its object. I
certainly am not going to stand here in
support of the further propagation or
dissemination of weeds. I agree that
weeds in Northern Ireland are the
greatest obstacle in the way of men who
want to make two blades of grass grow
where one grew before. I am also not
unmindful of an instructive illustration
exhibited in one of the Department of
Agriculture's exhibits at one of our recent shows. It showed that the area of
land in Northern Ireland rendered useless
by weeds was equal to the entire
area of, I think, the County of Fermanagh.
That is a very striking object lesson. I am
afraid we will have to admit that whatever demon seems to be in charge of
that particular branch of the curse of
Eden attends to his business with a
thoroughness worthy of a better cause.
Weeds are a very great nuisance. There
was an attempt made by the Act of 1909
to deal with the matter. That is about
the only attempt that has been made.
Now we are proposing to embark on
another undertaking with the same object, and it is proposed to give powers to
the police. I think we ought to ask
ourselves what are the powers we are
going to give the police. The Bill does
not tell us. The powers that will be
conferred on the police-that is, on such
constable as the Minister of Home Affairs
may authorise to undertake the job-are
to direct the cutting or destruction of
weeds and to provide a time within which
such cutting or destruction is to be accomplished. I think as the last speaker
(Mr. Campbell) has said, those powers
are trying a young constable perhaps
rather high. The last speaker spoke also
of the duties which are already cast on
the shoulders of the police, and he asked
where these duties were to end. As one
living in a rural district in which the
number of police barracks is not as great
as I could wish I am certainly not at all
anxious that such patrolling as the constabulary are now able to do should be
reduced in order that they may devote
their energies to investigation in another
direction.
I am afraid I do not think that the
casting of this duty on the constabulary
will enhance their popularity, nor do I
think that it will tend to attract
re-

THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
cruits to the force when it requires

At all events patrolling along
the roads does give the constabulary an

and in that capacity have visited every

the minimum fine.
no
Another thing, like my hon. and
gallant Friend (Colonel Pakenham), that
I object to-and I know the House
expects me not only to object, but
to oppose it-is the imposition of
I will say
more about it to-day, except, perhaps, I
may be permitted to say that I have been
a magistrate now for a great many years,
and I never knew any advantage whatever to come from deciding a case before
I heard it. I pass from the minimum
fine to draw attention to some omissions
which, it seems to me, the Bill contains.
There is nothing here to discourage the
sowing of bad seed-by which I mean
sowing a poor selection. We know there
is a certain power whereby a man can get
his seed tested. The Department have
helped farmers much in that way. Where
they break down in that respect is where
they are bound to break down, and that
is, they cannot get the testing always done
in time to prevent a man sowing the stock
which he has purchased. It is an insuperable difficulty. The only way we
can do it, is to bring more pressure to bear
and impose severe penalties on the person
who sells seeds which, to his knowledge,
are below the
standard.
proper
Under the Statute of 1920-I do not
mean the Government, of Ireland Act,
but another Statute of the same year—
there is a distinct penalty imposed on
people who sow bad seeds.
Of course,
that Clause does not apply to Ireland. I
cannot see why it should not. I think if we
cast our attention more to the discouragement of selling seeds below a certain
standard of purity we may do more than
we
can by
by this Bill. Again, the
Bill does not settle the vexed question
of the responsibility for the cleansing
of weeds from the land. The hon. Senator (Mr. Long) alluded to this matter.
He spoke of an entire farm that was
covered with the weed. I am speaking
of a field which is set, for crops, even for
roots or potatoes, and let from May to
November, according to the locality, or for
the purpose of pasture. This Bill does
not settle the vexed question on whom lies
the responsibility for cleansing such a
field?
I
Is it the rated occupier or is it the man
who has got the farm? That is a point
I will draw attention to. Passing on
from that, what do the noble Viscount's
remarks lead up to? Do they not lead
to a fairly founded complaint that this
Bill contains unfair and improper legislation by reference, and that the proper
step to be taken would be to consolidate
the law dealing with this matter.
should like to be assured that passing
this Bill is merely a step to consolidation.
If it is a step to consolidation a good deal
of what I have said about legislation by
reference of course falls to the ground,
but as it stands it is not fair, I think, to
impose permanently on the unfortunate
small farmers of Northern Ireland a Bill
containing a substitution for an important section and a reference to a subsection of another section and the effect
of it and so on. It really is not fair. I
will not go so far as to move that we do
not read the Bill a Second time, but I
do venture to hope that what I have said
will be borne in mind and that, perhaps,
the Government will introduce a Measure
dealing with some of the points to which
I have drawn attention.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I will
give the information asked for by the
hon. and learned Senator (Mr. Leslie) at
a later date, if I may. With regard to
the duties of the Royal Ulster Constabulary I should like to say that I think
their intelligence is sufficiently high to
enable them to grapple with what ragweed or ox-eye daisy looks like, but they
will be fully instructed in the details of
their duty by the Ministry of Agriculture before they take them up.
the sowing of impure seeds, which the
hon. Senator (Mr. Leslie) also mentioned, surely if this Bill is passed it
would be very much to the advantage of
the individual farmer to take every precaution he can that his seed is pure or
he may suffer the infliction even of a
minimum fine.

21 JUNE 1929
Mr. LESLIE: I meant bought seeds.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Then he
will have to buy somewhere else in
future. I think I can give the assurance
that the responsibility for cleansing the
land will fall on the occupier of the land,
whether temporary or permanent, but I
would like to give a more detailed assurance at a later date.
On Question, Motion ("That the Bill
be now read a Second time') agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for Wednesday next.
Adjournment.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: In moving "That this House do now adjourn
until to-morrow," I should like to give
some idea of the time I can allot, to the
consideration of the measures introduced
to-day. To-morrow, I think, I shall be
able to take the Committee Stage of the
School Teachers (Oath and Declaration)
Bill, to which I do not think there
was very much objection, and the
Second Reading of three Finance Bills.
With regard to the Derating Bill, the
Street Trading Bill, and the Noxious
Weeds Bill, I am not able, as a matter
of fact, to give very much time for consideration. All second chambers at the
end of a Session appear to me to suffer from
the same disease, that is to say, they have
anæmia of legislation at the beginning,
and apoplexy at the end; and I do not
know any way in which the House can
avert it, unless we have all-night sittings,
which I do not think would be popular
among Members of this House. But I
am perfectly willing to have as many as
hon. Members like. I meant, originally,
to have the Street Trading Bill in Committee to-morrow, but as there has been
a good deal of criticism of it I do not
think that would be a fair proposal. I,
therefore, have put it off till the latest
day I can, and that is Thursday, which I
hope will give sufficient time for Members of this House to consider it. I would
like to avoid, if possible, sitting on Friday, but if hon. Senators do not feel that
that is long enough, I will put it off till
Friday and have a Friday sitting. On
Thursday I hope also to take the Local
Government (Rating and Finance) Bill.
On Wednesday I will have the Third
Reading of the School Teachers (Oath and
Declaration) Bill, the Noxious Weeds
Bill, and one or two small Committees.
That is the best I can give the House in
the matter of consideration, and I hope
hon. Members will, at least, give me
credit for excellent intentions if I can do
no more.
Mr. LESLIE: It seems to me that
there is one possibility that the noble
Viscount has neglected to contemplate,
and that is that some of these Bills, if
there is any real difficulty about them,
might stand over till the Autumn Session.
I do not think myself there will be any
great difficulty in taking the Measures in
the way indicated, but to do so might
very possibly result in Amendments being
put on the Paper in a more or less halfbaked condition, as the saying goes, or
in Amendments being put down which may
not be moved. That is the only difficulty
I see, and that really might be the
simplest solution in the long run. I do
not suppose it matters much whether an
Amendment is not moved when it is put
down. I think everybody must have
made up their minds, even in the limited
time at their disposal, whether they want
to put down Amendments or not. The
length of the discussion, or whether an
all-night sitting may be necessary or not,
of course, one cannot forecast.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The House
will understand that I could not under
any circumstances postpone the derating
Measure.
Mr. LESLIE: Oh, no.
It might
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
be possible for some of the minor ones to
stand over.
On Question, Motion agreed to.

Expiring Laws Continuance Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I have to ask the leave of the
House to postpone this Bill.
Loans Guarantee Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: Hon. Members are, no doubt.
familiar with the system of Government
guarantees which this Bill proposes to
extend, and no one, I think, will deny
that the giving of these guarantees has
had a very substantial effect in securing
the return of a certain measure of prosperity to the industry of our Province,
more especially to the shipbuilding industry.
At present guarantees have been issued
amounting to about £10,300,000, leaving
available from the amount in respect of
which statutory authority exists a balance
of a little over £1,000,000.
The Government considers that in view
of the present state of unemployment it
is desirable to increase this margin by a
Loans Guarantee Bill.
Government Loans Bill
25 JUNE 1929
large amount and to provide for the extension of the time within which guarantees may be made to the 31st March, 1930,
in order that the Government may be in
a position to continue its policy of maintaining by co-operation with the shipyards
the improved conditions, which, I am
glad to say, have prevailed during the
past year.
For fear of misunderstanding I should,
however, like to point out that the system
is not likely to involve any direct charge
on the Exchequer, as adequate steps are
in every case taken to obtain ample
security in respect of any amount guaranteed. I beg to move.
Mr. MCALLISTER: I am sure this is
a Bill which will commend itself to all
the Members of this House. It is, no
doubt, a pity that the trade of this great
City, so far as shipbuilding is concerned,
was not able to develop and progress from
natural sources without this artificial assistance on the part of the Government
by way of guaranteed loans.
However,
we must approve of the part, the Government have played with regard to this particular industry. But it brings home to
me the influence and power of organised
forces, such as the shipbuilding associations and organised labour. I should
think, speaking as one who resides in an
urban and rural area, that if the people
of those areas, embracing the oldest, most
important, and greatest industry of all in
Northern Ireland, the farming community, would take a leaf out of the shipowners' book, and that of the organised
skilled and unskilled industry of this city.
and by proper organisation weld together
their forces I think they, by organised
effort, could have prevailed upon the Government to come to their assistance and
rescue at this particular time of unparalleled depression and disorganisation in
the agricultural industry.
I do not wish to delay the House longer
more than to say, as far as I am personally
concerned, although I recognise the
country districts do indirectly benefit by
the absorption of the unemployed in the
cities like Belfast, but although the
country districts do not directly benefit
by this, still I should think it, would have
the approval of every citizen of Northern
Ireland.
Colonel PAKENHAM: Senator
McAllister touched upon what is a very
important point, but I should like to
remind him of the fact that there is an
organisation which is out, to help the
farmer, the Farmers' Union of Northern
Ireland. We know, all of us who live in
the country, how extraordinarily diincult
it is to get farmers to combine. The
Farmers' Union has been preaching cooperation and combination for years and
years in Northern Ireland, but the farmers do not seem to see eye to eye
with the Farmers Union.. They are out
--and they say it quite openly--to benefit themselves and not their neighbours.
A good many of them would not think
twice of farmers helping the whole lot of
them. They want to be helped individually. I put it up to the Senator who
has just sat down it is a great thing for
all of us to preach co-operation.
But
co-operation, as the Minister of Finance
said the other day, does not come quite
easily. It is a thing that, has to be looked
after, and nurtured, and nursed, and kept
alive. I commend to Senator McAllister
that he should go and preach the doctrine
of co-operation in County Antrim and
see what it gets him.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Government Loans Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: The main objects of this Bill
are to remove the time limit which under
existing legislation is in force for advances from our Government Loans Fund
by reason of which there is no statutory
authority for the issue of advances to
local authorities and so forth since the
31st March last, and also to extend the
amount of loans which may be issued
from its present maximum of £4,250,000
to a total of seven millions. These objects are dealt with in Clause 1 of the Bill
before you.
Clause 2 gives power to the Ministry of
Finance to charge and recover interest on
arrears of the principal and interest of
loans which may be issued, and fixes the
rate of interest chargeable for this purpose at a rate not exceeding 6 per cent.
I beg to move.
Mr. LESLIE: I think we may take
it from what has been said and what appears in the Bill that this Bill will not
effect any release of money from Parliamentary control. All that it does is to
School Teachers' Bill. 96
Finance Bill.

I
[Mr. Leslie]
extend the time and increase the amount,
but leaves unaffected the purposes to
which the money could be applied. These
are the purposes, I understand, disclosed
in the schedule of the original Bill.
should like to be assured of that. I
should also like to be informed at some
stage what amount of money put down
as lent has in fact been repaid, in order
that we may get some idea of what is the
outstanding liability, because I imagine
the same principle applies to loans under
under this code as applies in the case of
the Loans Guarantee which we have just
read a Second time, that is, the money
once lent is charged in the books against
the total authorised issue, and that when
it is repaid the effect is not to increase the
authorised issue, but to keep alive what
is really a dummy figure. I suggest that
it is advisable that this House should
have that on record.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I do not rise to
oppose in any way the Bill now before the
House. But one point I would emphasise. This is an addition of 23 millions
to the existing amount of Government
loans. That is an extremely large.
addition. I am perfectly sure it will be
guaranteed. One must have noted how
in recent years large sums of money devoted to public purposes, are more or less
taken
from the direct control of
away
Parliament by different Acts. I do not
say it is so here.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I am unable at the moment to
give the information which the hon. and
learned Senator (Mr. Leslie) has asked
for, but I will do so at a later stage.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Finance Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: The object of this Bill is to
grant certain relief from payment of
double stamp duty on solicitors' certificates, and to give statutory authority for
the payment of the annual surplus in
respect of each of the three financial years
ending 31st March, 1928, 1929, and 1930.
into the Rating Relief Suspense Account.
established by the Exchequer and
Financial Provisions Act of last year.
This Suspense Account, will be available
for meeting the cost of derating. I beg
to move.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I wish to express
my complete approval of this Bill, for the
reason that it removes a source of friction
and trouble regarding the stamp duties
of solicitors in practice before 1921. As
this Bill accomplishes this object it has
my hearty approval.
Mr. McALLISTER: I would like to
add that, I think this Bill will commend
itself to everyone in this House, because
it removes what was, undoubtedly, an
injustice to many practitioners, and particularly those adjacent to the border
counties. These men entered their profession as solicitors of the Incorporated
Law Society of all Ireland, and they entered that, profession on the understanding that one single payment of stamp
duty would be sufficient and would be
all that would be expected from them.
Therefore, I think it is doing but mere
justice to the legal profession that the
state of affairs whereby solicitors were
penalised by having to pay two licence
fees instead of one should now be rectified by this Bill.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved, "That this Bill be committeed."(Viscount Masserecne and Ferrard).
On Question, Motion negatived.

Bankruptcy (Amendment) Bill (S.).
Order for Consideration of Commons
Amendments read.
Moved, "That this House do now take
into consideration the Amendments made
by the Commons to this Bill."—(Viscount
Charlemont).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Commons Amendments:
Leave out Clause 2 and insert the following new Clause:-
2. (Deeds of Arrangement).
(1) Where a debtor has entered into
a deed of arrangement for the benefit
of his creditors generally, to which the
Deeds of Arrangement Act, 1887, as
amended by the Deeds of Arrangement
Act, 1890, applies, and the following
conditions have been complied with,
namely:-
(a) the debtor has secured the assent to such deed of three-fifths in
number and value of those of his
creditors to whom he is indebted to
the amount of ten pounds and upwards, the assent of any creditor
being established by his executing
the deed of arrangement or sending
to the debtor or the trustee appointed by the deed his assent in
writing attested by a witness; and
(b) the debtor or the trustee under the said deed within one month
from the date of the first execution
by him or any creditor of the deed of
arrangement, or within such further
period as may be allowed by order of
the court applies to the court and
obtains an order of the court declaring that he has obtained the assent
mentioned in the preceding paragraph, and that in all the circumstances of the case, the proposals
contained in the deeds of arrangement are, in the opinion of the court,
reasonable and proper; and
(c) the application referred to in
the preceding paragraph has been
made on notice to all the creditors
given and vouched in the same manner as notices of sittings in arrangement matters, and for this purpose
the debtor has filed in the office of
the court a copy of the debtor's affidavit filed by him in compliance with
sub-section (1) of section six of the
Deeds of Arrangement Act, 1887;
Bankruptcy Bill-
Commons Amendments.
THE SENATE
then in such case a creditor of such
debtor shall not, unless the deed becomes void, be entitled to present a
bankruptcy petition against the debtor
founded on the execution of the dead,
or on any other act committed by the
debtor in the course or for the purposes
of the proceedings preliminary to the
execution of the deed, as an act of
bankruptcy, or to apply for a debtor's
summons against the debtor in respect
of any amount due or claimed to be
due at the date of the execution of the
deed.
(2) In case any creditor shall, prior
to the making of the application referred to in paragraph (b) of the preceding sub-section, or pending the
hearing thereof, file a petition in bankruptcy against the debtor, the court
may, on the application of the debtor
or the trustee under the deed of arrangement, stay further proceedings on
foot of such petition, or extend the time
for showing cause against the adjudication thereunder, upon such terms as
to costs or otherwise as to the court
shall seem fit, and if the court shall
make such an order as referred to in
sub-section (1) of this section, further
proceedings on foot of any such petition
shall thereupon be stayed without further order, or if adjudication has taken
place thereon such adjudication shall
stand annulled, but in either event upon such terms as to costs or otherwise
as to the court shall seem fit.
After Clause 21 insert the following
new Clause:-
22. (Revesting of property in bankruptcy matter).
(1) Where an order is made by the
court for the payment of the surplus referred to in section three hundred and
four of the Act of 1857 to a bankrupt,
his executors, administrators or assigns,
such order shall have the effect of vesting the said surplus in the bankrupt,
his executors, administrators or assigns.
(2) Where an offer of composition
after bankruptcy by a bankrupt or his
friends has been accepted by his
creditors, and the adjudication of
bankruptcy has been annulled by the
court, and there remains in the possession of, or vested in, or otherwise
subject to the control of the assignees
or trustee in the bankruptcy, any part
of the estate or effects of the debtor
whose bankruptcy has been anulled, it
shall be lawful for the court to order
that the same shall be vested in the
said debtor, his heirs, executors, administrators, or assigns and such order
shall have the effect of vesting the same
accordingly.
(3) Any order made in pursuance of
this section shall, for all purposes, be
deemed to be a conveyance or assignment of property, and may be registered accordingly.
Viscount
CHARLEMONT:
These
new Clauses have been introduced to meet
the views of solicitors and others who
have been engaged in bankruptcy practice.
The new Clause 2 relating to Deeds of
Arrangement reproduces all the features
of the original Clause with the following
additions:-(a) The clause as originally
drafted would not prevent a creditor
taking out a debtor's summons against the
debtor and making him a bankrupt for
failure to pay the sum claimed in the
summons. The addition now made will
prevent a recalcitrant creditor from
taking out a debtor's summons which in
most cases would be an act of simple
malice, but which would be harmful to
the interests of the creditors as a whole
under the Deed of Arrangement.
(b) It has been considered that the
period of one month given to the debtor
in paragraph (b) of Clause 2 was rather
short, especially if there were a number of
creditors whose assent had to be obtained.
and that it might be well to give the Court
power to extend the time for cause shown.
With regard to the new Clause to come
after Clause 21, hitherto any ultimate
surplus standing to the credit of a bankrupt or in a composition after bankruptcy
had to be reconveyed by a formal conveyance which involved considerable expenditure. By this Clause only an Order of
the Court will be necessary. I beg to
move, "That the Senate do agree with the
Commons in the said Amendments."
Mr. CAMPBELL: The Amendments
proposed are admirable from the standpoint of business men and of the legal profession, and Parliament is deeply indebted
to the gentlemen who composed the Departmental Committee, on whose Report
this Bill is based. These gentlemen devoted much time and study and a great
deal of labour to their task. In my humble
judgment the time is ripe for a codification
Bankruptcy Bill.
Adjournment.
25 JUNE 1929
of the entire bankruptcy law. It has been
codified in England even in very recent
years. It may be a task of some difficulty
and magnitude, but the sooner it is undertaken and the law here placed on
equality with the law across the water the
better it will be.
On Question, Motion agreed to.

Bangor (County Down) Improvement
Bill.
Amendments made by Joint Committee
considered and agreed to.
Law of Town Tenancies.
Mr. CAMPBELL: Before putting the
question that stands in my name I wish
to say how glad the friends of the reform
suggested by the question would be if the
matter could be treated in a calm and dispassionate spirit. Local authorities in
Northern Ireland have passed resolutions
calling for reform on the lines indicated
in the question. Deep dissatisfaction will
be felt by large bodies of opinion in Ulster
if the matter is unduly postponed. Sooner
rather than later the problem must be
faced and solved. I trust the Government
will face it with the largeness of view shown
by the statesmen across the water in the
case of the Agricultural Holdings Act,
1923. In that instance, as in others, the
Imperial Parliament revised binding contracts between landlord and tenant, as it
did likewise in the case of the Town
Tenants Act, the Irish Land Acts, and,
more recently by Mr. Baldwin's Government, in the case of the Landlord and
Tenant Act, 1927. Many of these landlord-cum-tenant contracts are not free
contracts. Many are made, even under
modern conditions, between men in unequal positions for bargaining. This
applies with particular emphasis to the
building lease which operates to deprive
the tenant at the end of his term of improvements he has covenanted to make,
and to deprive him of them without any
compensation. This is an injustice which
ought to be remedied. The Government
have a rare opportunity here. Will they
seize it? They can rectify a system
which legalises the confiscation of the
tenant's capital and labour. Good landlords will welcome this reform; bad
landlords who turn the screw tightly when
renewing leases may not welcome it. In
my humble judgment this change of the
law can be brought into operation without
injustice to any class or any individual in
the community. I beg to ask the Leader
of the House whether the Government have
vet decided to bring in an early
Measure for the amendment of the law of
landlord and tenant of town tenancies in
Northern Ireland, and in particular to
ensure to holders of expiring building
leases renewals of their leases on equitable
terms, giving them credit to the full value
for improvements effected by them irrespective of any covenants binding them to
effect such improvements.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): The Report recently submitted to the Government by the Commission appointed by the Minister of Home
Affairs is still the subject of careful consideration, but it would be too early yet to
make any statement as to the introduction
of legislation on the subject.
The hon. Member may, however, rest
assured that no time will be lost in arriving
at a decision on the matter.
Oral Answers.
Noxious Weeds Bill.
26 JUNE 1929
Mr. MCALLISTER: I just want to intervene for a monent, and to ask the
noble Lord, the Leader of the House, to
address himself to one particular aspect
of this particular case. The vital grievance in this question, in this whole matter of landlord and tenant, is the insertion in the present Town Tenants Bill of
the Clause that this Act shall not apply
to a lease containing a covenant for
valuable consideration, which means that
if a man expends a certain amount of
money on buildings at the end of the
term of his lease, whatever it may be-
40, 50, or 60 years-the landlord at the
present time acquires the whole benefit
of these improvements.
I would not have intervened at this
stage had I not wanted to refer to a speech
by the Prime Minister recently at
Ballymena, where he stressed the
point in reference to the sanctity
of contracts. Am I right in interpreting from the manner in which
the Prime Minister made that speech, and
the extent to which he stressed that particularly vital point-am I right in interpreting his views and forecasting his
ideas of legislation by saying that that
vital material grievance of the holders of
building leases is not to be met? Because that is the only material issue at
the Commission to inquire into this question which my learned Friend, the hon.
Senator beside me, and I were associated
with. If that grievance is not met legislation, in my opinion, will be simply abortive and of no material effect at all, because this is the only grievance of which
substantial complaint has been made. I
hope I am not interpreting the Prime
Minister's speech correctly in suggesting
that that speech leaves the impression that
it is not the intention of the Government
in any future legislation to deal with this
vital material question. I would like the
noble Lord, the Leader of the House, to
give us some assurance with regard to
that particular aspect of future legislation.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I can only
say that the matter raised by the hon.
Senator will have the attention of the
Government in arriving at the dispassionate and large-minded decision on the case,
which we are advised to do by the hon.
and learned Member who asked the question. Obviously, I cannot possibly forecast, or even interpret, what was in the
mind of the Prime Minister when he
made the speech alluded to.
Court of Criminal Appeal.
Mr. CAMPBELL asked the Leader of
the House, whether representations have
been made by the Government to His
Majesty's Government at Westminster,
regarding the establishment of a Court of
Criminal Appeal in Northern Ireland,
and to what effect; and whether His
Majesty's Government have informed
the Government of any decision on their
part on the subject.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: It is a fact
that representations have been made by
the Government of Northern Ireland to
the Imperial Government, of the desirability and necessity of obtaining the necessary legislative sanction for the establishment of a Court of Criminal Appeal
in Northern Ireland.
The matter is the subject of consideration by the Imperial Government authorities at present, and while there is reason
to believe that the representations put
forward by the Government of Northern
Ireland are being favourably and sympathetically considered, it is not possible
to make any definite statement at present
on the matter.
Mr. LESLIE: Arising out of that,
may I ask the noble Viscount whether we
are to understand that the provisions of
the recent Imperial and Northern Ireland Miscellaneous Provisions Act are not
held to be sufficiently wide to allow us
to establish such a Court without further reference to Westminster?
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I will let
the hon. and learned Senator know at
a later date if he will kindly raise the
matter again when I have the information.

CLAUSE 1 (Amendment as to declaration of Noxious Weeds. 9 Edw. 7, c.
31).
Mr. LONG: I think it is very desirable
that the Minister of Agriculture should
accede to a wish that has been expressed
to me in agricultural circles that all these
weeds that have been scheduled should
not be regarded as noxious, at any rate
for the first year, so as to give an opportunity to the farmers to familiarise
themselves with the law. I am sure they
will comply with the law if they are
acquainted with it.
as
The MINISTER OF AGRICULTURE
(Sir Edward
Edward Archdale):
Archdale): The four
species of weeds mentioned in the Bill
are ones that we think ought to Le
scheduled. We are willing to leave out
the ox-eye daisy for the first year,
there might be a little difference of opinion
amongst the members of the Constabulary
as to what an ox-eye daisy really was.
Mr. McHUGH: Do they know the
other weeds?
Sir EDWARD ARCHDALE: I think
there is no doubt they will know the
other weeds.
Clause ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
CLAUSE 2 (Provisions as to administration of Acts) ordered to stand part
of the Bill.
CLAUSE 3 (Amendments as to penalties and destruction of noxious weeds).
(1) In subsection (2) of section two
of the principal Act, for the words
"not exceeding, for the first offence.
five pounds, and, for the second or any
subsequent offence, ten pounds" there
shall be substituted the words "of one
pound, or any greater sum not exceeding three pounds, for the first offence,
and of two pounds, or any greater sum
not exceeding five pounds, for the
second or any subsequent offence."

Colonel PAKENHAM: I beg to move,
to leave out sub-section (1) of Clause 3.
I would ask for the indulgence of the Committee if I take a few minutes to develop
the arguments in favour of this Amendment. If this Amendment is accepted the
fines under this Bill will be the same as
the fines under the old Act of 1909, and I
submit that they are quite efficient and
sufficient for the purposes in view. We
have under this new Bill an entirely new
inspectorate. Under the old Act we had
an inspectorate of fourteen temporary inspectors appointed by the Department of
Agriculture, and they were only students.
We have now got an inspectorate composed
of the Roval Ulster Constabulary who are
capable of discharging any duty placed on
their shoulders. They will be able to
inspect not 10 per cent., as in the past.
but 100 per cent. of the farms in Northern
Ireland. I submit, therefore, that if the
fines under this Bill are the same as the
fines in the Act of 1909 they will be quite
adequate, because you will have a more
efficient inspectorate and far fewer delinquents. I think that will be quite
apparent to hon. Members if they will
imagine the whole of the farms in Northern Ireland being inspected and the weeds
detected very early.
I will develop that, if I may, by saving
that I think the idea of a minimum fine is
an unsound one. It has this effect, that
the "good" man and the "bad" man are
both practically fined the same amount of
money, because if you adopt a minimum
fine you will find that most Courts will impose the minimum fine and no more. If
you leave the amount of the fine in the
hands of the people who will try these
cases, and who will probably be the
Committee.

109 Noxious Weeds Bill-
26 JUNE 1929
ordinary Petty Sessions magistrates, they
are all men who know a weed as well as
in
anybody else. They know the men
their own district who do uproot these
weeds, and they also know the men who are
sinners in this respect and the ones who
ought to be more heavily fined than the
others. If you retain the minimun fine
you will probably find that both class of
offenders will be fined the same amount.
I contend the Government would be
amply safeguarded if they left the fines
as in the old Act and left it to the discretion of the magistrates to inflict a
heavier fine on the man who is in the habit
of neglecting to cut and clear his weeds,
and a more lenient fine on the man who
through some oversight has not done as
much as he should. I am very glad to see
the Minister of Agriculture here, and I
hope he will be impressed with the few
remarks I have made on this Amendment.
Mr. MCALLISTER: I rise to support
the Amendment proposed by the hon. and
I think the principle of
gallant Senator.
the minimum fine is an exceedingly bad
one from the point of view of the fair and
In the
just administration of the law.
first place, to a great extent it pre-judges.
a man by imposing a fixed penalty upon
him before his case is heard, and in the
second place it takes out of the hands of
the tribunal that hears the case the opportunity of differentiating between the
degree of the offence committed. In the
next place we must assume, and we do
assume, as I think it is perfectly right
that we should, that the men who compose the ordinary magistrates courts in
Northern Ireland are selected because they
are men of probity and honour and of
capacity for administering justice. They
are necessarily selected from the best men
in the community, and surely it is a reflection on the body of very honourable
men who constitute these Courts to sav
that they cannot be trusted to apportion
a fine for an offence under this Bill.
think that is going very far, and that it is
a very bad principle to adopt.
I
I have never known a lawyer, no matter
what his politics might be, who did not
agree that for the purposes of the administration of the law this fixing of a minimum
penalty was a very bad and unsuitable
principle. I have several times referred
to this matter, and I have always made it
a practice to oppose the minimum fine. In
such a small matter as this I do not think
I can make the accusation, as I have done
in other cases, that it is done for the pur-
While I was
pose of raising revenue.
aware that this principle was prevalent in
legislation up to a short time ago, I was of
the opinion that in their more recent legislation the Government had given way and
had gone back to the old practice of giving
the magistrates full jurisdiction to inflict.
whatever penalty they thought right and
proper. I support the Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: I have often spoken in
this House on the subject of minimum
fines, and I have invariably maintained my
opposition to the minimum fine. On one
occasion I went so far as to inquire whether
we would follow the example of another
Second Chamber across the water and provide a Protest Book in order that one's
protest might be registered. I have never
yet found a case where I could support a
minimum fine, and I am afraid I must add
to that that there is very little in the way
of fresh argument left for me to bring
forward. I have drawn attention--and I
may, perhaps, in the presence of the
Minister of Agriculture, draw attention
again—to such examples of minimum fines
as are still to be found in the Imperial
Statute Book; a fine on a railway company
-I think it was £500-which halts its
train in order that passengers may alight
to attend a prize fight. That is still on
the Statute Book of the United Kingdom.
One
There are other similar cases.
person who may suffer under the minimum
fine is the butcher who happens to enter
a place of refreshment on Sunday. There
are other fines expressed in shillings imposed on people who use language they
should not use. That is the class of case
in which a minimum fine has been imposed in past years, but in England the
practice is practically obsolete. As Senator
McAllister remarked, no lawyer of any
description could ever be found to defend
the principle of the minimum fine, and one
wonders why it is that the Department so
ably presided over by the right hon.
Gentleman, the Minister of Agriculture, is
the Government Department which invariably brings forward proposals for minimum fines in its Departmental Bills. It
is very curious indeed.
Magistrates hate being required to impose a minimum fine, and I think they are
right. They say, and they say rightly.
that to impose a minimum fine argues a
complete misunderstanding of the whole
question of punitive punishment, which
is not to raise revenue but to prevent a re-
Noxious Weeds Bill-
Committee.
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
currence of the offence. The noble Viscount told us in moving the Second
Reading of this Bill that during some
period or other there had been a certain
number of fines of 6d, rising to 10s, but he
did not tell us in how many cases the
persons convicted had offended a second
time. That is the point. Is the fine, even
if it be only 6d, sufficient to prevent a recurrence of the offence? If it is, that is
all society ought to aim at. That is, if I
may venture to say so, the modern view of
all punitive fines, to stop the offence and
stop it as early as possible.
If a person is so foolish as to offend in
the matter of allowing noxious weeds to
grow-I do not hold a brief for the weed
-that person is subject to something more
than the actual fine. He has the bother
of attending the Petty Sessions Court, and
he has to pay certain costs, which will
probably run to so many shillings. He
may put up an advocate to defend him,
and if he does I understand that advocates
in Ireland receive certain emoluments, although I have no personal experience of
that in this country. Therefore, I really
think the Government would be well advised to yield to the appeals made, and to
concur in the deletion of this sub-clause in
the Bill before us.
Mr. LONG: I may say that I would
be inclined to stretch every point, even the
truth, if I believed it would be in the best
interests of the farming community, but
I really think the fines set out in the Bill
are proper and reasonable for the offences
specified. Hon. Members can have no conception of the amount of damage that can
be done by weeds, and especially those that
fly over large tracts of country. The injury that can be inflicted on their neighbours by those people who will not obey
the law in this respect is incalculable.
Therefore I very earnestly suggest to the
Committee that they should adhere to the
fines set out in the Bill. One smart fine of
say £1 will, as Senator Leslie has suggested, put a stop to the offence in a very
large area, whereas a small fine, which in
my district amounted to pence and not
pounds, was totally ineffective. It would
certainly be a very good thing if this
offence could be stamped out.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I rise to re-echo
what has been said by different Senators
with regard to the minimum fine. To my
mind, one great objection to it is the lack
of confidence it shows in the gentlemen
entrusted with the administration of justice in the different petty sessions districts
in Ulster. They can be trusted. They
are men of integrity and independence.
I think it should be left to the discretion of the magistrates to say in each particular case what the amount of the fine
should be. A minimum fine may, as one
Senator said, lead to injustice.
Sir EDWARD ARCHDALE: The object of the minimum fine is to prevent a
recurrence of the offence of leaving weeds
uncut. The hon. and gallant Senator
who moved the Amendment said we had
not any experience. But we have had
since 1909, and the fines that have been
inflicted-you could hardly call them fines
at all-average 6d, and they have never
been higher than 10s. With all due respect to the magistrates who decide these
cases it is rather hard for a man, when
his neighbour is up alongside of him, to
decide justly and fairly if that neighbour
is up for having weed seeds blowing all
over the country. We are bound to defend
the man who farms his land properly, and
has his farm tidy. If he does not get
support from the Ministry to see that his
neighbour is bound to cut his weeds, it
comes rather hard upon him.
If a man is fined 6d, he may just as
well let the weeds stay there. That is
what happens in nearly every case.
We
do not wish, as the hon. and gallant
Senator who moved the Amendment said,
to do any harm to anyone.
When the
constabulary have a case it is reported
to the Ministry, and no prosecution takes
place without the sanction of the Ministry
of Agriculture. They review the whole
case, and do not ever prosecute a man
who has not had several warnings. To
show what is being done across the water
-and there is a good deal of talk about
going step by step with England-I may
say that during two months in 1927.
which is
is the last Journal we have
from England, there were prosecutions
by the county agricultural committees of
East Sussex, Bucks, Leicester, Lancashire. Conviction was obtained in each
case, and fines of £20, £5, £15, £5, one
guinea. 10s. and £5, respectively, imposed, each defendant having to pay costs
in addition,
Mr. McHUGH: That is not step-bystep.
Sir EDWARD ARCHDALE: We do
not want to go as high——

much against the minimum fine. (Laughter). We find that it is utterly impossible to have this Measure carried out

Government Loans Bill.
and
Order for the House to be put into Committee read.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: Before moving that the House
do resolve itself into Committee upon
this Bill, I should like to explain one or
two points which arose in the debate on
the Second Reading. In the first place,
I am able to give the assurance asked for
by the Hon. Senator towards my
left that
it is not possible to issue loans from the
Government Loans Fund for any purpose
other than one for which statutory
authority has already been given. If it
is desired to extend the scope of the fund,
the Government will have to approach
Parliament for the necessary authority.
Ulster
In the second place, I should like to
point out that the Government Loans
Fund is managed SO as to be
entirely self-supporting; its finances
are obtained by the issue of
Loans Stock and the rates of interest on loans issued to local authorities,
and so forth, are fixed so that the income
from the Fund is sufficient to meet the
outgoings, such as dividends, etc. There
is, therefore, no charge on the Exchequer
involved, save a contingent charge in the
case of default by borrowers which, in
view of the nature of the security obtained, is not likely to occur to any appreciable extent. The operations of the
Fund are, however, subject to audit by
the Controller and Auditor-General, and
may be brought by him to the notice of
the Public Accounts Committee.
Repayments of the principal of loans.
are paid into the Fund, and are under
Section 6 (2) of the Government Loans
and Exchequer Provisions Act of 19 .
treated as a sinking fund for the redemption of Ulster Loans Stock. They may
be, from time to time, invested either in
Government Loans or in trustee securities. The Sinking Fund is available for
of
the redemption and cancellation
Ulster Loans Stock.
The total amount of loans issued up
to the present time is approximately
three-and-a-half millions out of a total
authorised
of
maximum
four-and-aquarter millions.
I should like to say that the amount
which is necessary in Northern Ireland
for the financing of local expenditure of
a capital nature from our Government
Loans Fund is comparatively large, owing
to the fact that local authorities
here, with, I think, the exception of the
Belfast Corporation, are not in a position.
to make their own stock issues, as is done
by large numbers of local authorities in
Great Britain.
I beg to move, "That the House do
now resolve itself into Committee on the
said Bill."
On Question, Motion agreed to.

Viscount CHARLEMONT: In moving

should like to inform hon. Members that

Thursday, 27th June, 1929.

NATIONALIST REPRESENTATION
ON COMMITTEES.
Mr. CAMPBELL (by Private Notice)
asked the Leader of the House whether
his attention had been directed to the
fact that the Nationalist Members of the
House have at present no representation
on the Committees of the House, and if
he can give them an assurance that adequate representation will be allotted to
them at the first available opportunity?
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): As the House is aware,
the appointments to the Committees to
which the hon. and learned Member refers were made at the beginning of this
Session before the Nationalist Members
came into the House. I shall be very
pleased to give the assurance asked for
by the hon. and learned Member, and to
say that proper representation will be
allotted to the Members representing the
Nationalist party at the first available
opportunity.

CLAUSE 1 (Licensing of street traders).
1.-(1) On and after the first day of
January, nineteen hundred and thirty.
it shall not be lawful for any person to
sell, or expose or offer for sale, any
article or thing from or upon any
vehicle, barrow, cart, stall, or other receptacle occupying a stationary position
at a place in the carriage-way or footway of any street in an urban district
or in a town not being an urban district
but having commissioners under the
Towns Improvement (Ireland) Act.
1854 (in this Act referred to as "the
district") without a licence from the
council or commissioners of the district
Street Trading Bill-

(in this Act referred to as "the local
authority") authorising such person so

Provided that this section shall not

from or upon any barrow, cart, stall, or
other receptacle which he moves from
place to place in the urban district or
town in pursuit of, and while conducting, his trade.

Sir WILLIAM COATES: I beg to
in Clause 1, Sub-clause (1),
after the first word " district," in line 11,
to insert ("other than a county borough.')
It is obvious from the framework of the
Bill, and especially the power to adopt the
Act given to county boroughs by Clause
11, that the Bill is not intended to affect
county boroughs until so adopted. The
Belfast Corporation, however, who do not
welcome the Bill in its present form, are
uneasy lest the use of the words "urban
district" in the first Clause of the Bill,
which include a county borough under the
Local Government Act of 1898, might be
held to bring in Belfast. The words
which I propose to insert are for the purpose of setting at rest any question there
may be on the matter, and I do not think
that the Government will find any difficulty in accepting them—at least, I hope
not.
The MINISTER OF HOME AFFAIRS
(Sir Dawson Bates): May I, first of
all, thank this honourable House for
the courtesy they have extended to me in
allowing me to come here this afternoon.
With regard to the Amendment that has
been moved by Senator Sir William
Coates, I am personally of the opinion
that it is not necessary, but at the same
time, to remove any doubt on the subject, the Government are prepared to
accept it. Of course, hon. Members will
appreciate that the Bill as it now stands
does not apply to the Corporation unless
the Corporation applies at some future
date to have the Bill made applicable
to them.
Amendment agreed to.
I beg to

to

Sir DAWSON BATES: Hon. Members will appreciate that this Bill applies
to what are known as stationary vehicles
or stalls and not to movable vehicles,
such as motor cars, or ice cream carts,
and vehicles of that sort. This Bill will
not affect an ice cream cart going about
from place to place and stopping only for
the purpose of delivering goods.

CLAUSE 2 (Application for licences,
etc.,).
(1) A person requiring a licence or
the renewal of a licence under this
Act shall make application in writing
to the local authority, and shall in
such application state his full name
and address and the nature of the
articles and things which he intends to
sell or expose or offer for sale under
the authority of the licence if granted,
the place (if any) at which the articles
or things will be stored by him before
any sale or exposure or offer for sale,
and the street or streets or area in
which he intends so to sell or expose
or offer for sale. In the case of any
person intending to sell or expose or
offer for sale as aforesaid any article
or thing on, or within one month

Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in
Clause 1, sub-clause 1, to leave out lines
16 and 17 [the place (if any) at which
the articles or things will be stored by
him before any sale or exposure or offer
for sale. I myself have not any particular objection to these two lines if the
right hon. Gentleman, the Minister of
Home Affairs, would be so kind as to
explain to us what they really mean.
As they stand it seems to me that their
effect is to first of all apply a rigour to
the licensee from which a non-licensee is
exempt, and to put on an applicant
for a licence a somewhat onerous condition which is quite impossible in many
cases, and which finally would apparently
be quite useless if it were complied with.
I cannot help thinking that these two
lines are connected more or less with the
question of preventing the contamination
of articles of food, and that, therefore,
these two lines have some connection
with paragraph (b) of sub-clause (3) to
which I am also drawing attention in the
following Amendment, and also, perhaps paragraph (d) of sub-clause (1) of
Clause 6 may come into it.
The place at which the articles are to be
stored seems to me to imply something
more than just putting the goods away for
a night and taking them out again in the
morning. But, at all events, a licensee
under the Bill will be a man who moves
from place to place, not within a district,
but within the Six Counties. That is to
say, he is going about from town to town,
and it seems to me that he will not, in the
majority of cases, be able to give what
may be described as a standing description
of where his things are going to be kept.
Therefore, we might think, that as a rule
he cannot give the information, but suppose he does, what is the local authority
going to do with the information? There
is that power I spoke of in Clause 6 to
make by-laws dealing with the storage.
and supervision at the place of sale, or intended sale, but the Bill seems to stop
there.
There is no power anywhere to inspect
anything except apparently such power as
might be applied under Clause 6 at the
place of sale. Therefore, on the whole,
as I say, these two lines do not seem to me
to serve any useful purpose, but if it can
be explained to us that they do, or if
other words of a more explanatory
character can be introduced, the whole
argument would either fall to the ground,
or would have to proceed on entirely
different lines. At the moment, I beg to
move that these two lines be omitted.
are

very wide powers given to local

question arises-
The CHAIRMAN: I am not quite

Mr. McHUGH: I understand that
this particular Clause gives every Member

The CHAIRMAN: Yes. But we are

Mr. MCALLISTER: I have more or less

and the Clause does not sufficiently define

round with them. I also think there
will be difficulty in confining the licence
to the very restricted area with regard to

the street or area to which these licences

Mr. DORMAN: I hope this Clause
will be left as it stands. It is imposing
upon the perambulating dealers in various

to give the licensing authority the opportunity of knowing where they store the
things which they offer for sale to the

-if the licensing authority had the right
of seeking an inspection of the places in

which that food stuff was stored, not only
inside the boundary of the City of Belfast,
but in many country towns.

in the shape of a licence he should, at all

and

Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move on page

as
The hon. Senator
The CHAIRMAN:
(Mr. McHugh) can speak now.
Mr. McHUGH:
When I was speaking on the former occasion I was very
anxious to know whether the power was
conveyed to the county council or to the
urban council only. If it is conveyed
to the county council then we have jurisdiction over the entire county, and this
particular Act which comes in force, to
my mind, apparently is going to strike
a severe blow at what we may call the
poorer class of people who have been
trading in the streets for generations.
It may be said that these people have
only to state exactly the kind of food, or
other stuff which they may have for disposal. If every thing is not stated in
detail when making the application for
a licence the public authorities can bring
these people to account for disposing of
articles for which they did not hold a
licence, or, in other words, which they
had not specified when applying for a
licence.
The second important matter is that
all these
these grievances arise principally
from complaints of shopkeepers who say
that these people come to their door and
undersell them, and that these people do
Street Trading Bill-
Committee.
THE SENATE
[Mr. McHugh]
not contribute to the upkeep or expenses
of the county, and that, therefore, they
have no right to enter these towns. But
surely it will not be denied that these
people reside in some place in Northern
Ireland. At least they do not live in
mid-air. They have got to pay rents,
and to pay taxes wherever they reside.
This city is accused of being the principal
offender, and sending the largest
quantity to the rural towns. If that is
so, it is not right to accuse the merchants
of not paying They pay, both large
rents, and any amount of taxes, more
than they are wishful to pay.
The other point made by these local
shopkeepers is that the articles they sell
are of an inferior quality, and, therefore,
they are not able to compete with them.
I have a little experience of a gentleman
selling harness. I bought a head collar,
which every gentieman knows about who
has anything to do with horses, from one
of the local shops for 7s 6d. A few days
afterwards one of these people had a
head collar for sale which he purchased
in this city. I bought it for
Strange to relate, the one I bought from
the shopkeeper stood no wear at all. The
one I bought from the hawker is still in
existence. It was a superior article for
less money.
5s.
The danger I really see in this is that
these shopkeepers are people of influence
in their own small way. They are, so to
speak, the governing body within their
Own area, and they have a terrible
amount of influence in the county
council. A large number of them are
members of the county council. When
the power is conveved to the county
council the result will be they will wipe
out the traders altogether. They will do
this in a way which, perhaps, the right
hon. Gentleman would scarcely believe.
They will call upon them to exhibit their
goods in a particular street, in a particular town. This street will not be the
main street, but a side street, so that
these people will not have an opportunity
of disposing of their goods because the
general traffic is in another part of the
town. If the question is raised that thev
are entitled to exhibit these goods and
sell them in the main street, it can be
side-tracked by stating that the place is
congested by these lorries if they are
planted in the main street. Therefore,
I see the danger that the ultimate result
of this will be to wipe out these poor
people who, in the first instance, derive
their livelihood in this way, and in the
second place, it will strike at the farming community which purchases largely
from these people in the towns, and also
at the people generally.
I think myself that this question of
licence should be transferred solely to
the Minister of Home Affairs, who would
be entirely independent, and who, I am
perfectly sure, would not allow himself
to be influenced by the shopkeepers in a
particular town. I will say that in his
favour anyhow. (Laughter). If he
would consider that matter I would be
strongly in favour of it. I may be told
that these people if they have a griev
ance, or are compelled to exhibit goods
in a site utterly inconvenient they have
the right of appeal to the court, and that
court would be presided over by the
resident magistrate, so that the local
magistrates who might have an interest
in the selling will not have any jurisdiction in that particular court. But look
at the inconvenience you put these people
to. Their case can never be put fully
before the court, except they have some of
these legal gentlemen appearing on their
behalf. Therefore, it becomes a costly
matter, as everybody knows now who
touches the legal profession, you do not
require to touch them at all except you
are a millionaire. (Laughter). That is
the reason why I think I would be in
favour of the granting of these licences
through the Ministry of Home Affairs.
Mr. McALLISTER: I think it is a bad
principle that this Committee should delegate to the Ministry of Home Affairs
the carrying out of this particular Clause
under the Bill. I must object strongly to
it being possible for the Ministry of Home
Affairs to make regulations far-reaching,
more restrictive and more difficult to
comply with than the terms of the Bill
itself. I suggest that the first suggestion
made by the hon. Senator (Mr. Leslie)
should be adopted. He suggested that
the Ministry prepare an Amendment and
submit it to the House on Report, and
that the House approve of the conditions
under which this Sub-clause is to be
rectified.
Sir DAWSON BATES: I am afraid my
hon. Friend (Mr. McHugh) is under a
delusion when he referred to the county
councils. The county councils have nothing whatever to do with the issuing of
Street Trading Bill-

27 JUNE 1929
Committee.
licences. That will be done by the urban
district council, or where a rural district
council apply to have the Act put into
operation in any particular area it will be
carried out by the rural district council.
I suggest that street traders who apply
for licences are amply protected, because
if the local authority do not give the
licence they can go to the resident magistrates who are quite independent of any
local traders.
My hon. Friend (Mr. McAllister), I am
sorry to say, has not the same high
opinion of the independence of the
Ministry of Home Affairs as the hon.
Senator (Mr. McHugh). I think he cannot have read the Bill very closely, because he will find that there is no reference to the Ministry of Home Affairs
except this, that the Ministry is to prescribe the form, and then the Clause goes
on to say
the licence must contain conditions, that is to say, it must specify the
streets, the classes of articles sold and so
on, but the Ministry have nothing to do
with whether the licence is granted or
not. That is a matter entirely for the
resident magistrates.
Then, my hon. Friend (Mr. McHugh)
said that street traders are all poor men.
I can tell him from my own experience
that they have a strong association to look
after their entire interests. I am certain
that if any injustice is done to any individual trader there will be ample fees
available to pay a solicitor and counsel to
before the resident magistrates. I am
go
sure hon. Senators will realise there must
be some sort of control. We do not intend to do away with street trading. We
all realise that it is absolutely necessary
that street trading in some form should
continue. We do not propose to do away
with it. A local authority will be unable
to refuse a licence. They must show good
grounds, otherwise their refusal will be
upset by the resident magistrates.
It is ridiculous that, while shopkeepers
are bound to comply with the ordinary
by-laws of the town in which they live,
these street traders are responsible to
absolutely nobody. Take the town of
Lisburn. These street traders do exactly
as they like there. Sometimes they are a
great danger to the travelling community,
and, in addition, they do not pay towards
the cleansing of the streets. They leave
all the refuse behind.
Mr. McHUGH: Do they not pay a
licence?
Sir DAWSON BATES: I only mention those things to get it into the head of
the hon. Senator, that we are not attempting to stifle street trading. The intention
is that it should be carried on in a reasonable form.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 3 (Fees for licences) ordered
to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 4 (Duration of licences).
Every licence granted or renewed
under this Act shall, unless revoked, be
valid for a period of one year or, in the
case of any licence granted or renewed otherwise than at any annual
meeting fixed by the local authority for
the purpose of considering applications
under this Act, for a period expiring on
the thirty-first day of December next
after the date of such grant or renewal:
Provided that every such licence
granted before the first day of January,
nineteen hundred and thirty, shall, unless revoked, be valid until the thirtyfirst day of December, nineteen hundred
and thirty, or the date of any annual
meeting held by the local authority in
the year nineteen hundred and thirty
for the purpose of considering applications under this Act, whichever of those
dates shall be earlier.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in page
4, line 6, leave out from "period" to end
of line 20 ("be the earlier''), and insert
"expiring on the thirty-first day of
December after such grant or renewal next
ensuing, or if any annual meeting shall
be appointed by the local authority to be
held for the purpose of considering applications under this Act in the year next
succeeding the said thirty-first day of
December, until the date of such annual
meeting so appointed.
Provided that every such licence granted
before the first day of January, nineteen
hundred and thirty shall, for the purposes.
of this section, be deemed to have been
granted on the said first day of January."
This is the point to which I drew attention on the Second Reading, that is the
failure of the text of the Bill to provide
for the expiration of licences simultaneously at some given date, and to arrive at
other desirable state of things as speedily

its
[Mr. Leslie]
as possible. I do not think I need take
up much time on the matter. I think it
will be agreed it is imperative that the
licensing authority should have
licences under its own control at some
given time, and it will be agreed also, I
think, that it is intended that the licensing authority should hold annual meetings.
for the purpose of considering these
matters. It is not required by the
Clause, but, I think, it is the intention.
I thought, perhaps, it was best for the
Committee to assume that the annual
licensing meeting is held on the 15th
January in each year. I do not mind the
date, but I take the 15th January. If a
licence is granted on the 1st December,
1929 the earliest date possible-it will
under the Bill expire on the 15th day of
January, 1930, Under my proposal it
would not expire until the 15th January,
1931. In that case the licensee will get
a little extra grace. Where a licence is
granted on the 31st January, 1930, it will
under the Bill expire on the 31st
December, 1930. My proposal would
keep it alive until 15th January, 1931.
Again, a licence granted on the 15th
January, 1931, would under the Bill and
under my proposal alike, expire on the
15th January, 1932. I suggest that my
proposal will prevent gaps and inflict no
injustice on anybody, and, I think, will
make for the general convenience.
Sir DAWSON RATES: The Government propose to accept this Amendment. In accepting it I would like to
express my indebtedness to the hon. and
learned Senator for the very able
way
in which he drafted what appears to us
very difficult Clause. I think his
Clause is very much better than the one
in the Bill.
a
Amendment agreed to.
Clause as amended ordered to stand
part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 5 (Appeals against refusal
or revocation of licences)
(1) If the local authority refuse to
grant or renew a licence, or revoke or
vary a licence, under this Act they
shall, if required by the applicant or
holder (as the case may be) deliver to
him within seven days of the receipt
of such requirement particulars in
writing of the grounds for such refusal, revocation or variation.
(2) Any person aggrieved by such
refusal, revocation or variation, or by
any condition made by the local
authority by means of a licence, may
within fourteen days after the date on
which the refusal, revocation, variation or condition is notified to him,
appeal against it to a court of summary jurisdiction constituted of a
resident magistrate, sitting alone cr
with other resident magistrates; and
the decision of that court shall be final.
and effect shall be given thereto by the
local authority.
(3) The following provisions shall
have effect with respect to any such
appeal:--
(a) Notice in writing of the appeal and of the grounds thereof shall
be given by the appellant to the
clerk of the local authority;
(b) The court may make such
order in the matter as it considers
reasonable, and may award costs to
be recoverable as costs in cases of
a civil nature are recoverable under
section twenty-two of the Petty
Sessions (Ireland) Act, 1851.
(4) In this section the expression
"resident magistrate" means a magistrate appointed under the Constabulary (Ireland) Act, 1836, as amended
by section six of the Constabulary Act
(Northern Ireland), 1922.
Mr. LESLIE:
I beg to move in page
4, line 32, leave out "against it" ("appeal against it'). I think the printer
has been enjoying himself here. The
words are unnecessary, and they should
be left out.
Sir DAWSON BATES: I do not

Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
