Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in page
4, line 32, leave out from "of" ("court
of summary") to "and" ("and the decision") in line 34, and insert petty
sessions having jurisdiction. in
district."
the
The point is to get the appeal jurisdiction into the hands of the ordinary
magistrates of the district instead of the
resident magistrate. We have so far as
I know only one example up to date of
resident magistrates as a court of appeal
from the local authority. That is an
innovation which was introduced not
very long ago in connection with the Registration of Nursing Homes Bill, and in
it it is provided that in the event of the
county council refusing a licence
appeal should lie to the resident magistrate. I suggested at that time that the
appeal should lie to the Ministry of
Home Affairs, but the right hon. Gentleman did not see his way to assume the responsibility which I sought to confer upon
him. There were reasons for that course
which do not apply to-day.
an
I
It seems to me that resident magistrates
were never appointed or intended for
work of this description. They were appointed under an old Act of 1836 to
serve in places where there was a shortage
of other magistrates. It was never intended that they should do anything of
this kind. There are, I think, ten resident magistrates in Northern Ireland.
have found, or it has been brought to my
notice, that in country districts there is
sometimes a little difficulty owing to the
fact that the resident magistrate is not
able to be in attendance. Again, a resident magistrate, although he has jurisdiction throughout Northern Ireland,
certainly, does not live in every one of
the urban districts, and I do not think he
is the best judge of the local conditions,
which in most cases would be the governing factor in deciding this question.
I think the local bench would be the
better tribunal. I know it has been
said, that in these cases requiring a resident magistrate, it relieves the ordinary
magistrates possibly of an unpleasant
duty. If magistrates accept the Commission they must carry out the duties,
and they must do unpleasant things.
I have often to do it myself. Only the
other day I was one of seven magistrates
who had to make an order for the
destruction of a dangerous dog. It was
not for not keeping him under proper
control. The whole seven were unanimous. We did not like it a bit, but we
did it. Again, I have sometimes as a
magistrate to use my voice in favour of
dismissing a case against a man brought
before the court where evidence for the
conviction of a guilty man is not
available.
I venture to suggest it would be better
to leave this in the hands of the local
bench. If for any
reason there is
general dissatisfaction with the ordinary
bench of magistrates as now constituted
the proper remedy is surely to alter the
law generally. Abolish these magistrates if you like. If we do not do that
I do protest against what I call picking
and chipping in order to set up by a side
line a new system which has never, perhaps, been definitely established by any
general Act of Parliament. 1 think it
would be in accordance with the general
wish if the right hon. Gentleman would
accept this Amendment.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg leave to
support the remarks made by the hon.
Senator who has just sat down. This
particular Clause seems to me to be a
step towards the supersession of the voluntary, unpaid justices from their ordinary
functions as magistrates sitting at petty
sessions courts. I do not know of any
particular reason why they cannot be entrusted with this particular jurisdiction,
to sit on an appeal, from a decision of
the local authority either to refuse to
grant or renew a licence, or to revoke or
vary a licence. The issues raised in such
an appeal are pure questions of fact, not
questions of law. I hope it will not
be suggested that the ordinary volunteer magistrates in Ulster, who have
rendered such valuable service to the
public for many years past, are not on
questions of fact quite competent judges
and fair, disinterested men.
There may be a tinge of suspicion that
in the past some carried their private
opinions too far in certain cases. I cannot conceive in a matter of this kind that
they could possibly carry any prejudice in
favour of the local shopkeepers, or in
favour of the street traders. It will be
noticed that a member of a local
authority who happens at the same time
to be a justice of the peace could not sit
on an appeal from a decision of the local
authority to the petty sessions court.
I think, on the whole, this Clause is
an indication of a want of confidence in
the magistrates. I quite agree with the
Street Trading Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
[Mr. Campbell]
hon. Senator (Mr. Leslie) who has just
spoken, that if they are prepared to take
away the powers of the magistrates let
them be taken away in one bold.
courageous, decisive step. I do not
approve of hacking and frittering away
their jurisdiction. What they have they
exercise fairly and properly, and I am
quite satisfied they will exercise it fairly
and properly in this respect also. 1 have
great pleasure in supporting the suggestion
to leave this to the local court, which can
bring to bear its knowledge of the district
and its sense of impartiality and justice.
This is a more effective way than leaving
it to a resident magistrate who may know
nothing of the people or of the district
generally.
Colonel PAKENHAM: I should like
to support my hon. Friend (Mr.
Leslie, in his Amendment to this Clause.
I do honestly think that the local justices
would be quite sufficient jurisdiction to be
appealed to in these cases. After all,
they are on the spot. There may be a
good many appeals to them from the local
authority. The resident magistrates, as
my hon. Friend has said, are only very
few in number in Northern Ireland, and
unless their number is largely increased
they will be set such hard work that they
will not be able to do half of it. I think
this might be left to the justices of the
peace. I have been a justice of the peace
for thirty years, and, I believe, this is a
matter which the local justices can deal
with very well, indeed.
Mr. MCALLISTER: I have expressed
my
views with regard to the constitution
of these courts, and the general aspect of
the question during the week, and I do
not intend to repeat those remarks
now. I merely wish to point out
that this is a Bill which is
particularly unsuitable for the proposed
constitution of the courts of summary
jurisdiction. In the first place the most.
convenient tribunal is, undoubtedly, the
ordinary petty sessions court. As has
been stated by the hon. Senator, who
moved the Amendment, it is a physical
impossibility for the resident magistrate
to be present at all the courts held at the
present time, and if the Government persist in this Clause, it is only going to hold
up the administration of the Act, and
the administration of law generally in
these places.
If we look at the duties of the magistrates it will be seen that the licence has
to prescribe the area, or place, or street
tor which it is to be granted. I submit
that the local magistrates have infinitely
better knowledge of the proper districts,
and areas, and places in respect of which
these licences should be issued than any
resident magistrate can possibly have. If
you have a resident magistrate sitting
there you are bound to have a much more
elaborate hearing, because you are bound
to bring in much more evidence in order
to enlighten him on the geography and
customs of the whole district for which the
licence is sought, whereas the local magistrates who constitute a very fair and impartial court have the local knowledge
and can naturally come to a much better
conclusion with infinitely less trouble, and
with a much shorter hearing than would
be necessary before a resident magistrate.
If this Clause is going to be persisted in
the petty sessions court will be turned into
something like the county court when an
ordinary title case is being heard. Maps.
will be produced for the enlightenment of
the resident magistrate, who will be
pointed out the various streets, districts.
and areas. Every appeal before a resident magistrate for a simple licence at 5s
a year is going to cost the man who applies
for it, as far as I can see, as much as a
high court record if this is persisted in.
Sir DAWSON RATES: I think hon.
Members do not fully realise that if these
cases were come to before the ordinary
magistrates it would be absolutely impossible that those local magistrates should
know nothing about the case beforehand.
It has been suggested by my hon. Friend
-I am rather surprised at it-that it is
an essential thing that the magistrate
should know something about the case before the case is heard.
Mr. McALLISTER: No, no.
Sir DAWSON BATES: In the case
of traders in the town I can hardly conceive that the local magistrates would not
be canvassed. I do not suggest they
would be influenced by the canvass, but I
can suggest that it would be a possibility
that many of the local magistrates to
avoid controversy with their neighbours
and to avoid being placed in an unpleasant
position may not go on the bench.
Senator Leslie said that the resident
magistrate originally was appointed as a
Committee
Street Trading Bill-
27 JUNE 1929
sort of supplemental magistrate to do the
work where there was no other magistrate.
I think my hon. Friend will agree with
me that times have entirely changed.
Resident magistrates are now, I may
point out to him, all men of legal knowledge. They must either be solicitors, or
barristers, of a certain number of years
standing. They must be trained lawyers.
As regards the ordinary justices, I
think my hon. Friend will also agree
that originally when justices of the peace
were appointed they had only to deal
with local assault cases. That was in
the good old days of the past, but with
these modern Acts of Parliament where
they have to weigh up the evidence put
forward from time to time I think it is
most desirable that you should have a
trained lawyer who will simply attach
himself to the evidence and not to any
hearsay.
Would anyone suggest that it would
be a good thing to hand over the administration of the Liquor Acts, or of the
game laws, or the fishery laws, or nursing
homes to the local justices? I think that
is all done much more efficiently by the
resident magistrates. I do not think it
is right or fair to ask the local magistrates to adjudicate in cases affecting
their neighbours if it can be possibly
avoided. At the same time, if hon.
Members are prepared to take the responsibility, and I say it is a responsibility, because I want this Clause for
the purpose of protecting the street
traders, the Government are prepared to
leave it to the vote of the House.

will be something like the old quarter

Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in page
5, line 32, at end ("any such by-laws") to
insert the following new paragraph:
(g) For prescribing (without prejudice to the law applicable to obstructions in the district) the length of time
any person to whom the proviso to
Street Trading Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
section one of this Act applies, may
keep any vehicle, barrow, cart, stall, or
other receptacle halted, and the distance
required to be traversed, after the
expiration of such length of time, before such vehicle, barrow, cart, stall or
other receptacle can again lawfully be
halted."
The object here is to give the local
authorities an opportunity of making a
by-law, if they think it necessary, to prevent what I may call an itinerant icecream vendor, who seems to be the staple
example, from defeating the whole object
of the Bill by simply anchoring himself
until such time as he is moved on. As the
Bill stands at present you have no remedy
against such a person except the remedy
provided by the ordinary law of obstruction which in our times is not often resorted to, because you can never get anybody to come forward and say he was
obstructed. Unless there is some complaint the police will not interfere.
I presume the intention of this Clause
is that the police shall enforce the provisions of the Bill, and also the town
constable, if there is one. The Bill does
not say that, but I suppose it follows.
Since this Amendment was put down on
the Paper an Amendment has been inserted on the first page of the Bill, but I
do not think it makes any difference. It
seems to me that it might be necessary
that the power to make this by-law should
be given to the local authority, and for
that reason I offer it to the Committee.
If it is not wanted, it is not a matter
that I will press.
Sir DAWSON BATES: It is not
necessary.
Mr. LESLIE: It is not necessary, but
I think I had better move it in case anybody desires to address the Committee
upon the matter.
Sir DAWSON BATES: Might I suggest to my hon. and learned Friend that
it is unnecessary and undesirable that this
Amendment should be inserted, having
regard to the Amendment that is to be
moved by the Noble Viscount (Viscount
Charlemont). If my hon. and learned
Friend goes into the matter further he
will find that it really gives the Ministry
power to make by-laws over-riding the
Act. The Bill expressly sets out in the
first Clause the limits within which bylaws can be made.
Mr. LESLIE: I only saw the Noble
Viscount's Amendment a few minutes
ago. I do not want to press the matter
at all.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.

CLAUSE 9 (Penalties and Legal Proceedings).
(1) Every person who, or whose
assistant, after the first day of January,
nineteen hundred and thirty, without a
licence, under this Act authorising him
so to do, or contrary to any condition
of such licence, sells or exposes or offers
for sale any article or thing from or
upon any vehicle, barrow, cart, stall or
other receptacle occupying a stationary
position at a place in the carriageway
or footway of any street in the district,
or obtains a licence or the renewal of
a licence by wilful misrepresentation,
shall be liable on summary conviction
to a penalty not exceeding five pounds,
and to a penalty not exceeding forty
shillings for each day on which such
offence is continued after conviction
therefor.

Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Clause
9, page 6, line 12, to leave out "or whose
assistant" ("every person who, or whose
assistant').
in
Clause 8 has just provided that a man
who holds a licence does not want a
separate licence for his assistant, and the
words here do not run counter to that, in
principle, at all events. But then
this goes on to say here, or rather
to imply, that if the law is broken
any way by the assistant the
licensee is to be responsible. The
way in which an assistant can break the
law seems to me that he can, let us say.
go ramping about on the footway, and if
he does that the employer is to be liable.
I think we might get that just as well
without those words. But then it goes
on to say that if a man obtains a licence
by misrepresentation he is also guilty of
an offence. Who is going to be liable
there? If the assistant does that is it the
Street Trading Bill-
27 JUNE 1929
Committee
intention to make the licensee or his
assistant liable? It struck me that on
the whole it would be better simply to
leave those words out, and then the Bill
would cover everything. Therefore, 1
suggest leaving out the words.
Sir DAWSON BATES: As my hon.
and learned Friend pointed out, no difficulty would arise where the principal did
not take out a licence, or where the assistant did not take out a licence. They
would be both equally guilty. But a
difficulty would arise where the principal
takes out a licence, and where his assistant commits a breach of the conditions
under which that licence is issued. My
hon. and learned Friend (Mr. Campbell)
opposite will agree that in criminal
matters it is very difficult to prove agency.
It is with the object of making the
principal responsible for his assistant that
we propose to retain these words. I hope
that with that explanation my hon.
Friend (Mr. Leslie) will withdraw the
Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: I do not press it. I
only wanted to draw attention to the
matter.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the
Bill.

Rights for purposes of Act).
or
(1) Where any rights, powers,

Provided that no resolution for the

with the like requirements as are applicable to meetings and resolutions of

the Ministry of Home Affairs, after

to the said Ministry by persons in

(8) The omission by the local authority to give the prescribed notice to any
person in whom any market rights are
vested, but the existence of whose

to such market rights. The rights of

under this section to receive such compensation shall not be prejudiced by
the omission of the local authority to

to whom the local authority omit to

authority, within twelve months after

of the market rights, a notice in writing of his claim containing such particulars as are referred to in sub-section
(5) of this section.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in

("are enjoyed by any person or persons
entitling him or them to hold") and to
insert "entitling him".
The first Amendment to this Clause is
rather a technical little matter. I am
sorry I did not have more time to look
Street Trading Bill-

THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
""
into it. The object, of course, is to get
rid of the words "or persons" at the head
of page 7, and to get back to the simple
The reason for
expression "any person."
that is that these market rights may very
possibly, it seems to me, be held, owned,
or acquired by a body corporate. If the
market rights are in the possession of a
body corporate and we use the simple expression "person," that expression will include an individual or a number of individuals, whether corporate or incorporate.
But if we use the composite expression
"person or persons" I am not quite sure
that the same would hold good. I confess that I have not had time to look for
any authority on the point, and have not
done so, but I do find some confirmation
for my view in a Bill, which is just now
before Parliament and copies of which are
possibly in the hands of hon. Members,
namely, the Local Government (Rating
and Finance) Bill. If hon. Members will
turn to the Schedule of that Bill at
page 23 in the first line they will see the
expression "the conveyance of merchandise
It is conby persons or companies."
venient to use the term "persons" there
and then the words "or companies" are
carefully put in. I am inclined to think,
therefore, that it would be advisable, on
the principle of safety first, to accept the
Amendment on the Paper. It does not
seem to involve any further amendment of
the Bill, which seems to have started with
the general expression on the first page,
but I do think there is some little risk
if we do not use the exact phrase here.
Sir DAWSON BATES: I do not think
we really run the risk my hon. and learned
Friend anticipates. This is really inserted
for the purpose of making clear the meaning of sub-Clause (2). You will find the
word "persons" in line 29. I have gone
into this matter very carefully and I am
advised that these words here do not
violate the Interpretation Act. Might I
suggest that the words used in the Bill
which my hon. and learned Friend (Mr.
Leslie) quotes are not the precise words in
the Interpretation Act?
Mr. LESLIE: Oh, no!
Sir DAWSON BATES: If one violates
that Act the other certainly does, but I
am advised that they do not. To accept
this Amendment would involve a certain
amount of ambiguity in the drafting of
sub-Clause (2), but if my hon. and
learned Friend finds on further research
that I am wrong I will be only too glad to
rectify the matter.
Mr. LESLIE: I think it had better

The CHAIRMAN: If the Committee

Sir DAWSON BATES: The object of
the Amendment which has been moved
by the noble Lord is to give to local
authorities who acquire market rights
under this Bill exactly the same powers
as they would have had under the Act
of 1878, Section 103, under which they
carry on the work. It is most desirable
that this Amendment should be made.
Mr. LESLIE: I rise for the purpose
of supporting the addition of this subsection. The effect of it will be to render unnecessary the greater part of the
new Clause which stands in my name further down on the Paper. In fact, I am
almost inclined to complain that the noble
Viscount has not only murdered my
Clause completely, but has put it to a

be dealt with under another Amendment
which is to be moved by him. The whole
thing, of course, is much better done by
reference to Section 103, and I would,
in fact, have moved such a reference had
this not been a Government Bill.
Amendment agreed to.

:-

THE SENATE
Clause 11, page 10, line 40, leave out

Mr. LESLIE: I am afraid that it is
absolutely necessary for us to get rid of
the word town, the difficulty being that I
do not know what a town is, nor, I think,
does anybody else, for this purpose.
The word is used, as we know, in certain
Acts with definite populations, but size is
a necessity, and this Clause is applied to
small places in our rural districts that
might perhaps be described as villages.
Therefore for this purpose only I am
asking leave to move an Amendment,
which will not be exactly the Amendment
on the Paper, because since I put this
Amendment on the Paper an Amendment of the Lord Mayor of Belfast has
been accepted, which makes a trivial.
difference.
The Amendment I desire to move is,
to leave out from the word "area" in
line 40 (means such area") to "may"
in line 41, and to insert "not being a
county borough and urban district or".
Sir DAWSON BATES: I have difficulty in really seeing the effect of this
Amendment. Of course the word "town"
is used there in the popular sense. As mv
hon. and learned Friend knows, a rural
district council can, if it so desires, apply
to the Ministry of Home Affairs to have
the Act put into force in such areas as
may be defined by the rural district council. My hon and learned Friend will
realise that, for instance, Bushmills is a
town in the popular sense, and that there
are towns in the popular sense that are
not towns within the meaning of the
Towns Improvement Act. I think this
Amendment would defeat the object we
all have in view, that the Bill should
apply to what is popularly known as a
town.
Mr. LESLIE: What about Dervock?
I think this had better stand over to the
Report stage.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.

Viscount CHARLEMONT:

Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in Clause
12, page 11, line 24, to leave out, from
"enactments" to "shall" in line 26.
I am asking leave to leave out this
special reference to Section 28 of the Local
149 Street Trading Bill-

27 JUNE 1929
Committee.
Government Act of 1871, because I think
it will puzzle any Member of this Committee to tell what Section 28 of the Act
of 1871 says. The difficulty arises in this
way. Section 28 of the Act of 1871 will
not, be found in the issue of the Revised
Statutes, It was, as a matter of fact, repealed by Section 294 of the Public
Health Act. The Section has had a very
chequered career. We have not in the
Library upstairs-it is a terrible confession-a complete set of the Statutes, and
owing to that fact I am unable to find
a copy of Section 28. However, what I
It repealed.
understand it did is thsi:
Section 69 of the Towns Improvement
Act, which, I believe, had something to
do with accounts. Again, I can only say
what I believe, because, of course, that
Section does not appear in the Revised
Statutes, and the Statutes at large are
not available. It was found to be inconvenient to let the Section remain repealed.
and, therefore, in the year 1872, by a
general Section of the Local Government
Act of that year the Sections dealing with
accounts in the Towns Improvement Act
were revived, and, therefore, they are
still law. I do not think we ought to insert in any of our Bills any legislation
by reference which causes so much unnecessary trouble. I do not say there is
anything wrong in putting these words in.
but the enactment is now, owing to the
curious things that have happened, still
law, and I do think we ought to refer to
it in such a way as to enable anyone to
find out what the Section really is. I
beg to move that those words be left out.
Sir DAWSON BATES: If my hon.
and learned Friend will turn to Vanston's
Municipal Towns he will find the whole
subject dealt with there, and also the
words of the Section. I think he will
agree with me that, that is a standard
volume, to be found in almost every
solicitor's office. I believe it is to be
found in this building.
Mr. LESLIE: I appreciate what the
right hon. Gentleman says about this
building, but you cannot expect an ordinary person, who may have to refer to this
Act, to have an expensive book like Vanston's Municipal Towns at hand. I
would move this Amendment as a definite
protest against inserting in an Act or Parliament words which an ordinary man
cannot possibly understand. I hope the
right, hon. Gentleman will reconsider this
question.
Sir DAWSON BATES: If my hon.
and learned Friend will make a practical
suggestion as to how to get over the difficulty I should be very glad. I think the
Amendment will defeat the object he has
in view. If he can frame another Amendment that really meets the case I shall be
only too glad to consider it. I do not
want, to give anybody the additional worry
of referring to other Acts of Parliament.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I also wish to say
that I do not like legislation by reference.
The layman does not understand it; very
often the lawyer does not understand it
either, and it is a matter to which the
public may seriously object.
Mr. LESLIE: I will withdraw the
Amendment, and put it down again for
the Report Stage.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause 12, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.

14 (Saving for Sale of farm produce).

danger of that.

(2) Where any part of any premises
for the time being distinguished in the
valuation lists as an agricultural or industrial hereditament is let out to a
tenant, that part shall, notwithstanding that it is not separately valued for
rating purposes, be deemed for the purposes of this section to be a separate
hereditament, and the Commissioner of
Valuation shall, on the application
either of the landlord or of the tenant.
issue a certificate showing the part, of
the total valuation attributable to the
said part of the said premises, and the
certificate shall be final and conclusive.
(3) Any difference which may arise
as to the amount to be paid or allowed
to a tenant by way of deduction from
his rent under this section shall be referred to the Commissioner of Valuation, whose decision shall be final and
conclusive.
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Campbell:-
Clause 6, Sub-clause (2), page 7, lines
27 and 28, leave out "shall be final and
conclusive' and insert "shall be subject to
appeal to the Civil Bill Court, of County
or County Borough in which the premises
shall be situate."
Mr. CAMPBELL: This Clause, to my
mind, is rather a remarkable piece of
legislation. It deals with the case of
inclusive rents, and provides for conditions where the landlord has undertaken
to pay rates in respect of his tenant's
premises, and the tenant's rent includes
a sum in consideration of the landlord's
payment of rates. The Section provides
for the deduction from the inclusive rent
to be made by the landlord in respect
of rate exemption and rate relief. Subclause (2) provides that where any part
of any premises for the time being distinguished in the valuation lists as an
agricultural or industrial hereditament
is let out to a tenant, that part shall.
notwithstanding that it is not separately
valued for rating purposes, be deemed
to be a separate hereditament, and it
gives the Commissioner of Valuation
power, on the application either of the
landlord or the tenant, to issue certicate showing the part or the total valuation attributable to the said part of the
said premises, and says that such certificate shall be final and conclusive.
My Amendment purports to give the
right of appeal from the decision of the
Commissioner of Valuation to the Civil
Bill Court of the county or county
borough in which the premises are
situate, but by leave of the Committee I
do not propose to press that particular
Amendment. I therefore ask leave to
withdraw that Amendment, and I will
confine myself to the Amendment on
sub-section (3).
The CHAIRMAN: Is it the hon.
Member's intention not to move the first
Amendment on page 7, lines 27 and 28
1:3

Local Government Bill---
27 JUNE 1929
Committee.
Mr. CAMPBELL: My first Amendment is in lines 27 and 28. By leave of
the Committee I will withdraw that.
Amendment not moved.
The following Amendment stood on
the Order Paper in the name of Mr.
Campbell:-
Clause 6, sub-clause (3), line 33, leave
out "shall be final and conclusive" and
insert "shall be subject to appeal to the
Civil Bill Court of the County or County
Borough in which the premises shall be
situate."
Mr. CAMPBELL: Sub-Clause (2)
deals with the question of rating and
valuation, and that is a matter purely
within the province of the Commissioner
of Valuation. It is in his department,
and in almost all Acts of Parliament
dealing with the
the valuation of premises his certificate is taken as final
and conclusive. Under sub-Clause (3)
we come to a totally different matter,
the question of rent, and the segregation of rent as between landlord
and tenant. The power which is now
proposed to be placed absolutely in
the hands of the Commissioner of Valuation is a new power conferred upon him.
Hitherto any question which might arise
between a landlord and tenant as to rent
has been dealt with in a Court of Justice. I am not aware of any case in
which an official, however distinguished
he might be in his own department- and
the Commissioner of Valuation is a very
capable and distinguished official-is the
final arbiter in any difference that may
arise on the question of rent.
I think it is only fair to both parties,
the landlord on one side and the tenant
on the other, that in a matter of this
kind, the old and historic right of resort
to the Courts of Justice, and having the
Commissioner's decision reviewed there,
should still be preserved to the parties.
involved. The tribunal I propose is the
Civil Bill Court. No one can question
the impartiality of that Court. or
competency of that Court to deal with the
matter, and, in addition, it is the most
convenient and expeditious tribunal.
Very often it is referred to as the poor
man's Court. The proposal to make an
official the final arbiter on a question of
rent is, I submit, not only an innovation,
but it may be very unsatisfactory to both
the
parties and to the general public. The
Commissioner of Valuation has not the
machinery for dealing with a question of
rent such as the Court has. He has not
the power to bring witnesses to the Court
and to examine records, and he has not
the power to compel persons to give evidence. Those are matters that safeguard
the rights of both landlord and tenant.
and I do respectfully bring this matter
forward, not in any controversial spirit,
but in the interests of the improvement
of this Bill.
I do not altogether agree with the
proposed substitution of departments for
the Courts. The trend of legislation
latterly has been to refer matters such as
these to the jurisdiction of departments
and to take them away from the jurisdiction of the Courts. It is a growing evil,
and I think it ought not to be extended in
this case. I do not think that either
landlord or tenant will be satisfied that
the final decision should be in the hands
of the Commissioner of Valuation. It is
in both their interests that the question
of rent should be decided in the light of
the Court, especially such a convenient
and expeditious Court as the Civil Bill
Court. I beg to move the Amendment
standing in my name.
simple
Sir DAWSON BATES: I think the
hon. and learned Member is rather inclined to over-state the effect of this sub-
Clause. What we are asking the Commissioner of Valuation to do is to make
a purely mechanical calculation. I am
sure the hon. and learned Member will
remember that under the old Local Government Act of 1898, landlords, and in
some cases tenants, were relieved of half
the poor rate, and identically the same
Clause was inserted in that Act in order
that there should be some
machinery to enable both the landlord
and the tenant to get a ruling as to the
exact amount of poor rate the landlord
might have to pay back to the tenant.
After all, the Commissioner of Valuation
is the person who divides up the holding.
Of course, my hon. and learned Friend
will appreciate that this is only a temporary matter, because if a tenant or a
landlord is dissatisfied with the decision
of the Commissioner of Valuation, at a
later stage he can take the matter to the
County Court Judge. In the following
year he can apply to have a new valuation made.
Local Government Bill-
Committee.
THE SENATE
Sir Dawson Bates]
It is perfectly true that during the
temporary period of seven or eight
months there will be no further appeal
from the decision of the Commissioner
of Valuation. He will fix the amount of
the poor rate-not the amount of the
rent-that will have to be paid back to
the tenant, but at a later date, if the
landlord or the tenant is dissatisfied with
the decision of the Commissioner of
Valuation, he has the right of appeal
to the County Court Judge. The whole
object of this Bill is to prevent unnecessary litigation. My hon. and learned
Friend, like myself, belongs to a profession which seeks to make money by litigation, but in a case like this I think it
is most undesirable that unnecessary
litigation should be encouraged over a
matter that involves only a few shillings.
Therefore, I hope the hon. and learned
Member will not press his Amendment.
It
There
Mr. McALLISTER: I do not think
that a matter of mechanical calculation
alone is involved in this Amendment, as
the right hon. Gentleman has said.
is a question of the adjustment and apportionment of rent and rates.
may be very deep and involved legal
questions to be determined, and is it right
and proper that a gentleman, however
eminent he may be in the discharge of
his own official duties, but who is without any legal qualification, should be
called upon to be the final arbiter in a
question which may involve very difficult
legal problems? Further, this is a question that is not within the ambit of the
Commissioner of Valuation. It is a question of the adjustment, not of rates,
but of rent, and it would be a mistake
to create a precedent by foisting on this
gentleman duties which his qualifications
do not fit him to discharge. I am sure
the Commissioner of Valuation himself,
if he found himself in a difficulty in interpreting a question of law, would feel
on much safer and surer grounds if he
knew that some properly qualified legal
authority would review his decision in
case it was wrong. I should think that
would be the feeling of the Commissioner
of Valuation himself. Therefore, I
strongly support the Amendment.
Sir DAWSON BATES: Just to correct my hon. Friend, if he will look at
Sub-section (2) he will see that it says
that the Commissioner of Valuation
shall, on the application of the landlord
or the tenant, issue a certificate showing
the part of the total valuation attributable to the said part of the said premises. That is a purely mechanical duty
on the part of the Commissioner. Subclause (3) says that any difference which
may arise as to the amount to be paid
or allowed to a tenant by way of reduction from his rent shall be referred to the
Commissioner of Valuation. That is a
purely mechanical calculation, because
once the valuation is fixed then it is only
a matter of paying over the amount.
Mr. CAMPBELL: If I were not dissatisfied with this interpretation of sub-
Clause (3) I would not press the matter for a moment. My submission to the
House is that sub-Clause (3), and sub-
Clause (2), deal with totally distinct and
separate matters. Sub-Clause (2) deals
with the question of rating where it may
be necessary to have premises broken up
for the purposes of the Act, and it gives
the Commissioner power to strike a valuation for each part of the premises. I
had an Amendment on that point and
withdrew it on the ground that that was
within the ambit of the Commissioner
dealing with the question of valuation.
But sub-Clause (3) deals with a separate
subject. It is not connected with Clause
2. it is connected with Clause 1. What
it says is: "Any difference which may
arise as to the amount to be paid or allowed to a tenant by way of deduction
from his rent under this sectioncannot be under sub-Clause (2). There
is no question of rent in sub-Clause (2).
but it arises from sub-Clause (1). Sub-
Clause (3) is to be read in conjunction
with sub-Clause (1). Then in that case
any difference shall be referred to the
Commissioner of Valuation. He is not
bound by any mechanical system; he is
left at large. He is to fix whatever
figure he pleases. His decision is final.
I submit that it is not as it should be.
I think no harm would be done and much
good might result if in any case-they
may not be many and may arise seldom
-if he is in the wrong you have there
a cheap remedy in the county court.
It
Sir DAWSON BATES: Perhaps I can
meet the hon. Member if I assure him it
is not the intention of the Commissioner
to deal with any legal interpretation. I
am quite prepared to accept a clause in
which there will be an appeal to the
county court judge on a question of law.
Perhaps my hon. Friend will accept that.

That is,

There is the

valuation of the piece of land and the part

CLAUSE 16 (Power to reduce deficiency contribution).
(1) Where the Ministry of Home
Affairs is satisfied that a local authority
is not maintaining a reasonable standard
of efficiency in the discharge of any of
its functions, the amount of any deficiency contribution payable to or on
behalf of the said authority under any
head shall be reduced by such amount
as the said Ministry thinks proper.
Where a deficiency contribution is
reduced under the powers conferred by
this section, the Ministry of Home
Affairs shall lay before Parliament a
report stating the amount of, and the
reasons for making, the reduction.
(2) In the application of this section.
to an education authority the Ministry
of Education shall be substituted for
the Ministry of Home Affairs.
Sir WILLIAM COATES: I beg to
move that the following sub-clause be
added to Clause 16 of the Bill:-
"(3) This section shall not apply to
the council of a county borough,
borough or urban district, the commissioners of a town not being an urban
district, or the water commissioners."
a
This Amendment is one as to which I
do not think there ought to be any opposition if Members will consider for
moment the object of the Clause, which
was to prevent Local Authorities making
an undue use of the deficiency contributions provided by the Bill. It will be
observed, however, that a fundamental
difference exists between the deficiency
contribution as applied to county councils
and their dependent local authorities on
the one hand and county boroughs,
boroughs or urban districts, commissioners
of towns not being urban districts and the
water commissioners on the other hand.
In the case of what I may call for the
sake of brevity the country authorities,
the deficiency contribution consists of an
annual "standard grant" based upon the
normal annual net expenditure of the
Authority during a past period of years,
and an annual "supplementary grant"
payable in the case of increased expenditure.
I assume that the object of Clause 16,
to which I am moving the Amendment,
was to meet a case, for example, of a
local authority entitled to a standard
grant which might be tempted to reduce
its expenditure to a point where the
standard grant would cover, or nearly
cover, the total expenditure, and thus to
place itself in a position in which it would
be unnecessary to strike any rate, or, at
all events, a rate of only a very reduced
amount.
Local Government Bill.
Local Bills.
THE SENATE
[Sir William Coates]
In the case which I have assumed the
benefit of the Bill would be enjoyed by
non-derated premises as well as by derated
premises, and the object of the Bill might
to some extent be defeated. I quite appreciate, therefore, the necessity for the
clause in connection with the country
authorities, but in the case of what I
might call the town authorities, including
the water commissioners, a different situation entirely arises. In their case by
Clause 12 the deficiency contribution is
to be a sum equal to the deficiency on
account of rates leviable in respect of each
financial year. There is no standard
grant applicable to these authorities which
might be abused in the way suggested in
the hypothetical case which I have just
put, and therefore as far as the town
authorities are concerned, there is no
necessity for the clause.
To sum it up in a sentence, the Bill
leaves town authorities in exactly the same
position as before, with the exception
that for portion of their rates they look to
Government funds instead of to the rated
occupiers, and therefore there is no reason
why their position or responsibility in connection with spending their own funds
should be altered. If it is desired that
any further safeguards should be devised
in connection with the expenditure of
local authorities, in addition to those
which at present exist, I respectfully
suggest that the proper time to consider
the matter is when a scheme dealing with
changes in the system of local authorities.
is being considered by the House.
I hope I have given sufficient reason
to the House to show the desirability of
the Amendment which stands in my name
being passed. I hope it will be accepted.
In any case I beg to move.
Sir DAWSON BATES: We propose
to accept the Amendment which has been
moved by the hon. Senator.
Sir WILLIAM COATES: I beg to
thank the right hon. Gentleman for his
kindness.
Amendment agreed to.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.

Adjournment.
CHARLEMONT:
Before
moving the adjournment of the House, I
should like to deal with a matter raised
by the hon. Senator Leslie in a supplemental question yesterday, on a court of
criminal appeal. The Government is advised that powers beyond those provided
by Section 1, of the Northern Ireland
Miscellaneous Provisions Act, 1928, will
be required to enable us to establish a
court of criminal appeal in Northern
Ireland. I beg to move, "That this
House do now adjourn."
Mr. LESLIE: I should like to thank
the noble Viscount for looking into that
matter. I think it is generally known
that the provisions of Section 1, of the
Miscellaneous Provisions referred to are,
perhaps, a little ambiguous on this particular matter. It is, therefore, very desirable to have the opinion of the Government on the subject.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at a
Quarter after Five o'clock
until Monday next.
163 The late Senator McDowell

THE SENATE
Local Bills
THE SENATE.

Monday, 1st July, 1929.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
THE LATE SENATOR W. J.
MCDOWELL.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): Since this House last met we
have had the great misfortune to lose one
of our oldest Members in the person of
In a
the late Senator W. J. McDowell.
small community such as this I think the
loss of a man with such a sense of public
duty as was possesssed by the late Senator
is one that we can ill afford. He was a
Member of the Senate, I think, from the
time of its inception, and he attended
with the utmost regularity, even when it
was apparent to many of us here that his
health was in a very serious state, which
at the last
was especially noticeable
sittings of this House. I think it would
be unbecoming of me as Leader of this
House if I passed over an occasion like
this without expressing the regret felt by
every Member of this House at the untimely death of Senator McDowell, and
our condolences with the members of his
family.
more.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I wish to associate
my Nationalist colleagues and myself
with the fitting words of sorrow and
sympathy uttered by the noble Lord, the
Leader of the House. The hon.
Member of the House, whose death we
deplore, was with us so recently as last
Thursday, when he was keenly alive to
our proceedings. He will be with us no
His day's work is over. The time
is drawing near when the day's work of
most of us will be over. I had not the
pleasure of the acquaintance of the late
Senator, but from all I have heard of him
he was as straightforward in public life.
as he was in private life. Once again the
old message comes to us-"In the midst
of life we are in death." Once again
Edmund Burke's words, uttered under
not dissimilar circumstances, ring in our
ear: --"What shadows we are, and what
shadows we pursue!"
Mr. JOSEPH
CUNNINGHAM: I
would like to associate myself with the
very kind and sympathetic reference made
by the noble Leader of the House to the
death of our
Senator
late friend,
McDowell. It is rather a peculiar coincidence that of the three Labour Unionist
representatives who originally came into
this House two have passed away, first
Mr. Joseph Woods, and now Mr. William
J. McDowell. I knew Mr. McDowell
very well for a considerable number of
years. As a trade union official he was
a very faithful and honest representative
of the workers, and as a Member of this
House, although he did not make many
speeches, I know he took a great interest
in the business that was transacted here.
He was a very conscientious, kindly man,
and he had a very sympathetic feeling
towards his fellow-workers. It is very
sad to contemplate that he has now passed
away and that this House will never again
have the benefit of his services. We will
remember him with feelings of sympathy
and affection, and we extend our deep
condolences to his widow and family.
Mr. DORMAN: I desire to join in the
expressions of regret at the decease of
Senator McDowell. Although I differed
from him in political outlook I have spent
many hours in exchanging opinions with
him, and I join whole-heartedly in the
expressions of sympathy with his relatives.

Industrial Assurance and Friendly
Societies Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: This Bill
deals with two matters, both of which are
of urgent importance. to Industrial
Assurance Companies and Friendly
Societies. Briefly, it is proposed to
legalise what has for some time past been
the commonly accepted practice, but by
a recent decision of the Courts has now
been found to be illegal on a strict interpretation of the existing law.
surers
In the first place it is proposed to
legalise the issue of endowment policies
on the lives of certain relatives of inand
by Industrial Assurance
Friendly Societies. Under existing legislation it is possible to issue policies on the
lives of relatives under which amounts to
cover funeral expenses become payable on
the death of the person concerned, but
there is no legal sanction to the issue of
endowment policies.
There can be no objection in principle
to such policies, and we propose, therefore, to follow the example of the
Imperial Parliament and to amend the
law so that the position shall be what
everybody until recently believed it to be.
It will be noted that a proviso is made
whereby, should the life assured die before the date upon which the endowment
is payable, the amount receivable under
the policy is restricted to a reasonable
amount for funeral expenses.
The second point is of a similar nature.
Under the existing law the amount which
can be paid under an assurance policy on
the life of a child must not exceed certain
specified sums at various ages, but it has
been the practice for societies to issue
policies which provide for the return of
the premiums paid in the event of the
insured child dying before reaching the
endowment age. Legislation recently
undertaken in Great Britain provides
that in the calculation of any sum payable for funeral expenses and especially
of the sum payable on the death of a
child, the amount of any premiums refunded on an endowment policy may be
excluded, and in Clause 2 we propose
parallel legislation for Northern Ireland.
Clause 3 of the Bill gives an endowment policy holder the right to claim the
surrender of his policy aiter payment of
at least one year's premiums. It will be
remembered that under the 1924 Act in
Northern Ireland the right to such surrender value accrued after payment of
ve years' premiums, or in some cases
three years' premiums, but it has in fact
been the practice of the better companies
to allow surrender values after payment
of one year's premiums.
I trust that the provisions of the Bill
will commend themselves to all members
of the House. Having regard to the face
that a large proportion of the Industrial
Assurance business of Northern Ireland
s carried on by Societies which also
operate in Great Britain, there is, of
course, great advantage in having the
law on this matter in Northern Ireland
similar to the law in Great Britain. I
beg to move.
Mr. DORMAN: 1 rise to welcome the
introduction of this Measure, as I believe
it is one that is absolutely necessary in
the interests of working-class persons,
thousands of whom have taken
out policies that prevent them obtaining that which they expected to
receive. If the history of this whole
system
of industrial assurance was
written it would be a revelation to the
people. The institution of industrial
assurance originated in the "hungry
forties" of the last century, and was
necessary in order to enable the people to
meet the ordinary contingencies of human
life, inasmuch as when a death took
place in a working-class family they had
to appeal to the guardians for assistance
Street Trading Bill

Industrial Assurance Bill-
THE SENATE
[Mr. Dorman]
to inter the deceased. Some wise individuals induced the working classes to pool
their resources in the form of premiums
to meet those contingencies, and if the
history of these particular institutions
were written, the amount of profiteering
and exploitation of the industrial classes
that would be revealed would be amazing. Suffice it to say, that many of these
institutions that were founded with
practically no capital are now drawing
large interest, and, in addition to that
interest, large bonuses from year to year.
It was from the profits accruing from
the lapsing of polices attached to which
there was no surrender value and no right
to demand free policies that vast sums
of these insurance companies accumulated
to such an extent that many of them
now control the life and well-being of the
working classes from day to day. It will
surprise the House when I mention that,
from my personal knowledge, I know of
an instance of one of these institutions
being reconstructed and the directors paid
£54 in cash for one share. They knew
that on reconstruction they were going to
place themselves in the position of adding to the capital, and receiving three
or four times the amount of dividend
they previously received. These accumulations originated because there was no
law on the Statute Book similar to this
Bill for the purpose of protecting these
helpless members of the working class
against that outrageous exploitation that
took place on the lapsing of their policies.
At the present time, if a working
man desires to start his child in business
or to complete his education in a secondary school, the only means by which he
can do so is to take out what is rightly
termed in this Bill, an endowment insurance policy. The law at present confines
his payment to a penny a week. With this
penny a week on a life policy the full
liability can be incurred. I understand
by an interpretation of the present law
that if a working man insures his child
with the prospect of getting, say, £45 at
the end of fifteen years, and the child
died in the meantime, the unfortunate working man would be deprived
of any right to a refund of the premiums
paid. I congratulate the Government
in introducing this Measure, as I believe
it will protect hundreds of thousands of
the working classes from being deprived of
receiving for their children what they
entered into a contract to obtain, and
which the present law deprives them of
the right to obtain.
Mr. LESLIE: There is just one point
to be considered in connection with the
Bill lest any misapprehension should inadvertently arise, and it is this. There
is nothing in the Bill which in any way
militates against modern practice and
modern intention as regards infant life
protection. That is entirely protected as
before by the proviso to the first subclause in the first Clause of the Bill.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Appropriation Bill.

Money Bill, and its provisions are set out
in a very detailed manner in the text of
the Bill. I do not, therefore, propose to
detain the House with
any lengthy
explanation.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved.

(1) A person requiring a licence or
the renewal of a licence under this Act
shall make application in writing to the
local authority, and shall in such application state his full name and address
and the nature of the articles and

he intends so to sell or expose or

Mr. LESLIE : I beg to move, in
Clause 2, page 2, leave out lines 16 and
17 and insert "give such information as
may be practicable as to the places where
articles or
he generally stores such
things.'

This Amendment was designed to endeavour, if possible, to do something to
make the Clause of the Bill to which it
relates a little more explicit. It will be
remembered that during the Committee
Stage the right hon. Gentleman, the Minister of Home Affairs (Sir Dawson Bates),
said that he would have liked to make
the words a little stronger, and he found
it was very difficult to find suitable words
to meet the object which the Government
had in view. He gave the Committee
some information as to that object which,
I admit, made things a little clearer,
but it seems to me that we are still
in the same difficulty, and that we
might try by the use of the words I have
on the Paper to make the intention a
little more clear, and make the practice
a little more possible. Accordingly, I
am suggesting words at the beginning of
the sub-clause, which would make it
practicable for a man to give full information, instead of leaving it as it would,
I think, be at present-quite impracticable to give any information whatever.
The whole difficulty seems to me to depend on whether the particular trader, who
must necessarily work from a base of some
sort, whether, he is going to return to
his base day after day, or whether he is
I ungoing to make an extended tour.
derstand that it is thought more likely
that the great majority of such traders
will return day after day to their base
On the
where they keep the things they want to
sell. In that case they would not be
unable to give the information.
other hand, there remain cases of men
who pass here, there, and everywhere to
sell, and they cannot give the information. There will be opportunity in applications for licences for the local
authority to make an inquiry and that
might, perhaps, give the information
which would be of use to them in showing
that the articles of food were properly
stored, which is the object aimed at.
Having looked into the matter, I cannot
say that I attach much importance to the
Amendment, which I beg to move.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: My right
hon. Friend the Minister of Home Affairs
is of the opinion that the wording as it
stands is better than the Amendment
moved by the hon. and learned Senator.
It is not considered that an applicant will
have any difficulty in complying with the
words in the Clause, namely, in stating
the place, if any, at which the articles or
things will be stored by him before any
sale or exposure or offer for sale, and it
is desirable that the local authority should
have this information, particularly in the
case of such articles as second-hand clothing and food.
Mr. LESLIE: I do not press the
matter.
Amendment by leave withdrawn.

Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in page
10, leave out from "area" ("means such
area') in line 39 to "a" ("nor a town")
in line 40, and insert "not being a county.
borough, an urban district or').
This, again is the somewhat difficult
question of what is a town. It seemed
to me in Committee Stage that it was
necessary to make an alteration in subclause 4 of Clause 11, towards the bottom
of page 10 owing to the introduction of
the new expression, "county borough,"
which appeared in the original Bill.
There is also the difficulty of the expression of the word "town," That is a
difficulty which I myself cannot get over.
Town, as we know it colloquially, seems
to mean a collection of houses with no
restriction as to the percentage to be inhabited, or the reverse, and no limitation as to boundary. It is an area which
includes inhabited houses, and usually
extends beyond the inhabited houses.
When we use the word town we may
also mean an urban district or a town
having commissioners. What we do
mean ought to be made clear if a rural
district council wishes to apply the pr
visions of the Act.
It will be remembered that in the discussion the Minister of Home Affairs referred to Bushmills, a town which he
knows and which many Members probably know. I suggested Dervock, which
is on the way to Bushmills. These two
places have this in common, that
they have fairs and markets. They are
both mentioned in the Valuation Lists
published some sixty years ago as
the town of Bushmills and the town.
of Dervock. You find, following those
indications, that the town of Bushmills is not only in two district electoral
divisions, is not only in two parishes
but is in four townlands, none of
which are completely comprised in the
town of Bushmills. We go back to the
little place called Dervock, and you find
it is in two townlands, one Upper Ballyratahan, and the other Lower Ballyratahan. The proportions of the villag
of Dervock that is in Upper and Lower
Ballyratahan are practically identical.
The point remains that if a rural district
council desire to apply the provisions of
this Act to one of these places, is it not
unduly restricting them if we leave the
words as they stand in the Bill? The
words now are "such area being a town
which is not," and so on. I do not se
how, under the Bill, it is possible for an
area to be lawfully defined if we use the
word town. I am afraid unless we de
make an alteration we run grave risk of
defeating our object and preventing the
rural district council applying for the
right to make use of these provisions
on the solid ground that they do
ot know how far they can go, and they
are not going to risk the expense of a law
suit. I, therefore, beg to move the excision of the words as shown on the
Paper, and the insertion of the other
words, also shown on the Paper.
The
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
Clause at present is drafted in such a way
that a non-municipal town can only mean
a town in the popular sense, and it is
intended that the Bill should only be put
in force in a town in the popular sense
in a rural district. The Amendment proposed by the hon. and learned Senator
(Mr. Leslie) would recast the Clause in
such a manner that the Bill could be put
in force in any part of a rural district.
whether a town or not. This, of course.
is not what is intended, as it is only in
towns that the form of trading which it

is proposed to regulate under this Bill
exists. I may say that there are numerous precedents for the use of the word
"town" in its popular sense in statutes,

Summary Jurisdiction (Ireland) Act,
1851, dealing with the recovery of possession of similar premises. It is further
considered unnecessary that the expression "county borough" should be inserted

"(3) In the application of this section to a local authority being the commissioners of a town which is not an
urban district, the Towns Improvement
Ireland) Act, 1854, as amended by

(Ireland) Acts."
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, in Clause 12, page 11, line 25, to
leave out from "section" ("section
twenty-eight") to "shall" ("shall be substituted for") in line 26, and insert
instead thereof "four of the Public
Health (Ireland) Amendment Act,
1884."
The
The object of this Amendment is to
meet a point raised on the Committee
Stage on this Clause by the hon. and
learned Senator (Mr. Leslie), and in regard to which he has put down another
Amendment for the Report Stage.
hon. and learned Senator pointed out on
the Committee Stage that Section 28 of
the Local Government (Ireland) Act,
1871, was repealed by the Public Health
(Ireland) Act, 1878, and that anyone
searching for this Section in the Revised
Statutes would not find it. The section,
however, was specifically re-enacted as
regards towns under the Towns Improvement Act, and as regards other places
which were not urban sanitary districts
by Section 4 of the Public Health (Ireland) Amendment Act, 1884. After
careful consideration it is thought that
the best way to deal with the point
raised by the hon. and learned Senator
is to refer in the Clause to Section 4 of
the Public Health (Ireland) Amendment
Act, 1884, and thereby draw attention to
the Statute which re-enacts Section 28
of the Local Government (Ireland) Act,
1871.
Mr. LESLIE: I need scarcely say that
I welcome this Amendment, which, if I
may use the expression, tells the whole
story and makes it possible for anyone.
who may be afflicted with the misfortune
of having to search for Section 28 of the
Act of 1871, at least to find it. And such
a person can get, I imagine, from the
Stationery Office for a few pence a copy
of the Act of 1871, including Section 28,
which will have the great advantage of
being printed by His Majesty's printers,
an advantage which an extract, obviously,
would not have. The Section was quoted
as in Vanston's Municipal Towns, published, I believe, some forty years ago,
and probably out of print by now, but
if it is still obtainable at its published
price it would cost such a person 18s., and
would not then be evidence if he had
to produce it. Instead of that, by this
Amendment he has now the way pointed
out to him how to get the proper information. It will, of course, now be unnecessary to move the Amendment which
I have put on the Paper. I may say that
I much prefer this method of achieving
my object. I feel that this Amendment
greatly improves the Bill, and my only
regret is that it did not contain these
words earlier, as it would have saved a
very large amount of time and trouble.
Amendment agreed to.

UNEMPLOYMENT.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, "That this House do now adjourn
until to-morrow."
Mr. CAMPBELL: May I put to the
noble Viscount a question of which I have
given him private notice? It is to ask
whether, having regard to the acute
distress prevailing amongst many of the
people owing to unemployment, the
Government will, during the recess of
Parliament, give their most careful consideration to the works of public utility
and other measures undertaken by His
Majesty's Government at Westminster
for the purpose of coping with unemploy
ment in Great Britain, and if the Government will consider whether the necessities
of our unemployed and their dependents
require that corresponding works and
measures be undertaken with promptitude
in Northern Ireland.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I need
hardly say that the problem of unemployment is continually in the counsels of the
Government, and that they will do
everything in their power to mitigate the
severity of the problem.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at Five
Minutes before Four o'clock.

Motion for Adjournment.
UNEMPLOYMENT IN NORTHERN
IRELAND.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, "That this House do now adjourn
until such date as Mr. Speaker shall
direct."
Adjournment-
Unemployment
THE SENATE
Mr. CAMPBELL: Before the House
adjourns to-day, not to meet again for
the purposes of debate for a matter of
four months, when the winter months
will be upon us, I wish to mention a matter of urgent public importance, namely,
the gravity of the suffering and privation in our midst caused by unemployment. I hope that we can trust to the
assurance of the Government that before
we re-assemble very careful consideration
will be given by the Government to any
remedial measures that are undertaken
by His Majesty's Government in Great
Britain for the relief or cure of unemployment there, and that the Northern
Government will try to keep step with
the Imperial Government in that regard. Last month, according to the
official returns, over 33,000 persons sought
employment through the Labour Exchanges, and, including dependents, this
number probably represents some 100,000
of our population, or one in every twelve.
Last week I read in the Belfast Press
an appeal by Lord Justice Andrews on
behalf of the Belfast Distress Committee
from which I extract two sentences:-
There is genuine and widespread
distress in our midst, which shows but
little sign of diminution. During the
summer vacation, when free meals at
the schools will cease, many families
will, owing to this cessation, be in a
worse plight than ever.
It
At this moment unemployment is the
great problem of this Province. It is
bringing cruel suffering in its train.
is reducing the spirit, sapping the
morale, and lowering the manhood of
many of our people. Thorough investigation and statesmanlike remedies are
required, and mere hum-drum or routine
remedies will lead nowhere towards a
solution of the problem.
Mr. MCALLISTER: I would like to
add only a word or two to what the
hon. and learned Senator has stated.
An
assurance should be given to this
House that if any remedial measures are
introduced in the Imperial Parliament
the Northern Parliament will be immediately summoned to discuss such measures,
so that our unemployed will not be unnecessarily deprived during the four
months' adjournment of this House of the
benefit of any remedial legislation that
may be brought in in England.
Mr. SPEAKER: On the Motion for
the Adjournment an hon. Member is not
entitled to raise any matter that would
necessitate fresh legislation. He can
deal only
only with matters that have
to do with administration. Therefore,
if the hon. Member's suggestion would
incur fresh legislation he is not in order
on the Motion for the Adjournment.
Mr. MCALLISTER: I have already
said all I wanted to say on the subject.
Mr. DORMAN: I understand it is intended across the water to endeavour to
alleviate the suffering of the unemployed
by administrative effort, and that instructions will be issued to those responsible
for the administration of unemployment
benefit to revert back to the conditions
for which Labour Exchanges were originally established. The object of the
establishment of Labour Exchanges originally was to open up an agency through
which employers in various parts of the
country could be brought into contact
with the unemployed in the various districts. At the present time large numbers of men, and women also, who are
constantly signing the unemployment register are being turned off hecause they
are not considered to be genuinely seeking work. The object of Labour Exchanges having originally been to bring
employers and employees together, it
seems to me that much could be done by
sympathetic administration, if the powers
that be would issue instructions that
men who are genuinely attending at the
Employment Exchanges and are regularly signing the book should not be
turned off on the ground that they are
not genuinely seeking work.
I have had personal experience of
Labour Exchanges, and I know it is extremely offensive to conscientious menand there are many conscientious men
who attend the Labour Exchange; sober,
upright, industrious men, who have been
out of work for two, three, and four years
-to be told they are not genuinely
seeking work. I cannot conceive anything more offensive to an upright, sober.
and industrious man than to be told by
other people that he is not genuinely
seeking work, when he has almost
walked the boots off his feet looking
for work. Therefore, I suggest the
Government should take into consideration all the measures adopted by the
Government across the water and follow
in their footsteps.
Adjournment-
Unemployment
2 JULY 1929
I
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM:
hope we are not living in too great
expectation of very beneficent changes
taking place owing to the fact that
another Government is in office at Westminster. I, for one, am very anxious
that the Northern Government should
carefully watch and consider any scheme
that may be introduced across the water
for the benefit of the unemployed. Our
Government has done that up to the present, and I do not think it can be said
that they have been in any way lax in
that respect in the past.
the past. Indeed, I
think the encouragement given by the
Government to local authorities
inaugurate relief schemes and to provide
other forms of assistance for the unemployed workers has been much better
than it has been in Great Britain, and
I am quite satisfied that our Government
will continue in that direction. If the
new Government in England intend to
use the Labour Exchanges for the purpose for which they were originally intended it will be a good thing.
to
It is a good many years since the
Labour Exchanges were first introduced
in Great Britain, and not by a Unionist
Government. They were established at
that time for the purpose of transferring
workers in one district to other districts
where necessary, but more particularly
they were set up for the purpose of avoiding transfers, so that men would not be
required to travel from Manchester to
a job in Glasgow when there were men
unemployed in Glasgow who were qualified to do the work. That was the real
object of Labour Exchanges, but they
have never served the object for which
they were set up.
Our general secretary, Mr. George N.
Barnes, who was connected with the
Labour Party, told the Liberal Government afterwards that they were useless
because there was no need for Exchanges
unless they were able to increase employment. Had it not been for the introduction of the insurance scheme the Exchanges would have been a complete
failure. As I say, they were useless.
The trade unions refused to use them;
the employers refused to use them, and
the Government saw no use for them.
Had it not been for the introduction of the insurance scheme by Mr.
Lloyd George in 1912 they would
have been scrapped. They are being
used to-day for the insurance scheme and
nothing else. A poor fellow falling out
of employment may go and register and
get the small dole that the Government
assist in giving, but that is not what the
workers want or ever did want. They
want employment and the Exchanges
cannot provide employment, and neither
can the Government. (Hon. Members:
Hear, hear). If you are expecting so
much from the Government that is at
present in power, well, I hope you will
not be disappointed.
The question is not one of providing
doles and pensions. The trouble is to
provide employment for the workers.
throughout the British Isles. That has
been done to a considerable extent; is
being done, and, I have no doubt, will be
done, by the Northern Ireland Government.
I have such faith in the Government of
Northern Ireland that I am satisfied they
will do their very best to create employment in Northern Ireland. What could
be better than the derating scheme that
has been introduced? That is something, surely to goodness, in the direction.
of creating employment. It will relieve
the employer of a burden that will enable him to compete with the foreigner
and thus obtain orders. That will provide employment, and that is what is
really wanted. I want to say, in conclusion, that I hope our Government will
watch, and watch carefully, any legislation introduced by the Government in
Great Britain. I am perfectly satisfied
they will keep pace with that Government. I am also satisfied that they have
more interest in the unemployed workers
in this part of the country than is being
taken in them in other places. I believe
they will attend to the wants of those in
distress. Undoubtedly, there are a
good number in a distressed state in Northern Ireland at the present time in consequence, unfortunately, of the want of
employment.
Mr. BARCLAY: I would just like to
say that it is, to my mind, almost impossible for any Government to find employment for all those who are unemployed at the present time. It depends
a great deal upon the efforts of the employer of labour and the employee to produce goods cheaply, so that they can be
sold in the home markets. We are all
pretty well aware that we have an open
port. The foreigner can manufacture
his cheap goods, and he can put them on
our markets sometimes almost as cheap
Adjournment-
Unemployment
THE SENATE
[Mr. Barclay]
as our manufacturers can buy the raw
material. I cannot see for the life of
me how our manufacturers can compete in
these markets and
pay the
wages that
they are paying at the present time.
Then they are also called upon to pay
high rates and taxes, which are all put
on the goods when put on the market.
If
We have not the power here to protect
our markets, and I think it would be a good
thing if the Imperial Government, instead of giving increases to pensions and
increases to the dole, would do something
to protect and save the home markets for
our own people and for our own goods.
By doing so they would be finding employment for, perhaps, 30 or 50 per cent.
of those who are unemployed to-day.
That is the way I look at the matter.
we give the dole it means that the money
has got to come from somewhere. No
Government has the machine in its power
for manufacturing pounds, shillings and
pence. They have to raise the money
somewhere, and it has to come off the
These manufacturers, as I
taxpayer.
said before, are put out of the market
because they have to pay such high taxes.
I would be very glad if the present
Government in Great Britain would find
some means of providing markets for our
own home products. If they did so I have
every faith that they would find employment for those who are out of work at
the present time.
Mr. LESLIE: The previous speakers
have dealt with the possibility of some
earlier re-meeting of this Parliament from
one particular point of view, that of unemployment. I do not want to say anything more about it. I rather want to
speak for a moment on general questions.
I would like to take this opportunity of
urging upon the Leader of the House
that if we are to be called together suddenly, or at the Autumn Session, the
business of the earlier meeting should be
confined to matters of urgency, such as
those spoken of, and the business of the
Autumn Session should be kept to the
lowest possible limit. Further, if it is now
known what Bills are likely to be introduced in the Autumn it would be, I
think, of great assistance if these Bills
could be given a First Reading when we
ineet next week, or whenever we meet, in
order that they may be printed and circulated during the Recess
I would
I can
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
like to say, in reply to the Senator who
has last spoken, that I will bring his suggestion to the notice of the Government.
If it can be done I will give effect to it.
With regard to the question of unemployment, the hon. and learned Senator,
who raised the question on the Adjournment, required from me an assurance
that the Government would consider any
scheme brought in across the Channel to
relieve the matter of unemployment. Of
course, I cannot give any assurance of
that kind as we do not know what the
Measures to be produced on the other
side will be. What might be suitable in
Great Britain might, perhaps, be unsuitable in Northern Ireland.
say, on the general ground, that the problem of unemployment is constantly be
fore the eyes of the Government. It is
a most serious matter for all civilised
Governments, especially for the Government of this small community, where the
question of unemployment presses more
hardly on us than it does in other places.
I can only say, that during the Recess.
the Government will give as much attention to the question as they have
done in the past, and I think they have
done
a good deal. (Hon. Members:
Hear, hear). The hon. and learned
Senator (Mr. Campbell) did not suggest
anything else. Anyhow, I will give the
assurance that the Government will certainly pay the utmost attention to any
Measures brought forward that they
think may prove satisfactory for this
country.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at Twenty
minutes before Four o'clock, until such date as Mr. Speaker
may appoint.
