MEALS FOR NECESSITOUS SCHOOL
CHILDREN.
Mr. CAMPBELL asked the Leader
of the House whether, having regard to
the plight of many children of the unemployed in attendance at public elementary schools, he can state if the
education authorities within their respective areas have power under the
Education Act, 1923, to make provision
during the holidays, as well as during
school days, for meals for children likely
to suffer in their education by reason of
lack of food, and, if this power exists,
whether the Ministry of Education will,
where no funds are available, authorise
the education authorities to meet the cost
of food necessary for such children during
such period as the Ministry may approve.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The Ministry is advised that education authorities
have power, under the Education Act,
1923, to provide free meals for necessitous
children during periods while the schools
are closed.
With regard to the second part of the
hon. and learned Member's question, I
am prepared to consider sympathetically
any representations which may be made
to me by an education authority which
Oral Answers.
Adjournment.
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
desires to expend money in the exercise
of this power and is hampered by the
fact that its approved financial scheme
for the current year does not contain the
necessary provision.
RELIEF OF UNEMPLOYMENT.
to
Mr. CAMPBELL asked the Leader of
the House whether the Government have
come to any decision as to undertaking
measures for the relief of unemployment
of a similar character to the measures outlined last week by His Majesty's Government in the Imperial Parliament,
including (1) Government grants
public institutions, such as railways, harbours, docks (by way of loan or by the
payment of interest for a limited period),
and (2) road grants to local authorities
for immediate use on main-road and
secondary-road programmes; and whether
he is in a position to state what immediate
relief measures the Government have in
contemplation.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD:
Before the noble Leader of the House
answers the question I would like to ask
him a supplementary question. Although
he may not be able to give me an answer
owing to the fact that the House will not
be sitting again for some time, I hope
he will look into the matter. I desire
to ask why there has been so much delay
on the part of the Government in coming
to an arrangement with the Down County
Council to commence the proposed widening of the road between Belfast, Bangor,
and Donaghadee, which would provide a
large amount of employment.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: With regard to the question on the Order Paper,
the policy of the Government all along
has been to assist in providing as much
work as possible for the unemployed, and
the Government, through the Unemployment Relief Grants Fund, has from time
to time subsidised to a very large extent
each type of public institution to which
the first part of the question refers,
namely, railways, harbours and docks.
As regards the second part of the question, schemes of road maintenance, road
improvements and bridge building have
been supported to the full extent which
the financial position of the Road Fund
will admit. Moreover, the Government
has given very substantial financial assistance to the building of houses and
schools and has contributed to schemes of
land drainage, afforestation, etc. This
policy the Government will continue to
pursue sympathetically.
With regard to the question raised by
the right hon. and gallant Gentleman
(Colonel Sharman Crawford), it was only
given to me just before this House met,
and I am therefore not in a position to
make a definite statement, but I will
make inquiries into the matter.
Adjournment.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, "That this House do now adjourn
until such date as Mr. Speaker shall appoint."
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: 1
understand that the Education Bill will
be introduced in the Autumn Session.
Of course, that is a Measure in which
there is a great deal of interest and about
which there may be a good deal of contention, and for that reason I hope the
Bill will be in the hands of Members some
time before the House meets.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The Education Bill, to which the right hon. and
gallant Senator alludes, will not be introduced this year. I forgot to say, when
I moved the adjournment of the House,
that the hon. and learned Senator (Mr.
Leslie) asked last time that Bills should,
if possible, be circulated during the Recess. There are no first readings in the
Senate, and the First readings of Bills
that were taken the last time in another
place are not all available for circulation
yet, but as they become available they
will be circulated to Members who like to
leave their names in the Library.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD:
Do I understand that the Education Bill
will not be introduced next Session?
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Not in the
Autumn Session, but next year.
Mr. LESLIE : I would like to thank
the noble Lord for making the arrangement to which he has alluded. It seems
to me of great importance that Bills,
even if they are not introduced in the
Adjournment.
Adjournment.
11 JULY 1929
first instance in this House, should be
circulated as soon as possible in order to
give time for examining them, because,
as we know, when Bills do reach this
Chamber from another place, the time
left for examination of them is very often
not as long as could be wished.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: I
would point out to the hon. and learned
Senator (Mr. Leslie) that a Bill introduced in the House below may not be the
same Bill as introduced in this House.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at

date as Mr. Speaker shall
appoint.

Lower Bann Flooding
Lower Bann Flooding
6 NOVEMBER 1929
E

Patterson's Divorce Bill.
Brought from the House of Commons
and read a First time.
Message from the Commons.
The Commons have passed the following
Resolution to which they desire the concurrence of the Senate:-
That all Consolidation Bills in the
present Session be referred to a Joint
Committee of both Houses of Parliament, consisting of eight Members.
Flooding in Lower Bann Area.
The following Question stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie:-
To ask the Leader of the House
whether he can give any information on
the following points for the period 4th.
October last and succeeding days:-
(i) as to the height of the water in
Lough Neagh;
(ii) as to the estimated height, volume
and velocity of the water in the
Lower Bann and tributaries thereof at various times and places;
and
(iii) whether the situation would have
been affected had the Bann Drainage Scheme, now under consideration, been in actual operation.
Mr. LESLIE: I have framed my
question in the most general terms in
order to obtain any information which
may be available-and I recognise the information will be exhaustive as regards
the effect of the flooding early in October
in the Lower Bann area, so that that
information may be compared
and
examined with the Report dealing with
the Bann Drainage, which has been before
this House. I think I need not say any
more except perhaps to lay stress on the
fact that I do not suggest that the effects
of the flooding were felt so severely in
the Lower Bann valley as they were unhappily in other parts of the Province;
but, as I say, if there is anything that
can be communicated to us it seems to me
most desirable that it should be on record
in a form in which it will be available for
the information of the public.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): I have been able to obtain
a large amount of the information desired
by the hon. and learned Member.
On the 4th October the level of Lough
Neagh was some four inches below the
statutory level of 52 feet above ordnance
datum. The lake rose somewhat rapidly
from this level owing to flood discharge
off the catchment area to a level of some
ten inches above statutory level on the
10th October, and then more slowly to a
maximum level of some twelve inches
above statutory level on the 16th October,
after which date the level gradually fell
owing to a reduction in the flow off the
catchment area, and on the 22nd October
the lough had again fallen to a level of
some six inches above statutory level.
A definite reply regarding the height,
volume and velocity of the water in the
Lower Bann river can only be given for
the gauge stations at Portna, Movanagher,
Carnroe and the Cutts, as no records are
available for reference in respect of any
other points on the river.
Immediately above Portna weir the
water level rose from some six inches
above summer level on the 4th October to
21 inches above summer level on the 7th,
and then dropped very slowly to a level
of some 17 inches above summer level on
the 21st October.
Immediately above Movanagher weir
the water level gradually rose from some
eight inches above summer level to some
22 inches above summer level on the 7th
October, and then gradually fell to a level
of some 18 inches above summer level on
the 22nd October.
Immediately above Carnroe weir the
water level suddenly rose from some four
inches above summer water level on the
4th October to a level of some 36 inches
above summer level on the 7th October,
but this level only obtained for one day,
and on the 8th October the level had
fallen to some 16 inches above summer
water level, at which level it remained
steadily until 14th October, when
gradually fell to some 14 inches above
summer level on the 22nd October.
it
Immediately above the Cutts Weir the
water level suddenly rose from summer
water level on the 4th October to some
30 inches above summer level on the 7th
October, but this level only obtained for
one day, and on the 8th October the
level had fallen to some 14 inches above
summer level, and thereafter fell to a
level of some 12 inches above summer
level on the 22nd October.
The volume of discharge down the
River Bann past Portna Weir suddenly
D
195 Lower Bann Flooding

THE SENATE
Expiring Laws Bill
[Viscount Charlemont]
increased from some 200 million cubic
feet per 24 hours on the 5th October to
some 600 million cubic feet per 24 hours
on the 8th October, and then very
gradually decreased to some 500 million
cubic feet per 24 hours on the 22nd
October. As Portna Weir is situated
about half-way down the river, these discharges may be taken as an average for
the length of the river with a somewhat
lower discharge out of Lough Neagh at
Toome, and a somewhat higher discharge
past The Cutts Weir near Coleraine.
It would be almost impossible to give
figures to show velocity of discharge down
the River Bann as this velocity is constantly changing with a variation in the
depth and width in the River Channel,
and very many changes in velocity take
place between Toome and The Cutts at
Coleraine, but it is estimated that the
velocity approaching the Weir at Portna
must have been somewhere between 3
and 4 feet per second on the 8th October
when the discharge down the river was at
its maximum.
No records are available for obtaining
the height, volume and velocity of discharge in the tributary rivers of the Bann
as these rivers are not dealt with in the
Bann Drainage Scheme, and any improvements in their discharges is only
obtained by a lower outfall level at the
point where they enter the main river.
I now come to the effect which the proposed Bann Drainage Scheme would have
had on the circumstances. As hon.
Members are no doubt aware, the Scheme
proposes in the first place, by the insertion of floodgates in the existing fixed
weirs, to render possible the lowering of
Lough Neagh in anticipation of heavy
floods, and, secondly, the improvement of
the river channel generally, so as to give
a discharge capacity which will enable it
to pass all but the most exceptional floods.
If the Scheme had been in operation
prior to the October floods which we are
now discussing, it has been calculated
that by lowering the lough to a depth of
only one foot below statutory level, in
anticipation of winter floods, it would
have been just prevented from rising
above the present statutory level during
the period in question, returning, if
necessary, to a depth of one foot below
the statutory level on the 22nd October.
There would, therefore, have been
flooding by reason of the level of Lough
Neagh. The discharge down the Lower
no
mum
ex-
Bann river would have been increased
from approximately 600 million cubic
feet per 24 hours during the maxiflood-the discharge under
isting conditions-to somewhere between 800 and 900 million cubic
feet per
24 hours, and the river
itself would have been maintained at a
lower level than that which actually
obtained during the period. In no case
would the flood discharge have topped the
banks nor would it have impeded discharge from the Agivey river.
Mr. LESLIE: Thank you.
Mr. MCALLISTER: Arising out of
the answer to that question, might I ask
the noble Lord if he can give the House
any idea of how far the Bann Drainage
scheme has progressed, and what is the
approximate time in which practical work
will begin on the scheme.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I shall be
obliged if the hon. Member will put a
question down on the Paper. I cannot
give him the information off-hand.
Expiring Laws Continuance Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
con-
Viscount BANGOR: This Bill
tinues temporarily certain enactments,
whether of the Parliament of the United
Kingdom or of the Parliament of Northern Ireland, which may be regarded as
being still experimental or liable to revision. A single date only is fixed for
their continuance, namely, the 31st
December, 1930.
The Bill has been framed so as to fit in
with the corresponding provisions of the
Imperial Bill of the present year, which
applies to Northern Ireland in so far as
it deals with any enactment relating to a
subject with respect to which the Parliament of Northern Ireland has not power
to make laws. The present Bill supplements this provision by dealing with
enactments relating to subjects with respect to which the Northern Ireland
Parliament has legislative power.
the two Measures taken together deal
with all temporary laws applicable to
Northern Ireland, according as they
come within the power of one or other of
the two Parliaments. I beg to move.
Thus
Mr. CAMPBELL: I hoped the
Deputy Leader of the House, whom I
197 Expiring Laws Bill-

6 NOVEMBER 1929
Second Reading.

1:
recongratulate upon his first speech in that
office, would have been able to give us
some satisfactory reason for the inclusion
of some of the Measures which are to be
found in the Schedule of this Bill.
For instance, the Local Government
(Emergency Powers) Act of 1921 is proposed to be continued. That Act was an
Emergency Act, and does the emergency
that existed when it was passed still
continue? Glowing accounts have
cently been given as to the peaceful state
of all parts of Northern Ireland, but this
Act contains strong and drastic powers
that are vested in the Minister of Home
Affairs. That would indicate to an outsider that the central authority cannot
trust the local authorities, and that an
unstable central regime needed the support of the Act as a crutch to lean upon.
Then again, who in Northern Ireland is
concerned with Measure called the
Grey Seals Protection Act, 1914. I hope
the noble Viscount will be able to throw
some light on the reason why this Parliament should continue that particular Act.
You can give protection to grey seals but
It seems to me to be
a
not to white linens.
the merest trifling.
Colonel
SHARMAN CRAWFORD:
We all regret the loss to this House of the
services of the late Deputy-Leader,
Viscount Massereene and Ferrard, who
was always an adornment to the House.
I should like to convey the congratulations of the Senate to his successor, and
we hope that Viscount Bangor may fill
that post for a long time to come. (Hon.
Members: Hear, hear.) For the past
three years I have been trying to get excluded from this Bill a Housing Act
under which these "tin towns" were
erected, and I should like to ask if that
particular Measure is again included in
the Bill now before the House.
Viscount BANGOR: In reply to the
hon. and learned Senator opposite (Mr.
Campbell), the Local Government
(Emergency Powers) Act, 1921, was
passed originally to enable the Ministry
of Home Affairs to deal with conditions
that had arisen on the transfer of
powers. Certain local authorities refused
to recognize the jurisdiction of the Government, and the Act enabled the
Minister to dissolve such authorities and
replace them by paid Commissioners.
Between December, 1921, and April,
1922, seven Commissioners were appointed
to replace twenty-one local authorities.
These bodies were all re-instated before
the end of May, 1924, and, excepting the
recent case of the Donaghadee Urban
District Council, the Minister's power
under the Act has not been exercised
since.
The Local Government Board, and subsequently the Ministry of Home Affairs,
has always had power to dissolve a Board
of Guardians that failed to carry out its
duties, and the Act simply extended a
similar power in regard to other local
authorities It affords a simple manner
of taking action when necessity arises, that
otherwise could only be accomplished at
very considerable cost to the ratepayers
of the area affected, and, pending a
general revision of Local Government
law, the Government think it desirable
that these powers should. be retained on
been
the present basis. It has never
alleged that any person or any body of
ratepayers has suffered any loss or any
hardship as a result of the continuance of
the Act.
With regard to the Grey Seals Protection Act, I hardly think that that can be
dealt with by us. The noble Viscount
(Viscount Charlemont) reminds me that
at any rate no Measure of protection for
the linen industry can be passed by this
Parliament.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I am aware of that.
I said we cannot give protection to linen,
but we can to grey seals.
Viscount BANGOR: Dealing with the
question raised by the right hon.
and gallant Member (Colonel Sharman-
Crawford), the Housing Act to which he
referred has been dropped and does not
appear in this Bill. If the hon. and
gallant Member desires any further information on that matter I shall be
pleased to furnish it.
Colonel SHARMAN - CRAWFORD:
That is all the information I asked for.
Mr. LESLIE: It chances that three or
four years ago, on a similar Expiring
Laws Continuance Bill, I myself drew
attention to the question of the Grey
Seals Act. My recollection is that
the answer given by the noble
Marquis who was then leading the
House was to the effect that if we here
in Northern Ireland did not reproduce
the extension of the Grey Seals Act of the
Sittings of the House 200
Adjournment-
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
Imperial Parliament, there was a possibility that grey seals would not receive
that protection on the coasts of Northern
Ireland which they received in other
parts of the British Isles. That is the
reason, I understand, why we still continue year by year to include that
particular Act in our Expiring Laws
Bill.
On Question, Motion (That the Bill be
now read a Second time), agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
whole House for to-morrow.
Adjournment.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, "That this House do now adjourn
until to-morrow."
Colonel SHARMAN - CRAWFORD:
A great many of the Members would
like to know whether the noble Leader of
the House can give any information as to
the sittings of the House during the next
week or two.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I should
prefer to give that information tomorrow.
Mr. LESLIE: The noble Viscount
carried a Motion regarding Consolidation
Bills passed during this Session. I do
not know whether he is prepared to tell
us
whether there is any Consolidation
Bill on the stocks, so to speak, at the
moment, and, if so, whether it would be
practicable to read it a First time to-day
or to-morrow in order to clear the way
for the Committee which will no doubt
be appointed in due course.
I would
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
like to deal with that question also tomorrow.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at
Twenty minutes before
Four o'clock.

7 NOVEMBER 1929
THE SENATE.

Thursday, 7th November, 1929.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
Paper Presented to Parliament.
Ministry of Labour:
National Health Insurance (Joint Committee) Regulations, 1929.
Laid before the House (pursuant to
Act), and ordered to lie on the Table.
-
Patterson's Divorce Bill.
Resolved: "That this Bill be now read
a Second time.”
Consolidation Bills-Joint Committee.
Resolved: "That the Commons Message
relative to Consolidation Bills be now
considered."-(Viscount Charlemont).
Resolved: "That a Select Committee of
four Members be appointed to join with
a Committee appointed by the Commons,
as mentioned in the Commons Message,
to consider all Consolidation Bills
of the present Session."—(Viscount
Charlemont).
Resolved: "That the following Members be named of the Committee:-
Mr. Campbell,
Mr. Leslie,
Colonel Pakenham,
Mr. Quigley.
"That the Committee have power to
send for persons, papers and records.
"That three be the quorum of the Committee."-(Viscount Charlemont).
COURT OF CRIMINAL APPEAL.
The following Question stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr.
Campbell:-
To ask the Leader of the House
whether, as a result of the representations
made by the Government, there is any
immediate prospect of the urgent demand
for the setting up of a Court of Criminal
Appeal being complied with.
Mr. CAMPBELL: My justification for
putting this question is the delay that is
taking place in effecting this very necessary reform. I wish to hasten the
Imperial legislation necessary to set up
this Court. The reform is overdue.
Northern Ireland is the only self-governing unit in the British Commonwealth
where the security of the liberty or life
of a subject may depend on the determination of a single Judge and Jury.
The law allows an appeal from a decree
in a twopenny trespass or a sixpenny
debt, but bars a man sentenced to
death or penal servitude from stating his
case before a higher court. England has
had a Court of Criminal Appeal for 21
years past, and the machinery has worked
admirably. It has been effective and has
won public confidence. Here no appeal
lies from the verdict and sentence on the
trial of a subject for an indictable offence,
except when the learned judge gives permission on a question of law. Such
appeals are rare. No doubt a judge on
refusing leave to appeal acts from the
most conscientious motive, but an error
ought not to be left uncorrected merely
because the judge acted conscientiously.
An eminent English lawyer remarked.
when the Court of Criminal Appeal Bill
was being debated in the Commons that
there were three stages in the life of a
In the first he was nervously
judge.
anxious that his judgments should be
right, in the second he was quite sure
they were right, and in the third he did
not care a straw whether they were right
or wrong. I have never had experience
of a judge in the third stage. Even most
accomplished judges have often been not
immune from strange views. In 1810
Lord Eldon was alarmed by a Bill of Sir
Samuel Romilly's which had passed the
Commons to abolish capital punishment
for privately stealing in a shop to the
value of 5s, and in the debate in the
Lords he prided himself on having sent a
man who was convicted before him of
stealing a horse of the value of 7s 6d for
execution, upon the ground that the man
was a regular horse stealer.
The reform would in no way lessen the
sense of responsibility among jurymen for
their verdicts, while it would remedy a
defect in the administration of the law.
There is a general desire among the public
that this reform should be effected at the
earliest possible moment to secure not
only that no error of justice is done, but
also that all men will see and know that
no error of justice is done.
Oral Answers.
Oral Answers.
THE SENATE
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): I am glad to be in a position to state that as a result of representations which have been made to the
Imperial Authorities, to which reference
has already been made in this House, a
Bill to enable a Court of Criminal
Appeal is now in process of drafting and
will shortly, it is hoped, be ready to be
laid before the Imperial Parliament.
APPOINTMENT OF TEACHERS.
Mr. CAMPBELL asked the Leader of
the House whether his attention has been
called to a speech by the Rev. S. Mayes,
at Armagh Protestant Diocesan Synod,
on 2nd October last, eulogising the
action of the Government in bringing in
an amending Education Bill, on which
occasion the reverend gentleman is reported
to have stated that the appointment of
teachers should be left to the Ministry
and not to any Committee, adding,
"never has there been such wire-pulling
and jobbery in the appointment of
teachers as in the five years that have
passed since the Act became law.
The
qualifications of teachers are thrown to
the winds, and appointments are for
those who can pull the most wires;"
whether these charges were met with a
denial at a meeting of the Armagh
Regional Committee
on 15th October
last; and whether the Ministry has made
any inquiries as to the prevalence of the
practices alleged.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: My attention has been called to the statement
attributed to this reverend gentleman,
the implications of which have been
denied by the Armagh Regional Education Committee so far as their own duties
in the matter of appointments of teachers
have been concerned.
In reply to the last part of the question,
the Ministry has no reason to believe that
there is any justification for the serious
charges made against education committees in the statement quoted. In all
appointments of teachers the Ministry has
to be satisfied that the person appointed
possesses the necessary qualifications, and
I am not aware of any case in which an
unqualified person has been put forward
for recognition by an education
committee. I should like to add that the
Ministry can hardly be expected to undertake enquiries into a charge formulated
by a single individual and unsupported
by any definite instances.
ROMAN CATHOLICS AND
EDUCATION ACT.
Mr. CAMPBELL asked the Leader of
the House whether the policy of the
Government, indicated in the Report of
the Ministry of Education for 1928-1929,
of securing for "educational policy and
administration the sympathies of important sections of the community,"
extends to the 421,000 Catholics in Northern Ireland who desire a settlement
which, by safeguarding the rights of
parents, shall be just to all classes and
creeds; if so, has the Government sought
opportunities for conference with representatives of Catholic interests.
I am
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The Government is certainly desirous of securing
the sympathy of all classes and creeds in
its educational policv. and would welcome
the co-operation of those parties to whom
the hon. and learned Member refers, a cooperation which we have invited in the
past but not always received in the full
measure we would have welcomed.
not, however, aware that there is anything in the present educational system
which prejudices the just rights of
parents so far as these can be reasonably
safeguarded under a publicly controlled
system. I am at all times ready to meet
and confer with representatives of the important interests concerned, and have
already had the advantage of receiving
an authoritative statement on the subject
of the amending of the Education
Act from representatives of the Roman
Catholic Clerical Managers.
RELIGIOUS INSTRUCTION IN
SCHOOLS.
Mr. CAMPBELL asked the Leader of
the House whether his attention has been
drawn to a speech by a Member of the
Government, at Banbridge, on 31st
August last, in the course of which he is
reported to have said: "The determination of the Government to safeguard
religious instruction in the schools need
not be questioned for a single moment.
With a Prime Minister who was a great
Protestant Statesman, and with a Minister
of Education who realised the depth of
the religious feeling of Ulster, he could
assure them that they could make themselves absolutely at ease in regard to
that great educational question. Lord
Craigavon has undertaken to
see that
Bible instruction was secured in the

7 NOVEMBER 1929
schools;" whether this pronouncement represents accurately the policy of the Government; whether he will state on what
occasion and to whom the Prime Minister
gave this undertaking, and whether it
applies in the case of all public elementary schools which may be transferred
to or provided by local education
authorities.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I have not
seen the statement given in the first part
of the hon. and learned Member's question, but the assurance attributed to the
Prime Minister is, I think, substantially
the decision of the Government, so far as
all public elementary schools transferred
to or provided by the education authorites are
concerned. This assurance is
implied in the statement made by the
Prime Minister at a public meeting on
23rd April last.
Mr. CAMPBELL: May I ask if the
Prime Minister has given an undertaking
that certain denominations will receive
direct representation on all Education
Committees.
I should
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
like to have notice of that question.
MAGHERAFELT EDUCATION
COMMITTEE AND A SCHOLARSHIP.
Mr. CAMPBELL asked the Leader of
the House whether it is a fact that the
Magherafelt Regional Education Committee of the Londonderry County
Council on 26th September last, refused
by a majority vote a scholarship of £40 a
year, tenable for three years, to a
Catholic boy named H. C. McGurk, who
had obtained 1,700 marks, the highest
marks, and a £40 prize for his high scoring at the qualifying examination for the
scholarship, and awarded the scholarship
to a non-Catholic boy who had obtained
1,482 marks; whether this decision constituted a breach of the regulations of the
Ministry that such scholarships should be
awarded by merit; whether the Committee
on a later date rescinded this decision,
after an official intimation that there was
no option but to comply with the law; and
whether, in view of the events which have
happened, the Ministry will authorise a
grant of a scholarship to the student who
obtained the 1,482 marks.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The facts
in regard to the action of the Magherafelt Regional Education Committee at
their meeting held on 26th September
last are as stated in the Question.
The Ministry drew the attention of the
Committee to the fact that their decision
was not in accordance with the provisions
of their own approved scheme for the
award of University Scholarships.
The Committee have now reversed their
former decision and have awarded the
scholarship to H. C. McGurk.
Should the funds of the Committee permit, the Ministry will offer no objection
to the award of a scholarship to the candidate who obtained second
second place in
accordance with the scheme.
UNEMPLOYMENT IN NORTHERN
IRELAND.
Mr. CAMPBELL asked the Leader of
the House whether it is a fact that, on the
30th September last, the number of unemployed in Northern Ireland was 35,329,
exclusive of persons disqualified for unemployment benefit by reason of the
joiners' trade disputę; if he will state how
so disqualified; how many
many were
Government-aided schemes for alleviating
unemployment were in actual operation
at the date of the rising of Parliament;
and for how many persons did such
schemes provide employment; what the
corresponding figures are now; and what
Government Grants have been made or
promised during the recess of Parliament
towards reconstruction or improvement
schemes of public authorities or other
bodies for lessening unemployment.
The
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
answer to the first part of the question is
in the affirmative. It is understood that
the number of joiners originally involved
in the dispute was 1,050, but it is not
possible to say the number disqualified
for the receipt of benefit on the 30th
September, as the men on strike do not as
a rule attend at the Employment Exchange. The number of strikers who have
obtained work in other directions is,
moreover, not known. At the date of
the rising of Parliament there were in
operation 88 schemes for the relief of unemployment, providing direct employment for 1,966 men. The corresponding
figures at the 5th inst. were 87 schemes
and 1,592 men, the reduction being
accounted for solely by the fact that some
schemes terminated before others which
had been approved were commenced.

SCHOOL BUILDING GRANTS.
were
Mr. CAMPBELL asked the Leader of
the House whether the policy of the
British Government, administered by the
Commissioners of National Education
from the institution of National Schools
in 1831 to the outbreak of the Great War,
of making direct building grants of twothirds of the cost of new schools to
Catholic and Protestant managers, has
been followed by this Government;
whether any such grant has been made to
individual Catholic and Protestant managers in Northern Ireland without discrimination since 1921; whether education authorities have made any building
grants under the Education Act of 1923,
and, if so, on what conditions.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: In reply to
the first part of the question, the policy
referred to was continued by this Ministry pending the establishment of education authorities, but grants were limited
to cases which were under consideration
at the time the Ministry was established
and cases in which a new building was
urgently needed.
The answer to the second part is in the
affirmative. There was no discrimination.
The
In reply to the last part as to grants by
education authorities, no building grant for
a new voluntary school has yet been made
by an education authority, but the Ministry understands that several applications
are being favourably considered. In one
case agreement has been reached and the
conditions have been approved by the
Ministry, including the following.
Authority will make a grant of two-thirds
cost of erection, the plans and specification are subject to approval by the
Authority and the Ministry, and the
school will be conducted as a Public
Elementary School under the
management of a Committee constituted according to the provisions of Section 15 (2) of
the Education Act, subject to the guarantee required by Section 16 (2) of the Act.
Several grants have also been made by
education authorities for improvements,
including one for the rebuilding of a
school.
I may
add that in the provisions of the
Education Act for grants towards new
voluntary schools there is also no discrimination in regard to religious denomination.
Mr. CAMPBELL: May I ask if it is
a fact that in the twenty-six counties,
following the policy of the British Government for many years, it is the practice
of the education authorities to make
liberal grants for the erection and improvement of schools to Catholic and
Protestant managers alike, without discrimination.
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
like notice of that question.

PRIVATE MOTORS AND
COMPULSORY INSURANCE.
Mr. CAMPBELL asked the Leader of
the House whether, having regard to the
fact that motor vehicles capable of
travelling at upwards of sixty or seventy
miles an hour are being used on the high
roads in increasing numbers, and that
casualties from motor accidents, especially
to children, are of alarming frequency,
the Government will take into early consideration the question as to whether the
existing legislation on private motordriven traffic on the high roads needs
amendment with a view to securing inter
alia better public control over the practically unrestricted issue of licences to
drivers of private motor vehicles; and as
to the advisability of legislation making
insurance against third party risks compulsory on owners and drivers of such
vehicles, for the protection of the general
public.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am glad
that the hon. and learned Member has
put down this question for I think the
answer will serve to dispel a great deal of
the misconception which is abroad with
regard to the two matters mentioned in
the question, which are at present raising
a great deal of interest and discussion
amongst the public generally
The number of accidents in which
motor vehicles are concerned is causing a
great deal of anxiety to everyone, and
not least to the Government, and I think
that this Government can claim to be the
pioneers of legislation designed for the
purpose of making the roads more safe
for the public. and dealing with the pro-
Oral Answers.
Oral Answers.
7 NOVEMBER 1929
blem which has been created by the phenomenal increase in motor traffic.
As
hon. Members will no doubt be aware,
a Royal Commission is at present sitting
in Great Britain to investigate the problem, and it has presented two interim
reports containing recommendations for
legislation, the greater portion of which
is embodied in our Motor Traffic Act
passed three years ago, an Act which has
attained a very considerable measure of
success in checking the growth of the
appalling list of casualties due to motor
traffic.
I do not suggest for one moment that
legislation here has reached finality. In
point of fact a Bill will shortly be introduced with regard to this matter, and the
situation with regard to motor traffic is
developing with such rapidity that it
would be absurd to say that even that Bill
represents the last word. My right hon.
Friend, the Minister of Home Affairs,
has this matter constantly before his mind,
and I may say that he cordially welcomes
any practical suggestions towards the
ultimatę solution of the matter, and that
he will give the most careful consideration to any such suggestions that are put
forward from time to time.
I do not propose, however, to deal with
the general question of motor traffic this
afternoon, but rather to confine my remarks to the two specific points raised in
the question which is upon the Paper,
namely, first the necessity and desirability
of testing applicants for driver's
licence before the licence is issued; and
secondly, the desirability of making insurance against third party risks compulsory on the owners and drivers of all
motor vehicles for the protection of the
general public.
a
With regard to the first point, if we are
to judge from the statements of those who
advocate the testing of all drivers before
a licence is issued, it is assumed that the
introduction of a test of drivers would
make an immediate and drastic reduction in the number of accidents, and if
the Government had any reason to
believe that this was an accurate statement of the position they would not
hesitate to ask Parliament for power to
institute such tests in spite of the expense
and great administrative difficulties involved. But is this so? I find that in
the year 1927, out of a total of 1,428
accidents there were 6 in which the driver
concerned was inexperienced; in the year
1928, in which there were 1,467 accidents
there were 6 cases in which the driver
was inexperienced; and in the first nine
months of the year 1929, in which there
were 1,047 accidents, the number in
which inexperienced drivers were concerned was 7.
These figures certainly do not bear out.
the suggestion that any appreciable
number of accidents is due to inexperienced driving. Experience shows
that it is not the inexperienced driver
who gets into trouble, but rather the
driver with experience who takes risks.
and attempts feats which the beginner
would be afraid to attempt. In compiling our statistics we have taken as an
inexperienced driver any person who has
been driving for less than a period of six
months, and I think the House will agree
that any person who has been driving a
car for this period could pass any practicable test which could be set, but such a
test would not insure that he was a safe
driver upon the roads. The conclusion
which the Royal Commission on Transport
has arrived at on this point will be found
in paragraph 43 of their first interim report, where they make use of the following
expressions:-
we
ex-
"With regard to qualifications based.
on knowledge and experience,
recognise the force of the arguments
that no tests can be effective, and,
therefore, we do not recommend them."
It is hardly necessary to say that the
institution of such tests would be
pensive and would present great administrative difficulties, but, of course, as I
have already pointed out, if it appeared
that the benefit to be gained from the
institution of the tests would justify it,
such difficulties could not be allowed to
stand in the way.
As
I now turn to the second aspect of the
matter raised in the question, namely
insurance against third party risks.
the House is aware a system of compulsory
insurance is in force in Northern Ireland
in regard to public service vehicles, and
the Royal Commission, to which I have
referred, has recommended that a similar
system be instituted in Great Britain.
They have also recommended that there
should be compulsory insurance for
all drivers and owners of motor
vehicles, but only if such a scheme can
be arranged. I emphasise this. This
matter is now the subject of conferences
between the Ministry of Transport in
Great Britain and the Insurance Com-
Oral Answers.
Oral Answers.
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
panies, and my right hon. Friend, the
Minister of Home Affairs, is watching the
result of these conferences with the
closest attention. It is recognised in
Great Britain that the matter is one of
the greatest complexity and difficulty,
and it is not possible yet to say whether
it is a practicable proposition or not.
For reasons which I shall give shortly,
it is not possible that this Government
could come to any definite decision on the
matter pending the decision in Great
Britain. I do not, however, wish to
leave the matter just there. We have
had our own experience in the matter of
compulsory insurance in connection with
the public service vehicles which forms
but a small portion of the whole problem.
Still, the experience is of use in considering the general question.
Policies to cover third party risks are
drafted with two matters in view, first
to give protection to the insured person
against liabilities in respect of claims of
third parties, and secondly, to limit in
various ways the liability of the Insurance Company. The first point to be
borne in mind is that insurance merely
covers legal liabilities. As drafted at
present, the policies are not designed in
any way for the protection of the public.
They do not cover any case unless the
accident is due to the fault of the driver.
A very large proportion of the accidents
do not result in any legal claim against
the owner or the driver of the car, and
such cases would not be touched by third
party insurance. In the second place,
as we found when we came to examine
the policies which had hitherto been taken
out in respect of public service vehicles,
there are many ways in which the liability
of the Insurance Company is limited,
and to put it shortly we found that a
revolutionary change in the policies had
to be made if the public were to receive
reasonable protection against the inability
of the owner or driver of the vehicle to
meet the legal claims.
It
This was the subject of long and difficult negotiations with the Insurance Companies, which resulted in the issue of a
special Public Service Vehicle Policy for
Northern Ireland without any appreciable increase in the rates charged.
was, however, only possible to bring
these negotiations to a successful
clusion because the Insurance Companies
were satisfied that the special legislation
in force in Northern Ireland with regard
conto public service vehicles, and which was
not in existence elsewhere, would ultimately improve their risk considerably.
The Minister of Home Affairs, therefore,
had a lever whereby he was able to induce the Insurance Companies to fall into
line.
Difficult as was the question of compulsory insurance for public service
vehicles, that of compulsory insurance in
the case of the ordinary vehicle is infinitely greater, and my right hon. Friend
is satisfied that it would be impracticable
for Northern Ireland alone to attempt
to come to an arrangement with the Insurance Companies on the subject, and
to induce them to issue a special policy
containing special provisions of a character more onerous to the insurers than
are in force elsewhere.
We do not wish to say any final word
on this subject at present. All that we
are in a position to say to-day is that we
fear that for this Government to attempt
to carry out such a scheme alone would
be impracticable, however desirable a
scheme of the kind may be on the merits.
AMENDMENT OF WIDOWS'
PENSIONS ACTS.
Mr. CAMPBELL asked the Leader

Orphans' and Old Age Contributory
Pensions Bill (1929)
now before the
Imperial Parliament, with a view to conferring on widows and orphans in Northern Ireland benefits corresponding to
the benefits sought to be conferred on
widows and orphans in Great Britain by
the Bill referred to.
The
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
answer to this question is in the affirmative.

Viscount
Adjournment.
Before
CHARLEMONT:
moving the adjournment of the House I
should like to give an answer to one or
two questions asked yesterday. The
hon. and learned Senator opposite (Mr.
Campbell) asked what progress was being
made with the Bann Drainage Scheme.
I should like to say that the preliminaries
will be pushed on as quickly as possible,
and I think it would be well to await the
introduction of the Drainage Bill before
asking for any details about it.
our
With regard to the future business of
the House, about which I promised
yesterday to give some information, I
cannot say definitely of course, what will
happen in another place-and
business depends upon that--but I can
say with a good deal of certainty that the
House will not sit next week. I beg to
move, "That this House do now adjourn
until such date as Mr. Speaker shall
appoint."
Mr. LONG: I should like to know
whether it would be possible for the
Noble Leader of the House to arrange or
devise some method by which the Order
Paper could be in the hands of Members,
say, two days before the meeting of the
House. I would regard it as a great
boon as I am sure other Members of the
House would. It seems scarcely fair that
we should be called on to deal with an
agenda of such length as we have had
to-day without seeing it beforehand.
Mr. McALLISTER: I would like to
support the recommendation of Senator
Long. I think it would be a great advantage if we had a reasonable opportunity of seeing the Parliamentary
papers of the day before we are called
upon to deal with them. Speaking for
myself, I have not seen the Order Paper
at all to-day, although in justice to those
concerned it is possible that those papers
were delivered at my private house this
morning after I left for my office. It is
rather unfair that we should have to
come here without the slightest knowledge of what the business of the day is
and I earnestly support the appeal of
Senator Long that the Government
should devise some means whereby we
could have a reasonable opportunity of
knowing the business we are coming here.
to transact.
Mr. SPEAKER: Perhaps the hon.
Members will allow me to say on that
question that it is not possible to give
two days notice of the business when the
House is sitting from day to day. It is
possible when there is an interval between
the sittings, but it is evident that it is
impossible when the House meets from
day to day.
Mr. LESLIE : The Noble Viscount
alluded
Bann
to the question of the
drainage, and that gives me an opportunity of expressing my thanks—which
it was not possible for me to do yesterday-to him for the very elaborate and
detailed answer he gave to the question
I asked. I may say that the information given-some of which at all events
was published in this morning's papershas already been received with the
greatest interest in the locality to which
the question particularly related, and I
think everybody feels very much indebted
both to the Noble Viscount and to those

took.
On Question, Motion (That this House
do now adjourn until such date as Mr.

o'clock until such date
as Mr. Speaker shall appoint.

BALLYCASTLE
The
HARBOUR
ACCOMMODATION.
following question
question stood on
the Order Paper in the
Mr. McAllister :-
name
of
Oral Answers
Oral Answers
27 NOVEMBER 1929
To ask the Leader of the House whether
he is aware that, owing to the defective
harbour accommodation at Ballycastle,
communication between Rathlin Island
and the mainland is frequently impossible, and the lives of the people are endangered; and, if so, whether he can
state what steps the Government propose
to take to protect the property and lives
of those concerned.
Mr. MCALLISTER: In putting down
the question which stands in my name I
was interested from two points of view,
first of all from the material point of
view, and secondly from the point of view
of doing something to safeguard the lives
of the people of Rathlin Island. As
many Members of this House are no doubt
aware, owing to the defective harbour accommodation at Ballycastle communication with Rathlin Island is suspended
frequently for days, and sometimes for
weeks at a time. There are no proper
facilities for the proper delivery to the
nearest market town of Ballycastle of the
produce, cattle, sheep, and horses which
are tied to the bottom of the boats and
conveyed to the mainland. It frequently
happens that these islanders set out from
the island with a stock of produce in their
boats, and when they come to Ballycastle
they find the harbour so defective that it
is hazardous and risky to attempt a landing, and they have to try to return at
grave risk to their lives.
Whilst it is important for the development of their agriculture, industry, and
general trade that something should be
done to facilitate the landing of their produce, the thing that appeals to me most
of all is the fact of the grave risk to their
lives these islanders run on very frequent
occasions. I submit that the first duty
of a Government and of a Parliament is
to safeguard the lives of the people over
whom they rule and for whom they legislate. It is the highest trust that any
Government can have in its hands. The
life of an individual should be sacrosanct,
whether at the hands of a Government or
any individual.
If I am told that the Government
sympathise with these people, but that
the difficulty in the case is money, my reply would be this. Surely when the
Parliament of Northern Ireland can find
money to make the roads more comfortable to ride on, to make houses and homes
more comfortable for the people to live in,
to improve the railway lines of Northern
Ireland, and to train the youth of the
country so that they can emigrate to
Canada and other places, surely when
money can be found for these purposes,
it is the duty of the Government to take
reasonable precautions to safeguard the
lives of the people of Rathlin Island by
raising a relatively small sum of money
to improve the harbour accommodation.
Viscount BANGOR: I can assure my
hon. Friend that the question of harbour
accommodation at Ballycastle is one
which from humanitarian and other
grounds has been engaging the serious
attention of the Government for some
time past. It has long been clear that
the existing landing place at Ballycastle
is unsatisfactory, affording, as it does,
no adequate shelter, and being inaccessible from the sea during stormy weather.
In the autumn of 1926, when plans for
improved landing facilities at Rathlin
Island were under consideration, proposals were put forward for the construction of a new pier at Ballycastle, extending into deep water from a point close
to the present landing place, with provision for a new inner breakwater, and
the repair and improvement of the natural
breakwater upon which McGildowney's
pier was originally erected. I think that
was erected on a reef at Ballycastle, but
it has since been broken up. Towards the
construction of this pier, and the works
on Rathlin Island to which I have just referred, the Government was prepared to
make a contribution up to a maximum
of £6,000. It was contemplated, of
course, that the work should be carried
out by the Antrim County Council as the
local authority primarily responsible,
with, possibly, a contribution towards the
cost levied upon the Ballycastle Urban
District.
At this juncture, however, and before
negotiations were complete, proposals
were put forward in local circles for the
construction of a commercial harbour at
a cost of some £50,000. As a matter of
fact I think it was £46,000. These proposals met with keen support from the
Ballycastle Urban and Rural District
Councils, who pledged themselves to contribute towards the cost of the work, but
before the merits of this new scheme bad
been closely examined, a commercial undertaking declared its intention of floating a syndicate for the express purpose
of constructing a commercial harbour at
Oral Answers
Oral Answers
THE SENATE
[Viscount Bangor]
Ballycastle, on even more ambitious lines
than had been so far contemplated. The
Government has at no time been called
upon to express an opinion as to the
merits of development upon this scale.
and it was naturally reluctant in any way
to impede a movement which might in
itself have brought new industry and
considerable commercial advantage to the
Ballycastle district.
During the past few weeks, however, it
has become clear that the plans of the
commercial undertaking are not likely to
materialise in the immediate future, and
it has therefore become incumbent upon
the Government to turn its attention once
more to the vital feature of this problem,
that is to say, the provision of adequate
landing facilities for the Rathlin Islanders. To deal with this matter the local
authorities concerned are being called
into consultation, and it is the hope of the
Government that the position will be sufficiently clarified in this regard to enable
ameliorative works to be undertaken at
Ballycastle, as soon as suitable weather
conditions supervene in the spring of next
year.
EDUCATION OF YOUTHFUL BLIND.
The following question stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Colonel
Pakenham:
To ask the Leader of the House whether
there is any provision made in Northern
Ireland for the education or support of
the youthful blind of both sexes who are
over school age?
Colonel PAKENHAM: In rising to
ask the Deputy-Leader of the House the
question in my name on the Order Paper,
I am sure the sympathy of the Members
of the House will be with me in this
matter. There are a great many children
in Northern Ireland who are over school
age and who cannot get into any place of
instruction or any school. There are a
great many more of these children than
hon. Members know about. I have been
personally interested in one or two, and I
can find no means of getting these
children looked after or educated. Therefore, I thought I would put this question
down to see what were the views of the
Government on the matter.
Colonel
SHARMAN - CRAWFORD:
Before the Deputy-Leader of the House
replies to the question I should like to say
that each of the Six Counties has its own
blind persons' scheme, and that is where
the financial difficulty comes in. I should
like to ask whether the Government has
ever considered the possibility of a universal scheme for the Six Counties, because we want to have money to provide
the necessary workshops. The Belfast
Workshop is overcrowded, and if we had at
scheme for the whole of the Six Counties
it might get over the difficulty mentioned
by the hon. and gallant Colonel.
Viscount BANGOR: Before replying
to the question on the Order Paper perhaps I might be allowed to answer the
question raised by the hon. and gallant
Senator opposite (Colonel Sharman-
Crawford). The Government has in contemplation a universal scheme for the Six
Counties, but each of the Counties is being
called upon to formulate its own scheme.
Some of them have done so and some have
not, and until each county has formulated
its own scheme it will not be possible for
the Government to have a universal
scheme. The Government wants to get
uniformity if it can.
With regard to the question asked
by the
the hon. and gallant Member
(Colonel Pakenham), by the Education
Act (Northern Ireland), 1923, Part IX,
education authorities are required to
make provision for the education of blind
children up to the age of 16 years.
In addition to the powers conferred on
education authorities referred to, the
Councils of Counties and County Boroughs
are under the Blind Persons Act, 1920,
responsible for framing schemes for the
welfare of blind persons. In Belfast a
comprehensive scheme has been in operation since the passing of the Act, and in
County Down and County Fermanagh
tentative schemes have been framed within the last two years. The other Councils
are at present considering the framing of
schemes.
Such schemes may provide for the
education and training of blind persons,
for the care and supervision of home
workers, for supplying home teachers, for
augmenting the wages of blind workers.
and generally doing anything that would
tend to improve the position of blind
persons.
The institutions at present available for
blind persons are the Workshops for the
Industrious Blind, the Ulster Society for
the Education of the Deaf, Dumb and
Blind, the Home for the Blind, Clifton-
Oral Answers
Oral Answers
27 NOVEMBER 1929
Belfast. The
ville, and the St. Brigid's Home, Crumlin
Road, all
Government
makes grants to these institutions m
addition to any grants they may receive
from local authorities.
I might add that the Ministry of Home
Affairs has recently issued a circular
urging local authorities to make use of the
powers conferred on them by the Blind
Persons Act.
Mr. ARMSTRONG: When the Government is considering this matter I hope
they will bear in mind the fact that in
Armagh there is a blind institution which
at present gets no assistance.
It was
founded a great many years ago, and rt
would be capable of giving instruction to
many of the people contemplated by the
hon, and gallant Senator (Colonel Pakenham) if it only had the necessary money.
The institution has workshops and everything ready, but owing to the reduction
of the interest on Consols and other causes
it is unable to do anything. It is a great
pity, and now that this matter is going
to be taken up by the Government I hope
they will bear this institution in mind."
Mr. LONG: It might interest the noble
Lord, the Deputy-Leader of the House,
to know that a joint conference of all the
County Councils of Northern Ireland has
been called for one day next week to consider the framing of a joint scheme. In
the County of Londonderry there are only
47 blind persons, and 19 of these are over
15 years of age,
and it is felt that a
scheme for County Derry alone would not
be workable as the overhead charges
would be out of all proportion to the value
of the scheme. Therefore, I think a joint
scheme is absolutely necessary.
LAW OF LANDLORD AND TENANT.
The following question stood on the
of Mr.
Order Paper in the
Campbell:-
name
To ask the Leader of the House whether
the Government are prepared to introduce
legislation in the ensuing Session for the
amendment of the law of landlord and
tenant in urban areas; and, if so, will
they favourably consider bringing building leaseholders within the benefits of the
Town Tenants Act in respect of Improvements made by them in pursuance of their
leases.
Mr. CAMPBELL: In asking this question I must at least express the sense of
disappointment felt by many at the delay
of the Government in bringing forward
legislation to meet the grievances of leaseholders. This is a matter of vital concern
to many thousands of people in the
Province.
Viscount BANGOR: While no definite
pledge in the matter can be given at present, I can assure the hon. and learned
Member that every effort will be made to
bring forward a Bill dealing with the
matters which were recently considered
and reported upon by a Departmental
Committee of which he was a member. As
the hon. and learned Member is personally
aware, the recommendations of the Committee dealt with matters of an extremely
intricate and complex character and require the most careful and exhaustive consideration, but he may be assured that before a decision is arrived at the aspect of
the case referred to by him in the latter
part of his question will receive the
fullest and most earnest consideration.
one
was
Mr. McALLISTER: Might I ask the
noble Lord if he has noticed a speech made
recently at a luncheon in Ballymena by
the Prime Minister in which he stressed
the question of the sanctity of contract.
He went out of his way to stress that question to such an extent that public opinion
was inclined to interpret his speech as an
attempt to evade the real issue involved.
The interpretation that I think
rightly put upon his speech was that at
the present moment he does not intend to
bring what are known as building leaseholders within the legislation he intends
to introduce. That is the
vital
grievance the leaseholders suffer under.
In my judgment the present state of the
law in regard to leaseholders is one of the
greatest impediments to the development
of urban districts in Northern Ireland,
and I am sorry the noble Lord has not
given us a more definitę indication as to
what is the real view of the Government
with regard to this matter, because if
they do not bring within the ambit of
legislation the question of what is known
as building leaseholders it will have to remain under the ordinary law as it is today, and will have to be decided in a
Court of Appeal. I do not think that was
the intention of the public, and public
opinion was very clamant on this particular
question.
Oral Answers
Adjournment
THE SENATE
ADOPTION OF ENGLISH
LEGISLATION.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to ask the
Leader of the House whether the Government, having regard to the beneficial
working of the following recent changes
in the law in England, will take into consideration the question of the early
adoption in Northern Ireland of
(a) The provisions of the Administration of Estates Act, 1925, consolidating
the enactments relating to the administration of the estates and distribution
of the assets of deceased persons, and,
in particular, abolishing heirship and
substituting a new code of intestacy;
(b) The provisions of the Law of
property Act, 1925, facilitating and
cheapening the transfer of land and
assimilating land of every tenure to personal property in regard to its legal
incidents;
(c) The provisions of the Trustee Act,
1925, freeing trustees, executors and
administrators, who have given notice.
through newspapers of their intention
to distribute real and personal property,
from liability to the Crown as regards
the property distributed where they
have not had notice of any claim by the
Crown at the time of distribution.
Viscount BANGOR: The various Acts
passed by the Imperial Parliament from
time to time are subjected by the Government of Northern Ireland to the closest
scrutiny with the object of arriving at a
decision as to whether it is necessary,
appropriate, or desirable that similar
legislation should be introduced into Northern Ireland.
As the hon. and learned Member is
aware, the three Acts referred to by him
are of an extremely voluminous and extensive character, and are really in the
nature of consolidating Acts. He will
appreciate that an important factor in
connection with the bringing forward of
legislation before the Parliament of Northern Ireland is the pressure of other and
more urgent business, and under the circumstances I regret that I am not in a
position to give him at present any undertaking as to whether, and, if so, what
legislation on the above lines will be
brought forward before the Parliament of
Northern Ireland.

28 NOVEMBER 1929
THE SENATE.

Thursday, 28th November, 1929.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the
Chair.
Rankin's Divorce Bill.
Resolved "That the Bill be now read a
Second time.
Burns's Divorce Bill.
Resolved "That the Bill be now read a
Second time."
Warrenpoint Electricity Special Order.
Viscount BANGOR: I beg to move,
"That the Special Order made by the
Electricity Commissioners for Northern
Ireland, under the Electricity (Supply)
Acts, 1882 to 1919, and confirmed by the
Ministry of Commerce under the Electricity (Supply) Act, 1919, in respect of the
Urban District of Warrenpoint, in the
County of Down, be approved."
This special order granted by the Electricity Commissioners for Northern Ireland and confirmed by the Ministry of
Commerce will confer on Mr. R. W.
Hamilton, Kilkeel, statutory authority to
supply electric energy for public and private purposes to the Urban District of
Warrenpoint. The rules of procedure have
been complied with and the order follows
the usual form. This order will not, however, be fully operative until Mr. Hamilton transfers the undertaking to a company registered under the Companies Act.
Three applications were originally made.
to the electricity commissioners in respect
of the same area the Warrenpoint Gas
Consumers, and Waterworks Company,
Mr. Leonard Vincent, of Belfast, and
Mr. R. W. Hamilton. After holding a
local inquiry into the matter the commissioners decided to grant Mr. Hamilton's application. I beg to move that the
House confirm the order.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Oral Answers to Questions.
EXPENDITURE OF EDUCATION
COMMITTEES.
Mr. CAMPBELL asked the Minister
of Education whether it is a fact that, under the Local Government (Rating and
Finance) Act, 1929, educational expenditure by Education Committees will be
made good from Parliamentary funds
without any controlling limit to the
moneys so expended, and whether, under
that Act, the Government pays a very
large proportion of the rates in the counties; and, if so, whether in these circumstances the Government will consider in
framing their Amending Education Bill
the question of revising the powers of
Education Committees.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): The answers to the first and
second parts of the question are in the
affirmative. A limit has not been fixed
in the Local Government (Rating and
Finance) Act to the expenditure from the
rates on education which may be the subject of deficiency grants from the Government. This expenditure is, however,
under the terms of the Education Act
subject to the control and approval of the
Ministry of Education, so that a sufficient
safeguard against extravagance is, in the
opinion of the Government, already provided. These particular circumstances do
not appear to the Ministry to be such as
to require a revision of the powers cf
Education Committees in the proposed
Amending Bill.
BIBLE
TEACHING IN SCHOOLS.
Mr. CAMPBELL asked the Minister
of Education (a) Whether an undertaking has been given on behalf of the
Government that direct representation
on Education Committees will be given
by the Amending Education Bill to
certain denominations;
(b) Whether the Catholic children
attending voluntary elementary schools
number 72,000 out of a total of 199,000
pupils in all the public elementary.
schools, and form by far the largest
number of any demonination;
(c) Whether by rule 28 of the Rules of
the Commissioners of National Education
issued in May, 1915, it was re-affirmed
as a fundamental principle of elementary
education in Ireland that the reading of

Education Policy
THE SENATE
[Mr. Campbell]
the
the Holy Scriptures either in
"Douay" or the "Authorised" version,
and the teaching of catechisms, came
within the rules as to religious
instruction;
now
(d) Whether the Ministry are
facilitating in provided or transferred
schools the form of religious instruction
termed by some "simple Bible teaching."
In
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
answer to the hon. Senator's question
(a) I may say I am not aware that an
undertaking in the terms stated in the
question has been given by the Government. To give direct representation on
Education Committees to certain denominations would not be feasible, and
would probably constitute a preference.
which would not be in accordance with
