the powers of this Parliament.
however, the Government's intention to
associate more closely in the administration of local Education Committees those
managers, trustees, or religious bodies,
who have transferred their schools to the
Education Committees.
In answer to question (b). According
to the most recent return the number of
Roman Catholic children enrolled in the
public elementary schools is 71,998.
which represents 36 per cent. of the total
enrolment of pupils. For the information of the House I will give the total
numbers of each religious denomination.
Roman Catholic.
as I have stated.
Presbyterian, 64,405: Church of Ireland,
51.618; Methodists. 6,634; Others, 4.856.
I
With regard to question (c) the rule
referred to in the question is correctly
stated, but I see no reason for supposing
that in drawing up this regulation which
was clearly intended for administrative
purposes there was any intention of reaffirming a fundamental principle.
should rather think the object of the
Commissioners was to make it clear to
managers and others that, notwithstanding any opinions that might be held to the
contrary, they wished that Bible teaching should be treated as religious instruction, and be governed by the same series
of regulations.
The reply to question (d) is in the
affirmative. But I will have something
further to say on this question on the
Motion which will be reached presently.
Government's Education Policy.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to move,
That, in the opinion of this House, the
educational policy of the Government
differentiates unfairly against some fifteen
hundred elementary schools not under
and
Four-and-Two School Committees,
that, by sanctioning what is termed
"simple Bible teaching" in the curriculum of Provided and Transferred
Schools, it establishes denominational
education of a type acceptable to certain
sections only of the population.
You
In 1923 the Government passed an Education Act which met the Secularists'
claim. In 1929 they have undertaken to
amend that Education Act in a way that
will meet the Bible Christians' claim. You
have never tried to meet the Catholics'
claim. Rather you have antagonised it.
You have vetoed one penny of public funds
being spent on improving Catholic schools.
however efficient, or on building Catholic
schools. You have prohibited any contribution of public moneys to the amenities
of these schools except the paltry contribution of one half of the expense of lighting,
heating, and cleansing them.
exercise public supervision over
schools through official inspection, but
under the specious plea of public control
you want to put six managers in charge of
each school instead of one-one manager
who was always a scholar, who had the
fullest confidence of his people, and whose
heart was in the work. Having deprived
the managers of these schools of the buildso long
ing grants which they received
from the British Government, and which
are so necessary, you say you invite their
co-operation in the working of your
system. Are you in earnest ?
these
The present one-sided system of primary
education in Northern Ireland is a matter
of urgent public importance. I wish to
view the position as a detached observer
of a mistaken and mischievous policy.
The broad fact confronting us after six
years' working of the Education Act of
1923 is, that the whole system of primary
education in Northern Ireland remains
openly and undisguisedly denominational.
The Act contemplated undenominational
secular schools. But the best laid schemes
of Ministers gang aft agley. More than
75 per cent. of the elementary schools
have been neither transferred to Education Committees nor put under Statutory
Committees. Slightly over 100 elementary schools have been put under Four-and-
Education Policy
Education Policy
28 NOVEMBER 1929
1.
རྒྱུན་པོ་༥
1,
Two Committees. About 1,500 have not.
Some 13 new schools have been provided.
This handful of provided schools was at
first secular, but they are now becoming
sectarian schools inculcating Bible
Christianity. In the transferred schools
the Ministry facilitate the teaching of
Bible Christianity. Many of the free
voluntary schools are making a hard fight
and having a hard time. The last annual
report of the Ministry says:-
The majority of the voluntary schools
are still unsuitable and ill-equipped.
Many are barely tolerable.
You cripple them and then disparage
them. Yet one fact is clear; the vast
body of managers have not surrendered
a stone of their schools. The report of
the Belfast Education Committee for the
vear ended 31st March, 1929, states:-
None of the voluntary schools under
Roman Catholic management are transferred, and of the voluntary schools
now remaining under Protestant management very few will be transferred.
As regards the erection and improvement of the schools the Government has
made a complete breakaway from the
policy pursued in Ireland by the Imperial
Parliament for almost 100 years. As I
have said, not one penny is given towards
the building or improving of a voluntary
school, unless the school is working under
a Four-and-Two Committee. That, I say,
is the outcome of the spirit of persecution.
Quite different was the record of the
British Government. Building grants
were made by the Imperial Parliament to
individual managers of primary schools in
Ireland from the institution of the
National Board of Education in 1831, in
accordance with the letter of Lord Stanley.
then Chief Secretary of Ireland, setting
up the Board.
The Board will be entrusted with
absolute control over the funds voted
by Parliament, which they shall apply
to the following purposes,
the first of which was grants in aid of the
erection of schools. Where aid was sought
from the Board for building a schoolhouse it was required that at least onethird of the estimated cost should be
subscribed, and that the school, when
finished, be vested in Trustees, to be
approved by the Board. The Board's
estimate of expenditure from 1833-34 of
£30,114 included £10,463 for building,
and the following year's estimate of
£65,257 included £20,466 for building.
Grants were made on these lines until the
year 1845 when the Commissioners
obtained a Charter empowering them to
build schools to be vested in themselves.
This system was discontinued in 1861 as
it aroused dissatisfaction in Ireland, and
building aid amounting to two-thirds of
the cost was given for schools to be vested
in trustees. These building grants were
continued until the outbreak of the War
in 1914.
The Government of the Irish Free State
has continued since 1st January, 1925, to
make most liberal grants for building and
bettering schools to individual managers.
of Catholic, Episcopalian, Presbyterian,
Methodist and other denominational
.schools. That system worked with fairness to all creeds. It was efficient. Here
in Northern Ireland it has been uprooted.
The reason why is plain. The Government hoped that the system they substituted would destroy the managerial
system and satisfy the non-Catholic denominations. In the Regional Education
Committees the Catholic membership of
the total is small. Yet I believe that
apprehension exists among the supporters
of the Government lest in a very few
areas of which Derry City-not
school has been transferred there is one,
where Catholics are in a majority, an
election might place Catholics in a position
to control the management of non-
Catholic transferred schools. Part of
your new policy is to prevent popular control having any such result. Popular
control is your shibboleth so long as it is
non-Catholic popular control. What a
revelation of political cynicism!
one
You hoped that your brand new system
under the Act of 1923-mainly English
and fractionally Irish-would in time force
the Catholic minority to abandon the
managerial system. The Imperial Parliament knew better. Had they foreseen how
unfairly you would deal with the minority
in your midst I doubt if they would have
handed over to you the control of the
school system. The denominational
system is proving too strong for you.
From your own friends now rises the cry
that the provided and transferred schools.
shall be denominational in teaching, in
atmosphere, and in the prescnnel of the
staff. They want for their schools the
fullest aid of the State. You are willing
to give this aid.
Education Policy
Education Policy
THE SENATE
[Mr. Campbell]
One denomination you are determined
to shut out from a fair share of the public
moneys voted in aid of building schools,
not because their schools are inefficient.
You cannot say so. You cannot say they
are not as efficient in secular instruction
as your privileged schools, or that they
do not teach the subjects comprised in
your programme of instruction.
Your
inspectors know that the public moneys
are well spent on these schools. If these
schools fulfil faithfully and zealously the
aims of all sound education, formation of
character, training in good habits, and
development of intelligence, why not put
them on an equality with your favoured
schools in the distribution of public.
moneys? You remember Marshal Foch
with gratitude, and you have cause to.
There was no lack of efficiency in him. He
had also the moral fibre without which, in
his own words, nothing avails. He was a
product of the voluntary schools of France.
Some people here would starve the type of
schools which nurtured the heart and
brain of that great captain and great
Christian gentleman.
I observe from the report of the Ministry in public newspapers-that money is
pouring out in an almost exhaustless
stream for the building of palatial and
mammoth schools and other purposes.
Are you getting value for your princely
outlay? I read as follows in the leading
columns of the "Northern Whig"-I
believe a faithful friend although sometimes a candid critic of the Governmentof 26th October last:-
We spend close on two millions of
money each year on what may in many
cases not be education at all. We have
had no evidence, and we believe that
business men in this city and others
would concur, of any improvement in
the standard. We are simply an uneducated people.
An uneducated people! Is this to be
the be-all and end-all of your education
policy? It is futile for the Government
to pretend that what you call "simple
Bible teaching" in a school does not constitute religious instruction and does not
make the school denominational. Such
teaching is denominational. Was it not
treated as such under the rules of the old
National Board? Yes. Throughout its long
régime. In almost the opening paragraphs of his letter in 1831 establishing
the National schools Lord Stanley, the
ultra-Protestant Chief Secretary of an
ultra-Protestant British Government, declared that the teaching in the primary
schools of the Bible without note or comment would be denominational teaching
quite acceptable on principle to Protestants, but quite unacceptable on
principle to Catholics. On that basis the
National school system was built. On
that basis it rested for almost 100 years.
In the rules of the Commissioners issued
in May, 1915, that fundamental principle
of primary education was re-affirmed.
no
The Northern Government had
sooner got the reins of power into its hands
than it set about to sap and undermine
that basis. The minority do not need to
ask the reason why. To vary a profound
saying of Edmund Burke,
great public
matters and little minds go ill together."
This system satisfies the non-Catholic
bodies, and you argue that that satisfaction ought to satisfy us. It meets the requirements of the non-Catholic conscience.
In this teaching they think they have
everything that matters. The Bible is the
Protestant rule of faith. Bible teaching.
which is out and out denominationalism
is to be given at the public expense.
Catholic parents cannot permit their children to attend schools giving this form of
religious teaching. Religious instruction
to Catholic children must be given under
Catholic supervision. Thus those who do
not require definite religious teaching for
their children are the denominations
favoured by you--the denominations to
whom Biblical reading and explanation
severed from definite religion contain all
that in their eyes is necessary to salvation.
That form of denominationalism is or is
to be fully endowed by the Government.
Citizens who are not satisfied with it must
pay for that type of instruction and pay
for the education of their own children
as well.
Parents and teachers are penalised who
sincerely believe that the moral value of
education, the soundness of education,
must be founded on a religion that can be
stated in definite terms. Why should a
Presbyterian or an Episcopalian who is
asked to pay taxes for a Catholic school
have any more grievance than a Catholic
who has to pay taxes for the support of
schools which teach a type of religion or,
as the case may be, irreligion, which he
cordially dislikes? Are Catholics not entitled to an honest return in value for the
taxes they pay? Make the case your own,
Education Policy
Education Policy
28 NOVEMBER 1929
and try to be fair. A distinguished Member of this House, of whom personally I
can say nothing that is bad, but of whose
politics I can say nothing that is good-
Col. Pakenham-is reported to have declared on 12th July last that "the Orange
Order had been a party with the Government in settling the question of the proper
teaching of the Bible in their schools, and
they could trust the Government to see
that the Protestant children were taught
Mark
by Protestant teachers."
"the
proper teaching of the Bible.” That
phrase sounds to my ear more intelligible
than simple Bible teaching." I can
understand it. The gallant Colonel and I
would hardly agree as to what is "the
proper teaching of the Bible." And Protestant children must have Protestant
teachers.

The ex-Speaker of the House of Commons, Sir Hugh O'Neill, M.P., speaking
on the same day, is credited in the Press
with saying "the religious instruction in
the schools must be maintained at all costs,
so that they could maintain the great principles to which the majority of the people
in Ulster had so long been accustomed."
These speeches herald the coming changes
in education, and sound a note of triumph.
For whom or for what? Yet, may I say
this to the noble Lord, the Leader of the
House, who is responsible for the educational policy of the Government, and I say
it solemnly and in all sincerity, that it is
only by the co-operation of all creeds that
you can make the schools of Northern Ireland fit to give to Northern Ireland what
it asks and needs.
It is sometimes said that the Four-and-
Two Committee system was borrowed from
the Education Act of 1902, and that the
Catholics of England are content to work
under them. They are not. They are
profoundly dissatisfied. It was forced
upon them. There they number about
one nineteenth of the population. Here
in Northern Ireland Catholics are more
than one-third of the population. Here
there has been a long tradition of
managerial control. For years the
Catholics of England have been striving
for equality of their schools with the
Board schools. Their schools are crushed
down with financial burdens on the one
hand, and on the other they are gravely
hampered by the unfriendliness and the
secularising tendencies of the education
committees. A statement issued in 1927
by the Catholic Educational Council of
England and re-affirmed this year by the
Catholic Hierarchy of England states:-
These conditions now constitute a
burden which is beyond the strength of
the supporters of these schools to bear.
The Act (1902-3) was a compromise
which did not give equality of treatment with regard to assistance from
public funds as between the voluntary
schools and municipal schools
They ask the Imperial Government to
give encouragement to all forms of sound
educational endeavour, and to render ail
equally available to parents. The training in the English secular schools has led
to 60 per cent. of the English undergraduates having no connection with any
Church. I should like to ask if the
raison d'être of the Regional Committee
and the Four-and-Two Committee systems
has not been undermined by the Derating
Act. This Act has practically abolished all
local liability for rates and transferred the
burden to the Ministry of Home Affairs.
The local expenditure in aid of education
in Northern Ireland was, I think, at all
times small compared with such expenditure in England. It cannot now be said
that these committees exist to see to the
proper allocation of the rates raised by
the ratepayers in aid of the schools in
their localities. Taxation without representation offends you, but you give local
committees power over local schools to the
maintenance of which the local ratepayers'
contribution is almost negligible. The
central Government now pays the piper.
Are the local committees to call the tune?
Are you going to force the 72,000
Catholic children to be stunted and
dwarfed in their too brief school careers
for lack of school accommodation in the
hope that in time their voluntary schools
will be extinguished? The Catholic
I
schools are there. They will remain.
Does your six years' experience justify you
in hoping they will take shelter en masse
under the Four-and-Two umbrella?
Catholic managers as a body obviously fear
that the system is fraught with danger to
both secular and religious teaching.
believe that outside County Antrim only
three Catholic schools have, owing to severe
economic pressure, been put under statutory
committees. But the vast majority of
managers, as you must realise, are resolute
in holding by their schools, whatever be
the sacrifice. Do not cherish the delusion
that they stand alone. The Catholic
laity are proud of the work of the
Education Policy
Education Policy
THE SENATE
[Mr. Campbell]
managers and grateful for the sacrifices of
the managers, and are united with them.
The laity are resolved that the heritage
which their fathers bequeathed to them
they will hand down to their children free
from the blot and taint of secularism.
In the Rules and Regulations of the
Commission of National Education issued
in May, 1915, the second of the fundamental principles of the System of
National Education is stated in these
words:-
It is the earnest wish of His Majesty's
Government and the Commissioners
that the clergy and laity of the different
religious denominations should
operate in conducting National schools.
CO-
This has not been the spirit of your new
régime. The minority here ask for
equality for the schools, equality for the
parents and the pupils of all creeds. The
elementary system set up by the Education Act of 1923 aims at the extinction of
the voluntary schools, discriminates unfairly against the minority, and is in this
way detrimental to the interests of elementary education. Your latter-day innovations on it establish, I submit, denominational education for the denominations
save one. Your policy so far has been a
false policy. In the long run it will hold.
little profit for Northern Ireland. Under
it the Catholic schools in the North are
being "brayed together in a mortar" and
indignation at your attempt to uproot
these schools is not the less deep because
not loudly vocal. Your policy does not
conduce to peace, religious harmony, or
educational efficiency.
Why not act towards the very numerous
minority within your borders as the
British Government acted for upwards
of eighty years. Why not act as the
Irish Free State is acting towards the
very small minority within its borders.
Why not raise the whole question to an
atmosphere of reason and goodwill, and
recognise that the managers of voluntary
schools not under Four-and-Two Committees ought in equity to have help from
the public funds towards the building
and improving of their schools so long as
these schools provide an elementary
education which on its secular side meets
all the requirements of your programme
of instruction, and satisfies all the tests
of your official inspection. Think well,
think wisely, of the future of the tens
of thousands of children at these schools

and of the future of Northern Ireland at
large.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I remember Mr. Lloyd George said at one
time that if you removed all the grievances
that some people nursed they would have
nothing else to live for. I feel there is a
great deal in that statement. But I will
say this, that I am very sorry to see a
question like this raised in this House if
it is only intended to score a trivial point
against the Government.
Mr. CAMPBELL: No.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: It is
very liable to revive that bitter sectarian
hatred which we have known in the North
of Ireland, and which the Government
have been endeavouring to the best of
their ability either to kill or to moderate.
The Bill introduced by the noble Marquess that has been so much criticised
was really intended to try if possible to
bridge the difference that existed between
the two sections. He made a brave
effort to try to satisfy Roman Catholic
and other sections and to obtain cooperation in working a scheme which is
really good and which has proved to be
good. Unfortunately so far as the
Roman Catholic people are concerned
there has been little or no help from
them to try to make the education scheme
a success up to the present. I do not
think there has yet been a single school
transferred to the Government. They
have made it pretty clear that they are
not intending to do that. But I do expect that as far as the Act is concerned
they would take full advantage of the
financial provisions or benefits that may
be obtained from it.
Colonel
Hear, hear.
SHARMAN-CRAWFORD :
I
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM:
have noticed for a very long time there
has never been any objection to British
gold if it can be got in any way. Really
it is a great pity that this matter should
be raised in the tone it has been raised
in.
The hon. Member states that the
policy is unfair towards a certain section.
In my opinion it is rather unfair to the
others. If you take into consideration
that after the Education Bill was passed
the Ministry of Education had to make
special provision for the training of
Roman Catholic teachers, knowing they
Education Policy
Education Policy

28 NOVEMBER 1929
could not possibly associate with common
Church people, or ordinary Presbyterians.
or Methodists, to obtain the necessary
training to qualify them as teachers. A
special arrangement had to be made
which put the Government to considerable expense to send them to a special
bostel and training college in England
at Hammersmith. Yet they would not
recognise that as preferential treatment
It certainly was. I readily understand
if Presbyterians asked for such a scheme
they would be laughed at-or even the
Church people or Methodists. Why
should they get exceptional treatment for
the training of their teachers any more
than all these other sections?
The
I would go further and say that in
my opinion Protestants have been called
upon to make considerable sacrifices in
an effort to assist the Ministry of Education in working out the scheme.
Government had neglected what they
thought they were entitled to and that
was religious education in the schools.
And now that the Roman Catholic party
has made the position perfectly clear I do
not think that anyone can complain if
the Protestant section asks for a review
of the position. They are justified, in
my opinion, in asking for a guarantee
that Bible teaching will be given in the
schools. It is only proper.
I should be
very much surprised that even Roman
Catholics-whom we may look upon as
a Christian body——
Mr. CAMPBELL: Thanks.
Mr. JOSEPH
CUNNINGHAM--
that even they should object to Protestant children being given ordinary
Bible teaching at school. I want to go
further and say that the people of
Northern Ireland are determined after
the experience of the past few years, and
seeing how the Roman Catholic party
treated this scheme, to have a review,
and to have things set on a proper basis.
I do not think that it is at all proper that
the selection of teachers for Protestant
schools should be interfered with by
Roman Catholics of any class whether
laymen or clergymen. Furthermore, I
think it should be made perfectly clearand I do hope the Governraent, will make
it clear that only Protestant teachers
shall teach Protestant pupils. I think
that should be readily understood, because the people of Northern Ireland
have had an opportunity of registering
this determination at the recent election.
They have made it clear that nothing less
will satisfy them. Therefore, I do say
instead of the hon. Member having complained of unfair treatment of the section
he refers to, I think the Government were
unfair to the Protestants, and pandered
to the Roman Catholics for the purpose
of securing their co-operation.
Mr. McALLISTER: Who is tilting
at the Government now?
I
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM:
want to emphasise the fact that so far as
the Protestant people are concerned if
there has not been a great transfer of the
schools it has been simply because this
arrangement was being made and they
are not too ready on account of the want
of proper religious teaching. I am
satisfied that in the course of a very
short time when the Bill is properly
Amended the schools will be transferred
-at least such schools as have suitable
school accommodation. I am not intending to register my vote in favour of the
hon. and learned Member's Resolution.
I certainly will vote strongly against it.
Colonel PAKENHAM: As the hon.
and learned Senator who introduced this
Motion referred to me I would like to
make a few remarks about that subject.
Both he and I know perfectly well-and
we are sorry both of us to know-that
the children of his faith are very adverse
to going to Protestant schools. In fact
they will not go if they can possibly help
it. Therefore, in making the remarks I
did I was talking strictly of schools intended for Protestant children. I am
still very firmly of opinion that simple.
Bible teaching-I do not call that denominationalism-is necessary for the education of the children. If the hon. and
learned Senator and his faith do not like
it, of course, they are taking the way to
avoid it by not coming under the educational scheme. I am of opinion that
Protestant teachers should teach Protestant children. We know that Roman
Catholic teachers do not think in the same
way of religion, and they will not
educate Protestant children in the way
we would like. We like our own faith
just as much as Roman Catholics like
theirs, and I think we should insist on
getting the teaching we want. That is
all I have to say about the remarks of the
hon. Senator about me.
Education Policy
Education Policy
THE SENATE
[Colonel Pakenham]
I am sorry, like Senator Cunningham,
that he introduced this matter, because
when we introduce religion we generally
come to cross purposes. I am quite sure
the Government intends to do its best
for Roman Catholics. I do not think
they have been treated too badly.
Mr. McALLISTER: I do not intend
to follow the line of discussion adopted
by the hon. Senator (Mr. Joseph Cunningham) who sits behind me. I have
heard this matter discussed on many
occasions and never on any occasion have
I seen such a total lack of elementary
knowledge of the subject under discussion as has been displayed by the hon.
Senator. I do take exception to the
fact that on a question such as this.
vitally affecting the rising generation of
Northern Ireland, it should be suggested
that those of us who sit on this side of
the House should bring this contentious
matter before the House for the purpose
of scoring a debating point against the
Government. I think if there is anyone
tilting at the Government-although he
did not seem to appreciate the fact-it
was the hon. Senator who sits behind me,
because when this Measure was introduced
by the Government, time and again the
spokesmen of the Government on every
platform on which they spoke said it was
not to be altered by a single comma.
But now the hon. Senator who sits behind me tells me that at the last
election-I was not present at the meetings and he was-the fist was shaken at
the Government and they were brought
to heel. That is not my statement. That
is the statement of the hon. Senator who
sits behind me.
I
He has said that the Government got
no help in the administration of this Act
from the Roman Catholic body because
they did not transfer their schools.
only reply to that by asking how many
schools of all denominations-what percentage of schools at the end of two,
three, or four years after the passing of
this Act did transfer? Surely that is a
sufficient answer to the inept question
asked by the hon. Senator who sits behind me.
The hon. Senator also said he
was sure the Roman Catholic people were
out to take all the advantage of the
financial position under the Bill-
Mr.
They always do.
JOSEPH
CUNNINGHAM :
Mr. MCALLISTER: Surely the fact
that we would not transfer our schools
and run riot with our conscience in order
to get the two-thirds grant from the
building fund is an answer to the suggestion that we are out at all times to
take full advantage and reap the financial harvest. The hon. Senator has also
said as a tilt at us that the Irish people
were always out to plunder the Government. I reply to that by stating that a
financial commission set up by all sections
and all classes in England examined the
financial relations between England and
Ireland and found that in a normal year
we were robbed by two millions by your
British Government.
Mr.
JOSEPH
Poor lambs.
CUNNINGHAM ::
Mr. McALLISTER:
Now I will pass
to the Motion. We are not asking for
anything abnormal or unusual. I have
always discussed this question before in
the calmest possible manner, in the manner in which I think it should be discussed. We are asking nothing we have
not got authority for. A special committee was set up by the Government to
examine this question and formulate an
education scheme. We are only asking
that you put in operation the finding of
that committee. The Lynn Committee
recommended that for existing schools
for the purpose of building or extending
the contribution of a two-thirds grant
should still be continued. That is our
attitude, and that is our position to-day.
If the Government stand over the finding
of their own Committee, set up by themselves, upon which there was not a single
representative of these benches-if they
adopt the finding of that Committee this
matter will be practically adjusted.
I suggest that it is unfair to ask 34
or 35 per cent. of the people of Northern
Ireland to adopt a system which rightly
or wrongly from their point of view
conscientiously they cannot adopt. That
is not adopting an unusual position, because we are all aware that other churches
have conscientious objections from the
religious point of view to the present Bill.
And they have been very vocal in voicing
these objections, and substantiating their
position. Comparatively few of them
have agreed to transfer their schools.
And when they have conscientious objections to adopting the Bill as it stands I
admire them for not selling their convictions, or running riot with their con-
Education Policy
Education Policy
28 NOVEMBER 1929
WEB. 2.
science for the sake of a little pecuniary
advantage in the building and extension
of their schools. I have heard the Parliamentary Secretary of the Ministry of
Education state that the greatest impediment to the advancement of education in
Northern Ireland is the question of providing and erecting suitable school buildings. What is going to be the result if
this two-thirds grant for building is withheld? Can you expect those whom we
represent to contribute to the upkeep of
the schools of other denominations, and
at the same time provide, extend, and
improve their own schools?
I suggest
that that is an unfair proposition, and
one that should not be maintained.
Here you have these denominations in
Northern Ireland deprived of the advantage of a substantial part of the
Education Act. It is well known to
those of us who move about the country
that the cost of education is alarming a
great many people. I think it is
indefensible to lay it down that people
should either sell their conscience or run
riot with their conscience by adopting the
Measure the Government have passed, or
else let their schools be sacrificed. I hope
I have not added any heat or passion to a
debate in which no heat or passion should
be allowed to arise. After all whose interests are we considering to-day? It is
the interests of the young children of
Northern Ireland, and that, I suggest.
should be discussed in a logical and
reasonable way. That is the proper
spirit in which to approach this subject
if
you have the will to do so.
Viscount
CHARLEMONT:
Before
I reply to the Motion I would like to
remark that the speeches have been somewhat lengthy, and that a great many
points have been raised, more perhaps
than I can reply to to-day, but I hope
that if I omit to refer to any points
raised in the course of the debate hon.
Senators will put down a question for
answer at a later date. I quite agree
that the question of religion in the
schools ought to be debated without heat,
because it is a question in which the
conscience of the people is deeply involved, and it engenders heat without
our adding to it.
Dealing with the first part of the
Motion, there are at present something
over 1,900 public elementary schools
aided by the Ministry. About 400 of
these are provided and transferred
schools, and rather more than 100 are
carried on under Four-and-Two Committees. The remainder of the schools,
which amount to approximately 1,400,
are presumably the category to which the
Motion has reference. I would remind
the hon. and learned Member that there
are about 700 schools under Roman
Catholic clerical management, so that
about half of these 1,400 schools are
under the management of Protestant
clergymen or lay persons, and if there
is any differentiation in respect of this
class of school as compared with the
others that differentiation, whatever it
may be, is not a prerogative of the religious
denomination for which he speaks, but
is shared equally with other denominations. Whatever the charge of partiality against the Education Act may be,
it cannot, therefore, be argued from the
figures I have given that it is one that
relates solely to Roman Catholics.
But
The disadvantage in regard to grants
under which these schools suffer is, as
everybody knows, that they share only to
a limited extent in the funds administered
by the local education authorities. So
far as grants distributed by the Ministry from the parliamentary vote are
concerned and these grants make up
90 per cent. of the public expenditure on
elementary education in our Province--
these voluntary schools are in precisely
the same position as transferred schools.
They admit the control, and they observe
the regulations of the Ministry just in
the same way as all other schools.
they are unwilling to allow the local
authorities to have any jot or tittle of
authority over their schools, and it is
therefore obvious, I should think, that
they cannot justly receive the same
measure of local aid as those schools over
which the local authorities exercise full
jurisdiction. So far from this differentiation in grants, such as it is, being unfair, I should regard it as grossly unfair
if schools, in which the appointment and
dismissal of teachers, and the management generally are retained in the hands
of individual parties were to receive the
same assistance from the ratepayers as
those in which the control of the latter is
complete and assured.
I should like to remark, in reference to
what the hon. and learned Member said
about obtaining building grants from the
National Board, that the position under
the National Board was not quite
so assured as they assume. It is true
Education Policy
Education Policy
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
that the Board made building grants
amounting to two-thirds of the estimated
cost to vested schools, but the system was
for very
many years past strongly
criticised by the Treasury, and after the
War great difficulties were made to providing funds for such grants. In the
last years of the Board grants were available only in cases of the greatest urgency
and there was
no guarantee whatever
that funds would continue to be found
for this purpose.
The hon. and learned Member may say
that these individual managers have conscientious motives for refusing to admit
any measure of local control. I cannot
agree, however, that in the setting up
of a Four-and-Two Committee, in which
the managerial power remains with the
religious denomination and the trustees
and owners of the school, there can arise
any question of doing violence to religious.
convictions. In this connection I might
emphasise the fact that the voluntary
schools in England are bound to set up
a committee of a precisely similar type as
a condition of receiving any Government
assistance, but in no circumstances can
they receive any capital grants for buildings.
As this House is no doubt aware, the
voluntary bodies in England are, and
have been for some time past, applying
considerable pressure to the Government
to enable these schools to receive capital
grants and, in fact, to place them on precisely the same footing as they are at
present in Northern Ireland.
It is possible that the Roman Catholic authorities
in England would like something more.
but there is no doubt whatever that they
would be content for the time being with
the position as it is at present in
Northern Ireland. They would look
upon it as one step forward.
With regard to the second part of the
Motion. I cannot agree that the provision of what is known as simple Bible
teaching can be regarded as denominational religious education of a type which
is acceptable only to certain sections of
the population. For me to say that any
Church was not willing to accept the
Bible as a basis of religion would be for
me to assert that such a church was not
Christian, and I should be extremely
sorry to assert anything of the kind.
Surely the Bible is the historical foundation of all Christianity whether it be
Protestant or Catholic. Incidentally the
Bible is the foundation of the Jewish
belief.
I would like to refer for a moment to
the comments which the hon. and learned
Member made on the letter of Lord
Stanley to the National Board in 1831,
saying he did not approve of the determination to enforce in all schools the
reading of Holy Scripture without note
or comment Lord Stanley was referring
to the proposal of the National Board to
put it into the compulsory curriculum.
The question involved in this debate is
what is being put into the non-compulsory
curriculum, and that is an entirely
different matter.
Under the regulations of the Ministry
the Bible teaching that may be provided
by the local education authorities must
not contain anything distinctive of the
tenets of any religious denomination,
and must deal only with such fundamental portions of Christian belief as
are common to all. I understand that
such teaching forms a part, and of course
a very important part, of the religious
programme usually given in voluntary
schools of all denominations, including
Roman Catholic, in which it is permissible
for the managers to prescribe the fullest
degree of religious teaching that may be
suitable to the age and capacity of the
pupils, and I also know that most
ligious bodies would, if it were allowable.
extend their syllabuses of religious instruction to include a great deal more
than this simple Bible teaching of the
foundations of Christianity.
re-
It is, I am glad to note, the fact that
Protestant denominations generally are
willing to accept this modicum of religious
teaching as useful and desirable in the
schools without at the same time regarding it as complete and sufficient in itself
to satisfy fully their conceptions of an
adequate course of religious knowledge.
If the Roman Catholic attitude is unfavourable to admitting that this limited.
degree of religious instruction can be
accepted unless the full programme of
distinctive Catholic doctrine is taught, T
regret that it should be so, but I cannot
admit that this attitude justifies the
assertion that in providing simple Bible
teaching the system discriminates unfairly
against Roman Catholics.
Colonel
SHARMAN-CRAWFORD :
I am sorry that this debate has arisen,
245 Adoption of Children Bill-

28 NOVEMBER 1929
Second Reading
L
but I should like to give one instance in
my own town of Bangor where a Roman
Catholic School has been brought under
a Four-and-Two Committee. It is St.
Comgall's School, and I would like to
refer to its position to show how fairly
the Roman Catholic schools are treated
under the Education Act. They got a
two-thirds grant, and are now building a
new school, which is to be managed by
two members appointed by the Regional
Committee-Sir Thomas Wilson and Mr.
Fred J. Brice--and four members
nominated by the managers or trustees
-Very Rev. Canon Crolly, Belfast;
Rev. John O'Brien, P.P., Holywood; Mr.
W. P. O'Hara, Bangor; and Rev. P.
Scally, P.P. Therefore, the managers of
the school have four members on the
Committee against two nominated by the
Regional Committee. It seems to me
that nothing could be fairer than that,
and if the same principle was adopted in
the case of other schools there would be
a great deal more of that peace, love, and
harmony which we ought to have, but
which I am afraid will not be fostered
by debates such as this.
Question put, That this Motion be
agreed to.

Adoption of Children Bill.
Order for Second reading, read.
The ob-
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
ject of this Bill is to put on a proper
legal basis what is already a fairly common practice, namely, the adoption of
children. The present position
in
Northern Ireland is that this practice
has had no legal status. This was also
in Great Britain until 1926,
when an Act was passed by the Imperial
Parliament in the Bill now before the
House we are following generally that
Measure with certain adaptations and
Amendments which are considered to
make for improvement.
the case
The want of a legal basis in regard to
this practice of adoption has led at times
to considerable hardship in the past;
perhaps the most common instance of this
has been where the natural parents, who
had in the first instance consented to the
adoption of a child, have demanded the
return of the child when it had reached
an age at which it would be capable of
earning. In many such cases a natural
affection had sprung up between the child
and its adopted parents, and it was
certainly a hardship that after the child
Long, Mr. J. A.
Pakenham, Colonel H. A.
Dobbs, Colonel A. F.
Quigley, Mr. Andrew
had been reared and educated it could
be taken away again, perhaps against its
own wishes, by its natural parents. It is
hoped by means of the legislative sanction
which it is now proposed to give to the
system that desirable cases of adoption
may increase.
At the same time the House will, I
am sure, agree that it is most important
to take every precaution to ensure that
adoption will only be permitted when it
is in the best interests of all those concerned, and that no hasty or ill-considered cases are approved. You will
find in Clause I that it is necessary to
apply to the Court for an Adoption
Order, and Clause 2 gives some very
important restrictions which are imposed
on the making of such Orders. It will
be seen that under sub-section (1) of
Clause 2, the intending adopter must be
over twenty-five years of age, and must
be at least twenty-one years older than
the person adopted. This latter condition is, however, subject to a proviso
in the text of the Bill now before the
House. This proviso states (on page 4.
line 6) that:
where the applicant, or in the
case of joint applicants being spouses,
247 Adoption of Children Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Viscount Charlemont]
one of the applicants and the infant
are, or would be if the infant were of
the opposite sex, within the prohibited
degrees of consanguinity, it shall be
lawful for the Court, if it thinks fit,
to make the Order, notwithstanding
that the applicant is less that twentyone years older than the infant.”
That is to say, the rule regarding the
difference of twenty-one years in the ages
can only be relaxed when the infant and
adopter come within the prohibited degrees of consanguinity. There was some
discussion on this proviso of the Bill in
another place, and I would like to inform
the House that the Government intend
to move an Amendment at a later stage
so that this may be relaxed somewhat;
it is proposed to make the proviso rather
more general, and to give power to the
Court to allow an exception to this rule
as to a difference of twenty-one years in
ages, where it is satisfied that there are
special circumstances which would justify
it.
Clause 2 of the Bill prescribes further
restrictions in the remaining sub-sections;
sub-section (2) deals with the particular
case where the sole applicant is a male,
and the adopted child a female, and subsection (3) requires the consent of the
parent or guardian, except, of course,
where the child has been deserted, or
where the parent or guardian has manifestly refused to support it, these
matters being for the Court to decide.
The remaining Clauses of the Bill
hardly call for much comment except
that I might perhaps refer to another
safeguard which appears in Clause 3
and 9, whereby no monetary transactions
can take place between the parties unless
under certain conditions approved by the
Court.
Clause 6 gives the Court power to make
a temporary order for a probationary
period, which should be valuable where
it is not absolutely satisfied at the time
that a permanent order should be made.
Clause 10 deals with the existing de
facto adoptions. If a child has, in fact,
been adopted for a period of not less than
two years before the commencement of
the Act, the Court, after consideration
of all the circumstances, may make an
Adoption Order on the application of
the adopters notwithstanding the fact
that the applicant is a male, and the
adopted person a female, and without
the requirement as to the consent of the
parent or guardian.
By Clause 11 the Registrar-General is
required to keep a register of adopted
children with full particulars.
I hope the House will agree that this
Bill serves a good purpose, and that it
should help to make this practice of
adoption better for all parties concerned
I beg to move.
In
I
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM:
would just like to ask the Leader of the
House a question about the section
where a register is to be kept. In the
register the date of the birth of the child
is to be put down. If that register is
open for public inspection without any
reserve one could very easily get the
address where the child comes from.
many cases where a child is adopted we
want to break off connection between the
natural family and the adopted family.
I think if the age of the child could be
registered in a private register which
would only be open to inspection if
reasons were shown, it would make the
Bill answer the purpose intended.
Mr. LESLIE: I rise with great
pleasure to welcome this Bill.
I may
venture to say I think that I have some
right to welcome the Bill, because as long
ago as 1924 when the Industrial
Assurance Bill was before us our old
Friend Senator Greer and myself made
some endeavour to get something done in
order to promote, and regulate, and
establish adoption. That particular
season, or occasion was not suitable and
we could not press the matter at that
time.
Such a Bill as this has been long
desired. We have long wanted something
that would enable us to establish-not
the relation of ward and guardian but
the relation of parent and child, and
that is what we are now. I think, going
to get. Hitherto, apart from those adoptions to which boards of guardians had
powers of consent, to which I must refer
presently, we had no provision at all that
legalised adoption. The Bill before us
is somewhat wider than the old Roman
law dealing with adoption; it is somewhat wider than the modern French
law both of which appear to require the
condition of childlessness on the part of
the adopter.
249 Adoption of Children Bill-

28 NOVEMBER 1929
Second Reading
The Bill, as the noble Viscount has
told us, is modelled on the English Bill
which received the Royal Assent in
August, 1926. There are some trivial
modifications. The Bill does not give
the power of sanction to benches of
magistrates as is done in England. In
that I venture to suggest that the Bill
steers a wise course. We are embarking
on a new sea-an uncharted sea. It is
better that, in the beginning, at all
events, we should be careful, and restrict
the power of sanction. If necessary we
can at a later date extend it if we find
there is any demand for an extension of
this power.
For myself, I believe, and
I have strongly maintained in this House,
that the courts of quarter sessions sit
with sufficient frequency in Northern Ireland to meet all reasonable requirements.
I, therefore, support the exclusion of the
magistrates, and I should like to say a
special word of approval of the provision
that applications under this Act may be
made otherwise than in open court.
And again, I was glad to hear the
noble Viscount lay particular stress on
the prohibition of any monetary consideration in this matter of adoption. I
think too that the provisions as regards a special register for adoptions,
which are to found in Clause 11, will be
efficient, and will prevent-or certainly
will not assist, if not absolutely prevent
any person finding the origin of the child.
I cannot but regard this Bill as something
in the nature of a gift horse, and, therefore, I do not want either this afternoon,
or at a later stage, to look it in the
mouth more than seems necessary. I do
not propose to lay stress on the limitation
in the Bill to infants who have never
been married. I conclude it is the
deliberate intention of the Government to
adopt the English view that the patria
potestas must have been exercised once
or the infant could not be legally married
and that the power, like the sting of a
wasp, is exhausted by one exercise, and is
incapable of resurrection
I do not want to say anything about the
provisions towards the end of the second
Clause as regards limiting the adoption
orders to persons resident and domiciled
in Northern Ireland. That cuts both
ways. It will exclude from the power to
adopt persons normally domiciled in
Northern Ireland or in the Free State.
On the other hand it will, as I understand
it, retain the control of our Courts, and
there is nothing in the Bill, and nothing
can be put in the Bill, which would ensure what I may refer to as a kind of international courtesy between the different
islands making up what are called the
British Isles as regards mutual recognition
of adoptions which have taken place elsewhere. It is not a question of Great
Britain. The English Act does not apply
to Scotland, but as regards England and
Wales and Northern Ireland I presume
that adoption orders will be recognised.
in just the same way as an adoption order
in one State in America is recognised by
the other States. I do not want to direct
further attention to that matter.
It seems to me that the Bill stands in
but little need of alteration. There is one
point I want to lay stress on, but I am
not sure that the noble Leader of the
House has not anticipated me, although I
shall have an Amendment on the Paper.
I was going to point out that in the case
of de facto adoption it will be idle to insist
on the 21 years limit, and the noble Viscount spoke of making alteration in the
main part of the Bill in that respect. I
regard that alteration as the removal of a
possible obstacle and not as a reduction of
a safeguard. The difficulty of age has
been dealt with differently under different
regimes. Under Roman law it appears to
have been 18 years, in France 15, and in
Germany it is 18, but I have no doubt that
the best rule and the best answer will be
found in the discretion of the Courts.
I now pass from what is in the Bill to
something that is not in the Bill, and that
is a direct reference as to how this Bill is
going to affect boards of guardians and
their powers.
I spoke of that at the commencement of my speech. It is quite
plain that boards of guardians are included
as a body in sub-clause (3) of Clause 1, and
again in sub-clause (4) of Clause 8. That
is a body that may give consent to the
adoption of a child under their control.
But it is not, I am afraid, quite clear, that
for the purpose of this Bill a board of
guardians is a local authority. I shall ask
the House in Committee to make that quite
plain, because it seems to me necessary
that a board of guardians should be recognised as a local authority which can
appointed to act as a guardian ad
litem, owing to the fact that
law as regards illegitimate children
is somewhat different to that in England, and also because I think that
when the expression "local authority" is
used here we are in the habit of thinking
of rate-making authorities such as county
be
our
251 Adoption of Children Bill

THE SENATE
Betting Bill
[Mr. Leslie]
councils and councils of urban districts,
and not of boards of guardians, which are
not generally thought of as local bodies.
unless they are specially made so.
But it is not so much the question of
boards of guardians acting as guardians
ad litem that I am speaking. I mean more
to refer the House to the Poor Law Acts,
1899, and others, which give boards of
guardians power akin to adoption. That
is the power of declaring themselves in
control of a child under certain circumstances. That at the most is only power
to adopt and it is the antithesis of this
Bill, and goes no further than ward and
guardian. But there is, further,
stronger power, and that is the power
of a board of guardians to consent to
the adoption of a child by some other
person.
Whatever is to be understood
a
by that power it is perfectly clear it is not
the power under this Act, and such adoption will not effect the consequences of
adoption under this Act.
But it seems to me that unless we make
in this Bill some reference to these powers
the confusion in the public mind will be
endless. People will not know whether it
is intended that the old power should stand
or whether it is not. I believe it is intended here, as in England and Wales,
that the old power should stand, and I
believe it is hoped that in many of the
cases where it has been exercised, adoption
will come forward for regulation under the
Act as de facto adoption. However, even
at the cost of being accused of inserting
padding in the Bill we ought to make it
clear.
There remains the problem of having
safeguards. The Bill is full of safeguards,
but there is one omitted. Let us suppose that application has been made to
the Court for the adoption of a child
under the control of a board of guardians
by a certain person, and the Court has
seen fit to refuse to make the necessary
order. In that case that board of
guardians should, I think, be prohibited
from consenting to the adoption outside
the Act of that particular child, and they
should not be allowed to commit that
child to the custody of that person. The
difficulty must have been foreseen in
England, and it has, as a matter of fact,
been met in a rather roundabout manner,
a reference to which I will give the House
at a later date. It seems to me that the
best way to meet the question would be
to put a somewhat comprehensive Clause
on the Paper in order that we may, when
the time comes, consider whether anything ought really to be done, and if so,
arrive at a suitable form of words.
I do not think the discussion in Com
mittee will be of any great length. On
the whole—in fact, not only on the whole
but in detail as well, I most cordially
support this Bill, and I look forward to
the great pleasure and satisfaction it will
give to the people of this Province, who
have long looked forward to it.
On Question, Motion (That the Bill be
now read a Second Time) agreed to
Bill committed to a Committee of the
whole House for Wednesday next.
Betting (Juvenile Messengers) Bill.
Order for Second reading read.
Viscount BANGOR: The purpose of
this Bill is to ensure that children under
eighteen years of age will not be used for
conveying or delivering betting messages.
I do not think that it needs any elaborate
explanation. There is a pretty general
feeling that children should be protected
against becoming familiarised at an early
age with betting. You will notice that
in sub-section (3) of Clause 1 the age
limit is given as eighteen years. In the
first draft of the Bill as presented in
another place this was sixteen years, but
the Government thought it proper to
accept an Amendment raising it to
eighteen.
I hope that these proposals will commend themselves to right hon. and hon
Members. I beg to move.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I think the
principle of this Bill is a good one.
There is however, one point in the Bill
to which I would like to direct attention.
It may be that a man may accept a bet
from a person whom he has reasonable
and proper cause to think is over eighteen
years of age. We know that in connection with the Licensing Code, where
a defendant is prohibited from selling
drink to children, the legislature was
careful to say that an offence would not
be committed unless the defendant did
it knowingly, or sold it to a person
apparently under the age. Under this Bill
a person might commit an offence quite
innocently, believing bona fide that the
person bringing the message was over 18
years of age. In the interests of fair
play I would strongly appeal to the
Betting Bill-
Second Reading
28 NOVEMBER 1929
Noble Lord to consider whether some
Amendment should not be made to the
Bill to meet this point.
Mr. LONG: I do not like the words
on pages 19 and 20 of the Bill where it
says, "who in any manner holds himself
out, or permits himself to be held out."
I think those words are crude and undignified, and I would prefer to say
"purposes or professes to be." I quite
admit the words in the Bill are easily
understood and the point is not important, but perhaps in the Committee stage.
we might substitute some
euphonious expression. I am afraid it.
will be very difficult to obtain a conviction, because bookmakers' touts are
very astute and wary, and it would be
very difficult to detect offences of this
nature.
more
I understand that in rural districts
which adjoin towns, and boroughs, and
cities, there is a custom that if a person
wishes to make a bet he uses as messengers schoolboys, and in odd cases school
girls, to convey these bets to the bookmakers. In some cases the bookmakers
meet the buses at the depots, and în
other cases they go out a little way into
the country and get on the buses which
convey the school children and there receive these bets. It can readily be understood that this practice will familiarise
these messengers with betting procedure
and it will have a demoralizing effect
upon them.
As soon as these young persons have money of their own, or indeed,
in some cases, as soon as they can steal
or embezzle money, they will indulge in
this practice of betting, and in some cases
it may start them on a criminal career.
I therefore think the Bill will do some
good and that it is worth a trial.
I understand that a very highly
placed Member of one of the two legislatures of Northern Ireland sent a juvenile messenger to his club to say that he
would take part in a sweepstake on the
Derby. The result was that he won about
£45. Since then his conscience has been
troubling him and he has almost made
up his mind to disgorge his ill-gotten
gains. I think that that feeling is prevalent throughout the country, and the
public are determined to discourage this
evil which has such disastrous effects. My
only regret about this Bill is that it does
not apply to persons of all ages as well as
those up to 18 years.
Mr. BARCLAY: I rise to congratulate the Government on bringing in such
a Bill as this. It is a Bill that is long
overdue so far as the young people of the
City of Belfast are concerned. It is bad
enough for the parents to gamble and
put money on horses when they cannot
afford it. But to my mind it is worse
when they send children with it to the
bookmakers. It is bound to have a demoralising effect on the children.
A few weeks ago I happened to be
going along a certain street in Belfast,
and I saw a crowd of boys, perhaps twenty
of them. I asked a friend who was along
with me where these boys were goingwere they going on a picnic? He replied:
"Do you not know? It is to the penny
bookmaker." We have penny bookmakers in the City of Belfast. These
men are there and they are taking pennies from boys and girls day after day
when there is a race on. More than that,
their mothers are sending them with the
penny or twopence to the bookmaker,
and he receives this money. Of course,
if the horse wins they get
the same
price, 2 to 1, 5 to 1, or 10 to 1 or whatever it is. I am very pleased that this
Bill will do away with that kind of thing.
I asked
my friend where were the
police and he said some of the police were
just as bad as the youngsters. Senator
Long referred to the difficulty of putting
this Bill into force. We are depending
upon the police, and I think something
will have to be done with them also.
have great pleasure in supporting the
Second reading of the Bill. I hope the
influence of the Bill will have a great
effect on the rising generation, and instead of getting money for nothing, these
young people will be brought up to work
for their money.
I
Mr. LESLIE: This seems to be a
juveniles' afternoon. I have just had
the pleasure of supporting a Measure,
which I think will do a great deal of good.
I am afraid we are now approaching a
Measure, which, if we had the courage
of our real convictions, we should decline
to read a Second time. I think I will
make the necessary Motion to the end
that the Bill may be postponed.
course, I know I shall be held up as a
vessel of wrath predestined to destruction,
corrupt, and all sorts of things. That
does not matter. What does matter is
what will be said of this House if we do
pass this Bill. Shall we not be told we
Of
Betting Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
have lent ourselves to a nasty little bit of
class legislation, that we have added
another inefficiency to the already hopelessly complicated and unworkable code
which nobody desires to work?
As a matter of fact there has not been,
so far as I know, any great public demand for this Bill. I do not think the
public want it. What I think the public
really do want is pretty much this: They
wish that those in authority should from
time to time intimate their disapproval
of betting and gambling, but they do not
want to deprive the poor bookmaker of
his living. They want the establishments
of the bookmakers to whom they can resort when they desire, but for the sake
of appearances they like these establishments periodically raided by the policedue notice being given. That is really
what the public do want, because everybody wants to bet a little bit.
I am not going into the ethics of gambling. I agree that the sin of gambling
lies in losing money by it. I am quite
sure that is the view we entertain. But
I do not think, and of course, I could not
think that it is desirable that children
should be brought into close touch with
betting at an early age. I reflect with
satisfaction at times on that excellent
statute of the sixth year of Edward VII,
which prohibits children betting with
pennies on the public streets, and makes
it an offence for anybody to bet with them.
I sympathise very much with the disappointment of Senator Barclay's friend
when he asked for the police. The police
have powers on the statute book. They
do not want this at all. There might be
something in it if it could be said to tend
to the better bringing up of children, but
I am afraid it will not.
a
It seems to me that the title of the
Bill is good, but the execution of it is
extremely faulty. Nobody has yet said
a good word in favour of the details of the
Bill. I am afraid I must ask the House
to look at it and see what one-sided
Measure it is. Before I do that let me
remind the House of the history of it.
It is founded on a Bill passed by the Imperial Parliament at the instance of a
private Member, and limited in its application to Scotland. They have had the
Bill in Scotland for about 15 months.
They have not yet adopted it in England.
I am inclined to think it might be wise to
wait and see how they get on in Scotland
-to wait until they adopt it in England.
It is a little dangerous, I think, to rush
into imitation of a Scottish Bill, which
is, as I say, a private Bill, and not a
public Measure. And now let us look
at the one-sided nature of the Measure as
it comes before us. It deals with people
sending messengers to bookmakers. There
is nothing here about bookmakers. The
bookmaker can have the children to tout
for him. I do not know if that is the
intention of the framers of the Bill.
I scarcely understand the first part of
the Clause. I do not know what advice
the learned counsel in front of me intends
to tender to the House, but I find great
difficulty in construing the first part of
sub-clause (1) the prohibition of sending
a note to a bookmaker asking him to put
money on for him. It seems to me that
the first part of that sub-section is limited
to some communication in relation to a
bet which has already been made, because
on account of the large number of words
that come between the phrase relating
to that and the expression, "making of
any bet or wager," I cannot myself
very readily connect the two. That.
I think, will put to serious trouble the
like
people, who,
my friend, the
Chairman of Londonderry County Coun-
I
cil, will have to administer the Act.
think when it becomes an Act they will
find it difficult. You can, I think, send
a note to put money on under this Act.
Passing on I see, as is not infrequently
the way with people sometimes called
cranks, who rush in to get their own
ideas on the Statute Book-I find they
resort to the minimum fine. That is
always objectionable. There is no minimum fine in the Football Act. Then I
do not see how you are going to prove a
previous conviction in any part of Northern Ireland, except at very great
trouble and some expense, and I do not see
what answer there is, if, as the learned
Counsel in front of me suggests, the defence is raised of reasonable cause to believe the messenger was over the age of
18. Nor do I quite see how this Bill will
assist the young people who are clerks and
so forth, of about 18 years of age, to get
employment.
Then we come to the final passage in
Sub-clause (3), to which I would direct
attention. That is the exclusion of officers
from the Post Office from the Bill. Needless to say, an officer of the Post Office
in the execution of his duty is engaged
in a reserved service, and we cannot touch
him. We could not put him in the Bill.
Betting Bill-
28 NOVEMBER 1929
Petroleum Bill
Therefore, the morals of the young Post
Office boy have to be left outside the Bill.
We cannot touch that problem at all. The
Imperial Parliament, which passed this
Bill for Scotland, could have touched
them, and they did not do it. Could there
be a greater example of the humbug of the
position? They could have saved the
morals of the Post Office boys, and they
did not do it. May I suggest that a possible reason why the Post Office boys were
excluded is that these messages were
conveyed in closed envelopes? I
expect that this Bill is principally
directed against things
closed envelopes. It
not sent in
seems a pity
that the matter should not have been
made clear. I cannot see that this
Measure is really wanted. I think it
would be better if the Bill is not thrown
out, that it should be adjourned until we
see how they get on with it in Scotland,
and whether they adopt it in England.
Mr. DORMAN: I rise to express my
approval of this particular Measure-not
probably from the same reason that the
Government is promoting it. I welcome
it in the first instance as a recognition
on the part of the Government of the right
of the State to interfere with the freedom
of the individual. I also recognise it as
an acknowledgment on the part of the
Government of its right to interfere with
the incentive to gain, because this social
evil, which every right-thinking man deplores, the extension of betting amongst
the youth of the community, is nothing
more or less than an expression of that
incentive to gain which the supporters of
the Government time and again tell those
of my political opinions is absolutely necessary in the interests of society as a whole.
I, therefore, welcome this.
I do not think there is any more evil in
a working man putting a shilling on in
the hope of getting 20s-it is the same
incentive which induces the working man
to try to get five or twenty times his money
as induces the richer man to go on the
Stock Exchange with his hundreds or
thousands in the hope of a rise in shares
and immediately disposing of them. I do
not wish to occupy the time of the House,
but I do welcome this Bill as an acknowledgment on the part of the Government
that they are coming to recognise
the despised philosophy of Socialism
(Laughter).
Mr. SPEAKER: Does the hon.
Member, Mr. Leslie, move the rejection?
Mr. LESLIE: I do not move.
On Question, Motion (That the Bill be
read a Second time), agreed to.
Petroleum (Consolidation) Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount BANGOR: As can be seen
from the Title, this is a Bill that consolidates existing Acts in regard to
petroleum-the Petroleum Act of 1871,
the Petroleum Act of 1879, and our own
Petroleum (Amendment) Act of 1929. It
has been before the Joint Committee of
both Houses, and is now in the final form
as recommended by that Committee. In
Consolidation Bills it is not possible to
amend the existing law, but merely to
bring together the various enactments on
the subject, so that the law may be cleared
and in a more intelligible form. I think
it can be claimed that this has now been
done. I beg to move.
Colonel SHARMAN-CRAWFORD: I
would like to intervene for a moment, because this is a question that has been
before the County Councils of the Six
Counties. Of course, this is only a Consolidation Bill, but it refers to licences.
although it does not say who is to issue
the licences. At the present time the
licence duty is a dead letter. It used to
be in the hands of the police, and then the
county councils suddenly got a memorandum to say they were to take over the
duty of issuing licences for the storage of
petroleum. We got no instructions or
regulations of any kind, and as far as
I know, up to the present the Down
County Council has not issued a single
licence. When we remonstrated about
receiving no instructions we were told that
the procedure was the same as in the
English Act, which puts the duty of
issuing licences on the urban district
councils. I think the matter ought to be
cleared up, and that these licences
should be issued by the urban councils,
as in England, or that the police should
inform the county councils, when a licence
is applied for, whether or not it should
be granted. If something in this direction is not done I shall be obliged to move
an Amendment in Committee.
Mr. LONG: With reference to the
remarks of the right hon. and gallant
Gentleman, I quite admit that a tremendous fuss was made about this matter by
the county councils of Down and Antrim,
E
Petroleum Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Long]
who made mountains out of molehills. In
the county with which I am connected,
when the duty of issuing licences was
transferred to the county council, they
adopted the procedure of inviting the
opinion of the constabulary in the different
areas as to whether the applicant was a
fit and proper person to have a licence.
That practice has worked very smoothly
and without any trouble whatever. That
is my own experience as chairman of the
county council, but I also consulted the
secretary of the county council to see if
he had had any trouble, and his reply was
that it was negligible.
In the future County Councils can operate this Bill, if it becomes an Act, without any trouble whatever. They will be
able to consult the members of the
R.U.C. in the future, as they have been
able to do in the past. There is nothing
in the Bill to preclude them from appointing Sergeant Brown in Ballymoney to
make a report to the Council on all applications that come from that district, to
appoint Sergeant Smith to make reports
on applications for County Derry, and so
on. Regulations are to be issued, and
under these it will be competent for County
Councils to appoint these police to make
reports.
Col. SHARMAN-CRAWFORD: But
they have not been issued.
Mr. LONG: That is a matter I wish
to complain about, not in this single instance only, but in regard to all Bills
I think that every regulation made under
a Bill should be placed on the Table of
this House at the time the Bill is being
discussed, because the feeling prevails
that very often these Regulations go further than the Members of the Senate
intended they should go, and that it is
legislation by Regulation. That is a most
objectionable thing. I should certainly
have preferred, as the right hon.
and gallant Gentleman has suggested,
that the Regulations should have been
ready for submission to this House,
but I understand that
of the
Regulations is definitely settled. That
is that the County Councils can have
the advice of the R.U.C., and in that
case no difficulty will arise.
one
Mr. McALLISTER: I had no intention of intervening in this discussion, but
I must dissociate myself from the method
of procedure suggested by Senator Long.
After all, I think there should be some
final method-as to how and under what
circumstances licences should be issued.
If there is not some such method there
will be chaos and notwithstanding the
superior attitude Senator Long has taken
up, with regard to Down and Antrim,
I think the conduct of those two counties
in this particular matter is fully justified.
and the conduct of Derry is worthy of
the utmost condemnation. Down and
Antrim wanted to discharge their duty.
but Derry shirks its duty, and wants to
delegate it to police officers. I do not
think that is a course of conduct that
will commend itself to this House, and
I really think that in order to protect
Derry against itself this House should
insist that in this Bill there should be a
definite method laid down for the issue
of these licences.
Mr. LESLIE: I do not intend to make
a speech on this Bill, although it is a
serious Bill. But I want, as a member
of the Joint Committee to which the Bill
was referred, to express our indebtedness
to those concerned in the preparation of
the Bill for the admirable marginal notes.
As on
a former occasion, great trouble
must have been taken, and it was of the
greatest possible assistance in comparing
the draft of the Consolidation Bill with
the Acts which form the existing laws.
As regards the special point made by the
right hon. and gallant Member (Col.
Sharman-Crawford), I may remark that
he will find what he needs in the second
section of the Consolidating Bill, and he
would have found a good deal more about
it if he had been with us early in the year,
when the Petroleum (Amendment) Act,
1929, was before us. After a careful
perusal of Clause 2, and the previous
enactments referred to in the margin, I
do not think there can be any difficulty
about it.
On Question, Motion (That the Bill be
now read a Second time) agreed to.
Adjournment.
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
move,
I beg to
"That this House do now adjourn
until Tuesday," when I hope to take the
Second Readings of two Bills, and the
Committee stages of some that have been
before us this afternoon.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
