Special Order
Motor Accidents
3 DECEMBER 1929
THE SENATE.

Tuesday, 3rd December, 1929.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
Papers Presented to Parliament.
Ministry of Education:
Regulations for Staffing Public Elementary Schools (Northern Ireland),
1928, Amending Regulations, No. 1,
1929.
Education, Pupil Teachers, Regulations, 1929, Amending Regulations,
No. 1,
1929.
Laid before the House (pursuant to
Act), and ordered to lie on the Table.
Coalisland and District Electricity
Special Order.
Viscount BANGOR: I beg to move,
"That the Special Order made by the
Electricity Commissioners for Northern
Ireland, under the Electricity (Supply)
Acts, 1882 to 1919, and confirmed by the
Ministry of Commerce under the
Electricity (Supply) Act, 1919, in respect
of the townlands of Brackaville and
Gortnaskea, and parts of the townland of
Annagher, Derry, Derrywinnen Glebe,
Gortonis and Gortin, all in the County of
Tyrone, be hereby approved."
This Special Order, granted_by_the
Electricity Commissioners for Northern
Ireland and confirmed by the Ministry of
Commerce, will confer upon the Coalisland and District Electricity Supply
Company, Limited, of Coalisland,
statutory authority to supply electrical
energy for public and private purposes
in the townlands of Brackaville and Gortnaskea, and parts of the townlands of
Annagher, Derry, Derrywinnen Glebe,
Gortgonis and Gortin, in the County of
Tyrone. The Rules of Procedure have
been complied with, and the Order
follows the usual form. I beg to move.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Prevention of Motor Accidents.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to ask the
Leader of the House whether, in view of
the disquieting persistence of motor
accidents, the Government will regard
the safety of users of the high roads,
whether pedestrians or motorists, by
taking into consideration the expediency
of legislation-
(1) to vest in authorised officers
power to stop and examine at any time
motor vehicles of all classes on the
highways, to see whether their brakes
are in working order, and
(2) to regulate the working hours of
drivers and conductors of public service
vehicles on lines similar to those which
prevail in the railway industry.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): Every possible step of an
administrative or legislative character is
being taken by the Government with the
object of reducing the number of accidents on the public highways in Northern
Ireland, but care has to be taken in these
matters that the public are not being put
to any unnecessary inconvenience, and
that the measures taken are such as can
be entirely justified or deemed to be
effective.
As the hon. and learned Member is
aware, Parliament has already accorded
powers to the Ministry of Home Affairs
through its duly authorised officers, to
examine all public service vehicles carrying passengers for hire, and this power
is being freely availed of with advantageous results. At the same time it should
be borne in mind that a proposal to extend this power to cover all motor
vehicles is one that, in the first instance, would involve an additional staff
of inspectors, and, further, is one that
would be likely to be strongly opposed by
the general public, having regard to the
inconvenience and embarrassment which
would be caused to drivers of ordinary
motor vehicles in cases where the vehicle
was stopped on the roads for the purpose
of public inspection. It appears to the
Government that this inconvenience and
embarrassment would probably far outweigh any advantage that might be derived by way of reducing risks on the
public highway, and in the circumstances
the Government see no reason at present
for accepting such a principle.
With regard to the question of the regulation of the working hours of drivers
Dairy Produce Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
and conductors of public service vehicles,
some advantage might be derived from
the introduction of such a principle, but
it must be remembered that if such a
principle were introduced it would tend
to embarrass, probably serious in some
cases, proprietors of public service
vehicles who have services in operation
fixed to meet the needs of the public.
The principle would involve considerable
administrative and technical difficulties
which would have to be carefully considered in all their bearings, and while
being thoroughly spmpathetic with the
principle it is impossible, therefore, for
the Government to make any definite
statement on the matter at the moment.
Unemployment Insurance (State
Contribution) Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The genesis
of this Bill is the recommendation of the
Imperial Blanesburgh Committee that the
State contribution to the Unemployment
Fund should be one-half of the combined
contributions of employers and workpeople. At present the proportion is
something like 40 per cent. Apart from
the merits of the proposal it is essential
that the Bill should be enacted by the
Parliament of Northern Ireland under
the Re-insurance Agreement relating to
the Unemployment Fund. It is estimated that the increased provision for
State contribution will cost the Northern
Exchequer the sum of £70,000 this year.
I beg to move.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Marketing of Dairy Produce Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: This Bill
is a further instalment of the Government's policy of assisting Northern Ireland agriculture to adjust itself to modern
conditions. As a surplus-producing area
we are dependent on the export market,
and the special requirements of this
market demand our close and constant
attention. The ascendancy in the British
markets formerly enjoyed by foreign
competitors was due to the organisation
of their marketing methods with particular reference to carefulness in packing,
strictness in grading and within the
grades, uniformity of quality. Rigorous
insistence on these cardinal points made
possible large-scale transactions without
prior inspection of consignments by the
consignee. In short there was established
between buyer and seller a firm confidence
that resulted in a great economy of time
and trouble in the conduct of business.
In any contemplated attack on this
ascendancy one thing stands out clearly,
and that is that we must fight our competitors with their own admirable
weapons. That was the basic fact underlying the Marketing of Eggs Acts, and the
operation of those Acts has proved conclusively that organisation on these lines
has paid and paid handsomely.
The Bill falls into five parts. Part 1
deals with the registration of all premises
used as auxiliary creameries, central
creameries, butter factories and wholesale
depots from which butter is shipped. In
dealing with butter the suitability of the
premises and plant is all-important, and
the conditions relating to registration
have, accordingly, been set out in some
detail in Clause 2. They include suitability of premises, machinery, equipment and utensils, safeguards against contamination of butter, milk and cream,
and qualifications of the staff employed
at creameries. An annual fee of £1 is
charged for registration.
In order to avoid undue dislocation in
the business in any premises that should
be registered, arrangements have been
made in Clause 3 for provisional
registration. The cancellation of regis
tration by the Ministry is provided for,
and any person aggrieved by a decision of
the Ministry to cancel a registration may
appeal to the county court.
or
Part 2 of the Bill provides that butter
cream may not be exported except
under licence. The object of this proposal is to ensure that the quality,
methods of packing, and the manner of
marking of packages will be in accordance with such standards as may be prescribed by the Ministry. In regard to
the markings to be prescribed, it is proposed that they will not interfere with
the lawful use of any private marks or
brands.
A point of interest in connection with
licences is that it is the duty of a licensed
creamery to send samples to any centre
the Ministry may fix. This is in continuation of the Ministry's surprise
butter inspections which have hitherto been
265 Dairy Produce Bill-

3 DECEMBER 1929
Second Reading
worked on a voluntary basis, and which
have been most useful as a means of
collecting information as to the quality
of butter produced, and of rectifying
faults in manufacture.
'Part 3 of the Bill provides safeguards
against the presence of adulterants in
registered premises, and against their
being added to the butter or cream. The
principle of this provision is not new. It
is already enacted
as respects butter
factories in the Sale of Food and Drugs
Acts, and it has now been incorporated
here to make it applicable to all registered
premises. If unclean, contaminated, or
stale milk is offered to a creamery, the
person offering it shall be guilty of an
offence as shall also the occupier of the
creamery if such milk is accepted. This
provision is most important and cuts at
the root of one of the most serious weaknesses of the creamery system. It will be
appreciated that the creamery manager
cannot always refuse unsuitable milk. He
has to think of the good-will of the
suppliers. Good butter cannot be made
from dirty milk. The limit of moisture
allowable in butter prepared for sale at a
registered premises is fixed at 16 per
cent. This is the application of a further
provision of the Sale of Food and Drugs
Acts to registered premises.
Under Part 4 officers of the Ministry
are empowered to enter premises, or premises which should be registered, for the
purposes of examination and sampling.
In registered premises samples will be
taken in the presence of the manager or
other person employed on the premises,
and a part of the sample left with the
manager or person. The result of examination of the sample will also be communicated to the occupier of the premises.
Inspection during transit is also provided.
for. The arrangements for ensuring
that such inspections are carried out with
the minimum of inconvenience to the
owners of the produce are set out in
some detail.
The usual provision empowering the
Ministry to make Rules is included in
this part of the Bill. Such Rules are to
be laid before both Houses of Parliament and may be rendered of no effect
on a resolution, within the statutory
period, of either House. There are one
or two points of interest in the arrangements for prosecution of offences and
penalties. A prosecution for an offence.
may not be instituted except by or with
the consent of the Ministry. A maximum
penalty is provided for, but an offence
shall not, however, be regarded as a
second offence unless the former offence
was against the provisions of the same
part of the Act. As in the Marketing of
Eggs Acts an offence against the Rules is
regarded as an offence against the Act. It
is specially provided, however, that fines
for offences against the Rules shall not be
less than £1, and not more than £20 for
each offence. In other words the sliding
scale of fines does not apply to breaches
of the Rules.
In Part 5 of the Bill has been included
provision for the occupier of registered.
premises to keep such records and to
furnish such returns in relation to prices,
quantities, etc., as may be prescribed by
the Ministry. Without full information
as to the extent and nature of the trade
carried on in registered premises the
Ministry is obviously not in the position
to give the maximum assistance in marketing the produce. The information so
provided will, of course, be regarded as
confidential.
It is proposed to conserve the designation "creamery butter" to butter made
at premises registered as central creameries
when used in connection with butter
produced in and despatched from Northern Ireland. Similarly the use of the
word creamery in relation to premises is
restricted to registered premises. The
operative date of the Acts is 1st January,
1931. There is a good deal of preliminary work to be done before the Act
can be brought into force, particularly in
connection with the registration of premises. I beg to move.
Mr. MCALLISTER: I have always
approached all Bills dealing with agriculture in Northern Ireland from the
point of view that while other industries
are of substantial and material benefit to
Northern Ireland, agriculture is the only
industry indispensable to the people individually and collectively. I think it
goes without saying that by the success or
failure of agriculture the State is bound
to swim or sink. From that point of
view I cannot enthusiastically praise this
particular Bill, because in my opinion,
whilst it can do little harm, it can do
little good. I think it is the duty of the
Government to give a lead in this particular matter. I know that some hon.
Members will say the farmers of
Northern Ireland are a difficult people to
organise. With that view I quite agree,
267 Dairy Produce Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Mr. McAllister]
but I say that what has been done in
other countries can surely be done in
Northern Ireland. Are we, either as a
Parliament or as a people, to take the
stand that we are an inferior people.
Forty years ago, for instance, the Danes
were in a far worse position than we are
in to-day. We enjoy many natural
advantages which they were deprived of.
I do not think it can be said that the soil
of Ireland is inferior to the soil of Denmark or of any other country, because I
would suggest that when the soil here can
grow the Shamrock so green, and the
Orange Lily so orange, it is capable of the
abundant production of anything it is
called upon to produce.
I am sorry this is not a more comprehensive Bill. It is not even a substantial
Dairy Bill; in fact, I would describe it
as a milk and water Dairy Bill. Some
time ago a Committee was set up by the
Northern Government to inquire into the
state of agriculture. Three questions
were submitted to that Committee, and
one of those questions, and an important
one in my opinion, was, what was the
reason for the differentiation in the price
the farmer received for his produce and
the price the consumer paid for it. Of
course, to find a fair answer to that
question one would require to have the
evidence of all the interests concerned.
The great transport
transport companies. of
Northern Ireland were asked to submit
their evidence and they refused. I do
blame the Government, and I strongly
blame the Government, for allowing any
interest to withold information that they
could put before this Committee, a Committee appointed by the Government to
investigate a matter that was vital to the
interests of the people of Northern
Ireland.
I do suggest to this House that if the
Government of Denmark had from time
to time allowed any interest, no matter
how influential, to impede the work of
any Committee they had set up the Danes
would not have progressed as they have
done during the last forty years. Speaking of Parliament and the people
generally, the Danes took advantage of
every scientific instrument that was
brought to their knowledge. To a great
extent they advanced in agriculture on
account of the work of their schools, and
whilst they marched with the times, we
in Northern Ireland, the Government and
the people, have dug our two heels into
the ground and refused to move. In this,
at all events, we have been true to our
slogan: "Not an inch."
The finding of the Committee I have
referred to was that it had not sufficient
data before it to clarify the position on
that question. I suggest it is the duty
of the Government to follow up the work
of that Committee and appoint a Commission that will have power to compel
all interests in Northern Ireland to contribute to the solution of this very vital
question. It is the duty of the Government to take the initiative, and I am sure
that every section of thought in Northern
Ireland will give them the most genuine,
whole-hearted, and unselfish support in
the no doubt difficult task of organising
the farmers of Northern Ireland and promoting a system of collective and cooperative selling, and of buying wholesale and selling by retail, instead of vice
versa as to-day, I suggest that that is the
greatest and most important subject on
which the Government can concentrate
its attention at the present time. This
is not a question that should be left to
the Ministry of Agriculture alone to
deal with. I would urge the Government
as a whole to give their first and most
earnest attention to the question of developing the agricultural industry
of Northern Ireland, because in my
deliberate opinion-and I think my
opinion is shared by many others—unless
we can do something to foster and reestablish this industry, Northern Ireland
as a whole cannot possibly prosper and
cannot hold its own.
With regard to the Bill itself, whilst I
cannot offer any words of hope or encouragement or enthusiasm for it, yet
there is a principle involved in the Bill
which I think all parties in this House
will support, and that is the principle of
trying to improve the different products
of the farmers. There is just one remark
I would like to make in reference to the
speech of the noble Lord, the Leader of
the House. He referred to the financial
effect of previous Bills of this description.
I would like to remind the House that
last year a Bill was passed for the marketing of potatoes, and potatoes are being
sold in Northern Ireland to-day at 30s a
Taking that as a criterion of the
effect of legislation of this character I am
afraid the outlook for the success of this
Bill is not very hopeful.
ton.
Mr. LONG: I do not intend to follow
the previous speaker in the very wide
Dairy Produce Bill-
THE SENATE
Second Reading
One
though interesting survey he has taken of
the agricultural position generally, and
of the difference between what the consumer pays and what the producer receives. I support the Bill because
I believe it will be a useful Measure
and will tend to raise still further
the quality of the butter produced
in Northern Ireland. When this Measure was first introduced in another place
I was concerned with two points.
was that provision was not made for an
appeal from a decision of the Ministry.
That has now been set right, and there is
now a right of appeal to the County Court
Judge against the decision of the Ministry, and I am quite satisfied on that
point. Another matter that gives me
satisfaction is that the Regulations to be
made under this Bill are to be laid on the
Table of the House forthwith. I heartily
approve of that, and I suggest it is a precedent that might be followed in regard
to other Bills.
I approach the consideration of this
question from the point of view that the
Ministry of Agriculture are not lying in
wait to pounce on a person who may perhaps inadvertently offend against one of
their rules and regulations. I regard the
Ministry as being in the position of taking
a fatherly interest in this matter, and
anxious to promote in every way they can
the welfare of the people concerned.
am quite satisfied the Ministry of Agriculture will sympathetically administer
this Bill, and I believe it will produce an
improvement in the standard of the butter
produced in Northern Ireland.

Mr. PORTER-PORTER: I am not
quite sure whether Senator McAllister
was attacking the Ministry of Agriculture
or only this Bill, but I can say from my
own personal knowledge that the Ministry
are determined to put farming in
Northern Ireland on a par with that of
Denmark or any other country. They
are not going, as the hon. Senator said,
to dig their heels in the ground and stand
still. They are going to go ahead. With
regard to the effect that this legislation is
likely to have, we can point to the beneficial results of the Marketing of Eggs
Act, which was passed a few years ago.
Before the passing of that Act we sold a
million eggs a year, and now we are selling six millions. We can also point to the
improvement in livestock brought about
by the Act prohibiting the importation
into Northern Ireland of scrub bulls.
Now that the Ministry have put their
hands to the plough they are not going to
stop until they have placed agriculture in
Northern Ireland on at least as high a
plane as it is in Denmark. I do not
think there is anything objectionable in
the Bill, because our butter must be of
the highest quality if it is to obtain the
highest price in the markets across the
water.
Mr. LESLIE: I am sure everybody
will welcome this Bill, as we would any
Bill which purports to do something more
for agriculture, and we all hope that the
effects of the Bill will be, as regards
price, more satisfactory than either
potatoes or cattle, both of which are at a
very low ebb at present so far as return
is concerned. I am sure the House will
give the Measure a Second Reading, but
I hope that when we get to the Committee
Stage the Bill will be very carefully
examined. It will be seen that very wide
powers indeed are given to the Ministry
under this Bill. It will be seen too that
provisions as regards appeal are not yet
exhausted. It will probably be observed
that there are sundry points of detail
which are susceptible of improvement, and
that if the Bill is to be regarded as a bit
of advertising propaganda it would be better to stick to the extraction of cream
rather than to suggest, as has been done,
the possibility of the manufacture of
cream. That, I think, cannot be intentional, but it is an instance of a matter
that seems to require attention.
I have just said that the powers proposed to be conferred on the Ministry are
of a very extensive character. Those
powers, such as they are, have, I understand, been practically agreed to by the
representatives of the present central
creameries and auxiliary creameries. That
is to say, all those existing institutions
are perfectly willing to accept this wide.
measure of control. That is all very well
as far as it goes, and I do not think this
House need concern itself very much with
the matter. But there remains the possibility of future entrants into the ranks
of
creamery proprietors, and I think we
ought to watch to see that the effect of
this Bill is not to set up in Northern Ireland a kind of guild of creamery managers
and proprietors into which nobody can
come except under conditions which they
may find unduly onerous. That is a
point we must not lose sight of. Perhaps,
owing to the good fortune we enjoy in
many ways, we are apt to consider that
the present management will endure for
Dairy Produce Bill-
Second Reading
3 DECEMBER 1929
[Mr. Leslie]
ever, and I think we are sometimes inclined to forget the possibilities of changes
in the future.
I am sorry to see that the Bill contains
possibilities of minimum fines. A year or
two ago I spoke on the subject of minimum
fines, and the noble Viscount who now
leads the House was good enough to remind us that the Government was not
wedded to the principle of the minimum
fine. Having regard not only to this Bill,
but to other agricultural Bills, if the Government is not wedded to the minimum
fine I do not know what terms to apply to
the relationship which apparently exists.
It seems to me peculiarly unfortunate that
the minimum fine should be introduced in
a Measure which is a new Measure, because it cannot be urged in this case that
magistrates in the past have failed to inflict sufficiently severe penalties. Therefore, I am afraid it will be incumbent on
those of us who disagree with the principle
to draw attention to that matter at a
later stage.
I do not know that there is anything
more I can say to-day except that I shall
urge for an extension of the powers of
appeal so as to cover the refusal of a
licence to export butter. I shall also have
to draw attention to the fact that there
is apparently no provision allowing a man
living in Northern Ireland to send a pint
of cream in a sealed bottle to his aged
mother-in-law in Scotland, but I think
that can easily be set right. On the
whole, looking to the wide powers given
to the Ministry, I am very much inclined
to consider that it would be wise to insert
a provision that the Bill last for five years
it is no good fixing the time too short
--and in that way it would ensure that
the Bill would automatically come up for
revision. It could then, if thought desirable, be extended under the provisions
of the Expiring Laws Act, or the limiting
paragraph could be repealed. On the
whole, of course, I support the Bill.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: I rise to support the Bill, and the only fault I have
to find is that it does not go far enough.
It is an export Bill only, and we in Northern Ireland will have to suffer from all
kinds of butter, because this Measure will
not affect home produce. I am sure we
are all glad to see the Minister of Agriculture present. I should like to draw
his attention to one particular Clause in
the Bill which allows an individual
package of butter not exceeding 28 lbs. to
be exported. Any shopkeeper or collector
of butter can go round the country and
buy two stones of butter and send it
away without having to have it inspected.
I think that quantity of butter is too
large, and that if only half that amount
was allowed it would be quite sufficient.
I am inclined to move an Amendment to
that effect in Committee.
Regarding the question of the difference
in the price paid by the consumer and the
price paid to the producer, on one
occasion I sat on a Committee set up by
the Ministry of Agriculture in connection
with that subject. We agreed, as of
course everybody agrees, that the difference between what the consumer pays and
what the producer gets was too great, but
when we went into the facts and got the
evidence of a small shopkeeper in Belfast
we found that his overhead charges-rent,
taxes, wages, and so on-were such that
he had to make £1,600 before he was able
to turn a penny of profit. In other
words, he had to trade nine months to
cover his overhead charges, and he had
the remaining three months in which to
make his profit. That shows the diffi
culties there are in dealing with that
question, and I mention it as an illustration of the excessive rents charged in
Belfast.
Mr. DORMAN: It appears to me that
the object of the Ministry of Agriculture
in promoting this Bill is to establish a
standard of quality for butter produced
in Northern Ireland for export. The Bill
does not seem to affect the production and
distribution of these commodities in the
Northern area, but it rightly standardises
the quality of the butter and cream that
will be placed upon the markets in the
Free State and in Great Britain. Everyone who has come into contact with the
farmers knows perfectly well that they
are possibly the most conservative section
of the community, and there is probably
no body of men engaged in production
that more resent interference and advice
from those not engaged in the same calling
as themselves. No matter how wide may
be a man's powers of observation,
matter how far he may have travelled,
and how much he may have compared the
methods of other countries with those of
his own, if he attempts to advise a farmer
who thinks his adviser is not himself a
practical farmer, he will immediately be
disposed to send him to some other area
that is not very desirable to approach.
no
273 Dairy Produce Bill-

3 DECEMBER 1929
Second Reading
I say this because I am associated with
farmers all over this country. I know
the four Provinces, and I know from
observation how neglectful the farmers
have been as the custodians of a national
asset. The farmers of Ireland have never
been taught a sense of their personal responsibility to the nation for the use they
make of the nation's means of producing
all its food. There is probably not one
farmer out of hundreds of thousands
who recognises he has any responsibility to the nation.
I make
use
this suggestion to the Minister:
that the farmers of Northern Ireland, as
well as the farmers of the Free State, do
not set as their object the production of
the greatest possible proportion of
values from that portion of the surface
of the soil they have control of. As I
have mentioned once before in this House,
the Government and the governing class
have hitherto looked upon the land as a
source from which rent is to be extracted.
Mr. SPEAKER: I am afraid the hon.
Member is wandering rather far away
from the Bill.
Mr. DORMAN: I will not pursue that
line of argument, but I would like to see
the Ministry attack this particular matter
in the interests of the residents in our own
area. At the present time the difference
between the price the producer gets and
the price the consumer pays for foodstuffs
in Northern Ireland is anything from 200
to 300 per cent. I believe that in Belfast to-day the consumers are paying 300
per cent. more for potatoes than the
farmers are receiving in the market, and
the same applies to other products. It
may be said, as one hon. Member has
suggested, that the overhead charges are
very high, but why are these overhead
charges so high? Overhead charges are
very largely governed by the geographical
position of the shop. For instance, a
shopkeeper in the centre of the City
might be asked to pay a rent of £50 a foot
frontage, while at the top of the Shankill
Road the rent might be only 9d or 10d a
foot frontage. The consumer has to pay
for all that, and I would suggest to the
Ministry that the same protection should
be given to the consumer within our own
area as is given to the consumer outside.
Colonel SHARMAN-CRAWFORD: I
should like to join in the welcome to the
Minister of Agriculture. We know that
as a practical farmer he will take a more
sympathetic view of the remarks made in
this House than will those who have no
practical knowledge of agriculture. I am
sorry the Minister was not present when
Senator McAllister spoke. I believe this
Bill will improve matters, although it
only touches the very fringe of what everybody wishes to see, namely, the prosperity
of agriculture in this country. It is a
well-known fact that when agriculture is
prosperous the country generally is prosperous, and when agriculture is depressed
the country is depressed. And therefore.
we feel that agriculture is the principal
asset of any Government. Of course, we
have no power as long as the law continues as it is. But what position are the
unfortunate farmers in in this country or
in England? There is hardly anything the
farmer grows nowadays that is not being
dumped in by his competitors in other
countries. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear).
What is the good of controlling butter or
eggs or anything of that kind when we
know from the uttermost parts of the
world butter and eggs are being brought
here?
When I was in Siberia I saw people
there buying butter and eggs to be sent to
this country. We also know that the
principal competitors of eggs from this
country are eggs from china. An egg in
China is not worth much unless it has a
certain flavour about it. It is terrible to
think that with regard to every product of
this country the farmer has not the
slightest chance against these things. Unless something is done-and this Government has no power-agriculture must
remain in a very precarious position.
I was down the other day going through
the South of Ireland, through Cork and
into Limerick, where the best grass in the
world grows, and instead of its being full
of cattle there was nothing but haystacks
-no cattle at all. I welcome this Bill.
The Minister of Agriculture knows the
difficulties. We do hope this is only a
beginning, in conjunction with the
Imperial Parliament, of some steps being
taken to encourage the farmers and to try
to preserve agriculture on which the life
of both town and country depends. I do
hope something will be done, because it is
very serious and the farmers are hardly
able to live nowadays at all.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I should
like to say that I am very grateful to the
House for their kind consideration of the
Bill. No one can possibly complain that
Betting Bill-
Committee.
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
the discussion that followed its introduction has not been discursive. We have
wandered from shops at 10d a foot at the
top of the Shankill Road to Denmark,
and to Chinese eggs-away from the discussion of the Bill itself. I should like
to say that the hon. and gallant Senator
who last spoke got to the bedrock of the
whole matter in the unfortunate position
of foreign producers bringing down the
price of everything. The only thing to be
desired is that the standard of agricultural
produce in this country should be kept as
high as possible, because the lower it is
the more hopeless becomes our competition
with foreign countries. For the rest most
of the points raised, I think, can more
fittingly be considered at a later stage
when we come to Committee. (Hon.
Members: Hear, hear).

be now read a Second time') agreed to.

Betting (Juvenile Messengers) Bill.
Order for the House to be put into
Committee read.
Moved: "That this House do now resolve itself into Committee on the said
Bill." (Viscount Bangor).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
In Committee accordingly.
[The MARQUESS OF DUFFERIN
AND AVA in the Chair].
CLAUSE 1 (Prohibition of the use of
young persons in conveyance or delivery
of messages, &c., relating to betting).
(1) Any person who causes or procures, or attempts to cause or procure,
any young person to convey or deliver
any slip, note or message, verbal or
written, which relates to any bet or
wager, or to convey or deliver any information, advice or money for the
purpose of making or procuring the
making of any bet or wager, or which
relates to any bet or wager already
made, to any bookmaker shall be
guilty of an offence under this Act; and
any bookmaker who accepts from
young person any such slip, note, message, information, advice or money,
shall be guilty of an offence under this
Act.
a
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to move in
Clause 1, line 5, after the word "who"
("person who causes") to insert "knowingly," and in the same Clause in line
13, after the word "who" ("bookmaker
who accepts') to insert the word "knowingly." This is a Bill dealing with the
use of young persons in the conveyance or
delivery of messages relating to betting.
It concerns everyone who sends a young
person under the age of 18 years on a
betting mission, and also concerns every
bookmaker who accepts a betting commission from a young person under the
age of 18.
Betting legislation is full of anomalies.
We know that betting pervades all ranks
of society, and I do respectfully submit
to the House that there is very little use
in passing an Act of Parliament which
will not have the driving force of public
opinion behind it. (Hon. Members:
Hear, hear.) One of the serious drawbacks to all betting legislation is that
there never has been and is not now any
sufficient driving power of public opinion
in favour of its enforcement. The
present Clause, Clause 1, fixes criminal responsibility upon every person who
employs a young person under this age on
this mission. The result of this is this,
that a man who in perfect honesty, in the
bona-fide belief that the messenger is 18
years of age, employs a messenger not 18,
is ipso facto guilty of an offence against
the Act. It seems to me a very drastic
Clause in an Act of Parliament.
It may
hit an innocent man sitting in his club or
sitting in his lodgings.
The licensing law deals with the sale of
drink to children and young persons, but
there is this restriction in almost every
case. The earlier Acts of the seventies
prohibit the sale of drink to young persons in public-houses under a certain age.
But they qualify it in this way that the
publican is only guilty of the offence if
the young person is apparently under the
age. Later on there is the Act of 1901,
prohibiting the sale of drink to children
except in corked and sealed vessels under
a certain restriction. The publican is
only guilty of the offence if he has knowledge that the young person is under the
age. I think we should approach this
matter with a desire as far as possible to
do what is right and fair to all sections.
I can see no reason why the bookmaker
should not have the same defence as the
publican.
Betting Bill-
Committee.
3 DECEMBER 1929
I think, therefore, in the interests of
the public and for the purpose of preventing a miscarriage of justice it would be
advisable that this Amendment should be
adopted. I think a person should not be
deemed guilty of a criminal offence
punishable in the way the Act provides
unless at the time he sends the young person on a betting message he knows or
ought to know from the look of the young
person that he is under the age prohibited
by the Act. The same measure of protection should be afforded to the bookmaker. I beg to move.
Viscount BANGOR: These Amendments both cover the same point and if I
may I should like to take them together
in my reply to the hon. and learned
Senator. I think he is unduly apprehensive of convictions taking place in
cases where the offender is in fact morally
innocent, and it seems to me that he does
not place any reliance upon the Court
which would deal with such a case, should
such a case be brought before a Court.
I need hardly point out to a lawyer of his
experience the additional difficulties in
the way of proof which the inclusion of
the word "knowingly" would raise, and
this would in effect seriously impair the
effectiveness of the Measure. I venture
to assert that there can be little
danger of a bookmaker or anyone else unwittingly committing an offence if he takes
reasonable and ordinary precaution to ensure that young persons under the prohibited age are not permitted to deliver
messages. I may say that I am aware that
as a matter of fact, the majority of bookmakers are willing and anxious to assist
in every way in co-operating to prevent
young persons frequenting their premises.
They do not want to have them hanging
about their places at all. In all the
circumstances, and after careful consideration of the proposal put forward by
the hon. and learned Senator, I regret
that the Government cannot see its way
accept this Amendment.
to
Mr. MCALLISTER: I would like to
associate myself with the mover of this
Amendment. I think there is a grave
possibility of an innocent man not guilty
of intent being convicted under this particular section. I think the Court should
have the option of forming a judgment,
as to whether from the appearance of the
messenger the defendant in the court had
reasonable ground to believe that he was
the
As
age provided by the Act.
the hon. and learned Senator, who moved
over
the Amendment, said there are many instances where the word "knowingly" is
incorporated in the Acts of Parliament in
respect of criminal offences. I can conceive of anybody coming to the conclusion
and satisfying himself bona-fide in his
own mind that a certain person from his
appearance was over the prescribed age,
and in that way bringing himself within
the meshes of the criminal law. He may
give evidence of that fact and satisfy the
court on that evidence, and yet the court
would have no remedy and would have to
convict him even though they knew what
he did was done innocently, and that he
had reasonable grounds for the belief he
had come to.
I do not think it would deprive the Bill
of its usefulness in the slightest, or affect
the purpose for which it is intended, if
this Amendment were accepted. After
all it is the court would have to decide
whether the man's belief was bona-fide or
not.
Mr. DORMAN: This first Section deals
with an offence committed by two persons,
the person who sends the messenger and
the person who receives the message, and
I think there should be some means of
enforcing what the proposer of the
Amendment has suggested-knowledge on
the part of each of these parties as to the
age of the messenger.
If I desire to send
a message to make a bet, and the messenger
is under 18 years of age, I am committing
an offence. If I am a bookmaker and I
receive that particular message or bet
from another person I am also guilty. I
think the suggested Amendment would
make the matter clear and plain.
Colonel SHARMAN-CRAWFORD: I
am not often in opposition to the Government, but I must say that this afternoon if the Amendment is pressed to a
Division I must vote for it, because I made
particular inquiries why there should be
any difference between this Act and the
Liquor Act. I do not think any case has
been made out for the change for the
worse. I therefore wish just to explain
the reason why I am going to vote for the
Amendment if it is pressed. I believe it
is right.
Colonel PAKENHAM: As I am going
to vote against the Government in this
matter, I think it is better for me to say
so openly. I do not see what harm this
word "knowingly" would do. I think it
would make it much plainer, and therefore I will vote for it.

THE SENATE
Mr. DICKSON: I went down here
from the last meeting of the Senate when
this Bill was first introduced and saw the
boots of the club, who is, I understand,
in the habit of carrying bets for the
members. I would have taken that boy
to be sixteen years of age, but he told me
he was eighteen years and one month,
I told him he was quite at liberty now to
carry a betting message. I shall support
the Amendment.
SO
I
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM:
would like to appeal to the Government
to reconsider this matter. There does not
seem to me to be any possibility of the
Amendment weakening the Bill. It will
merely make it more clear and may safeguard some innocent persons who might
otherwise be caught by its provisions. It
is impossible to imagine a youth running
about with his baptism lines in his pocket,
and you cannot always judge a young
man's age by merely looking at him.
You might see a young person low
stature, and although you would not
think he was eighteen years of age, he
might be twenty. Another young person, taller and more manly looking, might
not be eighteen. On these grounds I
think I am quite justified in voting for
the Amendment. It will not weaken the
Bill, and will probably save someone
from falling into trouble unconsciously.

Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I

The expression young person
means a

eighteen years.
age
of
I believe there is nothing to prevent a
person of fifteen years of age placing a
bet with a bookmaker, and yet this Bill
actually seeks to prevent persons under
the age of eighteen being used for the
purpose of carrying a betting message for
any person. That seems to me to create
a very peculiar position. I have a good
deal of knowledge of the practice in the
shipyards and in shops, and I know that
the ordinary practice of those who are
going to place a bet is this. They go to
one of their mates, very often a boy, who
is an apprentice-because they would
scarcely ask another man to do a message
for them-and they say to him, "Are you
backing anything to-day?"
If he says
""
"Yes" they say, "Put that on for me
when you are there.' It seems to me
that by passing this Bill you are enacting
legislation that you will be unable to
carry out. If a young man of sixteen is
betting himself, how on earth can you
prevent him betting for his mate with
whom he is working? It is simply
ridiculous. I want to ask the Government if they have considered this aspect
of the matter, because it seems to me that
it will be impossible to administer the
Bill.
shall
a
I would be sorry to do anything to
weaken the Bill, because of all the sins I
may have to be responsible for I
never have to plead guilty to
gambling. I would say that anything that can be done to save
juvenile, or even a boy of fifteen-because
I look upon him as a juvenile--some of
them have more sense than you or I,
especially those boys running about with
"Telegraphs," and those heating rivets in
the shipyards-I would do anything to
save children or youths up to fifteen years
of age from being used to carry betting
messages that would enable them to become acquainted with the methods of

The aggregate amount kept would

Committee.
THE SENATE.

Wednesday, 4th December, 1929.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
Adoption of Children Bill.
Order for the House to be put into
Committee read.
Moved: "That this House do now resolve itself into Committee on the said
Bill." (Viscount Charlemont).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
In Committee accordingly.
[The MARQUESS OF DUFFERIN
AND AVA in the Chair.]
CLAUSE 1 (Power to make adoption.
orders), ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 2 (Restrictions on making.
adoption orders).
Provided that, where the applicant,
or in the case of joint applicants
being spouses one of the applicants,
and the infant are, or would be if the
infant were of the opposite sex,
within the prohibited degrees of consanguinity, it shall be lawful for the
Court, if it thinks fit, to make the
order notwithstanding that the applicant is less than twenty-one years
older than the infant.
(2) An adoption order shall not be
made in any case where the sole applicant is a male and the infant in respect
of whom the application is made is a
female, unless the Court is satisfied that
there are special circumstances which
justify as an exceptional measure the
making of an adoption order.
*
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to move, in
Clause 2, page 4, line 19, after the word
"order" at the end of paragraph (2), to
add "which special circumstances shall be
specified on the face of the order." The
order contemplated by that particular
Clause is an adoption order in the case
where the sole applicant is a male, and
the infant in respect of whom the application is made is a female, and in those
circumstances it cannot be made unless the
Court is satisfied that there are special
circumstances which justify as an exceptional measure the making of an
adoption order.
Mr. LESLIE: On a point of order, 1
am very sorry to interrupt, but would it
not have been more correct if we had
taken first the Amendment standing in
the name of the noble Leader of the
House. That Amendment relates to line
6, and it has some bearing on what the
hon. Member has said.
The CHAIRMAN: I think the hon.
Member is quite correct. The Amendment in the name of the noble Viscount
(Viscount Charlemont) should be taken
first.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): I beg to move: to leave out
from the word "Provided," in paragraph
(b), to the word "infant" at the end of
the paragraph, and insert
"Provided that it shall be lawful for
the court on being satisfied that there
are special circumstances which justify
as an exceptional measure the making of
an adoption order, to make the order
notwithstanding that the applicant is
less than twenty-one years older than
the infant."
In the original draft of the Bill it was
provided that the general prohibition
against the making of an adoption order
where the applicant was less than 21 years
older than the infant might, at the discretion of the Court, be waived in cases
where the applicant and the infant were
within the prohibited degrees of consanguinity. During the discussion on the
Bill in another place the right hon. the
Attorney-General undertook to examine
this Clause with a view to its simplification, and to a slight extension of the discretionary powers conferred on the Court.
In its amended form it will be seen that
the Section provides for giving the Court.
general discretionary power, if there are
special cicumstances which justify this as
an exceptional measure, to make
adoption order notwithstanding that the
applicant is less than 21 years older than
the infant. It is still regarded as
essential that, in general, persons adopting children must be not less than 21 years
older than the infant, but it is felt that
this regulation should not be absolutely
rigid, but that the Court should have discretionary powers to deal with special
cases that arise.

Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to move, in
Clause 2, page 4, line 19, after the word
"order" at the end of paragraph (2) to
add, "which special circumstances shall be
specified on the face of the order." The
Clause deals with the making of an adoption order in the case where the sole
applicant is a male and the infant in respect of whom the application is made is
a female. My own inclination would be
to delete the Clause, but I fear that that
would not commend itself to the Senate
as a whole. I apprehend that the Clause
might in some cases give rise to mischief
greater than the evils the Bill seeks to
remove. The words which I propose to
add simply impose on the Court the duty
of taking extra precautions in regard to
an order of this kind, and there will be no
difficulty in setting forth on the face of
the order the special circumstances which
justify as an exceptional measure the
making of such an adoption order.
We have a precedent for this in the
Intoxicating Liquor Act of 1923. Under
that Act, where a licence holder is convicted of an offence under the Act the conviction is directed to be recorded by the
Court in every case unless there are special
circumstances justifying the omission of
the record, and those special circumstances
are directed by the Act to be specified on
the face of the Order by the Court itself.
I beg to move.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: With regard to the Amendment proposed by the
hon. and learned Member, I may point
out that this Clause prohibits the making
of an adoption order where the sole
applicant is a male and the infant to be
adopted a female, unless the Court is
satisfied that there are special circumstances justifying the making of an adoption order as an exceptional measure.
The hon. and learned Member now
suggests that the Court should be required
to state specifically on the face of the
order the special circumstances which
justified the making of the adoption order.
It is, I think, unreasonable to suppose
that the Court to which the jurisdiction
in these cases is given will not exercise the
discretion conferred on it by this Section
otherwise than with a full sense of responsibility, and it is difficult to see what
useful purpose would be fulfilled in imposing on the Court the necessity for setting
out specifically on the face of the order
the circumstances which, in the opinion
of the Court, justified the order being
made.
It will be remembered, moreover, that
there is provision in the Act for hearing
cases in camera, and it would appear that
if part of the proceedings had in fact to
be disclosed upon the face of the order,
that to a certain extent the confidential
nature of the proceedings might be impaired. There is no corresponding requirement in the
the English Act, and
although inquiries have been made as to
the working of the Act in England, it has
never been suggested that any difficulty
has arisen in this particular direction. I
trust, therefore, that in these circumstances the hon. and learned Senator will
not press his Amendment.
Mr. CAMPBELL: By leave of the
House I will withdraw the Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.
CLAUSES 2 (Restrictions on making
adoption orders), 3 (Matters with respect
to which the Court is to be satisfied), 4
(Terms and conditions of order), 5 (Effect
of adoption order), 6 (Power to make interim orders), and 7 (Power to make subsequent order in respect of infant already
subject to an order), ordered to stand
part of the Bill.

Court may authorise the authority to
incur any necessary expenditure, and
may direct out of which fund or rate
such expenditure is to be defrayed, but
nothing in this section shall be deemed
to authorise the Court to appoint a
local authority to act as guardian ad
litem of an infant except with the consent of that authority.

Mr. LESLIE: Since the Amendment

the object I had in view can be more conveniently obtained by moving, instead of
the words on the Paper, to insert after

or other." I accordingly move that
Amendment. Those words will make it
perfectly plain, for the purposes of this
Bill at any rate, that a board of guardians
is a local authority, and they will prevent
any possibility of any question arising.
The use of the words in that form will,
perhaps, render it more unlikely than it
was before that the insertion of such a
passage in this Bill should interfere in
any way with other Acts of Parliament.
The expression is very often the cause of
difficulty. It is usual to have it defined
in the Act itself, or by reference to the
code to which the Act belongs. In this
case there is no code, and this seems to be
a Section in which we should make it perfectly plain that a board of guardians
which, after all, is a public body, is in-
Icluded in the term. I beg to move.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am prepared to accept that Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
A
Clause, as amended, ordered to and
part of the Bill.

CLAUSE 10 (Provisions as to existing
de facto adoptions).
Where at the date of the commencement of this Act any infant is in the
custody of, and being brought up
and maintained and educated by any
person or two spouses jointly as his, her
or their own child under any de facto
adoption, and has for a period of not
less than two years before such commencement been in such custody, aud
been so brought up, maintained and
educated, the Court may, upon the
application of such person or spouses,
and notwithstanding that the applicant is a male and the infant a female,
make an adoption order authorising
him, her, or them to adopt the infant
without requiring the consent of any
parent or guardian of the infant to be
obtained, upon being satisfied that ir
all the circumstances of the case it is
just and equitable and for the welfare.
It
Mr. LESLIE: This deals with the
rather special case of a de facto adoption,
and the effect of the Amendment which
I propose in the words on the Paper will
be to give the Court the same discretion
in the case of a de facto adoption of admitting the adoption where the difference
of age is less than 21 years as the Court

will have the further effect of allowing the
Court to sanction an adoption by a person
less than 25 years of age in the extremely
unlikely case of such an application being
made and the Court being satisfied that
the adoption ought to be made. It is remotely possible that in the country districts a case of that sort might arise where
a child has been adopted by a farmer who

about the child who has become his adopted
brother or sister. I think the words
moved will be found to satisfy all require.
ments and will definitely remove
principal difficulty, which is the requirement that in the case of a de fact

21 years in age between the parties.
beg to move.

THE SENATE
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie:-
After Clause 11 insert as a new Clause---
Nothing in this Act shall be construed
as in derogation of any power of a board
of guardians under the Poor Relief
Acts (Northern Ireland), 1838 to 1928.
Provided that where the court has refused to make an adoption (or an interim) order in respect of an infant, a
board of guardians shall not consent to
the adoption within the meaning of the
Poor Law Act, 1899 (62 and 63 Vict.,
c. 37), of such infant by the person or
persons whose application for authority
to adopt such infant has been refused
by the court, nor shall a board of
guardians commit such infant to the
control or custody of such person or
persons as aforesaid.
Mr. LESLIE: In order to keep myself
in order, I beg to move the Amendment
standing in my name. It may be remembered that when speaking on the
Second Reading I pointed out that there
was some difficulty, or rather some possibility of confusion, between the existing
powers which are called adoptions by
boards of guardians, and the new adoption
which was brought in and regularised by
the Bill, and I said that even at the cost
of being accused of doing something which
merely padded the Bill it might be well
to insert some words to prevent any confusion arising in the public mind. Since
the Amendment was put down I have
ascertained, that I have correctly interpreted the purpose of the Bill in so far as
it is not intended to interfere in any way
with that power of a board of guardians
to sanction adoption under the provisions
of the Poor Law Act of 1899, and that it
had not been intended to alter those provisions. In England all the arrangements were made under the Poor Relief
Act of 1927, which succeeded the English
Adoption Act of 1926, and it was made
perfectly plain by cross reference to the
1926 Act that there was no intention of
making any alteration.
But there are obvious difficulties in inserting the exact words I have on the
Paper to-day. In the first place, one will
obviously be told there was nothing in the
Bill that could be construed in relation to
powers of boards of guardians, and that
the Amendment might be held to belong
more to the Poor Relief Act than to the
Bill before us, and that therefore it
It has been
was rather outside the Title.
suggested that all that is required to
prevent confusion in the public mind can
be effected by administrative action, if the
Ministry of Home
Home Affairs acquaints
boards of guardians with the fact that the
new Bill will not in any way interfere with
the operations of the other Act. If the
noble Viscount can tell us that the Ministry is prepared to do that it will be unnecessary to trouble the House further
with the Amendment.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am willing
to give the required guarantee if the hon.
and learned Member will withdraw his
Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: I ask leave to withdraw
the Amendment, and I thank the noble
Lord.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.

Oral Answers
Oral Answers
THE SENATE
THE SENATE.

Thursday, 5th December, 1929.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
Papers Presented to Parliament.
Ministry of Education:
Parliamentary Grant (Education
Authorities) Regulations, 1930-31.
Ministry of Finance:
Statutory Order, Teachers' (Public Elementary) Superannuation (Amendment)
Scheme, 1929.
Laid before the House (pursuant to
Act) and ordered to lie on the Table.
Joint Committee on Fees.
The following Report was made from
the Joint Committee on Fees relating to
Local Bills and Divorce Bills:-
The Committee have examined the
scale of fees in respect of Local Bill and
Divorce Bill legislation, and recommend
to the House the Amendments specified in
the appendix.
(1) As regards Divorce Bills, the
Committee consider that there should be
a reasonable relation between the fees
charged and the time of Members and
Officers of Parliament expended in the
passing of such Bills. In this respect
they consider the existing scale of fees.
too high, and recommend reductions.
(2) As regards Local Bills, the Committee are of opinion that the scale of
fees is substantially correct, but recommend that there should be a reduction
in the rising scale of certain fees payable in accordance with the money to
be raised or expended under a Bill and
in the Committee fees.
PROTECTION OF NORTHERN
INDUSTRIES.
The following Question stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Campbell:-
To ask the Leader of the House whether,
in view of the fact that Northern Ireland
is predominantly a Protectionist Unit,
the Government will approach the Imperial Government with a view to such
modifications of the Government of Ireland Act, 1920, as will give Northern Ireland the power to protect its basic industries against foreign competition.
Mr. CAMPBELL: In putting this
Question I should point out that the words
"if deemed advisable" should have appeared in the Question after the word
"protect." I make no claim to be a Protectionist. My object is to clear the air
a little. Great Britain is predominantly
a Free Trade unit. The Unionist constituencies of Northern Ireland are represented here and in London by supporters
of Protection to a man. The British electorate have voted overwhelmingly for
Free Trade at least twice. At the last
General Election 13 million votes were
polled for and 8 million votes against Free
Trade. Ulster linen merchants want Protection, Ulster farmers want Protection;
but the Imperial Parliament will not give
them Protection, and the Northern Parliament cannot give them Protection.
are they to do? If this Parliament is
logical and staunch to its opinions
should it not seek the power every other
Parliament has of protecting within its
own area its own industries if it thinks
such Protection necessary or expedient?
What
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I cannot
be drawn into a discussion on the merits
of Free Trade or Protection, or on the
Therefore,
safeguarding of industries.
without expressing any opinion on the
merits of those questions, the answer to
the question on the Paper must be in the
negative.
"SIMPLE BIBLE TEACHING" IN
SCHOOLS.
The following Question stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Campbell:-
To ask the Leader of the House (1)
whether the Government, before making
their recent concessions in the domain of
elementary education in regard particularly to "simple Bible teaching," had any
communication with the Imperial Government as to whether these concessions were
in accordance with the intention of the
Irish Free State (Agreement) Act, 1922.
Mr. CAMPBELL: In asking this
Question I wish to state very briefly why I
put it down. I do not want to tread on
any ground covered last week in the de-
297 Dairy Produce Bill-

5 DECEMBER 1929
Committee.
bate on Education, but I would like to
mention that the Irish Free State
(Agreement) Act, 1922, is an Imperial
Act, expressing the mind of the Imperial Parliament. Recent concessions
of the Government in the matter of
elementary education have met the
wishes of
a very powerful body,
the United Association of the Protestant Churches of Northern Ireland.
At the same time they have intensified the
sense of unequal treatment felt by the
Catholic minority, who believe that in this
matter of education the Government have,
not for the first time, thwarted the intention of the Imperial Parliament as expressed in this particular Act of
Parliament.
This Act provides guarantees in respect
of the minorities both North and South
in respect of education, and enacts that
neither Northern Ireland or the Irish
Free State shall make any law giving preference, directly or indirectly, to the
schools of any denomination. The Government have given that preference, and
I desire to know whether, before doing so,
they sought the friendly advice of the Imperial authorities.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I would be
very sorry to think the hon, and
learned Senator had ever confused anything in his mind, or had ever thought to
confuse anybody else's mind. At the
same time, with all due deference, I think
there is a great deal of difference between
the promise of concessions and the actual
making of concessions. In point of fact,
no recent concessions have been made.
They have been promised, and will be
brought before this House when the
Amending Bill is introduced next year.
When people who speak on behalf of the
Government promise concessions it is only
reasonable to suppose that as reasonable
men they are satisfied in their own minds
that their proposals are within the legis
lative capacity of the Government.
they thought they were not they would
naturally take steps to obtain any advice and counsel they might think desirable. So far as the actual question on
the Paper is concerned, the answer is in
the negative.

(CLAUSE 1 Registration of Premises).
(1) All premises used as-
(a) an auxiliary creamery, that is
to say, for the purpose of extracting
from the milk supplies of
several cow-keepers; or
cream
(b) a central creamery, that is to
say, for the purpose of extracting
cream from the milk supplies of
several cow-keepers, or for the assembling of cream from auxiliary
creameries, for sale as cream or for
the manufacture of butter; or
(3) A fee of one pound shall be paid
to the Ministry in respect of the registration or renewal of the registration of
any premises.
*
That
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in Clause
1, page 1, line 10, of paragraph (a) to
leave out the word "several" ("supplies of
several cow-keepers'). I suggest that the
word "several" is of no use to us for the
purposes of this Bill, and that on the contrary it is a source of danger. There is
no legal definition of the meaning of the
word "several" in this connection, and I
assume it is used here as a term relating
to a number. The ordinary dictionary
definition of the word "several" is, more
than one or two; not very many.
leaves us in a hopelessly difficult position,
and it seems to me that, although the
Minister will not be bound to sanction a
prosecution under the Bill, he will not
therefore in that way be affected if the Bill
is left without any adjective; but if he does
sanction a prosecution he stands to be defeated before the court, which may say
that in the particular case before them the
auxiliary or the central creamery does not
exist for the purpose of extracting cream
from the milk supplies of several cowkeepers. I cannot see that the word does
the Bill any good, and I therefore move
its omission.
This is a
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
drafting Amendment which to my mind
rather weakens the structure of the subsection. Therefore I regret I cannot
accept it.

THE SENATE
Mr. LESLIE: I will not press it.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie :
Clause 1, page 1, line 13, to leave out
"several."
Amendment not moved.
Mr. LESLIE: In line 15 of paragraph
(b) I beg to move, after the word "cream"
("for sale as cream") to insert "other than
condensed cream." The origin of this
Amendment is to be found in the Act of
1907, which deals with margarine and
butter, and in that Act there is a saving
for cream sold as condensed cream.
Whether it is possible that the question.
of condensed cream can arise in connection
with the creameries we are now dealing
with or not I am not very sure, nor am
I sure that, even if it does not arise at
present, it cannot arise in the future.
Therefore, I thought it was well to put an
Amendment on the Paper in order to ascertain if it was within the scheme of
practical politics that condensed milk
could be affected. If the answer is in the
affirmative the words I suggest should go
in, but if the answer is in the negative
there would be no object in pressing the
Amendment, or indeed, in trying to get
it inserted. In order to get an explanation I beg to move the Amendment.
Mr. McHUGH: I am very glad the
Minister of Agriculture is present as I
want to draw his attention to one particular point. During the winter season
these creameries sell cream only, and the
reason they do so-
The CHAIRMAN: If the hon. Member
is not speaking on the Amendment before
the Committee it would be better for him
to defer his speech until I put the Clause
to the Committee. If his remarks do not
relate to the Amendment he would not
be in order in speaking now.
Mr. McHUGH: Very well.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: There is no
"condensed cream" known to the dairy
industry in Northern Ireland.
Mr. LESLIE: Nor likely to be?
Viscount CHARLEMONT: No.
Mr. LESLIE: Then I ask leave to withdraw the Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in Clause
1, page 2, to leave out sub-section (3).
This is a rather more difficult question.
This is the sub-clause which requires a fee
of £1 to be paid to the Ministry in respect
of registration or renewal of registration.
That means that anybody who wants his
premises registered, or his registration.
renewed will have to pay £1 every year.
Under the provisions of the Act of 1907,
which dealt with butter and margarine,
and which refers back to the earlier Act
of 1889, registration had to be effected
with the local authority and there was
no fee charged at all.
If it is the deliberate intention of Parliament that a man should have to pay
£1 a year I think it ought to be stated in
each House as the question arises. It is
a new tax, and my own personal feeling
is that we ought not to impose a penalty,
so to speak, on a licence. That is to say,
if it is necessary for general purposes to
require that this or that occupation shall
not be carried on except under licence we
ought not to impose a fee for that licence,
because that is simply charging a man for
exercising the ordinary rights he has as a
British subject. I do not suppose there
is any desire to make any great revenue
out of this, but as a matter of principle
I thought it right to draw attention to
this proposal to hear what the Government have to say about it and to give an
opportunity for discussion. I, therefore,
beg to move that the sub-section be left
out.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The effect
of this proposal is to call for no contribution from the industry for the benefits
conferred by the Act. This House has
already approved of the Bill in principle
and, accordingly, it is to be assumed, regards the Bill as desirable in the interests
of the organisations affected. That those
organisations should not be asked to contribute even the modest sum of £1 Os Od
per annum is not acceptable.
There are plenty of precedents for
charges of much greater weight than those
under consideration. It is almost safe to
say that in any country where the dairy
industry is organised, whether through
the agency of the Government or of the industry itself, for the proper conduct of
its export trade, the industry bears a proportion of the cost. Certain it is that in
Denmark and in the Dominions this is the
I regret that I am not prepared to
accept the Amendment.
case.
Dairy Produce Bill-
Committee.
5 DECEMBER 1929
Mr. LESLIE: I have made my protest,
but I do not wish to press the matter.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Question put, "That the Clause stand
part of the Bill.”
Mr. McHUGH: What I want to draw
the attention of the Minister of Agriculture to is a very wrong practice that is
carried on in the creameries in the winter
season. It is a well-known fact that
throughout the winter season these creameries sell nothing but cream because they
can get a good price for it. They do not
manufacture butter, but what they do is
to purchase Australian and other butter
in bulk. They make that butter into
rolls and put a wrapper on them certifying
that it is their own butter produced in
their own creamery, and they charge the
highest possible price for it. I suggest
that this is a practice that should be
stamped out, because this butter is of an
inferior quality to creamery butter and it
is strongly disapproved of by consumers.
I hope that on the Report stage of the Bill
the Minister will introduce a new Clause
to prevent this illegal practice being
carried on in the future.
The MINISTER OF AGRICULTURE
(Sir Edward Archdale): With regard to
what the hon. Senator has just said, there
is a law already in force to prevent that
practice, and we do prosecute in cases
where it is done. We do not require any
new Clause in this Bill, because we can and
do prosecute under an Act already in
force. We do not allow anything to be
marked "Irish Creamery Butter" and sold
unless it is manufactured in that particular creamery. New Zealand butter
bought in the winter may be sold as butter,
but it may not be marked as "Irish
Creamery Butter."
Mr. LESLIE: Before we pass from this
Clause I would like the right hon. Gentleman to consider, with a view to making
an insertion at a later date, whether it
would not be better to tell us what is
meant by butter. There is nothing in the
Bill which connects it up with the earlier
definition of butter-I think it is in the
old Act of 1887 or 1889-and although
we all understand what is meant by butter
it would seem desirable that we should
consider whether for completeness the Act
should not have a definition of what is
butter, probably by connecting it with the
Act on the Statute Book. I am sure that
is a matter that will be taken into consideration.
Sir EDWARD ARCHDALE: Butter
is a substance made by a creamery.
Mr. LESLIE: But the Bill does not
say so, that is all.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 2 (Conditions relating to
registration).
The Ministry may refuse to register
any premises as aforesaid unless the
owner or occupier applying for the registration of the premises undertakes to
comply with such rules as the Ministry
may prescribe as to the manner in which
the manufacture, blending, reworking
or treatment of, or trade in, butter and
cream is to be carried on, and unless the
Ministry is satisfied that-
(f) in the case of each central
creamery the
manager, assistant
manager or any butter-maker employed therein, and in the case of each
auxiliary creamery, the manager
thereof, holds qualifications of such
standard as the Ministry may prescribe: Provided that where a person
employed in premises eligible for
registration at the date of the passing
of this Act furnishes to the Ministry
particulars as to his qualifications and
experience, together with a certificate
by the owner or occupier of the premises as to the period and nature of his
employment, such person may, with
the approval of the Ministry, be
deemed to possess the necessary qualifications.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in
Clause 2, page 2, line 42, to leave out from
the word "blending" to the word "is"
(butter and cream is) in page 3, line 1, and
insert, "or reworking of butter, or the
treatment of or trade in, butter or
cream." This is a slight re-arrangement of
words which I suggest in order to keep
butter and cream distinct. If we look
back at the commencement of the Bill we
see that our concern with cream is limited
to its extraction from milk, and we are
here inserting words which, although I
am sure they do not cause any real misunderstanding in our own minds, do suggest that the Ministry may make rules to
prescribe the manner in which the manufacture, blending, and reworking of cream
can be carried on.
That cannot possibly be what it really
means, but I think it is not desirable
303 Dairy Produce Bill-

THE SENATE
Committee.
[Mr. Leslie]
that in a Measure which is intended to
be a bit of advertising propaganda we
should give any opportunity for casting
aspersions upon us. In the case of outbreaks of Foot and Mouth Disease it is
always said that it originates from Ireland, and there seems to be a slight difficulty here which is merely a matter of
words. I suggest we should leave out
certain words and put in others which I
think better express the intention of the
Bill.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: This is a
drafting Amendment. As it stands the
wording is not calculated to lead to any
ambiguity. My hon. Friend may not be
aware that it is a feasible proposition to
rework butter back to cream. The Clause
as drafted would enable the Ministry to
control any such practice if attempted in
Northern Ireland, whereas the position if
the Amendment were accepted would be
open to serious doubt. Therefore I regret
I cannot accept the Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: I do not press it.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
It is
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in
Clause 2, to leave out paragraph (f). This
is a very much more serious matter.
the question of requiring as a condition of
licence that a central or auxiliary creamery
shall not employ as a manager or assistant
manager, and, in the case of a central
creamery, any butter-maker unless they
have qualifications of such a standard as
the Ministry may prescribe. It seems to
me to be paying too high a price for the
possibility of commercial prosperity. There
is such a thing as liberty, and the proposal
here is to allow the Ministry to dictate to
people who are carrying on an undertaking
with money which is their money, not
public money, as to whom they shall employ. It is, I think, obvious that such a
power as this is open to the gravest abuse.
It is further obvious, I think, that so far
at all events as people already employed
are concerned, they stand to lose their
occupation, and their source of livelihood
is at the discretion of the Ministry, without any power of appeal whatever.
I do not know to what extent it is considered that these tremendous powers are
necessary for the working of the Bill, but
I feel that in a Second Chamber we ought
to consider very carefully indeed whether
such a power should be placed in the hands
of any Ministry. I cannot satisfy myself
that the Ministry should have these
powers, and therefore I move the deletion
of the whole paragraph.
Mr. McHUGH: I am very sorry that
I cannot agree with the hon. and learned
Senator who has moved this Amendment.
I have had some twenty years' experience
of creameries and I have found nothing so
destructive to a creamery as to allow an incompetent person to take charge of it,
because in that case the business becomes
bankrupt. I myself was connected with a
creamery to which was attached an unqualified person, and the result was that in
a few years we found the creamery was in
a state of bankruptcy. We then appointed
another person who was fully qualified and
he still continues in charge of this creamery, which at the present time has some
£3,000 invested in War Loan Stock and
is prospering. Therefore I cannot support the Amendment.
Mr. MCALLISTER: I rise to support
the Amendment on two grounds. The
first is because I think the Bill should state
in detail what are the qualifications that
the Ministry will require. Are these
qualifications to be subject to the whim of
an official in the Ministry of Agriculture
who may not be in a position to determine
what practical qualifications are necessary?
In the second place I think the paragraph
is quite unnecessary for the reason that I
understand that in another part of the Bill
there is provision for the inspectors of the
Ministry to go into a creamery and inspect
the butter, for the manufacture of which
the manager or assistant
is
manager
responsible.
I think that is as good a test as can possibly be applied as to the competence of
these persons.
If they are not turning
out the goods in a proper manner the
Ministry have their remedy under that
other Clause. I do not think the Ministry
should have uncontrolled discretion in this
matter. There is nothing in the Bill to
indicate what the qualifications are to be,
or whether they are to be practical or
theoretical. For these reasons I shall
support the Amendment.
Mr. DORMAN: I rise to oppose the
Amendment. I have had practical experience of creameries in that part of the
country where creameries are most extensive, and for some years I took some little
interest in them. I can confirm what was
said by Senator McHugh, namely, that an
incompetent person has very often been
Dairy Produce Bill-
Committee.
5 DECEMBER 1929
the cause of the destruction of an apparently prosperous co-operative creamery.
I would like to remind hon. Members that
it is not merely the quality of the product
on which the success of a creamery depends. That may be quite satisfactory to
the inspector, but a creamery manager may
by other means break up a co-operative
creamery and turn it over to some private
enterprise whose interests are not in sympathy with the co-operative movement. I
know that that has been done and that cooperative efforts have been ruined by such
means.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: I also oppose
the Amendment on the ground that the
Ministry of Agriculture have a better opportunity of knowing the qualifications
that are necessary for a creamery manager
and they have not acted arbitrarily in
cases where such appointments have been
made in the past. They will send the
applications to the creamery management
and ask them to recommend perhaps the
three best. That also relieves the management of the necessity of appointing a local
man where otherwise they would have to
do it against their better judgment.
This
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
proposal means that the Ministry could
not refuse to register creameries no
