conducted. Creamery work calls for
some degree of technical skill and a
standard of qualifications is accordingly
most essential. The people most concerned--the creamery managers-and the

provisions of this sub-section. The Ministry provides facilities for the training of
creamery staffs, and in the circumstances
no argument as to the lack of suitably
trained applicants for the positions enumerated can be adduced. I regret I
cannot accept the Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: The arguments against
the Amendment seem to be that it is possible a creamery concern may get into
financial difficulty if it is not managed
by a person of the description in the subsection. It seems to me that that is the
business of the proprietors of the
creamery and that it is not the business
of Parliament. I hate putting the House
to the trouble of a Division on small
matters, but this is a first-class matter
of principle touching the liberty of the
subject, and I am unable to withdraw
the Amendment.
Question put. "That the paragraph be
left out."

(1) Where in the opinion of the

before any premises can be deemed suitable for registration as aforesaid, and

such period as the Ministry may fix,
the Ministry may provisionally register
such premises.

Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in

beg to move.

Viscount CHARLEMONT: It is unnecessary to provide for the cancellation

to take and then only after all other

CLAUSE 5 (Restriction on sending
butter and cream to Great Britain, Irish
Free State, and Isle of Man).
Except as otherwise provided by this
Act, a person shall not send or attempt
to send butter or cream from Northern
Ireland to Great Britain, the Irish
Free State, or the Isle of Man unless
he holds a licence for that purpose
under this Act, and any person who
sends or attempts to send butter or
cream out of Northern Ireland in
contravention of the provisions of this
section shall be guilty of an offence
under this Act:
Provided that this section shall not
apply to-
(a) packages of butter, the total
gross weight of which does not exceed the maximum weight prescribed
by warrant under the Post Office
Acts for parcels sent by post; or
(b) individual packages of butter
not exceeding twenty-eight pounds.
in weight despatched by a producer

or occupier of registered premises.

Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in Clause
5, page 5, line 22, to leave out the word
"section" ("provided that this section")
and insert instead, "this part of the Act."
There seems to me to be a little
difficulty here. The Clause is intended to prohibit the sending of
butter
cream out of Northern
Ireland without a licence, and then it
goes on to give exemptions in the case of
packages of butter below a certain weight.
If the whole of this was in one Section I
should not be under the necessity of
troubling the Committee, but in Sub-
Clause (3) of Clause 6 the Ministry may
make rules for prescribing the standard
of quality and receptacles. As it stands
it seems to me to be controvertible that
the provisions of that Sub-Clause (3) will

Committee.
apply to the packages we have just been

is not right now, and it further appears
to me that it may be desirable at a later
stage, when the other Amendments to the
provision have been dealt with, to remove
the proviso altogether from where it is
and put it into Part II of the Bill. There
are at least two other Amendments dealing with that, and for the sake of keeping the matters dealt with in Sub-section
(3) separate, I think we should make
this change. I accordingly beg to move.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: This
Amendment would have the effect of debarring the Ministry from regulating as
to packages not coming within the
licensing provisions of this Part of the
Bill. That would, for instance, rule out
regulating as to the marking of packages
not under licence-a provision that might
well be found to be desirable. Needless
to say, it is not intended to place any
difficulties in the way of the small quantities referred to but freedom of action in
regard to such packages is very desirable.
Therefore, I cannot accept the Amendment.
I
Mr. DORMAN: I want to draw the
Minister's attention to the fact that in
other parts of Ireland much injury has
been done to the reputation of Irish
creamery butter by the carrying on of a
small postal parcel trade in butter.
know, personally, that in some districts
creamery butter was brought into
disrepute in Great Britain because of the

trade whose supplies were obtained from
miscellaneous sources, and butter made on
farms was sent by post to Great Britain
as creamery butter. I personally knew
men who carried on an extremely large
trade in those small postal packages, and
because of the lack of supervision on the
part of the authorities creamery butter
was brought into disrepute. I do not
know if the practice exists in Northern
Ireland, but I should like to draw the
attention of the Minister of Agriculture
to the facts I have stated.

or

auxiliary creamery any unclean, contaminated, or stale milk, or milk which

Mr. LESLIE: 1 beg to move in page
7, line 6, to leave cut "or internally
rusty." The object of this Amendment,
which is to leave out these words in the
first sub-clause and leave them undisturbed in the second sub-clause, is to ascertain if I can on whom the responsibility rests for the fact that the can in
which the milk is sent out is or is not
internally rusty. In my own part of the
country the cans used are mostly those
supplied by the creamery. They are not
the property of the man supplying the
milk. It seems to me that that prevails
generally. If that is so it would be unfair to place the difficulty of deciding
whether a can is internally rusty or not
on the man who supplies the milk.
would be quite right to place it on the
creamery which is responsible. For that
reason I put down the Amendment in
order that we may get an explanation.
I do think we ought to put the saddle on
the right horse, and it occurred to me
we could very well leave sub-clause (2)
alone and take the words out of sub-clause
(I).
It
Mr. McHUGH: I am very sorry to
find that the hon. Senator has fallen
completely into error when he states that
all the vessels supplied to the milk suppliers are the property of the creamery
company. Such is not the case.
Mr. LESLIE :
Mr. McHUGH:
I did not say always.
The ordinary course
of procedure is that the creamery companies purchase the cans wholesale, and
sell them to the supplier as required,
and the supplier is responsible for the
can or vessel which he may purchase
while it is in his possession. Carelessness
or indifference very often arises, and
small suppliers allow the cans to become
rusty. I think this is one of the most
important provisions that could be in the
Act. If you want to have really pure
butter you
can only make it out of
pure clean milk. It cannot be made with
dirty cans.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: That it is
highly undesirable to permit the use, by
milk suppliers to a creamery for the
carriage of milk, of internally rusty
vessels will not, I think, be disputed. If
the words "internally rusty" be deleted
it becomes a question as to whether the
term "dirty" includes "internally
rusty." It is debatable-and in any case
dirt can be removed, but rust cannot, at
least in the sense of permanency.
The
protective coating of the inside of a milk
can having once been corroded no amount
of cleaning will make it fit to contain
milk, for immediately the affected area
comes in contact with the milk oxidisation re-commences. As regards putting
the saddle on the right horse, what we
are doing is putting the two saddles on
the two horses-(Laughter)-the producer
and also the man who takes it into the
creamery. I cannot agree to the Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE : Again I think the
Amendment has served its purpose
ask leave to withdraw it.
Amendment by leave withdrawn.

CLAUSE 13 (Prosecution of offences
and penalties).
(1) All offences under this Act shall
be prosecuted, and all fines imposed.
thereunder shall
be
recovered, in
manner provided by the Summary
Jurisdiction (Ireland) Acts; and a person aggrieved by a conviction or order
made by a court of summary jurisdiction on determining any information
under this Act may appeal in manner
provided by the said Summary Jurisdiction Acts.
(2) A prosecution for an offence
under this Act shall not be instituted
except by or with the consent of the
Ministry.
(3) Any person who is found guilty
of an offence under this Act shall be
liable on conviction--
(a) in the case of a first offence, to
a fine of one pound or any greater
sum not exceeding twenty pounds,
(b) in the case of a second offence,
to a fine of three pounds or any
greater sum not exceeding fifty
pounds,
(c) in the case of a subsequent
offence, to a fine of five pounds or
any greater sum not exceeding one
hundred pounds, or, if in the opinion.
of the court, the offence was committed by the personal act, default, or culpable negligence of the
person accused, and the court is of
opinion that a fine will not meet the
circumstances of the case, to imprisonment for a term not exceeding
three months:
Provided that an offence under this
Act shall not be deemed to be a second
or subsequent offence unless the former
offence or offences were an offence or
offences under the same Part of this
Act
(4) Any person who fails to comply
with the provisions of any rules made
by the Ministry under this Act shall
be guilty of an offence under this Act:
Provided that the penalty to which a
person shall be liable on conviction for
such failure shall be a fine of one
pound, or any greater sum not exceeding twenty pounds, for each offence.

so often spoken about the minimum fine
without producing any effect the
Department of Agriculture. Though other

afraid, to get in the thin end of the

As
I am sure the Committee know I am

Therefore, not for the first time, I move

Colonel PAKENHAM: I rise once
again to support my hon. Friend on my
right on this question of minimum fines.
The last time I addressed the House was on
the Noxious Weeds Bill. We tried very
hard to allow the magistrates to adjudicate on these cases. It was said the fines
put on were perfectly ridiculous before
the Department demanded the minimum
fine. Ône had to yield with grace to the
Department's demand. I agree that
If you
this is a new Bill, and a new occasion,
and why not give the magistrates a
chance? Let them have some power
to deal with the cases in the way they
think right. Here you are putting on in
the first case a minimum fine of £1, and
not exceeding £20. I suggest to the
right hon. Gentleman the Minister of
Agriculture that he will find that even
serious cases will probably be treated just
the same as minor cases. Nobody will
put on more than the £1 fine.
leave it in the power of the magistrates
to impose fines according to
seriousness of the case I think
will find you will do just as well
and will probably get proper fines.
You will also approach the magistrates psychologically in the proper frame
of mind. I can assure you that sitting as
a magistrate adjudicating on these cases
we feel rather sore that our powers are so
small-that we are treated exactly like

the
you
an
Mr. MCALLISTER: I rise again to
I feel as one
support this Amendment.
associated with the law that this is
exceedingly bad basis. It has no justification and every lawyer that I ever came
across is entirely opposed to it. I myself
have seen cases in the courts where the
magistrates were embarrassed. They felt
it was a technical offence that had been
committed by someone without
any
criminal intent. They were compelled by
the Act to impose a fine out of all proportion to what they believed to be just and
fair. By the establishment of this system
of minimum fines it is Parliament that is
adjudicating; it is not the magistrates'
courts. We are prejudging a case the circumstances of which we have not heard at
all. I think that is a very wrong principle.
If
I have grave doubts, to put it mildly,
that this system of minimum fines is not
merely for the purpose of raising revenue.
I formed that opinion on the Motor Bill.
I am sure if those associated with the administration of justice carry their minds
back to the number of fines before and
after the Motor Act will see the revenue
raised by means of the minimum fine. I
think the Committee will come to the conclusion that at all events there is some
justification for suggesting that this is an
indirect method of raising revenue.
you take the collective effect of the minimum fines liable to be imposed under other
Acts of Parliament you will see there is a
substantial revenue coming to the State.
I think it is not a healthy source or healthy
means. I think if the State wants to impose revenue or collect money it ought to
be done expressly. But to raise money by
means of these wholesale minimum fines
cannot be justified or vindicated at all. I
have no doubt in my own mind that that
is the object of these minimum fines. I
say again that anyone who looks at the
papers will see the enormous sums raised
in this way.
ence.
Mr. McHUGH: I am very pleased to
admit that I fully agree with the Amendment. I do not approach it just from the
legal aspect, because I really cannot follow
it. I approach it from practical experi-
The people who will be affected by
this particular fine are persons who have
two or three or four cows. It arises in this
way. Owing to the very small quantity
of milk they do not think it is worth while
bringing it daily to the creamery. Very
often it remains two or three days if they
do not have more than two
or three
gallons altogether. These are the people
who, unfortunately, will be very hard hit
by this minimum fine which has been inserted in this Bill. Living as I do on the
borders of the Free State I know they have
the very same Act in force for some time
about cleanliness and so on, but there is
no minimum fine whatsoever.
Sir EDWARD ARCHDALE: I think
they have.
Dairy Produce Bill-
Committee.
5 DECEMBER 1929
Mr. McHUGH: The reason why I say
they have no minimum fines is that I have
been in the court when these cases have
been heard. The justice has imposed a
ane of 5s and has always given every individual that comes before him a lecture on
the subject, pointing out the seriousness of
the offence and stating that if it is repeated the defendant will be dealt with
far more severely.
But here we have a minimum fine of £1
on a poor unfortunate farmer in very low
circumstances. I really do not think it
is fair to these people that we should impose these heavy penalties for the first
offence. There should be some discretionary power given to the local magistratesalthough I must admit I do not admire
the local magistrates as a whole. Some
of them would shock you with their ridiculous decisions. Very often it is the individual and not the evidence. But in this
particular case I am fully satisfied they
w.ll deal out the law fairly. Some discretionary power should be given, especially as the Government are continually
appointing these magistrates. They must
be of opinion that they possess some qualities. I hope the right hon. Gentleman
will reconsider the matter.
Mr. DICKSON: I have always opposed
Amendments of the kind which has frequently been put forward by the hon.
Senator Leslie. I think that the last
speaker has given his case away when he
said that it was the individual and not
the evidence that was considered.
Mr. McHUGH: I said on some occasions. I hope you do not do it.
Campbell, Mr. T. J.
Dobbs, Colonel A. F.
Dorman, Mr. Robert
Belfast, The Lord Mayor of
Armstrong, Mr. H. B.
Bangor, Viscout
Barclay, Mr. William
Mr. DICKSON: I am absolutely convinced that we should have had freedom
from disease of many kinds if we had had
the minimum fines of a considerable
amount-in the Education Act and everything else. The thing is really turned
into an absolute farce. I do not wonder
at the legal profession always asking for
no minimum fine because they are often
asked to come down to defend a client to
get the fine reduced. But when they have
no power to get it reduced they are not
employed so often. I do wonder at
Colonel Pakenham. He must have a
better bench of magistrates than are generally distributed in several areas.
Mr. McHUGH: Then why should you
have them at all?
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Legal proceedings in these cases are instituted with
reluctance and fail in their object if
leniency, not fully warranted by the cir
cumstances of the case, is shown by the
Bench. The Statutes in which similar
provisions have been embodied have
worked most satisfactorily. For these
reasons I regret that I am unable to accept
the Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: What the noble Viscount
has just said exhibits the cloven foot in a
most marked way. He speaks of leniency
and admits that it is the Government
Department that tries the case and not the
court.

The following Amendment stood on the

Line 27, to leave out "of five pounds or

Mr. LESLIE: The next Amendment 1
move is in line 36, after "offence" ("subsequent offence unless") to insert "if a
period of twelve months or more has
elapsed since the last previous conviction
of the person charged and." I may say
Pakenham, Colonel H. A.
Glendinning, Mr. J. G
Long, Mr. J. A.
Quigley, Mr. A.
Sinclair, Colonel Thomas
at once that I should not have moved this
Amendment if the previous one had been
accepted. This is following the lead
which has been given by the Government
in the Motor Bill which, I understand, is
now before Parliament-that if a period
of twelve months or more has elapsed since
the last previous conviction the next conviction shall not be considered a second or
subsequent offence. It seems to me that
that is desirable in regard to penalties
which the court has no power to alter so
far as amount goes.
319 Dairy Produce Bill-

THE SENATE
Committee.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: It will be
noted that a second or subsequent offence
is not to be so regarded unless the former
offence was against the same part of the
Act. By this restriction the second or
subsequent offence is more nearly the repetition of the former offence. To have
twelve months elapse before such a repetition could be regarded as a second or
subsequent offence defeats the possibility
of dealing with the persistent offender. I
cannot but think that the safeguard already provided is adequate and I must,
accordingly, resist the Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: I do not press it.
Amendment by leave withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 14 (Application of fines and
payments) ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
CLAUSE 15 (Records and returns).
(1) Any person aggrieved by a decision of the Ministry-
(a) refusing to register or renew the
registration of any premises under Part I of this Act; or
(b) cancelling the registration of any
premises under Part I of this
Act;
may appeal to the county court having
jurisdiction where the premises concerned are situate, and the decision of
that court shall be final and conclusive.
(2) Rules of court regulating the procedure and costs of an appeal to the
county court under this section may be
made by the authority having power to
make rules and orders for regulating
the practice of the civil bill courts.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in line 17,
at the end ("of this Act") to insert "or
(c) refusing, revoking or suspending a
licence under Part II of this Act."
Campbell, Mr. T. J.
Dorman, Mr. Robert
Belfast, The Lord Mayor of
Armstrong, Mr. H. B.
Bangor, Viscount
Barclay, Mr. William
Charlemont, Viscount
The
CONTENT
Leslie, Mr. J. G.
McAllister, Mr. T. S.
suggestion here is that the Bill should give
the same right of appeal to the person
whose licence is refused, revoked, and so
on, under Part II as has been given to
persons aggrieved in respect of registration
under Part I. The Committee probably
are aware that when this Bill first made its
appearance there was no appeal given
either for Part I or Part II. But owing
to pressure which I understand it has been
impossible to resist, even if it had been desired, an appeal was given under Part I.
But Part II seemed to escape attention.
I do not know why the Government
offered an appeal in Part I and did not
offer it in Part II. But it rather suggests
I am afraid that the authors of the Bill
are trying to treat the public as some
foolish people treat a big dog-they try
to see how much it will stand. It is certainly not fair that there should not be
an appeal under Part II. It is illogical
that there should be an appeal under Part
I and not under Part II. It seems to me
that the arguments for the one are just as
strong as those for the other. I hope it
is the intention of the Government to give
this appeal under Part II.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The licences refer to the export of butter and any
revocation of a licence will be for such
offences as exporting butter of inferior
quality. There is no question of appeal in
such circumstances. The revocation of a
licence does not put a trader out of business, it merely prohibits the continuance
of his export trade. I am unable to
accept the Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: And I am afraid I am
unable to withdraw it.

5 DECEMBER 1929
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, at the
end of Clause 19 to add :
"and shall continue in force for five
years and no longer unless Parliament
otherwise determines.
This Bill, the discussion of which is now
nearing an end, does give very great and
very wide powers. To some extent it
breaks new ground. Whether it is or is
not going to succeed in its object is still
so to speak, in the lap of the gods.
It
seems to me that it is very desirable that
the Bill should come up automatically
again for review by Parliament within a
reasonable time. I do not suggest that
it should come up again within the next
twelve months. Time must be given to
see how we get on, and I suggest the Bill
should continue in force for five years
and no longer unless Parliament otherwise determines.
The effect of inserting that Amendment will be that at the end of five years,
if it is considered desirable, the Bill can
be prolonged under the Expiring Laws
Bill, or, if the provisions in the Bill, including these tremendous powers, have
not proved quite successful, they can be
reviewed. These powers are very farreaching, and I think it would be very
desirable to insert the words
Paper. Accordingly I beg to move.
on the
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The existing
legislative machinery surely provides
sufficient security for a review of any Act
at any time. I cannot, therefore accept
the Amendment.
of
I
Mr. McALLISTER: I hope the fact
that this Amendment has been put on
the Paper and moved will draw the attention of the Government to the uneasy
feeling that Members seem to have with
regard to the wholesale control
the assets, property, lives and liberty of
the people by officials in the various
Departments of the Government.
agree with the remarks of Senator
Leslie on the question of appeal, and
I do not think there could be any
harm done by giving the right of
appeal in the matters he has mentioned.
After all, it is a very serious thing to put
perhaps three-quarters of a person's entire
business into the hands of officials without the right of appeal.
The CHAIRMAN: I am afraid the
hon. Member is not in order on this
Amendment. He should speak on the
Clause.
Mr. LESLIE: I do not think I ought
to withdraw the Amendment.
Amendment negatived.
Question put, "That the Clause stand
part of the Bill."
Mr. MCALLISTER: I had practically
concluded what I wanted to say. I desire to protest against the practice in
nearly all Bills of placing practically the
whole future of the business of Northern
Ireland in the hands of Government
officials. If you take this Bill you will
find that three-quarters of the entire business of creameries, representing their export trade, is to be placed under the
control of Departmental officials. I do
not suggest that an official would deliberately run across a person, but human
nature being what it is, it is possible that a
man might be refused a licence for no
just cause, and it is a terrible thing to
think that his means of existence is gone
and he has no right of appeal whatever.
I think that is entirely wrong, and
it is contrary to the principle of the liberty
of the subject. It is a very dangerous
principle and one that I shall always
strongly oppose in any Bill.
This Bill
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
is to regulate the quality of butter exported to England and other places. As
for anybody in the business being deprived
of his livelihood by the action of an
official, if such an unlikely thing ever
took place, the person aggrieved has the
right to have the question raised in Parliament, and if any wrong had been done it
would be righted.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Clause ordered to stand part of the
Bill.

Petroleum Bill-
Third Reading.
THE SENATE
Betting (Juvenile Messengers) Bill.
Order for Third Reading read.
Viscount BANGOR: I do not propose
to proceed with the Third Reading of this.
Bill to-day.
Petroleum (Consolidation) Bill.
Order for Third Reading read.
Viscount BANGOR: In moving the
Third Reading of this Bill I would again
draw the attention of the House to the
fact that this Bill merely consolidates existing Acts and, therefore, that it is not
open to amendment.
Some information was desired at the
Committee Stage by hon. Members, and
I regret that I was not in a position to
reply at the time with the alacrity with
which these questions were put.
However, I have gone into the matters under
discussion, and I can now give the hon.
Members the information they seek. If
there are any other points on which hon.
Members are in doubt, I will do my best
to obtain the necessary information and
ive it to them later.
With reference to the point raised by
Senator Armstrong, namely, the regulations governing the quantity of petrol
spirit which can be stored in any one
storehouse, Section 10 of this Bill gives
the Minister of Home Affairs power to
make regulations as to the keeping of
petroleum spirit by persons intending
it for use in light motor vehicles, that is,
ordinary motor cars, motor boats, or any
air craft. This is the Clause that affects
the ordinary user of a motor car for
private purposes. At present the regulations governing this matter are those
which were made by the Secretary of
State in 1907 under the Locomotives.on
Highways Act of 1896, but of course in
the future they will be made by the
Minister of Home Affairs.
These regulations were allowed by the Ministry of
Home Affairs to remain in force until
new ones are brought in, and this was
mentioned in the Petroleum (Amendment) Act earlier this Session.
means a
Under the existing regulations the
amount of petroleum spirit which may
be stored in any one store-house (and here
the expression "store-house "
room, building, coach-house, garage, etc.)
is limited to 60 gallons. I do not think
that hon. Senators need be concerned
hat they will be in any way inconvenienced in the future, as the House is
well aware that the Minister of Home
Affairs is alive to the present-day needs
of private users, and his only care is to see
that petroleum is not stored even by
private persons thoughtlessly or in a dangerous manner.
If a private person wishes to store a
larger quantity of petroleum than the
regulations permit, then he must go to a
local authority for a licence in exactly
the same way as a dealer would obtain a
licence from the local authorities under
the scale of fees laid down in the First
Schedule to this Bill. I can assure this
House that as soon as this Bill becomes
law, the Minister of Home Affairs will
lose no time in issuing regulations to
govern this matter.
During the course of the debate on this
Bill, the question of the local authorities
who are empowered to grant licences for
petroleum spirit was also raised-I think
by Senator Colonel Sharman-Crawford
and Senator Long-and this question is.
I think, clearly dealt with in Section 2 of
the Bill which defines local authority. Its
enforcement is the duty of the local
authority concerned, and Section 17 permits any officer authorised by a local
authority to make certain inspections and
tests. The Government has under consideration at the present time a suggestion
forward to them by the county
councils, that certain members of the
R.U.C. should be allowed to perform
these duties for the county councils, and
I hope that at an early date the necessary
arrangements in connection with such a
scheme may be completed.
put
The
All licences must be issued by the local
authorities set forth in Section 2.
duties of inspection, obtaining samples for
test, etc., will be carried out for county
councils, should they so desire, on an
agency basis, by the police. As soon as
this Bill goes through the detailed arrangements will be made with the county
councils by the Home Office. I beg to
move.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved: "That this Bill do now pass."
--(Viscount Bangor).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
And a Message was sent to the House
of Commons to acquaint them that the
Senate have agreed to the said Bill, without any Amendment.

Standing Orders.
Local and Divorce Bills.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): I beg to move the Amendments to the Standing Orders which are
set out on the Paper. The Joint Committee have recommended the Amendments. The suggested alterations to the
fees for Local Bills have the effect of reducing the fees to be paid by promoters
of a Bill for the hearing before the Joint
Committee, and putting a further reduction on the fees to be paid in Committee for opponents to a Bill after the
first day. They also suggest a more
uniform scale of increase in the fees to be
paid in accordance with the money to be
raised under a Bill, as the previous scale
raised the fees disproportionately.
As regards Divorce Bills, the suggested
alterations have the effect of reducing the
total amount of fees to be paid from £44
to £12 10s Od, as this latter figure more
accurately represents the cost of such a
Bill to Parliament.
Mr. LESLIE: I thought perhaps the
House would have been informed that it
was the opinion of the Committee which
sat on this matter that the reduced scale
of fees should apply to every Bill now in
Parliament. It was, I think, intended
that that should be made clear.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I think
that is understood.
STANDING ORDER No. 192.
The Table of the Fees to be charged
at the Houses of Parliament, being Appendix B to the Standing Orders relative to Local Bills, shall apply to
Divorce Bills, unless otherwise directly
stated in the said Appendix, as if in
such Table the words "the Petitioner'
were substituted for the words "the
Promoters' and the words "the Respondent" for the words "the Opponents."
Amendment made:
Leave out Order 192 and insert the following Order :-192. "The Table of Fees
to be charged at both Houses of Parliament shall be the Table set out in
Appendix (B).”

STANDING ORDERS OF THE
SENATE.
STANDING ORDER No. XXV. (Two
stages of a Bill not to be taken the same
day).
No two stages of a Bill shall be taken
at the same sitting, but for the purpose of this Order, the Third Reading
and Passing of a Bill shall not be
treated as separate stages.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, in Standing Order No. XXV., line
3, after the word "the" ("of this Order
the ") to insert the words, "First and
Second Readings of a Bill, and". The
reason for this proposed alteration is to
enable the Senate to take the First and
Second Readings of a Bill on the one day
if it is advantageous to do so. Needless
to say, it will only be done in cases where
Bills have been previously circulated so
as to give hon. Members sufficient time to
become acquainted with their contents,
but it will avoid the necessity for calling
this House together to consider merely
the formal First Reading of a Bill. I beg
to move.
Mr. DORMAN: I hope the promise.
given by the Leader of the House will be
carried out. Hitherto we have had the
First and Second Reading of a Bill on
the one day and we have not had an
opportunity of reading the Bill before
being expected to deliver a Second Read-
Standing Orders-
Amendments
THE SENATE
[Mr. Dorman]
ing speech on it.
I hope that in the
future Bills will be placed in our hands
in sufficient time to enable us to master
them, before we are called upon to read
them a Second time.
Mr. MCALLISTER: I rise to oppose
this Amendment on the ground that I
do not think it is possible to do justice to
a Bill when it is read a First and Second
Time on the one day. That simply means
that a Bill is handed to Members at the
Sitting of the House and that the principal
discussion on that Bill takes place on the
same day. It is impossible to properly
examine a Bill immediately after it is
placed in one's hands. That is a principle
to which I do not feel justified in giving
my consent, because I do not think that
such a procedure will tend to produce
better legislation than is possible under
the present method.
Mr. LESLIE: I am afraid that I, too,
entertain some misgivings as to the desirability of making this change permanently
in the Standing Orders. In the past this
House has frequently consented to take
the First and Second Readings of a Bill
together, and I myself, as often as anybody else, have supported the proposal
when the state of public business seemed
to require it. But the proposal which is
now made will be, so to speak, permanent.
The noble Viscount says it is the intention
only to apply the Standing Order when
the Bills to which it relates have been
circulated beforehand. It seems to me
that if it is possible to circulate a Bill beforehand it is just as possible to circulate
with that Bill an intimation that it is proposed to suspend the Standing Order in
respect of that Bill.
Again, I do not know that, living as
many of us do in more or less remote parts
of the Province, we are entitled to rely
unduly on the expedition of the postman
in circulating Bills. I hold in my hand
a paper relating to the Orders of the Day.
I received that yesterday morning. I
observed a résumé of its contents in the
newspapers of Monday morning, and
therefore it does not seem that we do get
our papers through the post as rapidly as
we might. As the right hon, and gallant
Gentleman, who is absent to-day (Colonel
Sharman Crawford), reminded us last
Session, it is rather difficult to make
sure that a Bill introduced in another
place is as it comes to us in its
final form. Most of the Bills we
receive are marked on the back "As
amended on Report," and although it
may sometimes happen that Members of
this House are brought here for
apparently a few minutes, those few
minutes do not only serve to bring Members together in order that they may discuss in private matters relating to Bills,
but they also enable them to go to the
Library and consult books there, and so
they are much better able to express their
opinions when it comes to the Second
Reading. On the whole, I think it would
perhaps be better if this Motion were not
pressed, but if it is withdrawn I am
quite sure every Member of this House
will be ready to facilitate the business in
any given case in the ordinary manner.
Mr. LONG: I should also like very
briefly to support the objection taken to
the course suggested by the Noble Leader
of the House. As the hon. and learned
Senator (Mr. Leslie) has just stated, the
post is sometimes unreliable. For instance, the first time I saw the Orders
of the Day for to-day was on coming into
the House, and in my case it frequently
occurs that I receive the Order Paper the
day after the business on it has been
transacted. I certainly support the objection voiced by Senators McAllister and
Leslie, and in view of what the hon. and
learned Senator (Mr. Leslie) has said—
that we have never failed to agree to a
First and Second Reading being taken on
the same day when it was necessary-I
appeal to the Noble Lord to allow the
present arrangement to stand.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I thought
that the moving of this Amendment would
be entirely in the interests of the convenience of the Members of the Senate,
but I have no wish whatever to press
it. I may say that if it had been objected
to by any two or three Members I should
have withdrawn it with pleasure. I only
moved the Amendment because I thought
it would be in the interests of all concerned. While I am willing to withdraw
it I am going to take notice of what the
hon. and learned Senator (Mr. Leslie)
said about agreeing to facilitate matters
as much as possible, because in very few
cases has it been necessary for anybody
to make their promise good in such a
phenomenally short time, as the hon. and
learned Member will realize by looking at
the Order Paper. Under the circumstances I will withdraw the Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Oral Answers
Oral Answers
11 DECEMBER 1929
Questions to Minister.
PUBLIC BODIES AND
APPOINTMENTS.
Mr. S. CUNNINGHAM: I beg to ask
the Leader of the House, whether he can
state, if it is the intention of the
Government to introduce legislation preventing public bodies. from making
appointments involving the payment of
large salaries without either stating the
qualification of the candidate or inserting
the usual notice in the Gazette."

Viscount CHARLEMONT: As the
right hon. Member is aware, the Local
Government Commission made a recommendation regarding the question of the
appointment of officers of Local Government Authorities which is still under consideration by the Government with a view
to its being embodied in the new Local
Government legislation which is pending.
Until the Bill is introduced, it would
not be expedient or proper to express a
definite opinion in the matter, but it may
be stated that the gist of the recommendation of the Commission was that all
appointments of Local Government Officers
should be subject to the approval of the
Ministry of Home Affairs both in
nection with the qualifications as well as
the emoluments and in regard to removal
from office.
CON
Under present conditions, many local
government appointments are subject to
the approval of the Ministry of Home
Affairs, and in a considerable number of
cases the Ministry prescribes either by
general or special order the qualifications
to be fulfilled by the persons holding such
positions, but in a large number of other
cases the appointments are wholly in the
hands of the Local Government Authorities, and the Ministry has no jurisdiction
in regard to them. It was felt by the
Commission that this was a serious defect
in the code of Local Government laws, and
having regard to the fact that the Government is now contributing so much to
the Local Authorities by way of special
grants as well as in connection with the
derating proposals, there is undoubtedly
considerable justification for bringing the
appointments of Local Government officials
more under the purview of the Ministry,
Mr. MCALLISTER: I do not think the
noble Lord, the Leader of the House, has
met the question at issue. The point
raised by the right hon. Member is not
whether or not these appointments should
have the approval of the Ministry of
Home Affairs, but whether due notice that
vacancies exist should be published in the
appropriate papers in order that the
public generally should have an opportunity of knowing what these vacancies
are, and thus becoming candidates. for the
positions. That, I understand, is the
question raised by the right hon. Senator,
and it has not been met by the reply of
the Leader of the House. I think there
should be some assurance given that when
a public vacancy occurs it will be brought
to the notice of the general public, thereby preventing any semi-secret method f
showing favour towards a particular
candidate.
a
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Perhaps
the hon. Senator would put down
supplementary question on that point. It
is not a matter on which I can give him
an immediate answer.
UNION JACK ON CARRICKFERGUS
CASTLE.
Major DOBBS: I beg to ask the Leader
of the House whether he can state if
arrangements can be made for flying the
Union Jack from the keep of Carrickfergus Castle between the hours of 8-0
a.m. and sundown, particularly on Sundays and on special occasions.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I should
explain that Carrickfergus Castle is now
an Ancient Monument held by the
Government in trust for the public, and,
as such, would not in the ordinary course
fly a Union Jack on any day throughout
the year.
Having regard to the fact,
however, that the building had been for
many years a Military Flag Station, the
Government decided some time ago that
the Castle should be regarded as an exception to the general rule, and that the
Union Jack should be flown from it on
the days approved for the flying of flags
from Government Buildings in Northern
Ireland. The list of the approved days is
as follows:-
17th March-St. Patrick's Day.
6th May-His Majesty's Accession.
24th May-Empire Day.
26th May-Her Majesty's Birthday.
3rd June-His Majesty's Birthday.
22nd June--Coronation Day.
23rd June-Prince of Wales's Birthday.
11th November-Armistice Day.
Oral Answers
Oral Answers
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
It is desirable that uniformity of
practice in this matter should be maintained as regards buildings under Government control, but if it can be shown that
there is a pronounced desire on the part
of the local residents for such an arrangement, favourable consideration would be
given to the proposal to fly the Union
Jack from Carrickfergus Castle on Sundays between the hours of 8 a.m. and sundown, as well as on the approved days.
Mr. HILL-DICKSON: Would the
noble Lord not add 12th July to that
list (Laughter).
Mr. McHUGH: Certainly. He omitted.
that.
SCHOOL ACCOMMODATION AT
GREENISLAND.
The following Question stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Barclay :
To ask the Leader of the House whether
he is aware that the children in or near
the village of Greenisland, Co. Antrim,
of whom there are a considerable number,
have no public elementary school in the
neighbourhood at which they can attend;
whether these children do not at present
walk a distance of at least two miles to
the nearest schools, which are in unsuitable buildings and overcrowded; whether,
in some cases, the children travel daily
to Carrickfergus and Belfast, at considerable expense to their parents, owing to
the lack of a suitable local school; and
whether he will approach the Larne and
Carrickfergus Regional Education Committee in the matter.
Mr. BARCLAY: In rising to ask this
question I might say that when I went
to live in Greenisland a few months ago
the first thing I did was to inquire if
there was a church convenient, and I
was informed that there was no church.
a number of
Then, (seeing there were
children playing about I asked if there
was a school, and I was told there was
no school. Thirdly, I enquired where the
public house was, and I was informed that
there was no public house. I was greatly
surprised that I had landed upon
island where there was neither church,
school or public house. I interested myself more or less in the children, and
from inquiries I made I discovered that
an
the nearest school to the village of Greenisland was about two miles away. I
thought it was unreasonable to ask any
children of six or seven years of age to
walk four miles a day or twenty miles
a week to attend school, as they are compelled by law to do. I got into communication with one of the District
Councillors and I asked him how many
children he could guarantee would attend
a school in Greenisland if one was built,
and he said anything from 100 to 150
children. The present schools are old
and some of them are not very healthy;
they have served their day and generation
and I think they should pass away and
make room for a more modern and up-todate school.
A great number of the children of
Greenisland cannot find accommodation
in these schools two miles away. Many
of them have got to travel to Carrickfergus and Belfast to find school accommodation. There is a family living opposite
to me and the little girl has got to come
to Belfast every day to attend school.
She travels by train with her father in
the morning, and in the afternoon her
mother has to come to Belfast to take
her home. It costs the little girl about
£5 a year in train fares, and it costs
her mother about £10 a year to bring
her home. Therefore, I think pressure
should be brought to bear on the local
Regional Education Committee to provide school accommodation for the
children of this district. To-day I found
that there are 300 houses in Greenisland,
200 of which were built during the last
fifteen years.
At the present time two
builders are erecting houses in the village.
One is building 33 houses and the other
twelve or fourteen, and plans have been
passed for a number of other houses.
When those additional houses are occupied I think Greenisland will have a
strong claim for a school for its children,
and I ask this question in the hope that
the Noble Viscount will use his influence
with the Larne and Carrickfergus
Regional Committee to have a suitable
school provided for that district.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: In reply
to the hon. Member, I am aware that
there is no public elementary school in
or adjacent to the village of Greenisland.
There are, however, two schools within
two miles of this village, and a third
school somewhat further away, so that it
Ilousing Bill-
Second Reading
11 DECEMBER 1929
appears unlikely that many children have
at present as much as two miles to walk
to one of these schools. Although these
school buildings may be in some respects
defective, there is sufficient spare accommodation in them.
I have no information as to the number
of children who travel daily from Greenisland to schools in Carrickfergus or Belfast. It is, however, a fairly common
practice in districts adjacent to our cities.
and large towns that older children are
sent by their parents to the bigger urban
schools, and this, perhaps, accounts for
some, at least, of those from Greenisland
to whom the hon. Member refers.
In regard to the last part of the Question, I find in the minutes of the meeting
of the Larne and Carrickfergus Regional
Education Committee, held on 23rd
October last, it was agreed that the question of the site for a new school at
Greenisland should be considered by the
local School Committee; their proposals
do not appear to have yet been submitted
to the Regional Committee. I can, however, assure the hon. Member that my
Ministry will not lose sight of this
matter.
I regret that my Ministry cannot provide facilities for either churches or public
houses. (Laughter).
Business of the House.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, "That Standing Order No. XXV.
be suspended for this day's sitting, for the
purpose of taking the Second Reading of
the Housing Bill.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Housing Bill.
Brought from the House of Commons
and read a First time.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, "That this Bill be now read a
Second time." The main object of this
Bill is to prolong for a further year payment of subsidies under the Housing Acts,
that is, until 1st April, 1931. The opportunity has also been taken of inserting
some new proposals in the Bill. There is
a change in regard to the superficial area
which qualifies for the grant, and also
some provisions which it is hoped will have
the effect of checking certain abuses
which have grown up in connection with
the building of these houses.
In regard to the question of extending
the subsidy arrangement for another year,
I think the Government can claim that
very successful encouragement has been
given to the building of the particular
class of house which has in recent years
been eligible for the grant, namely, the
smaller type up to 650 superficial feet.
For the year ended 31st March last no less
than 5,596 such houses were approved for
subsidy, and from the latest figures it is
probable that this record will be beaten in
the following year. At the same time
there is evidence that the need and demand for this smaller type of house is
still acute, and the continuance of the
subsidy for a year longer would appear to
be fully justified.
It will be remembered that in this
House last year when the Bill similar to
this one was under consideration some
criticism was made that 650 feet was too
small an area. I am glad to say that
these views have been considered and it is
proposed in the Bill before us to increase
this maximum area to seven hundred
superficial feet. It is to be hoped that
this will not mean that houses will be
erected which the working man—the class
which it is intended to assist-will be unable to afford.
I now come to the measures taken in
the Bill to prevent abuses. Clause 2
gives the Ministry of Home Affairs power
to refuse the grant on a house if it is satisfied that there is an intention to enlarge,
alter, or amalgamate it with another
house, so that when so altered it would
have failed to qualify for the subsidy; and
if within three years of the payment of the
grant it is found that such an alteration
has been made, there is power to recover
any amount already paid. Sub-clause
(3) makes this amount a charge against
the property, and Sub-clause (4) puts the
local council in the same position as the
Ministry in regard to the recovery of any
grant which the council may also have
made towards the offending house. Subclause (5) of Clause 2 enacts further
measures to be taken by the local council
if approval is sought in the ordinary
way for alterations to these houses within
three years, and Clause 3 provides for the
cessation of exemption from rates in these
cases, where exemption had been granted
by the local authority.
Housing Bill—
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
Clause 4 deals with another class of
case which has become fairly numerous.
i has been open to persons in the past to
build subsidy houses at the seaside or in
other places for occasional or holiday
occupation, and as these houses are not
really the kind for which the subsidy is
intended, it is proposed that they should
not qualify for the grant.
I hope that I have given the House a
sufficient explanation of the new proposals
in this Bill. I beg to move.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I rise to express my
feeling of profound disappointment with
this Housing Bill as a contribution to the
solution of the housing problem in
Northern Ireland.
To my mind the Bill
is an instance of dealing with a grave
social problem by a policy of shreds and
patches. Eight years have gone since the
Government took office, and the housing
problem-the gravest of post-war problems-is far from a solution. Lord
Craigavon's policy has left largely unsatisfied the demand for houses at rents
within the reach of the better-paid working classes, and has wholly failed to provide houses at rents which the lower-paid
working classes can afford. Our slum
problem is worse to-day than in the year
1921. The work of housing the people
has gone briskly ahead in England. In
Northern Ireland the advance has been
dilatory and hesitating. About 1,300,000
houses of very good design have been built
in England and Wales by public and
private effort since the war ended. The
cost has been almost £10,000,000 a year.
These houses have met the necessities of
those of the clerks and well-to-do working classes who are able to pay a rent of
fifteen shillings a week or upwards.
There, as here, the slum problem awaits.
solution.
We have that problem in an acute
form. Within half a mile of Castle
Junction in Belfast belts of slums exist
which are a disgrace to this city and to
civilization. There are still about 2,700
families in Belfast occupying one room
each, over 8,000 families occupying two
rooms, and over 10,000 families occupying three rooms. In the other urban
areas this unhealthy overcrowding is repeated. Many slum houses are unfit for
healthy human habitation, and, worse
still, there are far too many people in
them. How many of the sufferers from
tuberculosis in Belfast have proper
breathing space in their homes? In the
estimates for the current year £110,000
is set apart for housing. That is the
average annual sum voted for the purpose. The sum is about one-eleventh of
your contribution to the Imperial
Exchequer for the year. One would
think Northern Ireland has no serious
housing problem. A high civic dignitary is stated to have said lately that
there are now so many vacant houses in
Belfast that further building is unnecessary. In fact, of the 55,000 houses
in Belfast rated at £8 and under only 150
are vacant. Of the houses over £8 valuation about 700 are vacant. At what
rents are these latter houses to be let?
At rents averaging from 14s a week and
upwards. To large numbers of workers
that is a sum altogether beyond their
power to pay.
What is the record of the Government
for housing? In Northern Ireland from
1919, to September, 1929, 14,667 houses
were built with the aid of public subsidies. By March, 1930, probably 3,000
more will have been built. Our population is to that of England and Wales
as 1 to 31. I do not say this test is apt,
but on the ratio of population at least
40,000 houses should have been erected
in Northern Ireland since the Armistice.
Large numbers of our better-paid working
classes still wait for proper housing.
Doubtless the rate of building has been
quickened under the Housing Act of
1927, which discontinued subsidies for
any houses completed after 31st March,
1928, the superficial area of which exceeded
650 square feet, and sanctioned the payment of an increased subsidy-£80 or
£100 per house-in respect of small
The rents of this type
type houses.
average 10s to 12s 6d a week. I believe
this type could be erected for about £340,
of which the building owner gets reimbursed £100 from the Ministry and £40
from the local authority, so that, for an
outlay of about £200, he has a house
yielding him a gross rent of from £26
to £32 a year.
Doubtless a fine business
proposition for an enterprising building
owner!
Every house built with the aid of a
public subsidy should have at least 3
good-sized bedrooms. In the case of a
growing family this accommodation ought
to be one of the first considerations. The
third room of the 650 feet house is too
often a boxroom. The rents of these
houses are beyond the means of the lower
Housing Bill—
Second Reading
11 DECEMBER 1929
wage-earners. More so are the rents of
the earlier 950 square feet subsidy houses,
which were a kind of semi-detached villas
with 3 good-sized bedrooms and 30 per
cent. of which were, I am informed, sold
at prices from £750 to £900. The rent
for such a house erected by the Belfast
Corporation averaged 16s a week. Who
has benefited by the subsidies? Is it an
occupying workingman with a family?
Thousands of workingmen with families
cannot pay these rents even if they pinch
to do so by providing their children with
. only the barest physical necessaries.
As against your 14,667 subsidy houses
one English city-Birmingham, with a
population about three-fourths that of
Northern Ireland-had built a year ago
23,000 post-war houses and had more than
23,000 applications for houses still on
its list. Every family-even the poorest
of the poor-should have a dwelling in
which the children can be reared in full
health. The slums will never be cleared
until houses are built to let at 7s a week
including rates. This necessitates
reasonable subsidy for each house. It is
impossible to build such houses by private
enterprise to let at economic rents.
a
The slum tenant pays 4s to 8s a week
rent. This fact must never be lost sight
of. He cannot pay more. The big
majority of the families in the older
houses outside the slum areas are paying
less than 10s a week rent. The new
standard minimum house is being let at
an average rent of 10s to 12s 6d. Thus
the rent at which a new standard house is
let is higher than the rent which the great
bulk of the working classes living in prewar houses outside the slums pay to-day.
They in their turn cannot pay more.
A working man earning 60s a week or
less with three or more children cannot
face the rent of the standard minimum
house, irrespective of the expenses of
travelling to and from his work. He
will live on where he is till he finds a
better house at a rent which he can pay.
The present subsidies meet partially the
demand for houses from 10s to 15s a
week, but not at all the demand for
houses at from 7s to 10s a week. The
slum tenants have not left the slums.
There has been no "filtering up" from the
slum houses to better houses. It must be
remembered that there is much profiteering in the rents of older houses. When

there is a change of tenancy in a house
over £8 valuation the house loses the protection of the Rent Restriction Acts, and
the landlord increases the rent to an
extent that often puts the house out of the
reach of the poorer classes. Then there
exists the old tenement house with a congeries of sub-tenancies where the conditions
worse than in the slums.
Senator Dorman and I recently visited a
house in a leading thoroughfare in Belfast
where a family occupying a front room on
the ground floor paid a rent of 9s a week,
and another family occupying a back room
on the first floor paid a rent of 7s a week,
and there were other one-room tenancies
in the house.
are even
Some say slum houses should be reconditioned. At best it would only be a
temporary expedient. In Belfast great
numbers of back-to-back houses are on the
rims of the slums. If the public authorities had power to purchase these compulsorily or by agreement, and make one
habitable house out of two of them and let
the combined dwelling at a rent less than
that of a minimum subsidy house, it might
also give partial temporary relief.
The Government has never shouldered
its due responsibility in respect of housing.
Something thorough is wanted. It ought
to be in sackcloth for its neglect of the
poorest class of tenant. The State must
come to his help. He cannot help himself. The State subsidises elementary
education, secondary education, and university education. The school career of
each child from 5 to 14 years of age costs
at least £50. The Provided school is
built and maintained out of the rates and
taxes. If the child of the poor man
manages to reach a university-a thing
sometimes known-his training there is
subsidised by the State. The State subsidises health insurance, unemployment
insurance, old age pensions.
subsidise the home-dwelling of the poorest
Why not
citizen where necessary for the health and
life of himself and his family?
If a tenant of this class should move
into a standard subsidy house he would
often have to pay rates, amounting to 2s
or 3s a week, either directly or indirectly
in his rent. Why should the principle of
derating not be applied universally in
such a case to lighten his burden? Why
not give him a full abatement of rating?
A simple amendment to the Derating Act
of 1929 should make this obligatory on
local authorities. A growing body of
Housing Bill—
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Campbell]
opinion holds that "A Children's Rent
Allowance" is in principle the best and
cheapest and even perhaps the only way
of getting the large families out of the
poorest houses. This could be done by
giving a rebate of rent on account of each
child of school-going age up to a certain
number. If the rebate were 1s a week on
account of each child, a family with 3
children paying 7s a week for a slum
house could afford a subsidy house at a
rent of 10s a week. Those without
families who can afford to pay the
standard rent should continue to pay it.
Until this housing difficulty is grappled
with courage and breadth of view the
children of the poor have no chance in the
struggle of life. The luckiest of them are
those who die young. Full mental and
physical development is beyond their hope.
and vision. House the children of the poor,
take them out of the slums and hovels. Give
them real homes. They and theirs will
respond to the new environment.
If you
do you will confer as great a boon as is
is in your power upon the
classes
poorer
of Northern Ireland and add beyond
measure to the strength and health and
happiness of the community.
Mr. LESLIE: I rise to congratulate
the Government on the production of this
Bill, not so much for the moment on
account of what the Bill contains as on
account of the indication which it gives
that the Government intend to pursue
still further that beneficent policy of improving the housing of the working
classes which they have followed for
several years past and with, in my judgment, very conspicuous success. I should
like to congratulate the Government on
the fact that their future proposals appear
to be based on the ancient and timehonoured practice-honoured, perhaps,
rather more in the past than it is in the
present, and more than I am afraid some
members of the community desire it to be
honoured in the future, and that is the
practice of cutting one's coat according
to one's cloth. We have not got ten
millions in the Northern Ireland
chequer. We have perhaps to some
extent a slum problem in Belfast. They
have that in all big cities.
ex-
The problem was alluded to by the hon.
and learned Senator who has just sat down
(Mr. Campbell). Approximately the
population of Birmingham is one million,
and approximately the population of the
whole of Northern Ireland is one and a
quarter millions. Birmingham therefore
has four-fifths of our population, which is
I think
a somewhat large proportion.
the hon. and learned Senator made himself responsible for the statement that the
Government has assisted in the building
of 14,600 houses during the ten years 1919
to 1929. If those figures are correct I do
not think they are figures of which we
have any cause to be ashamed, for what
is the population of Belfast? It is well.
under half a million-between 400,000
and 500,000, and I think that probably
those 14,000 odd houses can relate only
to Belfast, and not to the urban areas,
and that they leave out the question of
council houses or labourers' cottages built
in the country districts. I am not sure
at the moment whether that is so, but I
rather think from recollection of figures
given in another place that that must be
So.
I
I propose to support the Bill in its
entirety, and particularly do I support
the suggestion that the size of these new
houses should be limited to 700 feet.
know that demands have been made for a
still larger house, but on the principal
which I have alluded to I do not think
that we could possibly commit ourselves
to paying subsidies in respect of larger
houses until we have satisfied more fully
the demand for houses of the smaller
size of from 650 feet to 700 feet. It
seems to me to be certain, that if there is
a difficulty, as has been pointed out,
amongst working men in Belfast paying
the rent of a 650 feet or 700 feet house
there will be still greater difficulty in paying the rent of a larger house, because
a house larger than 700 feet will cost more
to build.
The proposal to take further powers to
deal with the methods of obtaining the
subsidy in cases where it should not be
paid has my entire support, because to
my mind those are frauds on the central
fund, that is, the amount of money available for the purpose of building houses
under the subsidy. Speaking generally,
I should be disposed to congratulate the
Government on the method they have
taken of protecting themselves. They
seem to have added the action to personam the action to rem. They have taken
power to have a charge against the house
itself in the event of any subsidy having
been improperly obtained. There is one
point in that connection that I will mention in case it is not clear. I am proposing to put something on the Paper about
Housing Bill-
Second Reading
11 DECEMBER 1929
it at a later stage, but I rather expect
there will be an explanation before that.
The passage I refer to is on the second
page, towards the bottom, where it says
that in the event of what I may call the
improper enlargement of a house a sum
equal to the amount paid in building
grant may be recovered by the Ministry
from the person to whom the grant is
paid. That is perfectly in accordance.
with the principle to which I have alluded,
but it goes on to say, "in the case of his
death, from his personal representative."
I do not quite follow that. I imagine
the intention can only be to pursue the
deceased man's executors in respect of a
debt that arose during the man's lifetime, that is, in respect of the unlawful
enlargement of a house for which the man
was responsible before he died.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: That is the
intention.
Mr. LESLIE: I am very much inclined
to doubt whether it has not gone a little
further than that, whether it has not so
left it that if a cause of action arose at
any time within three years after the
man's death you can still pursue his estate.
I am certain that cannot be the intention,
because if it happens that the builder is
honest and I believe it does occur
occasionally-his affairs could not be
settled for three years.
That is a matter
that should be looked into between now
and the Committee stage, and for that
purpose I will put something on the Paper.
Passing from that temporary digression,
I continue to support further the method
of extending the operation of the subsidy.
by preventing the funds available being
depleted improperly, and I find reason for
that support in the deliberate exclusion
of the seaside villa and also the exclusion
of flats. If the subsidy is taken for either
of those purposes it is in the nature of a
fraud on the common fund. I support,
too, the proposals under, I think, Clause
7, which will allow officers of the various
authorities to take advantage of the subsidy facilities. That is all that I have
to say on the Bill except perhaps that I
might be allowed to express the hope that
the printers will some day or other make
up their minds as to whether "local
authority" an expression that was before us a few days ago-should be spelt
with or without capital letters. It is not
a point of importance but it is a matter in
which one would like to see consistency.
Mr. MCALLISTER: I am
sure the
House has heard with great anxiety and
regret the picture drawn by the hon. and
learned Member (Mr. Campbell) of the
conditions of the working classes in Belfast. To me it is appalling to think that
2,700 families are living in one-room
houses. I do not intend to follow the hon
and learned Member in dealing with the
conditions in Belfast because I am sure
that those of us who live in urban and
rural areas do not want to be too closely
mixed up in the housing question in
Belfast after what we have seen in recent
years.
I would suggest that this Bill could be
greatly improved by extending the 700
substantially, and I think it could be done
without any alarming amount being
added to the cost of the house. After all,
in a 650 foot house we have to provide
doors, windows, fireplaces, plumbing
work, and so on, and the cost of such
work on a 650 feet house would be almost
as much as on an 800 feet house. I think it
is highly desirable that the latter type of
house should be provided for the
ordinary working man. There is another
type of citizen in a higher social scale who
is pursuing other avocations, and he
would naturally want to take advantage
of
this housing scheme and would
naturally want to house his family in a
better and larger house than one of 650
or 700 feet. I think that that sort of
enterprising ambitious citizen, who is in a
class above the ordinary working man,
ought not to be denied the benefits of this
housing scheme.
He
One remark made by the noble Leader
of the House I would like to clarify.
said the subsidy was to remain a charge
upon the house for three years. I hope he
is wrong in his interpretation of the Bill,
because that seems to me to be a rather
dangerous thing which might operate
against an innocent man. If the noble
Lord is right in his statement an innocent person who bought a house that had
been altered during the three years
might be called upon to refund the subsidy. I confess I have not read the Bill
as closely as I would have liked, but my
own impression was that it was only a
debt against the person who actually
built the house. If that is so no exception could be taken to it.
Housing Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
Mr. BARCLAY: I have great pleasure
in supporting the Second Reading of this
Bill. I might with Senator Leslie congratulate the Government on adding this
extra 50 feet. I think I was the only
person twelve months ago who advocated
fifty feet or more. I am very pleased to
see in the Bill that the Government in
their wisdom have added that 50 feet in
this Bill. It will make up the great deficiency which existed in the 650 feet
house. It will give more living room and
more sleeping room.
But there is one thing I cannot understand, and that is the price of houses in
the City of Belfast as compared with the
rural districts. Terrace houses built in
the City of Belfast cost anything up to
£370 or £375, and in the country or rural
districts, such as Greenisland, a terrace
house of 650 feet can be built and sold at
£170. Now, I know a builder of these
houses who has built fifteen of them and
sold them at £170. I was in conversation
with him a short time ago, and he tells
me he had profit out of the £170 for
the 650 feet houses. I asked him, "Had
you the subsidy over and above that?"
He said, "Yes." Therefore, this man is
able to build terrace houses in Greenisland
of 650 feet at £170 and sell them at that
price and have a profit, and I cannot
understand why they charge so much in
the City of Belfast. This man is building at the present time kitchen houses for
the poorer working classes. He is building 33 of them and he informs me that if
he could sell these houses he could sell
them at between £70 and £100, or he
could let them at 5s a week and have a
profit with the subsidy. Therefore,
there must be an enormous profit going
into the pockets of the builders in Belfast
for the subsidy kitchen house and parlour
house.
There is another man, Mr. Greer, who
is building semi-detached villas in Greenisland. He is building them of red brick
front, bay windows, and so on, and he informs me that he will be able to sell these
houses at anything from £250 to £300.
He expects to be able to sell these houses
at £270 or £275. Now, for the same
class of house in Belfast they are asking
£450 and the subsidy on top of that. I
cannot understand where the difference
comes in, because builders in Belfast
and builders in Greenisland have got
to go to the same merchants for
all
the building material. And I
might say bricks come into Greenisland
by rail and cost more. They have to go
up to the City of Belfast to the timber
merchant and the slate merchant and the
tile merchant and so on, and they have
to pay the same price as builders in Belfast have to pay. I would like to know
how it comes there is such an enormous
difference in price. Therefore, I have
naturally come to the conclusion that it
might have been wisdom on the part of
the Government if they had put a limit
on the price of the houses instead of on
the size of the house. If a limit had been
put upon the price of the house I expect
the working-class people would have got
a cheaper house. I have pleasure in
supporting the Second Reading of this
Bill, because I believe that in twelve
months more there will not be so much
overcrowding as there is at the present
time.
Mr. DORMAN: The hon. and learned
Senator in front of me has drawn a very
sorrowful picture of the housing of the
working classes in Belfast, and I can
truthfully confirm the picture the hon.
and learned Senator has drawn to hon.
Members of this House. I believe the
greatest problem confronting the Government to-day is the problem of the housing
of the working classes, and I believe that
that problem will never be satisfactorily
solved along the lines which the Government has launched out upon with a view
to finding a solution of the problem. The
present system of granting subsidies both
by the Government and by the Corporation may in a few years have erected a
sufficient number of houses to satisfy the
demands of the people, but it will never
in my estimation have those houses
erected at a price which will be within
the reasonable means of the working
classes of Belfast and the whole area of
Northern Ireland.
In my opinion this problem is great
enough for the Government to deal with
it from a national point of view and to
launch upon a Government system of
supplying houses. I believe that had the
Corporation continued on their original
idea through their Housing Department
erecting the houses required for the
housing of the working classes by direct
labour they would have come to a satisfactory conclusion sooner than by any
other method, especially the method the
Government is at present pursuing.
Housing Bill--
Second Reading
11 DECEMBER 1929
I sat in the gallery of the Belfast Corporation at the monthly meeting when
the old chairman of the housing department was permitted by the Lord Mayor
to deliver a valedictory address after he
had resigned membership of the Corporation and the chairmanship of the Housing
Committee, and he exhorted the Council
not to scrap its Housing Department, but
to continue to build houses by direct
labour. It is probable that the houses
the Belfast Corporation put up by direct
labour for the workers of Belfast have
been put up at from £50 to £100 cheaper
per house than those erected under the
subsidy by private enterprise. Now, if
the
Corporation and the Government
started out to build direct themselves or
even if they built by contract and allowed
a subsidy for each house they would be
enabled to build houses so as to bring
them within reach of the unskilled
workers, who are the persons most severely
hit by the shortage of houses in Belfast
to-day.

and five of a family paying ten or twelve

has only about 4s 9d per head of his population to feed and clothe, educate, and

it would be possible even inside a year,

at present put on the acre, even with the

Because, after all, before the War broke

houses before one brick was paid for for

after case, scores
I know of case
or hundreds of cases- -probably about
200 cases in Belfast of families

inside that particular room-that is what

dealt with along the lines the Government

Mr. DICKSON: I rise to support the
Bill, but my chief object in doing so is in
view of the remarks of the hon. and
learned Senator who spoke in the first instance as to the rents that working men
are being charged for these subsidy houses.
Take the local Rural District Council, of
which I happen to be chairman. We have
been giving £40 subsidy, and since March
last we have given £42,000 in that one
rural area in addition to the Government's £100. Now, what I have to bring
before the House is that it is not working
men who are getting the benefit of that
subsidy. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear).
Ninety per cent. of that £140,000 has
gone to speculative builders. They, as
the hon. and learned Senator mentioned,
have only to invest about £200 on top
of the subsidy to build these houses. They
let them at 10s per week, which means
they are getting about 12 per cent. for
their money.
Now, I hold that the Government ought
to insert in this Bill a Clause that a man
has no right to get more than 7 per cent.
for house property. That would reduce
the rent of the houses from 10s a week to
5s, which is as much as the working man
can afford to pay. But for the Government to present us with a Bill of this
Housing Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Dickson]
kind to subsidise people that have no need
of the money and give them an investment
that brings them 12 per cent, or 15 per
cent. I think is absurd. I have no doubt
that the reply of the Government will be
that when there is a sufficient number of
houses erected the rents will fall in due
course. It seems to me it will be a long
time, with the number of people crying
out for houses, before we can arrive at a
solution of that kind. I still hold that
there should be some restriction on the
rent charged. Otherwise I will support
this Bill.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I
would just like to say a word. I feel
inclined to offer the heartiest congratulations to the Government for persevering
with this Measure and introducing a Bill
to continue the subsidy and more
especially for the increase in the floor
space of the house. We have heard much
here to-day about the conditions of the
people in Belfast. But really I think it
is exaggerated. I believe I am living in
one of the oldest districts in Belfast, and
I am not acquainted with conditions such
as have been described here to-day. I know
housing accommodation is certainly not
as satisfactory as I would like it to be.
I do not suppose it will be for a considerable number of years, because there is a
great deal of old property that could not
possibly be destroyed yet until there is
other accommodation for the people at
present occupying them. And the people.
seem to be quite content, quite comfortable, and quite healthy. It is remarkable how those people living even in
the smaller streets and small houses are
a very healthy class of people. It is a
fact.
After all when you look at this matter
we have got to recognise that the Government are doing the best they can to produce the class of house suitable for the
people. The Government cannot build
houses themselves. They have to depend in
the first instance upon the local authorities.
The local authorities in the next place
have to depend upon the contractors,
the contractors have to depend upon the
workmen, and they are not all so very
patriotic and so very loyal to their own
class as to do a good honest day's work and
to make the houses as cheap as they ought
to be neither the contractors, nor the
workmen, nor probably anyone. Everyone, as far as I can see, is looking for a
good deal of profit. I have not yet come
across anyone who cares to do owt for
nowt, as the Yorkshireman said.
In this case the Government has offered
a subsidy of £100 and the local authority
is paying £40. It has certainly been an
inducement to builders to build the class
of house which is required for the people,
that is. the smaller class of house. Had
it not been for that subsidy I am perfectly
sure the builders would have been engaged
on the better class of house. Before the
subsidy was given one could see, in the
suburbs they were erecting the better
class of house and selling them. The result of the subsidy is that the contractor
is giving attention more to the smaller
class of house. We have heard the present subsidies by the Government condemned. I want to say this that there
are hundreds of these smaller houses
which have been built in Belfast on what
is known as the Seaview site. It is really
a very healthy place and a beautiful place,
and while the houses are small they are
very comfortable and very neat, and the
workers seem to be content, because it is
about the smallest rent yet. I think the
people are paying 8s per week which is
the best produced by the Corporation. I
know this much that the Corporation cannot build sufficient for the demand made
on them.
Surely that is sufficient proof that it is
a genuine good bargain for the people
that want them. It is impossible to build
sufficient, for the number of names the
Corporation have enrolled for these houses.
is enormous. I was speaking to a
councillor a short time ago in favour of a
man who wanted one of these houses, and
he said it was impossible to promise one,
the list was so long that had to be satisfied
yet. Surely the Government has done the
best they can, and the Corporation are
As to
evidently doing the best they can.
saying they are dearer than they are in

the country districts, I think
