One has to remember that at the present time the people most in need of houses
Housing Bill-
Second Reading
11 DECEMBER 1929
are the younger people-the young man
who is married and unable to obtain a

were the people who had to be accommodated. It was not the case of a man
with a wife and a big family. It was a

Mr. CAMPBELL: I think our Friend
is at sea just now.
Mr.
JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I
was born in Belfast.
Mr. CAMPBELL: So was I.

them extraordinarily cheap when told the

are putting up the right class of house and

think we have all benefited by hearing

are
Mr. DORMAN: The Belfast Corporation set up a Committee before the war
broke out of representatives of the house

to inquire into the cost of land inside the

Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: I

Viscount CHARLEMONT: Whatever
may be said about the housing conditions
in Belfast there is no doubt whatever
there is a very grave shortage of houses
throughout Northern Ireland, and in the
big cities more especially. And these
Bills are not designed to cure the problem.
They cannot do it, but they can at least
mitigate it very considerably. I think I
really can claim for the Government that
they have done so, since these Bills were
first introduced. It is a fact that we have
to pay in accordance with what we have
got in our pockets, and the Government
pocket is not as unlimited or nearly so
unlimited as any of us would like it to
be.
The sum paid by the Government for
housing last year was in or about
£385,000. This year it is anticipated it
will come to about £450,000. That is
a very considerable sum for the Northern
Government to pay. No doubt it is wrong
of the speculative builder, as Senator Hill
Dickson has mentioned, to expect so large
a return on his money. At the same time
if the subsidy or anything induced them

to build more and more houses the problem will become lessened as it goes on.
That is the real object of the subsidyto enable as many houses to be built as

There is the question Senator McAllister asked about a certain clause. I am

Poor Prisoners Defence Bill. [S.]
Order for Second Reading read.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to move that
this Bill be now read a Second time. This
is a non-party Measure and it is put forward in a non-party spirit. It is a Bill
to make better provision for the defence
of poor persons in criminal cases. In
Northern Ireland the only help a poor
prisoner gets from the public funds for
his defence is when he stands in the dock
indicted for wilful murder, on which
occasion he is assigned counsel and
solicitor at a modest fee each, for the first
time since his arrest. In England the
procedure is fairer to the accused persons
who are without means. There for years
past the prisoner, whose alleged crime has
been thought fit for trial by indictment.
in the superior court, can give a glance.
round the court and bestow what is called
a dock brief valued at one guinea on any
barrister in the court whom he cares to
select.
I have heard that a similar practice prevails in America. I have heard of a case
of a prisoner indicted and tried for theft
in the States who was too poor to pay
for
a lawyer, and the judge said to him:
"Well, Sam, I will provide you with
counsel. There is Mr. Smith you see, and
there is Mr. Jones you see! There is Mr.
Johnston downstairs whom you do not
see." And the prisoner, it seems, on
looking round the court replied to the
judge: "Well, judge, I will take Mr.
Johnston." Now, that happened in
America, and it could not possibly happen in England or in our courts.
England the right to a dock brief is in
every day use.
In
In the year 1903 power was given to
the magistrate to order payment of the
cost of defence in criminal cases if the
magistrates thought it desirable having
regard to the nature of the defence set up
before them. Often, however, in our
courts and in the courts in England no
defence is set up in petty sessions. The
prisoner, according to our law, is entitled
to reserve his defence until he is put on
his trial before the higher court, so that
in many cases the Poor Prisoners Defence
Act, of 1903, was of no value. In the
present session of the British Parliament
a Bill has been introduced removing this
defect. It is backed by Labour, Conservative, and Liberal Members. It has got
a Second Reading in the House of Commons unanimously, and it has received
the Home Secretary's assurance of the
approval of the Government.
The Bill which I have the honour to
English
present to the House is the
Bill with the necessary adaptations.
It entitles the poor prisoner committed for trial to free legal aid in
the preparation and conduct of his defence, and to have solicitor and counsel
assigned to him for that purpose.
That
right it gives unconditionally in the case
of any person committed for trial charged
with murder. In any other case it is conditional on the magistrate or judge being
satisfied that it is desirable in the interests
of justice that the accused should have
legal aid. The Bill also provides for legal
aid before the court of summary jurisdiction for the poor prisoner charged where
it appears to the court by reason of the
gravity of the charge or exceptional circumstances that it is desirable in the
interests of justice he should have legal
aid in the preparation and conduct of his
defence. The costs in the case of an
offence committed in any particular county
or county borough are to be payable out
of the funds of that county or county
borough.
357 Poor Prisoners Defence Bill-- 11 DECEMBER 1929

Second Reading
I submit that it is in the interests of
justice that the heavy handicap-for such
it is the heavy handicap of poverty of an
a presumably innocent man should be
removed.
The world is not his
friend nor the world's law." All accused
persons ought to be equal before the law.
That is our theory. In practice the
reality is that the rich prisoner has the
benefit of skilled legal help from the
earliest moment he is in peril. The poor
prisoner is destitute of such help in every
stage of the case. We have no court of
criminal appeal in Northern Ireland. I
believe there soon will be one. This Bill
thus contains no provision for the costs of
appeal by a poor prisoner after conviction.
I will briefly go over the Clauses.
Clause 1 entitles a prisoner committed for
trial on an indictable offence to free legal
aid in the preparation and conduct of his
defence and to have solicitor and counsel
assigned to him for the purpose if a certificate is granted in accordance with the
provisions of the Act. It also provides
that a certificate may be granted by the
committing justice on his being committed
for trial or by the judge of the court before which he is to be tried at any time
after the reading of the depositions. It
provides that a defence certificate shall
not be granted in respect of any person
unless it
appears to the certifying
authority, that is, the magistrate or
judge, that his means are insufficient to
enable him to obtain such aid.
But where it so appears the certifying
authority shall grant a defence certificate
in respect of any person committed for
trial upon a charge of murder; and may
grant a defence certificate in respect of
any person committed for trial upon any
other charge if it appears to the certifying
authority having regard to all the circumstances of the case (including the
nature of such defence, if any, as may
have been set up), that it is desirable in
the interests of justice that he should have
legal aid in the preparation and conduct
of his defence at the trial.
Clause 2 gives similar power to grant
legal aid before courts of summary jurisdiction to a poor prisoner and to have a
solicitor assigned to him. Clause 3 provides for the payment of the costs of the
defence out of local funds by direction of
the court. It also provides for the payment of expenses of witnesses and provides, what I think is reasonable, that no
allowance is to be made for witnesses as
to character, but only for those who testify
anything with regard to the facts of the
transaction concerned. The same Clause
provides for rules and regulations being
made by the Attorney-General of Northern Ireland with the approval of the
Lord Chief Justice and the Minister of
Home Affairs.
Clause 4 provides for the payment of
costs directed by the court and the manner
in which these costs shall be paid, and
provides for the reimbursement to the
clerk of the court by the town clerk of a
county borough or the secretary of a
county council. There
are supplementary provisions. Clause 5 establishes
the principle that costs in the case of
offences committed or supposed to have
been
committed in a county borough
shall be payable out of the fund or rate
prescribed by the Minister of Home
Affairs, and in the case of offences committed or supposed to have been committed
in a county, costs are payable out of the
fund of the county which shall be prescribed by the Minister of Home Affairs. .
Clause 6 provides for rules and regulations for the carrying of the Act into
effect to be made by the Attorney-General
with the approval of the Lord Chief
Justice and the Minister of Home Affairs.
Clause 7, which is the interpretation
Clause, includes in the expression "judge'
recorder or deputy recorder, and a county
court judge, and a chairman of quarter
sessions or his deputy. Very often cases of
a criminal nature are tried at quarter
sessions and in recorders' courts in Northern Ireland. I beg to move.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The prime
consideration of the Government in dealing with a measure of this character must
necessarily be the extent of the need or
demand for the principles embodied in
the measure. In arriving at a decision
in this respect one must have regard to
the statutory principles which already
apply in Northern Ireland, and to the
extent to which those principles and provisions have met, and still meet, the
public convenience.
In this connection it may be pointed
out that provision already exists in Northern Ireland for affording legal assistance
to poor persons who are charged with
murder. Further, certain provision is
made under the rule of the supreme court
under the Judicature Acts enabling poor
persons to sue before the courts in forma
359 Poor Prisoners Defence Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Viscount Charlemont]
pauperis and to have assigned to them a
counsel or solicitor, or both, to assist
them; while, finally, in summary jurisdiction cases, provision is also made under
the Petty Sessions Clerks Act, 1858,
Section 19, under which the courts may
remit the fees payable in respect of the
stamped form used in summary proceedings if the adjudicating justices are
satisfied of the inability of the party
liable to pay such fees.
It is a fact that the provision made in
this respect in England is somewhat more
extensive, as under an Act passed in 1903
by the Imperial Parliament entitled "The
Poor Prisoners Defence Act, 1903," provision is made enabling the committing
justices, or the judge of a court of assize,
or a chairman of a court of quarter
sessions, to certify that a poor prisoner
shall be entitled to have a solicitor and
counsel assigned to him in connection with
his trial.
The object of the Bill which has now
been introduced into this House is to
apply generally the provisions of that Act.
to Northern Ireland, and, further, to extend it with the object of providing for
legal aid before courts of summary jurisdiction in cases where it is considered desirable that such free legal aid should be
afforded.
As already stated, the Bill must be subjected to the test already referred.
above, namely, the necessity for such a
provision, and in this connection it is well
to emphasise that when the Act of 1903
came before the Imperial Parliament Ireland was deliberately excluded from its
application. It may reasonably be assumed that the Imperial Parliament at
that time considered that whatever
necessity there was for such provision in
Great Britain, there was no necessity for
it in regard to Ireland; and I submit on
behalf of the Government that the same
principles apply to-day, and that there is
really no necessity for this Bill at the
moment.
This subject has engaged the attention
of the Government of Northern Ireland
for some time past, and the opinion of the
Government expressed by me is not by any
means a new or hastily considered one.
Over four years ago the Government took
steps to consult the Incorporated Law
Society of Northern Ireland generally on
the question, and with particular reference to the advisability of establishing
}
in Northern Ireland a society for the purpose of advising the poor or assisting them.
in civil litigation. The Council of the
Society referred the matter to a special
committee for investigation, and on the
basis of the views of that committee, unanimously arrived at the opinion that
such a Society is neither necessary nor
desirable in Northern Ireland, as the
members of the profession in Northern
Ireland have at all times in the past
shown themselves, and will continue to
show themselves, ready and willing to
assist the deserving poor in obtaining
justice, while moreover, the formation of
such a society might lead to a volume of
vicious and unnecessary litigation.
The unanimous opinion of such a responsible body is not one that can be
easily brushed aside, and it is borne out
to a very considerable extent by the fact
that so far as the Government can ascertain there is no reason to suppose that
there has been in the past any cases of
a miscarriage of justice resulting from the
fact that the prisoners were not defended
by counsel or solicitor, and no case has
come under the notice of the Government
where an innocent person has been convicted owing to his inability to provide
himself with legal assistance.
The real difference between Northern
Ireland and England is, I submit, a fairly
obvious one. In the larger courts in
Great Britain it is a common practice to
find hundreds of persons coming before
the Court about whom little is known,
either to the police or to the legal profession, and in these cases there may be
a justification for the provision of legal
aid. On the other hand, in Northern
Ireland it would be a rarity to find any
person coming before any of the Courts
who is not well known in the locality, and
for whom legal aid is not immediately
forthcoming, unless, of course, in the case
of well-known criminals.
On the whole, therefore, it does not
appear to the Government that there is
any real urgency in connection with this
matter, but I might point out that the
Government have under consideration at
the moment the question of the revision
of the law in relation to summary jurisdiction, and if during the consideration
of those proposals it appears that there is
a need for legislation of this character,
the Government will be prepared to consider it after consulting the various interests affected. In the latter connection,
I should point out that the Bill, if ac-
361 Poor Prisoners Defence Bill- 11 DECEMBER 1929

Second Reading
cepted, would impose, un ascertainable
but undoubtedly substantial burdens on
the local authorities, and the Government,
therefore, could not in any event accept
this Bill as a private Member's Bill,
seeing that it involves the principle of imposing charges on public funds.
In conclusion, I think it well to state
that the Bill which is now before us
appears to be modelled on a private Member's Bill which was introduced into the
Imperial Parliament, but in respect of
which, I observe, further progress has
been postponed until 2nd May next.
From this it will be apparent that even
across the water the matter is not regarded as one of any immediate urgency,
and, accordingly, it but confirms the
view that the Government has taken in
connection with this Bill-that it would
be inadvisable to proceed further with it
at the moment.
Mr. MCALLISTER: I must join issue
with the noble Leader of the House in his
defence of the attitude of the Government
on this Bill. He is driven to argue that
the Bill is less necessary in Ireland than
it is in England. It is a notorious fact that
poverty is worse here than it is in England.
By any test that can be put that is undoubtedly so. Unemployment here is
very much higher than in England and
that
goes to show that there are, comparatively speaking, more poor people
charged with offences and unable to
obtain legal aid in Northern Ireland than
in England. With regard to the question.
stated to have been referred to the Incorporated Law Society, this is the first time
I personally have heard of it. In any
case, asking the Incorporated Law Society
its opinion about the setting up of a
certain Committee is entirely different to
inviting its opinion on this Bill. I would
now ask the noble Lord if he is prepared
to take and act upon the advice of the
Incorporated Law Society in regard to
this Bill.
One thought occurred to me when the
noble Lord indicated that the Government intend to deal with the whole question of summery jurisdiction. I assume
it is the intention of the Government to
take away from the ordinary magistrates
in Northern Ireland the jurisdiction
which they now exercise. If that is so I
think this Bill is more necessary than
eyer. If a poor person appears before a
local court of summary jurisdiction as at
present constituted, there is no doubt the
local magistrates can be of assistance to
him while honestly discharging their duty.
They know his character and his reputation and the whole circumstances of his
life. But if the jurisdiction is to be taken
from local magistrates and vested in a
Resident Magistrate, who is an entire
stranger to the district, he will know
nothing at all about a poor person who is
charged before him. I say it is essential,
if the interests of justice are to be served,
that that poor person should have legal
assistance in order that he may be in a
position to vindicate himself. This is a
democratic Measure. Why should a person who is able to afford legal assistance
to defend himself be in a better position
in that respect than a poor man who
through no fault of his own is unable to
secure legal assistance.
With regard to the remarks of the
Noble Lord that this Bill would cast a
burden on local authorities that is no
answer to the arguments in favour of the
Bill. If it is essential to secure the fair
and equitable administration of justice.
that a poor person should be provided
with legal aid, it should not be denied
on the ground that it would be a burden
on the local bodies or on the National
Exchequer.
Mr. LESLIE: If I understood him
correctly I think the Noble Viscount intended to indicate that the Government
was unable to offer further facilities for
the
of this Bill.
progress
I did not
understand that what he said was an invitation that the Bill should be summarily rejected. I think myself that the
Bill ought to be allowed to have its
Second reading.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Certainly.
Mr. LESLIE: The Bill will. I presume, be read a Second time by the House
and then it will go on the Paper, but I
do not think that time here, at all events,
will allow it to go any further, even if
there were no objection. I think it would
be better if the Bill were kept alive for a
short time in order that its details might
be considered, and in order that some of
us might refresh our memories as to the
Act of 1903, and endeavour to obtain a
copy of the Bill now in the Imperial Parliament, and also consult our friends in
the country districts as to whether they
think anything is required. I certainly
myself knew nothing of the consultation
63 Poor Prisoners Defence Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Mr. Leslie]
between the Government and the Incorporated Law Society-and there was no
reason why I should-but I am sure
people will be interested, and it may very
well be that the reading of this Measure
a Second time, even if it goes no further,
and it certainly would not go any further
until it had been perhaps rather severely
scrutinised, will awaken a certain amount
of public interest in the question, and
that, I think, will be all to the good.
In
Mr. DORMAN: I would ask the
Government seriously to consider the
advisability of accepting the principle of
the second Clause of this Bill. As
British subjects we are supposed to have
the rights to preserve our character.
many cases a person appears before a
court of summary jurisdiction and his
accusers are fortified with a legally trained
mind in order to bring about the conviction of that particular individual. It
is a basic principle of our law that every
man is innocent until he is proved to be
guilty. It would be far better if a
hundred guilty men should get off than
that one innocent
man should be convicted. I can picture a poor innocent
man brought before a court of summary
jurisdiction and being returned for trial,
whereas if he had had the advantage of
a legally trained mind to counteract the
effect of the legally trained mind against
him in the person of a lawyer or of a
District-Inspector of the R.U.C. the
decision might have been different. Thus
the poor man without legal aid appears to
be at a considerable disadvantage. If he
is returned for trial and is unable to procure bail one can imagine the intense
mental suffering of an innocent man who
is incarcerated in prison while his wife
and family are deprived of his income. In
many cases, if such a man had not been
deprived of legal aid, he would not be
kept in prison.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I hope the Government will take a more sympathetic
view of this Bill, having regard to the
fact that during the past few weeks it got
an unanimous passage through the
British House of Commons and received
the approval of all parties, including that
of the Home Secretary. It is a poor
man's Bill, and I am afraid the reply of
the noble Lord, the Leader of the House,
will only succeed in convincing many
people that our Government is not a poor
man's Government. I have heard no
convincing argument against the basic
principle of this Bill. The noble Lord
spoke last week about trusting the courts
in the matter of making orders under
the Adoption of Children Bill. Now this
Bill gives complete control to the court,
and it is only in cases where the court
thinks it is in the interests of justice that
a poor man standing undefended in the
dock should have legal aid that it will be
granted. Reference was made to consultations with the Incorporated Law
Society, but I understand from the noble
Lord's speech that this particular question
was never before the Society at all.
It has also been said that it would place
a burden upon the ratepayers, and it was
hinted that it might be a serious burden.
I cannot think that it will. I should
imagine that cases under the Bill will
rarely arise and the burden will be very
slight. Even if it were a little heavier
I do not see why the interests of strict
economy should be allowed to interfere
with the impartial administration of
justice.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I think
the hon. and learned Member who introduced this Bill has mistaken what I said
about the burden that would be cast on
local authorities. It is the fact that it
is a private Member's Bill that imposes
a burden on the local authorities that
renders it impossible for the Government
to accept it. With reference to the remarks of Senator McAllister, I should
like to say that in connection with the
revision of the law of summary jurisdiction, there are no proposals involving the
removal of Magistrates. I think it would
be more advantageous if the provisions of
the Bill now before the House were taken
into consideration when the proposals regarding the law of summary jurisdiction
are made, as I promised they will be.
It was stated by one hon. Member that
poverty in Northern Ireland is more
widespread than it is in England, but, looking at the law cases in the papers, and
with a knowledge of one's own neighbourhood, I do not think that greater poverty
necessarily means a greater amount of
crime, even petty crime. Crime in Nor
thern Ireland is considerably less than it
is in various districts I have known in
England. The fact is that nearly every
man charged before a court of summary
jurisdiction is known quite well to the
magistrates who are able to form a very
good opinion of his character. I am per:
fectly agreeable to the Bill being read a
Drainage Bill-

that the
any further
Second time, but I regret
Government cannot offer
facilities for its passage.
On Question, Motion (That the Bill be
now read a Second time) agreed to.
Drainage Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The main
object of this Bill which I have now the
honour to present to the House is to enable the Government to find a solution for
the age-long problem of the drainage of
Lough Neagh and the River Bann. I
need not take up the time of hon. Members by entering into great detail in
regard to the damage which is done and
of the hardships which are caused year
The
after year by floods in this area.
hon. Members, and particularly those of
us who live in the vicinity, know something of this side of the problem, and it
has been amply evidenced during the past
few months. It is to be hoped that when
the measures which this Bill renders
possible have been put into operation we
may regard the constant flooding in this
region as a thing of the past—in fact, as
only of historical importance.
The Bill is divided into three parts.
The first and main part deals with the
principal problem, namely, the measures
which are contemplated to relieve flooding.
It is provided in Clause 1 that the Ministry of Finance shall be the Drainage
Authority for this purpose. As hon.
Members are aware, a Scheme has been
prepared by Major Shepherd, our Director
of Works, and is contained in his report
of 1926. This report was submitted to
the Drainage Advisory Committee which,
in turn, issued two reports-an Interim
Report of 1927 and a final report last
year. As it happens, Major Shepherd's
Scheme corresponds very closely to that
prepared by Mr. Manning of the Imperial
Office of Works nearly 50 years ago, and
it is somewhat melancholy to think that
if this scheme had been then put in
operation the country might perhaps have
been spared the damage and loss which
has been caused since. I would emphasize
however, that Major Shepherd's scheme.
was prepared on entirely independent
lines, and has been approved by a noted.
English expert, so that I think the House
may rest assured that it represents the
result of the highest modern engineering
opinion and advice that it has been
possible for the Government to procure.
The scheme, in short, recommends that
two matters should be undertaken, firstly,
the improvement of the channel of the
Lower Bann so that it may be able to
carry off everything except the most exceptional floods; and, secondly, the insertion of sluices in the present fixed
weirs at the Cutts, Portna and Toome, so
that they may be capable of a greater discharge and also so that the level of Lough
Neagh can be lowered in anticipation of
heavy floods and that the lake thus
may
have a greater storage capacity.
scheme is, therefore, a comparatively
simple one.
The
I would now like to say a word in regard to some other matters which arise
in connection with the Bill. Firstly,
there was a proposal in the interim report
of the Drainage Advisory Committee that
the Government should connect up the
scheme with the privately promoted
hydro-electric scheme on the Lower Bann.
In the final Report the Advisory Committee dropped this suggestion as there
was some uncertainty whether the Hydro-
Electric Scheme would materialise. and
it was recommended that the Drainage
Scheme should be proceeded with independently. This independent scheme
will not, however, prejudice any electric
scheme which may eventually be found
possible.
The second matter is that concerning
fisheries. Clause 10 of the Bill gives
adequate safeguards for the protection of
fish, and in Clause 11 it is provided that
suitable compensation will be paid for any
depreciation which may result.
The third matter is the important
The cost of the
question of finance.
scheme, which is estimated at a total of
about £650,000, is being borne partly by
the Government and partly by the county
councils of the five counties which are
concerned. Clause 14 and the Third
Schedule deal with this, the payments of
the county councils being limited to onethird of the cost of the scheme, with a
maximum contribution of £200,000.
area which will be rated for the purpose
of this contribution will be in all cases
such part of the county, including urban
districts, as is comprised within the catchment area.
The
In Part II of the Bill opportunity has
been taken to Amend certain provisions
of the Drainage Act of 1925: the main
points are a simplification of the procedure in conection with county council
drainage schemes which cost less than
Drainage Bill—
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
£2,000, and some other minor matters
which are considered to make for improvement of that Act.
I believe that hon. Members will agree
that this considerable project which the
Government are proposing to embark on
has every prospect of success, and should
prove to be an inestimable boon to the
agriculturists, who have had such a hard
time in the past on account of the
periodic flooding of their land. The cost
is considerable, but the Government believe that it is well worth while. I beg
to move.
The Bill now
Colonel PAKENHAM:
before the House is very good proof of
the excellent result of Northern Ireland
having a Parliament of its own and a
Government responsible to it. (Hon.
Members: Hear, hear). Most of us I
think have read the remarkable speech
made by the Minister of Finance in introducing this Bill in another place, and
when we read of the abortive attempts
made by Governments in the past from
the year 1840 to the present day to settle
this very important matter-abortive,
because of the unwillingness of most
British Governments to let us spend our
own money on ourselves. (Hon. Members: Hear hear). We welcome the
change in all that, which is signified in
the moving of the Second reading of this
Bill.
on
We have now got a Government of
broader mental outlook
this very
Government
important question, and a
composed of men most of whom are
natives of the soil who know the difficulties with which we have to contend-a
Government in fact which said to itself:
"Here is a very big idea. We know it
will cost a lot of money, and we do not
quite believe we shall receive back in
pounds, shillings and pence the money we
shall spend on it. But we do believe that
by this scheme we shall alter for the
better the condition of a large number of
people living in the basin of Lough
Neagh, and we shall also improve the
land, and we shall have every chance
and possibility when we have desaturated
the country round that we shall change
the climate for the better."
very
Now these three things are
important, and I most heartily back the
Government up in their scheme to reduce the flooding in this great basin
which Nature SO verv inadequately
furnished with exits. If nature had
made two Banns or three Banns which
would let the waters of Lough Neagh
get out in their own time, but Nature
having only provided one, it is up to us
in this country to assist Nature in getting
rid of the surplus water, and this scheme
we are certain will do great things in
that regard. I should like to know-I
have not yet seen the draft scheme-but
one expression the Minister of Finance
speaking in another place used was "the
statutory level of Lough Neagh." I do
not quite know what is meant by that.
I presume it means by the statutory level
of Lough Neagh 50 feet above the level of
the sea.
Now the level of Lough Neagh
alters day by day. In the last fortnight
the level of Lough Neagh has risen by
about 23 feet. During the month of
August it rose very largely. I want to
know what level Lough Neagh is going to
be kept at when this scheme is complete.
I shall ask that at a later stage.
There is one other point, that is the
disappearance of two bodies who have
hitherto dealt with the drainage of Lough
Neagh-the Lough
Lough Neagh Drainage
Trustees and the Lower Bann Navigation
Trustees. We all welcome the disappearance and decease of these two bodies.
(Hon. Members: Hear, hear). They
were quite effete and a remnant of an old
system. To my own knowledge the
drainage of Lough Neagh has been most
inefficiently conducted for the past 40
years. I do welcome the Bill, and I congratulate the Government on the boldness
of their ideas, and the fact that they are
looking forward to the future, and all of
us lake dwellers will be delighted to see
the Bill in operation.
Mr. ARMSTRONG : As belonging to
a county which is perhaps most benefited
by this Measure-certainly pays more than
other counties-I should like to congratulate the Government also on the quick
passage it received in another place, and
which I think will be even more rapid
probably here. It is a great scheme. It
is a project for regulating the surface of
a lake which is the sixth largest in
Europe, and I look to the benefit which
is to be derived from the stoppage of the
flooding, or the diminishing, which is only
part of the scheme. I think the fact
that some 1,865 square miles are going to
be benefited by this is a most important
consideration.
In a county such as Armagh, for instance and it applies to other counties,
round the hills and the watercourses on
Drainage Bill-
Second Reading
11 DECEMBER 1929
each side of them the drainage has been
held up for years and years, and between
the hills there are swamps, which I hope
now, will be turned into the admirable
land which it really is if free from water.
There is no question that this is a Measure
which is being received by the public of
the different counties with great enthusiasm. But I think our gratitude is
specially due to the taxpayers, because the
taxpayers are shouldering an enormous
burden, which is of most indefinite extent because the estimates were exceeded
in the past, whereas the ratepayers have
a firm offer. No more than £200,000
can be paid by them, and, therefore, I
think the ratepayers really owe a debt of
gratitude to the taxpayers in this matter.
There is one other point I thought of.
The two Drainage and Navigation
Trustees are to be done away with, and I
understand there is to be a drainage advisory committee to take their place. I
do not know exactly what their functions
will be, but it has been suggested in our
part of the world that inasmuch as we
are paying 33.96 per cent. and the
County Down is only paying 6.28 per
cent. our representation on that advisory
committee might be increased-not that
I wish County Down to be diminished.
I think it is represented by a very
generous-minded Member of the Senate,
who, I am sure would be the last to
object. In conclusion I wish to say that
this Measure is one which has my heartiest
support.
Mr. McALLISTER: I am sure Members representing every view share the
Government's anxiety that this particular
Bill will successfully function and perform to the fullest extent the functions
that are expected of it. At the
same time after the disappointment—the
costly disappointments of past experiments the spending of large sums of
money must cause everyone identified
with the scheme a great amount of concern. I am sure we all do hope that at
last the Lough Neagh drainage scheme is
going to be successfully grappled with
and carried out. Many claims have been
made for the Government, but I think
the claim of the gallant Senator who sits
behind, that the Government of Northern
Ireland are going to transform the
climate of Northern Ireland is one of the
most welcome announcements that have
been made since the Government started
to function.
There is just one matter the Bill
does which I should like to deal with. As
I understand there will be a great deal
of land acquired compulsorily from
various owners. I think it is the right of
these owners to be heard by a judge and
jury if not satisfied with the amount of
compensation. I think where so much
land is at stake, and where so much is
going to be at stake, and such interests
are at stake of the various owners and
tenant farmers, it is merely elementary
justice to them to have the amount of
compensation to which they are entitled
determined by a judge and jury and not
left to the determination of one man. I
would strongly press the Government to
introduce a Clause giving that elementary right to the people concerned.
Mr. LESLIE: I do not imagine that
now there will be any opposition anywhere to the proposals of the Government
as embodied in this Bill. As we all know
the whole question of attempting to solve
the problem of the drainage of the Bann,
the lake, the Bann valley and the Bann
itself has been before us for very many
years. I think almost everybody now is
agreed that the proposals now before us,
or rather the proposals embodied in the
blue books on which the Bill is based, do
really afford good hope of accomplishing
something that will be really worth while.
There is some apprehension round about.
the locality near where I live myself-a
distinct apprehension in Coleraine and
immediately above Coleraine, that is,
below the Cutts as to whether the Measure
will not result in swamping or flooding
some of the lower lying houses there. I
hope that at a later stage the Government
will be able to give us some definite
assurance about that. I ought almost to
apologise for bringing before the House
a matter that more properly belongs to
the hon. Senator, the Chairman of the
Londonderry County Council (Senator
Long). But the Bann has two banks,
one in Derry, and the other geographically partly in Antrim. One would like
to know who is going to settle what is
known as the Bann catchment area.
own back gate drains into the Bann, and
my front gate into the Bush. In that
neighbourhood the country is somewhat
humpy and it may be that there are places
which still drain into the Bann although
they are apparently nearer the Bush.
The point derives its importance from the
position of the town of Ballymoney, because part of the town of Ballymoney also
My
Adjournment
Dairy Produce Bill-

THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
drains into the Bush, and part into the
Bann, and it would seem rather desirable
that the position should be accurately
ascertained. I do not think there is any.
thing more I shall trouble the House
with. It may be that on the Committee
stage there will be questions to ask on
the various Clauses, but I do not think
this is a Bill in respect of which the
Amendment Paper is likely to be overcrowded. Having read the Bill somewhat
carefully I cannot conceive any Bill
which is better calculated to avoid every
possible cause of friction. Everything
seems to have been foreseen which might
have given rise to trouble if it had not
been dealt with and provided for in the
Bill.
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
I am very
I
grateful for the kind reception given to
this Bill. I am afraid the Government
will not be able to do very much more
than hope as hard as they can for an
improvement in the Ulster climate.
doubt very much whether the drainage
of a thousand Bann's would make very
much difference to our climate, but my
personal opinion on that question will not
have any legal significance.
I would like to reply to two points |
raised. The statutory level of Lough
Neagh that is aimed at is 52 feet above
ordnance datum. Senator Leslie asked

area. It is the Ministry of Finance.
On Question, Motion (That the Bill be

Bill committed to a Committee of the

Marketing of Dairy Produce Bill.
Order for Third Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, "That this Bill be now read the
Third time."
Mr. LESLIE:
There was a point
raised on the Second reading of this Bill
by two hon. Members, and I thought
that perhaps we should have been told
something more about it.
The point was
raised rather indirectly. We all know
that the intention of the Bill is to
improve the standard of Northern Ireland butter with a view to obtaining a
better price and capturing the English
market. Those objects I support, but
the problem seems to me to be, what effect
will the Bill if it is to be a successful Bill,
have on the milk supply of Northern
Ireland. If it should be that we were
not producing enough eggs for export
and for home use the remedy would be
to keep more hens, but the same rule
will not apply to dairy produce. More
cows may be kept, but the man who owns
the cow will send his milk to the source
of disposal that pays him best.
If it pays
him best to send it to the creamery he
will do so. If it pays him better to send
it to the towns he will do that, and if it
pays him better to sell the milk locally
he will do that. But that is where the
difficulty comes in. I do not know
whether the noble Viscount has any information to give the House on that subject, but if not we might defer the final
stage of the Bill until to-morrow in order
that a short statement might be made on
that point. The point is of importance.
Is the success of this Bill likely to seriously
diminish the supply of milk available for
consumption as milk in Northern Ireland!
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I regret
that I have not got information on the
point raised, but I will agree to postpone
the Third reading of the Bill until tomorrow, when I will give the hon. and
learned Member an answer.

and read a First time.
Adjournment.
I beg to

move, "That this House do now adjourn.'
I am sorry to say that, as is usual at this

heavy. We shall, in fact, have to sit on

remaining Bills. I do not know whether
the House would consider the interval ton
short if we took the Committee stage of
the Drainage and Housing Bills tomorrow. If so we will meet on Friday.
and not to-morrow. I should like to have
the opinion of the House on that.
HON. MEMBERS: To-morrow.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Then we
will take the Committee stage of the

THE SENATE.

Thursday, 12th December, 1929.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
Message from the Commons.
The Commons have agreed to the
Amendments made by the Joint Committee to Patterson's Divorce Bill,
Rankin's Divorce Bill, and Burns's
Divorce Bill.
They having passed the Appropriation
(No. 2) Bill, to which they desire the
concurrence of this House.

-(Viscount Charlemont).

And a Message was sent to the House
of Commons to acquaint them that the
Senate have agreed to the said Bill without any Amendment.

1 of 17
CLAUSE 1 (Amendment of s.
and 18 Geo. 5, c. 15, as to grants in respect of construction of houses), ordered
to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 2 (Prevention of evasion of
requirements for building grant).
(2) Where a building grant has been
paid in respect of a house, and at any
time within three years after the payment of the building grant the house
is-
(a) enlarged or altered in such a
manner that it ceases to conform to
the requirements as
to type and
dimensions for the time being in force
for the purpose of the building grant,
or
(b) amalgamated with any other
house or houses, so that such houses
together form a single house,
then, apart from any other remedy or
proceeding a sum equal to the amount
paid as building grant may be recovered
as a debt due to the Ministry of Home
Affairs from the person to whom the
grant was paid, or in the case of his
death, from his personal representative,
or by means of a deduction from the
building grant payable to the said person in respect of any other house, or as
a debt due to the said Ministry from the
owner of the house at the time when
the house is enlarged, altered or amalgamated as aforesaid, or from the owner
of the house at the time when proceedings are taken by the said Ministry to
recover the amount of the said debt, or
to enforce a charge under the next
following sub-section.
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie :-
Clause 2, page 2, line 39, leave out from
"paid" ("grant was paid"), to the word
"or" ("representative, or"), in line 40.
Mr. LESLIE: I mentioned this matter on the Second Reading of the Bill,
expecting that I should probably receive
some explanation at this stage. In order
to draw attention to it I put down the
Amendment on the Paper. The difficulty seems to me to be this.
provides, and in my judgment,
The Bill
very pro-
Housing Bill—
Committee
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
perly provides, that if a house is enlarged
during the three years after the subsidy
has been paid, the amount paid in subsidy
should be recovered, and it is necessary to
provide the steps whereby that can take
place. The first proposal in the Bill before us is that the money may be recovered
as a debt to the Ministry from the person
to whom the grant was paid. That seems
quite right. It goes on, "or, in the case
of his death, from his personal representative". Then it passes on to other possibilities of recovery, including the sale
of the house, as if, in fact, the Ministry
were a mortgagee in possession.
The difficulty seems to be this. As the
Bill stands a man may get his subsidy,
build his house, sell it, and then die. But
within three years of the date of the payment of the subsidy, the man who has
bought the house may enlarge it, and
apparently, from the wording of the
Clause, it will still be open to the Ministry to pursue the personal representative of the deceased builder in respect of
a transaction for which he certainly was
not responsible. I am quite certain that
that cannot be the intention of the Bill,
but so far as I can make out I am very
much afraid that that is what is said.
It seems obvious that you cannot hold
up the estate of a deceased builder for
three years on the chance that someone to
whom he has sold his house may do something to that house. I think that is perfectly clear. I am afraid any builder
would find great difficulty in getting anybody to carry on as his executor if those
words stood in the Bill. Therefore, in
order to get an explanation, I move the
omission of the words on the Paper, but
it will be observed that I am not suggesting that any other words should be
added.
Mr. CAMPBELL: Reading Subclause (2) paragraph (b), it seems to me
that every contingency the hon. and
learned Member (Mr. Leslie) has referred
to is provided for. The subsidy may be
recovered, in the first place, from the person to whom the grant has been paid, or,
in the case of his death, from his personal
representative, or it may be deducted
from the building grant payable to the
said person in respect of any other house,
or as a debt due to the Ministry from the
owner of the house at the time when the
house is enlarged, altered, or amalgamated. I think a very important
principle is involved in that Clause, and
that by leaving out the words suggested
by the hon. and learned Member we
should leave untouched the abuse we are
trying to guard against.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The hon.
and learned Senator who has just spoken
has given the real reply to the question
raised. The Clause says that the Ministry, while the owner of a charge under
the Sub-section, shall have all the powers
of a mortgagee.
Mr. LESLIE: Then I take it that it
is not the intention that a deceased man's
estate should be pursued when he has
sold a house and is no longer responsible
for it, when the other remedy is available.
That must be the intention, but I think
we ought to be told that.
I will
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
answer that question more definitely
later on.
Mr. LESLIE: I ask leave to withdraw
the Amendment. I think it has served
its purpose.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
CLAUSE 3 (Cesser of rate exemption
in certain cases), ordered to stand part of
the Bill.
CLAUSE 4 (Restricted areas for purpose of payment of building grant).
Question proposed: "That the Clause
stand part of the Bill."
Major DOBBS: I understand that this
Clause is meant to stop the payment of
the subsidy on seaside houses intended
only for summer occupation. I have not
the least intention of taking up the cudgels
on behalf of that class of people, but I do
want to enlist the aid of the Ministry of
Home Affairs on behalf of a certain class
of the community who may be affected by
this provision. I refer to ex-soldiers.
Some of them rent houses during the
winter at a lower rate on the distinct
understanding that during the summer
they will give them up. It seems to be a
rather difficult question to deal with, but
I would ask the Minister of Home Affairs
to take that class of people into consideration when he is dealing with these
particular areas.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I will
consider this point between now and the
Drainage Bill-
Committee
12 DECEMBER 1929
further stages of the Bill, and I will give
the hon. and gallant Member an answer
at a later date.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.

CLAUSE 1 (Carrying out of drainage.
of Lough Neagh and River Bann by the
Ministry of Finance, and transfer of
powers).
Question proposed: "That the Clause.
stand part of the Bill."
Colonel PAKENHAM: I rise only to
ask a question on this Clause. I have no
intention of moving an Amendment, because we only read the Bill a Second time
last night and we have not had time to
get in touch with anyone. When the
Ministry of Finance takes over the duties
of the Lough Neagh Drainage Trustees,
I should like to know whether they will
continue to strike the drainage rate as
from the date they take over, or whether
the rate will go into abeyance until something is done.
Viscount
BANGOR: I will answer
that question on the Third Reading.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 2 (Provisions as to Drainage
and Navigation trusts).
Question proposed: "That the Clause
stand part of the Bill."
Mr. LESLIE: As regards paragraph
(e) of Sub-clause (1) I should like any
information that may be available as to
when the liability of the County
Councils is likely to begin. It is apparently to be on the appointed day, but
one would like to have some sort of information as to when, within a month or
so, the appointed day is likely to be.
Viscount BANGOR: Do I understand
that the hon. Member wants to know the
dates on which the Lough Neagh Drainage Trustees and the Lower Bann
Navigation Trustees will be abolished?
Mr. LESLIE: The question put was
when the liability of the county council
to defray any part of the expenses of the
undertaking would arise.
Viscount BANGOR: I understand
that will be when the work is completed.
Mr. LESLIE: Then it follows, I
suppose, that the drainage rate will have
to be paid until that time.
Viscount BANGOR: That is so.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 3 (Preparation of draft
scheme by the Ministry).
Question proposed: That the Clause.
stand part of the Bill.
Mr. LESLIE: Clause 3 refers both to
a draft scheme and to a map. I should
like to be informed as to what possibility
there will be of Members of this House,
and also of the general public inspecting
the draft scheme and the maps relating
to it. It will be remembered when the
blue book was published-
Drainage Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
Viscount BANGOR: I will give a reply
to that at a later date.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 4 (Publication and notice of
draft scheme).
Question proposed: That the Clause
stand part of the Bill.
Colonel PAKENHAM: There is a
I do not
question I would like to ask.
expect to get a reply immediately. As
everybody is aware there are certain
people whom the flooding of Lough
Neagh and the Upper Bann benefits very
largely. I refer to those who have
meadows flooded and the silt of the river
enormously benefits them for grazing ad
for some crops.
Under this Clause when
the draft scheme is prepared the Ministry
shall serve notice on every owner or reputed owner of property to be interfered
with. I want to ask whether these
people whose property is interfered with
and lessened in value are going to be
compensated.
Viscount BANGOR: I will give a
reply to that at a later date.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 5 (Inquiry in respect to
objections to draft scheme), ordered to
stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 6 (Confirmation of draft
scheme by the Ministry).
Question proposed: That the Clause
stand part of the Bill.
Mr. LESLIE: In Sub-clause (3) there
is a provision about serving notice. A
very similar clause appears in a great
number of other places in the Bill, and
this perhaps is as convenient a place as
any other to draw attention to the
matter I want to speak about. In this
particular case notice is served of not less
than one month, and I should say in all
probability, having regard to the knowledge the people likely to be affected
already have of the proposal, that is
quite ample notice. But I want to ask
that care will be taken in any case, where
it arises during the execution of the Act,
that full notice will be given to any person whose right to crop land for the
season is affected. Such a person would
require a good deal more than a month's
notice. Owing to the fact that the land
taken is not of any great width it is not
probable, but a case might arise. I
would like an assurance that the point
will be borne in mind, and if there is
any possibility of the
the cropping programme being interfered with he will
have ample notice.
Viscount BANGOR: It should be
pointed out that in the first place the
Ministry will make the draft Scheme, and
will publish notice in the various newspapers as provided by the Clause, and
serve an individual notice on every person
whose land is to be acquired. There will
be a period of at least one month during
which persons affected will have the
opportunity to lodge objections to the
Scheme, and in the event of objections being received the Ministry will have to hold
a public inquiry. The Scheme cannot be
confirmed until all this has happened, and
as soon as the Scheme has been confirmed
the Ministry must again serve notice of
the confirmation on the parties affected,
and a further month must elapse before
the Ministry proceeds to acquire any land.
In the circumstances it seems that everybody affected will have full and sufficient
notice before his property is interfered
with. I do not think there is any question of rushing anybody. I do not know,
but I do not fancy any of the lands
affected are likely to be suitable for crops.
They are mostly grazing.
Clause ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
CLAUSES 7 (Carrying out of the
scheme by the Ministry) and 8 (Acquisition of land and disposal of surplus land)
ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 9 (Mode of fixing the amount
of compensation).
Question proposed, That the Clause
stand part of the Bill.
Mr. LESLIE: Again on Sub-Clause
(3). It relates to compensation being
assessed in pursuance of the Section in
respect of land or property, regard is to
be had to any benefit to other land or
property owned or occupied by the person
I should
claiming such compensation.
have pointed out that in many cases the
land may have been overvalued
fore these proposals. The valuation may
or may not have been correct before the
flooding, but at the moment it is very
be-
Drainage Bill-
Committee
12 DECEMBER 1929
much below the poor-law valuation. The
question arises, and it ought to be borne
in mind, that until the man's land is put
back to the poor-law valuation it has not
received any benefit from the new scheme.
I think that is a point to be borne in
mind. There is no necessity to move an
Amendment. I think moving an Amendment would probably do more harm than
good.
Viscount BANGOR: The Clause is
designed primarily to deal with questions
of water rights and to prevent persons
claiming compensation for interference
with water rights when, in fact, alternative rights are provided under the scheme.
It is not the intention to take into account in fixing compensation benefits in
the shape of relief from flooding of agricultural lands in respect of which the
owner, in common with the other ratepayers of the catchment area, will, of
course, have to pay rates.
Mr. LESLIE: I thank the noble
Viscount. That is a very important
statement.
Mr. CAMPBELL: With regard to
Clause 9 there is one point on which I
should like to ask an assurance from the
Government. Sub-Clause (1) deals with
compensation to be paid to owners of land
compulsorily acquired under the scheme,
and the power to fix the amount of compensation is in the hands of a single
arbitrator. This scheme will deal with a
great number of farms and very large
tracts of land. I should like an opportunity on the Third Reading of taking
the opinion of the House as to whether or
not it would not be possible having regard to the many interests affected, that
the decision of the arbitrator should be
left open to appeal to a judge of the high
court by any party aggrieved.
Viscount BANGOR: In reply to the
point raised by my hon. and learned
Friend, it may be pointed out that the
system laid down under the Acquisition
of Lands (Assessment of Compensation)
Act, 1919, has been in force for the past
ten
years as regards the acquisition of
land by a Government Department or
local authorities both in England and in
Ireland. No complaints have been made.
of the system except a certain delay and
expense.
If there is now grafted on that
system provision for appeal to a judge.
and jury, or to a judge sitting alone, the
delay and expense must inevitably be
greatly increased. On the other hand,
the Act of 1919 secures that every case
shall receive the fairest possible treatment since the arbitrators are chosen from
a panel appointed by the Lord Chief
Justice and the President of the Surveyors' Institute. They are experts of
the highest calibre. obtainable.
reason has been given justifying in this
particular case a departure from the
system that has proved fair to all parties
when acquiring land for municipal undertakings and street widenings etcetera.
No

Question proposed, That the Clause
stand part of the Bill.
I
Mr. LESLIE : On Clause 12, subclause (2) paragraph (b) defining the area
to be taken for the purposes of the Bill
as the Bann catchment area, apparently
the Ministry is to define the area.
should like some information as to
whether the proposed definition will be
published, in order that those people who,
although very nearly, are not in, may have
an opportunity of objecting. The question happens to be of some little importance to the town where I live. The
greater portion is certainly in the Bann
catchment area, and the other part is
in the Bush catchment area.
It is very
likely that a similar condition of things
will prevail in other district catchment
areas.
Viscount BANGOR: It is not considered necessary to provide formally for
the hearing of objections against the
Ministry's Order. The catchment area is
in fact a thing which can be accurately
ascertained on physical principles, and the
Minister responsible will be answerable to
Parliament for the accuracy of the area
as defined. What my hon. and learned
Friend wants to know is if this area can
be actually plotted out and shown on at
map.
Mr. LESLIE: It would not be practicable as the scale would have to be so
large. A man who is not in a catchment
Drainage Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
area and is put into it, and therefore
would have to pay the rate, would naturally want some provision to safeguard himself. I do not know whether it could be
done by a notice intimating in the case of
a town that the right hand side of such
streets shall be in the catchment area, or
that this or that townland is in it. The
trouble would be that a townland may
be partly in the catchment area and partly
outside it.
Mr. DICKSON: The whole difficulty
about this Act is that the procedure is
altogether too complicated. We cannot.
get down to bed-rock bottom, and the
reason of that is not the fault of the
Drainage Commission, but the fact that
there are three Ministries represented on
that Drainage Commission. We have the
Ministry of Finance in control of the
money. We have the Ministry of Commerce and we have the Ministry of Home
Affairs. All three are represented on
the Drainage Commission and one representative from each of the different
counties, who are swamped by the officials
of the Ministries, and consequently that
complicates the procedure tremendously.
My hon. Friend (Mr. Armstrong) behind
me yesterday spoke of County Armagh
having the largest flooded area, under the
Bann drainage, and stated they had a
right to larger representation on that
account. That is quite so, but under the
major and minor schemes of the Drainage Act other counties are entitled to
representation too. When we have only
one representative, with two, includingthe Chairman-a very capable gentleman
-from Armagh we are swamped on the
Drainage Committee by officials.
Mr. LESLIE: Perhaps I may ask
whether it is the intention that an engineer should, so to speak, perambulate the
water sheds with a view to finding out
exactly where the catchment area should
begin, because if that is so it will settle
the whole difficulty.
Viscount
BANGOR: I understand
that that will be done. I do not see how
the catchment area can be ascertained
unless an engineer does as the hon. and
learned Member suggests. It is purely
an engineer's job.
Clause ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
CLAUSE 13 (Payments out of moneys
provided by Parliament).
Question proposed: "That the Clause
stand part of the Bill."
Major DOBBS: I would like to ask if
any estimate can be given as to the total
cost of this drainage scheme.
Viscount BANGOR: The only information I can give is that it will cost from
£600,000 to £650,000, of which £200,000
will be provided by the counties.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.

CLAUSE 20 (Procedure in connection
with county councils' drainage schemes).
(3) Where the county surveyor, or
other duly authorised officer of the
county council, certifies that the probable cost of a scheme for undertaking
drainage works will amount to a sum
less than one thousand pounds, and the
scheme will not involve the compulsory
acquisition of land, the scheme may be
carried out by the county council upon

to which this sub-section applies is in

scheme," and a minor scheme shall
specify such matters and contain such
provisions as are authorised by, or may
be prescribed under, this Act.
Mr. LONG: I beg to move, in Clause
20, page 19, sub-clause (3), to leave out
the words from "and" in line 32 (“and
the scheme'), to the second "the" in line
32 (of land, the). I am very much afraid
that unless the county councils get some
compulsory powers in this respect the usefulness of the Bill will be very much
curtailed. I recognise that the Amendment I am moving would be somewhat in
conflict with the general tenor of the
Bill, which proposes to leave all these
things to the county councils, without interference from the Ministry of Home.
Affairs, and I quite realise that the Ministry might have some difficulty in acceding to my wish in this matter. Might I
give you an illustration of how this thing
works out.
Drainage Bill-
Committee
12 DECEMBER 1929
The Londonderry County Council were
desirous of widening a dangerous corner.
They approached the owner of the land
to sell, and he asked a sum of about £840
an acre for the land. He calculated how
much it would bring at the building rate
in Belfast, although it was well out in
the country, and he fixed his price accordingly. I afterwards mentioned the
matter at the Roads Advisory Committee,
and in the Roads Improvement Act of
1928, Section 1, power was given to the
county councils to proceed with the
widening of dangerous corners, and an
arbitrator was to assess the value of the
land in case of a dispute. As soon as I
sent this man a copy of the Bill and explained our powers under it to him he
very readily agreed to accept £15.
In many of the streams and rivers
running through the country there are
very acute bends, and these bends are
sometimes the cause of flooding. If we
had power to straighten these bends it
would be of great advantage. I feel we
will not be able to do much in this direction unless we have compulsory powers,
and I, therefore, ask the Ministry to consider this and see if they can agree to
my Amendment, if not now, on the Report stage of the Bill. I feel that it
would increase the usefulness of the Bill,
although I see the difficulties in the way.
The CHAIRMAN: Does the hon.
Member move the Amendment?
Mr. LONG: Yes.
Mr. ARMSTRONG: I can quite confirm what my hon. Friend has said with
regard to the difficulties which the county
councils have in carrying out this work.
We have encountered extreme difficulties
where it is a question of straightening a
river in alluvial lands through which
these streams pass. I feel that unless
something is done on the lines suggested
by Senator Long this part of the Act will
become a dead letter.
Viscount BANGOR: If the hon. Member will be kind enough to withdraw his
Amendment and bring it forward again
on the Third Reading it will have the
consideration of the Ministry in the
meantime.
Mr. LONG: I will do that.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
CLAUSE 21 (Amendment as to expenses of preparation and confirmation of
schemes) ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 22 (Amendment as to appeals).
(2) A person on whom a notice is
served in pursuance of this section
shall, unless he appeals to the county
court against such notice under subsection (3) of this section, be liable to
scour out and cleanse the watercourse,
and, if he fails or neglects to do so
within the time specified in the notice,
the county council may take all such
steps and execute all such works as are
necessary for causing the watercourse
to be scoured out and cleansed in a
proper manner, and such works may
include such widening of the watercourse as may be reasonably necessary
and the deposit of spoil on the lands of
the said person. The whole or any
part of the expenses incurred by the
county council in executing such works
shall, if they so determine, be a debt
due to the county council by the said
person, and be recoverable from him in
a summary manner.
*
Mr. LONG: I beg to move, in Clause
22, page 22, line 18, of sub-clause (2),
after the word "widening" ("include
such widening') to insert the words "and
deepening." Some streams are not able
to accommodate the volume of water
coming down them, and I move this
Amendment to permit of their being
deepened as well as widened.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: I shall certainly support this Amendment, because
you get a greater flow of water with a
deep channel than you will get with a
wide channel. You may make a stream
as wide as you like, but it will still be
shallow unless at the same time you
deepen it. If the Amendment is agreed.
to it will improve the Bill very considerably.
Mr. LESLIE: I was under the impression, rightly or wrongly, that practically
the whole of this part of the Bill had
been discussed with and agreed to by the
Drainage Advisory Committee, and with

the various county councils.
so, one wants to know why the word

is not in the Bill.
Viscount BANGOR: I am informed
that the words "scouring out," which

Mr. LONG: No, no. It is absolutely
different.
It does not
Mr. HILL DICKSON:

If
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM:

the
Before
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
moving the Third Reading of this Bill I
should like to give a reply to the hon. and
learned Senator, Mr. Leslie, who last time
asked the question whether if this Measure
was passed the supply of milk in future
would be unreasonably curtailed. My
right hon. Friend, the Minister of Agriculture, whom I asked on the matter, said he
did not think that would be the case.
perhaps if the hon. and learned Senator
had a more particular question to ask on
the subject he would put down a question
at a future time. I beg to move that this
Bill be now read the Third time.
But
Mr. LESLIE: I thank the noble Viscount for looking into the question I raised.
I will keep the matter in mind and if it
should seem necessary I will put down a
Road Traffic Bill-

question sometime before the Bill comes
into operation, which is not until January,
