On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved, That this Bill do now pass.-
(Viscount Charlemont).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
And a Message was sent to the Commons
to acquaint them that the Senate agree to
said Bill without any Amendment.
Motor Vehicles and Road Traffic Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Mr. CAMPBELL: Before the noble
Lord, the Leader of the House, moves the
Second Reading I beg to draw attention to
the fact that most of us have only seen this
Bill since we came into the Senate House.
We did not even know yesterday that the
Bill would be on the Order Paper. I ask
the noble Lord to give an undertaking
that if necessary the debate shall not be
concluded to-day.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I may say
I have thought of the matter raised by the
hon. and learned Senator. Owing to the
exigencies of time to which I alluded
before, it has not been possible to get this
Bill so that sufficient time should elapse
between the First and Second Readings. I
am very glad to accede to the request of
the hon. and learned Senator. Indeed I
had intended some time before he asked
the question just to move the Second Reading and make the Second Reading speech
and after some of the Senators had spoken
on it to adjourn the debate until tomorrow. I think this procedure will give
hon. Senators a little more time to consider the Bill. I will be able to answer
any questions that arise more fully, I hope,
than I would be if I gave a cursory answer
to-day, and Members will be in a better
position to move Amendments or not as
they think fit when the Committee stage
is taken on Monday. I hope this proceeding will be as agreeable as I can make it
under the difficulties of time to which I
have alluded.
This Bill falls into three parts, as may
be seen from the title. The first part,
which is contained in Clauses 1 to 6,
amends the provisions of our Motor
Vehicles Act of 1926, and deals further
with certain motoring offences. The
second part, which is contained in Clause.
7, proposes to establish a tribunal to fix
passenger fares and rates for public service
vehicles, while in Clause 8 it is proposed
to give the Ministry of Home Affairs power
to make general traffic regulations for
both vehicular and pedestrian traffic on
our roads.
Some explanation as to the need for
amending the existing Act of 1926 may be
desirable. That Act contained two main
parts. Part 1 dealt with the law as to the
use of vehicles on public highways, and
Part 2 with the licensing and regulation of
public service vehicles. The penalties in
Part 1 of that Act for reckless or dangerous
driving were severe and their severity increased for the second and subsequent convictions. In regard to public service
vehicles, at any rate, I am glad to say, that
there has been a great improvement and
that drivers are as a general rule now
trying to observe the law. The present
position at the moment in regard to penalties is that if a public service driver— for
whom, of course, there is still a speed limit
which he must observe-is convicted of exceeding the authorised limit for a third
time, even though with considerable intervals between the offences, he must lose his
licence. It is considered that this causes
undue hardship and in Clause 1 of the Bill
now before the House it is proposed to
modify this severe penalty. Where such a
driver has had a clean sheet for a year any
previous convictions of a like nature are
not to be taken into account. It should
be noted that this only applies to offences
against the speed limit and not to other
offences.
Clause 2 of the present Bill deals with a
situation which has arisen and which has
caused considerable concern to the Government. Where a fatality occurs the driver
of the motor vehicle may be put on trial
for manslaughter instead of being dealt
with summarily for merely dangerous
driving; if, however, the jury find him not
guilty owing to insufficient evidence either
of manslaughter or of causing bodily harm,
as the law stands at present he must get
off scot free, although there may have been
ample evidence that he was guilty of reckless or dangerous driving. A considerable
number of cases of manslaughter have
been tried in recent years and in only very
few instances has a conviction been obtained. It is now proposed that, though
acquitting such a defendant of manslaughter, the jury
the jury may still find
him guilty on the evidence of reckless
or dangerous driving, and sub-Clauses
Road Traffic Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
2 and 3 of Clause 2 give the lesser penalties
for this offence.
In Clause 3 the difficult question of
drunkenness while in charge of motor
vehicles is again dealt with and the principal act suitably amended. It will be
noticed that in Clause 4 in connection with
offences under this Act or under the Act
of 1926 the Court of Summary Jurisdiction
consists of a resident magistrate sitting alone.
This provision was also introduced into the Bill in another place and
was agreed to without a division. The
same thing will apply here, that is, to take
a free vote of the House.
Clause 5 reduces the minimum age for
drivers of the smaller hackney vehicles
from 21 to 19. This is considered reasonable, as I am sure the House will agree
that the driving of one of our larger buses
and an ordinary hackney car is not quite
the same, and that there should be some
difference between them in regard to the
age at which such a driver
obtain a
may
driving licence.
to
in
Clause 6 gives some provisions as
conditions for licences to public service
vehicles. It is provided in the new subclauses which it is proposed to add to
Section 11 of the principal Act, firstly, that
in regard to the routes and time-tables on
which public service vehicles are running
the fares and rates which were in force on
7th November last shall be those to be
charged on that route, subject, however, to
any alteration which the tribunal set up
a later Clause of the Bill may determine;
secondly, as to the manner in which conditions in regard to licences are notified;
and thirdly, the extension of the duties of
the Roads Committee for Northern Ireland
to include advisory functions in regard to
the issue of licences for public service
vehicles, this Committee being suitably increased in numbers so as to be thoroughly
representative of the interests concerned.
Clause 7 deals with the important question of the tribunal. I hope that this
Clause in its present form will satisfy hon.
Members; three persons are to be appointed of whom two may form a quorum,
and they will have jurisdiction over fares
and rates in public service vehicles. Any
person or body of persons may appeal to
them or the Ministry may submit any
representations which it has received for
the reconsideration of any scale of fares in
force. It is, however, provided that in
vexatious cases the persons who make the
application may be charged with the whole
of the costs of the enquiry.
Clause 8 of the Bill gives the Ministry
of Home Affairs powers to make regulations for traffic and this may include mechanically worked signals such as are becoming fairly common in the large towns
in Great Britain. The Government is
fully confident that this Bill will be an
effective contribution to our road traffic
problem, and I beg to move.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I think this one of
the most important Bills that can be submitted to the judgment of this House. It
deals with a question of the greatest public
magnitude, because new features appear in
the emergence on the highroads of cars
which are capable of attaining a speed
equal to a mail train. Legislation with regard to road traffic requires to be brought
up to date. Glancing through this Bill
provokes the question whether it is wise to
deal with a subject of this magnitude piecemeal. I have no doubt that the vast majority of motor-car drivers are anxious to
make our roads as safe as possible and to
co-operate in every way with the authorities, but there is no use in disguising the
fact that there are certain motor-car
drivers who seem to display no sense of responsibility in regard to the safety of the
general public. And this to some extent
is a Bill in relief of that latter less desirable
class of motor-car drivers.
We have abolished the speed limit.
There is a very sharp divergence of opinion.
as to whether that is a wise step or not.
It is said that speed has nothing to do with
the terrible death roll that is being piled
up week by week on the highroads of
Northern Ireland and other parts of these
islands. I recall it is within my own
memory-that along the quays of Belfast
when an engine was propelling traffic
wagons and going at a speed of about two
miles per hour, it was paced in front by a
man with a red flag. That was supposed to
be done for the safety of the general public.
But now apparently the idea is that a
motor car, which can attain a maximum
speed of 70 miles an hour, can race along
the highways without any possibility of
danger to the general public owing to the
rate of speed.
The very first Clause in this Bill removes certain penalties for speed offences,
and the second Clause is a very remarkable
Clause. It deals with a case of death, and
it is a very saddening feature of modern
Road Traffic Bill-
Second Reading
12 DECEMBER 1929
times to read week by week of the number
of deaths that arise on the highways
through motor car accidents. The number
is terrible, and the number of motor car
casualties which do not end in death but in
very serious injury is also very high. What
is this Bill doing in Clause 2? Apparently
juries in motor-car cases are slow to convict. The reasons for that may be various.
We can only speculate as to that. It provides that in case a jury acquit a defendant
on the charge of causing death on the highway or of causing bodily harm, they may
then find him guilty of driving recklessly
or wantonly or at a speed dangerous to the
public. What speed? Having regard to
all the circumstances of the case. There is
no speed limit fixed now. What speed does
this contemplate in Clause 2-a speed
which is dangerous to the public having
regard to all the circumstances of the case?
What is the meaning of the phrase
"driving at a speed dangerous to the public
having regard to all the circumstances of
the case?"
Is it at all likely that a jury which has
acquitted a man of actual manslaughter
will then proceed to convict him of the
lesser offence of driving recklessly or
wantonly on the occasion that resulted in
a death? I think it is very probable that
if they acquit on one charge they will
acquit on both. They will not draw a
fine distinction because they will say the
real charge against the man was one of
manslaughter, and having acquitted him.
of that charge they will hesitate to convict him of the minor offence. But
suppose they convict him on the minor
offence, what is the punishment?
The Section says that any person who
is convicted under the Section shall
be liable to imprisonment for any
term not exceeding six months. This is
a Bill brought forward in the year 1929,
when a highly dangerous class of vehicles
are speeding about the highways.
.
In the year 1861, a matter of 68 years
ago, in the time of our grandfathers,
they were dealing with traffic on the highways, not motor cars or motor bicycles, or
even bicycles, but with broughams, and
farm carts, and the ordinary vehicular
traffic of those days. So zealous were
they of the rights of life and the protection of limb that they passed an Act
called the Offences Against the Person
Act, under which a person in charge of
any cart or other vehicle, who bv wanton
or furious driving or other wilful misconduct, or negligence did any bodily
harm to any person was held guilty of a
misdemeanour and was liable to imprisonment for any term not exceeding two
years. Sixty-eight years ago, when
people had more reasonable regard for
the rights of the public and the sacredness of human life, Parliament was so
jealous to safeguard the people that it
actually provided for a penalty of two
years imprisonment for the negligent
driver of a farmer's cart or a gentleman's
landau. I believe that is still the law,
and yet a
motor-car driver driving a
powerful and highly organised machine.
capable of great speed is, under this Bill
only liable to six months' imprisonment
for a similar offence.
I admit it would be very hard for the
driver of a farm cart to drive furiously,
but if some superman farmer did manage
to commit an offence under that Act of
1861 he would be liable to two years'
imprisonment, whilst the driver of a
motor car who passed him at a speed
twenty times as great is only liable to
six months' imprisonment. That seems
to me to be rather a remarkable way of
safeguarding the interests of the
pedestrian public.
When we come to sub-section (3) I
think there is an improvement there with
regard to the offence of drunkenness. In
ordinary life everyone knows when a man
is drunk, but there seems to be a difficulty in the case of the driver of a motor
car, and I think the provision in the Bill
is some improvement on past legislation.
in this respect. Clause 4 may provoke
controversy with regard to the withdrawal
of this class of case from the ordinary
court of petty sessions. I do not agree
with this policy of limiting the powers of
magistrates. If the Government have no
confidence in them why not pass an Act
of Parliament abolishing them?
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Might I
intervene to say that as regards this particular Clause the Government have got
no policy. This matter was left to the
free vote of the other House, and the
same procedure will be followed here.
Mr. CAMPBELL: Do I understand
the noble Lord to say that this Clause is
not an original Clause in the Bill?
Viscount CHARLEMONT: No, it is
not.
Mr. CAMPBELL: We pass on to
Clause 7 dealing with the tribunal of
three members. Is this to be a per-
Road Traffic Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Campbell]
manent tribunal? For what period are
the Members to hold office? Is it lifelong or only for a certain period? Subclause (5) raises the question of the remuneration of the members of this
tribunal. Surely in a matter of this kind
and on a question of such public interest
there ought to be some indication as to
what the remuneration of the members of
this tribunal is likely to be. Clause 8
gives the Ministry power to issue Regulations dealing with general traffic and
other matters. There is one serious
drawback to this Bill which I submit to
the better judgment of the House.
That
is in regard to the question of compulsory
third party insurance. Look at what it
actually means. Many of these motor
vehicles are in the hands of men who are
in popular language, men of straw. They
get hold of a motor car at some ridiculous
price for which you would hardly expect
to get a broken-down hack, and they can
get a licence for the mere asking. There
are no restrictions on the issue of licences.
An
One of these gentlemen gets hold of
such a motor car, and sets out for one
of our seaside resorts. Perhaps he
imbibes too freely on a week-day-he
cannot do it on a Sunday-and he proceeds to return home. A decent workingman with his wife and family is going
along the country road, and through
sheer neglect and lack of control the
motor car crashes into that small family
group, kills the father, and perhaps injures other members of the family.
action is brought in court, and after a
full investigation the jury may award the
family £1,000 damages. It is all the
law can do, and it is all the family can
look forward to. Yet that judgment of
£1,000 may
be not worth one thousand
farthings. I feel very strongly on this
question in the interests of the poorer
class in the community, who are very
often hard hit by an accident which deprives them of their bread-winner.
I
think it is a pity that the Government
did not bring forward some proposal with
regard to compulsory third party insurance. I understand that difficulties exist
with the insurance companies, but I think
the interests of the public should come
first. I really expect that in a short time
we shall have a Bill submitted to us to
license pedestrians to walk along our
highways.
Mr. LONG: I think the Government
will recognise, as I do, the very fair
manner in which the hon. and learned
Member has criticised this Bill. With
reference to his remarks about third
party insurance, I should like to say that
the Roads Advisory Committee of which
I have the honour to be a Member, were,
I think, the first body in the world to
recommend that all public service vehicles.
should have a policy of that nature, and
their recommendation was very kindly
adopted by the Government and passed
into law. At that time the question of
also recommending the compulsory third
party insurance of all vehicles was considered by the Roads Advisory Committee,
and after careful consideration of all the
circumstances they decided that for the
present they could not recommend the
Government to adopt that course.
I must confess that when the Minister
of Home Affairs first interfered in the
Belfast bus war I considered that he had
made a mistake, in usurping, in some
degree, the powers of local authorities.
Immediately the Home Secretary had
taken that step the local authorities
passed resolutions protesting in very
vigorous terms against his action. These
resolutions were couched in very vigorous
language, and contained a surprising
wealth of adjectives. Local authorities
were inundated with resolutions of this
nature, and many of them were adopted.
At a meeting of the local body to which
I happen to belong. I protested against
one of these resolutions being adopted, and
I gave the reasons for doing so.
It was
in consequence of a practical demonstration I had had since the Home Secretary
interfered in the matter. I should like
to tell the House what that practical
demonstration was. because it entirely
converted me to think that the Home
Secretary was abundantly justified in the
course he took
A person with whom I was on very
friendly terms consulted me as to the
wisdom of buying a passenger omnibus.
I tried to dissuade him, and I pointed
out the heavy overhead charges, and the
high price he would have to pay for the
bus. He was a man of moderate means.
but I did not succeed in dissuading him
from purchasing this omnibus. He bought
it on the hire purchase system. It
cost about £1,500, which he had to pay
at the rate of about £80 a month. A
very short time afterwards he bought
another bus at the same price and on the
same terms. He was succeeding verv
well and was quite pleased with his
prospects when another big bus combine
Road Traffic Bill-
Second Reading
12 DECEMBER 1929
came down with an opposition bus. They
did not content themselves with ordinary
opposition, but if my friend had a bus
about to start at ten o'clock, this big
opposition bus. called the "sweeper bus"
started at two minutes to ten on the same
route, and was followed by another bus
at two minutes past ten. For every bus
my friend had on the road this combine
had two. One passenger said it reminded
him of the barber in Glasgow who offered
to shave him for nothing and stand him
a drink.. The combine were really running their buses at an uneconomic price
that could not possibly give any return.
After a time my friend came to me and
pointed out that his takings for the
previous week had been only £9, and his
overhead charges were very much more,
not to speak of his petrol and his instalments on his bus. He asked me whether
I thought he should consider an offer from
this opposition company to purchase his
buses. I should have said that before
sending down these "sweepers" that company wished to buy my friend's two
buses, but he refused to sell on the ground
that they were his only means of livelihood. But after a time he was quite
pleased to be able to sell at cost and get
out of the business. And no matter what
one may think of the judgment of the
Home Secretary in entering into the
matter at first I think that the ultimate
result has abundantly justified him in the
course that he took.
He has, in my opinion, brought order
out of chaos, and the matter is of
immense importance and interest to the
local authorities who are responsible for
the upkeep of the roads for quite an unnecessary number of buses were travelling
the roadways and cutting up the surface,
and these people in very few instances
reside in the district, and do not in many
cases contribute to the rates. It was also
considered that the Ministry should consult the local authorities as to the institution of bus services or any other
matter of importance-not of course a
trifling matter such as whether or not a
licence should be transferred from a worn
ut bus to a new one. After consideration
it was felt that the only way the Minister
could deal with the matter was by consulting the county surveyor of the county
through which the bus operated. The
Ministry very kindly undertook to do
that. At the present time there is no
service started in our rural districts on a
road that is not fit to carry buses, because
the county surveyor will not give his consent to a service being started on a road
that is not prepared for bus service, and
the result has been a tremendous improvement in the roads in our district.
We have the greatest respect for the
Home Secretary in that Act of his, and
we certainly welcome this Bill. As to
whether the Home Secretary had any
real power to enforce this or whether he
was trying on a gigantic bluff on these
bus owners without any real power at
bottom is a matter of no importance to
me and I do not discuss it. Whether this
is Socialism or what it is, it has been
abundantly justified, and I heartily
support the Bill,
Mr. LESLIE: I must say at once I
did not expect to speak on this Bill this
afternoon, because I had not seen the
Bill in its final form until I entered the
House this afternoon. Therefore, I am
obliged to address the House without any
notes at all. I only mention that matter
in order to crave pardon beforehand if I
repeat myself in any way.
Now, coming to the Bill, I must follow
the noble Viscount and other speakers in
going through the Bill Clause by Clause
as the most convenient way. With regard
to Clause 1, that is the reduction of the
compulsory penalty in speed offences,
irrespective of second or third convictions,
I must congratulate the Ministry of
Home Affairs on having come round
partially at all events to the views which
I expressed in, I think, November, 1926,
when the principal Act was before us.
Those views will perhaps be remembered,
that anything in the nature of a minimum
fine or fixed penalty was a thing to be
abhorred by any conscientious Parliamentarians. I am glad that after about
three years experience the Ministry has
found it possible to insert such a subclause as sub-Clause (1) in the Bill before
us.
Now I pass naturally from Clause 1 to
Clause 2 and I may say I have
endeavoured to follow Clause 2 throughout its stages in another place. It is a
most difficult Clause, as it seems to me,
but I should like again to congratulate
the Minister on having the courage and
prescience to try to grapple at once with
what is becoming an increasing difficulty.
The fact is, I am afraid, incontrovertible
that fatal accidents caused by motor
accidents are not diminishing. It is very
rarely indeed that anybody who has
Road Traffic Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
caused a fatal accident is in fact brought
to book. Any man or woman of course
who kills a person by driving a motor car
recklessly or wantonly or at a speed or
in a manner which is dangerous to the
public having regard to all the circumstances of the case-any person who does
that and kills anybody is undoubtedly.
guilty of the felony of manslaughter.
That, I venture to say, is the law, and I
am sorry that it should be necessary to
suggest any change. Similarly any person causing bodily harm is, under Section
35 of the Act, which the hon. and learned
Senator in front of me quoted to us,
liable to the penalty which he mentioned
of two years' imprisonment, and that
offence, I may remind the House, is what
is called a misdemeanour. It is not a
felony.
Manslaughter is a felony. Causing
grievous bodily harm by furious driving
18 a misdemeanour. The maximum
penalty for manslaughter is penal
servitude for life. The maximum penalty
for that particular misdemeanour is two
years' hard labour. In a case of manslaughter a fine can be imposed at the
discretion of the court. There is no
limit fixed under the Section of the Act
of 1861 as to the amount of the fine which
may be imposed, and, therefore, the protection of the subject from excessive fine
depends solely on the Bill of Rights.
Now it seems to me that manslaughter is
in the eyes of the jury not only a serious
offence carrying very serious consequences,
but I am inclined to wonder very much
whether the difficulty which is experienced
in getting a conviction from a jury is not
to a great extent perhaps due to the wording of the indictment for manslaughter.
In Northern Ireland. where a man is
charged with manslaughter the wording
of the indictment runs: That A.B.. that
is the defendant, feloniously did kill and
slay C.D., that is the man killed, with
such other details as may be thought
right to insert. If the jury listens or
hears I do not think in Northern Ireland the petty jury ever really see the
indictment. I do not think they take it
out of court to their private room.
it may be that the word "feloniously"
frightens the ordinary juryman.
know from my own experience that that
expression gives some difficulty to a jury
of another class, and accounts for the
acquittals so to speak in the case of manslaughter which take place at the grand
jury. It is the grand jury who throw out.
But
I
a good proportion of the bills for manslaughter. The fact remains that the petty
jury find great difficulty in convicting for
manslaughter.
As the law stands at present in an
indictment for manslaughter there is no
alternative offence for which the man can
be convicted except in the very special
case provided for in the Children Act of
1908 under which the person over sixteen
who is charged with the manslaughter of
a person under sixteen may in the alternative be convicted of cruelty to or illtreatment of that particular child. Now
the particular offence of cruelty to or illtreatment of a child under the Act of
1908 is a misdemeanour. It is possibly
punishable on summary conviction. You
can proceed in a case of that sort either
way, but since 1889, at all events, the
offence of cruelty to a child has been a
misdemeanour. The Act of 1908 simply
followed on after the Act of 1904, which
followed the Act of 10 years earlier, which
followed at that time the Act I have referred to.
The proposal here is that if a man is
charged either with manslaughter or
doing bodily harm the jury are to have
the alternative of convicting him of something else. Now the something else is
driving recklessly, and so on, which is an
offence punishable on summary conviction
under the Act of 1926. It is not made
by any statute a misdemeanour, using the
word in that special sense. And I do not
know whether I am correct in saying that
in Northern Ireland the distinction in
ordinary language between a misdemeanour and an offence is preserved.
that is, an offence is a matter punishable
on summary conviction, while a misdemeanour can only be on indictment.
That is the ordinarily accepted method of
distinguishing. I think on the whole it.
holds good.
The fact I want to lay stress on at the
moment is that cruelty to a child under
the 1908 Act is a misdemeanour, just the
same as causing grievous bodily harm by
driving any vehicle whatever, a motor car
or otherwise. There is a precedent there
for not convicting for manslaughter. There
are plenty of other precedents, of course,
for convicting a man charged with one
offence of some lesser offence. But I
think in practice in all these cases-certainly in all the cases I have looked up
at the moment-the man who is so convicted is charged with that lesser as well
as with the greater offence, either by a
separate bill of indictment or by a separate
Road Traffic Bill-
17 DECEMBER 1929
Second Reading
count in the indictment. The distinction I imagine is of more technical
than practical importance, because a count
of an indictment may be treated as a
separate indictment, and really there is
very little difference made.
But it happens that it is not the
practice so far as I know in a serious
charge such as murder to join any second
charge in the indictment, in spite of the
fact that one of the sections of the
English Indictments Act of 1915 plainly
points to the possibility of joining two or
more charges in the same indictment. In
England I think as recently as 1918 the
courts expressed the definite opinion that
in serious charges no second charge should
be joined in the indictment.
The problem then, as it seems to me, is
how are we going to provide that if there
is to be an alternative introduced the man
charged is to know that he is charged alternatively with something else. It seems
to me this is a matter of first-class importance. I do not find any precedent whatever for descending from a felony such as
manslaughter to an offence punishable on
summary conviction. There are plenty of
cases of descent to or from a misdemeanour.
I suppose as good a case as any is the case
of rape which may be reduced as far as
indecent assault but not to common
assault. That seems to be a very great
difficulty. However, I think I have digressed a little into matters of detail and
I must get back to the principle of the Bill.
This difficulty of getting convictions.
from juries is not new. There have been
cases before where juries would not convict. I find it hard to use that expression,
because I cannot shut my eyes to the fact
that if a man has been tried for manslaughter and the jury does not convict it
is not for me or any other person to say
that man ought to have been convicted.
The jury are the judges of fact, and if they
are not satisfied in a case of manslaughter
that the man did either cause the death or
drive improperly it is for them and nobody
else. The difficulty seems to be what is to
be done.
a
The standing example, I think, of diffi
culty in securing a conviction in the past
was in those cases where a mother killed a
new-born baby. Ordinarily that was
matter of murder, and the mother was indicted accordingly, but the public conscience was offended and public sentiment
revolted against that and juries would not
convict. Parliament took a very long time
about the matter. They took more than
the three years which we have allowed,
but they did pass the Infanticide Act in
1922. That invented, so to speak, a new
kind of felony, and provided that a mother
who previously would have been guilty of
murder should be dealt with as though she
had committed manslaughter. I submit
now, and I am going to submit later on,
that the course we ought to take is the
same. We should get rid of the Clause
that is in the Bill now and boldly provide
for a different kind of homicide.
The whole trouble with manslaughter is
that the jury know perfectly well that
there is no intent in manslaughter, whatever else there may be. Anybody knows
that if there is intent it is murder. If
they know a man did not mean to kill anybody when he drove his car furiously they
will not convict him of a felony. I believe
our only hope is to invent a new misdemeanour after the manner of the Infanticide precedent and to try if we can get
on rather better with that, but as regards
bodily harm, I do not think it would do
any great harm if we left that out altogether. It is already amply provided
for.
Although I have had the honour of presiding as foreman of a Grand Jury for
ten years past, including two Winter
Assizes, I cannot recollect that I ever yet
saw an indictment for causing grievous
bodily harm by means of a motor car. It
is, as I reminded the House just now, a
misdemeanour. I would like the hon. and
learned Member (Mr. Campbell) to say
whether any such bills come before the City
Commission. He says "No." If that is
so I do not think we need trouble very
much about the question of bodily harm.
As regards punishment I am not disposed to cavil. As regards the question
of whether a maximum fine should be fixed
or whether discretion should be left to the
court, I think that may stand over for
later discussion.
I come now to Clause 3, the new suggestion as regards drunk in charge. I
know this new Clause has been criticised a
good deal, and criticism has been particularly directed against the words "while in
charge." The words appeared in the
principal Act, the 1926 Act, and I do not
think that introducing new words makes
any serious difference. I imagine everybody will be agreed that the words "in
charge of a motor car" mean in effective
charge, that is, in charge as a driver. It
Road Traffic Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
would not apply, I imagine, to a boy called
to look after a car while the owner was
away.
I shall not, of course, be able to support
Clause 4, which deprives a court of summary jurisdiction of part of its proper
powers, but that can stand over.
I welcome Clause 5, but I should like to
be informed whether it is clear that the intention is to allow a female to drive a small
public service vehicle such as that referred
to in this Clause. This is a matter which
has caused trouble before. In my own
neighbourhood a young woman was in the
habit of driving a motor car as a taxi-cab.
She lost her licence and was not at all
pleased about it. In fact, complaint was
made.
As regards this question of the route or
routes, I think that had better be dealt
with by somebody more immediately con-
I am
nected with the county councils.
afraid I cannot agree that it is right for
the Ministry to consult the county surveyor
on this subject. I think the Ministry
should consult the county council, whose
officer the surveyor is. If the county
council choose to delegate their powers
either to their own finance committee or
directly to the county surveyor, that is a
matter for themselves, but I do not think
we ought to give any sanction to a proposal
that the Ministry, however excellent their
intention, is to be empowered to go behind
the backs of the council, to whom the surveyor is responsible and whose officer he is.
But this is a small matter.
even
SO on.
I come now to the question of the
tribunal that is to have jurisdiction
in the matter of fares, and
I think it is perhaps worth while,
at the cost of being accused
of saying that which everybody knows,
to mention that the expression "public
service vehicle" in this Bill, and
the Act of which it is to be part, does not
include a tramcar. A great many remarks
have been made that seem to suggest that
this Bill will refer to tramcars.
not. Tramcars are not public service
vehicles.
It will
The establishment of a tribunal to run
the matter has been attacked, I do not
quite know on what ground. It seems to me
that the tribunal, especially now that the
number has been raised to three, is as
nearly as possible the counterpart of the
Railway and Canal Commission which attends to matters of this kind in England.
That body seems to do very much as regards railways and canals what is proposed
to be done here, and it seems to me the
proposals are such as should work satisfactorily. We know, at all events, the
chaotic condition of the streets of Belfast
which was in existence until the Minister,
We
as the saying goes, took a hand in it.
know that that chaos was very marked,
and we know that it has to a great extent
disappeared. There seems to be every
reason to hope that under the new arrangment we shall have even less cause
complaint than before.
for
Now I come to Clause 8, which deais
with powers to make regulations. I do not
think that that is a subject on which I need
detain the House at this stage. I may say
at once, having regard to the great and
increasing difficulties of traffic caused
partly by the increase in the number of
motor cars and perhaps even more so by the
extraordinary stupidity of the majority of
pedestrians who use the roads, I think it is
absolutely necessary that somebody should
endeavour to deal with this. I believe
that this Clause will be found effective.
It is nót, after all, a permanent Clause.
I may say that I think the whole Bill is
not unlikely to be somewhat ephemeral in
character. It is a stage on the journey.
No one can possibly get a mechanicallypropelled traffic Bill perfect or one that
would last for ever, but as a step towards
getting better control and possibly towards
greater public safety I believe this Bill will
be found effective.
I am afraid I must, before I sit down,
join issue with the hon. and learned Member (Mr. Campbell) who sits in front. I
am glad that third party risk has not been
included in this Bill. I do not really think
that third party insurance is within the
Title. Apart from the merits or demerits
of the question as a question I do not
really think that we can properly bring
third party insurance within the Title of
the Bill. Therefore, whatever merits it
may have I am glad it has been omitted.
I support the Bill heartily, but I will
endeavour during the Committee stage to
offer a Clause in place of the present
Clause 2, which I hope may be found to
meet the objections and will have the desired result. I, like the hon. and learned
Member in front of me, do not think that
the present Clause in the Bill if it becomes
effective will result in more convictions,
because if a jury will not convict under the
present law they will not convict under this
Adjournment
Rent Restriction Act
12 DECEMBER 1929
as altered. If you offer something new I
do think it is possible you may get a little
improvement. I say that with great hesitation recognising, as I said before, that
questions of fact are for the jury that hear
the evidence and not for any other person
or body of persons outside.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move that this debate be now adjourned.
It
Mr. DORMAN: I rise to draw the attention of the Government to attempts
that are now being made to evade the provisions of the Rent Restrictions Act.
was found necessary in the past for the
Government to amend the law in that

I must
Mr. SPEAKER: I am sorry.
put the question that the debate be adjourned.

On Question, Motion agreed to.
Widows', Orphans' and Old Age
Contributory Pensions Bill.
Brought from the House of Commons,
read a First time, and ordered to be read
a Second time to-morrow.
Criminal Lunatics Bill.
Brought from the House of Commons,
read a First time, and ordered to be read
a Second time to-morrow.
Adjournment.
BUSINESS OF THE HOUSE.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Before I
actually move the adjournment of this
House, I should like, if I can, to give the
hon. Senators an opportunity of knowing
what we intend to do to-morrow. We
intend to take the Housing Bill, Third
Reading; the Motor Vehicles and
Road Traffic Bill, the conclusion of the
debate; the Appropriation Bill, remaining stages; the Widows', Orphans', and
Old Age Contributory Pensions Bill, of
which
we have just taken the First
Reading. This is a Bill in which the
greatest possible haste is necessary, as we
must get the Royal Assent at the very
earliest moment. I do not think that
any hon. Senator here will object
in substance to the intention of the Bill,
but some of the Clauses are extremely
contemplated and difficult for any normal
person to understand. I would like, if
possible, to get my right hon. Friend,
the Minister of Labour (Mr. Andrews),
into the House so that he could give
answers on the particular Clauses, which
I cannot do. It might be possible by
suspending Standing Orders to take all
stages of the Bill to-morrow, and I would
like to do that if it were possible. In
any case, I will ask the Minister of
Labour to come here and take the Second
Reading Stage, and if the House would
consent to my doing so I would like to
suspend Standing Orders in order to take
all the remaining stages. Then we will
take the Criminal Lunatics Bill, of which
we have just passed the First Reading.
I beg to move, "That this House do now
adjourn."
Rent and Mortgage Interest Restriction
Act.
Mr. DORMAN: I rise to draw the attention of the Government to the
attempts now being made to evade the
provisions of the Rent Restrictions Act.
It was found necessary to amend the law
in this respect in the past, because
evasions took place by placing small portions of furniture into houses, of which
the rents were restricted, and charging inflated rents for such premises as furnished
houses. The attempt that is now being
made to evade the law is by issuing short
leases on houses that come under the provisions of the Act. Many agents, I
understand, are introducing a system of
granting leases for fourteen or fifteen
years for such houses, and are charging
rents 100 per cent. above those they
would be permitted to charge under the
Act. I just want to draw the attention
of the Government to this in the hope
that some steps will be taken to prevent
house owners from thus penalising the
people whom Parliament has protected by
the Act at present upon the Statute
Book.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I will
draw the attention of the Government to
the practice to which the hon. Senator
alludes.
Adjournment-
Adjournment
THE SENATE
Mr. LESLIE: The noble Viscount
spoke about our future programme of
business. Might I ask him, if I am not
going too far, if he is in a position to
assure us that the Widows' Pensions Bill
is the same as the Bill which is under
consideration-I think it has been
passed-in Great Britain, and if it is a
Bill in which, under our general arrangements for dealing with these matters, it
is necessary that we should conform to
the English practice? If the noble Viscount tells us that that is so I cannot see
that there can be any objection to taking
all the stages to-morrow as he suggests.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The
answer to both question is in the
affirmative.
Mr. CAMPBELL: Might I state ibat,
having regard to what the noble Viscount
has just said, there will be no objection
on the part either of myself or my colleagues to adopting the course he suggests
and taking all the stages of the Widows'
Pensions Bill to-morrow.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I should
like to thank the hon. Senator for what
he has said. Though it may appear to
the contrary from my actions, I really
am not at all anxious to ask this House
to suspend Standing Orders in order to
pass any Bill in a short time. On this
occasion I am, unfortunately, driven to
do so by the exigencies of time, but as a
rule I like to give all possible notice of
my intention to do so, so that hon. Senators may object if they wish. When they
do not object I am proportionately
pleased.
On Question, Motion ("That this
House do now adjourn") agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at
Five minutes after Five
o'clock.

the Chair.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): The attention of the Government has been drawn to the proposals
mentioned, and they will watch with interest any development which may take
place. The answer to the second part of
the question is also in the affirmative.

Questions to Minister.
NATIONAL MATERNITY SERVICE
SCHEME.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to ask the
Leader of the House whether his attention has been drawn to the National
Maternity Service Scheme for England
proposed lately by the British Medical
Association; and whether the Government have had under recent consideration
the adoption here of the medical benefits
of the English Health Insurance Acts.
NEWTOWNARDS GUARDIANS.

To ask the Leader of the House if his
attention has been drawn to the remarks
of Mr. Justice Wilson, in his address to
the jury, during the trial of Anna Sophia.
Hunter, with reference to the action of
the Newtownards Guardians, and if the
Government intend to make suitable provision for such children, and prevent the
possibility of similar crimes in future.
Mr. SPEAKER: I have come to the
conclusion that I must rule the question
of Senator Joseph Cunningham out of
order, owing to the fact that the sentence
on the woman concerned in the case is
still under consideration, and I think it
would be better to postpone the question.
until that matter is settled. If the question had only referred to the action of
the Newtownards Board of Guardians it
would have been a different matter.
I
I
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM:
submit to your ruling, Mr. Speaker.
had no intention of criticising the trial
or the person concerned. I intended
merely to draw attention to the conduct
of the Newtownards Board of Guardians
with a view to ascertaining if the law
could be amended to make provision for
such a child as the one concerned in this
case.

Appropriation Bill.

Viscount BANGOR: This is a money
Bill, and, accordingly, it is only necessary for me to formally move the Second
Reading. I beg to move.
Housing Bill—
Third Reading.
THE SENATE
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved: "That this Bill be now committed." (Viscount Bangor).
On Question, Motion negatived.
Housing Bill.
Order for Third Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: In moving
the Third Reading of this Bill I have one
or two questions to answer. One is in
regard to the point raised by the hon.
and learned Senator opposite (Mr. Leslie)
as to the personal representative of a deceased builder. I do not think there is
any difficulty created by the point raised.
by the hon. and learned Member. It is
clearly necessary to provide that if a
builder enlarges a house within the prescribed time and dies, recovery can be
made from his personal representative,
or, that if the enlargement is made by the
representative while the house is in his
hands, the subsidy should be recoverable
from him. If the builder or any subsequent holder during the three years sells
the house he will, of course, take the ordinary precaution which would be taken
by any person selling property subject to
a charge, of obtaining a covenant of indemnity.
It will, of course, in the case of a purchaser be necessary for him also to take
the ordinary precaution of ensuring, by
means of the usual requisitions on title,
that he is protected from any liability
which may have arisen from the acts of
his predecessor in title. This being so I
cannot see that any difficulty can arise
such as the honourable Senator suggests.
It is clear that in practice, as a matter
of ordinary common sense, if it should
become necessary to recover any subsidy
the Ministry would proceed on foot of
the charge created by the Section, for this
would be the obvious and simple method.
There was another point raised by the
hon. and gallant Senator (Major Dobbs)
about ex-Servicemen, under Clause 4.
This Clause does not prohibit the payment of subsidies in respect of a house
erected in any area that may be scheduled
by the Ministry. It merely requires that
where an area is scheduled a house is not
eligible for the subsidy unless the Ministry is satisfied that the house is required
and intended for continuous as distinct
from occasional occupation. The Clause
Such
is of course aimed at houses which are
built for mere summer letting and are
shut up for the rest of the year.
houses do not in any way assist to relieve
the housing shortage, and it is not right
that State funds should be used to pro-
If,
mote the building of such houses.
however, the number of houses in any
area, even though a seaside area, is insufficient for the needs of the permanent
population the position is
is entirely
different, and if the Ministry is satisfied
that houses are required for permanent
occupation, even though they may be let
for summer visitors for a couple of
months in the year, it is no bar to the
payment of the subsidy.
Mr. LESLIE: My point was, that if
necessary steps should be taken to prevent the possibility of the estate of a
builder who had bona fide sold and
parted with a house before his death
being attacked in respect of subsequent
enlargement. From what the noble Viscount has said I think it is quite clear
that it is not intended that that difficulty
should arise. On looking further into
the wording of the Clause, I. myself, have
come to the conclusion that the wording
is not strong enough to cover such a contingency as I have suggested. Therefore,
I do not think there is any occasion to
pursue the matter any further, but I am
glad to have had the explanation given
by the noble Viscount
Mr. McHUGH: There is one question
I should like to ask the noble Leader of
the House, and it is a very important
question. As everybody is aware, the
subsidy houses that have been erected are
of very small dimensions, only 650 feet,
and a large number of people who have
erected these houses are very anxious to
build sculleries to them. A scullery to
each of these houses is most essential,
and I would like to know whether people
who add a scullery are committing any
offence.
Mr. DORMAN: I hope the right hon.
Gentleman will be able to give an answer
to Senator McHugh's question. I have
been in a number of these subsidy houses
during the past week, and they have evidently been built with the object of
evading the conditions for the subsidy
and with the intention, in the immediate
future, of extending the premises by adding a scullery. As a matter of fact the

houses have been deliberately built with

to
