Motor Vehicles and Road Traffic Bill.
Order read for resuming adjourned
debate (12th December)-on Question,
"That the Bill be now read a Second
time."-Viscount Charlemont).
Question again proposed.
Mr. McALLISTER: I rise to oppose
this Bill on various grounds. In the first
place, I suggest to the House that there
has been no demand for this Bill from
any source. Not only has there been no
demand for the Bill, but it is a Bill which
is universally unpopular, at, least, outside the City of Belfast. Outside this city
the Bill is viewed with grave suspicion,
and, in my opinion, that suspicion is well
and truly founded. I think it cannot
be denied that this Bill has been introduced, because of a certain now very
notorious agreement entered into between
the Government and the Belfast Corporation and the bus owners plying for hire
in Belfast. A commission was set up by
the Government to deal with the result
of that bus war, and I am not going to
detain the House as to what was the
reason for the state of affairs that then
prevailed-whether it was owing to the
negligence of the Government or of the
Belfast Corporation, or both-I do not
intend to dwell upon that. The commission was set up to deal with the immediate cause of difference existing in
Belfast. Witnesses, I presume, were
examined, representatives attended from
the city and representing various viewpoints, but so far as I am aware no representative from the country of any
description appeared before that Committee, and for the first time, when the
agreement was signed, sealed, and published, the people of the rural and urban
areas found their interests were to be
sacrificed in order to accommodate the
views of the different interests in Belfast.
That, I submit, is the real ground for
the introduction of this Bill and it cannot be justified on that ground.
to
create a
In the second place this Bill is going
be a reactionary and
and retrograde
Measure. It is going to
monopoly. I think it can be suggested from what has been stated
in another place, and the evidence
of public opinion published through letters
written to the Parliamentary representatives--I think it can truly be said that
the administration of this Act by the
Minister of Home Affairs is open to the
suspicion that he at all events will be a
willing agent for the creation of those
monopolies. Instances have been published where the Ministry of Home
Affairs have actually complained that
Owners of buses whose vehicles have
passed every test of the Ministry for
structure and accommodation, and every
other qualification rendering them fit and
proner vehicles to carry the public on the
road-that complaints have been made
that the owners of those vehicles have
charged too little between various points
for the transport of the traveller, the
noor common people of Northern Ireland.
It seems to have arrived at this stage
that the Minister of Home Affairs is to
rule everv detail of the people of
Northern Ireland. That the liberty of
the subicet is a thing almost unknown.
Within recent years we have been told
that the Minister of Home Affairs can
dictate to the individual citizen when he
is to have a drink. Now he is to dictate
when, where, by what means, and at
what cost he is to travel from one point
to another.
Road Traffic Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. McAllister]
As to the details of this Bill I think it
should always be in the interests of the
public-and I would appeal to this House
always to encourage by legislation of
every description when at all practicable.
-that the person aggrieved should have
the right to appeal to the oldest and best
and most satisfactory tribunal, the one
tribunal that commands universal confidence, the tribunal of a judge and jury.
From that point of view in all matters
pertaining to this Bill, and governed by
it I would suggest that the House should
make provision for the citizen being protected by the right of appeal to a judge
and jury.
There is another aspect that occurs in
all these Bills we have been passing
recently, that is, the tremendously comprehensive powers which we delegate
from this Parliament-which it is the Parliament's duty to legislate specifically and
expressly we are delegating to various
officials and various Government Departments to make regulations and transform
the whole of our Bill that we have passed
in this House. After the Bill has become law we for the first time see some
extraordinary, reactionary, and restrictive administration passed by the Ministries who have power to make regulations under this Bill. I think this is a
fitting time to refer to and to jog hon.
Members' memories upon this particular
aspect of legislation. Power to make
regulations was given to the Ministry of
Home Affairs under the 1926 Motor Act.
I do not know whether any hon. Members
of this House have seen those regulations,
but I confess when I saw them they
seemed to me to be more comprehensive in
volume, and certainly much more farreaching in effect than ever it was
intended the Act itself should be
I submit with perfect confidence that
no Member of the Parliament of Northern
Treland ever for one moment thought
when we were empowering the Ministry
of Home Affairs to make regulations
under the 1926 Act. that he would take
the stand he did and that he should shut
the King's highway by his own interdicts.
To the surprise of everyone he stepped in
in Belfast and took control of the whole
traffic of Belfast under the regulations
which no one ever anticipated or suspected. Not only so but he took control
of the bus traffic of Northern Ireland,
and he imposed his will upon them, not
under any power expressly given him by
Statute but under regulations which he
had made and dictated to the public what
price they should pay for the transport
of themselves and their property in public
vehicles in Northern Ireland. I suggest
with perfect confidence that no Member
of this House ever anticipated or suspected for a moment that such a wholesale power would be taken upon himself
by the Minister of Home Affairs in providing these regulations, and the House
never intended that he should be a dictator far above Parliament, and far above
the will of the people in all these things
pertaining to the administration of the
Motor law.
What is the effect already before this
Bill is passed at all? It has given a
premium to certain large companies of
motor bus owners. We find already before the Bill is passed that 28 routes in
Northern Ireland are already in the
hands of the railway companies. These
large companies are financed, and run,
and engineered to a great extent by shareholders in England. I do say it is not
fair that the poor farmer and peasant, or
the common people of Northern Ireland
should be compelled to pay, through
regulations or through Acts of Parliament, for the transport of themselves and
their produce more than they would have
to pay were the field open to legitimate
private competition and private enterprise.
I
I think there is one defect in this Bill,
and I do not intend to develop it now.
I will develop it later on on another stage.
I think the fact that the speed limit of
public service vehicles is limited to 20
miles an hour, or any Act of Parliament
which does that is perfectly absurd.
am prepared at a later stage to argue,
and I hope successfully, that having the
speed limit so low is in its operation a
real danger to the public in Northern
Ireland, because anyone who is conversant
with the bus traffic knows that there are
only certain points along the road where
police traps can be laid. These positions
on the various routes are familiar to all
these bus drivers. and the result is that
passing through these well defined road
trap positions they go painfully and
ridiculously slowly. But the minute thev
are clear of this and round the bend at
the most dangerous parts of the roads
they travel at anything un to 40 or 50
miles an hour. Another thing with these
police traps, the driver, instead of being
allowed to go at 27 or 28 miles an hour to
Road Traffic Bill-
Second Reading
13 DECEMBER 1929
keep to his time-table and instead of concentrating all his attention on the road before him and driving his car, is watching
for police traps in this lane and behind
that haystack, and watching everything
but what he should be watching.
I do say that it is in the interests of the
safety of the public of Northern Ireland,
whether the pedestrian public or the
travelling public using the public service
vehicles, that this speed limit should be
raised to a reasonable amount. When I
say that I say there is no one in this House
more anxious for the safety of the public
and for the safest possible way of driving
these cars than I am; and as evidence of
that, if the speed limit of public service
vehicles is raised to a reasonable rate of
27 to 28 miles an hour, no one would support a more rigorous administration of
such an Act of Parliament more heartily
than I will do.
Major DOBBS: I rise with diffidence,
but I should like to say one or two words
on this Bill from the point of view of the
owner-driver who was driving when the
twelve miles an hour speed limit was in
force. The hon. Senator (Mr. Campbell)
was last night disturbed by the abolition
of the speed limit. The speed limit
for the last few years has become
an anachronism. I have seen

a big
Bentley going down the Kingston
by-pass road at a stately gait, threading
the traffic in perfect safety and at a speed
at times of 70 miles an hour. That was
perfectly safe I think, and in the opinion
of those motorists who witnessed it. I
have also seen a small Morris car going
down a village street at a speed of ten
miles an hour and driven-to use no harsher
term-in a most inconsiderate manner.
And, therefore I think it is quite obvious
that speed per se is no criterion of recklessness.
The hon. and learned Senator was also
very exercised in his mind because from 70
years ago until now he could get a
butcher's boy who drove furiously twentyfour months and when this Act is passed he
can only get me six months. I am sure he
has my sympathy. Clause 4 has my
whole-hearted support in leaving the
matter to the resident magistrate.
I would like to appeal to the Minister
of Home Affairs to take power to compel
the county councils to keep the footpaths
on all our roads in good preservation.
You cannot expect young girls to splash
through pools of water or walk on stony
pachs They naturally take the middle
course, and walk in the centre of the
road, and are a menace to motorists and
a danger to themselves. I would also
appeal to the Minister to deal with the
question of headlights. The present practice over here is, I think, that when two
motors approach each other they both
turn off their headlights and plunge the
road into a state of cimmerian darkness.
It is a barbaric act, and a great danger to
any pedestrian or cyclist who happens to
be clothed in sombre attire.
With regard to Clause 8, I see it provides power for the use of mechanically
worked signals. I hope if the Minister
takes this power he will take expert advice
from London or abroad, and not take local
advice on this matter. Most of the signals
on the roads in the city of Belfast are
placed in the one position where
they cannot be seen by the driver
of a modern motor car. I also hope
the Minister will not take the roundabout at Shaftesbury Square as a critérion
of what a roundabout should be. A more
puerile kind of roundabout has never been
invented. I should prefer to drive twentyfour hours around Trafalgar Square or
Piccadilly Circus than drive for one hour
between the hours of half-past five o'clock
and half-past six o'clock round Shaftesbury Square at the present time. I trust
that the Minister will take notice of those
very good signals on the roads near
London which are lighted up and
scintillate when the headlights of
motor car are thrown on them.
There are some on the Great West Road
which are excellent. One drawback in
this connection is that here and there
where you have these excellent signals
pointing out that there is a dangerous
corner or cross-roads, you find also advertisements put up such as "Winter on the
Riveria, " "Come to Hydres for the sun."
I hope when the Minister is taking power
to put up these signals he will take
power to abolish these advertisements.
One of the things most noticeable to the
English motorist in connection with the
roads in this Province are the hand signals.
These hand signals in Ulster are like the
proverbial snakes in Ireland. Another
thing to be noticed is that a great number
of the users of our roads think the roads
are made for their especial benefit.
Again, I would like to point out the
thoughtlessness of the pedestrian. He will
try to go across a crowded road looking
neither to the right nor left, and the time
he chooses to go across is just when it is
dusk and it is hardest to see.
I am
Road Traffic Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Major Dobbs]
sure if the Minister could only get
motorists and pedestrians alike to recognise the courtesies of the road there would
be very little use for these Bills.
Colonel PAKENHAM: I only want to
intervene for one moment in this debate,
and that is to draw the attention of the
House to Clause 7, which provides for the
setting up of a tribunal. This tribunal
is to be composed of three persons, whose
names we have not yet been favoured
with. I would like to express my view
very strongly, and that is that these three
persons should be quite impartial gentlemen connected with no Department of
State in this country and connected with
no interest whatever. They should
be people who can take a really
fair and entirely impartial view of
the whole matter. The Government
has not told us who these persons
will be, or from whom they are to be
selected. I think it is a very important
thing in a matter of this kind, especially
where the Ministry of Home Affairs has
control of a vast organisation, that no
officer or anybody connected with that
Ministry should be on that tribunal. I
want to say that that is my firm opinion
on this matter.
My hon. and gallant Friend (Major
Dobbs) made some remarks about pedestrians. I have listened in this House to
abuse largely of motorists, but I have
heard little abuse of pedestrians. I have
been driving a motor car since 1905. I
am therefore an old motorist. During the
whole of that long period I have never
knocked down a person or knocked down
a dog. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear). I
can assure you as one who drives about
the country that I live in absolute terror
when I am driving on the roads all over
Ireland. Most hon. Members know well
that wonderful road from Belfast through
Antrim to Ballymena. It is a concrete
road 17 miles long, about the longest distance of any road in the whole of the
United Kingdom. It is furnished with
very ample footpaths, but when I drive.
along it I never see anybody walking on
the footpaths. Every pedestrian along
that concrete road always walks either on
the middle of the road or at the edge of it
although he has a perfectly excellent footpath at his disposal. He is not only taking
the risk of getting himself killed but
of killing other people who are allowed to
travel on the road. I contend that a great
many accidents are caused by the stupidity
of the pedestrian not keeping on the footpath. The unfortunate motorist has to
keep on the road. If he could drive his
car on the footpath he could avoid a lot
of accidents. I put it strongly that it
should be the duty of the police, when an
accident occurs, to see whether the pedestrian is not as much at fault as the
motorist.
Every-
I would like to add one thing.
body knows that the Automobile Association and kindred body, the Royal Automobile Club, are placing road marks all
over the roads in Northern Ireland very
much to the advantage of motor drivers
and also to the advantage of a large
number of people. Yet we have inhabitants of this country who make cockshies
of them and nothing else. Many of these
road marks that have been put up are
so effaced that they cannot be read.
Surely such barbarity and incivility
should be very carefully watched by the
authorities. The county councils should
take some action in the matter. It is
quite a common thing all over Ireland.
In every county I have been in during
the last six months I have seen
enormous number of these road marks
used as cockshies. Whether it is grownups or young people are responsible I do
not know, but it is reprehensible conduct.
an
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: I
would like to say a few words in connection with this Bill. First of all, as to
the speed limit, the hon. Senator (Mr.
McAllister) said that 27 miles was a
reasonable rate at which a motor car
should travel. I would prefer to see it
go at 35 miles an hour. I would allow
a little more than the hon. Senator says.
It will be very hard to find unanimity on
this question as to the right speed on a
road. I think if the road was clear a
higher rate than suggested should be
allowed.
Then there is the question as to the
tribunal to be set up to try these cases.
I must say I welcome the idea that this
duty should be taken out of the hands of
the local magistrates and put into the
hands of a resident magistrate. Resident
magistrates. as I understand, are all
trained lawyers. They are either
barristers or solicitors, who have an idea
of what the law is. They can hear the
pros and cons of the case. A man who
finds himself in a motor accident should
be able to engage counsel, and a solicitor,
and his case can be looked after by them.
Therefore, it is only fair to have a trained
man to decide these cases.
Road Traffic Bill-
Second Reading
13 DECEMBER 1929
I know there are a few justices of the
peace who are as good as any judge on
the bench. They know the law, but they
are an exception. Then again it is
hardly fair to ask a local justice to decide
a case in which perhaps one of the parties
is a neighbour of his own who has got
into trouble. He has to decide whether
the man has had a little too much drink.
We know if he decides that there has
been no drink it is all right but if he
decides otherwise it gives a good deal of
offence. I hope, therefore, that these
cases will be tried before a resident
magistrate.
Mr. ARMSTRONG:
I wish to say
that on the whole I heartily support this
Bill. The hon. Senator (Mr. McAllister)
seemed greatly in doubt as to the regulations which he thought unnecessary.
It is very usual to find a Bill about half
an inch in thickness accompanied by regulations two or three inches thick. I
think this is a case in which regulations
are necessary. We have.confidence in the
Ministry of Home Affairs. The Senator
made a comparison between the railways
and the motor buses. He rather indicated that the railways were in some respects to blame. He mentioned that the
English shareholders were dominant, and
had a great influence in the railways. I
think that is entirely a mistake. The
chairman at one railway meeting stated
that the average holding in a railway is
£200. I do not believe English shareholders invest in this way.
From my
own personal knowledge I know that
many of the shareholders are people who
had saved a little money and believed the
railways were as safe as banks. Schoolmasters and schoolmistresses who retired
thought that was a very good way of investing their money instead of putting it
in savings banks.
one.
As far as I can judge I think the Tribunal to be set up will be a very excellent
It is right to take away from the
Minister as much as possible the power
of giving the final decision in matters
where there is a dispute. I prefer to
leave that in the hands of a judge. With
regard to the speed limit, it is a most
difficult question and I do not know how
it can be very satisfactorily solved.
doubt there are some points on the roads
where you might travel at any speed, but
there are other cases when you cannot go .
too slowly.
No
Reference has been made by my hon.
and gallant Friend (Colonel Pakenham)
to the stupidity of pedestrians, but we
must remember that the pedestrian has
some rights. I am not aware that the
motorist has a monopoly of the road. It
may be very desirable that the pedestrian should keep to the footpath, but I
do not know that he is obliged to do so
in law. My experience has been that so
much money has been spent on improving.
the roads that the footpaths are very
much neglected, and as a pedestrian on
many occasions I certainly walk in the
middle of the road, or at the side of the
road. I certainly do not walk on the
footpath. We must remember that there
are two sides to every question, and that
is a point to be considered. On the
whole, and with some little reservation,
I support the Bill as being a very satisfactory one.
Viscount
CHARLEMONT: I am
afraid it would be impossible for me to
reply adequately to all the points that
have been raised in this very interesting
debate, and a good many of them can be
more satisfactorily answered as definite
questions at a later stage of the Bill. I
should, however, like to say something
about the tribunal to which reference has
been made. This, as is known, is to sit
under the chairmanship of Sir Lynden
Macassey. The other two representatives.
are at present unnamed, but, on behalf
of the Minister of Home affairs, I can
promise they will be men of the strictest
impartiality. There are various matters
that were raised by hon. Senators
opposite to which I shall draw the attention of the Minister of Home Affairs.
I must say that I am in great sympathy
with what the hon. and gallant Senator
(Colonel Pakenham) said about road signs.
Within a radius of about five miles from
where I live in the country there is only
one road sign. The end of it having been
broken it now only says
To Cook,"
without saying who is to be cooked or how
one is to get it. (Laughter.) We can
only surmise that originally the sign was
"To Gookstown," but there is no reason
to suppose that from the sign as it stands.
I will have a personal interest, therefore,
in pressing this matter upon the attention
of the Minister.
The hon. Senator (Mr. McAllister)
opposite, who objected to the Bill in
principle, also raised many points which
Road Traffic Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
I cannot answer now.
He spoke a great
deal about the liberty of the subject.
That is a very good thing to speak about,
but in practice I do not think the subject
has very much liberty, because if he had
he obviously would not be a subject. In
any case he has no liberty whatever to be
killed. The whole principle of this Bill
is to save life. 1 might remind hon.
Senators that the most popular Acts of
Parliament are not always the best, and
that many Acts of Parliament which have
been amply justified have not been very
popular in their inception, income tax,
for instance, and others.
The hon. Senator complained that a
monopoly had been established, but I
really do not think there is any substance
in that charge. The bus services are
under the supervision of the Ministry,
and, though the Ministry have power to
make regulations, I would draw the hon.
Senator's attention to sub-section (3) of
Clause 7 in which it says:
"If an address is presented to the
Governor of Northern Ireland by either
House of Parliament within the
statutory period after any regulation
under this section is laid before such
House, praying that the regulation
may be annulled, the said Governor
may by Order in Council annul the
regulation.
I think that gives sufficient safeguard to
the representatives of the public.
The
I would like to refer for a few moments
to the questions raised yesterday by the
hon. and learned Senator (Mr. Campbell) about the speed limit. I cannot
agree with the suggestion that this Bill
will make the way of the transgressor
against the motor laws more easy.
hon. and learned Senator raised the
question of the abolition of the speed
limit. This Bill does not in any way
abolish a speed limit. That was done, in
so far as it has been done, by the Motor
Act of 1926, which was approved by
Parliament, so far as ordinary motor cars
were concerned. While that Act
abolished the speed limit, it strengthened
very materially the penalties incurred by
motorists for reckless and wanton driving
and other offences of a similar nature.
The only point where the automatic
penalties are in any way relaxed is in the
case of a driver who has committed the
offence of exceeding a speed limit and has
not been guilty of any similar offence for
In such a
a period of twelve months.
case his conviction is to be treated as a
first conviction. That does not mean that
he is to be subject to a light penalty.
Even for a first offence the Court can inflict severe penalties. They can fine him
from £1 to £10, and they can, if they so
desire, suspend his licence. These are
severe penalties, having regard to the
class of persons to whom they apply.
The hon. and learned Senator also
suggested that this Bill lays down the
principle that the taking of human life by
a motorist should not be punished by a
greater penalty than six months' imprisonment or a fine, or both. I do not think
that the Bill can be construed in that way.
Clause 2 provides that the jury may convict for an alternative offence, and fixes
the maximum penalty in the case of such
conviction at six months' imprisonment or
a fine, or both, in the discretion of the.
Court, but it is entirely a matter for the
jury whether they convict for manslaughter, where the penalties are very
much greater, or for the lesser offence.
The juries are the representatives of the.
public, and the Government have no
responsibility whatever for their action.
This Bill does not in any way derogate
from the powers of the jury, but it increases their powers, and it is for them,
and for them alone, to say how those
powers will be used.
The hon. and learned Senator also referred to third party insurance. In view
of the statement which was made in this
House some time ago in regard to the
position in this matter, I do not think it
is necessary for me to say very much. In
the first place, however, I would like to
repeat that the Government are not opposed to the principle of compulsory third
party insurance, but the matter is one of
great complexity and difficulty, and if we
are to introduce a system of compulsory
third party insurance we must be first
satisfied that we can produce a practicable,
workable, and effective scheme.
reasons which I have already stated to the
House, and which I need not repeat, it
would be impossible for us to embark on
such a scheme without a similar scheme
being put into force in Great Britain.
Since I made my statement the Imperial
Government have introduced legislation on
the subject, but the Government here have
not yet had time since its introduction to
look very closely into it, and, without in
any way suggesting any criticism of the
For
Road Traffic Bill
Criminal Lunatics Bill
13 DECEMBER 1929
scheme now before the Imperial Parliament, we have no guarantee that that
scheme will be embodied in an Act in its
present form.
I should like, however, to give the
assurance that this matter will be very
closely and carefully gone into, and if as
a result of these investigations my right
hon. Friend, the Minister of Home Affairs,
is satisfied that he can produce a scheme
which he can recommend with confidence
to Parliament he will have no hesitation
in coming to Parliament for the necessary
powers.
Now, I would like to deal with the
point raised by the hon. and learned
Senator Leslie with regard to Clause 2.
This has been the subject of very careful
consideration by the Attorney-General, in
consultation with the other legal advisers
of the Government and the Parliamentary
draftsman, and they are quite satisfied
that it will attain the object in view, an
object which has received general
approbation.
While the discussion of the points indicated by the hon. and learned Senator
will probably be more appropriate in
Committee, I may say that there are
numerous precedents for the principle of
the Clause, and also for the procedure
involved. There are many cases where
statutes have laid down that where a
prisoner is tried on an indictment for a
particular crime the jury may convict, if
they so desire, on another crime of a less
grave nature without that charge being
specifically set out. This is a convenient
and well-recognised procedure and no injustice is done to the prisoner. Where
he is legally represented his advisers are
well aware of the alternatives, and in the
rare case where a prisoner has not the
advantage of professional advice the
Court may be relied on to see that he is
fully informed of his position. Any
suggestion that there should be two indictments would lead to great difficulties.
The hon. and learned Senator referred
to the provision which makes it compulsory upon the Ministry to consult the
County Surveyor before authorising the
use of buses upon any road which is not
already an authorised route, and suggests
that it is the County Council which should
be consulted. This matter was carefully
considered by the Roads Advisory Committee, including the County Council representatives. The reason for the form
of the present Clause is that we are dealing with commercial undertakings and it
would undoubtedly hamper the development and smooth working of these
undertakings if there were undue delay
in giving decisions on matters of this
kind. County Councils, and even their
Committees, only meet at considerable
intervals, and if it were necessary to wait
for these meetings very considerable delays would be inevitable. The County
Council representatives saw the force of
this argument and agreed, without
hesitation, to the present position, being
perfectly satisfied that it sufficiently
guarded their interests. It is quite open
to a council, of course, to give general
directions to their surveyor as to how he
should exercise his functions in this matter, and to arrange for consultation with
the council or with the committee in such
cases as consultation might be necessary.
The Clause, however, has been designed
with a view to securing the interests of
the county councils without undue delay.
I hope I have now answered most of the
points raised during the debate, but if I
have not done so I shall be happy to
consider them at a future stage of the Bill.
On Question, Motion ("That the Bill
be now read a Second time') agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
whole House for Monday next.
Criminal Lunatics Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount BANGOR: This Bill, of which
I now beg to move the Second Reading,
has as its purpose the establishment of a
criminal lunatic asylum in Northern
Ireland.
The need for this arises owing to the
following circumstances. When the
Government here was set up there was
only one criminal lunatic asylum for the
whole of Ireland, namely, that in Dundrum, outside Dublin, and the existing
legislation in regard to criminal lunatics
could only be construed as applying to
that institution. Since 1921 we have, by
arrangement with the Free State Government, kept our criminal lunatics there,
and our Government have paid for their
maintenance, which was a very considerable charge, varying between £175 and
£200 per head per annum. There are at
present twenty criminal lunatics being
maintained in this manner.
427 Criminal Lunatics Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Viscount Bangor]
The Government here, of course, only
regarded this as a temporary arrangement, but it was a matter of very considerable difficulty to find a permanent
solution, as with such small numbers it
would probably be even more expensive
to set up an entirely new institution in
Northern Ireland. It has now, however,
been found possible to turn part of the
prison at Londonderry into a criminal
lunatic asylum, SO that the overhead
charges may be divided between the gaol
proper and the new institution.
It is,
accordingly, enacted in the Bill that a
part of this prison should be specified as
the Criminal Lunatic Asylum
for
Northern Ireland, and it is provided that
any enactments in existing legislation
shall be construed accordingly.
Clause 3 of the Bill provides for the
establishment of a practice which is
common in other countries, namely, that
of sending the less dangerous or the less
violent cases to a District Lunatic
Asylum,but no such cases will be sent to
these ordinary District Lunatic Asylums
unless after a full consultation with the
authorities concerned, and, of course, the
full cost will be reimbursed by the State
to these latter institutions for any who
may be sent to them. The effect of all
this will be that on the basis of the present numbers there will only be some of
these lunatics, that is the more violent
lunatics, in the new block in Derry
Prison, the remainder being distributed
over
other asylums. The result of the
whole arrangement will be that there
should be a considerable annual saving
in total cost.
The remainder of the Bill provides for
the proper supervision of these criminal
lunatics, and arranges proper conditions
of service for any officers whom it may
be necessary to employ. I beg to move.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I would like to
know from the noble Lord what particular reason there has been why the
historic city of Derry should be chosen as
the headquarters for criminal lunatics in
Northern Ireland?
Mr. LONG: I suggest the reason why
Londonderry Prison is unoccupied at the
present time is the peaceable state of the
district. As a Visiting Justice to that
prison appointed by the Grand Jury I saw
the preparations made for the reception
of these prisoners, who will, I have
no doubt, be
very well cared for
and properly attended to. Also,
as Chairman of the Asylum Committee
at Londonderry, I quite recognise that
criminals should be segregated from the
ordinary inmates of the asylums, and it
is not meet, or seemly, or right that they
should be allowed to mix.
In most of our lunatic asylums in the
past the Resident Medical Superintendent
had difficulty in segregating from the
other patients those with criminal
tendencies. Quite recently a patient of
that type in the asylum at Londonderry
ate the entire cheek off another patient,
and what disgusted everybody was the
fact that he seems to have swallowed the
cheek. At any rate, it was not found.
I do not like to tell this gruesome tale,
but I do so in order to illustrate the point
I am making that it is not right that
lunatics of that tendency should mix with
peaceable lunatics.
Mr. SPEAKER: I do not want to
interrupt the hon. Member, but I do not
think I should encourage the practice of
private Members answering questions
addressed to the Government.
Mr. LONG: I did not intend to answer
the question. I thought I had given the
Leader of the House an opportunity to
reply, and that he did not embrace it.
Viscount BANGOR: I think my hon.
Friend (Mr. Long) has given the information needed as to why the criminal lunatics
should be sent to Derry Jail. I understand it was the most suitable place for
them. There are not many who will be
sent there, and as there happened to be
this accommodation in the jail I suppose
that is why they are being sent. I quite
agree with the hon. Member that it is most
necessary to segregate the different classes
of lunatics. That is done very largely in
all lunatic asylums where refractory
lunatics are separated from the more peaceable ones. That is only a matter of
commonsense. There are epileptics. They
are put on one side. That is the practice
in all these asylums. I fancy Derry Jail
was chosen simply because the most suitable accommodation was there, and
because there were not many of these
lunatics of a violent character to be sent.
Mr. LESLIE: I would like to ask
whether I correctly understood the noble
Viscount to say that a criminal lunatic
would not be transferred to a district
Pensions Bill-
Second Reading
13 DECEMBER 1929
T.
frac
relea
ate
teasylum without the consent of the authorities of that asylum. I think that is what
he conveyed, but I am not quite clear. I
imagine that is so.
Viscount BANGOR: That is certainly
what I meant to convey. A lunatic will
not be sent to a district asylum without
the concurrence of the authorities of the
district asylum, and the concurrence of the
resident medical superintendent.

Widows', Orphans' and Old Age
Contributory Pensions Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, "That this Bill be now read a
Second time."
In 1925 when my right hon. Friend,
the Minister of Labour, whom we are
very glad to see here to-day, moved, in
another place, the Second Reading of
the Contributory Pensions Act of that
year he remarked that it was unlikely
that that Measure would be the last word
concerned.
When hon.
in so far as legislation relating to
pensions was
Members consider the twenty-six
Clause Bill which is now in their hands
they will, I hope, give the honour to my
right hon. Friend that is not always considered due to a prophet.
The main purpose of this Bill is to extend and amend the provisions of the
Act of 1925. That Act was a complicated Measure of extensive scope. It
provided pensions for certain classes of
widows with additional allowances for
their children, and old age pensions to
insured persons and their wives between
the ages of 65 and 70. These pensions
were continued after the age of 70 without restriction as to means. The total
number of persons in Northern Ireland
who have already benefited from the provisions of the Act of 1925 is in the
neighbourhood of 46,000.
It was inevitable in such a far-reaching
Measure that every contingency should
not have been foreseen when it was
drafted. Not a small part of the provisions of the Bill now in hon. Members'
hands is designed to set right certain
anomalies and inequalities which have
been brought to light during the four
years that the original scheme has been
in operation.
I desire, in the first place, to direct
attention of hon. Members to Clause 1
and Clause 9 of the Bill. These two
Clauses extend the provision made by the
original Measure for widows whose husbands, having died or having reached the
age of 70 before January, 1926, had
never an opportunity of contributing
to the pensions scheme. For convenience,
I shall allude to these widows as "noncontributory" widows. Under the scheme
of 1925, widows in this class might become entitled to pensions if they had
children alive under 14 years of age. The
pension, which, of course, was related to
insurance qualifications of the dead husband, continued until six months after
the youngest child's fourteenth birthday.
It then ceased; although any additional
allowance to which the widow might have
been entitled for dependent children continued until the child's sixteenth birthday
if the child remained at school.
Clause 1 of the Bill proposes to extend
the provision for "non-contributory"
widows so as to bring within the pensionable classes widows of 55 years of and
age
upwards, subject to proof that their
husbands within three years of death
were engaged in employments which
would have been insurable if the Contributory Pensions Act had been in force.
Hon. Members will find the conditions set
out in detail in paragraphs (a) (b) and
(c) of Clause 1. Furthermore, Clause 10
proposes to prolong the duration of a
pension of a "non-contributory" widow,
who is under 55 years of age, until the
31st July immediately following the
youngest child's sixteenth birthday if the
child remains at school so long.
Clause 2 of the Bill deals with a class
of elderly women whose omission from the
scope of the original Measure has led to
a considerable amount of criticism. If a
man is entitled to a pension under the
original scheme between the ages of 65 and
70 he confers a similar title on his wife
when she becomes 65. If, however, the
husband, owing to the accident of age,
was not entitled to a pension under the
contributory pensions scheme until he
reached 70, he could not confer the title
to old age pension on his wife until she
also reached that age. This clause proposes to remove whatever inequalities may
exist by conferring a title to old-age
Pensions Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont |
pension at 65 years of age on the wife of
any insured man who is entitled to an old
age pension under the scheme.
Clause 3 of the Bill will enable
pensioners to continue to draw their
pensions and insured persons to qualify
for pensions by continuing their insurance as voluntary contributors while
in any part of the British Empire. It
has been thought desirable that insured
people who emigrate should be given the
opportunity of carrying with them what
hon. Members must agree is a very valuable right.
The remaining provisions of the Bill
deal with minor anomalies and inequalities under the Act of 1925, and I do not
propose to refer to them in detail. Hon.
Members will find them explained in the
memorandum which has been published
with the Bill.
The total number of persons who will
benefit under this Measure is, it is estimated, about 13,000, the great majority
of whom belong to the class of "noncontributory" widows covered by the provisions of Clause 1. The cost of providing
pensions for these new beneficiaries will
be borne entirely by the Exchequer and
no addition is contemplated to the rates
of contributions from employers and
workers fixed by the Act of 1925. Opportunity has, however, been taken to rectify
in certain directions the finances of the
original scheme which, in the light of four
years' experience, have been found to require revision.
The Northern Ireland contributory
pensions scheme of 1925 was identical
with the Measure introduced by the
Government in office at Westminster at
that time. This Measure builds on that
structure and follows in every essential
particular the amendments and extensions of the original scheme which have
recently been enacted by the Imperial
Parliament. I beg to move.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I rise to give my
humble and very sincere and unreserved
support to this Bill. It is a good Bill.
This is the extension of a good principle.
We have from old an injunction to protect the widow and defend the children of
the poor. This Bill serves that good purpose. Some people may depreciate the
merits of these pensions Acts, but there
are others who remember what they have
done for countless multitudes of poor
homes in keeping the home fires burning.
an
To people whose lot it is to earn their
daily bread by their daily toil, and
especially those people who when the
struggle of life is over and their energies
are exhausted are "done," to use
Ulster phrase-we all know in Northern
Ireland what we mean when we speak of
a person as done "we know how valuable these Acts have been in keeping the
little homes together and saving the
humble occupants from cold and cheerless
I have the very
years of poverty.
greatest pleasure in supporting this Bill
wholeheartedly.
""
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I am
very pleased that the Government have
seen fit to introduce this Bill and continue
on parallel lines with the Imperial Parliament. No doubt there are many anomalies in the 1925 Act which required rectification. It was
a real hardship that
the widow of a man who had attained the
age of 70 before the 1925 Act came into
operation should be deprived of the
pension at 65 years of age. There is also
a great concession to the widow of 55, who
will now be able to draw the pension until
she reaches the age of 70. Certainly I am
very pleased to see this Bill introduced. A
short time ago we had in Belfast one of
the Under Secretaries of the Imperial
Government. When speaking to me, he
asked if the Northern Government would
introduce Measures such as had been
introduced in the Imperial Parliament,
with special reference to pensions and unemployment insurance. Off my own bat.
I said I was sure thev would, and I must
say I am very glad that our Government
have continued on parallel lines with
Great Britain, seeing that I had committed myself to such a good opinion of
the Government.
There is one thing that I feel very
much disturbed about. I noticed in the
Press this morning that there is some reference to the fact that the Minister has
made some alteration in this Bill as compared with that of the Imperial Parliament, and is shirking some of his duties
and placing the responsibility on some of
his officials. If that is the case I am very
sorry; that is not the Mr. Andrews I
have known. I have always thought he
was very willing to bear all his responsibilities without throwing them on to the
shoulders of other people. I shall be
very glad to have an assurance on that
point. It would look as if we were
making some departure which might not
Pensions Bill-
Second Reading
13 DECEMBER 1929
be to the advantage of the workers in
this part of the Kingdom. I have great
pleasure in supporting the Second Reading of this Bill.
Mr. BARCLAY: I have great pleasure in supporting this Bill which, I
think, will be of great benefit to a number
of widows whose husbands died previous
to January, 1926. I know of a number
of men who were insured persons and who
were in insured trades from 1912, when,
I think, the Act came into force, until
1925. Some of them died in July, some
in August, others in September and so
on.
They had no children of school age,
and the result was that their widows had
to go without the pension, while other
women who had children benefited by the
Act. I am very pleased, therefore, to
know that those widows will benefit under
this Bill.
I have not had time to read this Bill,
as I only got it to-day, and do not know
everything that is in it. But from what
I gather from the newspaper, I am afraid
there is onę small section of the community left outside the scope of the
Measure. It is a section that this Government and the Imperial Government,
besides a great many other people, seem
to lose sight of. I refer to aged unmarried women, especially those who are
not in insured trades. We have a
number of them in the city of Belfast.
They left their situations years ago, when
perhaps a mother died, to keep house for
the father. They stopped with the
father until his death, and after his
death perhaps they stopped with
bachelor brother until he died. There
are some people like that in Belfast today, and I believe they are very hard up.
The bread-winner has gone, and no provision has been made for them.
a
On the other hand, the childless widow
under 30 years of age is entitled to a
pension of 10s a week. I think there is
a little bit of injustice there. I think
that in the case of childless widows there
should be a specified age at which they
should receive the pension, say 35 or 40.
That would enable the Minister to have
some money in hand to pension those uninsured ladies who are forgotten by most
of us.
I think they might say with Job
that no man cares for their souls. I do
not know what would be the best thing
to do, whether to make it compulsory for
the father or brother to insure the
daughter or sister, but there should be
something done for those people, who
cannot receive any help until they are
70 years of age, so far as I understand
the Bill. Perhaps I am wrong.
I should like to know if this Bill covers
another class of case that I have in mind.
I knew a foreman in the shipyard of
Workman, Clark & Company. He was
insured from 1912 until 1918 or 1919,
when his wages were advanced, and as his
income was over £250 he was no longer
insurable. When the opportunity presented itself in 1926 of his becoming a
voluntary contributor, he availed himself
of it, and he was a voluntary contributor
from 1926. In August, 1927, he and his
wife went down to Bangor, and I am
sorry to say that he dropped dead while
he was walking along the sand on the seashore. His widow applied for the widow's
pension, but owing to the break in his insurance he had not the necessary 104
stamps to his credit, and she was disqualified. I should like to know if this
Bill will assist that woman in any way.
I have great pleasure in supporting the
Second Reading.
Mr. DORMAN: I rise to give this
Measure my whole-hearted support, and
again to congratulate the Government
upon introducing Socialist legislation.
This is an indication of the recognition
by the State of its responsibility for the
life and well-being of the individual
citizen. It is an indication that the
modern State is waking to a sense of its
responsibility, and is recognising that the
most valuable thing with which it has to
deal is human life.
Undoubtedly this scheme of old-age
pensions is the result of a prolonged
struggle. It is 150 years since the idea.
of old-age pensions was first suggested,
and at that time it was suggested on a
more humanitarian basis and on a more
just basis than any of the modern schemes
that the State has introduced.
It was
then proposed that a man who had no
means of livelihood but the sale of his
power of labour from day to day should
be provided with half subsistence by the
Society to which he had contributed
during all his years of labour. That
was to operate from the age of 50, and
when he became 60 the State was to
step in and give him complete subsistence for the last decade of the
average period of human existence.
Pensions Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Dorman]
At that stage a budget was drawn up
for the purpose of showing how the State
could, without injustice, provide the
means of giving these benefits at the ages
of 50 and 60. But the attention of the
public was diverted from these social ideas
by designating the promoters atheists.
Against those who proposed reforms in
advance of their day and generation wild
charges were brought of their being
atheists, advocates of free love, and so on,
with the object of withdrawing public attention from their schemes. I am pleased
to know that the State has now recognised
its duty, and I look forward to the time
when a more generous pension will be
granted to those who have given their life
of labour to the community.
The MINISTER OF LABOUR (Mr.
Andrews) I am very gratified at the
sympathetic reception which this Measure
has received at the hands of the hon.
Senators who have spoken. I did not intend to say even one word on this occasion
which could be construed to have any
party savour about it. But as the hon.
Senator who has just sat down (Mr.
Dorman) seemed to wish to gain some
credit for the Socialist party in Great
Britain for this Measure, which I happen
to think he is not quite entitled to claim,
I would just like to say this, that he is
no doubt aware of the fact that the principal Act of 1925 was introduced by a
Unionist Government. (Hon. Members:
Hear, hear).
That great beneficent Measure, as we
have heard to-day, benefited no less than
46,000 suffering people in Northern Ireland. This Measure I also consider a
great and beneficent Measure.
In my
opinion it contains no Socialist principle
whatever, nor does it establish any new
principle that was not contained in the
Act of 1925. It simply brings in another
group of non-contributory pensioners,
and it also remedies some of those inequalities which, in practice, the Act of
1925 was found to contain. This, in my
opinion, is another step in the right
direction, and will bring much comfort
into a great many homes in Northern
Ireland.
I feel that I ought now to endeavour
to answer a few questions that I have
been asked. The hon. Senator (Mr.
Joseph Cunningham) has referred to some
notices in the newspapers this morning
to the effect that I was shirking my re-
|sponsibilities in connection with this
Measure. I have not the least hesitation
in saying that there is not one word of
truth in those statements. All the responsibilities which the British Measure
placed upon the Imperial Minister of
Health are placed upon me by this
Measure. Those responsibilities I shall
not hesitate to exercise to the full.
do, if I may say so, deprecate these
statements of one gentleman in another
House, which are very likely to unsettle
public opinion and to make the community generally think that things are
different from what they are.
I
I would like to refer to the remarks
of Senator Barclay about the old lady
of whom he spoke not being able as yet
to get the old age pension. Of course,
the position is that her husband had not
of recent years been. a contributor to
the fund. As far as this Section of the
Measure is concerned it is upon a strictly
contributory basis. To say that we ought
to take something from the childless
widow in order to give to old ladies
similarly situated to the one which my
hon. Friend has described could not, I
think, be justified, and for this reason.
The husband of the childless widow was
a contributor for probably all his industrial career in order that his wife
should get a pension when he died, and
allowances for her children, if she had
any. The fact that there were no children deprived the widow of the allowances
which she would otherwise have had, and
to take from her the pension which her
husband's contributions entitled her to
would not, in my opinion, be just, nor
would it, I am sure meet with the general
approval of this House.
There is nothing further I feel I need
add except to repeat how gratified I am
to know that this Measure meets with
the approval of this House and also to
feel that it will be of tremendous advantage to those of our people with whom,
no matter in what part of the House we
sit, we have the greatest possible sympathy.
On Question, Motion, (That the Bill
be now read a Second Time) agreed to.
Moved, "That this House do now resolve itself into Committee on the said
Bill." (Viscount Charlemont).
On Question, Motion agreed to,
In Committee accordingly,

Adjournment.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: In moving
"That this House do now adjourn until
Monday," I should like to say something
about the business for next week. The
House will have to sit on Monday to
pass the remaining stages of the Criminal
Lunatics Bill. If the House agreed to
the suspension of the Standing Order we
should be able to pass the rest of the outstanding legislation, but if hon. Members
are adverse to that course there is no
reason why we should not also sit on
Tuesday. In the event of our making any
Amendments to the Drainage Bill or
other Bills it would give another place
more time to consider them if we disposed
of them on Monday, but I am not urging
that that course should be adopted, except to say that we must finish the
Criminal Lunatics Bill on Monday.
Whether we sit on Tuesday or not is a
matter for the House to decide.
Mr. LESLIE: I hope it is not intended
to complete both the Drainage Bill and
the Motor Bill on Monday.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Not if the
House objects.
Mr. LESLIE: I do not think we should
go as fast as that.
Mr. DORMAN: I wish to draw attention to a matter of importance to every
Member of this House. Frequently, in
fact constantly, the Order Paper does not
Adjournment
Adjournment
THE SENATE
[Mr. Dorman]
reach us until after that day's business
has been transacted. I hold in my hand
the Order Paper for yesterday, and the
postmark is 2-30 p.m. yesterday. That
obviously could not have reached any
Member in time to let him know what the
businesss coming before the House was.
I draw the attention of the Leader
of the House to this in the hope
that the convenience of Members in this
respect will be considered in the future,
and that some steps will be taken to enable them to receive the Order Paper
before the business commences.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I will see
what I can do in the matter. It is a
difficult question, because the fact is that
the printers do not work at night and
when the Senate sits on continuous days,
as it is inclined to do towards the end of
a Session, it is difficult to send out the
Order Paper in time, but I will see if any
amelioration can be effected.
On Question, Motion agreed to.

accordingly

USE OF VITA GLASS IN SCHOOLS.
Major DOBBS: I beg to ask the Leader
of the House whether his attention has
been drawn to the health-giving qualities
of Vita glass; and if so, will he approach
the various Regional Committees, so that
Vita glass may be adopted as the standard
glazing for the classrooms of all
schools?
new
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The use of
Vita glass in schools was first considered
by my Ministry some time ago in connection with a proposal to introduce it into a
school then being erected in Co. Londonderry. As the Ministry had very little
information in regard to the superior advantages of this type of glass over ordinary
glass the English Board of Education was
consulted, from which it was learnt that
this type of glass was at that time being
tried in England in certain schools but
that the Board was not prepared to
sanction its wholesale introduction until
more decided evidence of its advantages
was available.
Vita glass has since been installed experimentally in a few new schools in
Northern Ireland, but it is too early to
give any very definite opinion as to its
usefulness, especially in districts where the
atmosphere is pure. In these circumstances, while my Ministry is desirous that
its usefulness should be fully tested, it is
not at present in a position to advocate its
general adoption. The experiments in
Northern Ireland will, however, be carefully watched.
I should like to add that we
are at
present asking the Board of Education in
England if they have any further observations on the use of Vita glass.
RESOLUTION BY CHAPTER OF
DOWN AND CONNOR.
Mr. CAMPBELL: In putting the
question standing in my name may I just
say one earnest word at this season to the
noble Lord, the Leader of the House, and
that is to ask him to give due weight to the
resolution referred to in the question which
speaks for 35 per cent. of the population.
Appropriation Bill-
Drainage Bill
THE SENATE
[Mr. Campbell]
If the 65 per cent. are satisfied in regard
to education I ask him to be just to the
35 per cent. and their conscientious convictions.
I beg to ask the Leader of the House
whether his attention has been drawn to a
resolution passed on the 11th inst., in
Belfast, by the Chapter of Down and
Connor, under the presidency of the
Catholic Bishop, affirming that Catholics
are denied their just share of educational
grants except on conditions which they
cannot in conscience accept, and that
Catholics are determined to adhere to the
managerial system as a system of popular
control in the best sense of the phrase, the
Manager acting as their representative.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I have not
yet seen the Resolution referred to in the
Question. I would like, however, to refer
the hon. and learned Member to my reply
to the debate on the Motion in this House
on 28th November last, when I dealt with
this matter in some detail.
NUMBER OF UNEMPLOYED IN
NORTHERN IRELAND.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to ask the
Leader of the House how many persons
are at present unemployed in Northern
Ireland; also how many persons are engaged in Government schemes for the
relief of unemployment?
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am informed that the number of men, women
and young persons registering for employment at the Employment Exchanges
is 37,625. This figure includes in
addition to those who are wholly unemployed those who are on short time or
who are only casually unemployed.
1,621
are
men engaged
on

schemes for the relief of unemployment
apart from the employment indirectly
afforded.

Drainage Bill.

Viscount BANGOR: Before moving

Drainage Bill
Third Reading.
16 DECEMBER 1929
like to say that I am sure we are
all glad to have present the Minister of Finance this afternoon, who
will deal with the questions which
have been raised by hon. Members on the
Bill, and will also deal with the Amendments of hon. Senator Long. I beg to move
that the Bill be now read the Third
time.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
The
Mr. LONG: I beg to move in Clause
20, page 19, line 31 to leave out from
"pounds" ("two thousand pounds, and")
to the second "the" in line 32 ("land, the
scheme"). In moving this Amendment
I should like to say that the great
volume of opinion in Northern Ireland of
those who should know and who have had
experience in trying to operate previous
schemes, is that a measure of compulsion
is desirable. I have had a communication from quite a number of people in
Northern Ireland and they agree.
county surveyor of Londonderry writes:
"I believe the compulsory acquisition of
land would be desirable in fact probably
imperative in some cases in connection
with the improvement of rivers and
watercourses where such work is carried
out as a minor scheme. No doubt you
will remember Mr. Lane's remarks in
his recent report where he mentioned
that one crank who owns a square yard
of land could block the entire scheme. I
hope to get the schemes for the restoration of the River Roe embankments
ready for despatch to the Ministry in the
course of the next week or so, but I cannot, of course, ask for any tenders until
the Ministry accord sanction to the
scheme."
The latter part of this letter refers to
the enbankments washed away during the
recent abnormal flooding of the River Roe.
The estimate for repairing these breaches
amounts to £5,600, and they occur in
about 40 places on 35 farms. The County
Council were desirous of having these repairs effected at the earliest possible
moment to enable the occupiers of the
flooded lands to have crops put in the ensuing Spring. We found that under the
major scheme about seven months would
be required to have this done, and all our
ingenuity was of no avail. After several
consultations the Home Ministry were
most helpful and sympathetic in the
matter. A difficulty cropped up when one
of these persons on whose land a breach
occurred absolutely refused to do any
The scheme was that the County
thing.
Council should pay 40 per cent. of the
cost, the Government 40 per cent., and the
occupier 20 per cent. These terms, we
thought, were at least generous, but we
have one individual I will call Mr. A. who
positively refuses to do anything to repair
the breach on his land, or to allow anyone
else to enter. About seven miles of this.
river is tidal, and hon. Members can
imagine the condition of affairs when at
high tide or at the least rise in the river
the lands adjoining these breaches are submerged to a very considerable depth. The
land is not healthy to reside on, and some
of the people had to abandon their houses.
In that case we are held up by this oneman.
I feel that this state of affairs will continue and in my view-and I claim to have
some experience of trying to work the previous scheme-the Bill now before the
House will be practically waste paper, or
at least it will be of very little service
without compulsory powers. We may not
require to exercise the power of compulsion, in fact, we rarely would, but it is
found in practice that it is desirable to
have it. I do not see why we should pass
a measure that is not the best we can produce. My only desire is to be helpful to
the Government. I do not blame the
Government. They are dependent upon
the advice of their officials, and the
officials, I expect, know as much about this
matter as I do about the Ancient Order
of Hibernians. I really think we should
have the best Bill we can possibly produce,
and, therefore, I beg to move the Amendment.
The MINISTER OF FINANCE (Mr.
Pollock): I am sorry that on behalf
of the Government, I cannot agree to the
elimination of these words. The example
to which the hon. Member has referred is
really a major scheme of a very important
character which these lines have no bearing upon whatever. The object of this
part of the Bill is to amend the Act of
1925 so as to give the county councils
power over small minor matters without
reference to the Ministry of Home Affairs,
and if any difficulty arises they can go
direct to the necessary legal tribunal. If
the change that has been suggested is made
it will really throw this part of the
Measure back to the Act of 1925, and it
would then be necessary to go through that
rather tortuous proceeding of going to the
Ministry of Home Affairs.
Drainage Bill-
Third Reading.
THE SENATE
[Mr. Pollock]
Therefore, I hope the hon. Member will
realise that the intention of this part of
the Bill is to give the county councils
power, in individual cases where a farmer
allows his water course to be clogged with
vegetable matter, or weeds, or other vegetation, to compel him to clear away such
obstructions. It is not intended that
county councils should have the power of
compulsory purchase or to enable them to
go in arbitrarily and say to the farmer,
"We must acquire something." That is
not the intention of the Bill, and, therefore, I would ask the hon. Member to
carefully consider that aspect of the matter and not to press his Amendment,
because it is something to which we attach
some importance.

22, page 22, line 18, after the word

to insert the words "and deepening.
am of the opinion that the insertion of
these two words would be very useful in
working this scheme. I certainly dissent
from the view expressed in this House on

firm clay or rock. I think this is a common-sense Amendment that the House

Mr. POLLOCK: I again regret that
the Government do not look with favour
upon this Amendment. The Clause says:
All such works as are necessary for
causing the watercourse to be scoured
out and cleansed in a proper manner,
and such works may include the widening of the watercourse
That word "deepening" is deliberately

Sinclair, Col. Thomas
The whole object of this Clause, as I said
before, is to empower the county councils,
taking a general survey of the land, to
see that the farmers who occupy these
iands do not injure their neighbours by
permitting the unhealthy and clogged
condition of their watercourses. But if
large blasting operations are to be undertaken for the purpose of deepening then
you transfer it into a major county council
scheme. Surely if a farmer is compelled
to clear and cleanse his watercourse and remove obstructions, such as they areordinary vegetable obstructions that grow
in rivers and ditches and small watercourses-it is sufficient, and that is all
that is contemplated in this Bill.
major scheme would imply what the hon.
Member had in his mind, but if it is a
minor scheme the Bill provides for that,
and it is unnecessary that power should
be given to the county councils to go to
a farmer and say, "You must begin to
blast out the length of this river at your
own expense," without any question as to
the farmer's desires or rights in the matter. That word "deepening" has a far
wider application than appears on the
surface, and I think that Senator Long
would be well advised to re-consider his
views and withdraw his Amendment.

Drainage Bill--

16 DECEMBER 1929
Mr. DICKSON: I regret that I cannot
agree with the Minister, because in my
experience of very narrow streams I find
you get boulders and rock, and no matter
what scouring is done it will be of no benefit unless you have power to remove that
rock. It is not a question of blasting
yards of rock every here and there or long
rock bottoms in a river, but if you come
on a rock it will hold up your entire
scheme unless you have power to remove
it. Scouring and widening of the river
would be of very little use.
Far more fall
is got from deepening than from widening,
and unless you have power to remove an
obstruction where the water is held up by
a rock scouring is really valueless except
in the case of a country drain.
Mr. ARMSTRONG: I am sorry I am
unable to agree with the Government in
this matter, because I have had some practical experience of cleaning out these small
water-courses before this Bill came forward. I found on several occasions, as
my hon. Friend has said, projections of
rock in the bottom of the water-course not
extending to any great length, but at the
same time causing serious obstructions.
In fact, it is useless to attempt to clean
them unless the obstruction is removed.
Under those circumstances I feel that
deepening is absolutely necessary.
Mr. LESLIE: The impression left on
my mind by this debate is that the point
Armstrong, Mr. H. B.

now raised should have been dealt with

any alteration in the Bill. If the point
had been dealt with when raised in Committee we would have had more time to
consider it. I am inclined to think, as
far as I can form an opinion at such short

at first I thought there was. I was very
much disposed to support it.
The hon. Senator, the Chairman of the
Londonderry County Council, seemed to
convey that the obstruction he speaks
about is a rock projecting above the normal bottom surface of the stream. That
can be removed without anything called
deepening.
I think on the whole, in view
of what the Minister has just adumbrated,
it would be better that this Amendment
should not be passed. I think I am
right about these rocks projecting upwards
in saying that they can be dealt with
under the powers we already have.
Question put, "That those words be
there inserted."

One question was: When the Ministry
take over the powers of the Lough Neagh
Drainage Board and the Lower Bann
Navigation Board will they impose the

I speak somewhat feelingly about this

quite impossible for navigation to take

In regard to the first question that he

Colonel PAKENHAM: That was the
question of the drainage rate. At the
present moment the people in that drainage area pay a drainage rate to the Lough
Neagh Drainage Trustees. The Ministry
are going to take over the powers of the
bodies there, and I ask will that rate be
struck immediately the Ministry take
over or will it go into abeyance until the
scheme has come to fruition?
Mr. POLLOCK: As a matter of fact,
remission has already been made since 1st
April last of part of the rates struck in
connection with that work. It is contemplated that when works are undertaken
over and above what was expected to accrue from the imposition of the drainage
rates, it would be very unfair that those
people should be asked to pay a rate for
which they get no value. When the
scheme is completed the Government will
take over the entire management and
maintenance of the central river, but
country schemes and field drainage schemes
and such like will then be transferred to
the county councils and those bodies left
with complete control over the entire
catchment area of the lands that will be
affected, but so far as the main outlet is
concerned the Government have undertaken entire financial responsibility for
that work.
Mr. LESLIE: Now that we are back
on the main question, I may perhaps refer
to a point I raised when the Bill was in
Committee, that was, the question of the
fears entertained in and about the town of
Coleraine as to whether, particularly in
times of high tide, the low-lying lands
outside the town were likely to be
flooded or even submerged. If the right
hon. Gentleman could give us some information on that subject I am sure it will
appeal particularly to those in that
neighbourhood.
Mr. POLLOCK: I am sorry to say I
have not got sufficient engineering knowledge to answer with any definiteness the
question which my hon. Friend has submitted, but I can say that I am perfectly
satisfied that every possibility in regard
to flooding over the entire length of the
River Bann from its entrance at Lough
Neagh to its outlet at the sea has been
carefully considered by engineers who occupied something like two years in surveying it from its beginning to its ending.
It is quite inconceivable that a scheme of
such magnitude should be undertaken unless the whole of the difficulty was intended
to be removed. If a part of the line was
left in the condition my hon. Friend suggested the scheme would be very imperfect.
That is all I am prepared to say in answer
to the hon. Gentleman.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
And a Message was sent to the House
of Commons to acquaint them that the
Senate have agreed to the said Bill without
any Amendment.

CLAUSE 1 (Amendments as to penalties for speed offences), Ordered to
