CLAUSE 2 (Provisions as to indictable offences in connection with driving
of motor cars).
any
(1) On the trial of indictment
for manslaughter or
or for doing or
causing bodily harm to any person
with the
whatsoever in connection
driving of a motor car on a public
highway, the jury may acquit the
defendant of manslaughter or of doing
or causing bodily harm and find him
guilty of driving recklessly or wantonly
or at a speed or in a manner which is
dangerous to the public, having regard
to all the circumstances of the case.
(2) Any person who is convicted
under this section shall be liable, at
the discretion of the court, to be
imprisoned for any term not exceeding
six months, or to pay such fine as the
court shall award in addition to or in
lieu of any such other punishment as
aforesaid.
Mr. LESLIE : I beg to move in page
2, line 13, leave out from "manslaughter" to the end of line 26, and
insert in connection with the driving
of a motor car on a public highway, it
shall be lawful, notwithstanding that the
circumstances were such that but for this
Act the offence would have amounted to
manslaughter, for the jury to acquit the
defendant of manslaughter and convict
him of a misdemeanour, to wit, punishable homicide."
(2) "Where it is alleged that a person
has driven a motor car on a public highway recklessly or wantonly, or at a speed
or in a manner which was dangerous to
the public, having regard to all the
circumstances of the case, and has caused
thereby the death of any other person,
such first-mentioned person may, notwithstanding that the circumstances may
have been such that but for this Act the
offence would have amounted to manslaughter, be charged on indictment with
a misdemeanour, to wit, punishable
homicide.
(3) "In an indictment for punishable
homicide it shall be set out that the person charged 'caused the death' of the
person whose death is the subject of the
charge.
(4) "An indictment for punishable
homicide shall not be triable at any
court of quarter sessions.
(5) "Any person who is convicted of
punishable homicide shall be liable at the
discretion of the court to be imprisoned
for any term not exceeding six months,
or to pay such fine not exceeding two
hundred pounds, as the court shall award,
in addition to or
in lieu of such
imprisonment as aforesaid."
I do not desire to repeat, if I can
avoid it, anything that I said with regard
to the Clause now before us
on the
Second reading, but I do want in
the presence of the right hon. Gentleman
the Minister of Home Affairs, whom we
are glad to see this afternoon, to pay
tribute once again to him for the introduction of the Clause to deal with this
matter, and for the trouble which I know
he has bestowed upon it. The matter to
which the Clause is directly confined and
relevant is a very serious matter、 indeed.
It is a question of the prevention of
motor fatalities by means of punishing
those responsible for them if they are to
blame. That question is one on which
the public mind is very seriously disquieted and I think it is necessary to
deal with it. I am glad that the right
hon. Gentleman has endeavoured to deal
with it.
Perhaps the principal reason why it is
necessary to deal with it is the fact that
if a motor fatality occurs and if it is
thought that a motorist is to blame steps
are taken to put him on his trial for manslaughter. In these cases it is found
that juries, speaking generally, will not
convict. That being so one has to inquire why? I suggest the main reason
is that jurors are apt to be frightened by
the words in the charge of manslaughter.
They see the expression, "feloniously did
kill and slay." whereas their opposite
numbers, so to speak, in England in
similar circumstances are only presented
with the suggestion that the defendant
"unlawfully killed" the unfortunate person. Perhaps it is even more than that.
I do not know how far juries really
Road Traffic Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
do pay attention to the actual wording
of the indictment. I am not sure that
they hear it perfectly. I do think that
they may fail to recognise the elasticity.
of the crime of manslaughter, and that
they are prone to think of manslaughter as something which is only just
bit short of
sort of crime in which a
a
little
sort
a
a
murder,
man charged
will often plead guilty if the plea
is accepted, and also
of
crime in which he may not be convicted.
although his offence was an extremely
bad one. I think that jurors, as I say,
are inclined to be in doubt and to feel
that if they convict of manslaughter they
are putting a great slur on the offender,
and that they are perhaps leaving a little
too much to the judge.
I do not myself agree with these views.
I am quite content with the law of manslaughter as it stands. and I am certainly.
content to leave things to the judges as
they are. I think also that juries are
sometimes misled
misled by the expression
"criminal negligence" which is so often
thrown at them. As we know it is a
difficult expression. A modern writer
says that the difference between tortious
negligence the negligence which can
properly form the subject of damages in
a civil court-and the criminal negligence
to support a charge of manslaughter is a
difference which cannot be expressed in
abstract terms. The jury does not perhaps always know that. I think what
is wanted in the Bill is a halfway house between the ordinary
and criminal negligence. Particularly would the juror, I think, prefer
a half-way house between the tortious
negligence and the felonious killing by
manslaughter. I think he would like
that, and he has not got it up to the
present.
We must recognise that jurors want to
do right, and they generally try to do
right and more often than not succeed in
doing it. I think they may do their work
rather with the fear of God's laws as they
understand them before their eyes than
with any fear of the man made law to
which, properly, they attach less importance. I think we should not do by
enactment anything which has the appearance of being an attempt, so to speak,
to bluff the jury into, while acquitting a
man of the more serious charge of manslaughter, yet convicting him of the minor
offence. In the enactment it is proposed
to provide a more serious penalty than
that offence would have incurred if the
offence had been originally prosecuted at
petty sessions, and if in fact the result of
that alleged or actual offence had not been
to kill the man.
I know there are several precedents for
conviction on a lesser charge and for
higher punishment at the assizes than under summary jurisdiction. That is so particularly perhaps where by Statute the
case is one which can be sent forward to
the assizes for that purpose. I must say
after looking up the matter as well as I can
that none of these are strictly in point in
the present case. They do not deal
with cases where the lesser charge if established would necessarily involve the
greater. I think that is the case here.
If dangerous driving does result in killing
a man it is no use to my mind convicting
him of dangerous driving. What he has
done is manslaughter. I think that is
what the lesser penalty put on dangerous
driving is meant to do. It is meant to
punish a man because he killed another
I said just now that there were
precedents in favour of convictions on
lesser offences.
man.
The best example that occurs to me is
the old one of finding guilty of concealment of birth on the murder
charge, and I refer to the wording
of the section in order to see how care
fully the whole position is set out. I submit the conditions proper to a conviction
for the lesser offence where the greater is
charged can only be, not that the jury
convict on the lesser offence while thinking
the man guilty of the greater, but that
they think it is the lesser offence he should
be convicted of, even although he has
been charged with the greater. In this case
there cannot or should not be any doubt
on the question as to whether the man
was killed or not. If he was not killed by
criminal negligence that seems to be the
end of it. There should be an acquittal.
It seems to me that the mistake made in
the Clause as it is before us is that in
trying to right one wrong, we are, in
fact, proposing to perpetrate another.
What is proposed seems to be a differentiation of the punishment a more severe
punishment for the offence of dangerous.
driving than the ordinary law already provides. As I said we are doing that in
order to punish a man for dangerous driving, because his dangerous driving has
killed a man.
Road Traffic Bill-
Committee
16 DECEMBER 1929
I think if the jury get hold of that
idea they will not convict on the one
charge any more than on the other. That
was said on the Second Reading by the
hon. and learned Senator (Mr. Campbell).
I do not know that the proposed alteration
in the law will make the task of the
Grand Juries any easier, and I do not
want to express any opinion as to what
line the judge would take if there was a
conviction when the trial came on.
I think we ought not to accept the
proposal in the Bill, but that being so it is
no
use making any more destructive
criticism of the kind. I think we ought
to help the jurors in this matter, and the
only question is how are we to do it? The
method I propose is distinctly bold-it is
to abandon any attempt whatever to put
new wine into old bottles. I suggest that
we should take the bold, straightforward
course, that we should recognise that there
are new conditions, and free ourselves from
that old Section 7 of the Act to which my
hon. Friend referred the other day. Let
us cut right away from that and modify
the law so as to make it possible to punish
a man for killing another man.
There are two ways in which we can do
it. One is to amend the first section
of our Motor Vehicles Act dealing with
dangerous driving by providing that dangerous driving be dealt with in the same
manner as offences under the Children
Act which has been referred to. Leave
it that the magistrates, can either inflict
what seems a suitable penalty or, if they
think it too serious a matter, send it forward for trial. That course has been suggested, and I understand it has not found
favour. I am not sure that any course
suggested would find universal favour.
do not think it is possible in this case to do
anything that would please everybody.
There seems to be another course open,
and that is to follow the principle in the
Infanticide Act, 1922, the circumstances
applying to which are to some extent
similar. The provisions of the Infanticide.
Act apply to women in a certain mental
state, perhaps a mental state of recklessness. The Bill was brought in because
of the difficulty of securing convictions for
murder in circumstances to which that Act
applied partly for that and partly from
reasons of humanity. At all events the
Bill has worked quite satisfactorily.
Following that precedent it seems to me
that we might very well endeavour to
create some new offence or rather to find
some new name for the offence of killing.
It is not easy to find a new name, a word
that has not been used before. The expression "excusable homicide" is well
known, but "punishable homicide," as far
as I know, is not used in any of our Acts at
all. I think if we used that we should
be supplying that half-way house of which
I have spoken-the half-way house befelonious
civil negligence and
negligence. In that half-way house I think
many jurors would find the accommodation they desire.
tween
I submit this is a logical and strictly
fair course to take. I will not expatiate
at the moment on the details of the proposal. They are on the paper, but I
should like to say now that I myself have
not any insuperable objection to raising
the term of imprisonment suggested.

am not at all sure that I do not think that
six months is too low a limit to place upon
the discretion of the judge. Nor have I
any very definite objection to leaving the
court unfettered as regards the fine, and I
certainly have no objection at all to including a sub-section dealing with
causing bodily harm if it is thought really
desirable to do so. It may be and it is
not unlikely that it will be-that the Committee will think that the remedy I am
proposing is, perhaps, rather worse than
the disease exhibited in the Bill, a disease
which, I may say, is in itself the result of
an attempt to administer an anti-toxin to
a disease which it proposes to remedy.
It seemed right to me that this matter
should be discussed as much as possible
before we take refuge in a course such as
that in the Bill which introduces a new
punishment which, although it can be
perfectly justified in some ways, cannot, I
think, be justified in the manner in which
it is put in the Bill. I beg to move.
The MINISTER OF HOME AFFAIRS
(Sir Dawson Bates): May I first of all
express my thanks to this House for their
courtesy in allowing me to come here this
afternoon? May I say at the outset that
if I thought for one moment that the life
of one individual was going to be saved by
accepting the Amendment which has been
moved by the hon. Senator (Mr. Leslie)
I would not have the slightest hesitation in
accepting it. But I am sorry to say that
I think it would have exactly the opposite
effect. Let us come right down to the
present condition of affairs. Let us realise
that within the past three years fifty indictable cases involving deaths arising out
of motor accidents were sent forward by
the Attorney-General to the magistrates,
Road Traffic Bill-
Committee.
THE SENATE
[Sir Dawson Bates]
and returned by the magistrates for trial
at Assizes. Hon. Members, especially my
hon. and learned Friends opposite, will
appreciate that before a case is dealt with
in that way by the Attorney-General he
goes into it very carefully to see if there is
a clear prima facie case. The magistrates
do the same. It then comes before the
Grand Jury, and under the direction of
the learned judge the Grand Jury have to
say whether or not there is a prima facie
case. Those are the three different stages.
Out of those fifty cases I have
mentioned, cases in which those three
stages were taken in the course of three
years only six cases resulted in convictions. That may, of course, have been
due to the eloquence of learned Gentlemen such as I see opposite to me, but,
whatever the cause may be, there is
something wrong. Therefore, I would
like the House to realise that the present
state of affairs cannot be allowed to continue, and that we have to seek for a new
remedy. The remedy which my hon.
and learned Friend (Mr. Leslie)
suggests as I hope to show him-is
entirely wrong and would be entirely reactionary.
What do we propose to do? At the
present time a man is returned for trial
on a charge of manslaughter through killing a human being. Whether, as my
hon. Friend says, the small number of
convictions is due to the fact that juries
are frightened to convict I do not know,
but I cannot see any reason why a man
who has enough money to be able to drive
a motor car and who kills an individual
should be treated in a different way to,
say, an engine driver who runs past a signal and causes loss of life. In most of such
cases juries have convicted. For my own
part I do not want to be bitter about
this thing at all-I would say that until
the motorist realises that he has a
dangerous weapon in his hands, and that
he must take proper care or otherwise he
will run the risk of spending a term in
one of his Majesty's prisons, I do not
believe we will reduce this appalling loss
of life.
To try to meet such a case we propose
to enable a jury in a manslaughter case
-and of course hon. Senators will realise
that all these cases have different degrees
-we propose to enable a jury that is
conscientiously trying to do its duty to
find a verdict of wanton or dangerous
driving. Why should they not do so?
But we also provide that if a verdict of
dangerous and wanton driving is found
by the judge and jury will have power to
impose a sentence of up to six months
imprisonment. It is suggested that
there is something wrong about this
simply because in the lower court a man
is not sent to jail for wanton or
dangerous driving unless he has been
convicted for the third time. I am
dealing, of course, with cases which iuvolve loss of life. In England they have
gone a step further than we have, because
in the lower court for a first offence of
wanton and dangerous driving an
individual can be sent to jail for a period
of three months and in addition to that
the lower court can send forward a bill
for dangerous and wanton driving under
which the offender can get a term of six
months.
wanton
My hon. Friend suggests that, because
at the present time a man can only be
fined in the lower court, he must not
under any circumstances be sent to jail
in the higher court. He suggested that
one great danger is that juries are going
to be bluffed. I cannot imagine a
Northern Ireland jury being bluffed by
anybody, and I do suggest that his
Majesty's judges will take care when
they are charging juries in these cases
that the two alternatives-either a
verdict of manslaughter or a verdict of
and dangerous driving-are
But
clearly placed before the juries.
what does my hon. Friend suggest as an
alternative? And if I thought for one
moment that it met the case I would not
have the slightest hesitation in saying so.
What he proposes is to have two counts.
One is for manslaughter, and then he
creates a new offence of punishable
homicide. But I would like to point out
to my hon. Friend that he has so framed
this Clause-and I would like my hon.
Friends opposite to take particular notice
of this that I am advised that it would
have the effect of preventing a jury from
finding a verdict of manslaughter at all.
He will see that his Amendment provides
that
"it shall be lawful, notwithstanding
that the circumstances were such that
but for this Act the offence would have
amounted to manslaughter, for the jury
to acquit the defendant of manslaughter."
Mr. LESLIE: May I interrupt the
right hon. Gentleman? Is not that what
it says in the Infanticide Act?
Sir DAWSON BATES: I am not
dealing with the Infanticide Act. That
Road Traffic Bill-
Committee.
16 DECEMBER 1929
Act can take care of itself.
I do suggest
that in the form in which my hon.
Friend has drafted his Amendment the
terms of it are mandatory. I know he
intends that they should be optional, but
my right hon. and learned Friend the
Attorney-General has referred me to the
cases of Jackson versus the Sligo County
Council, and the Bishop of Oxford's case.
These are well-known cases in which it
was held that those terms were really
mandatory, although it might appear to
the ordinary lay mind that the term "it
shall be lawful" was optional. If the
Attorney-General's view is correct, and
if those decisions are correct, the Amendment would have the extraordinary
effect that the jury would be compelled
to find a verdict on the lesser count, and
the greatest penalty for this punishable.
homicide would be six months, regardless of the circumstances of the case.
know my hon. Friend does not intend
that.
I
Then, again, in this proposed Clause
he cuts out all reference to the offence of
causing grievous bodily harm. The
result would be that for killing a person
you could only get six months, while for
causing grievous bodily harm you could
get two years. Imagine a man walking
along the road with his pal, and a motor
car coming along and killing his pal. His
friend perhaps starts in and horse-whips
the driver of the car and kills him by
accident he does not intend it.
In one
case the individual could get two years,
and in the other, for killing the individual
under, perhaps, circumstances of drunkenness of the most aggravated form, he
can only get six months. That would be
the effect of this Amendment.
My hon. Friend admits the extraordinary difficulty we have had, and also
admits that this is the best he could do
as against the Government's suggestion
in the Bill. Ours may not be a perfect
Clause, but I hope hon. Senators will
agree that the Bill in its present form
meets the situation in a way that my hon.
Friend's Amendment does not. I hope
it will not go out from this House that
we are prepared to adopt reactionary
methods to deal with this appalling loss
of life. Motor traffic, I believe, is only in
its initial stages, and we should try to
encourage decent driving. The hon
Senator (Mr. Leslie) I know is a great
sportsman. I venture to say that when
he first took his son out to shoot he told
him that his gun was a dangerous
weapon, and that he must under all cir
cumstances
use it with the greatest
possible care. We want to make ou
young people realise that motor cars are
dangerous weapons and that those who
neglect to see where they are going when
they are driving, or who cause loss of life,
should stand their trial. I hope the
Committee will refuse to accept the
Amendment.
same
Mr. CAMPBELL: The right hon.
Gentleman (Sir Dawson Bates) who has
just spoken made reference-and proper
reference to the appalling loss of life
caused by motor accidents, a remark
amply justified when we remember that
last year in Great Britain 6,000 lives
were lost in motor car accidents. In the
year in the United States 27,000
lives were lost on the highways through
motor car accidents. I believe there are
now 2,000,000 motor vehicles travelling
on the highways of Great Britain, and it
is believed that in the course of five years
that number will have increased to
5,000,000. There will be a proportionate
increase, of course, in Northern Ireland
as a result of the magnitude of this new
phenomenon in civilisation, the appearance of the motor car.
a
very
The Clause, I candidly recognise,
is a clever effort to solve
peculiar and a very puzzling problem. It is a device a pardonable
device to attract juries to convict in
motor fatality cases by what I might call
camouflaging the charge of manslaughter.
The effect of it is that after a jury acquit
a motorist of manslaughter or causing
bodily harm under the Clause they may
convict him of the dangerous driving
which occasioned the death or hurt.
idea is that a jury would hesitate or
shrink from a direct verdict of guilty of
manslaughter-that is of killing the
victim-or of hurting the victim, but that
they will not shrink from the alternative
verdict of guilty of dangerous driving
which ended in the killing or hurt.
The
I fear that that is an optimistic view.
Jurors have strong common sense, and
they will know perfectly well that in trying this alternative count they are really
trying the same issue twice over. If they
hesitate to convict of manslaughter, I fear
they will equally hesitate to convict of
reckless or dangerous driving which culminated in death. I do not know that
the law is very much improved by the
clause in question. I have no doubt the
anxiety of the Ministry is to improve it, as
far as it can possibly be improved. It
Committee.

Road Traffic Bill-
THE SENATE
[Mr. Campbell]
limits the imprisonment for the dangerous
driving of a motor-car on an occasion involving death or bodily harm to six months'
imprisonment and a fine. I remarked last
week that since 1861 the punishment for
the dangerous driving of a brougham or
side car or push bicycle or farmer's cart
resulting in bodily harm ranges up to two
years' imprisonment. The punishment
under the Motor Act of 1926 for the dangerous driving of a motor car is on first
summary conviction a fine between £1 and
£10. The driver of a donkey cart on the
streets of Belfast guilty of dangerous.
driving has been for the past 78 years
liable to a fine of £1.
I am very much afraid that the standard
of driving on our roads by reckless drivers
will not be much improved by the Clause.
I hope it may. I am perfectly certain
that this Clause is directed not against the
general body of motor drivers, but against
the small and I fear increasing number of
people who have got motor cars into their
hands, sometimes at ridiculously low
prices, and who seem to have no sense of
responsibility to the general public, no
sense of their own duty, and no sense of
the care they ought to exercise in driving
along the highways such a powerful and
destructive weapon as a motor car is.
in
able.
a
With regard to the Amendment which
has been proposed by the hon. and learned
Senator (Mr. Leslie), it does credit to his
ingenuity and industry, and it indicates.
a desire to assist, as far as he possibly can,
Bill
making this
more work-
But I do not know that
it will work; I do not know that
it will even achieve the object he has
in view. It institutes a new species of
crime called "punishable
"punishable homicide."
Murder is form of homicide, manslaughter is a form of homicide, and this
creation of punishable homicide struck me
at first with some surprise. Then, for the
first time in English law, it makes the killing of a human being a misdemeanour.
Human life has always been held so sacred
in our courts that in every case where
killing has taken place it is felony or nothing. But the hon. and learned Senator,
in the Amendment before the Committee,
makes it a misdemeanour, and calls it
punishable homicide. Is this phrase not a
variation of the Anglo-Saxon word manslaughter? I think it is. The hon. and
learned Senator says:
for the
It shall be lawful
jury to acquit the defendant of manslaughter and convict him of misdemeanour, to wit, punishable homicide.
Further on, he states:
In an indictment for punishable homicide it shall be set out that the person
charged caused the death of the person
whose death is the subject of the
charge.
I am very much afraid that the mere variation of the word "manslaughter" to the
phrase "punishable homicide" will not
have any more attraction for a jury. They
may understand manslaughter. The judge
would take care to explain it to them.
But if they happen to be frightened at all,
they are more likely to be frightened by
"punishable homicide" than by "manslaughter." For these reasons, I regret
that I cannot support the Amendment of
my hon. and learned Friend (Mr. Leslie).
Mr. McALLISTER: Hon. Senators in
every part of the House seem to have grave
doubts as to the probable effect in operation of this experimental new alternative
charge. Its justification is based by the
Minister of Home Affairs upon the ground
that out of fifty prosecutions there were
only six convictions. I venture to suggest to the House that it is very possible
that, if this new Clause had been in operation during the period in which those
prosecutions were brought, instead of six
convictions out of fifty cases, there might
have been seven or eight cases in which
people would have got six months only for
taking human life. We are all agreed
that juries are very hesitant about convicting of manslaughter, and my own view
of the matter is this, that very possibly
this Clause is going to provide an easy way
out for a jury. When they have the
slightest doubt or hesitation as to whether
they will convict or not, instead of convicting a man, who has been guilty of killing another, of manslaughter, they will
take the alternative and easier way.
The jury do not know what the punishment is. The jury have no idea that
a man who is found guilty of the alternative charge is only liable to imprisonment
for six months. The jury have not that
information before them. I have grave
doubts as to the effect of this new Clause.
On the other hand, I do not think that the
Amendment proposed by the hon. Senator
is a better alternative. I think, of the
two, I prefer the original Clause in the
Bill. But I am opposed to this rather
experimental legislation, and to bringing
Road Traffic Bill-
Committee
16 DECEMBER 1929
forward this easier method for a jury to
get out of doing their duty when the number of deaths from careless and reckless
driving of motor cars is increasing from
day to day.
Mr. LESLIE: I do not intend to detain the House, but I feel that I must
take advantage of my right to reply. The
right hon. Gentleman, if I may say so,
seemed to devote the greater part of his
speech to showing how very much he and
I were in reality in agreement as regards
the basis on which we had to build.
Sir DAWSON BATES: Yes, in principle.
Mr. LESLIE: He talked about the
fifty cases in which he got only six convictions, and then he went on to say that
my proposal was reactionary. He did not
explain in what manner it was reactionary,
nor did he explain why he will not amend
Section 1 of the Motor Traffic Act of 1926.
Sir DAWSON BATES: I hope the
hon. Senator does not misuderstand me. I
pointed out that his Amendment was reactionary because, it it were carried, it
would have the effect of doing away entirely with the charge of manslaughter.
Mr. LESLIE: I understand. But the
Clause in the Bill would have the same
effect. As the Bill stands, the maximum
punishment is six months' imprisonment
and a fine, whether the driver of a motor
car kills another person or causes that person bodily harm. That is how the Bill
stands, surely?
Sir DAWSON BATES: If he is convicted of manslaughter, the maximum
term of imprisonment is twenty years.
Mr. LESLIE: The maximum term is
imprisonment for life, with or without a
fine.
Sir DAWSON BATES: In theory, yes;
in practice, twenty years.
Mr. LESLIE :
On the trial of any indictment for
manslaughter or for doing or causing
bodily harm to any person whatsoever in
connection with the driving of a motor
car on a public highway, the jury may
acquit the defendant of manslaughter or
of doing or causing bodily harm and find
him guilty of driving recklessly or
wantonly or at a speed or in a manner
which is dangerous to the public, having
regard to all the circumstances of the
case.
As regards the right hon. Gentleman's reference to the teaching of a boy to use a
gun, I think it is the custom to teach the
young the danger of a gun long before
they are allowed to take a gun outside the
house at all. The learned counsel paid me
a compliment on the drafting of my
Amendment. I thank him; it gave me
some trouble to find a phrase that had not
been used before for the alternative to
manslaughter. As regards making killing
a misdemeanour, that is the bold course I
suggested because hitherto manslaughter
has had to be dealt with as a felony. I
regret that this Amendment has not
found any support. I hardly expected
that it would, but at the same time I should
like to say that it was an honest attempt
to escape from an illogical position. I
sincerely hope the Clause in the Bill will
be justified by the result, and I ask leave.
to withdraw the Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 3.-(Drunk in charge of
motor vehicles).
(Sub-section (1) of section two of the
principal Act shall have effect as if the
words "drunk while in charge on any
highway or other public place of a motor
car" were omitted and the following
words inserted instead: "found when
driving or attempting to drive, or when
in charge of a motor car on any highway
or other public place, to be under the
influence of drink or drugs to such an
extent as to be incapable of having proper control of the vehicle."
""
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in page 3,
line 13, to leave out the word "drink,'
("under the influence of drink,”) and to
insert instead thereof the words "intoxicating liquor."
So far as I know, we have not used this
expression "drink" in an Act of Parliament before. We all use the phrase "under the influence of drink," but so far we
have never given it statutory recognition.
I think it would be better to delete this
word, which, after all, might mean anything potable, and substitute "intoxicating liquor.'
""
Road Traffic Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
Sir DAWSON BATES: May I suggest
that this Amendment would have a rather
I
limiting effect ? I looked up Webster's
Dictionary, and found that "drink" was
defined as intoxicating drink. I am inclined to think that the Amendment
would have a limiting effect in this way,
that it would place upon the authorities
the responsibility of ascertaining what the
man had drunk before he got drunk.
think it would be too absurd to put the
responsibility on the police of proving that
a man had drunk stout or whisky, when
as a matter of fact he might have been
drinking a so called teetotal drink. It is
well known that there is alcohol in
lemonade. It is a very infinitesimal
quantity, but I suggest that if you drink
a sufficient quantity of lemonade you
might possibly be intoxicated. Take
stone ginger. There is as much as 3 per
cent. alcohol in it. But on the whole in
this matter we are following the Imperial
draughtsman. It is a universal term,
and I suggest the Amendment would have
a decidedly cramping effect.
I do hope
the hon. Senator will not press it.
Mr. LESLIE: If the right hon. Gentleman prefers "drink" he can have it.
(Laughter).
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Mr. LESLIE: I am sorry to say this
is an Amendment I have not had time to
put on the Paper. I did not discover it
until too late. It is in Clause 3, page 3,
after line 15 to insert as
new subsection:-
a
"(2) For the purposes of sub-section
(3) of Section twenty-four of the principal Act an offence under this Section
shall be deemed to be an offence of
drunkenness."
The point is, if the Committee remembers, the proviso to Section 24 of
the principal Act relates to the offence of
drunkenness while in charge of a motor
car. The Committee by the decision it
has just taken has done away with this
offence under the principal Act altogether. We have substituted for
"drunkenness in charge" the new expression "under the influence of drink or
of drugs." Now" under the influence of
drink or drugs is not the same thing as
drunkenness." Therefore, it is necessary
to connect up the two. Accordingly, I
propose as the shortest way of doing that
the new sub-clause. I beg to move.
I hope my
Sir DAWSON BATES:
hon. Friend will not press that. The
Committee, of course, will appreciate that
under Section 24, which deals with preserving the rights of certain highway
authorities, such as the Belfast Harbour
Commissioners, there are certain specified
offences, and one of these is the offence
of drunkenness. I think my hon. Friend
will appreciate that this new definition
must be read in conjunction with the Act
of 1926, which retains Section 24.
Otherwise it would be perfectly meaningless. I hope he will not press the
Amendment. I can give him this assurance. In connection with another
matter, that is the insurance question, I
anticipate that in the Spring we will have
to bring another Bill. If we find in the
meantime that it is necessary I will be
only too ready to amend it then.
Mr. LESLIE: In that case,
as the
right hon. Gentleman says he will deal
with the matter in the Spring, it would
be idle to press the Amendment.
Amendment by leave withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 4 (Court to consist of resident
magistrates).
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to move that
this Clause be left out. The Clause has
to do with the constitution of the court
which is to deal with offences under this
Act and under the principal Act of 1926,
and it directs that the court shall be constituted of a resident magistrate sitting
alone. The Clause in my estimation is a
step towards snuffing out the jurisdiction
of the lay justices. Step by step that
jurisdiction is disappearing and soon the
only jurisdiction left to them will be adjudication on questions like vulgar squabbling, drunkenness, and petty matters of
that kind. I ask will the best type of lay
magistrate attend at petty sessions after so
much nibbling away of the jurisdiction of
the court. It is not done in England. It
is not suggested to take away jurisdiction in motor car cases from the ordinary
court of summary jurisdiction in England.
Surely the lay justices can lay claim to
have done the State some service in their
time. If they go wrong an appeal lies
from them as a matter of right in almost
every case. Many of them are men of
capacity and men of education, men who
stand well in their different districts. Why
a most accomplished law lord, Lord Mac-
Road Traffic Bill-
Committee
16 DECEMBER 1929
naghten, a very great luminary on the
English bench whose judgments are literature as well as law-
Mr. LESLIE: Hear, hear.
Mr. CAMPBELL -was accustomed
to sit at petty sessions in North Antrim
for years and years. If this particular
Clause passes a man like Lord Macnaghten
will be disqualified from trying a chauffeur
for driving without a licence. I never
heard it suggested that lay justices failed
to do their duty in motor car cases. They
bring into court a knowledge of men and
of life. The law to be applied in cases of
this kind is the A.B.C. law. I think the
ordinary type of magistrates in cases of
this kind would bring their free unfettered
judgment to deal with the cases fairly and
squarely as between man and man.
Common sense is what is needed, and the
lay justices as we know them in a body,
taking them all in all, have this qualification. This Clause to my mind throws
rather a slur upon that very efficient and
very honest body of men. At this time
when there is public interest in the solution of the motor car problem, and motor
car cases are on the increase, the general
public will distrust the swapping of horses.
Therefore I beg to move that Clause 4 be
deleted.
Mr. LESLIE: I rise to support the
Amendment that has been moved by the
hon. and learned Senator. I think he has
said nothing with which I do not cordially
agree, but I do not desire if I can help it
to repeat anything he has already said. I
think I have spoken in this House more
than once on this question, and as on
other questions I find that every time I
have to speak the circumstances are somewhat different.
On the proposal before us there are one
or two things that have to be remarked.
The expression "resident magistrate" itself is not defined as is usual in our Bills.
It is apparently provided that the resident
magistrate shall sit alone. He is not to
have another resident magistrate to help
him. We are not told when or where
he is to sit whether at petty sessions or
not. Although I do not attach very great
importance to that myself, I do attach
much more importance to where he is to
sit-whether he is to sit in the district
where the offence is alleged to have been
committed or whether he is to do as some
resident magistrates do, sit, say in Ballymoney, to deal with a case that occurred
in Ballycastle. That brings the alleged
offender and his counsel and witnesses from
one petty sessions district into another,
and the distance between the limits of the
district is, I suppose, not less than twelve
miles. It is obviously of great importance to everybody that the Court should
sit at convenient time and in convenient
places. You will have difficulty about
that if you pass this, because, as I said
before, there are I think, only twelve resident magistrates in Northern Ireland, and
I think there are 144 courts where these
resident magistrates have to sit. It does
not, therefore, seem practical to have a
resident magistrate sitting at each of these
courts.
or
Passing from that, the position is rather
curious. The Motor Vehicles Act of 1926
is not the only Act in what I may call our
motor code. The Act of 1896 has not yet
been repealed, and there are certain
matters under the Roads Act of 1920.
The proposal is, then, that the ordinary
magistrates should deal with the Act of
1920 and the Act of 1896, while they
should not deal with offences under this
new Bill or under the Act of 1926. Again,
one of the powers of these resident magistrates sitting alone is that he can fine a
man up to £100 with without six
months' imprisonment in certain cases,
and he can do that without the man
having any option whatever of being tried
by a jury. That is quite wrong. A resident magistrate, however able he may be,
is just as much an excrescence on the
common law as the ordinary magistrate,
and it is not right that one resident magistrate sitting alone should have the
power
of fining a man £100 with or without imprisonment when the man has not the
right of being tried by a jury. Of course,
if the man is dissatisfied with the sentence
he can appeal, but the appeal is also to
one man only, the county court judge.
Again no jury.
I think the hon. and learned Senator
(Mr. Campbell) dealt with the gross disrespect which will be shown to ordinary
magistrates if you exclude them from adjudicating in these matters. I cannot
help thinking that some of them will be
amused when they reflect that they are
still permitted to try ordinary cases of
drunkenness and cannot try whether
man is under the influence of drink or
drugs when in charge of a motor car.
They will also still be able to refuse an information in a case of manslaughter, but
a
Road Traffic Bill-
Committee.
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
they cannot convict a man of dangerous
driving, or for driving a motor car without a licence, or for having too many passengers in his omnibus. There are resident magistrates, perhaps, who are just
as likely to have prejudices as other magistrates. I must say I should like to have
heard the views of the newly-formed
Magistrates' Association on this Clause.
The right hon. Gentleman (Sir Dawson
Bates) laughs, but there is such an Association. It advertises itself in the papers,
and it asked me to join, but I did not do
SO. I have the views of the Chairman of
a bench in County Down which I received
this morning, and those views are rather in
favour of my own than in favour of the
Bill.
The only definite ground I have heard
suggested here as to why magistrates
should be removed from jurisdiction in
this matter is the reason put forward by
the right hon. Gentleman immediately in
front (Mr. S. Cunningham) on the Second
Reading. He suggested that magistrates
ought to be relieved of trying motor cases
because it must be an unpleasant duty to
sit in judgment on your neighbours. That
is so, but I do not think it is a good reason
for relieving a man of a duty he may
sometimes find unpleasant. I cannot help
wondering whether even a resident magistrate might not find it unpleasant to exercise his jurisdiction. That is the only
point I heard, but I was very glad to hear
the noble Viscount, the Leader of the
House, say on the Second Reading that
this was a matter to which the Government
was indifferent, and on which they proposed to allow a free vote. To pass over
the question as to how far this House
would ever allow itself to be deprived of
its free vote, the point is of importance.
There are Clauses that do gain something
in having Government support behind
them, and that is, in this case, happily
absent.
I know the Clause was not in the original
Bill. I understand it was moved by a
private Member in another place, who so
far as I know is not a member of the magistracy. It was opposed by one of the
Members from my own county who is a
magistrate, and I think the question perhaps got through rather more rapidly than
expected. I hope this House will decide to
exercise this Clause.
Sir DAWSON BATES: The hon.
and learned Senator (Mr. Leslie) is perup
fectly correct when he said the Government propose to allow a free vote on this
matter and I trust hon. Members will
give it their best consideration, as I am
sure they will, before they make their
minds how to vote. This Clause was inserted in the Bill, as Senator Leslie said,
on an Amendment moved by a private
Member when the Government followed
the course I have indicated, but I think
the hon. and learned Member is incorrect
when he suggested that there was any
opposition, because my recollection is that
the Clause was passed without a division.
Mr. LESLIE: I believe it was passed
without a division, but I think I was
right in saying that it was opposed by
the hon. Member for Antrim.
Sir DAWSON BATES: As this
House will have the opportunity of a free
vote I do not wish to appear dictatorial,
but perhaps I may make one or two
observations that occur to me. My hon.
and learned Friend rather suggested
there was no precedent for this. May I
point out that all the big cities in
England have their Stipendiary magistrates which correspond to our resident
magistrates, and all these cases are dealt
with by the Stipendiary magistrates
sitting alone. They are all trained
lawyers, and when this Government here
came into being we did away with the
old practice that laymen should be appointed to these responsible positions,
and at the present time all appointments
are made from barristers and solicitors
who have been practising for six years.
They are men of the world who know the
actual practice.
The hon. and learned Member raised
the question of administration. He need
not bother about that. I know that for
reasons of economy the number of our
resident magistrates is smaller than it
might be, but I can assure Senator Leslie
that arrangements will be made to ensure
that no inconvenience will be caused to
individuals who are brought before a
court. I think the hon. and learned
Member may rest easy on that point.
May I suggest another reason why I think.
it is desirable to retain this Clause.
doubtedly at the present time there are
excellent magistrates who are prejudiced
against motor cars, and there are others
who take exactly another view. Be that
as it may, I do not suggest for one
moment that these magistrates do anything else than act perfectly conscien-
Un-
Road Traffic Bill-
Committee
16 DECEMBER 1929
tiously. Naturally enough, some of the
people who come before them feel that
perhaps they are treated a little harshly.
I think hon. Members will agree with me
that when the whole principle of having
magistrates first established at the time
of the formation of our Constitution the
individuals who came before magistrates
were either those of the criminal class or
those persons who had committed what
one might call minor offences.
Now.
reference has been made to
drunkenness. It has been said that no
gentleman should get drunk, but, unfortunately, it happens that a great many
individuals who ought to know better and
who drive motor cars do get drunk. It
has always struck me as a very
hard
thing for magistrates to have to deal with
this class of cases because it may happen
that when a case comes on the son of a
neighbour may come up charged with
what I can only describe as a very serious
offence, the magistrate has to decide
whether he is going to do his duty or
avoid going on the bench. If he goes on
the bench he may perhaps lose a life-long
friend as the result of having to give a
decision according to his conscience.
I think it is asking magistrates to take
upon themselves too much if we ask them
to take this responsibility, because I do
not think anything could be more serious
to a man in business. Take the case of a
commercial traveller. He has to use his
vehicle for his business. If he unfortunately goes over the mark a little bit
and comes before a bench of magistrates
I think it is imposing a terrible responsibility on the magistrates. This class of
cases ought to be dealt with by trained
legal men who will carefully examine
from the legal point of view how much
importance to attach to the doctor's
evidence, and how much to the other
evidence that is given. As I said, this
is a matter entirely for this Committee,
and I leave it with it in the confidence
that it will arrive at a correct decision.
Mr. MCALLISTER: I can agree with
the right hon. Gentleman when he states
the appointment of trained lawyers who
have been in actual practice will minimise
to a great extent the fact that these courts
are constituted by resident magistrates
only. One of the reasons why I would
suggest to the Committee the advisability
of maintaining the ordinary courts is that.
we must assume, and should assume, that
appointments are properly made, and that
magistrates are selected from the most upright and responsible men in the community. If that is so, a great advantage
that I see from the ordinary magistrates'
court is that you have the collective judgment of seven or eight people, instead of
the individual judgment of only one
person,
The class of case they are called upon
to adjudicate does not involve any serious
question of law. It is nearly always a
question of pure fact, and I think it is
of great advantage to the administration
of the law that there should be the collective judgment of so many people rather
than the judgment of one. I, myself,
have known cases in the best constituted
petty sessions court I know which were
heard by seven or eight men, with every
one of whom I would leave any issue with
the fullest possible confidence. I have
seen the lay magistrates on that bench in
agreement, and rightly in agreement,
both in fact and in law, and the resident
magistrate in a minority dissenting from
their view, and when he came to deliver
his reason for his judgment he was manifestly ignorant of the law on that particular point. And that, I say, is one of the
best benches I ever had the privilege to
practise before.
The right hon. Gentleman has given as
his reason for depriving these magistrates
of jurisdiction under this Bill the fact that
they might be called upon to administer a
serious and perhaps unpleasant duty in
finding that a son of a neighbour had been
drunk in charge of a motor car. I think
that any man who is appointed a magistrate should have sufficient sense of responsibility to do his duty without any
difficulty, and I do not see how he could
lose a life-long friend, as the right hon.
Gentleman has said, of any value who
would find fault with him for discharging
his duty and protecting the public against.
the man who will, without any justification, get drunk in charge of a motor
car.
Surely there must be some other reason
for depriving the magistrates of jurisdiction in these cases, because, after all, it
does not require much legal knowledge
nor is it a very serious duty. The magistrates have been deprived of duty under
the Noxious Weeds Act, and the same
Road Traffic Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
[Mr. McAllister]
argument which the right hon. Gentleman
is now applying to this Bill would apply
to that Act. They have been deprived of
their jurisdiction under the Licensing Act,
the ordinary Fishery Acts, and I do not
know how many others. I think it is a
great pity that a great tribunal which has
served the country well and has done its
duty in ninety-nine cases out of a hundred
without fear, favour, or affection should
be scrapped, and a more or less experimental tribunal set up in its place.
The hon. Senator (Mr. Leslie) has referred to the fact that resident magistrates
may be as liable to be prejudiced in their
views as other magistrates. It is common
knowledge that various judges are referred to as difficult judges for one class
of cases and other judges difficult judges
for other classes of cases. Certainly resident magistrates are not particularly
impartial. Of course, it is perfectly unconscious partiality on their part. It is a
notorious fact that certain resident magistrates when adjudicating in certain cases
will suggest to solicitors to advise their
clients to plead guilty, even if they have
a fairly good case.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: I am sorry I
have to oppose this Amendment, and I
do so on the ground of experience. We
in County Down, when a matter comes
before the magistrates in connection with
a person driving without a licence thank
God there is a minimum fine. If there
was not there would be very little fines of
any kind. When they consider the case if
the evidence is clear they are bound to
convict, and the fine is imposed.
have had many cases where the evidence
was not absolutely clear, perhaps, according to the local bench, or according to the
friendship of the defendant with the
bench. In that case we have the right
to appeal, and in 90 per cent. of those
cases in which there was no conviction,
that decision was reversed when the case
went to a proper court.
We
I have lost a life-long friendship through
a conviction of that kind. I think it is
not fair to the local magistrates to ask
them to convict a man in their own
neighbourhood. It would be far better
in the interests of justice if these cases
were allowed to go to the resident
magistrates. In the Free State, Liverpool, and London they have had to
appoint stipendiary magistrates to carry
out the law in cases of this kind. I quite
agree there are outstanding benches of
magistrates, but there are others that are
not outstanding.
Mr. QUIGLEY: I rise to say a few
words with regard to the question of
whether a resident magistrate should try
these cases or not. The hon. Senator
(Mr. McAllister) has referred to a bench
of seven or eight magistrates in which he
would put entire confidence. I think
very often some of our benches would be
better without seven or eight magistrates.
I am quite in agreement with the hon.
Senator (Mr. Hill Dickson) that it would
be very much in the interests of everybody concerned if a resident magistrate
would be empowered to try such cases.
One of our justices might find himself on
the bench trying a very near friend who
In
is charged with a serious offence.
regard to the offence of drunkenness, I
really think such cases should be tried by
resident magistrates. Very often we have
men who are in a drunken state in charge
of motor cars on the public highways.
Possibly they know quite a number of
magistrates and they will appeal to those
magistrates when the case comes before
the bench. I strongly support the Clause
in the Bill that refers to resident
magistrates trying these cases.
Mr. LESLIE: As far as my experience goes the majority of magistrates
do not have a large number of friends
who are likely to appear before them on
a charge of drunkenness, and so forth. I
cannot speak for Newtownards or County
Down in general, but I think that is a fair
thing to say. The right hon. Gentleman
(Sir Dawson Bates) in his speech said that
arrangements would be made to make
this Clause workable, that is for appointing, I presume, enough magistrates to do
the work without fetching people from
Ballycastle, Ballymoney, and so on.
was just wondering if that was so why
the Government did not put this Clause
in the Bill at the commencement. I
think perhaps that is not germane to this
matter.
Question put: "That this Clause be
left out."
The Committee divided: Contents, 5;
Not Contents. 12.

Clause ordered to stand part of the

CLAUSE 5 (Amendment as to minimum age for drivers of certain public
service vehicles) ordered to stand part of

CLAUSE 6 (Provisions as to conditions attached to licences for public
service vehicles).
(2) The Ministry of Home Affairs
shall consult the county surveyor of the
county concerned, before that Ministry imposes, in respect of any licences
for public service vehicles used to
carry passengers for hire in a county
not being a county borough, conditions
which permit of any such vehicle being
so used on any route in the county
from which public vehicles are excluded
at the date of the passing of this Act.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in page 4,
line 41. leave out "county surveyor'
("consult the county surveyor'), and
insert "council."
This is a rather new proposal, and I am
satisfied is brought forward to save time.
The proposal is that the Ministry of Home
Affairs shall consult the county surveyor
of the county concerned in regard to the
question of the use of routes. That is an
entirely new proposal altogether. The
county surveyor is an officer of the county
council, and it is contrary to all practice
for the Minister to go behind the backs of
the county council and deal direct with
one of their subordinate officers. It does
not follow, I think, that time would necessarily be saved, because it is perfectly open
to the county council to leave matters of
this sort either to the Finance Committee
or direct to the County Surveyor if they
think fit to do so, and pass standing orders
accordingly. It is not fitting, however,
that their officers and the Ministry should
be in direct communication.
Pakenham, Col. H. A.
Dobbs, Major A. F.
Long, Mr. John A.
Quigley, Mr. Andrew
Sinclair, Col. Thomas
The result may very well be that an unfortunate county surveyor may find himself in opposition to his own council and to
his own Finance Committee, and he might
be saddled with a responsibility which he
is not able to bear. The responsibility must
rest with the council unless they think fit
to delegate it. As I say, I am sure the
intention of the proposal was merely to
save time. I am afraid that we must look
at it in another way. You have to regard
not only the county councils of the
present day, and advisory committees.
of the present day, but you
have
to look to the future county councils
and to the real desires of those that
these county councils may represent.
I am afraid that looked at carefully
one can only say that this proposal
to consult the county surveyor otherwise
than through the county council is quite
unacceptable. I am prepared to state
that it is turning the first sod of an underground railway between the Ministry of
Home Affairs and the officials of the
county council, and that is a sod which
had better be left unturned.
Sir DAWSON BATES: I do not think
my hon. Friend need be
very much
alarmed as to the effect this will have on
the county councils. Before drafting the
Bill we had the advantage of submitting
this to the county council representatives
on the Roads Advisory Committee. That
Roads Advisory Committee, I might point.
out, is represented by my hon. Friend who
sits behind me (Mr. Long) and also by
Senator Colonel Sharman-Crawford. One
is chairman of the County Council of
Derry and the other of the County Council
of Down. We submitted the entire Bill
to the Roads Advisory Committee, including these two gentlemen, before introducing it to the House. I may point out
further to my hon. Friend (Mr. Leslie)
that I am sorry he is not a member of the
county council, because if he had been he
I would have found that this Bill had the
general approval of the county councils,
and that this particular Clause was inserted at their express request, because
Road Traffic Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
[Sir Dawson Bates]
they felt that very serious damage might
be done to their roads through any delay
in this matter.
As a matter of fact I think my hon.
Friend is aware that from time to time we
do consult the county councils as to
whether or not certain roads ought to be
closed to bus traffic. I remember a certain
member of a county council-a most active
member at the time our Roads Bill was
going through, urging that the county surveyor himself should have absolute authority to close any road to all classes of vehicles. He said there was no use in leaving
such a matter to the county council, because the damage would be done so
rapidly that if they wished to save the
roads it must be left to the county surveyor so that he could go out and say that
a road was in a bad condition and that he
must stop all traffic of heavy vehicles on
it. This is only put in for the purpose of
consulting the county surveyor and of
getting his technical advice. It is not
mandatory in any way. It is only for the
purpose of getting the best information at
our disposal.
I would remind my hon. Friend that
county surveyors have statutory duties
which they must fulfil, and I can hardly
conceive a condition of affairs where the
county council would find fault with their
county surveyor for doing his duty and expressing his opinion. We might consult
the county councils and ask their views,
but they would eventually have to consult
their county surveyor, and the fact remains that the expression of opinion would
have to come from their technical adviser.
This is in no sense going behind the backs
of the county councils, with whom I am
glad to say we work on the most cordial
terms. We want to have no undue delay.
It may be that a new bus service is needed
at once. The application comes in and we
do not want to hang it up for a month or
so, because my hon. Friend is aware that
the county councils do not meet more often
than twice or at the most four times a
year. They can, as my hon. Friend says,
delegate their powers, but they need not
do so, and they have not delegated
their powers in this matter to
sub-committee which my hon. Friend says
meets once a fortnight. The result of this
Amendment would be that a new service
might be hung up for a considerable time.
I hope my hon. Friend will not press his
Amendment.
the
Colonel PAKENHAM: I cannot quite
see why the Government should object to
the words "county council" here instead
of "surveyor." After all, it is simply a
question of appealing to the county
council, who are the proper authority.
The county surveyor is only an officer,
and if the county council desire they
could allow the surveyor's committee to
deal with this matter. I am only giving
my own ideas about this question, bot
I do think the Government are making
undue trouble about it. I do not see
why the council should not delegate to
their surveyor the duties incumbent under this Clause. If you appeal to the
junior officer of any organisation over
the head of a senior you are possibly
going to make trouble. That is all I
have to say. I think the word "council"
would have met the case quite as well, and
by putting it in here as suggested you
would be appealing to the right person
in the first case.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: This matter
has been fully considered by the Roads
Advisory Committee and by the General
County Councils of the Six Counties.
The Council which I have the honour to
represent has also considered it, and I
have no doubt that every other individual
county council has done so, and they have
all agreed that the county surveyor is
the proper authority to deal with this
matter. We in County Down only meet
once in six months, and if we had to wait
for those meetings a road might be
worked to the quick before we could get
the traffic stopped.
Mr. BARCLAY:
boat on it.
You could put a
Mr. HILL DICKSON: You could put
a boat on it, as my hon. Friend opposite
suggests. When this delegation of power
is given to the county surveyor, there is,
of course, the Finance Committee, which
meets every fortnight and which he really
does consult. The work would be
better left in the hands of an expert than
delegated to the county council which
meets at such very long intervals. I
cannot support the Amendment.
Mr. LONG: I support the views of the
last speaker in regard to the question of
consulting the county surveyor. As to
whether the principle is a good one or
not I do not express an opinion. I am
rather inclined to think it is not, but in
Road Traffic Bill-
Committee
16 DECEMBER 1929
practice and that is the real test-it is
working admirably. Difficulty was experienced in the case of a service such as
that from Belfast to Enniskillen where
it passed through the territory of four
or five county councils. They met at
different times, and to obtain decisions
from those four or five county councils
meeting at different times entailed interminable delay. As my hon. Friend
Mr. Hill Dickson) has said irreparable
damage could be done to a road before
a remedy could be obtained.
In practice the county surveyor is very
slow to approve of a new service being
established. He, of course, is proud of
his road, and is extremely jealous of its
surface being injured in any way. In
cases where any important principle is
involved he consults the Finance Committee, which meets fortnightly, and in
the case of our own county surveyor he
very much more frequently than fortnightly consults me. We have no com-
Campbell, Mr. T. J.
Dobbs, Major A. F.
Bangor, The Viscount
Barclay, Mr. William
Charlemont, The Viscount
plaint whatever to make with the operation of this in the past. I would earnestly
plead with the Committee to continue it.
Mr. LESLIE: We have heard a great
deal about consultation with any body
whatever other than Parliament, who after
all are the proper people to consult in
matters of this kind. I thought I was
fairly careful to explain that, whatever
the present county councils or the Roads
Advisory Committee might say, what we
have to look at is what might happen in
the future if we once establish this
principle. For myself I prefer principle
to expediency. I am not going to withdraw this Amendment. The Committee
can do as they like with it.
Question put, "That the words 'county
surveyor' be left out, in order to insert the
word 'council.' '

other House. I think the expression intended must be "public service vehicles."
Sir DAWSON BATES: I think this is

remedied as such without putting the

The DEPUTY-CHAIRMAN: I am
perfectly content.
Mr. LESLIE: I have no objection at

come from another place agreement has to

Pakenham, Col. H. A.

have been remedied by some of the readers
