Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to move, in
Clause 6, page 5, after line 5, to insert as
a new sub-section :
"(3) It shall not be lawful for the
council of a county or county borough
to grant a licence to drive a motor car
on any highway to any person applying for it unless there is in force in
relation to the car while being so
used such a policy of insurance or
such a security in respect of thirdparty risks as complies with such
regulations as the Ministry of Home
Affairs may prescribe."
This Amendment brings forward the
question of insurance to be effected by
drivers of motor cars against third party
risks. It is intended to apply to grants
of licences to drive motor cars on highways in future, but not to renewals, and
the intention is to emphasise a very real
Road Traffic Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
[Mr. Campbell]
danger to the general public.
Steps
should be taken to ensure that a man
who cannot get an insurance policy to
cover third party risks should not get a
licence to drive a motor car on our highways. It is, I submit to the judgment
of the Committee, a very lamentable state
of affairs that a man may be disabled for
life by a motor car driven with gross
negligence and that when he proceeds to
court and secures damages he may find
the judgment of the court utterly valueless.
I know that there are administrative
difficulties in connection with this matter.
I know that those difficulties are not slight
and cannot be easily surmounted. But I
believe there is a very strong public feeling in regard to this matter, and a very
clear and definite public interest at stake.
Therefore, I beg to move the Amendment
standing in my name.
Sir DAWSON BATES: May I say at
once that the Government are entirely at
one with the hon. and learned Senator in
the principle he has laid down? But
there are, as he has already pointed out,
extraordinarily difficult administrative
matters that have to be dealt with beforehand. To show the sympathy that
the Government have with the general
principle, I would remind the Committee that long before the British Government touched this subject we dealt
with it in regard to public service
vehicles, but, as I have said in another
place, that deals with only 800 vehicles
as against 80,000 vehicles of the ordinary
kind. During the past eighteen months,
in which this law has been enforced, experience has shown the extraordinary
difficulties with which we are faced on
this question.
I have already pointed out that we
cannot force insurance companies to take
risks that they do not desire to take. I
am not prepared to say that the existing
arrangement in regard to public service
vehicles may not break down, and I may
be faced with the position either of
allowing ordinary public service vehicles
to go on to the road without being
covered or of depriving the public of the
service. That is due simply to the difficulty of getting insurance companies to
take these risks. Therefore, to attempt
to deal with this bigger problem in
Northern Ireland, without an arrangement being carried out in England,
would be absolutely impossible. We
must have the big insurance companies
with us in some sort of an arrangement.
I think everybody realises that insurance
premiums are bound to go up. I hope
the period will never be reached when instead of the Government being the
licensing authority the insurance
panies will occupy that position, and be
in a position to charge whatever rate they
like for insurance.
com-
I am in the closest touch with the
British Government in regard to the
arrangements they propose to make, and
I think I am not betraying any confidence
when I say that they are faced with enormous difficulties. I can give this Committee the undertaking that if a solution.
of the problem is arrived at in regard to
the British Bill I will come to Parliament with an amending Bill dealing with
insurance. I think the hon. and learned
Senator (Mr. Campbell) will agree with
me that he does not attempt to solve the
problem, but only lays down the principle.
Mr. CAMPBELL: Quite so.
Sir DAWSON BATES: When I tell the
Committee that in the British Bill it
takes some six and a half pages to try to
get over some of the difficulties, I am sure
my hon. and learned Friend will agree
that it is not possible to deal with the
matter in eight lines.
Mr. CAMPBELL: After the speech
of the Minister I beg leave to withdraw
my Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Mr. McALLISTER: I beg to move,
after Clause 6, to insert :-
"Notwithstanding anything in any
enactment it shall be lawful for the
driver of a public service vehicle to
drive at a speed not exceeding thirty
miles per hour on any public highway.
Of course this Amendment refers to
public service vehicles of over two and a
half tons weight. Lest the reason for my
putting down this Amendment should be
misunderstood, I want to make it perfectly clear to the Committee that I hope
to give cogent and substantial reasons to
show that I have drawn it up for the purpose of ensuring the public safety, and
rendering the use of the road safer than
it is under existing circumstances both
for pedestrians and motorists-motor
drivers.
Road Traffic Bill-
Committee
16 DECEMBER 1929
The speed limit of twenty miles an hour
is absurd, it brings the law into contempt,
and everyone knows that these public
service vehicles do not run within that
speed limit. The consequence is that speed
traps are set up all over the country.
These police traps must necessarily be
selected on straight stretches of road
which are necessarily the safest portions
of the road for motor traffic. They are at
well defined points, and the consequence
is that drivers drive at a very moderate
speed at these places. Immediately they
get through them, in order to make up
for lost time, they proceed at a speed
far in excess of thirty miles an hour and
on the worst parts of the roads, round
bends. Another reason why I suggest
that a reasonable speed limit of thirty
miles an hour should be fixed in the
interests of public safety is this.
With the anxiety that these motor
drivers have in knowing that
third conviction their licence
suspended for a year, their means of
livelihood during that year gone, and their
wives and families perhaps in a state of
destitution during that time, they are inclined to concentrate their attention on
places along the highways where a police
trap may possibly be placed, instead of
concentrating on what is immediately in
front of them.
a
on
is
Therefore I say that raising the
present speed limit from twenty to thirty
miles an hour would be for the safety
not only of pedestrians but of those travelling in public service vehicles. With the
great improvement that has taken place
in recent years in the braking system,
control, and structure of these motor
vehicles, with four wheel brakes of the
mostly highly efficient kind now in use, I
suggest to the Committee that these
buses are as safe now when travelling at
thirty miles an hour as their predecessors
of four or five years ago were when
travelling at twenty miles an hour. I do
not intend to detain the Committee
further, but I would like my position
made clear by saying that this is a bona
fide and a genuine effort on my part to
improve motoring conditions and to render
the roads more safe for the public.
Sir DAWSON BATES: I have followed
the arguments of my hon. and learned
Friend with very great interest, and the
thought that occurred to me was this.
Supposing for one moment that the speed
limit was increased, would the driver not
still be looking out for a police ambush, and
if so, he would have more time to make
up. After all, we have gone considerably
in advance of Great Britain in this respect, because the speed limit for motor
buses in England at the present time is
only twelve miles an hour.
Mr. MCALLISTER: It is thirty miles
under the new Bill.
Sir DAWSON BATES: But at present
it is only twelve miles an hour. Therefore I am sure we will be given credit for
not being narrow-minded in this matter.
Under the 1926 Act we have power to
make regulations, and we made regulations increasing the speed limit to twenty
miles an hour. I am satisfied that at that
time it would have been unadvisable to
have increased the speed limit beyond that,
because there was no proper inspection of
these vehicles, and the brakes were not
as efficient as they are now. We intend
to alter the regulations and to increase the
speed limit, although to what extent I
would not like to say. I feel that on certain roads it would be very dangerous
to permit a speed of thirty miles an hour.
and on other broad concrete roads I can
easily imagine it might be safe to allow
an even higher rate than this. On the
other hand, there are certain places such
as Carrickfergus, where my hon. and
gallant Friend (Major Dobbs) lives, with
its narrow streets and peculiar turns.
where there ought to be a speed limit
But if this Amendment was passed the
speed limit through that town would
automatically become thirty miles an
hour, and hon. Members will realise the
absurdity of that. We do not want to
harass these public service vehicles which
have opened up the country and have done
so much for Northern Ireland in that connection, but it is useless to bring in a law
that a man cannot obey. If the hon.
Member will withdraw his Amendment
and allow me to continue as hitherto I
will carefully consider-in fact we have
already been considering-increasing the
speed limit by regulations, which will, of
course, come before the House.
Mr. McALLISTER: I can assure the
right hon. Gentleman that if the speed
limit is increased to a reasonable extent,
such as thirty miles an hour, on first class
roads, no one in this House will support
him and the Government more strongly
in the rigorous administration of the law
Road Traffic Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
[Mr. McAllister]
than I will. In the circumstances, and
in view of the statement of the right hon.
Gentleman I will withdraw the Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 7 (Fares and Rates Tribunal)
ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 8 (Power of Ministry of
Home Affairs to make general traffic regulations).
(1) The Ministry of Home Affairs
shall have power to make regulations
with respect to the use of vehicles of any
kind whatsoever on public highways
(including roadways to which the provisions of the principal Act apply),
and for the control of traffic, both vehicular and pedestrian, thereon, including
control by means of mechanically
worked signals.
Mr. LESLIE: For the convenience of
debate I beg to move to leave out the
whole of sub-clause (1) of Clause 8. As
will readily be seen the acceptance of
that proposal would destroy the whole
Clause, temporarily at all events, but that
can be dealt with again. There are, it
seems to me, several little difficulties in
this sub-clause, and the course of the debate will no doubt explain to us how they
ought to be dealt with, if they are to be
dealt with at all. When I sit down, and
after other hon. Members have spoken,
the right hon. Gentleman will no doubt
tell us what he thinks about it. The Bill,
as I understand it, is introduced with the
view of the safety and convenience of the
public. The Clause purports to do two
things. It gives the Ministry power to
make regulations with respect to the use
of the road, and secondly, it gives power
to control the traffic on the roads.
not very much concerned as to whether
the Minister has not rather more power
than he wants under this Clause because
if he has any power he does not want he
need not use it. I am much more concerned in seeing he has got every power
he does want and in seeing that the Clause
itself is workable.
I am
Beginning with the first part, that is
the use of public highways, I want to ask
first of all whether it is intended that
vehicles of any kind whatsoever should
include tramcars. I am inclined to think
that it does not.
I said on
that the Bill
with tramcars.
in
the Second reading
had nothing to do
I have been troubled
whether the
to
as
mind
my
expression vehicles here includes tramcars. If it does I think it ought to say so.
If it does include tramcars it seems to me
it must also include railway trains, and
I am certain it was not the intention of
the right hon. Gentleman to take power
to control the movement of railway trains
on those occasions when they do use the
public highway at level crossings. I think
that is a point that ought to be cleared up.
I do not see off-hand why the Minister
wants power under that Sub-clause to
make regulations in respect of the use
of highways by tramcars.
Sir DAWSON BATES: I think my
hon. Friend is mixing up two matters. A
tram, so far as this Bill is concerned,
comes entirely within it for the purpose
of traffic. May I give an example of a
case in which this Clause will be very useful? We put up one of those mechanical
signals
Mr. LESLIE: I am sorry. I had not
finished speaking.
Sir DAWSON BATES: It was only to
save time I answered the question.
The DEPUTY-CHAIRMAN: Perhaps
the hon. Gentleman will finish his speech
now.
Mr. LESLIE: I think it would be
better if line 40 is to include tramcars
that it said so. What about railway
trains? Does the right hon. Gentleman
want to take power in regard to level
crossings? When we come to the control
of traffic I think it is plain from what
the right hon. Gentleman has said that
we both approach this from the same point
of view. I take it that when a bell rings
the traffic up and down is to stand still.
That is what I take the regulation in the
proposal to be. Now we have to look and
see if we have it right. If tramcars are
included as vehicles, presumably vehicular
traffic includes tramcars, and there is no
further difficulty about that.
There is no difficulty about pedestrians,
but it seems to me there is control of
other vehicular traffic or pedestrians which
the right hon. Gentleman has not covered.
What I am thinking of-I pass over
freak traffic such as people on stilts, who
Road Traffic Bill-
Committee.
16 DECEMBER 1929
are perhaps pedestrians, and people on
roller skates or scooters, which are perhaps
vehicles is a herd of cattle or a flock of
sheep. And then there are people on
horseback. I would, therefore, be inclined to suggest that on the Report stage
we should be given something that really
does cover the points I have endeavoured
to make. For the moment I move that
the Sub-clause be deleted.
Sir DAWSON BATES: I am not quite
sure if hon. Friend has moved to delete
my
the Clause.
Mr. LESLIE: No, only Sub-clause 1.
Sir DAWSON BATES: I think he may
take my assurance that we have tried to
bring every class of vehicle in so far as
the regulation of traffic is concerned. As
regards animals, if they are domestic
animals, sheep and cows, they will be
in charge of individuals, and if drovers
of cows and other animals think they
are going to break the regulations in regard to the use of the streets they will
find they are entirely in the wrong.
I do not think my hon. Friend is quite
serious in regard to tramcars not being
vehicles. We are trying to cover everything, but it is impossible to bring into
a Clause every conceivable class of traffic.
We might have to bring in perambulators
and baby cars, and so on. It is really impossible to define it more than we have
done. That Clause was specially designed
for the purpose of meeting mechanical
signals as without it we would have no
power to deal with that matter.
I suggest to my hon. Friend that he
will let this Clause pass. As I said before,
we will have to introduce in the spring
another Bill, and if my hon. Friend finds
there is a certain class of traffic not
gathered up we will try to improve the
Clause. I do not for one moment suggest that this Bill is the last word. Far
be it from me to do that.
Mr. LESLIE: I am perfectly prepared
to postpone this until the spring. I beg
leave to withdraw my Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 8 (Power of the Ministry of
Home Affairs to make general traffic
regulations) ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
CLAUSE 9 (Short title and construction).
Question proposed, "That the Clause
stand part of the Bill."
Mr. LESLIE: In Clause 9, being the
last Clause in this Bill, one naturally
looks to see whether there is any information as to when the Act is to come into
operation. There is nothing said about
it, and it occurs to me that this is a
matter which had better be dealt with now
and not postponed until the spring.
Something should be done as regards
Clauses 2, 3 and 4 in the matter of
offences, and so forth. It strikes me
there may be some little difficulty unless
we can postpone those three Clauses, 2,
3 and 4, until 1st February, 1930. The
Royal Assent may be expected some time
before Christmas. You must give the
Act time to get going and use up any
offences that may have been committed
before a certain period. I would suggest
on the Report stage, I will not fire it
at the right hon. Gentleman now, that
we should say that the provisions of
Clauses 2, 3 and 4 should not apply in
respect of offences committed or alleged
to have been committed before 1st
February, 1930.
Sir DAWSON BATES: I really do
not quite see the advantage of the
suggestion made by my hon. Friend.
This Bill must commence some time,
and may I suggest the sooner the
better. We propose it should take
effect from the time the Royal Assent
is given. Offences are going on from day
to day, and if this is postponed until
1st February the same condition of affairs
will arise. There will be an interregnum
period. We propose the Bill should come
into operation from the time the Royal
Assent is given.
Mr. LESLIE: My proposal was to
avoid bringing in an interregnum, which
could be easily done.
Sir DAWSON BATES: Unfortunately
people are still being run over and motor
offences are being committed. There will
not be a clean sheet on 1st February.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
Title and Preamble agreed to.
Ordered: That the Bill be reported
without Amendment to the House.

17 DECEMBER 1929
THE SENATE.

Tuesday, 17th December, 1929.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. GLENDINNING
(Deputy-Speaker) in the Chair.
Paper Presented to Parliament.
Ministry of Finance:
Teachers (Agricultural and Technical)
Superannuation (Amendment) Scheme,
1929.
Laid before the House (pursuant to
Act), and ordered to lie on the Table.
Motor Vehicles and Road Traffic Bill.
Order for Third Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): I beg to move, "That this
Bill be now read the Third time."
Colonel PAKENHAM: Before this
Bill passes through this House, I should
like to offer a few observations, if I may,
on a subject of which we have heard a
good deal during the course of the debates
on this Measure-that is, the question of
third-party insurance. I think, from
what fell from the lips of the Minister of
Home Affairs yesterday, this question is
going to engage the thoughts of the Government from now on until the promised
amendment of this Bill takes place in the
early spring. I suggest that it might be
possible to ascertain by some means the
number of motorists in Northern Ireland
who have already effected third-party in-
I think it would be possible, on
a voluntary basis, to discover that.
surance.
If a notice of some kind or other arrived
at the house of a motor owner, and he was
insured against third-party risks, he
would be only too glad to say so. If it
arrived at the house of a man who had
no third-party insurance, it might induce
him to effect it, and at the worst all he
could do would be to throw the letter into
ter.
the waste-paper basket. It might be of
assistance to the Government if, by some
kind of circular, the Minister would elicit
that information. Of course it would be
done entirely on a voluntary basis, as
there is no power of compulsion at present
in Northern Ireland in regard to this mat-
The Government might discover in
that way the number of owners in
Northern Ireland who have effected thirdparty insurance. I suggest that the
Minister would discover that a very large
number of motor owners are already insured against third-party risks, and it
would be of some help to him to know that
there was this number. It might help
him on some questions that might arise
when it comes to a conference with the
insurance companies to effect some means
of doing this. I put this forward merely
as a suggestion that may help, and not
for
any other purpose.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I do not intend to
offer any opposition to the Third Reading,
but I should like to draw attention to the
constitution of the Tribunal that is to be
established under Clause 7 to deal with
fares and rates. The Tribunal is to consist of such three persons as may be
appointed by the Governor of Northern
Ireland, and shall have jurisdiction to fix
the fares to be charged for passengers and
the rates to be charged for luggage and
parcels conveyed by public service.
vehicles. We already know that
member of the Tribunal has been appointed, a
very distinguished legal
gentleman who is resident in England,
and I respectfully submit that it would
be a wise thing that the remaining two
members should be selected from the Province of Northern Ireland.
one
Another matter that I wish to draw
attention to is this, this entire subject of
Motor Vehicle and Road Traffic legislation
will require at no distant date to be
brought thoroughly up-to-date in one
Bill.
Mr. LESLIE: Arising out of what has
just been said, I should like to emphasise
and to support what was said by the hon.
and gallant Senator on my left (Colonel
Pakenham) when speaking on the Second
Reading, and that is, the suggestion that
the other members of the Tribunal should
not be persons in any way connected with
the Ministry of Home Affairs.
Road Traffic Bill
Unemployment
THE SENATE
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I can only
say that I will convey the suggestions
made by the hon. and gallant Senator
(Colonel Pakenham), and by the hon. and
learned Senator opposite (Mr. Leslie), to
my right hon. Friend, the Minister of
Home Affairs.
On Question, Motion ("That the Bill
be now read the Third time') agreed to.
CLAUSE 9.-(Short Title and Construction).
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move,
in page
8, line 20, to insert as a new subsection:
(1) The provisions of sections two,
three and four of this Act shall
not apply in the case of any
offence committed, or alleged to
have been committed, before the
first day of February, nineteen hundred and thirty, but, save as aforesaid,
this Act shall come into operation on
the passing thereof."
Referring to the Amendment standing
in my name on the Paper, I have put it
down in pursuance of the intimation
which I gave during the Committee Stage
yesterday, in order that we might get a
definite assurance from the Government,
particularly in regard to the penal clauses
3, 4 and 5, that no difficulty of any sort
can possibly arise if this Bill comes into
operation immediately after its final stage.
Clause 2, as we know, gives the jury a new
power, Clause 3 takes away the old offence
of being drunk in charge of a motor car,
and substitutes a new offence, and Clause
4 provides for a differently constituted tribunal of summary jurisdiction. It is
obvious, I think, that the offences will go
on during whatever interval there may
be, and the difficulty is to know where
to draw the line. Accordingly, I suggest
that the dividing line should not be the
date of the actual or alleged offence, but
the 1st February next. As the House
knows, the Winter Assizes are being held
in Londonderry. We have no knowledge.
of what cases are going to be heard there.
We cannot forecast what may happen to
some of those cases they may be adjourned to the subsequent assizes; we do
not know.
There may be summonses pending for
offences, or occasion for summonses may
arise between this afternoon and the day
of the final stage. Again, cases may be
partly heard and adjourned, and so on.
A difficulty of something in the same
nature arose in 1905 in a well-known case
under the Prevention of Cruelty to
Children Act of 1904, which was the
statute then in operation. It did not relate to an Englishman, although it did
relate to a British subject. That is the
only case that has in any way a bearing
on the subject. It seems to me that, if
the House decides not to accept this
Amendment, we ought at least to receive
a definite assurance from the Government
that no inconvenience can possibly arise,
and we ought also to have the acceptance
by the Government of the responsibility
for any inconvenience, however unexpected, that may in fact arise in the
meantime. I beg to move.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: This Bill
will not receive the Royal Assent before
the middle of January, which is very near
the date named by the hon. and learned
Senator. I think the interval will give
ample time for all the necessary arrangements to be made. The various matters
contained in this Bill have been so widely
discussed, and such wide publicity has
been given to them, that I do not think
anybody will be taken by surprise. I
hope, therefore, that the Amendment will
be withdrawn on that ground.
Mr. LESLIE: It being understood that
the responsibility of this matter rests entirely with the Government, I ask leave
to withdraw the Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Moved: "That the Bill do now pass.'
-(Viscount Charlemont).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
And a Message was sent to the House
of Commons to acquaint them that the
Senate have agreed to the said Bill, without any Amendment.
UNEMPLOYMENT.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, "That this House do now adjourn
until Thursday next," when the Royal
Assent will be given to a number of Bills.
Mr. CAMPBELL: Before the House
adjourns, I should like to take the opportunity of directing attention to what, in
my opinion, is the regrettable failure of
Unemployment
Unemployment
17 DECEMBER 1929
the Government to take effective steps to
deal with the widely prevailing evil of
unemployment in Northern Ireland. The
unemployment figures are up compared
with what they were a few months ago.
The noble Lord, the Leader of the House,
stated yesterday that there are about
38,000 people at the present time, according to the Employment Exchanges, either
wholly or partially unemployed. Those
figures do not represent the total of the
workless population of this Province.
Only about 1,700 persons are employed
directly and indirectly in Government relief schemes. I do not recollect that
during the Recess of Parliament or since
the present session was continued, the
Government have taken any decisive, any
big, step towards solving this terrible
problem.
If they have I am not aware of it at
the moment. Contrast that with the position across the water. According to wellfounded statements the Government there
are making an effort, and a strong effort, to
deal with the unemployment problem. I
admit that there also the unemployment
figures are up, but in their case there is
a will, apparently, to deal with the position in a broad statesmanlike spirit. They
have under contemplation schemes which
involve an expenditure of £7,000,000 on
public activities, chiefly railways;
£11,000,000 on local government expenditure, £10,000,000 on trunk roads, and
£28,000,000 on other roads, making in
all a
sum of £56,000,000. Of course
these figures are altogether beyond the
capacity of Northern Ireland, and nobody
would dream of applying them here. I
know perfectly well that the resources
of Northern Ireland are limited,
although according to the expenditure in
some directions one would think otherwise.
They are, however, in England making an
effort to deal with this problem. They
mean business in this matter. They are
putting their shoulders to the clogged
wheel of unemployment, and I say it is
regrettable that we here seem to be marking time. If they fail in England it will
not be for want of will to succeed.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The problem of unemployment is a matter that
equally concerns the Government across
the water and our own. It is an extremely difficult problem to solve as the
hon. and learned Senator knows. Although the Imperial Government have
these schemes under consideration the fact
remains that the unemployment figures
in England are up at the present moment,
as also are our own. Whatever may be
the ultimate result of these schemes-and
everybody hopes the result will be good
-the present situation is that the unemployment figures have not changed in the
least except for the worse. At the same
time I can promise the House that the
Government of Northern Ireland have the
problem always before them, and they will
not relax their efforts to secure some
amelioration of the present conditions.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at
Twenty-five minutes before Four o'clock until
Thursday, 19th December.

14 JANUARY 1930
THE SENATE.

Tuesday, 26th February, 1929.
The Fifth Session of the Second Parliament of Northern Ireland, appointed to
meet 26th February, 1929, in the Nineteenth year in the reign of King George
V, was opened by Commission, the Commissioners being their Excellencies, the
Lord Chief Justice, Lord Justice Andrews
and Colonel the Right Hon. R. D. Percival Maxwell, D.S.Ö., D.L.
The House met at Twelve of the Clock,
Mr. SPEAKER in the Chair.
KING'S SPEECH.
His Excellency the Lord Chief Justice,
being seated on the Throne, commanded
the Gentleman Usher of the Black Rod
to desire the Commons to attend
immediately in this House.
The Commons being at the Bar with
their Speaker, His Excellency the Lord
Chief Justice said:-My Lords and
Gentlemen, I have it in command from
His Grace the Governor to read to you
His Grace's Speech on the opening of this
Session of Parliament.
His Excellency then read the following
Speech from the Throne.
Members of the Senate and of the House
of Commons.
It is with profound relief and a deep
feeling of thankfulness to Divine Providence that I am able to record a steady
improvement in the health of His
Majesty the King. In no part of His
Majesty's Empire has there been
evinced greater anxiety throughout His
protracted illness or more heartfelt
gratification at the progress being made
towards recovery than by His loyal
subjects in Northern Ireland.
I observe with satisfaction that the
improvement in the shipbuilding industry continues, and that although
the linen trade has not recovered from
a protracted period of depression its
condition during the past few months
has been somewhat better.
I am pleased to learn that my
Government has entered into an agreement with the British Government for
placing upon a permanent basis the
arrangement made in 1926 assimilating
the burdens on the Exchequers of the
United Kingdom and of Northern Ireland in respect of Unemployment Insur-
This agreement will require
ratification by both Parliaments and
the necessary measure will be introduced in due course.
ance.
A
King's Speech.
King's Speech.
THE SENATE
I shall observe with interest the results of the change in the law relating
to Health Insurance which came into
operation at the beginning of this year
extending the free period of insurance
of persons after they cease to be insurably employed, and enabling those who
are insured and are over 60 years of age
to qualify more easily for Contributory
Old Age Pensions. Other provisions
permit insured persons who cannot, by
reason of poor health, gain admission
to an Approved Society to participate
more fully in the benefits provided by
the Health Insurance Scheme.
The continued development of the
education system of Northern Ireland is
very gratifying and the striking increase in the numbers of the pupils attending secondary schools in particular,
bears witness to marked progress in the
extent to which the facilities provided
by the Education Act are taken advantage of by the public.
It is satisfactory to note that the
steady upward movement in the numbers of live stock which has taken place
since 1925 was substantially accelerated
in 1928; that there was also a considerable increase in the export of
cattle, sheep and poultry in that period;
and that the area under tillage was
over 11,000 acres above the corresponding figure for 1927.
Proposals will be laid before you for
the abolition of Proportional Representation in its application to elections to
the House of Commons of Northern Ireland, and for the adoption of the principle of single-member constituencies.
Proposals will also be laid before you
for relieving productive industry of a
substantial proportion of the rates
which it at present has to bear in connection with the cost of Local Government services and for exempting agricultural land and farm buildings
tirely from this burden.
Bills dealing with the following
matters will be submitted for
consideration:
of
your
Nursing and
Registration
Maternity Homes,
The storage and transport of
Petroleum,
The amendment of the Law relating to Bankruptcy,
To empower the Royal Ulster Constabulary to exercise the powers
of inspection under Part I of the
Weeds and Agricultural Seeds
Act, 1909,
re-
The amendment of the Law
lating to Industrial and Provident Societies,
The amendment of the Law in
connection with the control of
Licensing of Public Service
Vehicles,
To prevent the employment of
School children in connection
with betting operations,
Town Planning in Urban Areas;
and Unemployment Insurance.
Members of the House of Commons.
The Estimates for the Public Services
will be laid before you in due course.
You will be asked to make provision
for appropriate recoupment to the local
rating authorities of the rates in respect
of which industry and agriculture will
be exempt.
The publicity campaign organised by
the Linen Industry and assisted by substantial grants from public funds has
heen extended to cover the Home and
Dominion markets in addition to those
of the United States to which it was
confined in its initial stages. While it
is yet too early to gauge the results
which may attend the extension of the
campaign to the Home and Dominion
markets there is no reason to believe
that they will prove less encouraging
than those which have already disclosed
themselves in the case of the United
States. You will, accordingly, he
asked to vote an additional sum
wards this object for the year 1929-
1930.
to-
An adequate sum will be placed on
the estimates to establish and maintain
a Training Centre associated with a
new Juvenile Employment Exchange at
which classes will be held for the benefit
of young persons who are out of work.
Papers Presented.
Papers Presented.
26 FEBRUARY 1929
Members of the Senate and of the House
of Commons.
My Government will continue to give
sympathetic and unremitting attention
to all practicable measures for assisting
agriculture and industry; stimulating
trade and promoting employment.
In addition to the action which has
already been taken to achieve these
ends I trust that the proposals indicated for relieving agriculture and productive industry from the burden of
rates and the provision of a Juvenile
Training Centre will be of great benefit
to the whole community.
And I pray that the blessing of
Almighty God may rest upon your
deliberations.
House adjourned during pleasure.
House resumed.

Local Bills.
Mr. SPEAKER acquainted the House
that the Clerk of the Senate had laid
upon the Table the certificates from the
Examiner that the Standing Orders
applicable to the following Bills had been
complied with:--
Londonderry Corporation.
Bangor (County Down) Improvement.
