FREIGHT REBATES-DRIED
SUGAR-BEET PULP.
The following Question stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie:
To ask the Leader of the House
whether dried sugar-beet pulp is, or can
be, included among the feeding stuffs in
respect of which railway companies are
required to give freight rebates.
As
Mr. LESLIE: I think the question on
the Paper almost explains itself.
the House knows, under the provisions of
what is called the derating scheme,
which has Parliamentary sanction under
the Appropriation Act, rebates are now
being given on freight transport for certain commodities in anticipation of what
is to be done when the derating proposals
become law. Amongst the commodities
which receive rebates which have to be
passed on are feeding stuffs for live stock
and poultry feeding, including Indian
meal, oil cake, whole, broken, or ground,
dry brewers' and distillers' grains, and
malt culms, compound cakes and meals;
but dried sugar-beet pulp, which is now
being used, I understand, a good deal
for feeding livestock, and which contains
albumenoids, carbo-hydrates, and other
things which feeding stuffs ought to contain in some quantity, does not share in
the benefits extended to the other commodities I have mentioned. Accordingly
it occurred to me that we might ask for
information as to whether it could be
included amongst the feeding stuffs entitled to rebate.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): With regard to the feeding
stuffs which are included as subjects for
rebate under the scheme, I would refer
the hon. and learned Senator to the
White Paper (Cmd. 88), which was
issued towards the end of November last
when a supplementary Estimate was
taken to provide the money required to
finance the scheme. The list of feeding
stuffs set out in the Schedule to the
White Paper does not include dried sugarbeet pulp. I may say that the list of
traffics for relief was drawn up after the
most careful consideration, and the
feeding stuffs selected are those whose
inclusion in the scheme is calculated to
benefit most the agricultural industry.
The hon. and learned Senator, no
doubt, realizes that the amount of money
available for the purposes of the freight
rebates scheme is strictly limited, being
directly related to the amount of rating
relief which the railways and other transport concerns will receive under the derating scheme. In order that the scheme
may be really effective it is essential to
maintain at a reasonably high figure the
percentage rebate allowable on each
selected traffic, and, consequently, the
number of traffics included in the scheme
have been limited to the more important
only.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: Arising out of
the reply given by the Leader of the
House, may I ask if it is not a fact that
there are no sugar-beet factories in Northern Ireland, and that none of those in
existence either in the Free State or
elsewhere can exist without a large subsidy from the Government.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The answer
to both those questions is in the
affirmative. There are no sugar-beet
factories that I know of in the North of
Ireland, and no sugar-beet factory can
exist at present without a subsidy from
the Government.
LIQUID EGGS FROM CHINA.
The following Question also stood on
the Order Paper in the name of Mr.
Leslie:-
To ask the Leader of the House
whether any information can be given as
to the quantity and value of liquid eggs
imported into Northern Ireland from
China during recent years, and as to the
Oral Answers.
Oral Answers.

THE SENATE
precautions taken to ensure that such
eggs are fit for use in the preparation of
food for human beings.
Mr. LESLIE: The second question
standing in my name deals with a subject
that has been before this House more
than once. The opinion seems to be
gaining ground that the use of liquid
eggs imported into Northern Ireland from
China is on the increase, that those liquid
eggs are usually produced under the most
unsanitary conditions, and that they are
not at all the kind of ingredient which
should be used in the preparation of food.
I understand that these liquid eggs are
used principally for glazing buns and
tarts and things of that sort sold in confectioners' shops, and it is thought that
this is not a very desirable practice.
It is also thought that if the quantity
imported is great-and my information.
is that it runs into six or seven figuresthe use of this substance is really inimical
to the interests of the egg producers of
Northern Ireland. Whether there are
any figures available I do not know, and
I am of course aware that we labour
under some restrictions as regards what
we can do in the way of preventing the
importation of undesirable products, but
still, if anything can be done either to
restrict importation or, at all events, to
regulate the quality of the article imported I think it might be of advantage.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I regret
that separate figures as to the importation
of liquid eggs from China into Northern
Ireland are not available. The statistics
collected at the time of the arrival of
consignments in the United Kingdom do
not disclose the ultimate destination of
the liquid eggs, and the information
available as to eggs landed in Northern
between
Ireland makes no distinction
eggs in shell and liquid eggs. I understand that the Ministry of Agriculture is
endeavouring, in co-operation with the
Ministry of Commerce, to ascertain
whether machinery cannot be devised
which would enable the particulars of the
liquid egg imports of Northern Ireland
to be shown separately.
I am arranging to supply in the Official
Report some figures as to the quantities
and estimated value of imports of liquid
eggs into the United Kingdom for the
years 1924-1928, which may be of some
help to the hon. and learned Member.
As regards the second part of the hon.
and learned Member's question, liquid
eggs imported into Northern Ireland are
liable at any time to inspection by a
medical officer of health under the Public
Health Acts and Regulations made thereunder.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD:
Arising out of that answer I should like
to ask the noble Viscount whether he does
not agree that the importation of these
Chinese eggs should be stopped altogether, because when I was in Siberia,
and also in China a few years ago, an
egg was not considered of any value there
until it was six months old. I understand that the liquid eggs imported into
the United Kingdom from China are
used in the bakeries of Belfast and other
places, and I think the question of prohibiting their importation ought to be
seriously considered.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am unable to add anything to the answer I
have given, but I will bring the points
mentioned to the notice of the Minister
concerned.
The following is the statement referred to:-

Bill." (Viscount Charlemont.)

In Committee accordingly.
[The MARQUESS OF DUFFERIN
AND AVA in the Chair].
CLAUSES 1 (Alteration of method of
voting at contested Parliamentary elections), 2 (Redistribution of seats), 3
(Re-arrangement of polling districts and
places, and removal of doubts), 4 (Returning officer and place of election in
county constituency), 5 (Alteration of
scale of election expenses), 6 (Minor
amendments of election laws), 7 (Commencement of Act and first elections),
and 8 (Repeal and short title), ordered
to stand part of the Bill.
FIRST SCHEDULE agreed to.
SECOND SCHEDULE agreed to.

Ordered: That the Bill be reported
without Amendment to the House.
Ordered: That the House do now resume.

Bill reported without Amendment.

received." (Viscount Charlemont.)

Bill ordered to be read the Third time
to-morrow.
OPERATION OF MOTOR
OMNIBUSES.
Viscount
CHARLEMONT: Before
moving the Adjournment I should like to
give a reply to the questions which were
put to me on the Adjournment of the
House on Thursday last with regard to
the operation of motor omnibuses.
I
have made inquiries on the subject from
my right hon. Friend the Minister of
Home Affairs.
He informs me that one of the conditions upon which route cards are being
issued to motor omnibuses is that they
lodge with the Ministry a time-table
showing the hours at which their buses
are scheduled to run, and the Ministry
has informed the operators that they must
run their vehicles strictly to these timetables. He asks me to state that the
Ministry of Home Affairs will at once
take up any case which comes to their
knowledge of owners deviating from the
time-table which has been lodged.
I should point out that before it was
possible to issue route cards a great deal
of preliminary work had to be done, but
the Ministry is now issuing these cards,
and will be in a position to deal with any
case where time-tables are not being
adhered to.
With regard to the point raised by
Senator Hill Dickson, it is the duty of
the police to deal with any cases of obstruction which may occur. So far as
the Ministry is aware, these buses do not
remain stationary for any considerable
period on the county roads, and although
there may be isolated cases of buses taking up their stand in an inconvenient
position the Ministry has no reason to
believe that there is any serious obstruction of traffic. It must be borne in mind
that these bus services constitute a great
convenience to the public, and although
there may be inconvenience in certain districts caused by them it cannot be denied
that this inconvenience is small as compared with the great benefits which they
confer upon the community.
I
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM:
desire to thank the noble Leader of the
House for the information he has given,
but I am sorry to find that apparently
no attempt is being made to insist on the
omnibus time-tables being strictly adhered to. Evidently it is a matter that
is going to be attended to in the future,
but not at the present time. It is a pity
that the people in the country districts
are to be subject to the inconvenience
they suffer at the present time.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I do not
think the answer I gave would convey
that impression at all. I said the
Ministry is now issuing these route cards
and will be in a position to deal with
any cases in which the time-tables are
not being strictly adhered to.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: I
understand from the noble Viscount's
Adjournment-
Business of the House.
THE SENATE
[Colonel Sharman Crawford]
answer that it rests with the individual
to bring to the notice of the Ministry of
Home Affairs any case where the timetable is not being adhered to.
Viscount CHARLEMONT indicated
assent.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: I thank the
noble Viscount for the information he
has given in reply to the question I
asked.
Adjournment.
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
I beg to
move, "That this House
do
now
adjourn."
Mr. LESLIE: May I ask whether it is
proposed to sit on Thursday?
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I do not
think so.
Colonel SHARMAN
CRAWFORD:
May I ask the noble Leader of the House
whether there is any important business
likely to arise before the dissolution of
this Parliament?
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
I think
I will be in a better position to answer
that question a little later on.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at
Twenty-five minutes before Four o'clock.

Bangor (County Down) Improvement
Bill.

To move, "That the Senators following
be named of the Joint Opposed Committee
on the Bangor (County Down) Improvement Bill:-
Mr. Joseph Cunningham,
Mr. J. G. Leslie,

Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of

Belfast Water Bill.
Mr. LONG: In the unavoidable absence of Senator Hill Dickson, I beg to
move, "That the Belfast Water Bill be

Timbey's Divorce Bill.
Brought from the House of Commons,
and read a First time.
AMENDMENT OF THE EDUCATION
ACTS.
The following question stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Murphy:
To ask the Leader of the House
whether he can give any information
as to the manner in which the Government propose to meet the demand made
for an Amendment of the Education
Acts (Northern Ireland), 1923 and
1925.
Mr. MURPHY: I put this question
to the Leader of the House with
the object of ascertaining whether
the Government are as yet in a position
to allay some of the anxiety which undoubtedly exists as to the future operation of the Education Acts. I fully
recognise the differences of opinion that
exist as to many of the questions that
have been raised recently with regard to
those Acts, and I realise that the Government is bound to give the most careful
consideration to the various problems that
are now arising. But I would venture to
suggest that with regard to two main
principles there is not very much room
for disagreement. The first is that the
Bible teaching, which is at present being
given so well in all the schools by the
teachers should be continued as long as
the parents of the pupils in those schools
desire its continuance. The second is that
the possibility of the appointment of a
Roman Catholic teacher to a Protestant
school, if such appointment is contrary to
the wishes of the majority of the parents
and of those who have transferred the
school, should be obviated.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I welcome
this question, as it affords me an opportunity to dispose of any feelings that
may exist that the Government refuses
to consider that any suggested Amendment of the Education Act is desirable
or possible. There is no foundation for
such an assumption. Hon. Members,
will, no doubt, have seen that in the
Memorandum recently submitted to the
Government by the Committee of the
Protestant Churches and the Grand
Orange Lodge there are two distinct
series of proposals.
Oral Answers.
Oral Answers
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
One deals with the fear of some of the
Protestant community that at some
future time teachers might be
appointed to schools whose principles
were not in harmony with those of the
parents whose children attended the
schools; and also with the fear that in
the future the Bible teaching which is
now being given might be discontinued.
The other proposals are concerned with
the actual administration of the Education Act, and the principal ones advocate
that a greater share in this administration should be given to the representatives of educational and religious
interests.
Now, taking the first series of proposals those relating to religious questions as opposed to administrative ones--
the Government desire to give the
strongest assurance that they intend
both to safeguard the schools from any
possibility of interference in the manner.
feared and will ensure that nothing
shall interrupt the daily Bible instruction which is so efficiently carried out by
the teachers, and has been so carried
out since the Education Act was placed
on the Statute Book.
Hon. Members will, however, realize
the difficulties which lie in the designing
of legislation which is required to meet
hypothetical cases, and for that reason
I am not yet prepared to indicate how
the Education Act might be amended
or enlarged to meet the end desired,
but I wish to repeat in unequivocal
language that the Government intend
to bring in such legislation as will, I
hope, allay permanently the fears which
have been aroused.
Now, I should like to refer briefly to
the changes which are advocated in the
administration of the Act, some of which,
I may say, are of a very far-reaching
nature. I think that hon. Members will
agree that before any alteration of the
Act is determined on in these respects it
is preferable, if possible, that agreement
should be reached between those who
advocate change and those who for
years have been engaged in carrying out
the provisions of the Act.
Hon. Members will, no doubt, have
noticed in the Press that there are considerable differences of opinion in the
matter, and for that reason among
others any idea of hasty action on the
part of the Government is much to be
deprecated. No Government worthy of
the name should be rushed into legislation before it has heard the considered
opinion of all parties engaged in the
controversy, and has been given time
to weigh these opinions in the balance
before arriving at a decision.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: I
rise to make a remark on the second part
of the question put by the hon and learned
Senator, whom we are all delighted to
hear speak for the first time in this House.
I happen to be the Chairman of the
Association of County Councils of Northern Ireland, and I am also an ex-chairman of the Down County Council. There
is to be a meeting this week of the Association of County Councils, and it is probable that a resolution will be put forward dealing with the matter I am about
to refer to.
From the passing of the Education Act,
the county councils, as representing the
ratepayers, have been responsible for
finding the necessary funds to provide and
maintain the schools, and the county
councils would very much deprecate any
amendment of the Education Act which
would weaken the powers they already
possess under the Act. As I have said,
the county councils have to raise the
money, and they are strongly of the
opinion that nothing should be introduced
in any Amending Bill which would either
limit or take away their powers and give
them to other people who, although they
may be interested in education, do not
have to find any of the money for financing the scheme. Therefore, I trust that
nothing will be done to weaken the
powers of the county councils who have
done their duty in the past. To use a
common expression, those who pay the
fiddler should have a right to call the
tune.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: Might
I put a supplementary question to the
noble Leader of the House? I would
like to know if he can give the House an
assurance that Protestant children, who
are compelled to attend a Roman Catholic school, will not be forced to learn the
Irish language. It may seem somewhat
amusing, but only last week a clergyman
stated at a meeting of the Grand Orange
Lodge that thirteen children in the parish
of which he was minister were compelled
to go to a Roman Catholic school, and
they were there forced to learn the Irish
Third Reading

153 Voting and Redistribution Bill-
10 APRIL 1929
language. He also stated that although
he had made application for the necessary
transport facilities to take them to a Protestant school further away, that transport was refused. I would like to have
an assurance that in the case of Protestant children being compelled to attend
a Roman Catholic school, they will not be
forced to learn the Irish language.
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
No one
in the North of Ireland is compelled to
learn the Irish language, and I can certainly give the assurance asked for. So
long as this Government is in existence
I do not suppose such a thing would
I would like the hon.
be possible.
Senator to give me details of the case he
referred to, because I am at a loss to
understand how it could occur.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM:
will let you have the details.
Mr. MURPHY rose.
Mr. SPEAKER:
I
The hon. Senator is not entitled to speak a second
time.
House of Commons (Method of Voting
and Redistribution of Seats) Bill.
Order for Third Reading read.
I beg
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
to move, "That this Bill be now read
the Third time."
in
the
I rise to oppose
Mr. DORMAN:
the Third Reading of this Bill, and my
reason for doing so is that I believe the
existing system of election by Proportional Representation has accomplished
all that
Ireland
Northern
founders of that system expected would
result from its adoption in this area.
In examining the return of the Members elected for the various divisions of
Belfast at the last election I find that
in no division was the Government deprived of a majority of the representatives, and that including amongst the
Government representatives the Independent Unionists the Government has
at present a majority of approximately
forty per cent. of the Members in another
place.
During the last few years, since the
close of the War, many of the ancient
historic dynasties of Europe have fallen
and the statesmen of Europe have found
it necessary to establish a number of
new States, States that have been made
up of various races in which different
religious and political prejudices prevail. In their wisdom the statesmen of
Europe considered that the best method
of founding those States was to establish
them under a system of Proportional
Representation whereby the minorities
in all these countries would have a
representareasonable opportunity of
tion, and so that equal justice would be
done to the aspirations of all sections of
the people of those countries.
We in Northern Ireland occupy a peculiar position in this respect. Our history
has been peculiar. The agrarian, religious, industrial, and political history of
this particular area would lead one to believe that no system of election could
bring about the fulfilment of the aspirations of all sections of the people so well
as the existing method of election by
P.R. It would be interesting to go into
the history of this particular area from
these four points of view, but this is not
the place to do that. Suffice it to say
that in the history of the world there is
probably one country whose geography is
known to probably every hon. Member of
this House, and whose history, from
ancient times, is very much like the religious and political history of Northern.
Ireland. The result of that history was
that there had grown up in that community a feeling of religious prejudice
and enmity between the people that resulted in the disastrous position that is
well known as the Jews having no dealings with the Samaritans.
From that point of view I think the
abolition of P.R. will be more or less
disastrous, so far as the mass of the
is conpeople of this particular area
cerned. There is no doubt about it that
that inherited religious division has been
disastrous to the economic welfare of the
people of this Province. I will refer to
just one particular instance, from the
industrial point of view, to show what I
mean by that. About a hundred years ago
the skilled artisans in our great linen industry in Northern Ireland were working
for a wage which was anything between
one-third and one-half the wage paid to
A comsimilar artisans in Scotland.
mittee was set up here, I believe in the
thirties of the last century, to inquire
how it was that the rate of remuneration
paid to workers in Northern Ireland was
Voting and Redistribution Bill-
Business of the House
THE SENATE
[Mr. Dorman]
only about fifty per cent. of the rate paid
in Scotland for similar work. One of
the employers who appeared before that
committee gave as the only reason the
fact that the working classes of Northern
Ireland were divided into orange and
green, and that, because they were divided
into orange and green--and he used, if my
memory serves me right, the words, "the
'Prod' had nothing to do with the Pope"
-they refused to associate together for
the purpose of protecting their economic
interests.
From that point of view I think it
would be disastrous if the present
system of electing Members of Parliament was abolished and the old hatred
and ill-will was again introduced into
the political arena. The political war
flags are just now being unfurled, and
with one-member constituencies the possibility is that appeals will be made to
those inherited prejudices and passions
more readily than they could be made
if the elections were carried out under
the system of P.R.
I think the results of the last election,
whereby every shade of political opinion
obtained representation, especially in
the City of Belfast, fully justified P.R.
In North Belfast the Unionists had two
candidates returned, the Independents
one, and the Socialists one. West Belfast returned one Unionist, one Independent, one Labour, and one
Nationalist. In East Belfast two Union-
Coates, Sir Willem F.
Armstrong, Mr. H. B.
Barclay, Mr. William
Char.emont, Viscount
Clark, Sir George S.
CONTENTS.
ists were returned, with one Independent and one Socialist. The probability
is that in the coming elections these
minority parties will not secure representation in acordance with their numbers in the community. In addition to
that, the system of single-member constituencies makes it possible for a very
small number of electors to have a
majority in another
another place. I was
surprised, in looking over the figures of
the last general election in England, to
find that the present Unionist Government, with probably the largest
majority in the history of the Imperial
Parliament, really represented a minority of the electors to the extent of
over 800,000. For these reasons I feel
it my duty to oppose this Measure, and
to exercise my right to divide the House.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Having
heard the opinion of the House as a
whole in favour of this Bill I do not
think it is necessary for me to go into
it further. The Bill is desired by the
majority of the people of Ulster, and I
think it will work out perfectly fairly to
the minority. I believe that Independent Members will have an even better
chance of being returned under the
system of single-member seats than they
had under P.R.

Local Bills-Introduction in next
Session.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): I beg to move,
That the promoters of the Local Bills
which have been introduced into this
House in the present Session of Parliament shall have leave to introduce the
same in the next Session of Parliament, provided that notice of their intention to do so be lodged in the Library not later than three o'clock on
Tuesday, April 16th, and that all fees
due thereon, up to that period, be paid.
That such Bills shall be deposited in
the Library not later than three o'clock
on or before the third day on which the
House shall sit after the next meeting
of Parliament, with a declaration annexed thereto, signed by the agent,
stating that the Bill is the same in
every respect as the Bill at the last
stage of the proceedings thereon in this
House in the present Session.
That the proceedings on the Bills
shall be pro forma only in regard to the
stage through which the
same has
passed in the present Session; and that
no new fees be charged in regard to this
stage.
Mr. LESLIE: The Motion of the
noble Viscount is, I am sure, strictly in
accordance with precedents that have been
set in the Imperial Parliament, and to
some extent in accordance with precedents
we set ourselves during, I think, the last
Session but one, but as we have not had
much experience here in these matters I
would ask for an assurance that for this
purpose the expression "Local Bill" in-
Icludes a Divorce Bill. It is a matter
which is entirely within our own control,
but I think we ought to have the assurance for which I have asked. I presume
also that although this House is not
affected by a dissolution otherwise than in
a suspensory sense, the Motion will apply
to the reproduction of these Bills in a
Session of the ensuing Parliament if a dissolution should take place between the
present date and the time of re-introduction.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The answer
is in the affirmative.
On Question, Motion agreed to.

161 Unemployment Insurance Bill-
11 APRIL 1929

Second Reading.
Unemployment Insurance (Transitional
Provisions Amendment) Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am glad
to have the privilege of moving the Second
Reading of this Bill, which may, I think,
be regarded as another of the many proofs
which the Government has given from
time to time of the very real concern
which it feels for the members of the community who are in the unfortunate position of
of being unable, through the
depression of trade, to find employment.
This House recently passed an Act
which confirmed an agreement with the
Imperial Government in regard to the
re-insurance of the Unemployment
Insurance Funds. That agreement makes
it possible for the Government of Northern Ireland to maintain the same
standards as in Great Britain in the
matter of payment of benefits to insured
workers suffering from unemployment.
The Bill, which I hope will now receive
its Second Reading, will secure that conformity to British standards.
It will be within the recollection of
hon. Members that the Unemployment
Insurance Act of 1928 reconstructed to a
certain extent the conditions governing
Unemployment Insurance, and amongst
other things provided that a statutory
condition for the receipt of benefit would
be that the claimant had 30 contributions
in the previous two years. This alteration was made on the recommendation of
the Blanesborough Committee, which included representatives of all parties. The
Committee, in suggesting the new condition, recognised that it would be unreasonable to enforce it whilst trade conditions
continued to be so abnormal as they were.
They further recommended, therefore,
that a period should be allowed to elapse
before enforcement, and that during such
period modified conditions should be
arranged which would enable applicants
to draw benefit without showing that they
had the necessary 30 contributions to their
credit. The transitional conditions introduced in the Act of 1928 were briefly as
follows: In the first place the claimant had
to prove that at least eight contributions
were paid during the previous two years,
or not less than 30 contributions at any
time; secondly, it had to be shown that
he was normally employed in an insurable
occupation; and, lastly, it was made a condition that the claimant had during the
previous two years a reasonable amount
of insurable employment.
These transitional conditions were applicable to all claimants over 18 years of
age, but were limited in their operation
to a period which, according to varying
circumstances, may terminate for different workers any time between the 19th
of this month and the 19th April, 1930.
At the present moment there are a number of persons drawing benefit in Northern
Ireland under the transitional conditions
who would, if the Act of 1928 is allowed
to continue unamended, cease to be entitled to benefit at the 19th instant.
The Bill which the Government asks
this House to pass into law extends the
transitional period for a further twelve
months. Accordingly, the transitional
conditions will be continued in the case of
all adults until the 19th April, 1930, at
least, so that no adult claimant will be
denied benefit solely on the grounds that
he or she has not had 30 contributions in
the previous two years. The Bill is a very
simple one, dealing with the one point
only which I have explained to the House.
I beg to move.
I
Mr. DORMAN: In the first place I
wish to thank the noble Viscount for the
very clear exposition he has given the
House of the provisions of this Bill.
shall welcome it being placed on the
Statute Book, as it will be calculated to
alleviate the position of numerous heads
of families in Northern Ireland-
Mr. SPEAKER: Will the hon. Member please speak up?
Mr. DORMAN--who undoubtedly
during this month would be deprived of
unemployment benefit. I look forward
to the time when the Unemployment Insurance Act will be amended so as to give
statutory right to receive benefit to all
unemployed persons.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM:
While we all regret that this legislation
is necessary, and that it is necessary to
arrange for an extension of the transitional period, at the same time I am sure
every one of us feels that it is only proper
that such an arrangement should be made
to tide the workers over the difficult period
that is likely to exist between now and
the time when the workers will be able to
fulfil the normal conditions for receiving
unemployment benefit. There are a con-
D
163 Unemployment Insurance Bill-

THE SENATE
Adjournment
[Mr. Joseph Cunningham]
siderable number of workers who would
find it impossible to comply with the conditions imposed by the Act of 1928. They
would consequently be deprived of benefit,
and that would entail tremendous hardship not only on the workman but on his
wife and family.
Therefore, I am very pleased indeed
that the Government have introduced
this Bill to further extend the transitional
period. I hope that in the course of a
very short time trade will revive to such
an extent that all the unemployed will be
absorbed in work, and that they will no
longer have to depend on unemployment
benefit and on the extraordinary conditions that have to be introduced from
time to time. I am very pleased to see
that the Leader of the Opposition has
welcomed this Bill. It is very satisfactory
to find that all parties are in agreement
that the Measure should be placed on the
Statute Book.
Mr. LESLIE: It seems almost impossible to allow this Bill to pass without
drawing attention to two small matters
in connection with it. The first is that the
noble Viscount mentioned-although he
did not lay as much emphasis on it as I
should have liked that it is another example of the step-by-step policy, which I
need not further explain. The second is
that this great concession-for it is a concession and this great benefit and it
will be a great benefit to many of our
people that this concession and this
benefit is only possible as a result of the
financial arrangement as regards Unemployment Insurance and other matters
which our Government has been able to
make with the financial representative of
the Imperial Government. It should not
be lost sight of that this is one of the fruits
of many months of arduous work undertaken in the interests of the people of
Northern Ireland.
On Question, Motion agreed to.

Royal Assent.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): I am authorised by the
Prime Minister, on his behalf, to acquaint
the Senate that His Grace the Governor
of Northern Ireland, in the name of and
on behalf of His Majesty the King, has
been pleased to give his Assent to the
following Bills:
Unemployment Insurance (Transitional Provisions Amendment);
Petroleum (Amendment):
Midwives and Nursing Homes;
Industrial Provident Societies
(Amendment);
House of Commons (Method of Voting
and Redistribution of Seats).
House adjourned during pleasure.
House resumed.
Prorogation of Parliament.
His Grace the Governor, being seated
on the Throne, commanded the Gentleman Usher of the Black Rod to let the
Commons know "It is his Grace's pleasure that they attend him in this House."
The Commons being at the Bar with
their Speaker, his Grace delivered the
following Speech from the Throne:
KING'S SPEECH.
Members of the Senate and of the
House of Commons.
I thank you for passing the legislative Measures which have been placed
on the Statute Book, and which were
foreshadowed in my Speech at the
opening of Parliament on 26th February
last. I note, however, it has been
found impracticable to deal with certain other matters therein referred to,
but these will be submitted for consideration in the new Session.
Members of the House of Commons.
I thank you for the provision of supplies for the public services.
Members of the Senate and of the
House of Commons.
In bidding you farewell, I pray that
the blessing of Almighty God may rest
upon your labours.
End of Session
Proclamation

THE SENATE
Then the Proclamation Proroguing the
present Parliament of Northern Ireland
was read by the Speaker as followeth :
BY HIS GRACE THE GOVERNOR
OF NORTHERN IRELAND.
A PROCLAMATION
For Proroguing the present Parliament
of Northern Ireland.
WHEREAS it is provided by section
eleven of the Government of Ireland Act,
1920, as modified by and in pursuance of
certain enactments amending the said
Act, that the Governor of Northern Ireland shall in His Majesty's name sumdissolve the
mon, prorogue and
Parliament of Northern Ireland:
NOW I, JAMES ALBERT ED-
WARD, Duke of Abercorn, Knight of the
Most Noble Order of the Garter, Knight
of the Most Illustrious Order of Saint
Patrick, Governor of Northern Ireland,
by this Proclamation do publish and declare His Majesty's Pleasure that the said
Parliament be, as from the reading of
this Proclamation in the Parliament of
Northern Ireland, prorogued to Thursday
the sixteenth day of May, one thousand
nine hundred and twenty-nine, to be
then holden.
Given at the Council Chamber, Belfast,
this twelfth day of April, One Thousand
Nine Hundred and Twenty-nine.
(Signed by his own hand),
ABERCORN.
GOD SAVE THE KING.
End of the Fifth Session (opened
26th February, 1929) of the Second
Parliament of Northern Ireland in the
Nineteenth year of the Reign of His
Majesty King George the Fifth.

NON-CIRCULATING BOOK

Tuesday, 26th February, 1929.
The Fifth Session of the Second Parliament of Northern Ireland, appointed to
meet 26th February, 1929, in the Nineteenth year in the reign of King George
V, was opened by Commission, the Commissioners being their Excellencies, the
Lord Chief Justice, Lord Justice Andrews
and Colonel the Right Hon. R. D. Percival Maxwell, D.S.O., D.L.
The House met at Twelve of the Clock,
Mr. SPEAKER in the Chair.
KING'S SPEECH.
His Excellency the Lord Chief Justice,
being seated on the Throne, commanded
the Gentleman Usher of the Black Rod
to desire the Commons to attend
immediately in this House.
The Commons being at the Bar with
their Speaker, His Excellency the Lord
Chief Justice said: -My Lords and
Gentlemen, I have it in command from
His Grace the Governor to read to you
His Grace's Speech on the opening of this
Session of Parliament.
His Excellency then read the following
Speech from the Throne.
Members of the Senate and of the House
of Commons.
It is with profound relief and a deep
feeling of thankfulness to Divine Providence that I am able to record a steady
improvement in the health of His
Majesty the King. In no part of His
Majesty's Empire has there been
evinced greater anxiety throughout His
protracted illness or more heartfelt
gratification at the progress being made
towards recovery than by His loyal
subjects in Northern Ireland.
I observe with satisfaction that the
improvement in the shipbuilding industry continues, and that although
the linen trade has not recovered from
a protracted period of depression its
condition during the past few months
has been somewhat better.
I
am pleased to learn that my
Government has entered into an agreement with the British Government for
placing upon a permanent basis the
arrangement made in 1926 assimilating
the burdens on the Exchequers of the
United Kingdom and of Northern Ireland in respect of Unemployment Insurance. This agreement will require
ratification by both Parliaments and
the necessary measure will be introduced in due course.
A
King's Speech.
King's Speech.
THE SENATE

I shall observe with interest the results of the change in the law relating
to Health Insurance which came into
operation at the beginning of this year
extending the free period of insurance
of persons after they cease to be insurably employed, and enabling those wha
are insured and are over 60 years of age
to qualify more easily for Contributory
Old Age Pensions. Other provisions
permit insured persons who cannot, by
reason of poor health, gain admission
to an Approved Society to participate
more fully in the benefits provided by
the Health Insurance Scheme.
The continued development of the
education system of Northern Ireland is
very gratifying and the striking increase in the numbers of the pupils attending secondary schools in particular,
bears witness to marked progress in the
extent to which the facilities provided
by the Education Act are taken advantage of by the public.
It is satisfactory to note that the
steady upward movement in the numbers of live stock which has taken place
since 1925 was substantially accelerated
in 1928; that there was also a considerable increase in the export of
cattle, sheep and poultry in that period;
and that the area under tillage was
over 11,000 acres above the corresponding figure for 1927.
Proposals will be laid before you for
the abolition of Proportional Representation in its application to elections to
the House of Commons of Northern Ireland, and for the adoption of the principle of single-member constituencies.
Proposals will also be laid before you
for relieving productive industry of a
substantial proportion of the rates
which it at present has to bear in connection with the cost of Local Government services and for exempting agricultural land and farm buildings entirely from this burden.
Bills dealing with the following
matters will be submitted for your
consideration:
Registration of Nursing
Maternity Homes,
and
The storage and transport of
Petroleum,
The amendment of the Law
lating to Bankruptcy,
re-
To empower the Royal Ulster Constabulary to exercise the powers
of inspection under Part I of the
Weeds and Agricultural Seeds
Act, 1909,
re-
The amendment of the Law
lating to Industrial and Provident Societies,
The amendment of the Law in
connection with the control of
Licensing of Public Service
Vehicles,
To prevent the employment of
School children in connection
with betting operations,
Town Planning in Urban Areas;
and Unemployment Insurance.
Members of the House of Commons.
The Estimates for the Public Services
will be laid before you in due course.
You will be asked to make provision
for appropriate recoupment to the local
rating authorities of the rates in respect
of which industry and agriculture will
be exempt.
The publicity campaign organised by
the Linen Industry and assisted by substantial grants from public funds has
been extended to cover the Home and
Dominion markets in addition to those
of the United States to which it was
confined in its initial stages. While it
is yet too early to gauge the results
which may attend the extension of the
campaign to the Home and Dominion
markets there is no reason to believe
that they will prove less encouraging
than those which have already disclosed
themselves in the case of the United
States. You will, accordingly, be
asked to vote an additional sum
wards this object for the year 1929-
1930.
toa
An adequate sum will be placed on
the estimates to establish and maintain
a Training Centre associated with
new Juvenile Employment Exchange at
which classes will be held for the benefit
of young persons who are out of work.
Papers Presented.
Papers Presented.
26 FEBRUARY 1929
Members of the Senate and of the House
of Commons.
My Government will continue to give
sympathetic and unremitting attention
to all practicable measures for assisting
agriculture and industry; stimulating
trade and promoting employment.
In addition to the action which has
already been taken to achieve these
ends I trust that the proposals indicated for relieving agriculture and productive industry from the burden of
rates and the provision of a Juvenile
Training Centre will be of great benefit
to the whole community.
And I pray that the blessing of
Almighty God may rest upon your
deliberations.
House adjourned during pleasure.
House resumed.

Laid before the House (pursuant to
Act), and ordered to lie on the Table.
Local Bills.
Mr. SPEAKER acquainted the House
that the Clerk of the Senate had laid
upon the Table the certificates from the
Examiner that the Standing Orders
applicable to the following Bills had been
complied with:--
Londonderry Corporation.
Bangor (County Down) Improvement.
Newry Urban District Council and
Newry Port and Harbour Trust.
Belfast Water.
New Member Sworn.
Dudley E. B. McCorkell, Esquire, took
the Oath and his seat as Mayor of Londonderry.
Address in Reply to His Majesty's Most
Gracious Speech.
Mr. SPEAKER having read the
King's Speech,
Mr. McCORKELL (in morning dress):
I beg to move: That an humble
Address be presented to His Grace in the
following words:
Your Grace,
We, His Majesty's most dutiful and
loyal subjects, the Senate of the Parliament of Northern Ireland, beg
leave to offer our humble thanks to
your Grace for the Speech which your
Grace has addressed to both Houses of
Parliament.
In rising to propose that an address of
thanks be presented in reply to the
Speech from the Throne, I am fully conscious of the compliment paid to the city
of Londonderry in that, as Mayor, I
have been asked to move that Address.
Before touching on the legislative programme outlined in the Speech, I beg to
associate the city of Londonderry-and,
incidentally, myself with the expression
of thankfulness for the improvement in
the health of His Majesty the King.
The widespread and reverent appeal to
Divine Providence for the consummation
of that improvement is a fitting reflection
of the deep regard and esteem in which
His Majesty is held by his people.
It is a matter for deep regret that the
absence of His Grace the Duke of Abercorn, our esteemed Governor, is due to
illness, and it is to be fervently hoped
that he will soon be restored again to
health and enabled to take up again the
reigns of Government.
The tenor of the Speech is one of
optimism, not the optimism of faith without works, but of works worthy of fulfilment. There is throughout a spirit of
progress towards days better both practically and ethically. Three of Northern
Ireland's outstanding industries are
mentioned agriculture, the linen trade,
and shipbuilding, and I have mentioned
them in alphabetical order. Another point
touched upon is the progress of education.
This last is a vital question for this as for
every country since in the ultimate issue
all industries depend upon it, all social
services are interwoven with it, and, more
than that, our two greatest commercial
assets--capital and labour-are the
better for it. A little knowledge is undoubtedly a dangerous thing, but gross
ignorance is a very much worse one.
The agreement between the Governments of Great Britain and Northern
Ireland in regard to the financing of
unemployment insurance will be wel-
Debate on Address.
Debate on Address.
26 FEBRUARY 1929
comed, if ratified, as it is to be hoped it
will be, and as regards the changes in
the law relating to National Health Insurance these are such as must be wel-
Not the
comed in any modern State.
least of them is that which provides for
persons in poor health being admitted to
approved societies. It has been a great
drawback to some of these people that
heretofore they have laboured under considerable disability in that respect.
The increase in livestock is heartening,
as is the growth in the export of cattle,
sheep, and poultry, but a real menace to
these cheerful factors has to be faced on
account of the steady stream of meats,
poultry, and eggs, which flows into these
islands. This influx from other parts of
the Empire is one at which no loyal
British subject should cavil, but that we
should be a dumping ground for any and
every foreign country throughout the
world seems to present an evil which
surely calls for redress or adjustment by
some means whatever they may be.
The same line of thought may readily
be brought to bear on other industries,
an outstanding example being patent in
the linen trade, a trade which I am confident every citizen of Northern Ireland
will believe is one which might justly be
brought within the purview of the Safeguarding of Industries Act.
As regards the abolition of Proportional Representation as applied to the
election of Members to the House of
Commons of Northern Ireland, this
should prove a boon to those electors who
have failed to avail themselves of the
educational facilities at their disposal,
and the reversion to single-member constituencies should, I fancy, greatly help
the working of the franchise.
Other measures are outlined, all of
them worthy of the fullest consideration,
even if one or two of them may not meet
with universal approval. An outstanding measure above all others mentioned
is that in connection with derating.
Whether or not this will work out in
practice as in theory it promises to,
there can be no doubt as to its being a
very real and statesman-like effort to
assist productive industry both urban and
rural.
In conclusion, I would pay a very sincere tribute to the dominant theme of

the programme placed before us, because
it conveys a genuine wish to progress
towards those social and commercial conditions SO necessary to the health,
happiness, and prosperity of every Member of this beloved Province. It is with
great pleasure that I beg to move that
an Address of gratitude be presented in
reply to the Gracious Speech of His
Majesty the King.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM (in
morning dress): To enjoy the privilege of
seconding the Address of thanks for the
Gracious Speech from the Throne, which
has been proposed in such an eloquent
manner by the Mayor of Londonderry, I
consider a great compliment, not alone
to me personally, but more especially to
the working men of Northern Ireland who
have conducted themselves in such
praiseworthy manner since our Parliament
was instituted, and more particularly during the terrible trade depression, proving
themselves in every way deserving of the
favourable consideration which the Government continue to give them.
a
Before proceeding to the Speech I would
like to associate myself with the proposer
in his expression of regret that His Grace
the Governor was absent from our
Assembly this morning owing to illness. I,
too, hope he will be soon restored to his
usual good health.
With reference to the Speech, after the
many months of anxiety exhibited by our
people regarding His Majesty's health,
we all rejoice with His Grace to learn of
the satisfactory progress made towards recovery.
The continued improvement in our
shipbuilding industry, which has been
enormously assisted by our Government,
is now providing additional work for
hundreds of our workers, enabling them
to perform useful labour in return for
wages, which is much more acceptable
than unemployment benefit-(Hon. Members: Hear, hear) - and it is very
pleasing to know that the efforts of
the Government in this direction
much appreciated by the workers.
the linen industry was in a similar position we would all feel extremely thankful,
but I fear this will require special consideration and the adoption of a scheme
which will protect us against the unfair
competition of the foreigner,
are
If
Debate on Address.
Debate on Address.
THE SENATE
[Mr. J. Cunningham]
The agreement arrived at with the
Imperial Government respecting unemployment insurance will, I am sure, receive the necessary support, so that the
unemployed worker can be guaranteed
benefit when he is entitled to make a
claim. The concession made to our unemployed workers through the Health
Insurance scheme, giving them a free
period of insurance, will relieve the aged
worker of much anxiety and secure for
many the old-age pension.
Our educational system is, no doubt,
considerably improved, but several years
will pass before we can derive the full
benefit of the Act. Better school accommodation must be provided for the children in working class areas to enable them
to receive tuition under more healthy
conditions, and if new schools were erected
more expeditiously in the various districts
and large centres, such as Belfast, it
would also supply much needed employment. The increase mentioned in the
number of pupils attending the secondary
schools for further education is not so
much due to the desire for same as to the
deplorable lack of suitable positions for
boys and girls leaving elementary schools.
Although the workers in the industrial
centres have little knowledge of agricultural subjects, we are always pleased to
learn of improvements which benefit the
farmers, and more especially pleased to
know that the Government are able to
render such valuable assistance.
The proposed abolition of Proportional
Representation which is at present applicable to the elections of our Northern Parliament will meet with the approval of
the vast majority of the electors. (Hon.
Members: Hear, hear.) Our experience
of the working of this system is far from
satisfactory. The people get puzzled
using such a number of figures and selecting their choice out of a number of candidates, and I am sure very few will regret
the change which will enable them to revert to the old and simple method with
single-member constituencies, to which the
ordinary person can become easily accustomed.
I remember an amusing incident at one
of the elections. A rather intelligent
engineer, who was employed in the workshop with me, returned after dinner on
the day of an election in a very angry
mood. He told me that he had spent the
previous evening instructing his wife how
to mark her ballot paper, and being a
very sensible man he warned her not to
put any figures opposite the Socialist
candidates' name, as they were no good.
(Laughter.) But when he arrived home
for dinner, she explained as best she
could how she had marked her paper, and
he then discovered that she had been a
little excited and voted in error for
the Socialist candidates.
Mr. DORMAN: Wise woman!
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: The
poor fellow was so angry that he returned
to the workshop to tell of the terrible
calamity which had occurred. It is not
surprising that he told her that it would
have been better she had stopped at home.
(Laughter.) The case I have just mentioned is only one of hundreds, and proves
how necessary it is that we should adopt
the simplest method for election purposes
if we are to secure a truly representative
Government.
(Hon. Members: Hear,
hear.)
The derating scheme, which is also
mentioned, will be heartily approved by
all who have the interest and welfare of
this community at heart. It will relieve
agriculture and industry of a very heavy
financial burden, and assist the latter,
which has hitherto been placed at a great
disadvantage when competing with other
countries.
The other Bills which are to be submitted for consideration will, I feel sure,
receive the attention and support which
they deserve. I beg to second the Address
of thanks.
Mr. DORMAN: I wish to congratulate
the hon. Senator, the Mayor of Derry,
upon his very eloquent address in proposing this vote of thanks to the Governor
for his gracious Speech. I wish to
associate myself particularly with the expression of thanksgiving to Providence
for the recovery of the health of His
Gracious Majesty the King. (Hon.
Members: Hear, hear.) I should also
like to express the hope that His Grace
the Governor will rapidly be restored to
health, and be able to resume his duties
over this Province. I associate myself
Debate on Address.
Debate on Address.
26 FEBRUARY 1929
with that because I happened to serve on
the lower deck when His Gracious Majesty
was on the quarter deck.
I can truthfully say that all the men then serving
under him had the highest possible respect for him owing to the manner in
which he performed his duties as a naval
officer, and all of them placed in him
complete confidence when he became
commander of his own ship. I have,
therefore, as I have already said, great
pleasure in associating myself with the
expression of our thanks as I would with
an expression of thanks for the recovery
of the humblest of His Majesty's subjects.
We
The Speech itself, as the mover of the
Address has said, is rather optimistic, but
I do not think the present outlook affords
reasonable ground for that optimism.
We are rapidly rationalizing our industries.
are introducing the most
perfect system of machinery and efficiency
in all our industries with the unfortunate result that the future looks extremely black, notwithstanding the revival
there may appear to be in some of the
industries of the Province and other
parts of Great Britain. I was struck
during the last few weeks in reading reports relating to two of the leading
manufacturers in Great Britain who,
deservedly, have the reputation of being
probably the finest employers in the
world, and the most considerate to their
employees. I speak of the two celebrated
cocoa firms of Messrs. Rowntree and
Messrs. Cadbury. Both of these firms
are faced with increasing demands for
their products and an increasing trade,
but owing to this system of rationalizing
industry they have both found it necessary to dispense with the services of large
numbers of those engaged in their particular industry.
When leading employers like those two
firms, with an expanding market, find it
necessary to dispense with large numbers
of their employees, what hope is there in
the immediate future for the reabsorption into other industries of those
who will necessarily become unemployed
through this process of rationalization? I
noticed in the Press some figures which
show that Messrs. Rowntree found it
necessary to get rid of one hundred men,
and Messrs. Cadbury have found it
necessary to dismiss four hundred women
employed by them. Therefore, hon.
Members will see the difficulties that are
created by this speeding up of the
rationalization of industry. To me it
points to the necessity of the Government
facing this problem and introducing far
more comprehensive schemes to deal with
it than the Government either in Great
Britain or Northern Ireland has yet introduced to meet the emergency that has
undoubtedly arisen in our midst.
From day to day I am brought into
the homes of the working classes. I see
the extreme misery and want that is prevalent in the community, and I know that
all the efforts that have been made have
not been sufficient to meet the distress
which is undermining the health of the
future citizens. Thousands of the
children of those unemployed who are
turned off the Exchange are suffering
through mal-nutrition, and in consequence they are building up a physical
frame that will not stand in the future
stress of life.
I wish to express my approval of the
attempt to co-ordinate the obligations of
the Governments of Great Britain and
Northern Ireland in connection with the
Unemployment Insurance Fund. This
is necessary owing to the equality of
social services in the two countries and
to the fact that those who are compelled
to migrate from Great Britain to Northern Ireland to seek employment are
entitled to the advantages of the Unemployment scheme in both countries.
Therefore, I think it is only just that
under those circumstances both Governments should pool their resources to meet
the charge upon that fund. I desire to
voice my gratitude for the improved
educational facilities that have been
brought within the reach of the people of
Northern Ireland, for, as the mover of
the Address has said, the future wellbeing of this and every other community
depends upon the education of the
people.
With regard to Proportional Representation, I wish to express my disapproval
of the proposed abolition of this system
of election. I do not think there is any
part of the great Commonwealth of
Nations where Proportional Representation is calculated to work with such
justice as it is in Northern Ireland.
Unfortunately in this particular area we
have traditions that have associated
Debate on Address.
Debate on Address.
THE SENATE
[Mr. Dorman]
are
politics with religion, and it is quite
possible that under the single seat system
a minority of the electors may have an
overwhelming majority in the legislative
chamber, as has happened in Great
Britain. Having regard to the fact that,
as I view the situation, there
in Great Britain a million more voters at
the back of the Opposition than there are
at the back of the Government, I cannot
acknowledge that a system that brings
about such a state of affairs is a just
system. Under that system a Government with a majority of from 150 to 200
can be constituted on a minority vote.
In addition to that it is quite possible
that one vote in twenty-eight constituencies in Northern Ireland might form a
Government and in the other twenty-six
constituencies there might be a vast majority of the electors of the complete area.
It seems to me that the abolition of Proportional Representation means removing
the opportunity for all phases of political
opinion to have a fair share of representation in the legislative assembly of our
I should also like to say that I
approve of the proposed extension of
power to the R.U.C. under the Weeds
and Agricultural Seeds Act. Unfortunately lazy, thoughtless farmers very often
allow their land to get into a condition
that is an absolute danger to the more
industrious and careful cultivators of the
soil.
area.
Mr. LONG: I should like to join with
the hon. Senator who has just sat down
in congratulating the Mover and Seconder
of the Address on the very capable manner
in which they have discharged their duty.
I should also like to join Senator Dorman
in his expression of thankfulness for the
recovery of His Majesty the King and for
the improvement in the health of our beloved Governor. I have on previous occasions had an opportunity of expressing
the thanks of the agricultural community
for the introduction of the Derating Bill,
and I do not propose to delay the House
by a recapitulation of those thanks. I
also associate myself with Senator Dorman
in his approval of the proposal to allow
the R.U.C. to act as inspectors under the
Weeds and Agricultural Seeds Act. In
the course of carrying out their duty of
patrolling the country the members of the
R.U.C. have ample opportunities of detecting this shameful practice.
I am very pleased, indeed, to see that
an amendment of the law in connection
with the licensing of public service vehicles is to be brought forward. That is a
matter that eminently requires to be
dealt with. Of late the number of omnibuses on our country roads has increased
enormously, and they are doing incalculable damage to the surface of the roads.
Whatever amendment of the law is made
to deal with this matter I think the views
of the local authorities should be taken
into consideration. The Ministry, in a
circular they have addressed to local
authorities, have stated that they will
give careful consideration to the views of
local authorities. That careful consideration might mean something and it might
mean nothing. In the past a lady was
supposed to consult her spouse about the
amount of her dress allowance, but suppose a wife came to her husband and said,
"Don't you think, dear, that the more
expensive of these fur coats would really
be the cheaper in the end." The wise
man would agree and the average husband
would believe that he had really been consulted.
In this case I should like real consideration given to the views of local authorities. I would not ask that they should
be consulted on every minor matter connected with the licensing of buses. For
instance, if John Brown wished to scrap
an omnibus and provide a new one I
should think it would not be necessary
to consult the local council as to whether
he should be granted a transfer of his
licence to his new vehicle. But if the
Government have not already drafted
their new Bill, or if they are not inexorably committed to the details of it,
I should like them to see that on matters
of principle at least, and on any important matter affecting individuals, local
authorities will be consulted. I am in a
position to know something of the financial position of many of the bus proprietors in Northern Ireland, and their
financial standing is indeed very precarious. Most of them purchased their
omnibuses on the instalment system, and
they pay slightly over £120 per month as
the instalment to repay the price of the
bus, and in addition to that they have to
pay about £30 per month for petrol as
well as to provide for insurance, wear and
tear, and the wages of conductors and
drivers, so that the House will realise
that for the man of moderate means the
outlook is not very bright.

e
