Message from the Governor.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): I have received the following
Message from His Grace the Governor :-
I have received with great satisfaction the expression of your thanks for
the Speech with which the present Session of Parliament was opened.
(Signed) Abercorn.
Petroleum (Amendment) Bill [S.].
A Bill to amend the Petroleum Acts,
1871 and 1879, and other enactments relating to petroleum, in their application
to Northern Ireland, was presented by
Viscount Charlemont, read a First time,
and ordered to be printed and to be read
a Second time on Tuesday next. (Bill
No. 3).
LIBRARY AT NEW PARLIAMENT
HOUSE.
The following Question stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie :
To ask the Leader of the House whether
any information can be given with respect
to the Library to be provided in the new
Parliament House at Stormont, particularly in regard to accommodation and the
supply of books and other publications.
Mr. LESLIE: Time is getting on and
there are those of us who cherish the aspiration that we may live long enough
to get to the new Parliament House at
Stormont. It therefore occurred to me
that we might begin now to acquire information as to the arrangements which
are to be made there, not so much perhaps for our own comfort as for the
advancement of our learning.
As the
House knows, the apartment in this
building which passes for the Library at
present and has so passed since we began
to sit here is distinctly too small for its
purpose. It is not perhaps sufficiently
well equipped with the necessary literature; indeed I think I may say that it is
only redeemed from futility by the extreme efficiency and urbanity of those
who work there. It does give us a great
deal of assistance, but it is perhaps open
to question whether we might not derive
more benefit from a better equipped library in the future. If I may explain
what I mean, we have not got a set of
the complete Statute Laws of the United
Kingdom.
It is an extraordinary thing that although this Parl Parliament has been eight
years in being we still have not got a complete set of Statutes of the United
Kingdom. We have, of course, the revised Statutes, but that, as we all know,
is not quite the same thing. We have not
got such a standard work as Halsbury's
Laws of England, and the Empire Digest,
which is being published in instalments.
We ought to acquire it, and, parenthetically, I may say that if we do so before
next June we can get it at the subscription price, which would mean a great
saving to the Ministry of Finance.
There are a great many other books I
might name, but I think those I have referred to are sufficient for my purpose.
As regards the other publications which
I have mentioned in my question, I was
not thinking of newspapers and things of
that sort, but of Blue Books, not only
65 Unemployment Insurance-

14 MARCH 1929
Second Reading.
our own Blue Books, all of which are to
be found in the Library, but the Blue
Books issued by the Parliaments of the
United Kingdom, the Colonies and Dependencies and the Dominions which
have Parliaments of their own. It seems
to me to be desirable that we should have
a complete supply of records of that kind
so soon as we can find room satisfactorily
to house them. And we do want above
all some little shut-off rooms or recesses
wherein people who want to study in
the Library can work without the disturbance necessarily incidental to people
coming in in search of information. I
think I have sufficiently indicated the
object of my question.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The accommodation provided in the new Parliament Buildings in this connection consists
of a Library, Library Store, and Writing
Rooms for the use of Members of the
Senate and House of Commons respectively. The position of these several
apartments has been determined with a
view to meeting the convenience of the
Members of the two Houses. There will
also be separate rooms adjacent to the
Library for the accommodation of the
Librarian and the Vote Office.
on
As regards the supply of books and
other publications, this is a matter which
will require to receive consideration at a
later date, possibly in conjunction with
the recommendations which may be made
by the Departmental Committee
Libraries in Northern Ireland which was
recently set up by the Minister of
Finance, but the hon. Member may rest
assured that the arrangements to be made
will be in consonance with those obtaining
in other legislative assemblies.
Unemployment Insurance (Agreement)
Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I propose
to deal very briefly with the provisions of
this Bill, in according a Second Reading
to which hon. Members will, I am sure,
find no difficulty. It will be remembered
that the Unemployment Insurance Act of
1926 ratified an Agreement between the
Ministry of Finance and the Imperial
Treasury, under which the Northern Ireland Unemployment Fund and the Fund
of Great Britain were kept in parity,
with the exception that a deficit of almost
34 million pounds which had accrued on
the Northern Ireland Fund prior to the
Agreement was retained in a Special
Suspense Account.
The Agreement ratified by the Act of
1926 would, in the ordinary course of
events, terminate on the 31st of March,
1930, but a further Agreement has recently been made which, in the first place,
makes permanent the existing arrangement, and secondly, provides for the
gradual writing off over the next twenty
years of the Suspense deficit of £3,424,434
which I have referred to above, and
which is at present a debt due by the
Northern Ireland Unemployment Insurance Fund to our Exchequer.
The present Bill merely ratifies the
Agreement recently concluded, and provides the necessary statutory authority
for the various payments which may fall
to be made by the Northern Ireland
Exchequer thereunder. Section 4 of the
Bill provides for the necessary modifications following upon the reduction from
year to year of the Suspense deficit of our
Fund. I beg to move.
Mr. DORMAN: I rise, in the first
place, to thank the noble Viscount for his
explanation of this particular Bill, and in
the second place to say that I wholeheartedly welcome it as a Measure which
is designed to relieve the unemployment
position that exists in Northern Ireland
at the present time. The great question
confronting modern civilization is that of
unemployment. It is not a question of
burning public interest merely in Northern Ireland and in Great Britain, but in
many of the Colonies, all of which find
difficulty in absorbing those who have no
means of living by the sale of their labour
in the labour markets. As a Socialist, of
course, I do not believe that a permanent
solution of the unemployment problem is
possible in the present competitive state
of society, but it is not my duty to enter
into that question on this particular Bill.
I would like to say that I believe Northern Ireland occupies a unique position in
this matter, inasmuch as there is probably a proportion of our working classes
engaged in the supply of commodities to
the world greater than in any other part
of Great Britain or Ireland. Consequently, as the aftermath of the Great
War, we may look forward to this particular area being probably the last to
recover its normal condition so far as employment is concerned. I think the figures
B
67 Unemployment Insurance-

THE SENATE
Second Reading.
[Mr. Dorman]
given in the memorandum issued by the
Ministry of Finance bear this out. At
the time the original Agreement was entered into unemployment in Northern
Ireland was considerably over 100 per
cent. greater, in proportion to the population, than it was in Great Britain, but
I am pleased to see by the figures in the
memorandum that we are more or less recovering our position, and that whereas
in the latter half of 1925 we had 100 per
cent. more unemployed in proportion to
our population than they had in Great
Britain, last year the disparity was reduced to 6.4 per cent. as between Great
Britain and Northern Ireland.
I also welcome this Bill from the point
of view that if we had had to contend with
the abnormal conditions that have prevailed for the past five years in Northern
Ireland our Unemployment Fund would
have been in a disastrous position at the
present time. The memorandum strikingly
shows that we have received considerable
grants from the Imperial Excehquer.
Taking into consideration the estimated
grant to be received during the year
1928-29, we will have received
£3,800,000. One can visualise what a sad
and pitiable condition the Unemployment
Fund of Northern Ireland would have
been in now had not the Imperial Exchequer come to the relief of Northern
Ireland by giving this grant from year to
year, the total sum received during the
past three years amounting to approximately two and a half million pounds.
over
I look forward to the time when in
Great Britain, Northern Ireland, and the
Irish Free State we will have equality of
social service, so that those who migrate
from one country to another will be entitled to equal benefits in each of those
countries. I have much pleasure in supporting the Second Reading of this Bill.
I
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM:
look upon myself more or less as a representative of the workers in this House, and
I wish to congratulate the Government on
the Agreement which has been arrived at
with the Imperial Government. Although
I am not a Socialist I can see perfectly
clearly that this is the greatest boon that
could possibly have been secured for the
workers of this Province. I am at a loss
to understand what kind of position we in
Northern Ireland would have been in had
it been necessary for the Northern Government to have reduced unemployment
benefit and increased contributions, as
would probably have been necessary had
this Agreement not been concluded.
In both good and bad times we have
a large movement of labour from the
shipyards here to the shipyards of
the Clyde, Barrow, and Liverpool,
and we would have been in a peculiar
and difficult position if our workers who
crossed to the other side to work had received smaller benefits and been compelled
to pay higher contributions than the
workers resident across the water. Therefore, I earnestly and sincerely congratulate
the Government on behalf of the workers
of Northern Ireland on the splendid
arrangement they have made with the
Imperial Government.
Mr. BARCLAY: I would just like to
give my support to the Second Reading
of this Bill, and to associate myself with
the previous speakers in what they have
said.
I know quite well that if this
Agreement had not been come to it would
not have been so well with the workers of
Northern Ireland. I know that those
who are working are not in a position,
owing to their small wage, to pay any
higher contribution, and I also know that
those who are unemployed are not in a
position to take any less than they are
getting from the Unemployment Fund at
the present time. The fact of the matter
is that while it may seem to some to be a
large amount, it is not very large when
it is taken into some of the homes I know
where there are four or five small children
who can neither work nor want.
I am very pleased to know that this
Agreement has been arrived at, and I
would like to join with Senator Joseph
Cunningham in congratulating the Government on that arrangement. At the
same time I would like to say that this
Agreement does not solve the unemployment problem. Something more will have
to be done. It does, as it were, close the
gap for the time being. There have been
several suggestions made by different
public men as to how this problem should
be solved. Many of them are inclined to
put the whole blame on the Government,
and say that the Government of Northern
Ireland has done nothing to abolish unemployment. Personally I cannot see
how a Government can solve the problem.
It is a national problem, and it is a problem the nation will have to take an
interest in. All sections of society will
have to lend a helping hand.
6) Unemployment Insurance

14 MARCH 19-0
Nursing Homes Bill
{
The Government can do its part, and
the Government has done a great deal in
the way of giving subsidies and trying to
create work, but when all is said and aone
that has not solved the problem. To my
mind all that the Government has done
up to the present has just simply kept the
wolf from the door, but we need something more than that. The sooner a
movement is afoot so much the better,
because if this question of unemployment
as not removed it is going to do a tremendous amount of harm to tradesmen and to
temperamental and high-spirited men.
It is like a canker
Mr. DEPUTY-SPEAKER: I do not
think it is quite in order to discuss the
general question of unemployment on this
particular Bill, which merely deals with
an Agreement arrived at between the
Imperial and Northern Ireland Governments.
Mr. BARCLAY: I am very sorry, but
it seems I have a tendency that way.
When a Member gets up to talk on a
question such as this he naturally drifts
away from the strict contents of the Bill,
but I had no intention of transgressing
the Rules of Debate. I was only making
one or two suggestions. However, I am
very pleased that this Bill has been brought
before the House to-day, and I hope that
in the future something of a practical
nature will be done to relieve this problem of unemployment which we have in
our midst. I have great pleasure in supporting the Second Reading.
Mr. McDOWELL: I cannot let the
occasion go by without also congratulating
the Government, or shall I say both
Governments, on the Agreement they have
made to help our unemployed workers in
Northern Ireland. While the hon. Member in front (Mr. Dorman) says he is a
Socialist, yet we are pleased to see he is
in favour of the action the Government
have taken under the circumstances that
exist at the moment. I hope the department that administers the Unemployment
Insurance Acts will do so more sympathetically in the future than they have
done in some cases in the past. A case
came under my observation last Friday
morning. A man had been employed by
one particular firm continuously for
twelve years up to December, 1926. The
premises were burned down and the man
received six weeks unemployment benefit.
He was unable to get another job, but
being one of those men who would rather
work than draw unemployment benefit he
bought a little motor lorry and engaged
in the carrying trade.
He did his very best up to January
this year, when he could not possibly
carry on any longer, and he had to sell up.
He signed on at the bureau, but because
he could not comply with the first
statutory condition of having thirty contributions during the preceding two years
he was debarred from benefit. Of
course, I know it is a difficult thing to get
over the law
Mr. DEPUTY-SPEAKER: I do not
think the hon. Senator is in order in discussing particular cases, because this Bill
deals only with the Agreement, and the
discussion should be confined to that.
Mr. MCDOWELL : I do not agree
with your ruling.
On Question, Motion (That the Bill be
now read a Second time) agreed to.

Mr. LESLIE: Perhaps I should take
this opportunity of directing attention to
line 20, page 10, Clause 13 of the Bill.
It is in reference to the definition of a
Maternity Home. We are told that a
Maternity Home means any premises used
or intended to be used for the reception
of pregnant women, or of women immediately after childbirth. That passage, of
course, brings us back to our discussion
of yesterday, on the first Clause of the
Bill, and in that Clause, as will be
remembered, we left the word "childbirth' to speak for itself as meaning or
conveying all that we intended to mean.
That being so, we are now faced with the
difficulty here. We took "childbirth” in
the former Clause as meaning not only the
actual successful act of delivery but as
covering an indefinite period immediately
succeeding that episode, during which
competent assistance or attendance was
necessary.

second passage relates to the time subsequent to the indeterminate period.
thought it right to point this out, in
order that, if necessary, any alteration
could be made at a later date.

Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am not

Petroleum (Amendment) Bill. [S.]
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): This Bill is introduced for
the purpose of Amending and bringing up
to date the enactments dealing with the
storage, transport, testing and general use
of petroleum and similar liquid fluids.
In its main provisions it follows closely
on the lines of legislation recently passed
in Great Britain, which does not apply to
Northern Ireland.
The last enactment touching on this
subject and now in force in Northern Ireland is in the Locomotives on Highways
Act, 1896, and is over thirty years old;
the main portion of the existing legislation is contained in the Petroleum Act of
1871. In those days the use of the lighter
and more volatile forms of petroleum
derivatives, such as petrol, was practically
nil, and the enormously extended use now
made of such liquids and its many attendant dangers call for wider and more effective methods of supervision and control.
The present Bill-although it contains
a large number of Clauses-does not, save
in a few instances, seek to introduce any
new principles, but merely aims at bringing legislation into line with modern requirements. I may mention, for the information of hon. Senators, that after this
Bill becomes law it is intended to introduce a Consolidation Bill which will combine in one enactment all the legislation
dealing with petroleum. For the information of hon. Senators I will now indicate shortly the object of each Clause of
the Bill.
Clause 1 merely amends the definition of the term 'harbour" and "harbour
authority" as laid down in Section 2 of
the Petroleum Act, 1871, and defines the
term "dock." It is entirely explanatory
and the alterations are not of serious importance.
Clause 2 repeals Section 3 of the Petroleum Act of 1871 and substitutes a fresh
definition of petroleum and of the lighter
and more volatile derivatives of petroleum, such as petrol, which is now for the
first time given the statutory designation
of "petroleum spirit." The test of
whether a liquid is petroleum or petroleum
spirit is the temperature below which
such liquid gives off an inflammable vapour.
This temperature is now fixed at 73 degrees.
Clause 3 deals with the verification of
the apparatus for testing for petroleum
spirit as mentioned in the previous clause.
Clause 4 refers to the First Schedule to
this Act, in which is described in somewhat complicated detail the apparatus
which is to be used for carrying out the
tests referred to in the last two Clauses,
and the method of using it. The description is highly technical and I trust that
hon. Senators will not cross-examine me
too closely on the details, which, I can
assure them, have been drawn up after
very careful consideration by our technical
advisers.
Clause 5 extends the powers of an
officer of a local authority under Section
11 of the Petroleum Act of 1871, so as
Petroleum Bill-
Second Reading.
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
to enable him to exercise supervision not
only over persons who deal in petroleum
-which includes petroleum spirit-but
also over any person who keeps petroleum
spirit for the purposes of any trade or industry carried on by him.
In view of the dangerous nature of
petroleum spirit I think that these wider
powers are called for, and I may say
that they have been in force in Great
Britain for some time.
Clause 6 deals with the by-laws which
are to be framed under Section 4 of the
Petroleum Act by Harbour Authorities,
and lays down that such by-laws shall
include provisions for the loading of ships
with petroleum spirit.
Clause 7 amends the penalties to which
persons who are guilty of a breach of
Section 6 or 7 of the Petroleum Act, 1871,
as regards the keeping, sending, or selling
of a petroleum spirit are liable. Under
that Act the forfeiture of all petroleum
kept or sent in contravention was compulsory, and in addition a penalty of £5
for breach of Section 6, or £20 for a breach
of Section 7, was laid down. Under the
present clause the forfeiture of the petroleum spirit in respect of which the offence
was committed is at the discretion of the
Court. In view of the large quantities
which are now frequently kept, the forfeiture of the whole stock would constitute a somewhat too drastic penalty in
certain cases, and it is felt that there
should be some discretion as regards its
enforcement.
a
Clause 8 lays down penalties for
breach of any conditions under which a
licence for the storage of petroleum is
issued, and requires a copy of such conditions to be posted up by the occupier of
the premises.
Clause 9 amends the scale of fees which
are chargeable for licences to store petroleum. Under former legislation a flat
rate of 5s was chargeable no matter what
quantity was authorised by the licence.
The fees now payable, which are laid down
in the Second Schedule to the Bill, vary
from 5s in the case of a quantity not exceeding 100 gallons to £5 for quantities
exceeding 50,000 gallons. This alteration will not affect the small user and it
is, I think, more equitable that
graduated scale should apply to the
larger users.
a
Clause 10 applies certain provisions of
the Explosives Act of 1875-which are
set out in the Third Schedule to the Bill
-to petroleum spirit, the most important
of these being the power which is conferred upon the Minister of Home Affairs
to make regulations regarding its conveyance by road, and to regulate the description and construction of carriages used for
such conveyance. The Clause also empowers that Minister to direct an inquiry
to be made as to the cause of any accident
which is due to the conveyance or storage
of such spirit, and defines the powers exercisable by the persons holding such an
inquiry as regards calling witnesses, administering oaths and other matters which
are necessary for the purposes of the
In view of the fact that a
inquiry.
petroleum spirit, owing to its highly inflammable and explosive nature, partakes
of many of the properties of substances
which come under the Explosives Act,
1875, this Clause is absolutely necessary.
Clause 11 provides for the exercise by
the Minister of Home Affairs of the
powers formerly exercised by a Secretary
of State under Section 5 of the Locomotives on Highways Act, 1896, as regards
making regulations as to the keeping and
use of petroleum or any other inflammable
liquid for the purposes of light locomotives.
It was under the Locomotives on Highways Act that the regulations now in
force, which enable limited quantities
of petrol to be kept by owners of motor
vehicles, motor boats, aircraft, etc., were
made. Such regulations will in future,
if necessary, be made under the authority
of this Act, but the regulations already
made will continue in force unless cancelled or revoked.
Clause 12 empowers the Minister of
Home Affairs, if he is satisfied that any
petroleum spirit is dangerous or likely to
be injurious to health, either to prohibit
its sale and use altogether or to prescribe
certain conditions and precautions which
are to be observed. It will, doubtless, be
within the recollection of hon. Senators
that an acute controversy took place comparatively recently regarding the alleged
injurious nature of a new form of petrol,
and although the results of that controversy were somewhat inconclusive it is easy
to imagine that certain forms of fuel for
motor vehicles might produce fumes which
would be dangerous or injurious to human
life.
Clause 13 lays down that all regulations
made by the Minister of Home Affairs
under the Act shall be laid before each
Petroleum Bill-
Second Reading.
19 MARCH 1929
House of Parliament, and that if an
Address be presented to the Governor of
Northern Ireland by either House within
the statutory period, the regulations shall
be annulled.
Clause 14 empowers the Council of any
county, county borough, borough, or
urban district, to make by-laws either
prohibiting the establishment of petroleumfilling stations or regulating their appearance. This is an entirely new principle as
regards legislation of petroleum, and is
designed to prevent the disfigurement of
scenery or places of historic interest by
unsuitable and unsightly erections. The
Clause provides that any occupier of a
petroleum-filling station already in existence may be called upon to remove it
within six months after receiving a
notice to that effect, but entitles
such
to owner
from the
Council any expeness reasonably incurred
in carrying out the removal, and also compensation for any loss sustained by him.
In the event of a dispute the matter will
be decided by the County Court judge.
recover
Clause 15 confers upon Canal Companies
the power to make by-laws for the loading, conveyance and landing of petroleum
spirit in and upon the canal, and provides
that such by-laws shall not be effective
until they have been confirmed by the
Ministry of Commerce. Under former
legislation Canal Companies did not
possess this power, which seems to be very
necessary in the interests of the public
safety.
Clause 16 provides that the Governor of
Northern Ireland may, by Order in
Council, apply the provisions of the
Petroleum Acts to any substance which
may be specified in the Order. This is
merely the transfer of the powers vested
in the King by Section 14 of the Petroleum Act of 1871 to the Governor of
Northern Ireland, and has in practice no
new effect. There are certain substances,
such as calcium carbide, and bisulphide of
carbon, which under certain conditions
give off highly inflammable and dangerous
fumes and require special precautions in
the handling and transport.
Clause 17 is merely an adaptation of the
Petroleum Acts of 1871 and 1879, so as
to make them applicable to Northern
Ireland, but paragraph (c) of this Clause
lays down a fresh definition of "local
authority" for the purpose of granting
licences to store petroleum spirit.
former definition of "licensing authority"
The
in Section 8 of the Petroleum Act, 1871,
was complicated and unsuitable to
Northern Ireland.
Clause 18 merely defines certain expressions used in this Bill and in the
Petroleum Acts of 1871 and 1879.
Clause 19 provides for minor amendments in previous Acts; a list of such
amendments will be found in the Fourth
Schedule to the Bill. Clause 20 deals
with the title of the Act, and provides for
the repeal of certain enactments which
are specified in the Fifth Schedule to the
Bill, and provides, as mentioned before,
that regulations made under Section 5 of
the Locomotives on Highways Act, 1896,
shall have effect as if made under Clause
11 of this Bill. I beg to move.
Mr. LESLIE: I was very glad to
hear from the noble Viscount that it is
intended that the Bill before us, if it becomes an Act of Parliament, should be
of an ephemeral character. I think we
all welcome the consolidation of any small
codes, and even of greater codes whenever
that is possible. The lack of codification
in the law dealing with petroleum would
be responsible for any difficulty hon.
Members may have in endeavouring to
follow the provisions of the Bill before us,
because I think the noble Viscount claimed
that it was based on the Consolidating
Measure of last year passed by the Parliament of the United Kingdom, a Measure
which, if I remember rightly, came into
operation on the 4th August last. But
if anybody endeavours to look into the
details of the Bill before us and tries to
find their opposite numbers in the English
Act I think it is to the Act of 1926 they
will have to have recourse and that confusion of the two Measures makes it rather
more difficult to follow the Bill than it
otherwise would be. However, as we are
to have consolidation I do not think we
should complain of a little difficulty in
the early stages, and I do not think there
will be any great difficulty at all.
Going through the Bill, I do not know
whether the noble Viscount meant to say
how soon we may expect the Consolidating
Measure. I was a little afraid myself
that we might have to wait two years
owing to the provision as regards the
existence of standards stamped and verified. In England two years did elapse
between the 1926 and 1928 Measures.
That is a point we can no doubt be told
about at a later stage. The noble Vis-
Second Reading.
Petroleum Bill-

THE SENATE

[Mr. Leslie]
count spoke about, I think, Clause 8 of
the Act of 1871 as not being altogether
suitable for Northern Ireland. I do not
think that was at all fair to Clause 8 of
the Act of 1871. It is unfair to condemn
a Clause for not being in accordance with
Local Government arrangements which
were not made for nearly thirty years
afterwards. When the Act of 1871 was
passed we had no County Councils nor
some other bodies that I need not mention.
As regards the same Clause 8, I am not
quite sure that we are wise in proposing
to follow Great Britain in abolishing the
licence granted by magistrates, and giving
the work to the County Councils. I do
not think that the business of granting
such petrol-holding licences as are required
occasions any serious trouble, but the
application has to be made in a Court and
the police do have an opportunity of objecting. It seems to me that if the County
Councils are to do the work in the future
there will not be much opportunity of
objecting to any undesirable person who
may require a licence. However, that is
entirely a matter of administration. I
do not think the applicant will complain,
because it is far less trouble to write a
letter to the County Council than to appear
on one stated day of the month at
Petty Sessions Court and ask for one.
a
I presume from what the noble Viscount
said that there is no reason to apprehend
that we shall be required to take out a
special licence for keeping that reasonable
amount of petrol we desire to have in our
private garages. We have not had to do
that hitherto.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: You will
not have to do that now.
Mr. LESLIE:
of tendering congratulations, I would like
to draw attention to Clause 11 of the Bill.
Clause 11 and the subsequent references
to it in the Schedule dealing with repeals
will have the effect of taking Section 5
of the Locomotives Act out of that category of enactments for offences against
which the police have a right of appeal.
The House will remember that the right
of appeal is given in our Motor Traffic
Act of, I think, 1926, but it has not met
with universal acceptance or applause. We
shall get rid of that as far as Section 5
of the Act of 1896 is concerned. I am
not sure that I am disposed to congratulate the Government on the new possibility of confiscation introduced there.
It seems to me to run counter to what the
noble Viscount said as regards the proposal in Clause 7, where it is desired to
soften the law on account of the greater
use of petrol and the large stocks that
might be kept.
We come now to Clause 14, which enables by-laws to be made to prevent the
disfigurement of scenery. That Clause is
a new departure which does appear in the
later English Act, but I should like to
know whether any county council or other
authority has asked for its application in
Northern Ireland. It seems a little hard
to fall upon the petrol-filling stations in
a matter of this kind because, after all,
if you must disfigure the countryside there
are many other forms of building that can
do that just as well as a petrol-filling
station. I hope we may have the assurance that the power to make such bylaws will only be used with very great
caution. I am perhaps a little bit afraid
of it, because there might be too great a
tendency to disregard the provisions of
the Bill with reference to having regard
the reasonable requirements of
A petrol-filling station need
not be a disfigurement. There are some
in England which are quite attractive to
look at, but there are others which are
No doubt that point will be conto
motorists. That is very satisfactory. I also understand we are relying
on the Clause which incorporates certain
provisions of the Explosives Act to take
the place of the Hawkers Act of 1881,
which it is proposed to repeal. Passing
from that sort of questions I should like
to tender my most respectful congratulations to the Government on Clause 9 of the
Measure before us. I am sure the House
will understand at once why I am inclined
to be congratulatory on Clause 9. Clause 9
preserves for us in effect an appeal from
the decision of the County Council to the
Ministry of Home Affairs.
That is
form of appeal which of late has not always
been adopted. Whilst still being desirous
a
not.
sidered.
I was a little anxious at hearing that
it was thought probable that carbide of
calcium might be brought within the
sphere of legislation primarily intended
for petroleum and the like. I do not
know whether any attempt has been made
to use the new powers in the corresponding Measure in Great Britain as regards
carbide of calcium, but I should have
thought that the use of carbide of calcium
was somewhat declining at present.
It
Unemployment Insurance Bil) 82
Petroleum Bill
19 MARCH 1929
H


used to be used in a small way for bicycle
lamps, and it is now used principally in
connection with acetylene lighting, but
I should not have thought that its use was
growing. I should have thought that
electric light had rather put it off the market, but if there is any increase in public
danger owing to the transit of carbide of
calcium it ought to be regulated, and
there is no reason why it should not be.
I entirely associate myself with what
has been said as regards inability to discuss the First Schedule, dealing with technical matters, but I understand that
problems of that class have been more
than once before our harbour authorities
in this country, and I am hoping that we
may hear something about the matter
from that point of view. The only other
point is the question of the statutory
period during which the Regulations are
to lie on the Table. We have adopted
ten days, including five sitting days. In
England there are forty days. The proposal of five or ten days is not new, but I
am not quite sure that it is quite sufficient
for our purposes on the present occasion.
I have offered these several observations
on the Bill and I hope that when it becomes an Act we may find that it is of
some use and that its place will be
speedily taken by the Consolidating Act.
Sir GEORGE CLARK: I am given to
understand that this Bill is based entirely
on a similar Measure passed in England,
and I think it is extremely important that
our legislation dealing with a matter of
this kind should be in accord with that
of Great Britain. The Bill deals with a
very technical subject and one on which
we require the advice of experts. I
should have thought that the most sensible thing for this Government to have
done would have been to adopt the English Act in its entirety, and I would like
to ask the noble Leader of the House if
that is what has been done. The hon.
and learned Senator opposite (Senator
Leslie) referred to harbour authorities.
We in Belfast have had considerable difficulties in connection with this matter, but
we got over them without much trouble.
The Harbour Board has practically full
control of the shipment and storage of
petroleum and the law as at present in
force seems to be ample for the purposes
60 far as Harbour authorities are concerned. That is one reason why I would
like to see this Bill in accord with the
Act passed by the Imperial House.
a
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I should
like to say that the Bill before the House
is in complete accord with the English
Act. Only a few words in the English
Act have been altered in this Bill to make
it more suitable to local authorities here.
I may say that the Consolidating Measure
it at present well advanced, so I think
there is
reasonable expectation of
having it within a short time, although
I am not prepared to say how many
months will constitute a short time or a
long time. However, I do not think it
can be a very long period. Regarding
the point raised by Senator Leslie as to
a possible injustice being done to the
owners of petrol-filling stations if such
stations were cleared away in order to
enhance the amenities of the countryside,
I think that might very well be left to
the discretion of the county councils.
They would not be likely to take any
very severe action.
I think anybody who has toured England in a car will have realised that there
are many petrol-filling stations which are
not only a disfigurement but are absolutely
hideous. It is only fair that the county
councils should have a certain amount of
power in the matter, but I do not think
there can be any doubt that they would
not use that power in a sensible way. With
regard to the question of carbide of calcium, and one or two other points that
were raised by Senator Leslie, I should
like to give the information asked for at
a future date.

Bill committed to a Committee of the

"Maternity home' means any premises used or intended to be used for the
reception of pregnant women or of
women immediately after childbirth.

Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move in Clause 13, page 10, line 18, to
delete the word "or" (any premises used
or) and to insert instead thereof the word
"and". A similar Amendment has already been made in line 1 of page 10 of
this Clause, and this Amendment is necessary to make the Clause consistent.
Mr. LESLIE: There is another question arising out of the same paragraph.
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
deal with that in a moment.
Amendment agreed to.
I will
Viscount CHARLEMONT: There was
a further question asked on this Clause
with regard to a definition of a Maternity
Home. I may point out that the words
used "pregnant women or women immediately after childbirth" do not conflict
with the provisions of Clause 1, Sub-
Clause (2) of the Bill. In the latter case
we are dealing with the actual attendance
at childbirth, whether in a nursing home
or in a private house, and are not specially
concerned with the subsequent nursing or
the arrangements of the premises in which
the birth takes place. In the case of a
maternity home we are concerned with the
structure and equipment of the premises
and with the nursing arrangements as
such. We have, therefore, in the case of
the home to provide for the whole period
during which the women may be nursed
in it.
Mr. LESLIE: That is a satisfactory
explanation.
Moved, "That this Bill do now pass."
-(Viscount Charlemont).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
And a Message was sent to the House of
Commons to carry down the said Bill and
desire their concurrence thereto.
Adjournment.

(Amendment) Bill, the Second Reading of

Mr. GLENDINNING: I should like
to ask if the House will sit on Thursday.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Yes, I
think so.

CLAUSE 11 (Repeal and re-enactment with amendments of 59 and 60 Vict.,
с. 36, с. 5).
Section five of the Locomotives on
Highways Act, 1896 (which provides
for the making of regulations as to the
keeping and use of petroleum or of any
other inflammable liquid or fuel for
the purpose of light locomotives) shall
cease to have effect and the following
provisions shall have effect in lieu
thereof:
"(1) The Minister of Home Affairs
for Northern Ireland may make

Committee
THE SENATE
regulations as to the keeping and use
of petroleum-spirit by persons intending to use it for the purpose of
any class of motor vehicles, motor
boats, aircraft, or engines specified
in the regulations, and any such
regulations may exempt the keeping
and use of petroleum-spirit by persons intending to use it for the
purpose of any such class as aforesaid
to which the regulations apply from
the operation of any of the provisions
of the Petroleum Acts, 1871 to 1929,
relating to by-laws as to ships carrying petroleum-spirit, and as to the
loading, conveyance, and landing of
such spirit in and upon canals, notices by the owner or master of ships
carrying petroleum-spirit, labels on
vessels petroleum-spirit, licences for
keeping petroleum-spirit, and regulations as to the conveyance of
petroleum-spirit by road.
"(2) If any person contravenes or
attempts to contravene any regulation made under this section, he shall
be liable on summary conviction to
a fine not exceeding twenty pounds
for each day on which the offence
occurs or continues, and the court
before whom any person is convicted
under this sub-section may order that
the petroleum-spirit in respect of
which the offence was committed, and
any vessel in which it is contained,
be forfeited or otherwise dealt with
in such manner as the court thinks
fit."
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie:-
Clause 11, page 7, line 18, leave out
from "petroleum-spirit" to "notices" in
line 20 (and as to the loading, conveyance,
and landing of such spirit in and upon
canals).
Mr. LESLIE: Instead of moving the
first Amendment standing in my name
on the Paper, I think, perhaps, it will
be sufficient to draw attention to the
words which at first sight it might seem
desirable to amend in order that the
noble Viscount can give us an explanation
of the intention of the passage, and in
order that an opportunity may be given
for considering whether any alteration at
all should be made, and, if so, in what
part of the Bill. The difficulty that
occurs to me as regards those words in or
about line 18, that is "and as to the loading, conveyance, and landing of such spirit
in and upon canals," is that apparently
there is an opportunity for a conflict between the Minister of Commerce and
the Minister of Home Affairs, who is
taking power under this Clause to make
regulations exempting people using or intending to use petrol for various purposes
from regulations which have been made
in respect of the loading, conveyance, and
landing of spirit in and upon canals.
These regulations can be made under
Clause 15 of the Bill by the Minister of
Commerce. They are, in the first instance, normally made by the canal companies concerned, and those regulations
have to be submitted to and approved by
the Minister of Commerce, and the Minister of Commerce can, if he chooses, modify
the regulations. If the Minister of Commerce does modify the regulations they become, in fact, his regulations, and that is
where there is a possibility-probably not
more than a possibility--of a conflict between the two Departments. In the English Bill, the first Bill of last year, Chapter
20, the one that is in the most respects
immediately allied to the Amending Bill
before us that is, the Bill which came
into operation on 3rd August, and which
endured for one day, its place being
taken on the following day by the Consolidating Bill in the first Bill, Chapter
20, the words were exactly the same in
Clause 15 as they are here to-day. In the
Consolidating Bill of the next day, Chapter
32 of that year, the particular Sub-clause
in Clause 15 is omitted, and the matter
was dealt with by a more general Section
-Section 22-at the close of the English
Measure. The Sub-clause omitted was
Sub-clause (3) of Clause 15, allowing the.
Ministry of Transport to modify the
regulations.
The intention of this proposal is, I
understand, to enable the Minister to do
what he already does under the Act of
1896, exempt people who are actually
using, or are about to use, spirit from
the regulations applicable only to people
who are really transporting spirit in the
course of trade, or keeping it for the purposes of trade and industry. But there is
an apparent possibility of conflict of jurisdiction, and I think the Committee might
like to have some information about it.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: The point raised by the hon.
and learned Senator in the latter part of
his speech is correct. What would happen would be that if the words in the
Committee
Petroleum Bill-
20 MARCH 1929
tr

d
Amendment were left out the ordinary
user of petroleum-spirit, for example, a
man in a motor launch, would be subject,
in respect of the spirit which he required
for driving the launch in the canal or
other place, to those elaborate restrictions which a canal company may find it
necessary to make for dealing with the
loading, conveyance, and landing of
petroleum-spirit in bulk under Clause 15.
It will be observed that this Clause simply
gives power to the Minister of Home
Affairs to exempt a very limited class of
people, who only require petroleum for
actual use in various forms of vehicles,
from the restrictions which are intended
to deal with large quantities of petroleumspirit.
With regad to the suggestion of the hon.
and learned Senator that there might be
a conflict between the Ministry of Home
Affairs and the Ministry of Commerce, it
seems very improbable that this could
ever take place, but I would like to say
that the arrangement here is on all fours
with that in England, where the exempting power is given to the Secretary of
State, while the authority to confirm the
general regulations made by the canal
companies is given to the Ministry of
Transport. Under these circumstances
the Government do not feel inclined to
accept the Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: The Amendment was
not moved, and I have no doubt the matter will be amicably arranged by the
respective Ministries.
Amendment not moved.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in
Clause 11, page 7, Sub-clause (2), to leave
out lines 30 to 35 inclusive (and the court
before whom any person is convicted under
this sub-section may order that the petroleum-spirit in respect of which the
offence was committed, and any vessel in
which it is contained, be forfeited or otherwise dealt with in such manner as the
court thinks fit).
This is an Amendment which I do move,
because unless we have some explanation
of it it seems to me that we ought not
to insert the words of Sub-clause (2) of
Clause 11. The effect of the whole Subclause (2) is to increase a penalty which
under the Act of 1896 was limited to £10.
It is now to be increased to a possible
penalty of £20- that is, it is to be doubled.
I do not particularly object to that owing
to the growth of the use of petroleumspirit, but in addition to that power is
given to the court to order the confiscation
of the petroleum-spirit in respect of which
an offence has been committed. That is
quite a new penalty. We have got on
without that perfectly well apparently
since 1896, a matter of over thirty years.
The public generally do not like new penalties imposed, particularly penalties for
breach of departmental regulations. It is
not a popular thing to introduce new forms
of confiscation, and, therefore, unless some
very good explanation can be given of this
I do not think we ought to allow the words
which I suggest should be omitted to stand.
I admit, of course, that those words do
appear in the last English Act, and that,
therefore, we are only following the
English Parliament, but I do not know
that we are bound to follow every bad
example set to us, and I do not like taking
possession of any property in Northern
Ireland without compensation. That is,
perhaps, a virtue peculiar to ourselves.
Perhaps some explanation might be given
as to why this new penal Clause should be
introduced here. It will be observed that
a
similar penalty is not sought to
be imposed under Clause 15 for the
possible offence of contravening the
by-laws of the canal company. Under
that Clause the money penalty may be the
same, £20, but there is no power taken
there for the court to order the confiscation
of the spirit in respect to which the offence
occurred. For some reason or other a
distinction has been made, and, perhaps,
we shall be told what that reason is. I beg
to move the Amendment standing in my
name.
The
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: The Amendment which the hon.
and learned Senator has moved seeks to
remove the power of the Court to order the
forfeiture of the petroleum-spirit in respect
of which an offence was committeed.
exemptions granted by this Clause are in
fact exemptions from the provisions of
Section 6 of the Petroleum Act of 1871.
The hon. Member will notice that in
Clause 7 of of the present Bill persons
contravening the provisions of Section 6
of the Act of 1871 are liable to a precisely
similar penalty. It does appear to be
logical that persons to whom a concession
has already been granted shall be subject
to no less a penalty than persons who have
not received such a concession. In any
case, as the amount of petroleum-spirit
Petroleum Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
[Viscount Massereene and Ferrard]
which under the regulations at present in
force can be kept under this Clause is
limited to sixty gallons, forfeiture, even
if ordered, of that amount is, I venture
to say, not a particularly drastic penalty,
in view of the danger to life and property
a disregard of the regulations may entail.
I would also like to emphasise the fact
that the imposition of this penalty is entirely at the discretion of the Court, which,
we have no doubt, will see that it is only
imposed in cases where the gravity of the
offence really merits this course. The
Court is not compelled to order the forfeiture of the spirit, and can use its
discretion.
Under these circumstances the Government are not prepared
to accept the Amendment.
own
Mr. LESLIE : If I may say so, the vital
point of the noble Viscount's explanation
is that the penalty is limited to a small
matter of sixty gallons.
That does not
appear in the Bill, and without the regulations before us we could not imagine
that that would be the case. As regards
the question of a concession, I must point
out that the Act of 1871 has endured up
to now, and the position is the same now
as regards that. However, as it is a
matter of only sixty gallons, and the
power to confiscate is discretionary, I do
not think we need pursue the question
here. If necessary, it can be taken up in
another place.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.

of petroleum likely to be dangerous or
injurious to health).

Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in Clause
12, page 8, line 22, after the words
"1871" to insert words "as amended by
this Act." This is a small apparent
difficulty, and I think it is only in this
particular instance that it will be necessary to insert the words I have on the
Paper. If the Members of the Committee will look towards the end of the
Bill they will see in Clause 20, Sub-clause
(2) that-
Except where the context otherwise
requires, references in this Act to the
Petroleum Act, 1871, and the Petroleum Act, 1879, shall be construed as
references to those Acts as amended by
this Act, and this Act shall be construed as one with those Acts.
In all the other cases in the Bill where
the expression "Petroleum Act of 1871"
occurs, that Sub-clause protects them,
but in this particular case there seems to
me to be a doubt as to what it is the context does require. The difficulty arises
in connection with those moribund
licences which are being issued by Justices of the Peace. They are a local
authority under the Act of 1871, as
originally enacted, and the question is,
where is the context going to take us now.
The immediate context is the actual words
"local authority empowered," and if we
refer to Clause 7, Sub-clause (3), paragraph (c), we see that "nothing in this
agreement shall affect such offences," and
so on. Under these circumstances I
think that for the sake of clearness it will
be better in this particular instance to
insert the words, as amended by this
Act," and that those words should endure
for the currency of this Bill when it becomes an Act. When we get the Consolidating Measure they will naturally disappear, but to carry us over the intervening time, I think those words are necessary, and I beg to move accordingly.
،،
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: The Government have given this
matter a good deal of consideration, and
after due deliberation they are prepared
to accept the hon. and learned Member's
Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Clause ordered to stand part of the

Petroleum (Amendment) Bill [S.].
Order for receiving Report of Amendment read.

The said Amendment was, accordingly,

Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): In reference to the Petroleum
(Amendment) Bill I have a little information to give to Senator Leslie in reply
to a question he asked on an earlier stage
of the Bill. The Petroleum (Hawkers)
Act of 1881 lays down the amount of
petroleum to which the Act of 1871 applies, which may be conveyed at one time
in any one carriage by persons hawking
it, prescribes certain precautions which
must be observed in the construction of
such carriages and legislates generally
for the protection of life and property
from accidents due to the hawking of
this petroleum.
The Third Schedule of the present Bill
gives power to the Minister of Home
Affairs to make regulations prescribing
such precautions in the case of all vehicles
used in the conveyance of petroleum
spirit by road, and as the greater includes
the less, the provisions of the Petroleum
(Hawkers) Act, 1881, will, after the passing of the present Bill, be no longer
required.
a
Mr. LESLIE: I am obliged to the
noble Viscount for looking into the matter and for giving the House that information. It was, I think, perhaps a
necessary concomitant of
somewhat
complicated Bill that the necessity for a
particular repeal should not be imme,
tely observable, but I think it is better
that the enactment that is to be substituted should be laid down, and that the
House should be told accordingly.

Business of the House.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I beg to move, "That Standing
Order No. XXV be suspended for this
day's sitting, for the purpose of passing
the Exchequer and Financial Provisions
Bill."
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Message from the Commons.
The Commons have passed the Exchequer and Financial Provisions Bill, to
which they desire the concurrence of this
House.
Exchequer and Financial Provisions Bill.
Moved, "That this Bill be now read a
First time." (Viscount Massereene and
Ferrard.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: In rising to move, "That this
Bill be now read a Second time," I should
like to preface my remarks by saying that
I asked my right hon. Friend, the Minister
of Finance, to come here this afternoon
to reply to any points that may be raised
on the debate. This Bill, which is a short
Bill containing but three Clauses, deals
with two objects, the first being the allocation of £420,000 to a Rating Relief Suspense Account-£120,000 of which will be
taken from the annual surplus of the year
ending 31st March, 1928, and £300,000
from the Exchequer before the end of the
present financial year. If this provision
were not made, the total surplus in respect
of each of these years would, under Section
2 of the Exchequer and Financial Provivisions Act, 1923, be payable into the
Reserve Fund to be utilised as assets available towards the reduction of capital debt.
The Rating Relief Suspense Account set
up under this Bill will be available to meet
expenditure which would arise under the
Governments' De-rating proposals.
will be remembered that in Great Britain
it is similarly proposed to allocate portion
of the annual surplus of the two years in
question towards the expense of De-rating
proposals.
It
The second object of the Bill is to give
statutory authority to the Ministry of
Finance to guarantee short-term loans to
be issued to farmers to enable them to
purchase seeds and artificial fertilizers.
The amount which may be guaranteed is
limited to £250,000. It will be noticed
that under Sub-clause (5) of Clause 2 of
the Bill, all applications for loans must
be made on or before the 31st December
next. Hon. Members will, I am sure,
agree that the farmers should derive benefit from this scheme, which will enable
them to obtain credit at a cheaper rate
than would otherwise be available for
them. I beg to move.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved, "That this Bill be now committed." (Viscount Massereene and Ferrard.)
On Question, Motion negatived.
Moved, "That this Bill be now read
the Third time." (Viscount Massereene
and Ferrard.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved, "That this Bill do now pass."
-(Viscount Massereene and Ferrard.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
And a Message was sent to the House
of Commons to acquaint them that the
Senate have agreed to the said Bill without any amendment.

Adjournment.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: In moving "That this House
do now adjourn" I should like to inforin
hon. Members that this House will have
to meet on Monday next to receive the
Royal Assent to the Appropriation Biil,
and on Wednesday next for the Royal
Assent to the Exchequer and Financial
Provisions Bill, after which it will adjourn for the Easter Recess. The business to come before the House after the
Recess will be the Method of Voting and
Redistribution of Seats Bill, and I would
like to take this opportunity of suggesting
to the House-and it is but a suggestion,
although I hope it will be accepted by the
House that in order to avoid a formal
sitting for the purpose of taking the
First Reading of that Bill we should on
some suitable date take the First and
Second Reading together. I only make
that suggestion on the clear understanding that the Bill is circulated to Members
in plenty of time for them to thoroughly
study it before being asked to accord the
Bill a Second Reading.
Mr. LESLIE: I should like, if I may,
to support the suggestion with which the
noble Viscount concluded his remarks.
There is not, so far as I can see, any
necessity for bringing us here for two
or three minutes to go through the formality of giving a First Reading to a
Bill the main provisions of which we are
all perfectly acquainted with. As the
House knows, the etiquette of a Second
Chamber imposes certain restrictions
which have long been accepted as a convention of any Second Chamber dealing
with matters of that kind, and, therefore, it is even less necessary than might
otherwise be the case to have any interval
between the First and Second Reading.
I think it will suit the convenience of all
of us if we adopt the suggestion of the
noble Viscount.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at
half after Three o'clock.

NEW MEMBER SWORN.
Mr. DEPUTY-SPEAKER informed
the House that he had received a certificate from the Clerk of the Crown, that
Edward Sullivan Murphy, Esquire, of 30
Windsor Park, Belfast, was, on the 22nd
day of March, 1929, elected a Senator to
serve in the Parliament of Northern Ireland in the room of Sir William Frederick
Coates, resigned, on becoming Lord
Mayor of Belfast.
Mr. MURPHY took the oath and his
seat.
Belfast Water Bill.

Royal Assent.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (leader of
the House): I am authorised by the
Prime Minister, on his behalf, to acquaint
the House that His Grace the Governor
of Northern Ireland, in the name of, and
on behalf of His Majesty the King, has
been pleased to give his assent to the following Bills:-
