Brown's Divorce.
ROMAN
CATHOLIC CHURCHES
AND EDUCATION GRANTS.
The following Question stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Colonel
Pakenham:-
To ask the Leader of the House whether
it is a fact that Roman Catholic churches
receive any sums out of Public Funds in
connection with the Education Act which
are not equally available for Protestant
churches.
Colonel PAKENHAM: In asking this
question of the Leader of the House I am
moved to do so by the fact that certain
rumours are prevalent and are getting
more prevalent through the country that
the statement which is in the first part
of my question is fact and not fiction.
Whilst hon. Members in this House know
quite well that the statement is inaccurate
and is not really a fair charge to bring
against the Government, yet while
rumours are rumours, and particularly
in the present state of politics in this
country, they have a way of turning a
molehill into a mountain, and I thought
that before the molehill became
mountain we should, if possible, destroy
the molehill, extract the mole from its
lair, and see what the origin of the whole
trouble was.
a
Mr. LESLIE: May I, perhaps, add a
supplementary question of which I may
say I have given the noble Viscount private notice. I want him, if I may say so,
before he relinquishes the hammer which
he is about to take into his hands, and
before he finally upsets the box of tin
tacks which are already at his elbow, to
tell us, if he will, whether there is any
truth in another rumour which is also
very prevalent, and that is that the cost
of the training of teachers who happen
to profess the Roman Catholic religion is
greater than the cost of the preparation
of teachers who do not belong to that
faith. It will, I think, be within the recollection of the House that some four
years ago, in the month of May, 1925,
a somewhat similar question was asked
by my hon. Friend on my left (Mr.
Joseph Cunningham) of the Marquess of
Londonderry, who was then Minister of
Education.
The information given at that time was
that the cost at St. Mary's-that is an establishment, I understand, on the River
Thames which is frequented by those of
Education Grants
Education Grants
27 MARCH 1929
the Roman Catholic faith-was £100
18s 4d per annum, which included £3 for
books and 25s as a subscription for games,
and of that sum the Ministry at that time
was paying £76 13s 4d and the student
£24 5s Od. On the other hand, the cost
at Stranmillis, without allowing for books,
was £97 10s Od, whereof the Ministry
paid £75 and the student £22 10s Od.
Those were the figures given at that time,
but they are necessarily probably somewhat out of date now. I do not know
that this particular question has been, if
I may say so, authoritatively raised, because in the statement which we all saw
in the newspapers there is no direct allusion to the cost of the training of
teachers, but it is possible that the matter does arise rather indirectly in so far as
the financial question may affect the question of acceptability or otherwise to the
various churches.
I think that if the noble Viscount can
give us that information it will be of assistance in promoting the cause of truth,
for we all agree that any form of misrepresentation as regards the manner in
which public funds are expended is most
reprehensible and most damaging, not
only, one may say, to the prestige of the
particular Minister concerned, but it also
tends to bring the whole Parliament of
Northern Ireland into serious disrepute.
Mr. DORMAN: I have to ask another
supplementary question, and that is
whether equal facilities for obtaining religious instruction are afforded to all sections of the Christian Churches, and also
if facilities are given to the children
whose parents desire that they should
abstain from any religious instruction
whatever.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: In reply
to the hon. and gallant Senator's question.
I wish, in the first place, to point out
that in the question as printed, Churches
are referred to, but Churches as such,
whether Roman Catholic or Protestant,
receive no grants out of public funds in
respect of educational services, these
grants being payable to the education
committees, managers, and teachers, as
well as to other individuals who render
service in the schools. I know, however,
that my hon. Friend's question is
directed to ascertaining whether it is
true, as has been stated recently in communications appearing in the Press, that
are
Roman Catholic clergymen who
managers of public elementary schools receive any sums out of public funds which
are not equally available for Protestant
clerical managers, and I wish to assure
him most emphatically that this statement is absolutely incorrect and misleading.
The same grants, whether towards
capital expenditure or towards the annual
upkeep of schools and salaries of teachers,
are equally available for schools of every
religious denomination, and if Roman
Catholic managers receive grants for
building, for example, there is nothing in
the Education Act to prevent clergymen
or bodies connected with other religious
denominations from receiving precisely
similar grants on similar conditions.
The
Education Act recognises three forms of
school management, according as schools
are provided by or transferred to the local
education authority, are placed under the
management of statutory committees
known generally as "four and two' committees, or are retained under the individual voluntary manager.
course.
The choice between these three forms
of management rests with the manager
and owners of the school, and there is no
legal obligation on any Protestant clerical
manager to hand over his school to the
management of the local education
authority. If it is considered that
Roman Catholic managers by abstaining
from transfer retain advantages of a substantial kind, it is open equally to Protestant managers to adopt a similar
I may point out, however, that
the retention of a school under voluntary
management entails certain sacrifices on
the managers and people supporting the
school owing to their ineligibility to receive full grants from the rates, and I
am glad to be able to inform the House
that an increasing number of schools under Protestant management are being
transferred to the local education committees, the local parties interested in the
schools being apparently persuaded of the
advantages of transfer and of thus avoiding liability for maintenance.
As the House is aware, the Education
Act was framed with the object of enabling the schools to be conducted in
accordance with the wishes of the parents
and people concerned, and having regard
to the attitude. of Roman Catholics and
Protestants respectively towards education, it was not expected that a policy
of transfer would be found equally accept-
Education Grants
Adjournment
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
able to both these communities. The Act,
however, is so devised as to secure as
closely as may be consistent with the doctrine of popular control equal facilities
and equal grants for all classes of public
elementary schools.
With regard to Senator Leslie's supplementary question about the relative
cost of the training of teachers at Stranmillis and St. Mary's in Belfast, and St.
Mary's at Strawberry Hill, at Stranmillis
the Capitation grant at present payable
by the Ministry of Education is
£67 10s Od, and at St. Mary's, Strawberry
Hill, it is £70 16s 3d. But it must be
remembered that the Capitation grant I
have mentioned as payable at Stranmillis
is really not an accurate sum, because it
does not take into account the original
cost of the alterations at Stranmillis,
which is borne on a different account entirely. The entire capital cost of building and equipping Stranmillis Training
College is being borne by the Exchequer
as a separate charge, and if that was
taken into consideration it would add
considerably to the figure I have quoted.
At St. Mary's, Strawberry Hill,
£70 16s 3d is the total amount we pay, because the College having been originally
built by the British authorities we have
no further liability in regard to it. For
women the Capitation grant at Stranmillis is £60, which is the same as that
at St. Mary's, Belfast, which is for women
only. I hope that information will
satisfy the hon. and learned Member.
With regard to Senator Dorman's
question, I should like to say that equal
facilities for Bible instruction are given,
so far as I am aware, in every school in
Ulster under the control of the Ministry
of Education.
Adjournment.
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
I beg to
move, "That this House do now adjourn
until to-morrow week," when I propose to
ask the House to suspend Standing
Orders in order to take the First and
Second Reading of the Method of Voting
and Redistribution of Seats Bill.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at
half after Three o'clock
until Thursday, 4th April.
109 Voting and Redistribution Bill-
Second Reading
4 APRIL 1929
-
U
a

P
THE SEΝΑΤΕ.

Thursday, 4th April, 1929.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
Business of the House.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): I beg to move, 'That Standing Order No. XXV be suspended for
this day's sitting, for the purpose of
taking the Second Reading of the House
of Commons (Method of Voting and Redistribution of Seats) Bill.”
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Message from the Commons.
The Commons have passed the House
of Commons (Method of Voting and Redistribution of Seats) Bill, to which they
desire the concurrence of this House.
House of Commons (Method of Voting
and Redistribution of Seats) Bill.
Moved, "That this Bill be now read a
First time." (Viscount Charlemont.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The Bill,
the Second Reading of which I now rise
to move, is a comparatively short one,
but it is a Bill which I am sure all hon.
Senators will agree is of the utmost importance, dealing as it does with the
method of election of the Members of
our House of Commons. Briefly, the
Bill provides for the abolition of the system of Proportional Representation from
our Parliamentary election, and substitutes in its place the system of singlemember constituencies.
I do not propose on this occasion to enter
into the various arguments as to the
merits, or the demerits, of Proportional
Representation. You are all familiar with
the arguments about this very vexed question. Suffice it to say that the Government are convinced that however admirable the system of Proportional Representation may be in theory it is not in practice
suitable to Northern Ireland, that is to
say, with the exception of the University
constituency, where no change is to be
made. We believe that in proposing to
adopt the very much simpler method of
single-member constituencies we are interpreting the wishes of the vast majority of
the electorate, and, I trust, the wish of
almost every member of this House.
A great deal of criticism has been advanced in another place against the
Government's proposals, especially with
regard to the fixing of the boundaries of
the new constituencies. I will, however,
ask hon. Members to remember that in
dealing with a task of such importance
there are a great many difficulties to be
surmounted, and circumstances to be
taken into consideration. We have had
to avoid, so far as possible, deviating from
the boundaries of the local electoral
areas; we have had to bear in mind the
possibilities of development and increasing population in various areas, and so
forth. The result of the Government's
deliberations, which appears in the First
Schedule to the Bill before you, may not,
perhaps, be perfect, but the Government
consider that, having regard to all the
circumstances which can be foreseen and
provided for, it is the fairest and most
suitable to the conditions now existing,
and likely to exist.
Coming now to the various Clauses of
the Bill, Clause 1 provides for the abolition of Proportional Representation, save
in the case of the University constituency,
and prescribes that the Members of the
House of Commons of Northern Ireland
shall be elected in the same method as
Members of the Imperial Parliament.
Clause 2 establishes the proposed new
constituencies, as set out in the First
Schedule to the Bill, and provides for the
modification of the existing laws to ineet
the altered conditions.
Clause 3 deals with the rearrangement
of polling districts, where necessary, to
suit the new areas, and gives power to the
Ministry of Home Affairs to determine
any matter of doubt which may arise in
the interpretation of the First Schedule
to the Bill.
Clause 4 names the under-sheriffs as
returning-officers, and gives to the returning officers the task of fixing, subject
to the approval of His Grace the Governor, the places of election.
111 Voting and Redistribution Bill—

THE SENAΙΕ
Second Reading
[Viscount Charlemont]
Clause 5 allows the spending of an increased sum by candidates in election expenses. It will be realised that where
every individual candidate is fighting an
election on his own, the expense is likely
to be greater than under the existing system, where it is the custom for several
candidates of the same party to fight an
election in unison.
Clause 6 deals with certain minor
amendments of the existing election laws.
Clause 7 provides for the coming into
operation of the Act on the cessation of
the present Parliament, and gives power
to the Ministry of Home Affairs to take
action by Order in any matters of difficulty which may arise in the formation
of the first register, the rearrangement of
polling districts or polling places, or the
holding of the first elections after the
coming into operation of the Act.
Clause 8 deals with the repeal of certain enactments and gives the short title.
I beg to move.
It
Mr. DORMAN: I rise to oppose the
Second Reading of this Bill.
I occupy
in this House the position of a pioneer
representative of the youngest political
party in the State and in the great
British Commonwealth of Nations.
has been my lot through life, fortunately
or unfortunately, to have been a pioneer
in advocating many of the reforms which
are now upon the Statute Book of Great
Britain and some of which, in modified
form, are upon the Statute Book of Northern Ireland. I took that stand when
neither of the great historic parties of the
State could approve of, but rather resisted, all the attempts to get these reforms placed upon the Statute Book of
Great Britain and Ireland before the
separation of the Free State from Great
Britain, namely, old-age pensions, health
insurance, widowed mothers' and
orphans' pensions, and the right of
women to the vote on a footing of perfect
equality with their brothers.
I have also been a pioneer in the advocacy of the introduction of the system
of election which this Bill proposes to remove from the Statute Book, namely,
Proportional Representation. As an
Irishman, and especially as a resident in
Northern Ireland, I really believe that
no measure of reform which has been
placed upon the Statute Book has done
more to abate the inherited prejudices
and enmities that have without rational
thought animated the minds of many of
the electors-no movement has done as
much to abate that hatred and ill-will as
the introduction of the system of election
by Proportional Representation has done
during the short period of its existence
in Northern Ireland and in the Free
State. I do not intend to refer to its results in the Free State, but those who
know the Free State and who know the
previous political outlook of prominent
members of the legislative bodies there
will immediately admit that the election
of representatives under Proportional
Representation has resulted in fair representation for the minorities. In Northern Ireland the same result was produced in connection with our local
governing bodies by the system of Proportional Representation, which was
abolished by this Government some time
ago.
I do not intend to enter into the
religious aspect of the question because I
deprecate the introduction of the
marriage-may I use the expression?-of
politics and religion. Wherever religion
and politics have been indissolubly
wedded together and a man's religion or
his politics can be judged the one by the
other-it generally means the degradation
of the ethical standard of his politics and
the possible corruption of his religion,
because it removes from the mind of the
individual the capability for taking a
broad, and comprehensive, and Christian
view of his fellow creatures who may
differ from him in politics and religion.
The result of that was that in this particular area one particular party had a
predominating influence in all the representative bodies from the political point
of view, because they constituted the
majority from the religious point of view.
The minority, under those circumstances,
could never expect to get that fair and
reasonable representation on the local
governing bodies or in Parliament that
they otherwise might expect to get.
The results have been striking in the
extreme. Before the system of Proportional Representation was introduced for
the election of the local governing bodies
in this area, a small party-as I have
said, it is the youngest party in the State
-then had no opportunity of getting
representation on those bodies, even
though their rank and file was mainly,
113 Voting and Redistribution Bill-
Second Reading
4 APRIL 1929


if not altogether, composed of those in
whose interest various Acts of Parliament, such as the Public Health Act, the
Weights and Measures Act, the Food and
Drugs Act, and the Shop Hours Act,
and other Acts for the protection of the
weak against the strong had been
delegated by Parliament to the local
governing bodies I think a body composed mainly of the rank and file of that
character would be thought to be
entitled to representation. Prior to the
introduction of Proportional Representation we had no hope of attempting to seek
representation in the city authority for
the administration of the laws which had
to do with the health and well-being of
the community from day to day. Strange
to say, when Proportional Representation
was introduced the minority parties
immediately commanded in the City
Council of Belfast a representation of
exactly one-third. If my memory serves
me right the small party that I belong
to and represent in this House secured
ten representatives on the City Council.
The Nationalist party obtained five
representatives, and the Sinn Fein party
succeeded in obtaining five representatives, making in all twenty representatives
that law-administering body
which is composed of sixty members.
on
a
So far as I have been able to make out
there was no clamant demand on the
part of any large organised body of
workers for the abolition of Proportional
Representation in this area. So far as
my memory serves me I have seen no
clamant demand from the district councils, county councils, and borough councils for the abolition of Proportional
Representation for local government
purposes. If there has been such
demand it has not been a demand which
was tabled in the boardrooms of these
bodies, and consequently as it did not
appear in the public Press the general
community did not get an opportunity
of discussing the pros and cons of the
merits of the abolition of P.R. It may
be, and probably is true, as one eminent
statesman in Northern Ireland stated, that
applications had come from over fifty
public bodies, but we all know that governments have supporters in all representative public bodies which may be inspired
often by the Government itself to make
certain demands upon the Government.
I think I am justified in coming to the
conclusion that that is really what has
happened with regard to the abolition of
Proportional Representation in Northern
Ireland that inspired individuals on
some district councils, county councils and
borough councils have communicated
their personal desire for the abolition of
Proportional Representation, and the
Government has accepted that as a satisfactory demand for its abolition in this
area.
I have already said that in a local
governing body the minority parties
were able to command one-third of the
representation. What has been the result of the abolition of Proportional Representation so far as local government in
Northern Ireland is concerned? Here,
again, I do not wish to introduce the
religious question. That, in my opinion,
is not the right way to approach a question such as this, which affects all the
citizens of the State, irrespective of how
or where they worship God from day to
day. It is a question which affects the
life and well-being of the community, and
it affects especially the administration of
the laws, which I have no hesitation in
stating are not being administered
they ought to be administered by many
of the local bodies at present.
as
If the House will permit me, I will give
an illustration. If any person had told
me that before the end of January I
would make a discovery such as I did
make when I had the privilege of contesting St. George's Ward at the municipal
Election, I would not have believed it
possible. I discovered numerous instances where families of twelve, thirteen
and fourteen human beings were living
in kitchen houses. If the local governing
body were inspired with the determination
to enforce fully the provisions of the
Public Health Act, and saw to it that
they, at least, would collect the necessary
information to inform the public as to
the exact conditions that prevail in our
midst, the public would be shocked to
think that free, independent working men
and their families were living in a cubic
space of anything from 100 to 300.cubic
feet each, whereas if any one of them was
fool enough to commit a crime and was
incarcerated in gaol, he would have 800
cubic feet for a sleeping apartment. I
need not now refer to the report of the
admirable superintendent of the tuberculosis institution in Belfast, but I would
like to suggest to hon. Senators that they
should at least obtain
copy of that
gentleman's report. They would ascertain from it how many victims of that
a
115 Voting and Redistribution Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Mr. Dorman]
dread disease have been living under conditions which were disseminating the
disease from day to day.
The result of the abolition of Proportional Representation has been that instead of the party I represent in this
House having one-sixth of the membership of the Corporation their representation was immediately reduced to less
than one-twelfth. I understand that in
whatever conference was held prior to the
setting up of this Government here between representatives of this area, the
Free State and the Imperial authority,
it was collectively agreed that Proportional Representation was a necessary
foundation upon which to build this new
State, because it, and it only, was calculated to give fair representation to
minorities.
I believe that every hon.
Member, taking into consideration the
figures which I have already given, will
acknowledge that the outlook of the
Imperial representatives, the Free State
representatives and the Northern Ireland
representatives who came to that conclusion was amplified and proved to the
hilt by the results of the first elections
held under Proportional Representation
in Northern Ireland for the purposes of
local government.
I quite believe that the present Government having passed through that experience, having seen the result of
the abolition of Proportional Representation for local government purposes, is
probably banking on a similar result
being produced by the abolition of Proportional Representation for the purpose
of returning Members to this Parliament.
I
am justified in that because from
platforms the statement has gone out
warning the people that if they do not
remain united and support the Government, and only the Government, there
is a possibility that their present small
balance of power may be displaced. The
loss of seven seats may mean their displacement from power and the introduction of a different Government.
have no doubt whatever that the
banking upon similar results to fortify
themselves and prolong their position as
the Government of this particular State.
I
are
But the Government may discover
after all that the short experience of
Proportional Representation has done
very much to abate those old inherited
prejudices and traditions that the people
were liable to follow in the past. Some
few years ago when the party I represent in this House sought the promotion
of a candidate for a seat in the Imperial
Parliament we went out in danger of
our lives to speak in favour of our nominee as a most suitable man to represent
the people of that particular area. If it
had not been for the loyalty to duty of
the Royal Ulster Constabulary not one
of us when coming down the Shankill
Road would have reached our homes
alive. No fewer than five who were on
the platform with me on that night had
to proceed to the Mater Hospital for the
purpose of getting their wounds dressed
-wounds caused by the violence of
the supporters of a party that claims to
have a monopoly of the spirit and principle of civil and religious liberty at a
time when these men were performing
their duties as citizens of the British
Commonwealth of Nations and advocating the return of their candidate to the
Imperial Parliament. We took our lives
in our hands for daring to proceed on a
public platform for the purpose of holding a meeting to advocate the suitability
of our candidate to represent us in Parliament.
We can go up the Shankill road now.
It has been my privilege, as a pioneer,
since then to have contested no fewer
than four seats on the Belfast City
Council. These were four seats in four
Unionist strongholds, and the result in
some cases was that without a tallyroom, without a motor car, and without
a personation agent in a booth, I polled
one-third of the electors who walked to
poll. Even last month I had the privilege of contesting a seat in the Clifton
Ward, which is a well-known, historic
Unionist stronghold in the City of Belfast, and without motor cars, or tallyrooms, or personation agents I polled no
fewer than 2,254 votes out of the 6,000
which were recorded. This proves that
the results, not only in Clifton but in
Woodvale, St. George's and Court Wards,
where I personally contested seats, justify
me in saying that if Proportional Representation had not been abolished for
local government purposes in Northern
Ireland, instead of having a representation of four members, in comparison to
the ten we had under Proportional Representation, we would probably have
twenty members on the local governing
body. I regret that my physical condition prevents me from elaborating this
question at greater length.
Voting and Redistribution Dill-
Second Reading.
4 APRIL 1929
I wish to express my regret that the
Government, in the last Franchise Bill,
in the consideration of which I took no
part, or in this Franchise Bill, have not
seen fit, in response to the various applications that have come from the Rotary
Club and other bodies, to institute legislation for the prevention of personation
in our midst. This, I know, is a very
knotty question. On a previous occasion
on which I mentioned this question in
this House, the party with which I am
connected was accused of also being guilty
of that particular offence, but, so far as
I am personally concerned, I have never
known that, though for the last twelve
or fourteen years I have occupied every
position in connection with local government elections in the area.
are
I do hope the House will bear with me
when I say that I have probably had
greater experience than any Member of
this House for the past twelve years on
this particular question. I say here that
it is a scandal and a shame that thousands
of upright, self-respecting citizens
deprived of their votes by methods which
an eminent member of the Belfast Corporation has described, and which have been
quoted in another place that photographs could be taken of the place. They
actually shift their quick-change artists'
depots when discovered, and I have spent
five and six hours trying to discover where
they had been shifted to. And here I
want to say something on this particular
question. As an Orangeman, and a member of the Institution-
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: Nonsense.
Mr. DORMAN: My hon. Friend behind me says, "Nonsense." I would tell
my hon. Friend that I took the obligation
of that Institution fifty-two years ago. I
have continuously followed it out during
my life, and I find it easier to fulfil the
conditions of that solemn obligation outside the Institution than I would to fulfil
it inside. Six years ago last January it
was my privilege to contest the Court
Ward as a representative of the Labour
party of Northern Ireland. During the
day I met with an accident, at least I
broke my glasses. The personation
agents had been asking all the Catholic
voters who went into the polling booth
did they know they were swearing on a
Protestant Bible. One of my officials
came up to McTier Street and told me,
and I took a car down to the City Hall
to see the Returning Officer, Sir Robert
Meyer. I saw his deputy and got
authority to return and tell the presiding
officer that none of these men should be
allowed to ask any question of the electors, that if they wished to put any question they should put it through the poll
clerk.
a
Having defective sight, I accidentally
entered the great centre of the Orange
Institution in Northern Ireland in Clifton Street, instead of the Methodist
School, and I there discovered in
robing room a miscellaneous collection of
all kinds of women's clothing. Before I
left the precincts of that hall I was
assaulted. It was thought that I had
gone in as a spy, but I went in by accident, because I had not my glasses to see
where I was entering. The party that
can descend to the defiling of the headquarters of an institution that makes any
man take the solemn obligation of an
Orangeman, by transforming it into a
quick-change artists' depot for the purpose of helping people to steal other
people's votes, would descend to anything. And then they go forth to the
community and say that the Socialists
and the Labour men are unconstitutional
and disloyal, that they have the monopoly
of all these good things, whereas we are
really the constitutional party, who wish
to use all the power and the law the
Constitution affords us for the purpose of
protecting the weak from the strong, and
for alleviating the sufferings of those who
suffer in any way unnecessarily in the
community. I do hope the Government
will take early steps. I regret they are
not taking those steps before the coming
general election. Let me tell you another
story. I do not mention names.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: Russia.
Mr. DORMAN: Russia. I am not
concerned with Russia. I am concerned
more about the people in Matilda Street
and the slums of Belfast than I am about
the people of Russia. I am concerned
more about the poor creatures of Northern Ireland who live in inhuman homes
that justify them in demanding that
which the law says ought to be afforded
to them, but that which they have not
got to-day. I am not concerned with
Russia. The Russian people will look
after themselves, but if the hon. Senator
who has interrupted me would go to the
119 Voting and Redistribution Bill--

THE SENATE
Second Reading.
[Mr. Dorman]
Library or would get possession of the
late Count Leo Tolstoi's "The Kingdom
of Heaven is within you" and would read
the twelfth chapter, and then after he
has read that let him come if he can and
interject "What about Russia?" making
a comparison between Czarist Russia and
the present conditions that prevail in that
particular country.
I find it necessary, in accordance with
my lifelong conviction, to use all the
power that is placed at my disposal in
this House to prevent this Measure becoming the law of Northern Ireland. I
believe it will be disastrous, particularly
for the working classes of this area, if
ever again they are led by the old traditional hatreds and animosities, instead
of recognising that, as citizens of a common country, they should join together
to make the best possible use of that
country in the interests of all the citizens,
and that cannot be done by stirring up
strife, either religious or political, in this
area.
I
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM :
wish to support the Second Reading of
this Bill. I am sure we are all very sorry
to learn from Senator Dorman of his
physical condition. I am glad he made
it known to us, because it enables us to
take charitable view of the long
harangue to which we have listened. I
have always understood that an unhealthy body causes an unhealthy mind.
a
Mr. DORMAN: No, no.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNINGHAM: Well,
that a healthy body causes a healthy
mind. It is evident that our Friend
is suffering from some complaint at the
present time. He tells us of the number of seats he has contested for the Corporation. But he never won one, and
any self-respecting man who is refused
more than two or three times would not
submit himself again for election.
Mr. DORMAN: I have proved that
under Proportional Representation we
would get the seats.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: Evidently my hon. Friend has no selfrespect, for he has submitted himself
time after time to people who did not
want him, and has been continuously rejected. For what body of opinion does
Senator Dorman speak when he states
that this Bill will deprive his people or
his party of representation? Where in
Northern Ireland could a constituency te
mapped out exclusively for the party to
which he belongs?
Mr. DORMAN: Wait and see.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I
must admit and every Member of this
House will admit, that the Bill we are
considering to-day, and the constituencies
that have been drafted, have been
drafted in an extremely honest way so
as to give fair representation to those
who are opposed to the Government as
well as to give proper representation to
the supporters of the Government. Let
me say further, with reference to the
workers, that Senator Dorman cannot
speak with any knowledge of the workers. I have had considerable experience
of the workers in Northern Ireland.
have spent over twenty years as one of
their officials. Up to this day not one
of the principal trade unions has adopted
Proportional Representation for the
election of their officials, nor would they
touch it.
The nearest approach they
have made to it is the second ballot, and
that is something reasonably fair where
there is a considerable number standing
for a particular office.
I
But where is the body of workers who
are anxious to retain this Proportional
Representation system? There are none
more opposed to it than the workers, because it has been extremely confusing to
them. The workers are anxious to have a
simple, honest scheme, so that they will be
able to select the candidate they desire to
represent them either in Parliament, in
the Corporation, or in any other body. I
am surprised at the statements made by
Senator Dorman about the body he represents. And then, again, he has told us
of the great pioneer work he has done.
Well, there are some people who are able
to convince themselves that they have done
wonders in this world, and I do not think
it is right to deprive them of that belief
in their old age.
Mr. DORMAN: Hear, hear.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: Just
imagine the number of Bills which Senator
Dorman places to the credit of the party
he represents, and then remember the
length of time they have been in existence.

Where were they when old-age pensions
were introduced? There was nobody
opposed the insurance scheme more than

I cannot for the life of me see how he can

Then,
again, take his reference to personation. I

in Belfast.
Mr. DORMAN: No, no.

has practised it at elections in
than the party which
Senator Dorman says he represents. I
have known women to crowd into our
polling places and tally-rooms. I mention one very respectable place, just to
show there is no such thing as personation on the part of the Unionist
party there. It is the Baptist Church
on the Antrim Road, where the electors
are of the more respectable class of
people, who would not think of
stooping to such thing
personation. Yet, at one of the elections
in which some of Senator Dorman's
party were interested, we had dozens of
women coming to that particular polling
booth, and it was impossible for us to
stop them. They had the numbers.
They went forward, called out a number
and gave a certain name. The personation agents were not acquainted with
those who lived in that residential quarter, and were not able to say whether
those women were entitled to vote in that
particular district or not. Yet they
voted, and I am perfectly satisfied that
there have been a tremendous number of
bogus votes put in from time to time in
that particular polling station.
I defy Senator Dorman to contradict
that, for I know it too well. I set myself to try to stop it, for there is nothing
I detest more than personation. I said,
when I was a trade union official, that I
was satisfied to stand or fall provided the
vote was an honest vote, but I did not
like to go down by a dishonest vote, and
that is what we have had to put up with
in Belfast. And then these people talk
about personation as if only the Unionist
party was responsible for it. I admit
that some of our people were overenthusiastic, and when they saw that
other people were practising it to such an
extent, there may have been isolated cases
where they did indulge in personation,
but not to such an extent as was done on
the other side. Then, again, Senator
Dorman tells us of the change that has
taken place because he is able to go up
the Shankill Road.
Mr. DORMAN: Hear, hear.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: The
man has not told you that he lives in one
of the most loyal districts of Belfast, and
that he never has been molested. He has
lived there for years and years, and yet
he would try to make the House believe
that it is only recently that the Protestants and Orangemen have become so
tolerant that they would allow him to
walk up the Shankill Road.
Mr. DORMAN: I never said anything
of the kind.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: You
have conveyed to this House that now the
people are so tolerant you are able to go
up the Shankill Road.
Mr. DORMAN: The Unionist party
is not synonymous with Orangeism.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: The
Unionist party is undoubtedly composed
of the Protestants and Orangemen of
Belfast and the North of Ireland. Senator
Dorman has referred to the religious
aspect, and there is no doubt about it
that religion in Ireland has a considerable influence on one's political views. I
only wish to contradict the statement
that has been conveyed to this House
that the people are becoming so tolerant
that he is able now to walk up the Shankill Road, after he has lived for the past
twenty years in one of the most loyal
districts.
The only objection the people
of Belfast have to Senator Dorman is not
to him personally, but to the views he expresses on the platform, and the class
hatred he has preached.
Mr. DORMAN: No; I never preached
class hatred.
Mr. SPEAKER: I think the hon.
Member is devoting most of his speech to
Senator Dorman and not to the Bill.
123 Voting and Redistribution Bill—

THE SENATE
Second Reading.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I am
almost as near to it as I think Senator
Dorman was on several occasions. However, I submit to your ruling. I wish to
support the Second Reading of the Bill.
I certainly strongly support the arrangement of single-member constituencies and
the old system of voting. As I have
said, the workers are strongly in favour
of a simple, honest system, and I believe
it will give the greatest possible satisfaction, not only to the Unionists but to
those opposed to them. I beg to support
the Second Reading.
Mr. LESLIE: As the noble Viscount
has told us, this is undoubtedly a most
important Bill, and I venture to think
it is sufficiently important for us to concentrate entirely upon the Bill and the
problems it presents. It seems to me this
Bill is an example of the greatest use we
have yet made of the powers entrusted to
us nine years ago. The effect of the Bill
on political parties is quite an unknown
quantity. It may be that the Bill will
have other effects, possibly in the direction of an alteration of Standing Orders
in another place, following the example
of Westminster, as regards elections.
Possibly an effect of the Bill will be to
kill the suggestion that has been made
from time to time that a referendum
should be added to our Constitution. The
House will remember that in the Free
State Proportional Representation still
continues, and they have also the referendum. The two run together, and it may
be that the possibility of the second may
disappear with the abolition of the first.
The Bill has undoubtedly been asked
for. Resolutions have been passed in
different places in the country asking for
it. I think other Senators will bear me
out in saying that they have been
approached time and time again asking
them to use their influence, not only to
get Proportional Representation removed
but to get it done at the earliest possible
moment. There was undoubtedly some
disappointment that this step was not
taken before the election of 1925. (Hon.
Members: Hear, hear). Most of us are
aware of certain reasons that rendered it
impracticable to take that step at that
time, but the fact remains that it was
regretted generally. The country has
asked for the abolition of Proportional
Representation, and it is as certain as
anything can be that the country in that
respect is going to get what it wants.
a
If
I am not going to deny that the system
has some merits as a method of election
when you have your college of electors
confined in one space and remaining there
until the proceedings are completed.
the votes are non-transferable and the
quota is shifted, it is to some extent voting in
ballot box with
glass sides. But these conditions do
not prevail in a Parliamentary election.
I am not altogether of those who think
that Proportional Representation is the
special protection of cranks. It seems to
me that, if you have a constituency of
seven Members and a Member elected must
have something more than an eighth of
those votes which are actually cast before
he is elected, you must, I think, admit
that a man who obtains that proportion
of votes in a large constituency, such for
instance as County Antrim, in which the
number seven prevails at the momenta constituency of nearly 200,000-it is
scarcely perhaps fair to describe a man
who does get that proportion as a crank.
You may not agree with him, but I think
one should stop short of abuse. However
that is not the question again.
The main question is what the electors
want and what they are really likely to
get. I cited the example just now of
County Antrim, partly because it is the
county I know most about but principally
because I intend to use that throughout
my speech as an illustration, where I
want one, of the apparently admirable
manner in which the Bill has been drawn.
It will be remembered that the arrangements made as regards County Antrim
have been singled out for special and
ferocious attack, and I think it is only
fair that one Senator at least should devote his speech principally to defending
the proposals in the Bill in so far as they
affect County Antrim. I have just
spoken of such merits as Proportional
Representation may possess. I shall not
take time over its demerits. We all
agree, I think, that its chief demerit is
that it divorces the Member from his constituency. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear).
That in itself is a sufficient condemnation.
Those who are supporting the retention
of Proportional Representation have, however very kindly, but I am certain unintentionally, furnished us with an
additional argument. They say that
under Proportional Representation they
should receive I think I am correct in
saying something between 30 per cent.
and 40 per cent. more Members than they
125 Voting and Redistribution Bill-

4 APRIL 1929
Second Reading.
{


actually did get. I think the numbers
quoted were 17 they should have got,
and 12 which they did get. If Proportional Representation goes as it is to go
-a substitute must be found. There are,
I think, only two possible substitutes.
One is representation by class which has
no support. Nobody at this time of day
would suggest producing the possibility
of the three tailors of Tooley Street, or
any other number of other traders of any
other street describing themselves as the
people of England. As a matter of fact
class representation possesses all the vices
of Proportional Representation and none
of its compensating advantages.
a
The only other is the old-fashioned
territorial system. That is the system
which is to be adopted, and the Government has deliberately selected the singlemember constituency. I believe that
proposals were put forward from here and
there about the Six Counties for the provision of
certain number of twomember constituencies. These having
been rejected, I think it will be agreed
that the single-member constituency is
the class of constituency that is most in
accord with present-day desires. Now it
is, as I think, incumbent upon us as a
second chamber to examine the proposals
in this Bill with some care for we must
be satisfied-we ought to be satisfiedthat the proposals are fair and reasonable,
and such as will work out in practice.
We have also to be satisfied that we are
not in passing the Bill doing any violation
to those special requirements of the 1920
Act, which have been often quoted during the discussion, and have been misapplied almost as often. I am referring,
of course, to the Sections which say you
shall have due regard to the population
of the constituencies other than university
constituencies, and that you shall not
confer an advantage or disadvantage and
so forth on those who make a particular
profession. We must see how this idea
has been carried out in this Bill, and I
have no doubt that this House will be
satisfied.
The population of the Six Counties
according to the 1926 census-I draw attention to the date of the census because
one of the attacks launched against this
Measure appears to be that it is based
upon the figures of 1911-according to
the 1926 population census our population is 1,256,000 and some hundreds.
Those figures give us, if divided by 48that is the number of Members of our
House of Commons exclusive of the four
university Members the figure of 26,178.
That is the ideal number that ought to
represent a constituency. It will be
found on looking through the Bill that no
county, no county of a city, no division
of a parliamentary borough or parliamentary county, has fewer Members than it is
entitled to on that basis. Each parliamentary division has one Member for
every 26,178 of its population.
There are, of course, fractions in every
case. That always has been so and must
be so, but these fractions have been dealt
with uniformly, for the Bill has not in
any case either increased or diminished
the number of seats which were allotted
to any particular unit in the Schedules
of the 1920 Act. No difference has been
made as regards fractions. The deficiency
in County Down, which is 196 short, as
the right hon. and gallant Gentleman
knows, of eight times 26,000, and the
great surplus of over 24,000 in one of
the West Belfast divisions receive the
same treatment. That is strictly in
accordance with both the letter and the
spirit of the Government of Ireland Act.
It might no doubt have been possible
within the strict terms of the Act to have
gone about the business in a different
manner.
The proposals are attacked on yet
another ground. It is said that undue
favouritism has been shown to County
Fermanagh. It can be calculated that
the County Fermanagh has, I think, only
5,500 of a population to put towards the
26,000 theoretically required for a third
Member. It is suggested that that third
Member should be taken from County Fermanagh. It will be remembered that
under the 1920 Act Fermanagh and
Tyrone were joined together and given
eight Members between them. One of
the changes made is to separate Tyrone
and Fermanagh and to share the eight
Members equitably on the whole between
them. The proposal is that the third
Member should be taken from County
Fermanagh. If that were done Parliament would be under the necessity, since
they are forbidden to vary the total
number of 52 Members, of giving that
third Member to some other constituency
or some other unit.
a
I do not know if anyone who has
studied the figures can suggest
solution of the problem that is presented
as to the disposal of that third Member.
He must of course be given either to a
county or to a borough. I will take the
Voting and Redistribution Bill-
Second Reading.
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
county first.
The county that has the
greater claim is apparently the county of
Antrim, which has 8,397 of a population
more than seven times the ideal, but
County Derry would at once put in a
claim having only 200 or 300 behind that,
and Derry County would say that it had
contributed to the city. Tyrone would
say we are 1,900 short represented. You
cannot establish that anybody is entitled
to the Member if you are going to give it
to a county unit. The alternative is to
give it to a borough, and I shall deal first
of all with Belfast, regarded
borough unit. Of course, the case is at
once put up of West Belfast, West Belfast being a constituency with an approximate population of 129,500.
if you take Belfast as a whole, you will
see that Belfast as a whole is not entitled
to the sixteen Members it has got already.
It falls nearly 4,000 short of sixteen
times the 26,000.
as
a
But
The division of Belfast into North,
South, East and West is a division for
which this Parliament is not originally
When the four divisions
responsible.
were first formed, which was under the
Act of 1885, the populations of the four
divisions were approximately very nearly
equal. They ran from 50,000 to 60,000.
When the matter came up again in 1918,
and nine seats were given to Belfast, the
figures before the Imperial Parliament
were those of the 1911 Census.
Under
that Census East Belfast had 96,000,
North Belfast 94,000, South Belfast
83,000, and West Belfast 122,000.
Those figures that were available in 1918
were still available in 1920 when the Imperial Parliament made the allocation
which we know. It is a curious fact that
between 1911 and 1926 the population of
each of those four divisions had increased
with practical uniformity by 7,000
apiece. We are not doing any injustice,
I think I may fairly claim, to West Belfast by not giving it the additional
Member. It is safer and wiser to leave
each unit the same number of seats that
were given to it in 1920.
to
If we accept that basis in the
County of Antrim, of which I wish
speak rather specially, we find
that we have seven seats to be disposed
of. In the old days there were four
Members for the County of Antrim in the
Imperial Parliament. At present in this
Parliament there are seven, and there
will be seven in the future, so what has
to be done is to cut out of the old territorial divisions seven new seats. How is
it to be done? It so chances that the
County of Antrim is divided into seven
rural districts, each of which, of course,
may contain one or more urban districts.
Those rural districts are composed of
unions, as I think are all the rural districts of Ireland.
In 1898, when we passed the Local
Government Act, we did not make the
new rural districts coterminous with the
old baronies, and it was mostly on the old
baronies that the division of the county
took place in 1885. We have then seven
rural districts in the County of Antrim,
and we have seven seats to find. It is
not unnatural that we should look first
at the rural districts to see the possibilities there. The first rural district that you
take, that of Antrim, happens to have a
population of just over 28,000. That includes the urban district of Ballyclare,
and it is quite a convenient number, and
quite a convenient area. Then we come
to Ballycastle rural area, with a population of only 11,400. We look a little
further and we find Ballymena, with a
population of 45,000. Now one would
be far too small, and the other far too
large. You cannot do anything with the
existing local government divisions or
baronies. You have then to start quite
fresh and to face the question as to
whether a particular party is to receive
special consideration or whether it is not.
The opponents of this Bill, who are
usually the opponents of the Government,
claim apparently that all Roman Catholics are Nationalists.
Mr. DORMAN: No, no.
Mr. LESLIE: It is on that basis that
they found their objections to the provision that has been made for the County
of Antrim. I do not know that the
figures of the last contested election bear
that out, but the claim is that in dealing
with the County of Antrim, unfairness
has been shown to Roman Catholics.
I spoke just now of certain provisions in
the Government of Ireland Act, but it
is a two-edged sword. We cannot use it
to favour anybody, even if they are Roman
Catholics. We are not required to carve
out for them a seat, doing defiance to the
requirements of the population, and doing
violence to other requirements.
What is the position? They tell us
repeatedly that out of a population of
191,000 odd people in the County of
Antrim they have 38,000. That is quite
129 Voting and Redistribution Bill-

4 APRIL 1929
Second Reading.

true; the census bears that out, but
where are they? Are they scattered or
are they in a convenient place? A great
deal is made of Ballycastle and the
Glens. I have just told the House that
the Ballycastle Rural District, which includes some part of the Glens, has a total
population of 11,400, of which 5,587less than half are returned as Roman
The largest number of
Catholics.
Roman Catholics to be found in any of
the rural districts are to be found in
Ballymena, where they have nearly 8,000.
The largest number in a town are to be
found in Larne. The average over a
rural district appears to be 4,000, and in
urban districts 1,200. There are not then
a sufficient number in any one place for
it to be practicable for us to deal with
them separately in any way on that basis.
I cannot imagine why it does not occur
to the opponents of this Bill that from
the party point of view anybody would
have been only too glad to have given
38,000 people one seat, if they could
have been got together, shut up in one
corner, rather than scattered throughout
the county. However, we must pass
from that.
fact that Larne and Ballymoney are 32
miles apart as the crow flies, and no candidate that I have heard of is a crow.
There is also a suggestion that we might
have a constituency of 19,500 on the one
hand, and 34,000 on the other, by an
arrangement of the county which entirely
ignores Whitehead.
I think we may fairly claim that we
have made a map of County Antrim on
the fairness of which we can stand or fall,
and that we have nothing to be ashamed
of. We have deliberately rejected proposals like the provisions of the Scottish
Boroughs, and I am sure the electors will
be with us in so doing. The main part
of the Bill, as the noble Viscount has explained, is very short. There are two
Clauses dealing with the alteration of the
method of voting and the redistribution
of seats. The fifth Clause, of which the
noble Viscount gave an explanation,
relates to the alteration of the scale of
election expenses, but that is a matter
which I think is not for this House. The
machinery of the Bill itself is, I may say,
perfect; it is water-tight, and I think
The election code is a little
complicated, perhaps, but it is all there,
and you will find, I think--and I have endeavoured to study it most carefully—
that there will be no difficulty or trouble.
foolproof. That is what we have not
done. Let us see what we have done.
Up in Ballycastle and the Glens we have
followed the south-eastern boundary which
was fixed by the Imperial Parliament in
1885. We have not divided any of our
urban districts; we have not sub-divided
any district electoral division. The result
of that, of course, is to give some of our
constituencies a somewhat irregular outline.
I do not know whether anybody
has studied the electoral map of the
County of Antrim, but if anyone has done
so he will see that the constituency of
Carrick bears the resemblance of some
heraldic wild beast crouching for its
It follows the outlines of the
spring.
undivided electoral divisions, and they
in turn follow the outlines of the
townlands of which they were composed
thirty years ago before this Parliament
was even heard of.
There are alternatives, no doubt.
Alternatives have been put forward, but
they do not seem to come very near to
what has been done in England. It has
been found possible in Antrim to find one
seat with a population exactly one-seventh
of that of the county, and exactly the
same as the population of one of the
divisions of Londonderry. An alternative
gives us Larne, Ballymoney, and Portrush
all in one constituency, irrespective of the
The House will remember that when
somewhat similar Bills were under discussion at Westminster in 1885 or 1918
attempts were made in the Second
Chamber to introduce some variations,
but it is a matter of common knowledge
that those proposals did not go very far.
I think we should be well advised to leave
the Schedules alone. That is all that the
Bill contains. It does not contain any
reference to purely extraneous matters,
such as the provision of a polling station
on Rathlin Island, which has not been
referred to this afternoon, or proposals
to deal with the evil of personation which
has been referred to this afternoon, and
which I venture to say, is entirely outside
the scope of the Bill before us.
The Bill keeps itself strictly to its objects, and I think we shall perhaps accept
it even more readily when we reflect that
it is deliberately intended to be the precursor of an appeal to the country. That
is, it is the intention of the Government,
shortly after this Bill has become an Act,
to seek a renewal of the confidence of the
electors, and to go to the new bodies
which will be constituted in their new
C
131 Voting and Redistribution Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading.
[Mr. Leslie]
constituencies, and to ask them for a renewal of the confidence which has been
placed in the Government, and which, it
may be, the electors will say the Government has earned. However, I must not
go into that. I conclude by supporting
the Bill, and by asserting that there is no
unfairness in it against anybody.
Mr. LONG: I should like very briefly
to welcome the Bill on behalf of the
people of the County of Londonderry. I
can bear out what the hon. and learned
Senator (Senator Leslie) has stated,
namely, that there was undoubtedly a
demand for this Bill. I was, I think, to
blame to a considerable extent for the
fact that no resolutions were passed by
local bodies asking for this Bill to be
brought in. I happen to serve on a considerable number of local bodies, and I
assured the members of those bodies that
it was quite unnecessary to move resolutions of this nature, because the understanding was that Proportional Representation was to be only a temporary method
of election, and that the Government intended to revert to the old system of
voting at the earliest possible opportunity. The hon. Member opposite
(Senator Dorman) seems to feel very
acutely the abolition of P.R., and he
attributes to it a reduction in the number
of members of his party from one-sixth
to one-twelfth. I suggest that that reduction was not all due to the abolition of
P.R., but to the fact that experience had
enabled the electors to take the measure
of these persons, and that at the first
opportunity they merely got their deserts.
In the County of Londonderry there
will be no regrets about the abolition of
P.R., which was always regarded as cumbersome and difficult to understand.
There were, no doubt, compensations for
its demerits, only two of which occur to
me at the moment. The first was that
we had quite a number of Parliamentary
representatives to solicit for subscriptions
for halls, churches, and that sort of
thing, and I must say that the Members
for the County of Londonderry did their
duty in connection with that matter very
well. Another compensation we feel we
had in the County of Londonderry was
that we in North Derry shared with
South Derry the honour of being represented by a brilliant, capable, and painstaking lady, who took any amount of interest in her constituents. It is a matter
of regret, not only to the Unionist party
but to quite a number of other people of
a different political faith, that she does
not intend to again offer herself for reelection.
I feel that in the past Members were
representing too large a constituency.
Their interests were too much diffused,
and I suggest that it will be very much
better for the electors and for the Mem
bers of Parliament as well, that singlemember constituencies should be re-introduced. I should say I was already aware
of the feelings of my brother farmers, and
they were unanimously in favour of reverting to the old system of voting. I
took the opportunity of also obtaining
the views of eighteen agricultural
labourers who reside near my home, and
without any lead from me, and before
this Bill was introduced, seventeen of
them declared themselves in favour of
what they call the old system of voting.
It was simple, easily understood, and they
never made mistakes. One of the eighteen informed me that he had never yet,
in his experience, found a political candidate who entirely satisfied him. He preferred a large number to select from, and
his plan was to give his No. 1 vote to the
candidate whom he regarded as the least
obnoxious, his No. 2 vote to the next
least obnoxious candidate, and so on. I
can, therefore, bear out the statement of
the hon. and learned Senator opposite
(Senator Leslie) that this Bill is desired,
and I most heartily support it.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: I
will not detain the House with a description of the county of Down such as
Senator Leslie has given of the county
of Antrim, but I want to give a few practical reasons why I support this Bill.
Proportional Representation was imposed
on this area by an authority which did
not adopt it for its own country, and,
therefore, that authority did not show
much faith in the system. At first, after
the Treaty, P.R. was the system under
which county council members had to be
elected, but after an interval they were
allowed to choose whether they would
continue to be elected under P.R. or
under the old system. I may say that we
of the Down County Council have always
been a very happy family and the question of religion was never mentioned in
connection with any matter that came
before the Council. Under the system
of Proportional Representation it was
133 Voting and Redistribution Bill-

4 APRIL 1929
Second Reading.
found that when a number of members
were elected for one particular part of
the county what was everybody's job was
nobody's job, and no individual member
took any particular interest in his own
constituency.
After it was decided to revert to the
single-member constituencies everything
worked smoothly, and I should like to
inform Senator Dorman that under the
system of election at present in force for
county councils there are just as many
members of his political party returned
as there were under P.R. Each member
has now his own district and his own
share of roads to look after, and the
position is much more satisfactory than
it was under P.R. Senator Dorman
seems to think that after the passing of
this Bill his particular party-I do not
know which party he belongs to, he seems
to be a member of various parties will
be reduced in strength, but I venture to
prophesy that in the county of Down
there will be as many members of his
party elected to the county council as
there were under the system of Proportional Representation. I have great
pleasure in supporting the Bill.
Colonel PAKENHAM: In the few
remarks I desire to make I wish to lay a
little more stress on the relief that the
working man will feel that this absurd
system of Proportional Representation
is to be abolished.
I have very often
spoken with my neighbours on the matter and I have been in two contested
elections under that system, and I have
always heard the opinion expressed that
people do not see why they should go to
the trouble of voting for seven people
when they know only two of them. In a
single-member constituency it will be an
enormous advantage for a voter to be
able to vote for a man he knows, and for
a man he will be able to meet. It will
solve a great many troubles and it will
make politics far more interesting for the
ordinary countryman and for the ordinary Member. Senator Leslie referred
to the seven Members for Antrim. Living in a little corner of that county as I
do, I do not believe that a voter in my
particular village has seen any of those
seven Members for a long time. and some
he has never seen at all. This is not
likely to occur after the passage of this
Bill because the voter will get to know
his Member and will be able to interest
him in the things that require attention
in the constituency. I think the change
from P.R. to single-member constituencies is an excellent one, and I heartily
support the Bill.
Mr. BARCLAY: I wish to give my
support to the Second Reading of this
Measure, and as one who represents the
intelligent section of the working-class
people-
Mr. DORMAN: Hear, hear.
Mr. BARCLAY: I am very pleased to
have their authority to support the Bill.
I agree with all the hon. and gallant
Member (Colonel Pakenham) said as to
the voters being glad that Proportional
Representation is to be abolished. During the time that system has been in
operation it has caused a great deal of
inconvenience, and it was very irritating
to a large number of working-class people.
Under that system we have held two
elections for this Parliament, and for a
week prior to those elections taking place I
have been engaged voting every night.
People have come to my house bringing
their example cards, and I have marked
them for them to take to the polling booth.
Of course I did not actually mark the
cards on my own initiative, but I asked
the people for whom they desired to vote,
and I marked the cards in the order of
their preference, and they copied it when
they went to the polling booth.
All
these people will be glad that this change
is to take place. When they have only
two names it is quite easy to put a cross
opposite the name of the candidate they
wish to vote for.
At the last Parliamentary elections I
had the privilege of being in the City
Hall at the count, and there was a tremendous number of spoiled votes. They
were taken to one side and representatives
of the different parties were invited to
search through them and see if they could
find any that would pass. There was, of
course, an official present, and the amount
of ignorance that was manifested in the
marking of those voting papers was remarkable. Some people started at the
top and voted for the whole lot of candidates. Some were not even content with
that, but at the opposite side of the
names they put a whole row of crosses.
It was obvious that these people did not
know how to vote under P.R. The
change that is taking place at the pre
sent time will make things more con
135 Voting and Redistribution Bill- THE SENATE

Second Reading.
[Mr. Barclay]
venient for these people, and when the
time comes they will understand how to
act.
I cannot understand the difference
there is between the opinion of the Labour
party in Northern Ireland and that of
the Labour party in Great Britain. The
Labour party in Northern Ireland say
the Bill which is before the House to-day
is a rich man's Bill, that the man with
the gold will get the votes. They say
Proportional Representation is the system
that suits them, and that to do away with
it by adopting this Bill creates smaller
areas in which the rich man will have a
better chance of buying votes with his
gold. I have heard expression given to
that view. Now, what do they say in
England? In England they say that
Proportional Representation would be the
rich man's system, that it would favour
the rich man as he has the gold and the
motor-cars to bring the electors a considerable distance to the polling booth on
the day of the election.
Some time ago the Labour party at a
conference in England was asked to adopt
Proportional Representation for Parliamentary elections, and what was the
answer? The answer was that to adopt
Proportional Representation would be to
load the dice against Labour.
That is
how they look at it in England. Here in
Northern Ireland they say that to remove
Proportional Representation and to adopt
this Bill would be to load the dice against
Labour. In the mind of a working-man
like myself, who is looking for knowledge,
the question would naturally arise, which
of these two theories would be the best
for me to believe? If the one is right
the other must be wrong.
to be smaller, it is going to cost a candidate less to get round his constituency,
and, as has been said here to-day, the
candidate will get to know the people
better and they will get to know him.
As it is at present, in the larger constituencies men and women are asked to vote
for candidates whom they have never
seen.
He may be a black man, he may
be a white man, he may wear whiskers,
or he may be clean-shaven for all they
know. Under the straight-voting system they will both see and hear him, and
they will know how to register their votes.
What does Mr. Ramsay MacDonald say
on this question? Speaking a short time
ago on Proportional Representation being
adopted in the English constituencies, he
said it would give more opportunities to
the rich than to the poor. That is one
statement he made. Another statement
he made was this:
It will make it easy for the man of
the stump orator class, or the man with
the advertising faculty, to find a seat
in the House of Commons.
We
And then he said that it would make the
House of Commons more a debating
assembly than a business assembly. That
is what Ramsay MacDonald says.
have had proofs of this here in the past.
We know well-I am not going to mention where-how debates have continued
hour after hour into the middle of the
night, and even past midnight.
That
meant waste of time and energy on the
part of those Members, who had to sit up
at night, and the expense they were put
to in getting ham and eggs and Paris
buns at 2 o'clock in the morning. This
Bill will do away with all that, according
It will bring
forward the right class of men, who will
be capable of conducting the business on
business lines.
to Ramsay MacDonald. It would seem
to me to be something like the house that
is divided against itself, and, according
to the Old Book, the house that is
divided against itself cannot stand, and,
therefore, must come to naught.
In 1924 when the Labour party were
in office we find a Bill was brought forward for the purpose of introducing
Proportional Representation for Parliamentary elections, and it was turned
down by the Labour party themselves by
258 votes to 144 votes. That is the way
the Labour party across the water treat
Proportional Representation. Here we
are told that Labour is going to suffer by
the change, but it is impossible for Labour
to suffer by it. The constituency is going
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am very
pleased with the reception this Bill has
had in this House. The other hon. Members who have spoken have really replied
to the hon. Senator (Mr. Dorman) who
urged the case against it. Here I should
like to pay a tribute to the eloquence
with which he did so, although suffering
from a very severe physical disability.
(Hon. Members: Hear, hear.) I really
think the only reply I need give is to say
that Proportional Representation fails,
both in efficiency and in ability, to be
easily understood by the body of people
137 Voting and Redistribution Bill- 4 APRIL 1929
1:8
Second Reading.
throughout the country. Belfast voters
may understand it, but that does not
apply to the country, where a great many
mistakes have been made in regard to registering the votes. Senator Dorman
spoke of the evils of personation, which
hardly comes within the scope of the Bill.
What I think will really put an end to
that is the growth of an enlightened
public opinion. Until that time comes,
Armstrong, Mr. H. B.
Barclay, Mr. William
Charlemont, Viscount
Crawford, Col. the Right Hon.
R. G. Sharman
the only thing the Government can do is
what they have done, that is, to set up a
Committee to consider the best means of
dealing with this question.

read a Second time."

Adjournment.
BUS COMPANIES AND THEIR
TIME-TABLES.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I bego
move, "That this House do now adjourn
until Tuesday next."
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: There
is a matter I would like to bring before
the notice of the noble Lord the Leader
of the House. It is with reference to the
bus companies running from the city and
the time-tables which are issued in connection with them. I would be pleased
to know if the Government have any
means of insisting on the bus companies
maintaining their time-tables correctly.
I had an unfortunate experience this
morning. It is not altogether from a
selfish point of view that I raise the question. I am satisfied that a good number
of people through the country are put to
similar inconvenience. I travelled almost
two miles this morning to catch a bus.
The time-table states that the bus-
M'Neilly's bus-would arrive at Browndod at 9-50 o'clock. To my surprise
when I got within a field of the roadand that was at twenty minutes to ten
o'clock-I found the bus had left the
position and had gone further down the
road, and it was impossible to catch it.
I made inquiries as I went along the
road. It caused me some little trouble
to have to walk four miles to catch
another bus to bring me to the city. I
asked a few passing along the road if the
bus had passed, and they replied "The
time-table is no use as far as buses are
concerned." Surely the Government has
some authority to see that even country
people, much less innocent townsmen, are
not humbugged and treated in this
manner. It is not the first time I have
suffered this inconvenience although I did
not mention it on a previous occasion.
The Home Secretary has stated that he
irtends to issue licences to bus companies
which are prepared to carry on a regular
and reliable service for the convenience
of the public. Surely what I have stated
could not be described as a regular and
reliable service. The bus was timed to
leave the particular point at 9-50 o'clock,
and yet it had left before that time. I
would like the noble Lord to take
the matter up and see about it for the
benefit of those travelling in the country.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: In connection
with the question which has been put forward by the hon. Senator, I would like to
draw the attention of the Leader of the
House to the fact that buses outside the
city boundary stop on the county roads,
and when we consider they are an obstruction to traffic, we are told that we have
no control, and that it is a matter for
the police. Although the county council
has to keep the roads in order, they are
blocked by buses sitting at the end of the
city boundary. When a complaint is
made to our county council we are told
it is a matter for the police. I would
like to know from the Leader of the
House who is really in control.
Adjournment-
Bus Time-Tables.
THE SENATE
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD :
As chairman of the Roads Advisory Committee, I brought this matter of the buses
which have stopping places at the city
boundary before that body, and they
made certain recommendations. The
county councils at present have no power
to regulate the traffic on county roads.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I will
direct the attention of my right hon.
Friend (Sir Dawson Bates) to the two
questions raised, and will be able, I hope,
to give the information required on Tuesday next.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at
Five o'clock until Tuesday next.
Oral Answers.
Oral Answers.
