Laid before the House (pursuant to
Act), and ordered to lie on the Table.
Local Bills.
Mr. SPEAKER acquainted the House
that the Clerk of the Senate had laid
upon the Table the certificates from the
Examiner that the Standing Orders
applicable to the following Bills had been
complied with:-
Londonderry Corporation.
Bangor (County Down) Improvement.
Newry Urban District Council and
Newry Port and Harbour Trust.
Belfast Water.
New Member Sworn.
Dudley E. B. McCorkell, Esquire, took
the Oath and his seat as Mayor of Londonderry.
Address in Reply to His Majesty's Most
Gracious Speech.
Mr. SPEAKER having read the
King's Speech,
Mr. McCORKELL (in morning dress):
I beg to move: That an humble
Address be presented to His Grace in the
following words: -
Your Grace,
We, His Majesty's most dutiful and
loyal subjects, the Senate of the Parliament of Northern Ireland, beg
leave to offer our humble thanks to
your Grace for the Speech which your
Grace has addressed to both Houses of
Parliament.
In rising to propose that an address of
thanks be presented in reply to the
Speech from the Throne, I am fully conscious of the compliment paid to the city
of Londonderry in that, as Mayor, I
have been asked to move that Address.
Before touching on the legislative programme outlined in the Speech, I beg to
associate the city of Londonderry-and,
incidentally, myself-with the expression
of thankfulness for the improvement in
the health of His Majesty the King.
The widespread and reverent appeal to
Divine Providence for the consummation
of that improvement is a fitting reflection
of the deep regard and esteem in which
His Majesty is held by his people.
It is a matter for deep regret that the
absence of His Grace the Duke of Abercorn, our esteemed Governor, is due to
illness, and it is to be fervently hoped
that he will soon be restored again to
health and enabled to take up again the
reigns of Government.
The tenor of the Speech is one of
optimism, not the optimism of faith without works, but of works worthy of fulfilment. There is throughout a spirit of
progress towards days better both practically and ethically. Three of Northern
Ireland's outstanding industries are
mentioned-agriculture, the linen trade.
and shipbuilding, and I have mentioned
them in alphabetical order. Another point
touched upon is the progress of education.
This last is a vital question for this as for
every country since in the ultimate issue
all industries depend upon it, all social
services are interwoven with it, and, more
than that, our two greatest commercial
and
assets capital
labour-are the
better for it. A little knowledge is undoubtedly a dangerous thing, but gross
ignorance is a very much worse one.
The agreement between the Governments of Great Britain and Northern
Ireland in regard to the financing of
unemployment insurance will be wel-
9 Debate on Address.

26 FEBRUARY 1929
Debate on Address.
comed, if ratified, as it is to be hoped it
will be, and as regards the changes in
the law relating to National Health Insurance these are such as must be welcomed in any modern State. Not the
least of them is that which provides for
persons in poor health being admitted to
approved societies. It has been a great
drawback to some of these people that
heretofore they have laboured under considerable disability in that respect.
The increase in livestock is heartening,
as is the growth in the export of cattle,
sheep, and poultry, but a real menace to
these cheerful factors has to be faced on
account of the steady stream of meats,
poultry, and eggs, which flows into these
islands. This influx from other parts of
the Empire is one at which no loyal
British subject should cavil, but that we
should be a dumping ground for any and
every foreign country throughout the
world seems to present an evil which
surely calls for redress or adjustment by
some means whatever they may be.
The same line of thought may readily
be brought to bear on other industries,
an outstanding example being patent in
the linen trade, a trade which I am confident every citizen of Northern Ireland
will believe is one which might justly be
brought within the purview of the Safeguarding of Industries Act.
As regards the abolition of Proportional Representation as applied to the
election of Members to the House of
Commons of Northern Ireland, this
should prove a boon to those electors who
have failed to avail themselves of the
educational facilities at their disposal,
and the reversion to single-member constituencies should, I fancy, greatly help
the working of the franchise.
Other measures are outlined, all of
them worthy of the fullest consideration,
even if one or two of them may not meet
with universal approval. An outstanding measure above all others mentioned
is that in connection with derating.
Whether or not this will work out in
practice as in theory it promises to,
there can be no doubt as to its being a
very real and statesman-like effort to
assist productive industry both urban and
rural.
In conclusion, I would pay a very sincere tribute to the dominant theme of
the programme placed before us, because
it conveys a genuine wish to progress
towards those social and commercial conditions SO necessary to the health,
happiness, and prosperity of every Member of this beloved Province. It is with
great pleasure that I beg to move that
an Address of gratitude be presented in
reply to the Gracious Speech of Ilis
Majesty the King.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM (in
morning dress): To enjoy the privilege of
seconding the Address of thanks for the
Gracious Speech from the Throne, which
has been proposed in such an eloquent
manner by the Mayor of Londonderry, I
consider a great compliment, not alone
to me personally, but more especially to
the working men of Northern Ireland who
have conducted themselves in such a
praiseworthy manner since our Parliament
was instituted, and more particularly during the terrible trade depression, proving
themselves in every way deserving of the
favourable consideration which the Government continue to give them.
Before proceeding to the Speech I would
like to associate myself with the proposer
in his expression of regret that His Grace
the Governor was absent from our
Assembly this morning owing to illness. I,
too, hope he will be soon restored to his
usual good health.
With reference to the Speech, after the
many months of anxiety exhibited by our
people regarding His Majesty's health,
we all rejoice with His Grace to learn of
the satisfactory progress made towards recovery.
The continued improvement in our
shipbuilding industry, which has been
enormously assisted by our Government,
is now providing additional work for
hundreds of our workers, enabling them
to perform useful labour in return for
wages, which is much more acceptable
than unemployment benefit-(Hon. Members: Hear, hear)-and it is very
pleasing to know that the efforts of
the Government in this direction are
much appreciated by the workers.
the linen industry was in a similar position we would all feel extremely thankful,
but I fear this will require special consideration and the adoption of a scheme
which will protect us against the unfair
competition of the foreigner.
If
Debate on Address.
Debate on Address.
THE SENATE
[Mr. J. Cunningham]
The agreement arrived at with the
Imperial Government respecting unemployment insurance will, I am sure, receive the necessary support, so that the
unemployed worker can be guaranteed
benefit when he is entitled to make a
claim. The concession made to our unemployed workers through the Health
Insurance scheme, giving them a free
period of insurance, will relieve the aged
worker of much anxiety and secure for
many the old-age pension.
Our educational system is, no doubt,
considerably improved, but several years
will pass before we can derive the full
benefit of the Act. Better school accommodation must be provided for the children in working class areas to enable them
to receive tuition under more healthy
conditions, and if new schools were erected
more expeditiously in the various districts
and large centres, such as Belfast, it
would also supply much needed employment. The increase mentioned in the
number of pupils attending the secondary
schools for further education is not so
much due to the desire for same as to the
deplorable lack of suitable positions for
boys and girls leaving elementary schools.
Although the workers in the industrial
centres have little knowledge of agricultural subjects, we are always pleased to
learn of improvements which benefit the
farmers, and more especially pleased to
know that the Government are able to
render such valuable assistance.
The proposed abolition of Proportional
Representation which is at present applicable to the elections of our Northern Parliament will meet with the approval of
the vast majority of the electors. (Hon.
Members: Hear, hear.) Our experience
of the working of this system is far from
satisfactory. The people get puzzled
using such a number of figures and selecting their choice out of a number of candidates, and I am sure very few will regret
the change which will enable them to revert to the old and simple method with
single-member constituencies, to which the
ordinary person can become easily accustomed.
I remember an amusing incident at one
A rather intelligent
of the elections.
engineer, who was employed in the workshop with me, returned after dinner on
the day of an election in a very angry
mood. He told me that he had spent the
previous evening instructing his wife how
to mark her ballot paper, and being a
very sensible man he warned her not to
put any figures opposite the Socialist
candidates' name, as they were no good.
(Laughter.) But when he arrived home
for dinner, she explained as best she
could how she had marked her paper, and
he then discovered that she had been a
for
little excited and voted in error
the Socialist candidates.
Mr. DORMAN: Wise woman!
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: The
poor fellow was so angry that he returned
to the workshop to tell of the terrible
calamity which had occurred. It is not
surprising that he told her that it would
have been better she had stopped at home.
(Laughter.) The case I have just mentioned is only one of hundreds, and proves
how necessary it is that we should adopt
the simplest method for election purposes
if we are to secure a truly representative
Government. (Hon. Members: Hear,
hear.)
The derating scheme, which is also
mentioned, will be heartily approved by
all who have the interest and welfare of
It will relieve
this community at heart.
agriculture and industry of a very heavy
financial burden, and assist the latter,
which has hitherto been placed at a great
disadvantage when competing with other
countries.
The other Bills which are to be submitted for consideration will, I feel sure,
receive the attention and support which
they deserve. I beg to second the Address
of thanks.
Mr. DORMAN: I wish to congratulate
the hon. Senator, the Mayor of Derry,
upon his very eloquent address in proposing this vote of thanks to the Governor
I wish to
for his gracious Speech.
associate myself particularly with the expression of thanksgiving to Providence
for the recovery of the health of His
(Hon.
Gracious Majesty the King.
I should also
Members: Hear, hear.)
like to express the hope that His Grace
the Governor will rapidly be restored to
health, and be able to resume his duties
I associate myself
over this Province.
Debate on Address.
Debate on Address.
26 FEBRUARY 1929
with that because I happened to serve on
the lower deck when His Gracious Majesty
was on the quarter deck. I can truthfully say that all the men then serving
under him had the highest possible respect for him owing to the manner in
which he performed his duties as a naval
officer, and all of them placed in him
complete confidence when he became
commander of his own ship. I have,
therefore, as I have already said, great
pleasure in associating myself with the
expression of our thanks as I would with
an expression of thanks for the recovery
of the humblest of His Majesty's subjects.
The Speech itself, as the mover of the
Address has said, is rather optimistic, but
I do not think the present outlook affords
reasonable ground for that optimism.
We are rapidly rationalizing our industries. We are introducing the most
perfect system of machinery and efficiency
in all our industries with the unfortunate result that the future looks extremely black, notwithstanding the revival
there may appear to be in some of the
industries of the Province and other
parts of Great Britain. I was struck
during the last few weeks in reading reports relating to two of the leading
manufacturers in Great Britain who,
deservedly, have the reputation of being
probably the finest employers in the
world, and the most considerate to their
employees. I speak of the two celebrated
сосоа firms of Messrs. Rowntree and
Messrs. Cadbury. Both of these firms
are faced with increasing demands for
their products and an increasing trade,
but owing to this system of rationalizing
industry they have both found it necessary to dispense with the services of large
numbers of those engaged in their particular industry.
When leading employers like those two
firms, with an expanding market, find it
necessary to dispense with large numbers
of their employees, what hope is there in
the immediate future for the reabsorption into other industries of those
who will necessarily become unemployed
through this process of rationalization? I
noticed in the Press some figures which
show that Messrs. Rowntree found it
necessary to get rid of one hundred men,
and Messrs. Cadbury have found it
necessary to dismiss four hundred women
employed by them. Therefore, hon.
Members will see the difficulties that are
created by this speeding up of the
rationalization of industry. To me it
points to the necessity of the Government
facing this problem and introducing far
more comprehensive schemes to deal with
it than the Government either in Great
Britain or Northern Ireland has yet introduced to meet the emergency that has
undoubtedly arisen in our midst.
From day to day I am brought into
the homes of the working classes. I see
the extreme misery and want that is prevalent in the community, and I know that
all the efforts that have been made have.
not been sufficient to meet the distress
which is undermining the health of the
future citizens. Thousands of the
children of those unemployed who are
turned off the Exchange are suffering
through mal-nutrition, and in consequence they are building up a physical
frame that will not stand in the future
stress of life.
I wish to express my approval of the
attempt to co-ordinate the obligations of
the Governments of Great Britain and
Northern Ireland in connection with the
Unemployment Insurance Fund. This
is necessary owing to the equality of
social services in the two countries and
to the fact that those who are compelled
to migrate from Great Britain to Northern Ireland to seek employment are
entitled to the advantages of the Unemployment scheme in both countries.
Therefore, I think it is only just that
under those circumstances both Governments should pool their resources to meet
the charge upon that fund. I desire to
voice my gratitude for the improved
educational facilities that have been
brought within the reach of the people of
Northern Ireland, for, as the mover of
the Address has said, the future wellbeing of this and every other community
depends upon the education of the
people.
With regard to Proportional Representation, I wish to express my disapproval
of the proposed abolition of this system
of election. I do not think there is any
part of the great Commonwealth of
Nations where Proportional Representation is calculated to work with such
justice as it is in Northern Ireland.
Unfortunately in this particular area we
have traditions that have associated
Debate on Address.
Debate on Address.
THE SENATE
[Mr. Dorman]
politics with religion, and it is quite
possible that under the single seat system
a minority of the electors may have an
overwhelming majority in the legislative
chamber, as has happened in Great
Britain. Having regard to the fact that,
as I view the situation, there are
in Great Britain a million more voters at
the back of the Opposition than there are
at the back of the Government, I cannot
acknowledge that a system that brings
about such a state of affairs is a just
system. Under that system a Government with a majority of from 150 to 200
can be constituted on a minority vote.
In addition to that it is quite possible
that one vote in twenty-eight constituencies in Northern Ireland might form a
Government and in the other twenty-six
constituencies there might be a vast majority of the electors of the complete area.
It seems to me that the abolition of Proportional Representation means removing
the opportunity for all phases of political
opinion to have a fair share of representation in the legislative assembly of our
I should also like to say that I
approve of the proposed extension of
power to the R.U.C. under the Weeds
and Agricultural Seeds Act. Unfortunarea.
ately lazy, thoughtless farmers very often
allow their land to get into a condition
that is an absolute danger to the more
industrious and careful cultivators of the
soil.
Mr. LONG: I should like to join with
the hon. Senator who has just sat down
in congratulating the Mover and Seconder
of the Address on the very capable manner
in which they have discharged their duty.
I should also like to join Senator Dorman
in his expression of thankfulness for the
recovery of His Majesty the King and for
the improvement in the health of our beloved Governor. I have on previous occasions had an opportunity of expressing
the thanks of the agricultural community
for the introduction of the Derating Bill,
and I do not propose to delay the House
by a recapitulation of those thanks.
also associate myself with Senator Dorman
in his approval of the proposal to allow
the R.U.C. to act as inspectors under the
Weeds and Agricultural Seeds Act. In
the course of carrying out their duty of
patrolling the country the members of the
R.U.C. have ample opportunities of detecting this shameful practice.
I
I am very pleased, indeed, to see that
an amendment of the law in connection
with the licensing of public service vehicles is to be brought forward. That is a
matter that eminently requires to be
dealt with. Of late the number of omnibuses on our country roads has increased
enormously, and they are doing incalculable damage to the surface of the roads.
Whatever amendment of the law is made
to deal with this matter I think the views
of the local authorities should be taken
into consideration. The Ministry, in a
circular they have addressed to local
authorities, have stated that they will
give careful consideration to the views of
local authorities. That careful consideration might mean something and it might
mean nothing. In the past a lady was
supposed to consult her spouse about the
amount of her dress allowance, but suppose a wife came to her husband and said,
"Don't you think, dear, that the more
expensive of these fur coats would really
be the cheaper in the end." The wise
man would agree and the average husband
would believe that he had really been consulted.
In this case I should like real consideration given to the views of local authorities. I would not ask that they should
be consulted on every minor matter connected with the licensing of buses. For
instance, if John Brown wished to scrap
an omnibus and provide a new one I
should think it would not be necessary
to consult the local council as to whether
he should be granted a transfer of his
licence to his new vehicle. But if the
Government have not already drafted
their new Bill, or if they are not inexorably committed to the details of it,
I should like them to see that on matters
of principle at least, and on any important matter affecting individuals, local
authorities will be consulted. I am in a
position to know something of the financial position of many of the bus proprietors in Northern Ireland, and their
financial standing is indeed very precarious. Most of them purchased their
omnibuses on the instalment system, and
they pay slightly over £120 per month as
the instalment to repay the price of the
bus, and in addition to that they have to
pay about £30 per month for petrol as
well as to provide for insurance, wear and
tear, and the wages of conductors and
drivers, so that the House will realise
that for the man of moderate means the
outlook is not very bright:
Debate on Address.
Debate on Address.
26 FEBRUARY 1929
""
was
The statement I made at the outset,
that I thought some supervision over the
licensing of buses was necessary, will be
borne out by two illustrations which the
House might permit me to give, and these
cases really happened. Mr. A was the
pioneer in a motor service in a certain
district, and he ran it very efficiently.
Mr. B came along and said, "I want you
to sell your motor service. Mr. A replied that he was not inclined to sell,
whereupon Mr. B replied, "Well, if you
refuse to sell I will put on three buses
for each one you put on," which
actually done. He put on three for every
one Mr. A had on his time-table, with
disastrous results. I am aware of another
case in which the circumstances were
almost similar. But that state of affairs
will eventually result in detriment to the
public. The stronger will very soon shut
out the weaker, and the result will be in
a very short time that a monopoly will
be established and fares will go up. Now,
another reason why I think the local
authorities should be consulted is that
many of these proprietors, as well as doing
enormous damage to the roads, pass
through the territory and pay no rates
whatever. I should like very much if the
Government would give most careful consideration to the points I have mentioned.
I beg to support the Motion.
Mr. LESLIE: I cannot help thinking
that one's first view of a King's Speech
is to look at it and see to what extent the
legislation it adumbrates is likely to meet.
with universal acceptance in this House.
Taking the House, if I may say so, as a
whole, we are more remarkable for
unanimity than for anything else, and this
afternoon we shall certainly give an
example of that unanimity in our sense
of the debt we owe to the mover and
seconder of the Address in Reply to the
Gracious Speech. The City of Londonderry has done much for the Province in
the past. We hope, and we have reason
to believe the hope is not unfounded, that
it has begun to do something for the
Senate. The hon. Senator who moved
the Address in Reply is not the first Mayor
of Londonderry to sit here. He is, I
think, the fourth. Of his predecessors one
can only say that there were occasions
on which we were not permitted to hear
their voices. We hope that precedent is
not to be followed. (Hon. Members:
Hear, hear). And, again, we are completely unanimous in supporting what was
said both about His Majesty and about his
Grace.
Now, coming more directly to the
Speech itself, I am afraid that the
speeches that have been delivered, apart
from those of the proposer and seconder,
show that we shall not be quite agreed
with everybody about everything.
I was
not myself quite able to follow the remarks of the hon. Senator (Mr. Dorman)
in front of me as regards rationalization. I did not quite appreciate what it
was our Friend intended to convey.
Mr. DORMAN: The increase in un
employment.
Mr. LESLIE: But I did follow, I
think, what he said about the proposed
abolition of Proportional Representation.
With that I am able at once to disagree.
I do not think that we should at any
time allow the cry to be so often set up
that in a single-member constituency
where perhaps there are three candidates
and where perhaps the successful candidate does not obtain as many votes as the
aggregate cast for those who come second
and third on the list-I do not think we
are entitled to say that that man who was
successful is a representative of only a
minority. It does not follow that because
certain people vote respectively for B and
C, who are unsuccessful, while A is successful, that those who vote for B or C if
they had a transferable vote would vote for
C or B and not for A. That is one of those
matters on which we can never speak with
certainty and which I think we ought not
to take too much into account.
There is no doubt whatever that Proportional Representation, whatever academic merits it may have-and I confess
personal agreement that in theory it is
an admirable system-whatever merits it
may have in that way it has failed to
attract popularity. In Northern Ireland
the electors, the great majority of them,
demand its abolition. And, after all, it
is the electors who are entitled to the last
word in regard to the manner in which
arrangements are to be made under which
they are to cast their votes.
And when we come to public service
vehicles my hon. Friend (Mr. Long) opposite, the Chairman of Londonderry
County Council and a member of the
Roads Advisory Committee, indicated
Debate on Address.
Debate on Address.
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
very clearly the necessity there will be for
the most careful consideration and examination of whatever proposals may be put
before us. Like the hon. Senator I am
myself living in a country district, and I
see these public service vehicles in the
shape of omnibuses on the roads. I feel the
vibration of these omnibuses as I sit opposite the cottages on the main road. But I
see people waiting for them at the crossroads, and I know what a convenience they
are to the country districts, and how bitterly the country districts would resent
any material reduction in their methods of
transit over the roads. That problem will
be a most thorny one, but I have no doubt
with goodwill on both sides-and there will
be goodwill with goodwill on all sides
something satisfactory will be arrived at.
And now
one passes automatically, I
think, from what the Gracious Speech contains to what it does not contain. I did
not notice in the Speech any reference to
a small matter of criminal law amendment
to which I directed the attention of this
House during last Session. The matter
in question is so small that it seems
scarcely necessary to give it the prominence of an allusion in the Speech. I do
not see in the reference to education that
there is any proposal for an amendment of
the law. I do appreciate that the reference that is made in the Speech to
education is one which is justified by
events. I am not of those who are
desirous apparently of tearing away the
very foundations of the great Act of 1923.
I should not myself be able to support any
proposal that would cut across the face
of that Act. The Act was based on the
principle of democratic control, and it
seems to me that that principle has to be
preserved. (Hon. Members: Hear hear).
It is a little difficult to find out the
foundation of the almost touching faith
in the omnipotence and perpetuity of any
Act of Parliament. It is a little difficult to
find out from where that faith comes and
a little hard to make out what it is they
really desire. When the Act in question.
was under consideration everybody did
think, as intimated in the Speech, that
the proposals then made were very good
in educational matters, and that they
would in fact show continual development.
Everybody thought that and nobody, I
think, believed that that Act was the last
word and that it would last unchanged
for ever. We know that proposals have
been made both to the noble Viscount, the
Leader of the House, and Minister of Education, and to the Prime Minister himself
for certain alterations, and I am sure that
any proposals that are brought forward
will be considered most carefully and most
sympathetically for the objects which have
induced the deputation which is in my
mind, are objects which have our sympathy.
I do not know whether when these proposals are under consideration it will be
possible to give any further examination
to the possibility of development by means
of trusts. Such a possibility of the
Ministry accepting a trust to carry on a
school might be a possibility worthy of
investigation. Although I do not desire
-and indeed this is neither the time nor
the place either to support or condemn it
I throw it out as a possibility.
There remains one matter which I alluded to before, because it is specifically
in the Speech, that is the question of
maternity and nursing homes. That is a
matter which has been mentioned in this
House more than once during the last few
years. It is a matter in which the hon.
Senator who represents the University at
Westminster, although I do not see him
in his place to-day, takes the greatest interest and a matter on which he has special
knowledge. I hope that when the Bill
comes before us we shall have the advantage of his counsels. It is a matter which,
as I
say,
has been mentioned before, and I
hope it will be taken up without any further unnecessary delay. Taking the
speech as a whole I need scarcely say I
am in full support of the Address in Reply
which has been moved and seconded.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): It is with pleasure that I
rise as Leader of the House to address
you on the opening of a Session which
promises to prove a most interesting one.
At the outset I must say that it is a real
pleasure to me personally, as I am sure it
is to us all, to be able to join in the expressions of gratitude at the progress of
His Majesty. Everyone, I think, must
have been impressed at the general nature
of the anxiety which has prevailed
amongst all classes and creeds, not only in
the United Kingdom, but throughout the
Empire and beyond it, during the dark
months through which we have passed,
P
Debate on Address.
Debate on Address.
26 FEBRUARY 1929
and it is with heartfelt thanks that we
welcome the news of our King's increasing
return to strength.
We are deeply concerned at the illness
which prevents the presence of His Grace
the Governor at our opening ceremony,
and trust that he will soon be restored to
health again.
I am delighted to welcome here to-day
the hon. Senator who has moved the
Address in such eloquent terms.
I hope
that we shall often hear him in our debates, both for the sake of his own personal ability and on account of his official
position as Mayor of the City of Londonderry. Nor must I omit to congratulate
the Seconder of the Motion, who has long
been a Member of this House, and to
whose contributions to the debates we
always listen with interest. (Hon.
Members: Hear, hear).
I shall now deal as briefly as I can with
the proposed legislation indicated in the
Gracious Speech from the Throne. With
regard to the Local Government Finance
Bill, this is one of the most important
Bills we shall be required to consider. It
will provide the arrangements for bringing into force the derating scheme which
has already been announced. In the Imperial Parliament the corresponding Bill
was very much wider in scope, and, in
addition to dealing with derating, provided for a_re-arrangement of local
government. The Government of Northern Ireland did not consider it advisable
at this late stage in the life of this
Parliament to undertake a comprehensive
scheme of local government reform, because in Northern Ireland a very much
more comprehensive scheme is required
than in Great Britain, and a Bill for the
purpose would be one of very great magnitude. How formidable is the task may
be gauged by the fact that the Imperial
Bill contains 115 Clauses and 12 lengthy
Schedules, and, as I have already pointed
out, many matters which were not included in that Bill will have to be dealt
with in any comprehensive scheme for
local government reform in Northern
Ireland.
The Bill which will be brought before
you will provide for freeing agricultural
land from all rates other than rates from
malicious injuries and drainage rates, and
for freeing hereditaments in which productive industry is carried on from threefourths of the local rates. It will also
provide that freight-transport hereditaments, such as railways, docks, and so
forth, shall in future only pay to the local
authorities one-fourth of the local rates,
but in this case the relief must be passed
on by means of a reduction in the freights
charged for certain specified commodities.
The Government trusts that the relief
given will bring about an improvement in
the present condition of industry and
agriculture.
That our industry and agriculture need
some such help is only too well known to
all. Conditions in the linen industry remain difficult, due to a reduction in exports, and the difficulty experienced in
competing with the cheaper and less
durable linens produced by Continental
competitors in the home market-with
regard to which I may say that I am personally in complete sympathy with the
Mover of the Address in his hope that
some means may be found for dealing with
such competition, which in addition to
the increase in price of raw materials,
large supplies of which are controlled by
monopolies of foreign Governments, and
the considerable decline in the world consumption of linen goods, is responsible to
a large extent for the depression in this
particular industry.
Steps are being taken for the carrying
out of an extended publicity campaign
throughout the home, dominion and
foreign markets, towards the cost of which
the Government has undertaken to contribute 15s for every £ subscribed by the
trade, up to a maximum of £17,500 for
one year. A scheme of price standardisation has also been formulated, and a
very comprehensive and striking exhibit
of linens has been organized for the British
Industries Fair. It will be seen, therefore, that those responsible are by no
means inactive in the taking of steps for
the improvement of this industry.
I am glad to say that a brighter picture
can be painted of present conditions in
the shipbuilding industry. Last year's
output showed an increase on the previous
year's, when allowance is made for a
period during which one of the yards was
closed down altogether. Both firms have
at present a large volume of new work on
hand, and as work proceeds on these orders
employment will steadily increase.
Debate on Address.
Debate on Address.
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
Agriculture has suffered severely from
the general depression, but it is hoped
that the large amount of relief from rates
which the farmers will enjoy under the derating proposals, coupled with the beneficial effect of the recent legislation for
the inspection of potatoes for export and
the marketing of eggs, which is now actually in operation, will tend to improve
the farmer's lot, which has not been a
happy one for some time.
In 1926 an agreement was made between the Ministry of Finance and the
Imperial Treasury-which agreement was
subsequently ratified by Acts of both
Parliaments-with the object of maintaining the Northern Ireland Unemployment Insurance Fund in parity with the
British Fund by means of special payments from the Treasuries of Great
Britain and Northern Ireland. The
original agreement was due to terminate.
on the 31st March, 1930, but it will be
gratifying to us all to learn that the
Treasuries have further agreed to put the
matter on a permanent statutory footing.
This further agreement will, of course, require the legislative sanction of the Imperial and Northern Ireland Parliaments,
and a Bill for this purpose will, in due
course, be introduced.
The total number of persons registered
for employment now stands at 38,844.
The number, though higher than it was
a year ago, shows a decrease of 10,025
since the end of July, due to improved
conditions in the linen industry and to
the increased activity in the shipbuilding
and engineering group. The additional
relief schemes which have been sanctioned
bring the total number of such schemes
which have been approved to 1,020,
representing a capital expenditure of
£6,052,748. Of this sum, £2,758,770
represents wages in respect of which the
Government grant amounts to £1,609,838.
With regard to what'Senator Dorman said
about rationalizing machinery, I am afraid
that is no new thing. Many hon. Senators
will probably remember that a similar
outcry arose some hundred years ago
when Arkwright introduced his improvement in spinning, and one can only hope
that the conditions of trade will enable
those who are displaced in one industry
to be re-absorbed into another.
As regards unemployment in general, I can only
say it is a spectre that stands behind the
Governments of both Great Britain and
Northern Ireland, and we can only take
every step in our power to remove it. I
think we have done
a great deal to
eradicate the disease, the principal causes
of which lie outside our scope of action
It has been decided to establish a
Juvenile Employment Exchange, where
young people can be dealt with apart from
adults, and, in conjunction with it, a
Juvenile Training Centre.
The training at the Centre will provide
continuation classes in certain subjects,
and there will be a curriculum which will
interest and benefit, I hope, the juvenile
unemployed and make them more readily
employable.
From the beginning of this year two
important changes in health insurance.
practice came into operation. There is
first of all the extension of the "free
period" for unemployed persons-formerly one year in normal cases- -which will
now be at least eighteen months, and may
be almost two years. Moreover, persons
who have a reasonably good insurance history will be granted, in the event of
genuine unemployment, a further free
period of one year during which they will
be entitled to reduced benefits; and persons who were sixty years of age or upwards at the cesssation of employment,
and have been at least ten years in insurance, can have this period still further extended, but without ordinary cash benefits, until they reach the age of sixty-five
years, when they will normally become
entitled to contributory pensions.
as
The other change is the establishment
of a special insurance section for those
persons who, by reason of ill health, are
unable to gain admission to an Approved
Society and who hitherto have not,
deposit contributors, been able to participate fully in the benefits provided under
the National Health Insurance scheme.
In future such persons will be enabled to
enjoy the full statutory benefits.
The Irish Bankrupt and Insolvent Act,
1857, still stands as the corner stone, as it
were, of our Bankruptcy Code, and it may
therefore be said that the law of bankruptcy in Ireland is just over seventy years
old. In this respect we are far behind
Great Britain. There the whole law of
insolvency, relating both to bankruptcy
Debate on Address.
Adjournment.
26 FEBRUARY 1929
and to private arrangement, was revised
and consolidated in 1913. Bankruptcy
was given a separate Act in 1914, and
even this Statute was further amended in
certain important particulars in 1926 by
an Act which owed its inspiration largely
to Chambers of Commerce, bankers and
business men generally, who desired to see
the law still further modernized to bring
it more into accord with post-war business
conditions.
In September, 1926, a representative
expert Committee was set up by the
Minister of Commerce to consider what
amendments in this important branch of
commercial law experience had shown to
be desirable. The Government has decided to give legislative effect to this Committee's Report in a Bill which will be
submitted to Parliament in the Session on
which we are about to enter. This Bill
will not only modernize the law in a way
best suited to local conditions in Northern
Ireland, but it will also embody most of
the best features of the recent developments in bankruptcy law in Great Britain
to which reference has already been made.
The Industrial and Provident Societies
Amendment Bill is designed to remedy a
defect in Section 22 of the Industrial and
Provident Societies Act of 1893. That
section provides that the rules of a registered society shall bind it and all its
members and all
persons claiming
through them just as if each member had
covenanted to conform thereto. It occasionally happens that the rules of societies include a rule requiring a member to
take up and pay for further shares from
time to time; or, similarly, one society
may be under obligation to take up further shares from time to time in another
society such as a parent society or an
organizing body, or rules imposing such
liabilities may be passed. It may thus
happen that a member of a society is
placed in the position of having to take
up and pay for additional shares against
his will.
The effect of the present Measure, which
in non-contentious, will be that members
of Industrial and Provident Societies will
be protected in the same way as shareholders of a company are protected by the
existing law from having increased liabilities imposed upon them without their
consent. The Measure is similar in every
respect to an Act passed by the Imperial
Parliament last Session.
Many of us will welcome the Bill providing for the abolition of the system of
Proportional Representation and the
creation of single-member constituencies.
Certainly the simplicity of the alternative.
system which is proposed will be welcome
to all-not forgetting the unfortunate
lady and gentleman to whom the Seconder
of the Address referred. Only one, so far
as I know, voted, but unfortunately the
nan also was implicated.
The Betting (Juvenile Messengers) Bill
will follow the lines of a Measure recently
passed by the Imperial Parliament, prohibiting the utilization of young persons
as messengers for the purpose of placing
bets. I feel sure that a Measure of this
nature, which aims at protecting children
from the demoralizing effect of association
in such traffic, will receive the support of
every Member of this House.
The Petroleum Bill is introduced with
the object of assimilating the law in Northern Ireland to that now in force in
Great Britain, under which additional
safeguards to meet the ever-increasing use
of petrol and other inflammable fuels are
provided.
The Imperial Parliament has already
dealt with the Registration of Nursing
and Maternity Homes, and legislation
dealing with this question in Northern
Ireland will be brought before you at an
early date.
Now, as regards traffic control, it was
announced last Session that a Bill would
be introduced dealing with the control
of road passenger transport. A system
on the lines which were discussed last Session in another place has already been.
put in force under the existing powers,
but it is desirable that the position should
be clarified, and a Bill will be submitted
for this purpose. I am sure the Bill will
provide for consultation with the local
authorities as Senator Long has suggested.
I might say that the whole Bill has been
designed to remove such misfortunes as
befell the Mr. A of Senator Long's speech.
As the housing question is one of continuous and pressing interest, I think it
might be well to refer to it. Up to the
end of last month the total number of
houses approved under the Housing Acts
was 14,086, of which 9,989 were completed. The numbers of houses completed
in the last three months, as compared
with the corresponding figure for the previous year, will give some idea of the extent to which the production of houses has
Debate on Address.
Debate on Address.
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
been increased by the policy of increasing
the subsidy and limiting it to the type
of house which was most urgently needed,
that is, the small house within the means
of the working class. For this year the
figure for houses completed during the last
three months is 1,169 as against 482 in
the same period of the previous year.
These numbers will make it plain that
homes of this type are being provided at
a rapid rate.
In conclusion I should like to say a very
few words about my own Department. I
am happy to be able to report that for
the most part satisfactory progress is being
made by the Borough and Regional Committees in the assumption of their duties
under the Act of 1923, and the results are
apparent in the provision of new and commodious schools, medical attention to
school children, and the treatment of
afflicted children. The establishment of
a special school for mentally defective
children is also under consideration.
(Hon. Members: Hear, hear.)
Scholarships have been established and
school attendance has been considerably
improved the practice of leaving school
before the age of 14 having been checked.
The numbers of pupils in both secondary
and technical schools show a marked increase an increase which I do not think
any Minister of Education would admit
was caused by what the Seconder of the
Address mentioned, that is to say, entirely
caused by lack of employment. Possibly
that may have a contributory bearing on
it, but I think it is a desire for education
that is affecting all classes.
It was, of course, inevitable that the
change in the system of management
brought about by the Act of 1923
should give rise to criticism, and that
in the working of the new system
cases of dissatisfaction would arise. It
is quite true what Senator Leslie referred
to that some changes in the Act may
eventually be contemplated, because there
is no Act that can be expected to last for
ever, otherwise we should be bound by
legislation which came during the time of
William the Conqueror or possibly before. I can only say any legislation of
the kind contemplated will be subject to
most earnest consideration for a fairly
long period, during which all the authorities in being will have to be consulted
and it will be gone into with the greatest
sympathy on my own part. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear.)
I have endeavoured, and shall always
endeavour, to meet such cases as have
been put before me to the best of my
power, and to receive with the utmost
sympathy suggestions which may be put
forward from any quarter which will help
to secure the best possible educational
facilities for every one of our children.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Resolved: That a humble Address be
presented to His Grace in the following
words:
Your Grace,
We, His Majesty's most dutiful and
loyal subjects, the Senate of the Parliament of Northern Ireland, beg leave to
offer our humble thanks to Your Grace
for the Speech which Your Grace has
addressed to both Houses of Parliament.
Ordered: That the said Address be presented to His Grace by Privy Councillors
of Northern Ireland.

31 Provident Societies Bill

THE SENATE
Nursing Homes Bill-
Industrial and Provident Societies
(Amendment) Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: The brief Measure to which I
ask the House to give a Second Reading
to-day is designed to remedy a defect in
the Industrial and Provident Societies
Act, 1893, which, as hon. Members will
be aware, is the principal Act under which
co-operative societies are constituted and
registered. It is provided in Section 22
of that Act that the rules shall bind the
society and all its members, and all persons claiming through them, just as if
each member had covenanted to conform
thereto. Now it happens in some cases
that a rule is introduced requiring members of a society to take up and pay for
additional shares from time to time; such
a rule may apply to societies as well as
to individuals, so that a society may find
itself forced to take up further shares in
a parent society or an organizing body
of which it is a member. Hon. Members
will agree that it is very undesirable that
the members of
a society should be
obliged to incur such liabilities against
their will.
The matter came under notice in Great
Britain a few years ago where a case was
contested in the courts and carried to the
House of Lords, whose decision, based on
a strict interpretation of the existing
law, compelled a certain society to take
up shares and thus incur obligations
which had never been envisaged by the
society or its members. The matter has
recently been put right in Great Britain
by the passing of a short Measure in almost identical terms with the Bill now
before the House. I may say I am not
aware that any cases of the kind have
arisen in Northern Ireland, but hon.
Senators will agree that it is desirable to
safeguard the position of the members of
Northern Ireland societies.
Briefly, the purport of the Bill is that
members of Industrial and Provident
Societies will be protected from having
increased liabilities imposed upon them
until their consent is given in writing.
I beg to move.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
whole House for to-morrow.
Midwives and Nursing Homes Bill
(N.I.) [S.].
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): I am sure that the House
will welcome the introduction of this
Bill. The first part deals with some
matters in connection with the Midwives
(Ireland) Act, 1918, which have been.
found, as a result of experience, to require amendment. The second, and
perhaps more important, part of the Bill
deals with the registration of nursing and
maternity homes with the object of ensuring a higher standard for these institutions. In the majority of cases these
homes do maintain a high standard, both
as to premises and as to staffing arrangements; but it is very desirable that this
standard should be general, and the Government are of opinion that the provisions
of this Bill, which is similar to that
passed by the Imperial Parliament some
time ago, will make for increased
efficiency.
As I have pointed out, the first part of
the Bill deals with certain amendments
to the Midwives Act of 1918, and of these
the important one is that contained in the
first Clause, which amends the second
Sub-section of Section 1 of the Act of
1918. That Section provided that no
woman should take or use the title of
"Midwife" unless she was certified
under that Act, and, further, that no
woman should habitually attend women
in childbirth otherwise than under the
direction of a registered medical practitioner unless she was certified under the
Act. The object of the Section was, of
course, to ensure that a person with
proper qualifications should be in charge
in every case of childbirth, and the importance of this cannot be exaggerated,
having regard to the dangers of unskilled.
treatment in this matter.
It has, however, been found that the
provisions of the Act of 1918, to which
I have referred, leave loopholes for practice by unqualified persons. For instance, the Section only forbids attendance by an unqualified woman; it
does not forbid attendance by an unqualified male person, and, accordingly,
it is proposed to substitute for the
second Sub-section of Section 1 of
the Act of 1918, a new Sub-section, making it an offence for any unqualified
person, whether man or woman, to
Nursing Homes Bill--
Second Reading
5 MARCH 1929
attend a woman in childbirth otherwise
than under the direction and personal
supervision of a duly qualified medical
practitioner. It will not be sufficient
for the medical attendant merely to give
directions to an unqualified person.
He
must remain in personal charge of the
case. Provision, of course, is made to
meet cases where assistance is rendered in
the case of a sudden emergency; and
also it is necessary to provide for the
case of persons undergoing training with
a view to becoming a duly qualified medical practitioner or certified midwife.
the
The remaining amendments to
Midwives Act are matters of minor importance, and I need not detain the
House very long over them. Section 22
of the Act provides for the summoning
of medical assistance by the midwife in
attendance in cases of emergency, and
makes the local authority, that is, the
County Council or County Borough, responsible for the payment of the doctor's fee
"unless the patient is entitled to medical
relief under Section 9 of the Poor Relief
(Ireland) Act, 1851." This wording has
given rise to some administrative difficulty,
and to disputes between the local authority and the Guardians as to the liability
for payment. On the one hand, it was
contended that these words meant that
if the circumstances of the patient were
such as would have entitled her to apply
for relief under the Medical Charges Act
the Guardians were liable whether she had
taken the necessary steps or not.
On the other hand, it was contended
that the patient was only entitled to
receive relief when she had taken the prescribed steps to obtain it, that is, that
she had applied to the Guardians and
had received the usual ticket. It is
desirable that there should be some
practical test for determining whether the
Guardians or the local authority are liable
in a particular case, and, accordingly,
the Bill lays down, in effect, that where
the patient has obtained the ticket under
the Poor Relief Act, entitling her to
medical attendance, including the attendance by a certified midwife, the Guardians are to bear any necessary expense
of calling a doctor in an emergency, and
that in any other cases the expense is to
be borne by the County Council.
Although the County Council is liable in
the first instance to the medical practitioner, it is entitled to recover the fee
if the circumstances of the patient enable
her to make repayment, and a small
amendment is contained in the Bill to
enable the County Council to accept repayment by instalments.
A
The third Clause enables the Joint
Nursing and Midwives Council to divide
the roll of midwives into two parts.
number of midwives for various reasons
from time to time cease practising, and
it may be convenient to divide the roll
into two parts, namely, the first part,
containing the names of persons who are
in active practice, and the other part
containing the names of persons who are
still entitled to have their names upon
the roll, but who do not practice. This
is a matter of convenience, and will tend
to make the first part of the roll containing the names of midwives in active
practice, which is published annually,
more useful, especially to medical practitioners.
It is desirable that midwives who are
qualified should wear a distinctive badge
when practising their profession. There
is some doubt as to the power of the Joint
Nursing Council to make a rule to this
effect, and the fourth Clause puts this
matter beyond doubt.
Section 11 of the Act of 1918 provides
for the reciprocal treatment of midwives
who have become qualified in the various
parts of the Empire. The Joint Nursing
and Midwives Council may accept their
existing qualifications and enter their
names upon the register, but under the
existing provisions they must exact the
same fee as would be required from a
midwife who had undergone her training,
and obtained her qualifications under the
supervision of the Council. This is not
considered reasonable, and provision is
made enabling the Council to fix a fee,
provided it is not to exceed the fee which
they would have charged under the existing conditions.
I now come to the second part of the
Bill, which deals with the registration
and inspection of nursing and maternity
homes. The sixth Clause requires that
every nursing and maternity home shall
be registered with the local authority,
and for this purpose the local authority
is the County Council or the County
Borough Council, as the case may be.
The Clause sets out the reasons for which
the local authority may refuse to register
an applicant. They are set out on pages
4, 5 and 6 of the Bill, which hon. Mem-
35 Nursing Homes Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Viscount Charlemont]
bers have in their hands.
speaking, the grounds on
applicant may be refused are:-
Broadly
which
an
(1) The unsuitability of the applicant
or of some person employed by him
in the home.
(2) The unsuitability of the premises
and equipment.
(3) The unsuitability of the staff.
First, they may refuse it if the applicant or any person employed by him at
the home is not a fit person, whether by
reason of age or otherwise, to carry on
or be employed at a nursing home of such
a description as applied for. In the
next place, they may refuse the application on the ground that for reasons connected with the situation, construction,
accommodation, staffing, or equipment,
the home or any premises used in connection therewith are not fit to be used
for a nursing home of the kind for which
application is made. In the third place,
the application may be refused on the
ground that the nursing home or any
premises used in connection therewith are
used or to be used for purposes which are
in any way improper or undesirable in
the case of a nursing home of the description applied for.
The remaining reasons are those connected with the qualification of the staff,
and here it is necessary to draw a distinction between homes which are in existence
at the date of the passing of the Act and
those which come into existence afterwards. There are a number of homes
where the person in charge is not a qualified nurse though she has at her disposal
a staff which is properly qualified, and
it would create hardship to apply the
stringent provisions which are appropriate to new homes to these cases.
Taking first the case of nursing homes
other than maternity homes, the Bill provides that where a nursing home was not
in existence at the commencement of this
part of the Act the local authority may
refuse the application on the ground that
the home is not under the charge of a
person who is either a duly qualified
medical practitioner or a qualified nurse,
who is resident in the home, or that there
is not a proper proportion of qualified
nurses among the persons having the
superintendence of or employed in the
nursing of the patients in the home. In
the case of a home which was in existence
at the commencement of this part of the
Act, the requirement is that the nursing
of the patients in the home shall be under
the superintendence of a qualified nurse,
who is resident in the home, even though
the person who is in general charge
of the home may not be qualified.
In the case of a maternity home not
in existence at the crucial date, the
application may be refused on the ground
that the person having the superintendence of the nursing of the patients in
the home is not either a qualified nurse
or certified midwife, or that any person
employed in attendance upon a patient
in the home is not either a duly qualified
medical practitioner, certified midwife, or
a pupil midwife.
Lastly, in the case of a home which
was in existence at the commencement of
this part of the Act the authority may
refuse the application on the ground that
any person employed in attending on any
patient is not either a duly qualified
practitioner, a certified midwife, or a
pupil midwife.
The House will thus see that the Bill
provides that in all cases the staff actually
engaged in nursing the patients must be
properly qualified whether the home was
in existence at the commencement of the
Act or not, but that in the case of existing homes the person in general charge of
the home need not necessarily possess
nursing qualifications. The Government
does not consider that this distinction
should be permanent. It is reasonable
to provide that the unqualified persons in
charge at present shall not be disturbed,
subject to proper arrangements being
made as to the nursing of the patients by
qualified persons, but when the unqualified persons in charge cease to hold office
it is considered that they should be replaced by others holding proper nursing
qualifications, and, accordingly, the Bill
makes provision to the effect that when
an unqualified person ceases to hold office
in an existing home that home shall become subject to the rules laid down for
a home not in existence at the commencement of the Act.
As hon. Members will have realized, the
Bill proposes to put very considerable
powers in the hands of the local authorities, and it is considered that the applicant for registration should have some
form of appeal from the decision of the
Nursing Homes Bill-
Second Reading
5 MARCH 1929
This is
authority refusing registration.
an instance in which it is desirable that
decision on an appeal should be given at
an early date, and the Government is of
opinion that the most suitable tribunals
for hearing these appeals are the courts of
summary jurisdiction, but that, on the
whole, it is better that for this purpose
the court should be composed of one or
more resident magistrates.
I think that the only other Clause to
which I need refer is that which provides
for inspection. Clause 10 authorizes inspection of nursing homes by an officer
duly authorized either by the local
authority or by the Ministry of Home
Affairs. Having regard to the matters
which the local authority must take into
account in considering applications for
registration, it is obvious that powers of
inspection are necessary, but it is made
clear in the Clause that this power of inspection does not include the power to
inspect the medical records of the various
cases in the home. Any such power
would clearly be open to strong objection.
These are the main features of the Bill,
which I trust will commend itself to the
House. There has been an insistent demand for this legislation, both on the part
of the medical and nursing professions,
and the Government has no doubt that
this Measure of supervision of nursing
homes will be to the public advantage. I
beg to move.
Colonel SINCLAIR: When the noble
Viscount, in the last Session, indicated
that this Measure would be brought forward in the Senate, I ventured to express
the hope that the corresponding Measure
passed by the Imperial Parliament would
not be too minutely followed. and that
we in Northern Ireland would be presented with a Bill that would be universal
in its application, and would meet with
the fullest public approval. I am happy
to think that this is the character of the
Bill that is now presented to this House,
and after the very lucid explanation the
noble Viscount has favoured us with it
will not be necessary to discuss on a
Second Reading speech the various details
of the Measure. But with your permission, Mr. Speaker, I would like to
refer to one or two matters that I think
should be emphasised and which will perhaps lead the Senate to welcome the Bill.
In the first place, at the foot of page 7,
line 40, there is a provision for the noti-
It
fication of any death occurring in a
nursing home, and the cause thereof.
is of the very greatest importance that
the cause of deaths occurring in a nursing
or maternity home should be ascertained.
It is very desirable, in the first place,
that the cause should be ascertained before death takes place, and that some
guarantee should be forthcoming that at
any rate the certificate of the doctor
should be based upon some conception of
the nature of the disease. But if hon.
Senators have been reading the London
newspapers during the past two or three
weeks they cannot readily have failed to
be struck by the comments of coroners,
magistrates and judges on the occurrence
of death at institutions or nursing or
maternity homes where there was no person resident who was entitled to certify
the cause of death, and it was necessary
in such cases to ascertain the cause of
death by means of a post-mortem examination.
any
met-
It
There are a great variety of methods
of treatment, but in the case of
hod of treatment requiring the assistance
of a nursing home for the purposes of
that treatment there is no reason why
that home should in any way be exempted
from the provisions of this Measure.
should be inspected, and anything in the
nature of partiality or favouritism would
be deplorable. It would destroy the
whole object of this Bill, and if one particular kind of treatment is exempted
then another might reasonably on the
same ground claim exemption from inspection.
I am happy to think that the nursing
and maternity homes in Northern Ireland, so far as I know them, need have no
fear of this Bill. I think a large number
of them will welcome it, because in most
cases they are admirably conducted, and
The certificate of an inspector will be more
or less a substantial testimonial as to the
efficiency and orderliness of a nursing or
maternity home, as the case may be.
Therefore, not only on the ground of
public policy and in the interests of the
community in general do I welcome this
Measure which the noble Viscount has
just expounded to us so clearly, but even
those who are more directly affected by it
will welcome it also.
The other part of the Bill to which I
might allude is that which deals with the
definition of a nursing home. In the
Imperial Act there is a distinction drawn
421777
Nursing Homes Bill--
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Colonel Sinclair]
between a nursing home and a nursing
house. If sick people are admitted to an
institution for the purposes of treatment,
then it is only playing with words to draw
a distinction between a nursing home and
a nursing house. However, the definition
is quite clear in the Bill before us, and I
am glad the Imperial Act has not been
closely followed in this respect and that
In this Bill the term is simply nursing and
maternity home, a description which the
public will best understand. I also am
glad to notice in this Bill what I think
most medical men and certainly all
patients will deem to be an unalienable
right, and that is that while inspection is
carried out in a thorough manner, no
inspection of the personal records of the
patients that might be kept for medical
purposes is permitted. The details regarding the patient's medical record,
which are regarded as sacred between the
patient and the physician will not be
inspected or dealt with in any way.
I will not detain the House any further
except to say how warmly I welcome the
presentation of this Measure, and I hope
that other members will see their way to
support it. I thank the Government for
having introduced it and the noble Viscount for his clear explanation of the details, which, I think, will make perfectly
clear to anyone without any technical
practice or knowledge the true import of
the Bill and the necessity for it at the
present stage.
Mr. LESLIE: The Bill which has been
presented to us this afternoon by the
noble Viscount, and which has been
supported so eloquently, if I may say so,
by the hon. and gallant Member opposite
(Col. Sinclair) is so sure of a sympathetic
reception in this House that I confess that
I am somewhat afraid lest its importance
may be minimised and lest it may be
thought that it does not need so full an
examination as appears to me to be
necessary. As a matter of fact I very
nearly asked the noble Viscount, the
Leader of the House, to give us a few
more days to consider this Measure before
approaching the Second Reading, particularly in order that I might fortify myself by reference to the Imperial Debates
on the Measures of 1927 and 1928, and
ascertain, if I could, whether some of the
ideas formed in my mind really had any
basis, or whether they related to matters
already dealt with and disposed of.
However, I do not think that delay is
really necessary, because after all, if one
makes a suggestion on imperfect information, there is no great harm done provided.
no false impression remains behind.
The points of the Bill are rather difficult, and we do need all the assistance we
can get. We want, to some extent, the
assistance of the lawyer, in order that we
may be quite sure that we are saying what
We
we mean and meaning all we say.
want the assistance, as we have just had
it, from the medical profession, in order
that we may be kept straight in technical
details, and we want the assistance of
ordinary common sense and philanthropic
instinct, in order that we shall not go too
far, and that in our attempts to set too
high an ideal we fail to make the best use
of such material as may be available and
to lead people into difficulties which might
otherwise be avoided. But I am confirmed in my feeling by reflecting on the
very short life which the English Act of
1926 enjoyed, the bulk of which, relating
to maternity homes, was repealed in the
following year, and, accordingly, I certainly do not think we should feel ourselves bound to follow the English Act.
I am glad we do not do so, for I welcome
the omission of what was Section 7 of the
English Act of 1927, to which I need not
refer. I recognise there is certainly no
undue hardship in the Bill anywhere, but
there is difficulty.
to
in
It seems to
me, speaking subject
correction, and as one very much
need of correction on this technical matter, that we have difficulty in the
first part of the Bill. It happened to me
last month for the first time in my life,
that when sitting on an ordinary magisterial bench in the country, a woman was
charged with offending against that Subsection (2) of Section 1 of the Act of
1918, for which it is now desired to provide a substitute. The facts were that an
unqualified woman had been attending
several women and one case occurred
where there had been a child and the child
had died, and there had been a coroner's
inquest. The coroner thought it necessary to warn the woman before she gave
evidence. She was charged that she had
""
habitually and for gain attended." and
so on. The bench was a fairly strong one.
The chairman and one other magistrate
senior to myself attended, and the resident magistrate was there. It was found
necessary to dismiss the case, but with a
very strong warning to the defendant as
to her future conduct.
Nursing Homes Bill-
Second Reading
5 MARCH 1929
The impression left on my mind-and I
did not then know the provisions of this
bill now before us-was that in ordinary
circumstances the old provision, which it is
proposed to repeal, is now unworkable.
You had to establish far too much, and
the opportunity for defence by way of excuse was far too wide. That really brings
me to what I am about to say about the
most important word in Part 1, and that
is the word childbirth." I am very
much afraid that that word is unduly restricted. I have tried as best I can in
the time at my disposal to get some legal
definition of the word. I can get none,
and I am therefore driven to the conclusion that the act of childbirth, in the eyes
of the law, terminates when the child is
born. The point used to be of importance in criminal trials, but it has been
less so since the Infanticide Act was
passed, and for those purposes a child was
held to be born when complete extrusion
had taken place, irrespective of the severance of the umbilical cord.
If that is so and if that is the absolute
definition of the word I think it will be
agreed that that moment is far too soon
for the introduction of an unqualified
person and far too late for summoning
proper assistance. We do not want to
encourage procrastination in a matter of
that kind, and we do not want to admit
such a defence as "the child was born
before I got there." I shall attempt at
a later stage to ask the House to insert
words there, probably the words "immediately after," which appear in a later
place in the Bill, because I think it might
be better than to attempt a definition.
If we can do that I believe Part 1 of the
Bill will be all that we could make it. I
think I am right in saying the term is,
as I have mentioned, restricted, and if
we can we must extend it. We have to
contend against both carelessness and
ignorance as well as poverty, but I think
we ought to do all we can.
Passing to Part 2, the machinery of the
Bill, I do not myself greatly like giving
this power to the County Councils. It is
adding to their burdens. I know they
have already to act as the local supervising authority, under the old Act of 1918.
I cannot myself recollect that when I
served on a County Council many years
ago it gave us any great trouble.
imagine those matters were dealt with
then, and probably the Chairman of the
Londonderry County Council will be able
to tell us whether they are now dealt with
I
through the Finance and Law Committees. But the question does arise of
removing or cancelling a certificate that
has been issued. There is no provision in
this Bill, and there is none in either of
the English Acts, allowing a summary
cancellation of a licence by a Court which
has convicted somebody connected with
the home of an offence.
We have provided, as the noble Viscount said, for an appeal from the decision
of the County Council. I think that perhaps the House knows I am always in
favour of giving anybody the right of
appeal if they are aggrieved, and I do not
think this case is any exception to the
rule. At the same time, I do not think
an appeal to a Resident Magistrate sitting alone is a proper appeal or an appeal
of any value whatever. In England an
appeal given is to an ordinary bench of
magistrates with a further appeal over to
a Court of Quarter Sessions. I do not
myself think that a bench of ordinary
magistrates is on the whole a good appeal
in a matter of this kind. They have advantages, perhaps, and disadvantages.
You might find on a bench of magistrates,
in fact you would find, local knowledge,
which might be good and might be bad.
You might find women magistrates, and
there women would be of value.
might find the dispensary doctor, whose
opinion might be of value, but he might
already have come into the matter, and,
therefore, he ought not to sit. You might
find a county councillor or the chairman
of the rural district council, and they
ought not to sit either. Those are objections to an ordinary bench of magistrates.
You
Taking a resident magistrate sitting
alone, he has not got local knowledge, he
is not a doctor, he is not a woman, possibly he is something of a lawyer; but I
greatly doubt whether appeals likely to be
brought against the decisions of county
councils would be really matters of
law at all. If you want a lawyer I would
prefer you should make the appeal to the
County Court Judge or Recorder sitting
alone. There is no object in making an
appeal to Quarter Sessions, because generally it would be as bad as appealing to the
local bench, and I do not think it is quite
in accordance with the dignity of a county
council that we should put appeals before
either a bench of local magistrates or a
resident magistrate. This is an Amendment which I will move later.
It seems to me that this is a case in
which we must get the best appeal we can,
Nursing Homes Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
and appeal to an authority which can combine all those possible attributes which
I suggested might be found on a
thoroughly satisfactory bench, and the
authority I suggest is the Ministry of
Home Affairs. I think it would be far
better that an appeal should lie to the
Ministry, because the Ministry has a
legal adviser, and it has got the valuable
assistance of its medical department and
medical inspectors. I think everybody
would be satisfied with an appeal to the
Ministry, and it would be far better than
an appeal to the magistrates. I will
make that suggestion.
I pass now to the nursing home, again,
I am afraid, rather as one seeking for information. I am a little in the dark
about it, but I am inclined to wonder
whether we do not want something more
in the way of definition. I am entirely
at one with the hon. and gallant Member
(Colonel Sinclair) in desiring that there
should be no distinction between a nursing home and a nursing house, but we
have to look at the expression "nursing
home." We are embarking on an undertaking which is entirely new. It is the
first time we have tried to deal with
homes of this kind. We all know perfectly well what we mean by nursing
home. The question is whether we have
made ourselves clear. The word "nursing," so far as I have been able to ascertain, is only used in one other Statute,
or perhaps Code of Statutes, and that is
in the Children Act. In that Act it is
always "children, nursing and maintenance," and that is a composite expression
which is not intended to be separated.
Nursing, on the other hand, means
curative, remedial, perhaps only palliative treatment, and may go on for an
indefinite length of time. It may be
applied to any person, young or old, and
I think we have to ask the noble Viscount
to tell us that he is quite satisfied that
we are not in any way running counter to
the Children Act by any of our proposals.
It will be remembered that where the
Children Act deals with children boardedout, and requires that regulations should
be observed, and notification should be
given and so on, that that is applied to
people who receive children for reward,
just as in the Section of the Midwives
Act of 1918, which we are repealing, we
spoke of "attendance habitually and for
gain." We have not got the qualification
"for reward" to help us here in regard
to nursing homes. I think we are better
without it, because I can readily understand that a nursing home that advertises itself as being a place where assisttance was given without payment might
be an extremely undesirable establishment.
I think we must be sure about that,
and we must be quite clear that we have
not got at the ordinary private-house. I
do not mean
a private house which is
really used as a nursing establishment,
but, as we know, there are instances of
illness occurring in the most fortunate of
families, and if a person who meets with
an accident or illness belongs to a house
and is nursed there, obviously that is
outside the sphere of this Bill. Suppose
it is not quite so plain as that, and suppose some householder takes some friends
or relatives to live with him in order that
they may be nursed there, and are nursed
there, the question is whether those
premises are in themselves being used for
nursing. That is not what we mean, I
am sure; but the question is if we have
said it, and if we have gone a shade too
far, and if so, can we set it right. You
want to stop that which is wrong, and you
do not want to interfere with that which
is normal. I do not think I need labour
that further. It may be that there
any
are decisions which will be quoted, and
which will keep us quite straight. I
support the Bill, and I hope it will effect
some, at least, of the good which it sets
out to accomplish.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I should
like to thank the House for the very
favourable reception it has given this
Bill. I am afraid I cannot, at this stage,
give the assurance the hon. and learned
Member (Mr. Leslie) wishes me to give,
but I will make inquiries into the points
he has raised before the Bill comes up for
further consideration.
On Question, Motion (That the Bill be
now read a Second time) agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
whole House for Wednesday, 13th March.

move, "That this House do now
adjourn."
